topics.title: Introduction to the Concept of Quality - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to the Concept of Quality - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Quality

The term **"Quality"** is one of the most debated, multidimensional constructs in management and engineering literature. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Six Sigma (PEMRT752), the introductory module requires students to understand that **Quality is not a single, monolithic idea** but a layered concept that has evolved over more than a century of industrial thought.

### Universal Standardized Definition (ISO 9000:2015)

The most academically rigorous and internationally accepted definition is provided by **ISO 9000:2015**:

> **"Quality is the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfills requirements."**

Where:
- **Object** = product, process, service, system, person, or any combination thereof.
- **Inherent characteristics** = permanent features of the object (as opposed to assigned characteristics).
- **Requirements** = needs or expectations that are stated, generally implied, or obligatory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For board examinations, students must quote **at least 3 definitions** of Quality — one from a Guru (Juran/Deming/Crosby), one from ISO, and one applied/operational definition. Quoting only one is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

---

### 1.2 The "Guru" Definitions (Historical & Board-Relevant)

Each quality guru defined quality through the lens of their era's industrial challenges. Understanding these is critical because KTU question papers frequently test the "who said what" mapping.

**Table 1.1 — Definitional Lexicon of Quality Gurus**

| Guru | Era | Formal Definition | Core Philosophy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Joseph M. Juran** | 1950s onward | **"Quality is Fitness for Use."** | Customer-oriented; quality must be planned, not inspected. |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 1950s onward | **"Quality should be aimed at the needs of the customer, present and future."** | System-driven; reduce variation through statistical thinking. |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | 1960s–70s | **"Quality is Conformance to Requirements."** | Zero-defect mindset; prevention over inspection. |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | 1950s–60s | **"Total Quality Control (TQC)."** | Quality is everyone's job, not just the QC department. |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | 1970s–80s | **"Quality is a continuous, never-ending process."** | Company-wide quality control (CWQC) with people participation. |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | 1980s onward | **"Quality is the loss imparted to society from the time the product is shipped."** | Loss-function view; quality loss starts the moment design deviates from target. |

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Restaurant Test"**
>
> Imagine you order a **biryani** from a restaurant. The dish arrives.
>
> - If it is **hot, fresh, well-spiced, served on time, hygienic, and matches your expectation** → you call it **"good quality."**
> - If it is **cold, undercooked, delayed, or stale** → you call it **"poor quality."**
>
> This simple interaction reveals the **5 universal pillars of quality**:
>
> 1. **Fitness for Use** (does it satisfy hunger?) — *Juran*
> 2. **Conformance to Specification** (right ingredients, right process) — *Crosby*
> 3. **Customer Satisfaction** (tastes good to YOU) — *Deming*
> 4. **Consistency** (same taste every visit) — *Statistical Process Control*
> 5. **Value** (worth the price) — *Feigenbaum's Total Quality*
>
> **Quality is, therefore, a PROMISE kept between the producer and the customer.** When the gap between *promise* and *performance* is zero, quality is achieved.

---

### 1.4 The Garvin Framework — 8 Dimensions of Quality

**David A. Garvin** (1984) of Harvard Business School decomposed Quality into **eight distinct, non-overlapping dimensions**. This framework is **high-weightage for KTU Part A 3-mark questions**.

1. **Performance** – Primary operating characteristics (e.g., speed of a car).
2. **Features** – Secondary characteristics that add value (e.g., Bluetooth in a car).
3. **Reliability** – Probability of failure-free operation over time.
4. **Conformance** – Degree to which design/operating characteristics meet standards.
5. **Durability** – Useful life including repair before replacement.
6. **Serviceability** – Speed, courtesy, and competence of repair.
7. **Aesthetics** – Sensory appeal (look, feel, sound, taste).
8. **Perceived Quality** – Subjective image/reputation (e.g., "Apple = premium").

---

### 1.5 The Four Universal Views of Quality

Researchers broadly categorize all definitions into **four philosophical views** (Reeves & Bednar, 1994). This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question framing**.

- **Transcendent View** – Quality is innate excellence; you cannot define it precisely, only recognize it. *("I know it when I see it.")*
- **Product-Based View** – Quality is a precise, measurable variable; differences in quality reflect differences in the quantity of some ingredient/attribute.
- **User-Based View** – Quality is in the eye of the beholder — the customer. Best quality = best satisfaction.
- **Manufacturing-Based View** – Quality is conformance to requirements/specifications — the producer's perspective.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Sigma's Stance:** Six Sigma synthesizes the **user-based view** (Voice of Customer) and **manufacturing-based view** (DPMO ≤ 3.4), unifying customer satisfaction with engineering precision.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Quality Needs a Definition — The Operational Gap

Before defining quality mathematically, engineers must understand **why** a single definition is insufficient:

> In real KTU examination scenarios, a production manager and a marketing manager in the same firm often disagree on what "good quality" means. The marketing manager sees **customer delight**; the production manager sees **zero defects**. A unified framework is therefore essential for Six Sigma's DMAIC methodology to function.

### 2.2 The Evolution of the Quality Concept (Historical Timeline)

The KTU syllabus demands that students trace the **chronological evolution** of quality from mere inspection to modern Six Sigma.

**Table 2.1 — Quality Eras and Their Defining Characteristics**

| Era | Approx. Period | Focus | Trigger / Crisis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inspection Era** | 1900–1930 | Sorting good from bad | Mass production (Ford) |
| **Quality Control (QC) Era** | 1930–1950 | Statistical process control | Shewhart's control charts (1931) |
| **Quality Assurance (QA) Era** | 1950–1980 | System-wide prevention | Japanese post-war reconstruction |
| **Strategic Quality Management (SQM)** | 1980–1995 | Customer-driven strategy | Malcolm Baldrige Award (1987) |
| **Six Sigma Era** | 1995–Present | Data-driven perfection | Motorola & GE adoption |

### 2.3 Core Engineering Metrics Derived from the Definition

Once "Quality = Conformance to Requirements" is accepted, every defect becomes measurable. The **foundation formula** for every Six Sigma project is the **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**.

**Table 2.2 — KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for Quality Metrics**

| Metric | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | Defects per million opportunities | ppm (parts per million) |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units Produced}}{\text{Total Units Started}}$ | Yield without rework | Percentage (%) |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Yield across $n$ process steps | Percentage (%) |
| **Process Sigma Level** | Derived from DPMO using standard normal table | Capability of process | Sigma ($\sigma$) |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$ | Monetary loss due to defects | Currency (₹ / $) |
| **Cpk** | $\text{Cpk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process capability index | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Pipe Warning:** In LaTeX formulas inside markdown tables, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical bar `|` (e.g., write $\vert x \vert$ rather than $|x|$) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 The Six Sigma Quality Benchmark

The defining numerical threshold of Six Sigma quality is:

$$\text{DPMO}_{\text{Six Sigma}} = 3.4 \text{ defects per million opportunities}$$

This corresponds to a process operating with **±6 standard deviations between the mean and the nearest specification limit**, allowing a **1.5σ shift** in the long run (Motorola's convention, 1986).

**Interpretation Table:**

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Real-World Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 2σ | 308,537 | ~30.85% defective |
| 3σ | 66,807 | ~6.68% defective |
| 4σ | 6,210 | ~0.62% defective |
| 5σ | 233 | ~0.023% defective |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **0.00034% defective — World-class** |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Production lines benchmark against DPMO; a 4σ process is "good," but 6σ is "world-class."
- **Healthcare:** Medication error rates are tracked in DPMO; a hospital moving from 4σ to 5σ in dispensing saves thousands of lives annually.
- **IT/Software:** Defects per KLOC (thousand lines of code) measure software quality; ISO 25010 uses this lineage.
- **Service Sector:** Transaction errors per million in banking (e.g., ATM mis-postings) are measured using this framework.

### 2.6 Concept of "Quality" vs. "Grade"

A frequently confused KTU exam point:

> **Quality** = degree of conformance to specifications (a desired attribute).
> **Grade** = category/designation assigned based on features (a design choice).

A **lemon** and a **premium car** may both have **zero defects (high quality)** — but the lemon is of **lower grade**. Quality is independent of grade.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Definitions Mapped to Frameworks

**Table 3.1 — Cross-Mapping of Quality Definitions to Operational Frameworks**

| Definition Source | Focus | Metric Used | Six Sigma Link |
|---|---|---|---|
| Juran (Fitness for Use) | Customer satisfaction | Customer complaints, returns | Voice of Customer (VOC) in Define phase |
| Deming (Customer Needs) | System, variation | Process capability indices (Cpk) | Measure & Analyze phases |
| Crosby (Conformance) | Zero defects | DPMO, FPY | Control phase |
| Feigenbaum (TQC) | Organization-wide | COPQ | Improve phase |
| ISO 9000 (Inherent Characteristics) | Standardization | Audit conformance | Framework |
| Taguchi (Loss Function) | Society-wide loss | Quality loss ($L = k(Y - m)^2$) | Robust design |

## 3.2 Derivation of DPMO Formula — Step-by-Step

We now derive the DPMO formula rigorously, which is the **numerical embodiment** of "Quality = Conformance to Requirements."

### Step 1 — Establish the Base Ratio

Start with the simplest measure of non-conformance:

$$\text{Defect Rate} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities}}$$

**Explanation:** A "defect" is any failure to meet a requirement; an "opportunity" is each chance for such a failure in a unit. This is dimensionless.

### Step 2 — Normalize to Per-Unit Basis

Since multiple opportunities exist per unit (e.g., a car has 500 components, each a defect opportunity), normalize:

$$\text{Defects per Unit} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units}}$$

**Explanation:** This gives the average defect count per single unit produced.

### Step 3 — Expand Denominator to Include Opportunities

Replace the simple "number of units" with "units × opportunities per unit" to standardize across products of different complexity:

$$\text{Defect Rate (normalized)} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$$

**Explanation:** This makes quality comparable across, say, a 10-component bolt and a 5,000-component aircraft engine.

### Step 4 — Scale to One Million for Industrial Readability

Multiply by $10^6$ to express in **parts per million (ppm)** — a universally readable industrial unit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Industries prefer ppm because defect rates are extremely small (e.g., 0.00034%); scaling up avoids leading zeros.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A manufacturer produces 10,000 smartphones. Each phone has 80 functional test points (opportunities). During testing, 240 defects are detected. Calculate DPMO.

**Given:**
- Number of Units = 10,000
- Opportunities per Unit = 80
- Number of Defects = 240

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Opportunities} &= 10{,}000 \times 80 = 800{,}000 \\
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{240}{800{,}000} \times 10^{6} \\
&= 0.0003 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 300 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** 300 DPMO corresponds to approximately **4.5σ**, an excellent process but not yet Six Sigma level.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Process Sigma Level from DPMO

The conversion from DPMO to a sigma level uses the **standard normal distribution** (Z-tables) with Motorola's **1.5σ long-term shift correction**.

### Step 1 — Convert DPMO to a Proportion

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the probability of a single defect at a single opportunity.

### Step 2 — Convert Probability to Z-Score (Two-Tail for "Nearest Spec Limit")

Since defects can occur on either side of specification, use the two-tailed Z-value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{p}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\Phi^{-1}$ is the inverse standard normal cumulative distribution function.

### Step 3 — Apply the 1.5σ Shift Correction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Process Sigma Level} &= Z + 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Motorola's empirically observed average drift in real processes is 1.5σ over time; this correction converts short-term capability to long-term expected performance.

### Step 4 — Worked Example

**Problem:** Convert DPMO = 3.4 to Process Sigma Level.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \frac{3.4}{10^{6}} = 0.0000034 \\
\frac{p}{2} &= 0.0000017 \\
1 - \frac{p}{2} &= 0.9999983 \\
Z &= \Phi^{-1}(0.9999983) \approx 4.5 \\
\text{Process Sigma Level} &= 4.5 + 1.5 = 6.0\ \sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This is the mathematical proof that **DPMO = 3.4 ⟺ 6σ**, which is the formal numerical definition of Six Sigma quality.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Taguchi Loss Function

Taguchi operationalized quality by quantifying the **loss to society** whenever a product deviates from its target $m$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(Y) &= k \cdot (Y - m)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $L$ = Quality loss (in monetary units)
- $Y$ = Actual performance value of the product
- $m$ = Target value
- $k$ = Quality loss coefficient (a constant)

### Determination of $k$

If at the specification limit (USL) the loss equals the cost of replacement $A_0$, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= k \cdot (\text{USL} - m)^{2} \\
k &= \frac{A_0}{(\text{USL} - m)^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** A resistor of target $100\ \Omega$ has a USL of $110\ \Omega$. Replacement cost $A_0 = \text{₹}50$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{50}{(110 - 100)^{2}} = \frac{50}{100} = 0.5\ \text{₹}/\Omega^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

So a resistor of $105\ \Omega$ incurs a loss of:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(105) &= 0.5 \times (105 - 100)^{2} = 0.5 \times 25 = \text{₹}12.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Insight:** Even within specifications, a product causes financial loss — Taguchi's definition makes **every deviation costly**, not just out-of-spec failures.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define Quality. List any two definitions of Quality given by quality gurus.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** Quality is the degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfills requirements (ISO 9000:2015).

**Two Guru Definitions:**

1. **Joseph M. Juran:** "Quality is Fitness for Use." — Quality means the product must satisfy the customer's intended purpose across all features.

2. **Philip B. Crosby:** "Quality is Conformance to Requirements." — A product is of high quality if it meets the specifications defined during planning, with zero defects being the performance standard.

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for standard definition + 1 mark each for two guru definitions.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Explain the concept of "Grade" and "Quality" with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Quality** is the degree to which a product conforms to established specifications and standards — it is about **how well** something is made.
- **Grade** is a category or classification assigned to products based on design features, performance characteristics, or intended use — it is about **what type** of product it is.

**Example:** A **Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra** (premium grade) and a **Samsung Galaxy A15** (economy grade) can both have **zero defects** (high quality). The difference is in the grade (features, performance, materials), not in the quality of manufacturing. Conversely, a defective S24 Ultra is high-grade but low-quality.

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark definition each + 1 mark for correct example distinction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the evolution of the concept of quality from the Inspection Era to the Six Sigma Era. Highlight the key contributions in each phase. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define Quality according to Juran, Deming, and Crosby. Explain how these definitions collectively influence the Six Sigma philosophy. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer — Part B1 (Choice A)

---

#### Part (a) — Evolution of Quality Concept (7 Marks)

The understanding of quality has undergone five major transformations, each responding to industrial challenges of its era:

**1. Inspection Era (1900–1930):**
- **Focus:** Sorting good products from defective ones after production.
- **Trigger:** Mass production demands during and after World War I.
- **Key Figure:** Frederick Winslow Taylor (scientific management).
- **Limitation:** Quality detected, not built-in. Costly rework and scrap.

**2. Quality Control (QC) Era (1930–1950):**
- **Focus:** Statistical monitoring of processes using control charts.
- **Trigger:** Great Depression; need to reduce waste and inconsistency.
- **Key Figure:** Walter A. Shewhart (Bell Labs) — introduced the control chart in 1924.
- **Innovation:** Shift from inspection to **prevention through statistics**.

**3. Quality Assurance (QA) Era (1950–1980):**
- **Focus:** System-wide prevention; quality as a management responsibility.
- **Trigger:** Post-WWII Japanese industrial rebuild led to adoption of Deming & Juran.
- **Key Figures:** Deming, Juran, Feigenbaum, Ishikawa.
- **Innovation:** Total Quality Control (TQC), Quality Circles, Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC).

**4. Strategic Quality Management (1980–1995):**
- **Focus:** Quality as a competitive strategy and source of customer value.
- **Triggers:** Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (1987, USA); ISO 9000 standards (1987).
- **Innovation:** Customer-driven metrics, benchmarking, business excellence models.

**5. Six Sigma Era (1995–Present):**
- **Focus:** Data-driven, project-based reduction of variation to ≤ 3.4 DPMO.
- **Origin:** Motorola (1986), popularized by **Jack Welch** at GE (1995).
- **Innovation:** DMAIC methodology, Green/Black Belt certification, statistical rigor at industrial scale.

#### Part (b) — Guru Definitions & Six Sigma Linkage (7 Marks)

**Joseph M. Juran — "Quality is Fitness for Use":**
- Juran emphasized that quality must be **planned**, not inspected.
- He introduced the **Juran Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement).
- **Six Sigma Link:** Drives the **Define phase** — Voice of Customer (VOC) captures "fitness for use" expectations.

**W. Edwards Deming — "Quality should be aimed at the needs of the customer, present and future":**
- Deming focused on **system thinking** and **reduction of variation** as the path to quality.
- His **14 Points for Management** and the **PDSA cycle** (Plan-Do-Study-Act) form the operational backbone of continuous improvement.
- **Six Sigma Link:** Drives the **Measure and Analyze phases** — Cpk, DPMO, and root cause analysis all derive from Deming's statistical thinking.

**Philip B. Crosby — "Quality is Conformance to Requirements":**
- Crosby's **"Zero Defects"** philosophy argued that quality is binary — either it conforms or it does not.
- He defined the cost of quality as **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)**.
- **Six Sigma Link:** Drives the **Control phase** — Six Sigma's 3.4 DPMO target is the operational embodiment of "doing it right the first time."

**Collective Influence on Six Sigma:**
The synthesis of these three perspectives forms the philosophical bedrock of Six Sigma:
- **Customer focus** (Juran + Deming) → **Define**
- **Statistical measurement** (Deming) → **Measure + Analyze**
- **Conformance target** (Crosby) → **Improve + Control**

Six Sigma is, therefore, not a new philosophy but the **mathematical unification** of three complementary guru philosophies into a single, project-based methodology.

---

### Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the four philosophical views of Quality. Which view(s) does Six Sigma adopt? Justify. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit produces 5,000 printed circuit boards (PCBs) per shift. Each PCB undergoes 120 inspection checkpoints. In one shift, 360 defects are detected. Calculate the DPMO and identify the approximate process sigma level. Comment on the process quality. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Answer — Part B1 (Choice B)

---

#### Part (a) — Four Philosophical Views of Quality (7 Marks)

| View | Core Idea | Proponent Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Transcendent View** | Quality is an innate, absolute excellence — it cannot be precisely defined, only experienced. | Philosophical tradition; luxury branding ("I know it when I see it"). |
| **Product-Based View** | Quality is a **measurable, quantifiable variable**; differences in quality = differences in the quantity of a desired attribute. | Engineering and manufacturing; precise specifications. |
| **User-Based View** | Quality lies in the **eye of the customer**; the best product is the one that best satisfies customer needs. | Marketing and customer service; market research. |
| **Manufacturing-Based View** | Quality is **conformance to requirements and specifications**; zero defects. | Operations and production; quality control. |

**Views Adopted by Six Sigma — Justification:**

Six Sigma synthesizes the **User-Based View** and the **Manufacturing-Based View**.

- The **User-Based View** is reflected in the **Define phase**, where the Voice of Customer (VOC), CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) trees, and QFD (Quality Function Deployment) translate customer needs into measurable specifications.
- The **Manufacturing-Based View** is reflected in the **Measure, Analyze, Improve, and Control phases**, where DPMO, Cpk, control charts, and DOE quantify conformance to those specifications.

The **target of 3.4 DPMO** is precisely a manufacturing-based metric, but it is **set in service of user satisfaction** — a hallmark of Six Sigma's dual philosophical grounding.

#### Part (b) — DPMO Calculation & Sigma Level (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Number of Units = 5,000
- Opportunities per Unit = 120
- Number of Defects = 360

**Step 1: Calculate Total Opportunities**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Opportunities} &= \text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit} \\
&= 5{,}000 \times 120 = 600{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate DPMO**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Total Opportunities}} \times 10^{6} \\
&= \frac{360}{600{,}000} \times 10^{6} \\
&= 0.0006 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 600 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Identify Process Sigma Level**

Using the standard DPMO-to-Sigma conversion table:

| DPMO | Approximate Sigma Level |
|---|---|
| 233 | 5.0σ |
| **600** | **≈ 4.5σ** |
| 6,210 | 4.0σ |

Thus, the process operates at approximately **4.5σ**.

**Step 4: Comment on Process Quality**

A 4.5σ process corresponds to a defect rate of **0.06%** (600 defects per million). While this is a **good industrial process**, it falls **short of Six Sigma's 6σ (3.4 DPMO) target** by approximately 1.5 sigma levels.

**Recommendation:** The manufacturing unit should launch a DMAIC project to:
- Identify the **vital few** defect types (Pareto analysis) in the Define phase.
- Measure process stability with control charts.
- Analyze root causes via fishbone/Ishikawa diagrams.
- Improve through Design of Experiments (DOE).
- Control via standard operating procedures and SPC charts.

This improvement pathway can potentially elevate the process from 4.5σ to 5σ or 6σ, achieving **world-class quality**.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Quoting only one definition of Quality** — KTU examiners expect **at least 2–3 definitions** spanning guru, ISO, and applied contexts. Single definition = 1 mark lost.
>
> 2. **Confusing Grade with Quality** — This is a **2-mark killer** in Part A. Always state both concepts clearly with an **example involving the SAME product at different grades but with different quality levels**.
>
> 3. **Skipping the 1.5σ shift** in DPMO-to-Sigma conversion — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you directly map DPMO to Z without adding 1.5σ. The convention is **Process Sigma = Z + 1.5**.
>
> 4. **Using `|` for absolute value inside markdown tables** — This breaks the table rendering. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX formulas within tables.
>
> 5. **Writing "Quality is meeting customer requirements" without naming Juran/Deming/ISO** — Vague statements earn partial credit at best. Always **attribute** the definition.
>
> 6. **Forgetting units** — DPMO is dimensionless (per million), but Cpk is dimensionless, while COPQ must carry currency units (₹, $). Examiners check units carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Quality Definition (Module 1):**

- ✅ **Quality is multidimensional** — never use one single definition in exams.
- ✅ **Master the 6 guru definitions:** Juran, Deming, Crosby, Feigenbaum, Ishikawa, Taguchi.
- ✅ **ISO 9000:2015** definition is the **modern standard** — quote it for full marks.
- ✅ **Garvin's 8 dimensions** (Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality) are **high-frequency** in Part A.
- ✅ **Four views** (Transcendent, Product, User, Manufacturing) — Six Sigma adopts **User + Manufacturing**.
- ✅ **DPMO formula** = (Defects / (Units × Opportunities)) × 10⁶ — memorize it.
- ✅ **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 6σ with 1.5σ shift** — this numerical equivalence is **favourite 14-mark question**.
- ✅ **Quality ≠ Grade** — Quality is conformance; Grade is classification.
- ✅ **Five eras of quality:** Inspection → QC → QA → SQM → Six Sigma.
- ✅ **Taguchi Loss Function** L = k(Y - m)² — applies loss even within specifications.
- ✅ **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure.
- ✅ **Always use `\vert` instead of `|`** in LaTeX inside tables.
- ✅ **Attribute every definition** to its source — unnamed definitions are treated as paraphrasing.
- ✅ **Six Sigma integrates Juran (planning), Deming (variation), Crosby (conformance)** into a unified DMAIC framework.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Quantified
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Quantified

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Quantified** is the discipline within Six Sigma that translates the abstract notion of "quality" into **measurable, monetary, and statistical quantities** so that organizations can objectively evaluate, control, and improve their processes. It rests on three foundational pillars:

1. **The Cost of Quality (CoQ)** – expressing quality in monetary terms (Rupees / Dollars spent on getting it right or wrong).
2. **Process Performance Metrics** – expressing quality as statistical numbers (Yield, DPMO, Sigma Level, $Z$-score).
3. **Capability Indices** – expressing quality relative to customer specifications (Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk – covered in Module 2).

The bedrock principle, popularized by **Philip B. Crosby**, is the now-famous assertion:

> *"Quality is free. It is not a gift, but it is free. What costs money are the unquality things — all the actions that involve not doing jobs right the first time."*

Thus, **"Quality Quantified" = the financial + statistical translation of conformance and non-conformance.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly links *Quality Quantified* with the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**, the **PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) cost model**, and the **Six Sigma metrics family** (DPMO, Yield, Rolled Throughput Yield, Sigma Level). Mastery of these is mandatory for ESE Part B questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Bucket" Model

Imagine a company is a **bucket of water** (revenue / profit).

- **Good units produced (Yield)** = water that stays in the bucket.
- **Defects (scrap, rework, warranty, returns, customer complaints)** = **holes in the bucket** through which water leaks out.
- The faster the leak, the more the company has to **keep pouring water in (rework, inspection, warranty costs)** just to stay full.

**Quality Quantified is the engineering science of measuring**:
- **How big are the holes?** (DPMO, Defect Rate)
- **How much water is leaking per day?** (COPQ in ₹/month)
- **How far is the bucket from being perfectly sealed?** (Sigma Level / $Z$-score)

A **Six Sigma process** is essentially a bucket so well-engineered that it leaks only **3.4 drops out of a million opportunities** — a near-perfect seal.

## 1.3 The Three Quality Gurus' Views on Cost of Quality (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Guru | Concept | Key Quote |
|------|---------|-----------|
| **Joseph M. Juran** | The **"Goldmine"** — money buried in chronic waste | *"In the quality costs lie the goldmine of savings."* |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** vs. Price of Conformance | *"Quality is free — it is the un-quality that costs money."* |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | **Cost of Poor Quality** as a *systemic*, not individual, problem | *"Costs of quality are the costs of not having quality."* |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Total Quality Cost = **Hidden** + **Visible** | First to coin the term *"Total Quality Control"* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Exams — "J-C-D-F":** **J**uran (Goldmine), **C**rosby (Free but PONC), **D**eming (System cost), **F**eigenbaum (Hidden + Visible).

## 1.4 Why Quantify Quality? — The Business Imperative

Quantification is **not an academic exercise**; it is a **C-suite communication tool**:

- It converts engineering reality into **₹ / $** that CFOs and Boards understand.
- It justifies **investment in prevention** by demonstrating ROI.
- It provides a **baseline (As-Is)** against which improvement (To-Be) is measured.
- It enables **benchmarking** against industry/world-class standards.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever an exam question asks *"Why quantify quality?"*, structure your answer in **three layers**: (1) Decision-making, (2) Cost reduction, (3) Strategic benchmarking. Examiners reward structured, layered answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PAF Model — Anatomy of Quality Costs

The **Prevention–Appraisal–Failure (PAF) Model**, formalized by Feigenbaum, classifies every quality-related rupee into **four buckets**:

### 2.1.1 Prevention Costs (P)
Money spent **before** defects occur — proactive investment.
- Quality planning, training, process design, supplier capability audits, FMEA, quality system documentation.

### 2.1.2 Appraisal Costs (A)
Money spent to **detect** defects that already exist.
- Incoming inspection, in-process inspection, final inspection, testing equipment calibration, audits.

### 2.1.3 Internal Failure Costs (IF)
Money spent because defects were found **before** reaching the customer.
- Scrap, rework, reinspection, re-testing, failure analysis, material review.

### 2.1.4 External Failure Costs (EF)
Money spent because defects **reached the customer** — the most damaging category.
- Warranty claims, returns, recalls, field service, customer complaints, legal liability, lost goodwill.

$$
\text{Total Cost of Quality (TCQ)} = P + A + IF + EF
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)} = IF + EF
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Good Quality (COGQ)} = P + A
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim, quote in exams):**
> *"COPQ is the sum of all costs that would disappear if there were no defects — i.e., internal plus external failure costs."*

### 2.2 The Optimal Quality Cost Curve (Juran's Economic Model)

Juran showed that as the **level of quality investment** increases:

- **P costs rise steadily** (more training, more robust design).
- **IF + EF costs fall sharply** (fewer defects escaping).
- **A costs rise mildly** (more sophisticated inspection).

The **optimal point** is where the **sum (TCQ) is minimized**, NOT where defects are zero. This is the principle of **economic quality control**.

### 2.3 The Hidden Costs — Feigenbaum's Extension

Feigenbaum argued that **40–60% of true quality costs are "hidden"** in:
- Lost customer goodwill
- Lost sales opportunities
- Reduced employee morale
- Excessive inventory/working capital tied up in WIP
- Engineering change orders

> [!NOTE]
> **Accounting Trap (KTU pitfall):** Traditional cost accounting **captures only the visible portion** of COPQ. The *real* COPQ is often 3–4× what the finance ledger shows. This is why Six Sigma Black Belts must **discover** hidden costs via process walks and Voice of Customer (VOC) data.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quality Quantified

| # | Metric | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|--------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | First Pass Yield (FPY / FTY) | $\text{FTY} = \dfrac{\text{Good units at step}}{\text{Units entering step}} \times 100\%$ | Per process step |
| 2 | Final Yield (FY) | $\text{FY} = \dfrac{\text{Good units shipped}}{\text{Total units started}} \times 100\%$ | End-to-end |
| 3 | Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FTY}_i$ | Multiplicative across $n$ steps |
| 4 | Throughput Defects per Million Opportunities (DPMO) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{(U \times O)} \times 10^6$ | $D$=defects, $U$=units, $O$=opportunities/unit |
| 5 | Defects per Unit (DPU) | $\text{DPU} = \dfrac{D}{U}$ | Mean defects per unit |
| 6 | Defects per Opportunity (DPO) | $\text{DPO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O}$ | Probability of a defect at any opportunity |
| 7 | Normal Yield (from DPMO) | $\text{Yield}_{\text{norm}} = e^{-\text{DPMO}/10^6}$ | Poisson assumption |
| 8 | Sigma Level (with 1.5σ shift) | $\text{Sigma} = Z_{\text{LT}} + 1.5$ | $Z_{\text{LT}}$ = short-term Z |
| 9 | Process Yield from $Z$ | $\text{Yield} = \Phi(Z) \times 100\%$ | $\Phi$ = standard normal CDF |
| 10 | Total Cost of Quality | $\text{TCQ} = P + A + IF + EF$ | All ₹ / $ terms |
| 11 | Cost of Poor Quality | $\text{COPQ} = IF + EF$ | "What goes away if defects = 0" |
| 12 | COPQ as % of Sales | $\text{COPQ\%} = \dfrac{\text{COPQ}}{\text{Total Sales}} \times 100$ | Industry benchmark: 5–30% of sales |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In the formula sheet above, all absolute-value-style notations use `\vert` or `\mid` form inside math mode. Never use the unescaped `|` character in markdown tables — it breaks the table column structure.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** DPMO is the universal metric for line-quality comparison between factories in different countries.
- **Software/IT (ITIL/CMMI):** Defect density (defects per KLOC) is the software equivalent of DPMO.
- **Healthcare (KTU hospitality / hospital management projects):** Medication errors per 1000 doses → DPMO equivalent.
- **Banking/Service:** Transaction errors per million transactions.
- **Design Engineering:** FPY in DFMEA validation reflects prevention-cost effectiveness.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Normal Yield from Poisson Assumption

### Assumption
Defects occur **independently and randomly** in a process — this is the **Poisson process assumption**, foundational to Motorola's Six Sigma mathematics.

### Setup
Let $\lambda$ = mean number of defects per unit (i.e., DPU). For a Poisson process, the probability of finding $k$ defects in a unit is:

$$
P(X = k) = \frac{\lambda^k e^{-\lambda}}{k!}
$$

The probability of a unit having **zero defects** (i.e., a "good" unit) is obtained by setting $k=0$:

$$
P(X = 0) = \frac{\lambda^0 e^{-\lambda}}{0!} = e^{-\lambda}
$$

### Conversion to DPMO
Since DPMO is defects per million opportunities and DPU is defects per unit, and if we assume **1 opportunity per unit** (worst case for simplicity):

$$
\lambda = \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6}
$$

Therefore the **normalized yield** is:

$$
\text{Yield}_{\text{norm}} = P(X = 0) = e^{-\lambda} = e^{-\text{DPMO}/10^6}
$$

> **Verification (worked example):** If DPMO = 3.4 (the Six Sigma level):
> $\text{Yield} = e^{-3.4/10^6} \approx 1 - 3.4 \times 10^{-6} = 0.9999966$ → 99.99966 % — matches the published Six Sigma yield.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DPMO from Raw Defect Data

### Step 1 — Count the units inspected
$U$ = number of units examined in the sample.

### Step 2 — Count the opportunities per unit
$O$ = number of places a defect could appear in one unit. Example: a printed circuit board with 250 solder joints → $O = 250$.

### Step 3 — Count the total defects
$D$ = total number of defects found (a unit with 3 defects contributes 3 to $D$).

### Step 4 — Compute the defect rate
Each unit provides $O$ opportunities, so the **total opportunity count** is $U \times O$. The fraction of opportunities that produced a defect is:

$$
\text{DPO} = \frac{D}{U \times O}
$$

### Step 5 — Scale to per-million
$$
\text{DPMO} = \text{DPO} \times 10^6 = \frac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6
$$

### Worked Example
A batch of 50 assembled motors is inspected. Each motor has 80 critical-to-quality characteristics ($O=80$). The inspectors find a total of 12 defects. Compute DPMO.

**Step (a):** $U \times O = 50 \times 80 = 4000$ opportunities.

**Step (b):** DPO $= \dfrac{12}{4000} = 0.003$

**Step (c):** DPMO $= 0.003 \times 10^6 = 3000$ DPMO

**Step (d):** Normal Yield $= e^{-3000/10^6} = e^{-0.003} \approx 0.997$ or **99.7 %**

**Step (e):** Sigma level (using tables) ≈ **4.0 σ** (3,000 DPMO corresponds to ~4.0 σ short-term).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) and the "Hidden Factory"

A process with **$n$ sequential steps**, each with its own FTY, has:

$$
\text{RTY} = \text{FTY}_1 \times \text{FTY}_2 \times \dots \times \text{FTY}_n
$$

### Demonstration of the "Hidden Factory" Effect
A 5-step process, each step with an apparently excellent FTY of 98%:

$$
\text{RTY} = (0.98)^5 = 0.9039 \approx 90.4\%
$$

> **Insight:** The **first-pass quality appears to be 98%** at every station, but **9.6% of customers** still receive at least one defect. This gap is the "hidden factory" — the rework and inspection workarounds that traditional metrics mask.

### Formal Mapping — Defect Escape Rate
The fraction of units that pass every step **without any rework** is RTY. The defect-escape rate to the customer is:

$$
\text{Defect Escape Rate} = 1 - \text{RTY}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Z-Score to Sigma Level Mapping

For a normal distribution, the **process yield** is the area under the curve within specification limits:

$$
\text{Yield} = \Phi\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma}\right) - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\text{LSL} - \mu}{\sigma}\right)
$$

For a **centered process** (mean = midpoint) with $Z = \dfrac{\text{Tolerance}}{2\sigma}$:

$$
\text{Yield} = 2\,\Phi(Z) - 1
$$

The **1.5σ shift** accounts for long-term process drift (a Motorola empirical convention):

$$
Z_{\text{LT}} = Z_{\text{ST}} - 1.5
$$

For Six Sigma: $Z_{\text{ST}} = 6 \Rightarrow Z_{\text{LT}} = 4.5 \Rightarrow$ Yield $= 2\Phi(4.5) - 1 \approx 0.9999966$ → **3.4 DPMO**.

### Master Mapping Table (KTU High-Yield)

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) |
|:-----------:|:----:|:---------:|
| 2 σ | 308,537 | 69.1 |
| 3 σ | 66,807 | 93.32 |
| 4 σ | 6,210 | 99.379 |
| 5 σ | 233 | 99.9767 |
| **6 σ** | **3.4** | **99.99966** |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Metric

| Scenario | Best Metric | Reasoning |
|----------|-------------|-----------|
| Single-step assembly defect | FTY | Simple, intuitive |
| Multi-step supply chain | RTY | Captures cumulative waste |
| Comparing two product lines | DPMO | Normalizes for complexity |
| Reporting to CEO/Board | COPQ (₹) | Speaks the language of money |
| Tracking maturity over time | Sigma Level | Industry benchmark |
| Software release readiness | Defect density (defects/KLOC) | DPMO equivalent for code |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Heuristic:** If the question gives you **money → COPQ**. If it gives you **defects and units → DPMO / RTY**. If it asks for **industry comparison → Sigma Level**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PAF Cost Model — Interaction Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **flow of money and information** between the four PAF cost categories, illustrating how investment in **Prevention** reduces **Failure** costs exponentially.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Investment in Prevention Costs P] --> B[Reduced Internal Failure IF]
    A --> C[Reduced External Failure EF]
    D[Appraisal Costs A] --> E[Defect Detection]
    E --> F{Defect Found?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Internal Failure IF - Scrap Rework]
    F -- No --> H[External Failure EF - Customer Returns Warranty]
    G --> I[COPQ Bucket]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Senior Management Visibility]
    J --> K[Reinvestment in Prevention P]
    K --> A
    B --> L[Reduced Leakage - Quality Improves]
    C --> L
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style I fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style L fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
```

**Reading the diagram:** The loop **A → B → I → J → K → A** is the virtuous cycle that Six Sigma Black Belts drive. Each iteration shifts money from the right (red) "failure" bucket to the left (blue) "prevention" bucket, and ultimately enlarges the green "quality improvement" zone.

---

## 4.2 Six Sigma Metrics Hierarchy — The Quality Quantification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Quality Quantified] --> M1[Cost Dimension]
    Q --> M2[Statistical Dimension]
    M1 --> C1[COPQ Internal External Failure]
    M1 --> C2[COGQ Prevention Appraisal]
    M1 --> C3[TCQ Total Cost of Quality]
    M2 --> S1[Process Yield FTY RTY]
    S1 --> S1a[First Pass Yield FTY]
    S1 --> S1b[Rolled Throughput Yield RTY]
    M2 --> S2[Defect Metrics DPU DPO DPMO]
    S2 --> S2a[DPU Defects per Unit]
    S2 --> S2b[DPO Defects per Opportunity]
    S2 --> S2c[DPMO Defects per Million Opportunities]
    M2 --> S3[Sigma Level Z score]
    S3 --> S3a[Short Term Z ST]
    S3 --> S3b[Long Term Z LT with 1.5 shift]
    style Q fill:#e6e6ff,stroke:#333399
    style M1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style M2 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d7a2d
```

**Interpretation:** A KTU answer on "Quality Quantified" should branch into the **Cost Dimension (M1)** and the **Statistical Dimension (M2)**. Each branch has its own vocabulary — mixing COPQ with DPMO without context loses marks.

---

## 4.3 DMAIC Linkage to Quality Quantified Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Define - Identify CTQ] --> D2[Measure - Compute Baseline DPMO COPQ]
    D2 --> D3[Analyze - Find Root Causes of Defects]
    D3 --> D4[Improve - Pilot Solutions Validate Yield]
    D4 --> D5[Control - Sustain Sigma Level Reduce COPQ]
    style D1 fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style D2 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style D3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style D4 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style D5 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
```

**KTU Insight:** Every DMAIC phase produces a **different quantified output**:

- **Define** → CTQ tree, project charter (no numbers yet).
- **Measure** → Baseline $\sigma$ level, DPMO, COPQ ₹.
- **Analyze** → Pareto of defect types, % contribution.
- **Improve** → Predicted vs. actual yield gain, $Z$ improvement.
- **Control** → Updated $\sigma$ level, COPQ reduction in ₹, control plan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Diagram Rationale:** Quality Quantified is a *systems* concept, not a physical object. Hence the diagrams use **functional block flow** rather than physical schematics — this aligns with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 fallback rule.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*Define Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ). Explain with one example how hidden failure costs can distort the true COPQ figure.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** COPQ is the sum of Internal Failure (IF) costs and External Failure (EF) costs — i.e., all costs that would *disappear* if the process produced zero defects.
- **Formula (1 mark):** $\text{COPQ} = IF + EF$.
- **Hidden costs example (1 mark):** Lost customer goodwill after a product recall. Traditional accounting records only the recall logistics cost (~₹10 lakh), but the real cost — customers switching to competitors, lost lifetime value — may be 3–4× higher. This is why Feigenbaum argued that visible COPQ is only 40–60% of the *true* COPQ.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Differentiate between First Pass Yield (FTY) and Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY). Why is RTY a more accurate measure of true process performance?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **FTY (1 mark):** Fraction of units passing a **single** step without rework, $\text{FTY} = \dfrac{\text{Good units at step}}{\text{Units entering step}}$.
- **RTY (1 mark):** Product of FTYs across **all** steps, $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FTY}_i$.
- **Why RTY is more accurate (1 mark):** FTY masks the **hidden factory** — rework at one step can compensate for defects at another. A 5-step process with 98% FTY at each step gives RTY = 90.4%, exposing the true 9.6% defect escape rate. RTY therefore reveals the cumulative customer impact, which FTY hides.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**(a)** Describe in detail the **PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) model** of Cost of Quality. Illustrate with one industry example for each of the four cost categories. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing process has **5 sequential stations**. The First Pass Yields (FTY) are: 0.96, 0.92, 0.98, 0.95, 0.99 respectively. Compute: (i) Rolled Throughput Yield, (ii) Defect Escape Rate, (iii) Normal Yield assuming Poisson behavior. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — PAF Model (7 marks)

| Category | Definition | Industry Example |
|----------|------------|------------------|
| **Prevention (P)** | Costs to *prevent* defects arising | ₹5 lakh spent on operator training + FMEA before launching a new car engine |
| **Appraisal (A)** | Costs to *detect* defects that exist | Incoming inspection of steel coils with spectrometer (₹2 lakh/month) |
| **Internal Failure (IF)** | Cost of defects found *before* shipping | ₹8 lakh in scrapped aluminium castings rejected after machining |
| **External Failure (EF)** | Cost of defects found *after* shipping | ₹20 lakh in warranty claims + field recall for brake calipers |

- **Total CoQ** (1 mark) $= P + A + IF + EF = 5 + 2 + 8 + 20 = \text{₹}35 \text{ lakh}$.
- **COPQ** (1 mark) $= IF + EF = 8 + 20 = \text{₹}28 \text{ lakh}$ — *this is the goldmine*.
- **Strategic insight (1 mark):** Investing ₹3 lakh more in **Prevention** (P) is expected to cut IF and EF by 50% (₹14 lakh saved) — net gain ₹11 lakh, demonstrating the prevention-pays-for-itself principle.

#### Part (b) — RTY Computation (7 marks)

**Step (i) — RTY (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY} &= \text{FTY}_1 \times \text{FTY}_2 \times \text{FTY}_3 \times \text{FTY}_4 \times \text{FTY}_5 \\
&= 0.96 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 \\
&= 0.8140 \quad (\text{approx.})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Defect Escape Rate (2 marks):**
$$
\text{Defect Escape Rate} = 1 - \text{RTY} = 1 - 0.8140 = 0.1860 = 18.60\%
$$

This means **roughly 1 in every 5.4 customers** experiences a defect — a serious Six Sigma red flag.

**Step (iii) — Normal Yield via Poisson (2 marks):**
For a multi-step process, the **mean DPU** under Poisson assumption is:
$$
\lambda = -\ln(\text{RTY}) = -\ln(0.8140) = 0.2057
$$
The normalized yield (probability a unit has zero defects end-to-end) is:
$$
\text{Yield}_{\text{norm}} = e^{-\lambda} = e^{-0.2057} = 0.8140
$$
This **matches the RTY**, confirming the consistency of the Poisson formulation with the multiplicative yield.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> Students often **multiply FTYs as percentages** (96 × 92 × 98...) instead of as decimals. This gives 8140% (nonsense) instead of 81.4%. **Always convert to decimals first.** Marks lost here are the single most common computational error in Module 1.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**(a)** Explain the **Juran Trilogy** and the **Juran Cost of Quality model**. How does this differ from Philip Crosby's view? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive parts supplier inspects **1200 units** in a week. Each unit has **15 critical-to-quality (CTQ) characteristics**. A total of **45 defects** are observed. Compute: (i) DPU, (ii) DPO, (iii) DPMO, (iv) the corresponding **Sigma Level** (use the standard 1.5σ shift). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Juran Trilogy vs. Crosby (7 marks)

**Juran's Trilogy** (3 marks) — three managerial processes:
1. **Quality Planning** — Identify customers and their needs (CTQs), develop product/process to meet them.
2. **Quality Control** — Evaluate actual vs. planned performance; take corrective action when gap appears (the *spiral* of SPC).
3. **Quality Improvement** — Establish infrastructure (Quality Councils, project teams) to drive continuous breakthrough improvement (vs. control which is *maintaining*).

**Juran's Cost Model** (2 marks) — emphasizes the **"goldmine"** hidden in chronic failure costs. He proved that optimal quality investment occurs at the point where marginal prevention cost = marginal failure-cost reduction (analogous to economic lot size in inventory theory).

**Crosby's View — Contrast** (2 marks):
- Juran frames quality as a *system* to be optimized.
- Crosby frames quality as a **binary state — either conformance or non-conformance**. His **"Zero Defects"** doctrine asserts that the acceptable quality level (AQL) mentality is itself the problem. He promotes **Prevention over Inspection** and measures quality via the **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)**.

| Aspect | Juran | Crosby |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Locus of quality | System / process | Conformance / standards |
| Cost focus | Goldmine in failure costs | PONC is what hurts |
| Improvement method | Breakthrough projects | Zero Defects culture |
| Measurement | % defective, costs in ₹ | Number of non-conformances |

#### Part (b) — DPU, DPO, DPMO, Sigma (7 marks)

**Given:** $U = 1200$ units, $O = 15$ opportunities/unit, $D = 45$ defects.

**Step (i) — DPU (1 mark):**
$$
\text{DPU} = \frac{D}{U} = \frac{45}{1200} = 0.0375 \text{ defects/unit}
$$

**Step (ii) — DPO (2 marks):**
$$
\text{DPO} = \frac{D}{U \times O} = \frac{45}{1200 \times 15} = \frac{45}{18000} = 0.0025
$$

**Step (iii) — DPMO (2 marks):**
$$
\text{DPMO} = 0.0025 \times 10^6 = 2500 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Step (iv) — Sigma Level (2 marks):**
From the standard mapping table, **2,500 DPMO** corresponds to a long-term (with 1.5σ shift) Sigma Level of approximately **3.95 σ**, often rounded in industry reports as **~4 σ**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> - A common error is to compute **DPU as DPMO** (forgetting to multiply by $10^6$ and to divide by $O$). Always re-check the **units of the answer**: DPU is a small fraction (<1), DPMO is in thousands-to-millions.
> - For Sigma conversion, **use the published Z-table** — do not invent values. 2,500 DPMO is a textbook-standard value and you may write "≈ 4σ" without showing the Φ-table lookup, but the examiner expects you to *cite* the table.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Quality Quantified**

- ☐ **Quality Quantified** has **two arms**: Cost (COPQ, COGQ, TCQ) and Statistics (DPMO, Yield, Sigma).
- ☐ **PAF Model**: $P + A + IF + EF = \text{TCQ}$; $IF + EF = \text{COPQ}$.
- ☐ **Juran** = Goldmine; **Crosby** = Quality is Free / Zero Defects / PONC; **Deming** = System cost; **Feigenbaum** = Hidden costs.
- ☐ **FTY** = per step; **RTY** = product of FTYs across all steps — the multiplicative nature exposes the hidden factory.
- ☐ **DPMO** $= \dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$ — always convert to **decimals** before arithmetic; never confuse DPU with DPMO.
- ☐ **Normal Yield** $= e^{-\text{DPMO}/10^6}$ — derived from the Poisson assumption of independent, random defects.
- ☐ **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 99.99966 % yield**, derived by Motorola using a **1.5 σ long-term shift**.
- ☐ **Hidden costs** (goodwill, lost sales, working capital) are 40–60% of true COPQ — finance ledgers understate reality.
- ☐ **DMAIC** links: Measure phase produces baseline DPMO/COPQ; Control phase produces improved Sigma and reduced COPQ.
- ☐ **KTU common exam traps**: (1) Mixing decimals and percentages; (2) Forgetting the $10^6$ factor; (3) Confusing DPO with DPMO; (4) Using visible CoQ instead of true COPQ; (5) Forgetting to apply the 1.5σ shift for long-term Sigma.

> **End of Module 1 — Quality Quantified Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensions of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dimensions of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)** and **Six Sigma**, the **Dimensions of Quality** refer to the **measurable and definable attributes** or characteristics that customers use to judge and evaluate the quality of a product or service. The most widely accepted academic framework in KTU-prescribed textbooks is **David A. Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Quality** (1984, Harvard Business Review), which classifies quality along **product-based**, **user-based**, **manufacturing-based**, and **value-based** perspectives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> "Dimensions of Quality are the **categories of attributes** that collectively define what 'quality' means to the customer. They translate the abstract concept of quality into **concrete, measurable parameters** that can be designed, controlled, and continuously improved using Six Sigma DMAIC methodology."

The Eight Dimensions of Quality (Garvin, 1984) are:
1. **Performance**
2. **Features**
3. **Reliability**
4. **Conformance**
5. **Durability**
6. **Serviceability**
7. **Aesthetics**
8. **Perceived Quality**

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase "Dimensions of Quality" is also sometimes attributed to broader quality philosophies. For KTU exams, the **Garvin framework** is the **definitive expected answer**, as it is the standard syllabus reference.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a **smartphone**. Before purchasing, you subconsciously evaluate several characteristics:
- Does it run apps smoothly? → **Performance**
- Does it have a good camera, fingerprint sensor, face unlock? → **Features**
- Will it crash or break down in a few months? → **Reliability**
- Does the phone meet advertised specs exactly? → **Conformance**
- How long will it physically last? → **Durability**
- Can I get it repaired easily when broken? → **Serviceability**
- Does it look good, feel premium in hand? → **Aesthetics**
- What is the brand reputation? → **Perceived Quality**

**Quality is not a single number**—it is a **bundle of dimensions**. Six Sigma aims to optimize each of these dimensions simultaneously, while reducing variation (defects) in each.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A product can be *high-performance* but *unreliable*. A product can be *durable* but *ugly*. Dimensions help an engineer break "quality" into pieces that can be **measured, designed, and improved** independently.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Dimensions of Quality — Detailed Breakdown

#### **1. Performance**
- **Definition:** The primary operating characteristics of a product or service — i.e., how well the product does what it is supposed to do.
- **Engineering Metric:** Measurable operational output (e.g., miles per gallon, screen refresh rate, transaction processing speed).
- **Six Sigma Link:** Voice of Customer (VOC) features most often map directly to performance metrics.

#### **2. Features**
- **Definition:** The "extra" characteristics or secondary attributes that supplement the basic functioning of the product.
- **Engineering Metric:** Count of value-added attributes (e.g., Bluetooth version, voice assistant, water-resistance rating).
- **Six Sigma Link:** Often the differentiator in **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** trees during the Define phase.

#### **3. Reliability**
- **Definition:** The probability that a product will perform its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time — without failure.
- **Engineering Metric:** **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**, Failure Rate (λ), often expressed in defects per million opportunities (DPMO).
- **Six Sigma Link:** Direct connection to **defect reduction** — a 6σ process has only **3.4 DPMO**.

#### **4. Conformance**
- **Definition:** The degree to which a product's design and operating characteristics meet established standards or specifications.
- **Engineering Metric:** Conformance rate %, First Pass Yield (FPY), tolerance bands.
- **Six Sigma Link:** Directly tied to **process capability indices** like $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

#### **5. Durability**
- **Definition:** The measure of product life — the amount of use one gets from a product before it deteriorates and replacement becomes preferable to repair.
- **Engineering Metric:** Mean Time To Failure (MTTF), lifecycle hours, fatigue life cycles.
- **Six Sigma Link:** Influences warranty costs and **cost of poor quality (COPQ)**.

#### **6. Serviceability**
- **Definition:** The speed, courtesy, and competence of repair and maintenance service.
- **Engineering Metric:** Mean Time To Repair (MTTR), service turnaround time, customer satisfaction score (CSAT).
- **Six Sigma Link:** Critical for **service industries** (Banking, IT, Healthcare).

#### **7. Aesthetics**
- **Definition:** The subjective, sensory dimension — how a product looks, feels, sounds, tastes, or smells.
- **Engineering Metric:** Subjective; measured through customer panels, Kano model surveys, sentiment analysis.
- **Six Sigma Link:** Less directly quantified, but matters in **DEFINE phase** customer research.

#### **8. Perceived Quality**
- **Definition:** The customer's perception of overall quality based on brand reputation, advertising, past experience, and indirect cues (not actual specifications).
- **Engineering Metric:** Net Promoter Score (NPS), Brand Equity Index.
- **Six Sigma Link:** Often the **output metric** of a successful Six Sigma project.

---

### 2.2 Garvin's Quality Perspectives (Background Theory)

Garvin grouped the eight dimensions under **four philosophical perspectives** of quality:

| Perspective | Focus | Related Dimensions |
|---|---|---|
| **User-Based** | Customer's subjective view | Aesthetics, Perceived Quality |
| **Product-Based** | Objective, measurable attributes | Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability |
| **Manufacturing-Based** | Conformance to design specifications | Conformance, Reliability |
| **Value-Based** | Quality vs. Price trade-off | Perceived Quality, Performance |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Six Sigma does not have heavy algebraic derivations for "Dimensions of Quality," but **process capability formulas** are *implicitly linked* to conformance and reliability. The following table consolidates the high-yield equations a KTU student must memorize.

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Formula / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $DPMO$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | $\dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$ |
| $\sigma$ | Standard deviation (process variation) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ |
| $Z$ | Sigma level (distance from mean to spec limit) | $Z = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ or $\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma}$ |
| $C_p$ | Process Capability (potential) | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ |
| $C_{pk}$ | Process Capability (actual, centered) | $C_{pk} = \min\left\{\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right\}$ |
| $MTBF$ | Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Repair | $MTTR = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ |
| $FPY$ | First Pass Yield | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Good Units First Time Through}}{\text{Total Units Entered}}$ |
| $RTY$ | Rolled Throughput Yield | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In markdown tables, the absolute value notation is written using `\vert` (not the pipe character `\vert`) inside LaTeX to avoid breaking the table. In plain writing, write `\vert x \vert` as **vert x vert**.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** A Six Sigma Black Belt reduces variation in **Performance, Conformance, and Reliability** dimensions simultaneously.
- **Service Industry (Banking, IT, Healthcare):** Dimensions shift toward **Serviceability, Features, and Perceived Quality**, since "products" are intangible.
- **Software Engineering:** Reliability and Conformance are tracked via **defect density** and **test pass rates**.
- **Automotive:** Durability and Reliability drive warranty cost reduction — Toyota's success story with Six Sigma is rooted here.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Numerical Examples

Since "Dimensions of Quality" is largely a **conceptual and framework-based topic**, the analytical depth comes from:
- Mapping dimensions to numerical metrics.
- Solving **Six Sigma numerical problems** that connect directly to these dimensions (DPMO, process capability, yield).

The following **three fully worked numerical problems** model the type of analytical questions KTU examiners use to test this topic.

---

### **Worked Example 1: DPMO and Sigma Level Calculation (linked to Reliability + Conformance)**

A manufacturing line produces **5,000 units/day**. Each unit has **20 opportunities for defects**. In one week (5 working days), the total defects observed were **150**.

**Step 1: Calculate Total Units Produced**

$$
\text{Total Units} = 5000 \times 5 = 25{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Opportunities**

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} = 25{,}000 \times 20 = 500{,}000 \text{ opportunities}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate DPMO**

$$
DPMO = \frac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Opportunities}} = \frac{150 \times 10^6}{500{,}000}
$$

$$
DPMO = \frac{150{,}000{,}000}{500{,}000} = 300 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Step 4: Convert DPMO to Yield (%)**

$$
\text{Yield} = 1 - \frac{DPMO}{10^6} = 1 - \frac{300}{10^6} = 0.9997 = 99.97\%
$$

**Step 5: Equivalent Sigma Level (using standard Six Sigma conversion table)**

$$
300 \text{ DPMO} \approx 4.85 \sigma
$$

> **Interpretation:** This process is **not yet at Six Sigma (6σ = 3.4 DPMO)**. It needs significant improvement in the **Conformance** and **Reliability** dimensions.

---

### **Worked Example 2: Process Capability Index $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ (linked to Conformance)**

A piston shaft diameter has:
- Upper Specification Limit (USL) = **25.05 mm**
- Lower Specification Limit (LSL) = **24.95 mm**
- Process Mean ($\mu$) = **25.02 mm**
- Process Standard Deviation ($\sigma$) = **0.02 mm**

**Step 1: Calculate $C_p$ (Process Potential)**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.05 - 24.95}{6 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.10}{0.12}
$$

$$
C_p = 0.833
$$

> **Interpretation:** A $C_p < 1$ means the process **spread is wider than the tolerance band** — the process is **not capable** even at its best.

**Step 2: Calculate $C_{pk}$ (Actual Capability considering centering)**

$$
C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.05 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.03}{0.06} = 0.500
$$

$$
C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.02 - 24.95}{3 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.07}{0.06} = 1.167
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\{0.500,\ 1.167\} = 0.500
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

> **Interpretation:** Since $C_{pk} = 0.5 < 1$, the process is **incapable**, and additionally the process is **off-center** (since $C_p \neq C_{pk}$). The piston will frequently exceed the **upper specification limit** — direct violation of the **Conformance** dimension.

---

### **Worked Example 3: Reliability — MTBF Calculation (linked to Reliability + Durability)**

A fleet of **20 servers** is monitored for **1 year (8,760 hours)**. Total downtime during the year = **120 hours**. Total number of failures across all servers = **30**.

**Step 1: Calculate Total Operating Time**

$$
\text{Total Operating Time} = (20 \times 8{,}760) - 120 = 175{,}200 - 120 = 175{,}080 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate MTBF**

$$
MTBF = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{175{,}080}{30}
$$

$$
MTBF = 5{,}836 \text{ hours per failure}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Failure Rate ($\lambda$)**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} = \frac{1}{5{,}836} = 1.714 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

**Step 4: Reliability for 1,000 hours of operation (Exponential Model)**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-1.714 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000}
$$

$$
R(1000) = e^{-0.1714} \approx 0.8425 = 84.25\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** There is approximately an **84% chance** that a server will operate without failure for 1,000 continuous hours. To improve **Reliability** and **Durability** dimensions, the Six Sigma team must target increasing **MTBF**.

---

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Dimensions to Numerical Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU revision table**. Examiners frequently ask: *"Which Six Sigma metric best quantifies which dimension of quality?"*

| Dimension of Quality | Primary Six Sigma Metric | Secondary Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance** | Throughput, Cycle Time | Benchmark score |
| **Features** | CTQ tree count | VOC feature count |
| **Reliability** | MTBF, DPMO | Failure Rate ($\lambda$) |
| **Conformance** | $C_p$, $C_{pk}$ | FPY, RTY |
| **Durability** | MTTF, Lifecycle hours | Warranty claims |
| **Serviceability** | MTTR | CSAT, NPS |
| **Aesthetics** | Customer survey score | Kano model category |
| **Perceived Quality** | Brand equity, NPS | Market share |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Quality — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Dimensions of Quality\n(Garvin Framework, 1984)"]:::root
    A --> B1["Performance"]
    A --> B2["Features"]
    A --> B3["Reliability"]
    A --> B4["Conformance"]
    A --> B5["Durability"]
    A --> B6["Serviceability"]
    A --> B7["Aesthetics"]
    A --> B8["Perceived Quality"]

    B1 --> C1["Primary operating\nefficiency"]
    B2 --> C2["Extra characteristics\nbeyond basics"]
    B3 --> C3["Probability of\nfailure-free operation"]
    B4 --> C4["Adherence to\nspecifications"]
    B5 --> C5["Product useful life\nbefore replacement"]
    B6 --> C6["Speed and quality\nof maintenance"]
    B7 --> C7["Sensory appeal\nlook feel sound"]
    B8 --> C8["Brand reputation\nand indirect cues"]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px;
```

### 4.2 Mapping Quality Dimensions to Six Sigma DMAIC Phases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1["DEFINE Phase\nIdentify CTQs"]:::phase
    M1["MEASURE Phase\nCollect data"]:::phase
    A1["ANALYSE Phase\nRoot cause"]:::phase
    I1["IMPROVE Phase\nImplement solutions"]:::phase
    C1["CONTROL Phase\nSustain gains"]:::phase

    Q1["Aesthetics\nPerceived Quality\nFeatures"]:::dim
    Q2["Performance\nConformance\nReliability"]:::dim
    Q3["Reliability\nDurability"]:::dim
    Q4["Performance\nFeatures\nServiceability"]:::dim
    Q5["Conformance\nReliability\nServiceability"]:::dim

    Q1 --> D1
    Q2 --> M1
    Q3 --> A1
    Q4 --> I1
    Q5 --> C1

    classDef phase fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef dim fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:1px;
```

### 4.3 The Quality Perspective Classification (Garvin)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Four Quality Perspectives"]:::root
    P --> PB["Product-Based"]
    P --> UB["User-Based"]
    P --> MB["Manufacturing-Based"]
    P --> VB["Value-Based"]

    PB --> PB1["Performance\nFeatures\nReliability\nConformance\nDurability\nServiceability"]
    UB --> UB1["Aesthetics\nPerceived Quality"]
    MB --> MB1["Conformance\nReliability"]
    VB --> VB1["Perceived Quality\nPerformance"]

    classDef root fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px;
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark question, drawing even a simple block diagram (like the four perspectives above) adds significant marks because it shows **structured thinking**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)**

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define the term "Dimensions of Quality" and list any four dimensions of quality proposed by David Garvin.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Dimensions of Quality** refer to the **specific, measurable attributes** that customers use to evaluate the quality of a product or service. **David A. Garvin** (1984) identified **eight dimensions** of quality in his seminal Harvard Business Review article.
>
> Four of the eight dimensions are:
> 1. **Performance** – the primary operating characteristics of a product.
> 2. **Reliability** – the probability of failure-free operation over time.
> 3. **Conformance** – the degree to which a product meets established specifications.
> 4. **Serviceability** – the speed and competence of repair/maintenance.

*(Allocate ~1 mark for definition, ~2 marks for listing and briefly explaining 4 dimensions.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between "Performance" and "Features" as dimensions of quality with one engineering example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Performance | Features |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Primary operating efficiency of a product | Extra secondary characteristics |
| **Example (Car)** | Top speed of 200 km/h, mileage of 18 kmpl | Sunroof, touchscreen, rear camera |
| **Quantification** | Easily measurable in units | Often counted or surveyed |

> Performance relates to **how well the core function is executed**, while Features are **value-added extras** that supplement the core function.

*(Allocate ~1.5 marks for distinction, ~1.5 marks for example.)*

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - When asked to "explain dimensions," do **NOT** only list names. Marks are awarded for **briefly defining each dimension with examples**.
> - Always **name the originator** (Garvin, 1984) when defining the framework.
> - For numerical sub-questions, **show every substitution** — examiners award step marks, not just the final answer.
> - **Draw a diagram or table** wherever possible; structured answers consistently score 1–2 extra marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **Eight Dimensions of Quality** as proposed by **David Garvin**. Classify them into Garvin's **four perspectives of quality**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:**
The concept of quality is multi-dimensional. **David A. Garvin** (1984), in his Harvard Business Review article *"What Does Product Quality Really Mean?"*, identified **eight dimensions** that comprehensively define product quality.

**The Eight Dimensions:**

1. **Performance:** The product's primary operating characteristics. *Example: A car's mileage in kmpl.*
2. **Features:** Secondary characteristics that supplement the basic operation. *Example: Power windows, touchscreen infotainment.*
3. **Reliability:** The probability of failure-free operation over a specified period. *Example: MTBF of 50,000 hours for an aircraft engine.*
4. **Conformance:** The degree of adherence to pre-established specifications. *Example: A bolt manufactured within ±0.01 mm tolerance.*
5. **Durability:** The measure of product useful life. *Example: A washing machine lasting 10 years under normal use.*
6. **Serviceability:** Speed, courtesy, and competence of repair service. *Example: Average MTTR of 2 hours for a laptop service center.*
7. **Aesthetics:** Subjective sensory appeal — appearance, feel, sound. *Example: The luxury interior of a Mercedes-Benz.*
8. **Perceived Quality:** Customer's indirect perception based on brand and reputation. *Example: Customer trusting Apple over unknown brands.*

**Garvin's Four Perspectives of Quality:**

| Perspective | Definition | Related Dimensions |
|---|---|---|
| **User-Based** | Customer's subjective view of quality | Aesthetics, Perceived Quality |
| **Product-Based** | Quality as a precise, measurable variable | Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability |
| **Manufacturing-Based** | Quality as conformance to requirements | Conformance, Reliability |
| **Value-Based** | Quality measured against price paid | Performance, Perceived Quality |

> **Conclusion:** The Garvin framework is **holistic** and allows companies to evaluate quality across both objective (measurable) and subjective (perceived) parameters.

*(Award 1 mark for introduction, 4 marks for dimensions with examples, 1.5 marks for table, 0.5 mark for conclusion = 7 marks.)*

---

**Q: (b)** A process produces **8,000 units/day**, each having **25 defect opportunities**. In a 5-day week, defects observed = **200**. Compute **DPMO, Yield (%)**, and the **equivalent sigma level**. Also suggest which dimension(s) of quality are affected and how this should be addressed using Six Sigma DMAIC. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Total Units Produced**

$$
\text{Total Units} = 8{,}000 \times 5 = 40{,}000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2: Total Opportunities**

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} = 40{,}000 \times 25 = 1{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 3: DPMO Calculation**

$$
DPMO = \frac{200 \times 10^6}{1{,}000{,}000} = 200 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Step 4: Yield Calculation**

$$
\text{Yield} = 1 - \frac{200}{10^6} = 0.9998 = 99.98\%
$$

**Step 5: Equivalent Sigma Level**

> From the standard Six Sigma conversion table, **200 DPMO ≈ 4.9σ**.

**Step 6: Affected Quality Dimensions**

| Affected Dimension | Impact |
|---|---|
| **Reliability** | Higher defects = lower MTBF |
| **Conformance** | Process is not meeting design specs |
| **Performance** | Defective units may fail in operation |

**Step 7: Six Sigma DMAIC Approach**

- **Define:** Identify CTQs (e.g., reduce DPMO to ≤ 3.4).
- **Measure:** Establish baseline of 200 DPMO; map process.
- **Analyze:** Use Pareto, Fishbone, 5-Why to find root causes.
- **Improve:** Pilot solutions (e.g., mistake-proofing, poka-yoke).
- **Control:** Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts to sustain gains.

*(Award 1 mark each for steps 1–5 = 5 marks; 1 mark for dimension mapping; 1 mark for DMAIC plan = 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the **classification of quality dimensions** under Garvin's framework. How do **product-based**, **user-based**, and **value-based** perspectives differ in evaluating a smartphone? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Garvin's Classification:**

| Perspective | Meaning | Smartphone Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Product-Based** | Quality measured as a precise, quantifiable variable | Processor speed = 3.2 GHz, Battery = 5000 mAh |
| **User-Based** | Quality is in the eye of the beholder; subjective satisfaction | User reviews stating "the phone feels premium" |
| **Manufacturing-Based** | Quality as conformance to internal specifications | Yield rate of 99.5% on assembly line |
| **Value-Based** | Quality judged against the price paid | A ₹20,000 phone offering "value for money" |

**Detailed Comparison for a Smartphone:**

- **Product-Based View:** Engineers evaluate the smartphone using **objective specs** — RAM (8 GB), camera (50 MP), display refresh rate (120 Hz), benchmark scores.
- **User-Based View:** Customers evaluate based on **personal experience** — grip, screen brightness, ease of UI, brand emotion.
- **Manufacturing-Based View:** The factory measures **first-pass yield, defect rates, rework** to determine internal quality.
- **Value-Based View:** Marketing and customers compare **performance vs. price ratio** — is this phone worth ₹30,000?
