<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deming's Three Pillars — Structured Analysis

Deming's contribution can be organized into **three philosophical pillars**, each addressing a different layer of organizational life.

### Pillar 1: The 14 Points for Management (1982)

Deming condensed his entire philosophy into **14 actionable management obligations**. KTU often asks students to list and explain 4–6 of these points.

**The 14 Points — Full List with Plain-English Explanation:**

| **#** | **Deming's Point (Official)** | **Intuitive Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Create constancy of purpose for improvement of product and service | Stop chasing short-term profit; commit to long-term quality and innovation. |
| 2 | Adopt the new philosophy | The era of accepting poor quality is over. Top management must lead. |
| 3 | Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality | Inspection is too late; build quality *into* the process. |
| 4 | End the practice of awarding business on price tag alone | Lowest-cost supplier often delivers lowest quality; consider total cost over time. |
| 5 | Improve constantly and forever the system of production and service | Adopt **Kaizen** — small, continuous improvements. |
| 6 | Institute training on the job | Workers cannot do their job properly if they were never properly trained. |
| 7 | Institute leadership | Supervision should be replaced with *leadership* — help people do a better job. |
| 8 | Drive out fear | Fear destroys initiative, innovation, and morale. Workers must feel safe to report problems. |
| 9 | Break down barriers between departments | Silos between design, production, and sales cause systemic failures. |
| 10 | Eliminate slogans and exhortations | "Zero defects" posters are useless if the system prevents quality. |
| 11 | Eliminate numerical quotas | Quotas force workers to game the system, often producing poor quality. |
| 12 | Remove barriers to pride of workmanship | Poor tools, faulty equipment, and unclear instructions destroy morale. |
| 13 | Institute a vigorous program of education and self-improvement | Quality begins with the educated mind of every employee. |
| 14 | Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation | Top-down commitment; quality is not a department — it's everyone's job. |

---

### Pillar 2: The System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)

Deming proposed that to manage an organization well, one must understand **four interrelated knowledge domains** that form a system. This is Deming's *meta-philosophy* of management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4 Components of the System of Profound Knowledge:**
>
> 1. **Appreciation for a System** — Understanding how components interact to achieve the organization's aim. Optimization of subsystems ≠ optimization of the whole.
> 2. **Knowledge of Variation** — Distinguishing between **common cause variation** (inherent to the system) and **special cause variation** (due to external shocks). Acting on the wrong type is dangerous.
> 3. **Theory of Knowledge** — Understanding what knowledge *is* and how predictions are made and tested. A plan is a theory; its execution is a test.
> 4. **Knowledge of Psychology** — Understanding human behavior, motivation, and how people interact. People are different; intrinsic motivation beats extrinsic controls.

**Why This Matters in Six Sigma (KTU Context):**
The Six Sigma DMAIC framework (Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control) is a direct extension of Deming's SoPK. Specifically:
- DMAIC's *Measure* and *Analyze* phases rely on **Knowledge of Variation** (statistical tools, control charts, ANOVA).
- DMAIC's *Improve* phase uses **Theory of Knowledge** (hypothesis testing, pilot studies).
- DMAIC's *Define* and *Control* phases require **Appreciation for a System** and **Psychology** (change management, stakeholder engagement).

---

### Pillar 3: The PDSA Cycle (Deming Wheel / Shewhart Cycle)

The **PDSA Cycle** is Deming's version (modified from Walter Shewhart's PDCA) of the scientific method applied to business processes. It is **THE** most-tested Deming concept in KTU exams.

| **Stage** | **Name** | **Action** | **Six Sigma / DMAIC Mapping** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | Plan | Identify a goal, formulate a hypothesis, design a plan to test it. | Define + Measure (set baseline) |
| **D** | Do | Execute the plan on a small scale, collect data. | Analyze + Improve (pilot) |
| **S** | Study | Analyze results; compare predictions to outcomes. | Control (validate results) |
| **A** | Act | If successful, standardize and scale up; if not, revise hypothesis and repeat. | Standardize / Loop back to Plan |

**The cycle is iterative and continuous** — once one loop ends, the next begins at a higher level of performance. This is the engine of **continuous improvement (Kaizen)** in Six Sigma.

---

## 2.2 Deming's Seven Deadly Diseases (KTU Favorite Question)

In his 1982 book *"Out of the Crisis,"* Deming listed **7 obstacles** (diseases) that destroy quality in an organization. Examiners frequently ask: *"Explain any 4 of Deming's Seven Deadly Diseases."*

| **#** | **Deadly Disease** | **Symptom / Effect** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lack of constancy of purpose** | Short-term focus, no long-term planning, layoffs over quality. |
| 2 | **Emphasis on short-term profits** | Neglects R&D, customer satisfaction, employee development. |
| 3 | **Evaluation by performance, merit rating, or annual review of performance** | Demotes teamwork, creates fear, destroys collaboration. |
| 4 | **Mobility of management** | Frequent job-hopping prevents long-term systems thinking. |
| 5 | **Running a company on visible figures alone** | Ignores invisible costs, long-term consequences, and qualitative factors. |
| 6 | **Excessive medical costs** | Rising healthcare costs absorb capital that should go to quality improvement. |
| 7 | **Excessive costs of warranty, fueled by lawyers who work for contingency fees** | Defensive litigation instead of systemic improvement. |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Pitfall: Students often write *"merit rating"* as a positive practice. **Deming was AGAINST merit ratings** — he believed they create fear and destroy teamwork.

---

## 2.3 Deming's Chain Reaction (Quality → Profit → Jobs)

This is a critical concept often depicted in KTU slides and exam answers.

**The Chain Reaction Sequence (Draw it from left to right):**

$$\text{Improve Quality} \longrightarrow \text{Decrease Costs} \longrightarrow \text{Decrease Prices} \longrightarrow \text{Stay in Business} \longrightarrow \text{Provide Jobs}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Better quality** (fewer defects, rework, waste) **→**
2. **Lower production costs** (less scrap, less rework, less warranty) **→**
3. **Lower prices for customers** (competitively positioned) **→**
4. **Increased market share & company survives** **→**
5. **More jobs and economic stability** (the social benefit of quality).

> [!TIP]
> KTU Exam Tip: When asked about Deming's contribution to Japan, link it to this chain reaction. Toyota, Sony, and others adopted Deming's statistical methods, reduced defects, lowered costs, captured global market share, and contributed to Japan's economic miracle.

---

## 2.4 The Red Bead Experiment (Conceptual, Not Formula-Based)

Deming's famous **Red Bead Experiment** is a live demonstration he used to teach the folly of blaming workers for system defects. A worker dips a paddle into a box containing white and red beads. **No matter how hard the worker tries, the paddle always brings up some red beads.** Deming then asks the supervisor to "coach" the worker — the result is the same. The lesson: **the system (the bead mix and paddle design) is the source of the problem, not the worker.**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Exam Tip: The Red Bead Experiment illustrates Deming's point that **94% of problems are system-related, 6% are worker-related.** This figure (94/6) is sometimes asked in MCQs.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Six Sigma Context)

Since Deming's philosophy underpins Six Sigma, the following formulas are essential. **Important:** The pipe symbol is rendered as `\mid` or `\vert` in LaTeX, never as `|`.

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process Capability Index ($C_p$) | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Measures how well a process fits within specification limits. | Used to assess process capability in DMAIC's *Measure* phase. |
| Process Performance Index ($C_{pk}$) | $C_{pk} = \min \left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$ | Adjusts $C_p$ for process centering. | Reveals whether the process mean is off-target. |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities} \times \text{Units}} \times 10^6$ | Standard Six Sigma metric. | Six Sigma quality target: $\leq 3.4$ DPMO. |
| Six Sigma Level (Z-score) | $Z = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Distance of process mean from specification in sigma units. | 6$\sigma$ quality means $Z = 6$ (process mean is 6 sigmas from the nearest spec). |
| Deming's 94/6 Principle | $94\% \text{ System} + 6\% \text{ Worker} = 100\%$ | Attribution of quality problems. | Justifies management responsibility and root cause analysis. |
| Cost of Quality (COQ) — Quality Costs | $COQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\ failure} + C_{external\ failure}$ | Total cost associated with quality. | Demonstrates Deming's claim that quality reduces cost. |
| Kaizen (Continuous Improvement) | $\Delta P_t = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta_i$ | Cumulative small improvements compound over time. | Drives long-term process excellence. |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Deming's principles are not abstract — they are operational in:
- **Manufacturing**: Statistical Process Control (SPC) in semiconductor fabrication.
- **Software Engineering**: Six Sigma DMAIC applied to defect reduction in code releases.
- **Healthcare**: Reducing hospital-acquired infections through system redesign.
- **Logistics**: Reducing delivery variance using Deming's common/special cause thinking.
- **Service Industry**: Reducing customer complaint variation at call centers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deming's Core Insight for Engineers:**
> Variation is everywhere. The job of the engineer is not to eliminate variation (impossible) but to **understand** it, **separate common from special causes**, and **reduce common cause variation through system redesign.**

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Process Capability from Deming's Variation Theory

Deming's central teaching — **"Knowledge of Variation"** — is mathematically operationalized in Six Sigma via the Process Capability Index. Below is a step-by-step derivation that links Deming's philosophy to the formula.

### Step 1: Understand the Process Distribution

For any in-control process, output measurements $X$ follow a **normal distribution** (per the Central Limit Theorem, valid when many small independent causes combine).

$$X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = process mean (the average output of the system)
- $\sigma$ = process standard deviation (a measure of **common cause variation**)

> **Interpretation per Deming:** $\sigma$ reflects the system's inherent variation. It is *not* the worker's fault — it is the system's "natural" spread.

---

### Step 2: Define Specification Limits (USL and LSL)

Customers (or engineering design) impose specification limits:
- **USL** = Upper Specification Limit (maximum acceptable value)
- **LSL** = Lower Specification Limit (minimum acceptable value)
- **Tolerance** $T = USL - LSL$

The Six Sigma philosophy requires that the process variation $\sigma$ is small enough that the output stays well within these limits.

---

### Step 3: Express the Tolerance in Units of Standard Deviation

The total "natural" spread of the process (per the Empirical Rule) is approximately $\pm 3\sigma$ from the mean, i.e., a total spread of $6\sigma$ for $\sim 99.73\%$ of output.

Therefore, the ratio of the specification width to the process spread is:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{\text{Allowable Spread}}{\text{Natural Spread}} \\
&= \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\
&= \dfrac{T}{6\sigma}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Line-by-line explanation:**
> - **Line 1:** Definition of capability — the ratio of what the customer allows to what the process naturally delivers.
> - **Line 2:** Substituting the empirical 6$\sigma$ natural spread and the tolerance.
> - **Line 3:** Compact form used universally in Six Sigma texts.

---

### Step 4: Adjust for Centering — Derive $C_{pk}$

$C_p$ assumes the process is perfectly centered (mean exactly halfway between USL and LSL). In practice, the mean often drifts. Deming taught that **detecting special causes** and re-centering the process is essential.

Define the distance from the mean to each specification limit:
- Upper distance: $d_U = USL - \mu$
- Lower distance: $d_L = \mu - LSL$

The capability is governed by the *smaller* of the two distances:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{pk} &= \min \left( \dfrac{d_U}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{d_L}{3\sigma} \right) \\
&= \min \left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

> **Line-by-line explanation:**
> - **Line 1:** The process is only as capable as its weakest side.
> - **Line 2:** Substitution of upper and lower distances, each expressed as a 3$\sigma$ ratio.

---

### Step 5: Apply Deming's Six Sigma Standard

Six Sigma requires $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$ (which means the tolerance is at least $12\sigma$ wide). Deming's 1950s Japanese students applied this rigorously, achieving world-class quality.

**Numerical Example (KTU-typical):**

Suppose a process has:
- $USL = 110$ mm, $LSL = 90$ mm
- $\mu = 100$ mm, $\sigma = 2$ mm

$$\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{110 - 90}{6 \times 2} = \dfrac{20}{12} = 1.667 \\
C_{pk} &= \min \left( \dfrac{110 - 100}{3 \times 2}, \dfrac{100 - 90}{3 \times 2} \right) = \min(1.667, 1.667) = 1.667
\end{aligned}$$

The process is centered ($C_p = C_{pk}$), but the capability is only 1.667 — below the Six Sigma benchmark of 2.0. **Improvement needed.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Deming vs. Other TQM Gurus

KTU often asks: *"Compare Deming with Juran or Crosby."* Use this matrix.

| **Dimension** | **Deming** | **Juran** | **Crosby** | **Ishikawa** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Focus | Statistical control + System thinking | Quality planning + Customer focus | Conformance to requirements + Zero defects | Company-wide quality control + Cause-and-effect |
| Famous Tool | PDSA Cycle, SPC, 14 Points | Juran Trilogy (Q-P-C), Pareto | 14 Steps to Quality Improvement, Quality is Free | Ishikawa Diagram (Fishbone), Quality Circles |
| View of Worker | 94% of problems are system-related, 6% worker | Worker has a role in the system | Worker must be educated to achieve zero defects | Workers are central; quality circles empower them |
| View of Quality | Continuous, statistical, system-wide | Fitness for use (customer-defined) | Conformance to requirements (prevention-based) | Customer satisfaction, company-wide |
| Geographic Influence | Japan + USA | Japan + USA (later) | USA-centric | Japan-centric |
| Cultural Phrase | "System of Profound Knowledge" | "Quality is fitness for use" | "Do it right the first time" | "Next process is the customer" |
| Best for (Engineering Context) | Statistical Six Sigma DMAIC | Quality planning for new products | Manufacturing conformance (aerospace, automotive) | Problem-solving with root cause analysis |

> [!TIP]
> KTU Exam Strategy: When comparing gurus, use a table like this. It shows the examiner you understand each thinker's *unique contribution*, not just generic quality slogans.

---

## 3.3 Deming's PDSA — Mathematical Iteration Concept

While PDSA is iterative, it can be conceptualized as an **asymptotic convergence** of process performance $P_t$ over time.

$$P_{t+1} = P_t + \Delta_i \cdot \mathbb{1}(\text{improvement adopted})$$

Where:
- $P_t$ = process performance at time $t$
- $\Delta_i$ = improvement from the $i$-th PDSA cycle
- $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ = indicator function (1 if improvement is adopted, 0 otherwise)

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= P_0 + \Delta_1 \cdot \mathbb{1}(\text{Adopt}_1) \\
P_2 &= P_1 + \Delta_2 \cdot \mathbb{1}(\text{Adopt}_2) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
P_n &= P_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta_i \cdot \mathbb{1}(\text{Adopt}_i)
\end{aligned}$$

> **Line-by-line explanation:**
> - **Line 1:** Initial performance baseline.
> - **Line 2:** Performance after cycle 2.
> - **Line 3:** Generalization — performance is the baseline plus cumulative adopted improvements.

**Engineering Implication:** Even tiny $\Delta_i$ values, when compounded over many cycles, produce dramatic gains — this is the mathematical basis of **Kaizen**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define W. Edwards Deming's contribution to TQM. Why is he called the "Father of Statistical Quality Control"?**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993) was an American statistician, engineer, and management consultant who introduced statistical quality control methods to Japanese industry in 1950. He is called the "Father of Statistical Quality Control" because:

1. He trained top Japanese engineers in **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** using control charts, sampling, and variation analysis.
2. He authored the **14 Points for Management** and the **System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)**.
3. He created the **PDSA Cycle** (Plan–Do–Study–Act), which is the foundation of Six Sigma DMAIC.
4. His methods transformed Japanese products (Toyota, Sony) into global quality leaders, leading to the establishment of the **Deming Prize** in 1951.

He emphasized that **94% of quality problems are system-related**, and managers must use statistical thinking to redesign systems rather than blame workers.

*(Word count: ~110 words; 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2: What is Deming's PDSA Cycle? List its four stages.**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **PDSA Cycle** (also called the **Deming Wheel** or **Shewhart Cycle**) is a four-stage iterative method for continuous improvement, derived from the scientific method.

| **Stage** | **Name** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | **Plan** | Identify the problem, set objectives, formulate a hypothesis, and design an improvement plan. |
| **D** | **Do** | Execute the plan on a small scale (pilot), collect data, and document observations. |
| **S** | **Study** | Analyze the data, compare actual outcomes with predictions, and identify learnings. |
| **A** | **Act** | If the change works, standardize and implement it broadly. If not, revise the hypothesis and re-enter the Plan stage. |

The cycle is **continuous** — after one rotation, the next begins at a higher level of performance, embodying **Kaizen** philosophy.

*(Word count: ~110 words; 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> KTU ESE Module Question Paper Format: One Module-based question of 14 marks with internal choice. Sub-parts typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain Deming's 14 Points for Management in detail.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Deming's 14 Points for Management, published in his 1982 book *Out of the Crisis*, represent a comprehensive management philosophy for organizational transformation. They are:

1. **Create constancy of purpose** — Focus on long-term improvement, not short-term profits. Plan for the future of the company and its products.

2. **Adopt the new philosophy** — Accept that the era of accepting poor quality, delays, and errors is over. Top management must lead this cultural shift.

3. **Cease dependence on inspection** — Inspection is too late, costly, and ineffective. Quality must be **built into the process** from the start.

4. **End the practice of awarding business on price alone** — Lowest-price suppliers often deliver lowest quality. Consider total cost of ownership, including service, delivery, and reliability.

5. **Improve constantly and forever** — Adopt the Japanese concept of **Kaizen** — small, continuous, incremental improvements.

6. **Institute training on the job** — Workers must be properly trained to perform their tasks. Many quality problems stem from inadequate training.

7. **Institute leadership** — Replace mere supervision with leadership. Leaders help people and systems do a better job; they don't just enforce rules.

8. **Drive out fear** — Fear of reprisal, fear of admitting mistakes, fear of questioning supervisors — all destroy innovation, morale, and quality.

9. **Break down barriers between departments** — Silos between R&D, production, sales, and service cause systemic failures. Foster team-based, organization-wide thinking.

10. **Eliminate slogans and exhortations** — "Zero Defects" posters are useless if the system prevents quality. They generate resentment when workers can't meet unattainable goals.

11. **Eliminate numerical quotas** — Quotas force workers to game the system, produce poor quality, or hide defects to meet numbers.

12. **Remove barriers to pride of workmanship** — Faulty tools, unclear instructions, and poor materials prevent workers from being proud of their work.

13. **Institute a vigorous program of education and self-improvement** — Every employee should continually upgrade skills and knowledge.

14. **Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation** — Quality transformation is everyone’s responsibility, led by top management. It requires total organizational commitment.

**Conclusion:** Deming's 14 Points are not isolated suggestions but a **system** of interrelated principles. Implementing them requires leadership commitment, cultural change, and statistical thinking. They are foundational to modern Six Sigma DMAIC and TQM frameworks.

*(7 marks — full marks awarded for thorough coverage and clear, bullet-style explanation)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Explain Deming's System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK) with its four components. How does it relate to Six Sigma DMAIC?

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Deming's **System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)** is the philosophical foundation of all quality management. Deming argued that to manage an organization effectively, leaders must deeply understand four interrelated knowledge domains.

**The Four Components of SoPK:**

**1. Appreciation for a System:**
- A system is a network of interdependent components working together to achieve the organization's aim.
- **Key Insight:** Optimizing a subsystem (e.g., the sales department) does **not** optimize the whole organization. Sometimes improving one part hurts another.
- *Engineering Example:* Reducing the procurement cost of a cheap supplier may increase warranty costs, lowering total profit.

**2. Knowledge of Variation:**
- All processes exhibit variation. Deming distinguished two types:
  - **Common Cause Variation** — Inherent to the system; predictable, statistical, requiring **system redesign**.
  - **Special Cause Variation** — Due to external shocks; requires **root cause investigation** and removal.
- **Key Insight:** Acting on special causes as if they were common causes (or vice versa) makes the system worse.
- *Engineering Example:* A machine tool may drift gradually (common cause) or break a tool suddenly (special cause). The response differs.

**3. Theory of Knowledge:**
- Knowledge is gained through **prediction, observation, and revision**.
- **Key Insight:** A plan is a theory. Its execution is a test. Without theory, you cannot distinguish improvement from random noise.
- *Engineering Example:* A Six Sigma Black Belt hypothesizes that a 5% reduction in curing time will not increase defect rate — the pilot tests this theory.

**4. Knowledge of Psychology:**
- People are inherently different in motivation, learning style, and behavior.
- **Key Insight:** Intrinsic motivation (pride, achievement) beats extrinsic controls (quotas, threats). Fear destroys quality.
- *Engineering Example:* Quality circles (Japanese style) work because they respect worker psychology, not because they impose rules.

**Relation to Six Sigma DMAIC:**

| **DMAIC Phase** | **SoPK Component** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Define** | Appreciation for a System | Identify the system, its aim, customers, and stakeholders. |
| **Measure** | Knowledge of Variation | Establish baseline, analyze variation, use control charts. |
| **Analyze** | Theory of Knowledge | Form hypotheses about root causes, test them with data. |
| **Improve** | Theory of Knowledge + Psychology | Pilot solutions, manage change, respect human factors. |
| **Control** | Knowledge of Variation + System | Sustain improvements, monitor new variation, prevent regression. |

**Conclusion:** SoPK is the *philosophy* that DMAIC is the *practice*. A Six Sigma practitioner who masters SoPK becomes a more effective change agent than one who only knows statistical tools.

*(7 marks — complete coverage of 4 components and DMAIC linkage)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain Deming's Seven Deadly Diseases of Management. Why did Deming consider them so harmful?

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

In his 1982 book *Out of the Crisis*, Deming identified **Seven Deadly Diseases** — systemic management practices that destroy quality, productivity, and morale in organizations. He considered them "deadly" because they are deeply embedded in Western management culture and are often celebrated as *good* practices, making them especially dangerous.

**The Seven Deadly Diseases:**

**1. Lack of constancy of purpose:**
Planning for the short term (quarterly profits, annual bonuses) instead of the long term (product quality, market leadership, customer loyalty). Companies cut quality to meet quarterly numbers, then suffer long-term decline.

**2. Emphasis on short-term profits:**
Driven by shareholder pressure, companies sacrifice R&D, employee training, and quality investments. The result is short-term gain but long-term erosion.

**3. Evaluation by performance, merit rating, or annual review:**
Deming was vehemently against performance ratings. They create fear, destroy teamwork, and force workers to compete rather than collaborate. He argued that **94% of variation is system-induced** — rating a worker for outcomes that the system controls is unfair and demotivating.

**4. Mobility of management:**
Managers changing jobs every 2–3 years (typical in Western corporations) prevents long-term systems thinking. They optimize for their tenure, not for lasting quality.

**5. Running a company on visible figures alone:**
Visible figures (sales, profits, market share) are easy to measure. Invisible figures (customer loyalty, employee morale, future capability) are ignored. Decisions based on visible-only data are dangerously incomplete.

**6. Excessive medical costs:**
In the US context, Deming observed that rising healthcare costs absorb capital that should fund quality improvement, training, and innovation.

**7. Excessive costs of warranty, fueled by lawyers who work for contingency fees:**
Litigation-driven warranty costs incentivize companies to defend poor quality legally rather than improve it systemically.

**Why Deming Considered Them So Harmful:**

- They are **systemic** — they are built into the management system itself, not isolated mistakes.
- They are **culturally normalized** — managers do not recognize them as diseases.
- They are **mutually reinforcing** — short-term focus drives mobility, mobility drives performance ratings, and so on.
- They **prevent transformation** — no organization can implement the 14 Points while these diseases persist.

**Conclusion:** Deming argued that curing these diseases is the **prerequisite** for any quality improvement initiative. Without addressing them, Six Sigma and TQM efforts will fail.

*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Describe the Deming Cycle (PDSA) with a real-time engineering example. How does it support continuous improvement in a manufacturing plant?

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Deming Cycle (PDSA)** is a four-stage iterative method for testing and implementing improvements in any process. It embodies the **scientific method** applied to operational work.

**The Four Stages (with Manufacturing Example):**

**Context:** A CNC machining cell in an automotive plant produces brake discs. The defect rate (discs out of roundness tolerance) is currently 8%. Target: reduce to 2%.

| **Stage** | **Action in the CNC Plant** |
|---|---|
| **PLAN** | The plant engineer hypothesizes: *"Replacing the cutting tool every 100 parts (instead of 150) will reduce tool wear and lower out-of-roundness defects."* A plan is designed: track defects for 2 weeks, compare with the baseline. |
| **DO** | The new tool-change schedule is piloted on one CNC machine. Defect data is collected for 250 parts. |
| **STUDY** | Data shows defects dropped from 8% to 2.5%. The improvement is statistically significant (p < 0.05). |
| **ACT** | The new schedule is standardized across all 12 CNC machines in the cell. A control chart is set up to monitor ongoing performance. |

**How PDSA Supports Continuous Improvement:**

1. **Iterative** — The cycle repeats. After standardizing tool changes, the engineer may next tackle coolant flow, then material handling, then scheduling — each via a new PDSA loop.

2. **Data-Driven** — Every stage is grounded in measurable evidence, not opinion or authority. This aligns with Deming's *Knowledge of Variation* and *Theory of Knowledge*.

3. **Small-Scale Piloting** — Testing on one machine before scaling prevents large-scale failures and builds confidence.

4. **System-Focused** — PDSA targets the **process**, not the worker. Workers are partners in the experiment, not subjects of blame.

5. **Compounding Gains** — Each cycle's small improvement (e.g., 8% → 2.5%) adds up. Over 10 cycles, the process may reach 6$\sigma$ quality.

**Mapping to Six Sigma DMAIC:**

| **PDSA Stage** | **DMAIC Equivalent** |
|---|---|
| PLAN | Define + part of Measure |
| DO | Analyze + Improve (pilot) |
| STUDY | Improve (validation) + part of Control |
| ACT | Control (standardize) + start new cycle (re-Define) |

**Conclusion:** The Deming Cycle is the engine of Kaizen — the relentless, incremental, and scientific improvement of work processes. It is the operational expression of Deming's philosophy and the heartbeat of Six Sigma cultures.

*(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PDCA with PDSA** — Many students write PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act, the Shewhart version). Deming deliberately replaced "Check" with "Study" to emphasize *learning*, not just verification. Writing PDCA costs you the Deming-specific marks.
>
> 2. **Mislocating Deming in time/country** — Deming's first visit to Japan was in **1950**, and the Deming Prize was established in **1951**, not the other way around. Mixing up years is a common error.
>
> 3. **Listing the 14 Points without explanation** — KTU awards marks for the *meaning* of each point, not just numbering them. Always add 1–2 lines of plain English.
>
> 4. **Calling merit rating a "good practice"** — Deming was **AGAINST** merit rating. Writing it positively will lose full marks.
>
> 5. **Forgetting SoPK's 4 components** — These are not optional: Appreciation, Variation, Theory, Psychology. Missing one costs 1.5–2 marks in a 7-mark question.
>
> 6. **Writing about the 14 Points but not linking to Six Sigma** — KTU 2024 Scheme expects Six Sigma integration. Always close your answer with: *"This is foundational to Six Sigma DMAIC..."*
>
> 7. **Spelling error: "Demming" instead of "Deming"** — Appears surprisingly often. The correct spelling is **Deming** (one 'm').

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deming in TQM**
>
> ☐ Deming (1900–1993) is the **Father of Statistical Quality Control** — remember this title.
>
> ☐ He went to **Japan in 1950** under General MacArthur's invitation; the **Deming Prize** was created in 1951.
>
> ☐ His most influential book: ***Out of the Crisis* (1982)**.
>
> ☐ **The 14 Points** are the operational philosophy — memorize at least 6 for Part B.
>
> ☐ **System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)** has **4 components**: Appreciation, Variation, Theory, Psychology.
>
> ☐ **PDSA** stands for **Plan, Do, Study, Act** — *not* PDCA. The "Study" is Deming's signature.
>
> ☐ **Seven Deadly Diseases** — know at least 4; especially: short-term focus, merit rating, mobility of management.
>
> ☐ **94/6 Principle** — 94% of quality problems are system-related, 6% are worker-related.
>
> ☐ **Chain Reaction:** Improve Quality → Decrease Costs → Lower Prices → Stay in Business → Provide Jobs.
>
> ☐ **Red Bead Experiment** demonstrates that the system, not the worker, causes defects.
>
> ☐ Deming's framework directly underpins **Six Sigma DMAIC** — the KTU 2024 Scheme integrates these.
>
> ☐ Famous Quote to anchor your answer: *"It is not necessary to change. Survival is not mandatory."*
>
> ☐ Deming was **AGAINST** quotas, merit ratings, and slogans — keep this consistent in all answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
