> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to *"Discuss the stages of Crosby's QMMG,"* always end your answer by stating that **Stage 5** is the *destination*, and any organization can self-assess its current stage by answering the **35 diagnostic questions** Crosby published.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Cost of Quality | $TCOQ \;=\; P + A + \text{IF} + \text{EF}$ | Currency units (\$, ₹, etc.) |
| 2 | Cost of Good Quality | $COGQ \;=\; P + A$ | Discretionary / proactive spending |
| 3 | Price of Nonconformance | $PONC \;=\; \text{IF} + \text{EF}$ | Reactive / corrective spending |
| 4 | Quality Cost Ratio | $QCR \;=\; \dfrac{COGQ}{PONC}$ | Dimensionless; ideal $\rightarrow \infty$ |
| 5 | Cost Avoidance Ratio | $CAR \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Cost Avoided (in period)}}{\text{Prevention Spending (in period)}}$ | $\geq 1$ indicates healthy prevention |
| 6 | Defects per Million Opportunities | $DPMO \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | Industry-wide Six Sigma benchmark |
| 7 | Rolled-Throughput Yield | $RTY \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Where $Y_i$ is the yield at each process step |
| 8 | Conformance Percentage | $C\% \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}}\right) \times 100$ | Crosby's primary quality metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crosby vs Juran vs Deming — KTU One-Liner Comparison:**
> - **Crosby** → Quality is **conformance**, driven by **prevention**, measured by **PONC**.
> - **Juran** → Quality is **fitness for use**, driven by the **Juran Trilogy** (planning, control, improvement).
> - **Deming** → Quality is **system-driven**, optimized through the **PDSA / PDCA Cycle** and **14 Points**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

> [!NOTE]
> Crosby's framework is **management-oriented**, so derivations here are **numerical/financial**, not algebraic. All COQ computations are standard KTU exam-format problems.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing TCOQ & PONC for a Manufacturing Plant

**Problem (KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style):**
A medium-scale electronics assembly plant reports the following annual quality costs:

| Category | Annual Cost (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| Quality training and process capability studies | 12 |
| Inspection of incoming raw materials | 8 |
| In-process testing of sub-assemblies | 6 |
| Scrap and rework inside the plant | 25 |
| Warranty claims from customers | 18 |
| Product recalls and field-service engineer visits | 10 |
| Defective items returned by customers | 7 |

Calculate:
1. **Total Cost of Quality (TCOQ)**
2. **Cost of Good Quality (COGQ)**
3. **Price of Nonconformance (PONC)**
4. **Quality Cost Ratio (QCR)**
5. **Comment on the plant's quality posture.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Classify each cost into P, A, IF, EF**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prevention Cost (P)} &= \text{Quality training + Process capability studies} \\
&= 12 \; \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Appraisal Cost (A)} &= \text{Incoming inspection + In-process testing} \\
&= 8 + 6 \;=\; 14 \; \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Internal Failure Cost (IF)} &= \text{Scrap + Rework} \\
&= 25 \; \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{External Failure Cost (EF)} &= \text{Warranty} + \text{Recalls} + \text{Returns} \\
&= 18 + 10 + 7 \;=\; 35 \; \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Cost of Quality**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCOQ} &= P + A + \text{IF} + \text{EF} \\
&= 12 + 14 + 25 + 35 \\
&= 86 \; \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cost of Good Quality**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COGQ} &= P + A \\
&= 12 + 14 \\
&= 26 \; \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Price of Nonconformance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PONC} &= \text{IF} + \text{EF} \\
&= 25 + 35 \\
&= 60 \; \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Quality Cost Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{QCR} &= \frac{COGQ}{PONC} \\
&= \frac{26}{60} \\
&= 0.433
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

- PONC (₹60L) accounts for **69.8 %** of TCOQ.
- This means the plant is **fire-fighting** defects after they occur (reactive).
- Crosby would recommend **increasing Prevention investment** (Step 1 of his 14 Steps) to **drive PONC down**.
- Ideally, the long-run target is **PONC trending toward zero** and **COGQ rising as a strategic shield**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crosby's Golden Rule:** *"As Prevention Cost (P) increases, Failure Cost (F) decreases much faster. The net effect is a lower TCOQ."*

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Reduction in PONC after Prevention Investment

**Problem (KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style):**
A firm spends ₹5 lakhs on additional prevention training. As a result:
- Internal Failure Cost drops from ₹40 L to ₹22 L.
- External Failure Cost drops from ₹30 L to ₹18 L.
- Appraisal Cost remains unchanged at ₹12 L.
- Prevention Cost (including the new training) becomes ₹18 L.

Compute the new TCOQ and the savings achieved. Also compute the **Cost Avoidance Ratio (CAR)**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Old Failure Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{old}} &= \text{IF}_{\text{old}} + \text{EF}_{\text{old}} \\
&= 40 + 30 \;=\; 70 \; \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — New Failure Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{new}} &= 22 + 18 \;=\; 40 \; \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Savings in Failure Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= F_{\text{old}} - F_{\text{new}} \\
&= 70 - 40 \;=\; 30 \; \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — New TCOQ**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCOQ}_{\text{new}} &= P_{\text{new}} + A_{\text{new}} + \text{IF}_{\text{new}} + \text{EF}_{\text{new}} \\
&= 18 + 12 + 22 + 18 \\
&= 70 \; \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Old TCOQ**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCOQ}_{\text{old}} &= (18 - 5) + 12 + 40 + 30 \\
&= 13 + 12 + 40 + 30 \;=\; 95 \; \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Note: $P_{\text{old}}$ was ₹13L because the firm had not yet incurred the extra ₹5L.)

**Step 6 — Net Savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Savings} &= \text{TCOQ}_{\text{old}} - \text{TCOQ}_{\text{new}} \\
&= 95 - 70 \;=\; 25 \; \text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Cost Avoidance Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAR} &= \frac{\text{Cost Avoided}}{\text{Prevention Spending}} \\
&= \frac{30}{5} \\
&= 6.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Every ₹1 spent on prevention saved the firm ₹6 in failure costs — a classic Crosby-style **"quality is free"** outcome.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:**
> Crosby empirically claimed that **for every ₹1 spent on prevention, ₹10–100 is saved in PONC**. Numbers like CAR = 6 fall comfortably within his published case studies.

---

## 3.3 Crosby's Triangle of Quality Performance

Crosby used a **triangle** to depict the relationship between **Prevention Cost, Appraisal Cost, and Failure Cost** as quality maturity grows.

At **Stage 1 (Uncertainty)** — failure cost dominates the triangle (PONC ≫ P, A).
At **Stage 5 (Certainty)** — prevention cost dominates; failure cost approaches zero.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCOQ}_{\text{stage1}} &\approx 100\% \; \text{failure} \\
\text{TCOQ}_{\text{stage5}} &\approx 100\% \; \text{prevention}
\end{aligned}
$$

This explains the **paradox**: total quality spending may *increase* initially (more prevention) but eventually **decreases** as failure cost collapses.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Crosby's 14 Steps — Sequential Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1 Management Commitment"] --> B["Step 2 Quality Improvement Team"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Quality Measurement"]
    C --> D["Step 4 Cost of Quality Evaluation"]
    D --> E["Step 5 Quality Awareness"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Corrective Action System"]
    F --> G["Step 7 Establish Zero Defects Program"]
    G --> H["Step 8 Supervisor Training"]
    H --> I["Step 9 Zero Defects Day"]
    I --> J["Step 10 Goal Setting"]
    J --> K["Step 11 Error Cause Removal"]
    K --> L["Step 12 Recognition"]
    L --> M["Step 13 Quality Councils"]
    M --> N["Step 14 Do It Over Again"]
    N -.->|Annual Recycle| C
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted loop from **Step 14 → Step 3** signifies Crosby's **continuous improvement** mandate: every year, re-measure COQ and start a new improvement cycle.

---

## 4.2 Crosby's Quality Management Maturity Grid (QMMG) — 5 Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Stage 1 Uncertainty No understanding of quality"] --> S2["Stage 2 Awakening Quality blamed on QC dept"]
    S2 --> S3["Stage 3 Enlightenment Formal program and COQ"]
    S3 --> S4["Stage 4 Wisdom Prevention institutionalized"]
    S4 --> S5["Stage 5 Certainty Zero defects as a habit"]
    style S1 fill:#ffcccc
    style S2 fill:#ffe0b3
    style S3 fill:#fff7b3
    style S4 fill:#d4f4dd
    style S5 fill:#b3e0ff
```

---

## 4.3 Crosby's Four Absolutes — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Crosby Quality Philosophy"]
    P --> A1["Absolute 1 Quality equals Conformance to Requirements"]
    P --> A2["Absolute 2 Prevention not Inspection"]
    P --> A3["Absolute 3 Zero Defects Performance Standard"]
    P --> A4["Absolute 4 Measure of Quality equals Price of Nonconformance"]
    A4 --> Metric["PONC acts as the universal quality metric for the CFO and CEO"]
```

---

## 4.4 Crosby's Cost of Quality — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TCOQ["Total Cost of Quality TCOQ"]
    TCOQ --> COGQ["Cost of Good Quality COGQ"]
    TCOQ --> PONC["Price of Nonconformance PONC"]
    COGQ --> P["Prevention Cost P"]
    COGQ --> A["Appraisal Cost A"]
    PONC --> IF["Internal Failure Cost IF"]
    PONC --> EF["External Failure Cost EF"]
    P --> Pex["Training Supplier Audits Capability Studies Preventive Maintenance"]
    A --> Aex["Incoming Inspection In-process Inspection Final Inspection Calibration"]
    IF --> IFex["Scrap Rework Reinspection Downtime"]
    EF --> EFex["Warranty Returns Recalls Lawsuits Lost Goodwill"]
```

---

## 4.5 Crosby's Triangle — Maturity-Based Quality Cost Shift

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Stage1["Stage 1 Uncertainty Dominated by Failure Cost"] --> Stage2["Stage 2 Awakening PONC still dominant"]
    Stage2 --> Stage3["Stage 3 Enlightenment COQ begins to be measured"]
    Stage3 --> Stage4["Stage 4 Wisdom Prevention increases Failure drops"]
    Stage4 --> Stage5["Stage 5 Certainty Failure approaches zero Prevention dominates"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual takeaway:** The arrow shifts from *red* (failure-heavy) to *green* (prevention-heavy) as the organization climbs the QMMG ladder — the core narrative of *"Quality Is Free."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the "Price of Nonconformance" (PONC) as per Philip Crosby. Why does Crosby consider PONC to be the most appropriate measure of quality?**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The **Price of Nonconformance (PONC)** is the **total cost incurred by an organization because things were not done right the first time**. Mathematically,

$$
\text{PONC} \;=\; \text{Internal Failure Cost (IF)} \;+\; \text{External Failure Cost (EF)}
$$

Crosby prefers PONC over soft metrics (such as customer satisfaction indices) because:

1. **It is monetary** — the language understood by the CEO and the Board.
2. **It captures the full iceberg** — including hidden costs such as rework, downtime, lost goodwill, and warranty exposure.
3. **It is actionable** — every rupee of PONC can be traced to a preventable defect.
4. **It aligns quality with finance** — making quality a boardroom agenda rather than a QC-department agenda.

*(Word count: ~120 — ideal for 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Crosby's definition of Quality and Juran's definition of Quality.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | **Crosby** | **Juran** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Quality = **Conformance to Requirements** | Quality = **Fitness for Use** |
| **Perspective** | Producer / Engineer-oriented | Customer / Market-oriented |
| **Measurement** | Binary (meets spec / does not meet spec) | Subjective (depends on customer need) |
| **Metric** | PONC, Cost of Quality | Number of complaints, returns, customer surveys |
| **Focus** | Process conformance | Product suitability |

> Both definitions are **complementary**, not contradictory. Modern Six Sigma uses **Crosby's process view** for internal control and **Juran's customer view** for external validation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of Question A or Question B]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Explain in detail Philip Crosby's 14 Steps to Quality Improvement. Discuss how each step contributes to building a prevention-based quality culture.**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`

#### **(a) Explain the first seven steps with their rationale. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Crosby's **14 Steps to Quality Improvement** is a structured, sequential roadmap designed to embed quality into the DNA of an organization. It is not a set of optional practices — it is a **mandatory sequence** in which each step prepares the organization for the next.

**Step 1 — Management Commitment:**
Quality cannot be delegated. Top management must make a **written, signed, and publicly announced commitment** to quality. Without this, employees will treat quality as a temporary slogan.

**Step 2 — Quality Improvement Team (QIT):**
A cross-functional steering committee (typically chaired by a senior vice president) is formed to coordinate all quality activities. The QIT acts as the bridge between management intent and shop-floor execution.

**Step 3 — Quality Measurement:**
Every activity in the organization is measured in terms of its **Cost of Quality (COQ)**. This converts abstract quality goals into tangible financial numbers.

**Step 4 — Cost of Quality Evaluation:**
The COQ data collected in Step 3 is communicated to every manager. The objective is to **create awareness** of how much the organization is losing due to nonconformance — this is the **PONC**.

**Step 5 — Quality Awareness:**
Every employee undergoes orientation about quality. The goal is to replace the **"quality is the QC department's job"** mentality with **"quality is everyone's responsibility."**

**Step 6 — Corrective Action:**
A formal, documented system is established for employees to **report, log, and resolve** problems. The system must guarantee feedback within a fixed time window.

**Step 7 — Establish Zero Defects Program:**
The organization officially adopts **Zero Defects** as its performance standard. This is a **declaration**, not an aspiration — it changes the reference point from *"how many defects are acceptable"* to *"no defects are acceptable."*

#### **(b) Explain steps 8 to 14 and conclude with the significance of Step 14 (Do It Over Again). [7 Marks]**

**Step 8 — Supervisor Training:**
First-line supervisors are trained in the *14 Steps* and Zero Defects philosophy. Crosby believed supervisors are the **critical interface** between management policy and workforce behavior.

**Step 9 — Zero Defects Day:**
A one-day event is held where every employee signs a **Zero Defects pledge** in the presence of senior leadership. The day acts as a **psychological anchor** that makes the abstract commitment tangible.

**Step 10 — Goal Setting:**
Each department, team, and individual sets **measurable, time-bound quality goals**. The goals follow **SMART** criteria (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).

**Step 11 — Error Cause Removal:**
Employees are encouraged to identify and remove the **root causes** of errors. Crosby recommended simple tools such as the **5-Whys** and **Pareto Analysis**. The focus is on **system correction**, not **blame allocation**.

**Step 12 — Recognition:**
Individuals and teams who achieve quality targets are recognized publicly. Crosby strongly preferred **zero-cost recognition** (e.g., certificates, appreciation letters, public announcements) over monetary rewards, to avoid creating a *"pay-for-quality"* culture.

**Step 13 — Quality Councils:**
Inter-departmental Quality Councils are formed to share best practices, lessons learned, and to coordinate cross-functional improvements.

**Step 14 — Do It Over Again:**
The most distinctive step. Crosby insisted that quality is **not a project with an end**; it is a **continuous discipline**. After one cycle of the 14 Steps, the organization must restart the cycle with **new, more aggressive goals**.

> **Conclusion:** Crosby's 14 Steps convert quality from a *technical function* into a *management discipline*. The framework is **comprehensive, sequential, and cyclical**, and remains the most widely used Crosby implementation tool in industry.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Define the Cost of Quality (COQ) as per Philip Crosby. With a suitable numerical example, compute the Total Cost of Quality (TCOQ), Cost of Good Quality (COGQ), Price of Nonconformance (PONC), and Quality Cost Ratio (QCR). Comment on how an organization should re-balance its COQ to move up the Quality Management Maturity Grid.**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

#### **(a) Define COQ with Crosby's classification. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the **total financial burden an organization bears because quality is imperfect**. Crosby reorganized the classical four-category COQ into **two macro-buckets** for executive clarity:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{TCOQ} \;=\; \underbrace{(P + A)}_{\text{COGQ}} \;+\; \underbrace{(\text{IF} + \text{EF})}_{\text{PONC}}\;}
$$

**1. Cost of Good Quality (COGQ):** Money spent *proactively* to *prevent and detect* defects.
- **Prevention Cost (P):** Training, supplier audits, capability studies, preventive maintenance.
- **Appraisal Cost (A):** Incoming inspection, in-process testing, final QA, gauge calibration.

**2. Price of Nonconformance (PONC):** Money spent *reactively* to *fix the damage* caused by defects.
- **Internal Failure Cost (IF):** Scrap, rework, reinspection, downtime, wastage.
- **External Failure Cost (EF):** Warranty, returns, recalls, lawsuits, lost goodwill.

Crosby's breakthrough was to **highlight the iceberg nature of PONC**: most managers see only the visible tip (warranty) while ignoring the submerged bulk (rework, opportunity cost, lost morale).

#### **(b) Numerical Example and Interpretation. [7 Marks]**

**Numerical Problem:**

An automotive component manufacturer reports the following annual quality costs (in ₹ Lakhs):

| Category | Cost |
|---|---|
| Preventive maintenance and training | 14 |
| Inspection of raw materials | 6 |
| In-process inspection | 9 |
| Scrap and rework | 22 |
| Warranty claims | 16 |
| Product recalls | 8 |
| Customer returns | 5 |

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 14 \\
A &= 6 + 9 \;=\; 15 \\
\text{IF} &= 22 \\
\text{EF} &= 16 + 8 + 5 \;=\; 29
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCOQ} &= 14 + 15 + 22 + 29 \;=\; 80 \; \text{lakhs} \\
\text{COGQ} &= 14 + 15 \;=\; 29 \; \text{lakhs} \\
\text{PONC} &= 22 + 29 \;=\; 51 \; \text{lakhs} \\
\text{QCR} &= \frac{29}{51} \approx 0.57
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation and Maturity-Grid Mapping:**

| Indicator | Value | Diagnosis |
|---|---|---|
| PONC share of TCOQ | $51/80 = 63.75\%$ | **Fire-fighting mode** — too much reactive spending. |
| QCR | $0.57 \;\; ( < 1 )$ | Failure costs dominate. |
| Maturity Stage | **Stage 2 (Awakening)** | The firm sees quality as a problem, not a system. |

**Recommended Re-Balancing:**
- **Increase Prevention (P)** through targeted training, supplier development, and **DFM (Design for Manufacturability)** initiatives.
- **Reduce Appraisal (A) over time** by replacing it with in-process **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**.
- **Drive IF and EF toward zero** through root-cause analysis (Pareto, 5-Whys, FMEA).

**Target State (Stage 5 — Certainty):**
A mature firm operates with **P ≈ 70 %, A ≈ 30 %, IF ≈ 0, EF ≈ 0** of TCOQ, meaning **TCOQ shrinks dramatically** while **COGQ rises as a strategic investment** — exactly the *"Quality Is Free"* phenomenon Crosby publicized.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in Crosby questions (leading to 2–4 mark deductions):**
>
> 1. **Confusing PONC with TCOQ** — PONC = failure costs only, not the total. Many students wrongly include Prevention and Appraisal in PONC.
> 2. **Defining Quality as "goodness"** — Crosby explicitly rejected this; quality = **conformance to requirements**.
> 3. **Listing Crosby's 14 Steps without explaining rationale** — Examiners award marks for **why** each step matters, not just **what** it is.
> 4. **Ignoring the maturity-grid context** — When asked to *"suggest improvements"*, you must explicitly state the **target stage** (Stage 5) and **how to climb the QMMG ladder**.
> 5. **Mixing Crosby with Deming** — Do not attribute **14 Points of Deming** to Crosby, or **Zero Defects** to Deming. They are distinct.
> 6. **Skipping units** in COQ numericals — Always state **₹ (or $) lakhs/crores**; evaluators deduct marks for missing units.
> 7. **Forgetting Step 14** — Crosby's *cyclical* mandate is a favourite 1-mark question in Part A; never omit it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Crosby (Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> - **Crosby's era:** 1970s–1980s; founded PCA in 1979; books *Quality Is Free* and *Quality Without Tears*.
> - **Core philosophy:** Quality = **Conformance to Requirements**, not goodness or elegance.
> - **DIRFTF** = Do It Right The First Time — Crosby's foundational slogan.
> - **Four Absolutes:** (1) Quality = Conformance, (2) Prevention, (3) Zero Defects, (4) PONC as the measure.
> - **14 Steps** — mandatory, sequential, cyclical (Step 14 loops back to Step 3).
> - **QMMG** — 5 stages: Uncertainty → Awakening → Enlightenment → Wisdom → Certainty.
> - **COQ structure:** $TCOQ = P + A + IF + EF = COGQ + PONC$.
> - **QCR (Quality Cost Ratio)** = $\dfrac{COGQ}{PONC}$; ideal value $\rightarrow \infty$.
> - **CAR (Cost Avoidance Ratio)** = $\dfrac{\text{Cost Avoided}}{\text{Prevention Spending}}$; Crosby claimed CAR of **10:1 to 100:1**.
> - **Zero Defects is a standard, not a goal** — it changes the reference point of quality.
> - **PONC is the CFO-friendly metric** — preferred over soft customer-satisfaction indices.
> - **Crosby vs Juran:** Conformance vs Fitness for Use — both valid, complementary.
> - **Crosby vs Deming:** Prevention & PONC (Crosby) vs System & PDSA Cycle (Deming).
> - **Modern application:** Crosby's COQ model is embedded in **Six Sigma's DMAIC**, **ISO 9001:2015** cost-of-quality reporting, and **CMMI Level 5** appraisals.
> - **One-line KTU recall formula:**
>
> $$
> \text{Crosby} \;\equiv\; \text{Conformance} \;+\; \text{Prevention} \;+\; \text{Zero Defects} \;+\; \text{PONC} \;+\; \text{14 Steps} \;+\; \text{QMMG Stage 5}
> $$
>
> **Memorize this string verbatim** — it covers 95% of any Part-A question on Crosby.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feigenbaum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Feigenbaum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Dr. Armand V. Feigenbaum

**Dr. Armand V. Feigenbaum (1922 – 2014)** was an American quality management expert, industrialist, and author who is globally recognized as the originator of the concept of **Total Quality Control (TQC)**. In **1956**, he first introduced the revolutionary idea that quality is not the responsibility of a single department (like the production or inspection department) but rather a **company-wide organizational philosophy** that must involve every single function — from marketing to finance, from design to after-sales service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Feigenbaum is one of the **Founding Quality Gurus** of the modern Total Quality Management (TQM) movement. His work forms the philosophical foundation upon which later quality frameworks like **Six Sigma, ISO 9000, and TQM** were built.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Total Quality Control (TQC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Feigenbaum, 1956):**
> *"Total Quality Control is an effective system for integrating the quality-development, quality-maintenance, and quality-improvement efforts of the various groups in an organization so as to enable production and service at the most economical levels which allow for full customer satisfaction."*

In simpler academic KTU terminology, **TQC is a management philosophy that embeds quality consciousness into every employee, every process, and every department of an organization**, with the ultimate goal of achieving complete customer satisfaction at the lowest possible cost.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of TQC as **running a hospital**:

- A hospital does not just rely on the doctors to keep patients healthy. It needs **cleanliness staff (sanitation = process quality)**, **receptionists (service quality)**, **pharmacists (input material quality)**, **accountants (financial quality)**, and **administrators (management quality)**.
- If any one of them fails, the patient (customer) suffers.
- Feigenbaum's TQC says exactly the same thing: **a factory is like a hospital for the customer.** Quality is the joint responsibility of *everyone* — from the CEO to the floor sweeper.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Customer-First" Rule:** According to Feigenbaum, **the customer is the ultimate judge of quality**, not the engineer, the manager, or the marketing team. Quality exists only when the customer says it does.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **TQC** | Total Quality Control — integrated quality system |
| **Quality Gurus** | Pioneers of quality (Feigenbaum, Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Taguchi) |
| **Hidden Plant** | 20–40% of plant capacity wasted on rework/scrap (Feigenbaum's concept) |
| **Customer-Defined Quality** | Quality is what the customer perceives, not what the producer assumes |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Feigenbaum's Core Philosophy (The 4 Pillars)

Feigenbaum's Total Quality Control rests on **four foundational pillars** that every KTU student must memorize:

1. **Quality is a company-wide process** — not confined to the production floor.
2. **Quality must be managed, not just inspected** — prevention over detection.
3. **Quality is an investment, not a cost** — it reduces the "hidden plant" of rework.
4. **Quality is defined by the customer** — internal and external customers both matter.

## 2.2 The "Hidden Plant" Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hidden Plant:** Feigenbaum famously stated that in many manufacturing organizations, **20% to 40% of total plant capacity** is consumed by activities that do not add value — namely **rework, repair, warranty claims, inspection, and scrap**. This non-productive capacity is the **"hidden plant"** — a *phantom factory* operating inside every real factory.

**Real-world utility:** Identifying the "hidden plant" is what gives Six Sigma its economic justification. Reducing defects by even a few percentage points frees up massive capital, which is the basis of Six Sigma's **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** and **cost-of-poor-quality (COPQ)** metrics.

## 2.3 The 9 M's of Quality Control (Feigenbaum's Framework)

Feigenbaum identified **nine fundamental areas** that an organization must control in order to achieve total quality. These are collectively known as the **"9 M's"** and are extremely high-yield for KTU examinations.

| # | The 9 M's | Field / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Markets** | Customer research, market intelligence, feedback |
| 2 | **Money** | Financial planning, quality cost accounting |
| 3 | **Management** | Leadership commitment, quality policy |
| 4 | **Men** (Manpower) | Training, employee involvement, motivation |
| 5 | **Motivation** | Morale, incentive systems, recognition |
| 6 | **Materials** | Incoming material control, vendor quality |
| 7 | **Machines and Equipment** | Preventive maintenance, capability |
| 8 | **Methods** | Process design, work standardization |
| 9 | **Measurement** | Inspection, testing, statistical process control |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hack:** The 9 M's can be remembered with the mnemonic **"5 M's are Man/Material/Machine related, and 4 M's are Management related"** — but you must write all 9 in the exam to score full marks.

## 2.4 Feigenbaum's Three Steps to Quality

According to Feigenbaum, a successful TQC program must be built in **three sequential steps**:

- **Step 1 – Quality Leadership:** Top management must provide *visible, committed, and continuous leadership* for quality. Without CEO-level commitment, TQC fails.
- **Step 2 – Modern Quality Technology:** Deploy *statistical quality control, engineering capability analysis, and modern inspection methods* across the organization.
- **Step 3 – Continuous Organizational Commitment:** Embed quality into the *culture* of the organization through training, motivation, and reinforcement.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Six Sigma is largely concept-based, but the following mathematical expressions are used to quantify Feigenbaum's philosophy (especially the "Hidden Plant" and quality cost concept).

$$
\text{Hidden Plant Capacity (\%)} = \frac{\text{Capacity lost to rework + scrap + inspection}}{\text{Total Plant Capacity}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)} = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}
$$

$$
\text{Six Sigma DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities}} \times 1{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Yield Level } (\sigma) = 0.5 \times \left[ 1 + \text{erf}\!\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{2\sqrt{2}\,\sigma_{\text{process}}} \right) \right]
$$

**Boundary Conditions & Units:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{\text{prevention}}$ | Cost of preventing defects | Currency (₹, \$, €) |
| $C_{\text{appraisal}}$ | Cost of inspecting/appraising | Currency |
| $C_{\text{internal failure}}$ | Cost of defects found before delivery | Currency |
| $C_{\text{external failure}}$ | Cost of defects found by customer | Currency |
| DPMO | Defects Per Million Opportunities | Defects / 10^6 |
| $\sigma_{\text{process}}$ | Process standard deviation | Units of measurement |
| USL / LSL | Upper / Lower Specification Limit | Units of measurement |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Feigenbaum's framework is the philosophical backbone of:
- **Automotive Industry:** Used by Toyota, Ford, and Bosch to embed quality in every employee.
- **Software Industry:** Inspired CMMI and ISO 9001 standards.
- **Healthcare:** TQC principles drive hospital accreditation systems (NABH in India).
- **Six Sigma (the subject of this course):** Directly inherits Feigenbaum's idea that **quality must be data-driven, customer-focused, and organization-wide.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

Since this topic is **conceptual-management** in nature rather than a pure mathematical derivation, the analytical matrix below walks through the **step-by-step application** of Feigenbaum's TQC philosophy in a quantified manner using the COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality) and Hidden Plant formulas. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Quantifying the "Hidden Plant"

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Style — 14 mark type):**
> A manufacturing plant operates **3 shifts per day, 8 hours per shift, 300 working days per year**. Due to rework, scrap, and excessive inspection, it is estimated that the plant loses **35% of its effective capacity** to the "hidden plant". Calculate the equivalent lost hours and lost production cost, given the hourly operating cost of the plant is **₹50,000 per hour**.

### Step 1: Identify the Given Parameters

- Operating hours per shift, $H_s = 8$ hours
- Number of shifts per day, $N_s = 3$
- Number of working days per year, $D = 300$
- Hidden plant fraction, $f_{\text{hidden}} = 35\% = 0.35$
- Hourly operating cost, $C_{\text{hr}} = 50{,}000$ ₹/hour

### Step 2: Compute Total Available Operating Hours per Year

$$
H_{\text{total}} = H_s \times N_s \times D
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
H_{\text{total}} = 8 \times 3 \times 300
$$

$$
H_{\text{total}} = 7{,}200 \text{ hours/year}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Hours Lost to the Hidden Plant

$$
H_{\text{lost}} = H_{\text{total}} \times f_{\text{hidden}}
$$

$$
H_{\text{lost}} = 7{,}200 \times 0.35
$$

$$
H_{\text{lost}} = 2{,}520 \text{ hours/year}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Equivalent Lost Production Cost per Year

$$
C_{\text{lost}} = H_{\text{lost}} \times C_{\text{hr}}
$$

$$
C_{\text{lost}} = 2{,}520 \times 50{,}000
$$

$$
C_{\text{lost}} = 12{,}60{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} = \text{₹} 12.6 \text{ Crore per year}
$$

### Step 5: Interpretation in the Context of Feigenbaum's TQC

According to Feigenbaum's philosophy, this **₹12.6 crore loss** represents the *true cost of poor quality*. A Six Sigma project targeting this plant would aim to **eliminate the hidden plant** by moving the process from its current state to a higher $\sigma$ level — directly recovering this lost capacity and saving the company crores annually.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Answer Framing Tip:** Always close your numerical answer with a one-sentence conceptual link to Feigenbaum's TQC. This is what earns you the **application-level marks (L4–L5 Bloom's level)** in the ESE.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Style):**
> A company reports the following annual quality costs: Prevention = ₹5 lakh, Appraisal = ₹8 lakh, Internal Failure = ₹20 lakh, External Failure = ₹40 lakh. Total revenue = ₹5 crore. Compute: (a) Total COPQ, (b) COPQ as a % of revenue, and (c) the ratio of failure costs to prevention costs.

### Step 1: Total COPQ Calculation

$$
\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}
$$

$$
\text{COPQ} = 5 + 8 + 20 + 40
$$

$$
\text{COPQ} = 73 \text{ lakh ₹} = \text{₹} 73{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 2: COPQ as a Percentage of Revenue

$$
\text{COPQ \%} = \frac{\text{COPQ}}{\text{Total Revenue}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{COPQ \%} = \frac{73{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{COPQ \%} = 1.46\%
$$

### Step 3: Failure-to-Prevention Ratio

$$
R_{\text{FP}} = \frac{C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}}{C_{\text{prevention}}}
$$

$$
R_{\text{FP}} = \frac{20 + 40}{5} = \frac{60}{5} = 12
$$

### Step 4: Interpretive Conclusion

A **12:1 failure-to-prevention ratio** means the company is spending **₹12 on failure for every ₹1 spent on prevention**. Feigenbaum's TQC philosophy advocates **shifting this ratio in favor of prevention** — investments in training, design, and process capability cost less than the failures they prevent. This is the economic logic that powers Six Sigma.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Feigenbaum's Total Quality Control (TQC) Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Customer Ultimate Judge of Quality] --> nodeB[TQC Company Wide Philosophy]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Quality Leadership Top Management]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Modern Quality Technology SQC SPC]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Organizational Commitment Culture]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Quality Mindset]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Eliminate Hidden Plant]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Reduce Rework and Scrap]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Reduce COPQ Cost of Poor Quality]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Customer Satisfaction]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Six Sigma DPMO Reduction]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Higher Process Sigma Level]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Organizational Excellence]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The 9 M's of Feigenbaum

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeM1[Markets] --> nodeCore[TQC Implementation]
    nodeM2[Money] --> nodeCore
    nodeM3[Management] --> nodeCore
    nodeM4[Men Manpower] --> nodeCore
    nodeM5[Motivation] --> nodeCore
    nodeM6[Materials] --> nodeCore
    nodeM7[Machines and Equipment] --> nodeCore
    nodeM8[Methods] --> nodeCore
    nodeM9[Measurement] --> nodeCore
    nodeCore --> nodeOut[Total Customer Satisfaction]
    nodeOut --> nodeSigma[Six Sigma Achievement]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Feigenbaum's TQC → Six Sigma Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeInput[Input Customer Requirements] --> nodeDesign[Design Phase Methods M]
    nodeDesign --> nodeMaterial[Material Selection Materials M]
    nodeMaterial --> nodeMachine[Machine Capability Machines M]
    nodeMachine --> nodeMeasure[Process Measurement Measurement M]
    nodeMeasure --> nodeMan[Manpower Training Men M]
    nodeMan --> nodeMotivate[Motivation and Morale Motivation M]
    nodeMotivate --> nodeMgmt[Management Oversight Management M]
    nodeMgmt --> nodeFin[Financial Control Money M]
    nodeFin --> nodeMarket[Market Feedback Markets M]
    nodeMarket --> nodeOut[Output Customer Satisfaction and Six Sigma Level]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram for KTU exams:** Each "M" block represents one of Feigenbaum's 9 M's. Notice how the diagram forms a **closed feedback loop** — this is Feigenbaum's central message: quality control is **continuous and cyclic**, not linear. Six Sigma inherits this PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) logic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define Total Quality Control (TQC) as proposed by Armand V. Feigenbaum.**
> [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Total Quality Control (TQC), introduced by Dr. Armand V. Feigenbaum in 1956, is defined as *"an effective system for integrating the quality-development, quality-maintenance, and quality-improvement efforts of the various groups in an organization so as to enable production and service at the most economical levels which allow for full customer satisfaction."* The term "Total" emphasizes that quality is **everyone's responsibility**, spanning every department and every employee, not just the production floor. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. What is meant by the "Hidden Plant" concept introduced by Feigenbaum?**
> [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The "Hidden Plant" is a concept introduced by Feigenbaum to describe the **20% to 40% of plant capacity** that is consumed by non-value-adding activities such as **rework, scrap, warranty repairs, and excessive inspection**. This represents a *phantom factory* operating inside the real factory, costing the company enormous sums without producing any useful output. Identifying and eliminating the hidden plant is one of the key motivations for adopting Six Sigma. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model ESE Question A]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the **philosophy of Total Quality Control (TQC)** as given by Dr. Armand V. Feigenbaum. Discuss its key principles and how it differs from the traditional inspection-based approach. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* List and explain the **9 M's of Quality Control** as identified by Feigenbaum, with suitable examples from a manufacturing industry. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Feigenbaum's TQC Philosophy (7 Marks):**

1. **Background:** Dr. Armand V. Feigenbaum introduced the concept of Total Quality Control in **1956** and published it in his landmark book *"Total Quality Control"* in **1961**. He was a pioneer in recognizing that quality cannot be achieved by relying solely on the inspection or production department.
2. **Core Philosophy:** TQC is built on the principle that **quality is a company-wide responsibility** that must be embraced by every function — marketing, design, procurement, production, sales, finance, HR, and after-sales service. **(1 mark)**
3. **Customer-Defined Quality:** According to Feigenbaum, the **customer is the ultimate judge of quality**, not the engineer or manager. Quality exists only when the customer perceives it. **(1 mark)**
4. **Quality is an Investment:** Feigenbaum argued that money spent on prevention is cheaper than money spent on failure. This is the foundation of the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** concept. **(1 mark)**
5. **Hidden Plant:** He identified that **20–40% of plant capacity** is wasted on rework, scrap, and inspection — which he called the "hidden plant". **(1 mark)**
6. **Difference from Traditional Inspection Approach:** Traditional quality relied on **end-of-line inspection** (detection). Feigenbaum advocated **prevention and process control** (prevention) — a paradigm shift. **(1 mark)**
7. **Three Steps to Quality:** Quality Leadership, Modern Quality Technology (SQC, SPC), and Continuous Organizational Commitment. **(1 mark)**

**(b) The 9 M's of Quality Control (7 Marks):**

| # | The 9 M's | Explanation (1 mark each for any 7) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Markets** | Customer research, feedback systems, market analysis to understand quality needs. |
| 2 | **Money** | Financial planning, quality cost accounting, budgeting for quality programs. |
| 3 | **Management** | Top management leadership, quality policy formulation, strategic direction. |
| 4 | **Men (Manpower)** | Recruitment, training, skill development of employees at all levels. |
| 5 | **Motivation** | Employee morale, recognition systems, incentive schemes to drive quality. |
| 6 | **Materials** | Incoming material inspection, vendor quality control, supplier management. |
| 7 | **Machines & Equipment** | Preventive maintenance, machine capability studies, equipment calibration. |
| 8 | **Methods** | Process design, work standardization, SOP development, capability analysis. |
| 9 | **Measurement** | Inspection, testing, metrology, statistical process control (SPC) charts. |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model ESE Question B]**
*(a)* Discuss the concept of the **"Hidden Plant"** as described by Feigenbaum. How does this concept justify the economic need for Six Sigma programs? **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Explain Feigenbaum's **three steps to quality** and describe how they form the basis for implementing a Six Sigma project in an organization. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) The Hidden Plant (7 Marks):**

1. **Definition:** The "Hidden Plant" is Feigenbaum's term for the **phantom capacity** within a manufacturing facility that is consumed by non-value-adding activities such as **rework, repair, scrap, warranty servicing, and excessive inspection**. **(1 mark)**
2. **Magnitude:** Studies cited by Feigenbaum show that this hidden plant typically consumes **20% to 40%** of total plant capacity. **(1 mark)**
3. **Cost Impact:** The hidden plant is essentially a **financial drain** — labour, materials, machine time, and energy are spent producing defects instead of good products. **(1 mark)**
4. **Visibility Problem:** Unlike the visible factory (which produces saleable output), the hidden plant is *invisible* to most managers because its costs are buried in overhead accounts. **(1 mark)**
5. **Six Sigma Justification:** Six Sigma directly targets the hidden plant by **driving down the defect rate (DPMO)**. Each $\sigma$ improvement frees up hidden capacity. For example, moving from 3$\sigma$ (66,807 DPMO) to 6$\sigma$ (3.4 DPMO) virtually eliminates the hidden plant. **(2 marks)**
6. **Engineering Example:** In a chip fabrication plant, rework on defective wafers can consume 25% of clean-room time — the clean room is the "visible" plant, the rework stations form the "hidden" plant. Six Sigma projects aim to convert hidden capacity into productive output. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Three Steps to Quality (7 Marks):**

1. **Step 1 — Quality Leadership (Top Management Commitment):** Feigenbaum emphasized that without **visible and continuous commitment from top management**, TQC programs collapse. Leaders must set quality goals, allocate resources, and personally review quality metrics. **(2 marks)**
2. **Step 2 — Modern Quality Technology:** Organizations must deploy **statistical quality control, control charts, capability analysis (Cp, Cpk), Six Sigma DMAIC, and design of experiments (DOE)**. This is the *technical backbone* of TQC. **(2 marks)**
3. **Step 3 — Continuous Organizational Commitment:** Quality must become embedded in the **culture** of the organization through continuous training, employee involvement (kaizen teams), motivation, and reward systems. **(2 marks)**
4. **Linkage to Six Sigma:** Six Sigma's **DMAIC (Define–Measure–Analyze–Improve–Control)** framework mirrors Feigenbaum's three steps: Leadership corresponds to **Define**, Modern Technology corresponds to **Measure–Analyze–Improve**, and Organizational Commitment corresponds to **Control**. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Feigenbaum with Deming or Juran.** While all are quality gurus, Feigenbaum is uniquely associated with **TQC** and the **Hidden Plant** concept. Mixing their contributions will cost you marks.
> 2. **Always write all 9 M's.** Examiners award 1 mark per M. Writing only 5 or 6 will leave marks on the table.
> 3. **Always close the answer with a customer-satisfaction or Six Sigma link.** Feigenbaum is the philosophical foundation of Six Sigma — this linkage earns you the L4–L5 application marks.
> 4. **Use bullet points and tables** in Part B answers. Long paragraphs lead to examiner fatigue and lost marks.
> 5. **Hidden Plant is a *concept*, not a formula.** Do not present it as a math equation; frame it qualitatively with a numerical example for full marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Feigenbaum**

- **Who:** Dr. Armand V. Feigenbaum (American, 1922–2014), quality management pioneer.
- **When:** Introduced TQC in **1956**; published the book in **1961**.
- **What:** **Total Quality Control (TQC)** — an integrated, company-wide quality management philosophy.
- **The "Total" in TQC:** Quality is **everyone's responsibility** (marketing, design, production, sales, finance, HR, service).
- **The Customer is King:** Quality is defined by the **customer**, not by the producer.
- **Hidden Plant:** **20–40%** of plant capacity wasted on rework, scrap, and inspection — the economic justification for Six Sigma.
- **9 M's of Quality Control:** **Markets, Money, Management, Men, Motivation, Materials, Machines & Equipment, Methods, Measurement** — all 9 must be listed in exams.
- **Three Steps to Quality:** **(1) Quality Leadership (2) Modern Quality Technology (3) Continuous Organizational Commitment.**
- **Prevention vs. Inspection:** Feigenbaum pioneered the shift from **detection-based** to **prevention-based** quality.
- **Linkage to Six Sigma:** Feigenbaum's TQC is the **philosophical parent** of Six Sigma; DMAIC is the operational child.
- **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ):** $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}$.
- **Hidden Plant Capacity:** $\text{Hidden Plant \%} = \frac{\text{Lost Capacity}}{\text{Total Capacity}} \times 100$.
- **Examiner's favorite question patterns:** "Define TQC", "Explain Hidden Plant", "List the 9 M's", "Explain three steps to quality", "Differentiate TQC from inspection-based quality".
- **Mnemonic for the 9 M's:** **"4 M's Manage, 5 M's Make"** — Management, Men, Motivation, Money (the 4 management M's); Markets, Materials, Machines, Methods, Measurement (the 5 making-related M's).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ishikawa
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ishikawa

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ishikawa Diagram (Fishbone Diagram / Cause-and-Effect Diagram / Herringbone Diagram):**
> A structured graphical analytical tool used in Six Sigma's **DMAIC** (Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control) methodology, primarily within the **Analyze** phase, to systematically identify, organize, and visualize the potential root causes of a specific problem (the "effect") by categorizing them into related cause groups branching off a central spine.

It was conceptualized by **Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa**, a Japanese quality management pioneer, in **1968** at the Kawasaki shipyards. It is formally registered as one of the seven basic quality tools (7 QC tools) by the Union of Japanese Scientists and Engineers (JUSE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Doctor's Diagnosis Analogy:**
> Imagine a patient walks into a doctor's clinic complaining of a chronic headache (the **EFFECT**). The doctor does not simply prescribe a painkiller. Instead, she investigates systematically:
> - Is it **lifestyle**? (lack of sleep, stress, dehydration)
> - Is it **dietary**? (too much caffeine, skipped meals)
> - Is it **environmental**? (loud noise, bad posture at the desk)
> - Is it a **medical** issue? (vision problems, migraines, sinuses)
>
> The doctor categorizes every potential cause under these "branches" and traces each one until the root cause is found. The Ishikawa diagram works exactly the same way: the **head of the fish is the problem**, the **spine is the causal pathway**, and the **ribs are the categories of causes**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Categories (The 6Ms Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Six Sigma, the standard categorization of causes is known as the **6Ms**, representing the six classical sources of variation in a manufacturing/service process.

The **6Ms** categories are:

| # | Category | Description | Typical Causes |
|---|----------|-------------|----------------|
| **1** | **Man (People)** | Human factors, skills, training | Operator error, lack of training, fatigue |
| **2** | **Machine (Equipment)** | Hardware, tools, technology | Machine wear, calibration drift, downtime |
| **3** | **Material (Inputs)** | Raw materials, consumables | Defective inputs, supplier variation |
| **4** | **Method (Process)** | Procedures, workflows, policies | Inefficient SOPs, missing work instructions |
| **5** | **Measurement (Inspection)** | Data systems, gauges, metrics | Calibration, gauge R&R issues |
| **6** | **Mother Nature (Environment)** | External/internal environment | Temperature, humidity, lighting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variant Frameworks:**
> - **4Ms** (older, used in pure manufacturing): Man, Machine, Material, Method.
> - **8Ps** (used in service industries & marketing): Price, Promotion, People, Process, Place, Policies, Product, Procedure.
> - **4Ss** (used in software/services): Surroundings, Suppliers, Systems, Skills.

## 1.4 Position in the DMAIC Framework

$$\text{DMAIC: Define} \rightarrow \text{Measure} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Analyze (Ishikawa)}} \rightarrow \text{Improve} \rightarrow \text{Control}$$

The Ishikawa diagram is the primary qualitative root-cause analysis tool in the **Analyze** phase, often paired with quantitative tools like **Pareto Charts** (to rank causes) and **5-Whys Analysis** (to drill down).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Causal Logic

The Ishikawa diagram operates on the fundamental Six Sigma principle that **every defect or variation in a process output is the cumulative effect of multiple input variables interacting across known cause categories**.

The underlying causal chain can be expressed as:

$$\text{Effect (Problem)} = f(\text{Cause}_1, \text{Cause}_2, \ldots, \text{Cause}_n)$$

Where each $\text{Cause}_i$ belongs to one of the 6Ms (or variant) categories.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### Step 1: Define the Effect (Problem Statement)
- The team must write a **clear, specific, measurable** problem statement.
- The statement must answer: **What** is wrong, **Where** it occurs, **When** it occurs, and **How big** the gap is.
- *Example:* "Customer complaints on Order #C-104 have risen by **22%** in the Bangalore warehouse during Q3 2024."

### Step 2: Draw the Spine
- Draw a horizontal arrow pointing right. The arrowhead represents the **effect**.

### Step 3: Add Major Branch Categories
- Draw diagonal lines (the "ribs") branching off the spine, each labeled with a 6M category.

### Step 4: Brainstorm Causes
- Conduct a **team-based brainstorming session** (typically 4–8 cross-functional members).
- For each category, ask: *"What in this category could cause the effect?"*
- Use sticky notes or markers; **no criticism** during this phase (per Brainstorming rules).

### Step 5: Drill Down with Sub-Causes
- Attach smaller "fishbones" off each main rib to represent **sub-causes** (often derived using the **5 Whys** technique).

### Step 6: Identify the Vital Few
- Apply the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** to rank causes.
- Mark the most likely **vital few** root causes for further investigation (FMEA, Regression, Hypothesis Testing).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept Map for Ishikawa**

| Concept Element | Formal Definition | KTU Exam Significance |
|----------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Effect** | The problem, defect, or undesirable outcome on the right side of the diagram. | Often given as the "effect" in 14-mark questions. |
| **Cause** | Any factor that contributes to the effect; attached as branches. | Must be brainstormed exhaustively. |
| **6Ms** | Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature | **Default categorization** for manufacturing. |
| **8Ps** | Price, Promotion, People, Process, Place, Policies, Product, Procedure | Used when effect is **service/marketing** related. |
| **4Ss** | Surroundings, Suppliers, Systems, Skills | Used in **software/IT services**. |
| **Brainstorming** | Group ideation technique (Osborn, 1953); non-judgmental idea generation. | Foundational step for cause identification. |
| **5 Whys** | Iterative questioning technique (Toyota Production System) to reach root cause. | Sub-cause drill-down methodology. |
| **Pareto** | 80/20 rule; vital few vs. trivial many. | Used post-Ishikawa to prioritize causes. |

## 2.4 Relationship with Other QC Tools

$$\underbrace{\text{Check Sheet}}_{\text{Data Collection}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Pareto}}_{\text{Prioritization}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Ishikawa}}_{\text{Cause Identification}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Histogram / Control Chart}}_{\text{Verification}}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Identifying root causes of surface defects in CNC machined components.
- **Healthcare:** Diagnosing sources of hospital-acquired infections (HAIs).
- **Software:** Analyzing causes of deployment failures in DevOps pipelines.
- **Service Industry:** Pinpointing reasons for customer churn in telecom.
- **Civil Engineering:** Investigating causes of concrete cracking or foundation settlement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Construction of an Ishikawa Diagram: Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Scenario:** A Six Sigma Black Belt project at a precision bearing manufacturing plant observes that **"Dimensional rejection rate of Shaft Bearing SB-204 has increased from 2% to 11% over the last quarter."**
> Construct the Ishikawa diagram using the 6Ms framework and identify the vital few root causes.

### Step 1: Define the Effect
$$\text{Effect} = \text{"Dimensional Rejection of SB-204} = 11\% \text{ (Target: } \leq 2\%\text{)"}$$

### Step 2: Identify the Categories (6Ms)
$$\text{Categories} = \{\text{Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature}\}$$

### Step 3: Brainstorm Causes per Category

#### (i) Man (People)
- Operators not trained on new CNC program v3.2
- Shift handover communication gap (night to morning shift)
- Operator fatigue due to overtime (>12 hrs/day)

#### (ii) Machine
- CNC lathe spindle bearing worn (overdue maintenance at 5,200 hrs)
- Coolant pump pressure below specification (4.2 bar vs. 6.0 bar)
- Tool wear on cutting insert (unmonitored)

#### (iii) Material
- Incoming bar stock diameter variation: $\pm 0.15$ mm (tolerance: $\pm 0.05$ mm)
- Hardness of AISI 52100 steel batch inconsistent (HRC 58 vs. spec 62)

#### (iv) Method
- SOP not updated after CNC program revision (still references v2.8)
- First-article inspection skipped for 3 weeks

#### (v) Measurement
- Optical comparator calibration expired by 14 days
- No gauge R&R study performed on the digital caliper

#### (vi) Mother Nature
- Ambient temperature variation: $18°\text{C}$ (night) to $34°\text{C}$ (day) — exceeds thermal stability spec ($20°\pm 2°\text{C}$)
- Humidity causing minor rust on bar stock storage

### Step 4: Apply Pareto Prioritization

$$\text{Rejection Contribution (estimated)}:$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CNC Spindle Wear} &= 32\% \\
\text{Incoming Material Variation} &= 24\% \\
\text{SOP Not Updated} &= 18\% \\
\text{Gauge Calibration} &= 12\% \\
\text{Other Causes (combined)} &= 14\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Vital Few Identification
$$\boxed{\text{Vital Few} = \{\text{Spindle Wear, Material Variation, SOP Gap}\}}$$

These three causes account for **74%** of the rejection problem (consistent with the Pareto 80/20 principle).

### Step 6: Drill Down with 5 Whys (Example: Spindle Wear)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Why 1: Why is rejection high?} &\rightarrow \text{Diameter is oversize.} \\
\text{Why 2: Why is diameter oversize?} &\rightarrow \text{Tool deflection during cut.} \\
\text{Why 3: Why is there tool deflection?} &\rightarrow \text{Spindle bearing has play.} \\
\text{Why 4: Why does spindle have play?} &\rightarrow \text{Bearing is worn.} \\
\text{Why 5: Why is bearing worn?} &\rightarrow \text{No preventive replacement at 5,000 hrs.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\text{Root Cause} = \text{Absence of preventive maintenance schedule for CNC spindle.}}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Ishikawa vs. Other Root Cause Tools

| Parameter | **Ishikawa Diagram** | **5 Whys** | **FMEA** | **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** |
|-----------|----------------------|------------|----------|-------------------------------|
| **Primary Purpose** | Cause identification & categorization | Deep drill-down to root cause | Risk quantification (RPN) | Boolean logic of failure paths |
| **Output Type** | Qualitative (visual) | Qualitative (linear chain) | Semi-quantitative (RPN score) | Quantitative (probability) |
| **DMAIC Phase** | Analyze | Analyze | Analyze / Improve | Analyze (Design) |
| **Data Requirement** | Low (expert opinion) | Low | Medium (severity/occurrence scales) | High (failure rates) |
| **Complexity** | Low–Medium | Very Low | Medium | Very High |
| **Team Size** | 4–8 people | 2–4 people | 4–10 people | Specialists |
| **Best Use Case** | Brainstorming causes | Verifying one suspected cause | Risk-ranking known failure modes | Safety-critical / aerospace |

## 3.3 Mathematical Underpinnings of Cause-Effect Logic

Although the Ishikawa diagram is qualitative, its logic can be formally expressed using **set theory** for advanced analysis.

Let $C$ be the set of all potential causes:

$$C = \{c_1, c_2, c_3, \ldots, c_n\}$$

Let $M_j$ be the $j$-th category (e.g., Machine):

$$M_j \subseteq C$$

The complete cause set is the union of all categories:

$$\bigcup_{j=1}^{k} M_j = C \quad \text{where } k = 6 \text{ (for 6Ms)}$$

The probability that the effect $E$ occurs is bounded by the union bound:

$$P(E) \leq \sum_{j=1}^{k} P(E \mid M_j) \cdot P(M_j)$$

In practice, eliminating the **vital few** causes (typically 2–3) can reduce $P(E)$ by **70–90%**, validating the Pareto-aligned Ishikawa approach.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Fishbone Diagram (Cause-and-Effect Structure)

The following Mermaid `flowchart` mimics the visual layout of a classic Ishikawa diagram, with the effect on the right and the 6M branches as main ribs leading to specific causes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    %% Main spine and effect
    effectNode["EFFECT: Dimensional Rejection of SB-204 = 11%"]
    spineNode[/"SPINE (Causal Pathway)"/]

    %% 6M Categories
    manNode["MAN"]
    machineNode["MACHINE"]
    materialNode["MATERIAL"]
    methodNode["METHOD"]
    measurementNode["MEASUREMENT"]
    natureNode["MOTHER NATURE"]

    %% Sub-causes
    manA["Operator untrained on CNC v3.2"]
    manB["Shift handover gap"]

    machineA["Spindle bearing worn"]
    machineB["Coolant pressure low"]
    machineC["Tool wear unmonitored"]

    materialA["Bar stock diameter variation"]
    materialB["Hardness inconsistency"]

    methodA["SOP not updated"]
    methodB["First-article inspection skipped"]

    measurementA["Comparator calibration expired"]
    measurementB["No Gauge R&R done"]

    natureA["Ambient temp variation 18 to 34 C"]
    natureB["Humidity rust on stock"]

    %% Spine connections
    spineNode --> effectNode

    %% Main rib branches
    manNode --- spineNode
    machineNode --- spineNode
    materialNode --- spineNode
    methodNode --- spineNode
    measurementNode --- spineNode
    natureNode --- spineNode

    %% Sub-causes attachment
    manNode --> manA
    manNode --> manB

    machineNode --> machineA
    machineNode --> machineB
    machineNode --> machineC

    materialNode --> materialA
    materialNode --> materialB

    methodNode --> methodA
    methodNode --> methodB

    measurementNode --> measurementA
    measurementNode --> measurementB

    natureNode --> natureA
    natureNode --> natureB

    %% Styling
    classDef effect fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef category fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E3A5F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cause fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef spine fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class effectNode effect
    class manNode,machineNode,materialNode,methodNode,measurementNode,natureNode category
    class manA,manB,machineA,machineB,machineC,materialA,materialB,methodA,methodB,measurementA,measurementB,natureA,natureB cause
    class spineNode spine
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Ishikawa in the DMAIC Workflow

This block diagram explains how the Ishikawa tool integrates within the broader Six Sigma DMAIC framework and what feeds into it / what it feeds into.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    %% Phase blocks
    blockDefine["DEFINE PHASE: Define the Problem and CTQ"]
    blockMeasure["MEASURE PHASE: Baseline Capability and Data Collection"]
    blockAnalyzeA["ANALYZE PHASE: Brainstorming Session"]
    blockAnalyzeB["ANALYZE PHASE: Ishikawa Diagram Construction"]
    blockAnalyzeC["ANALYZE PHASE: 5 Whys Drill Down"]
    blockAnalyzeD["ANALYZE PHASE: Pareto Prioritization"]
    blockImprove["IMPROVE PHASE: Pilot Solutions for Vital Few Causes"]
    blockControl["CONTROL PHASE: Statistical Process Control"]

    %% Inputs to Ishikawa
    inp1["CTQ Definition from Voice of Customer"]
    inp2["Baseline Defect Data"]
    inp3["Cross-functional Team 4 to 8 members"]
    inp4["Brainstorming Output"]

    %% Outputs from Ishikawa
    out1["Categorized Cause List 6Ms"]
    out2["Sub-cause Hierarchy"]

    %% Connections
    blockDefine --> blockMeasure
    blockMeasure --> blockAnalyzeA
    blockAnalyzeA --> blockAnalyzeB
    blockAnalyzeB --> blockAnalyzeC
    blockAnalyzeC --> blockAnalyzeD
    blockAnalyzeD --> blockImprove
    blockImprove --> blockControl

    inp1 --> blockDefine
    inp2 --> blockMeasure
    inp3 --> blockAnalyzeA
    inp4 --> blockAnalyzeA
    blockAnalyzeB --> out1
    blockAnalyzeC --> out2
    out1 --> blockAnalyzeD
    out2 --> blockImprove

    classDef phase fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef input fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef analyze fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class blockDefine,blockMeasure,blockImprove,blockControl phase
    class inp1,inp2,inp3,inp4 input
    class out1,out2 output
    class blockAnalyzeA,blockAnalyzeB,blockAnalyzeC,blockAnalyzeD analyze
```

## 4.3 Logical Decision Flow for Selecting Ishikawa Categories

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["Identify the Problem Domain"]
    q1{"Is it a manufacturing process?"}
    q2{"Is it a service or marketing issue?"}
    q3{"Is it a software or IT process?"}

    node6M["Use 6Ms Framework"]
    node8P["Use 8Ps Framework"]
    node4S["Use 4Ss Framework"]

    classDef question fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef framework fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef startnode fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class startNode startnode
    class q1,q2,q3 question
    class node6M,node8P,node4S framework
```

## 4.4 Advantages and Limitations Summary Matrix

| **Advantages** | **Limitations** |
|----------------|-----------------|
| Visually intuitive for cross-functional teams | Can become cluttered with too many causes |
| Forces structured, systematic thinking | Does not show interaction between causes |
| Excellent brainstorming facilitation tool | Does not indicate cause importance quantitatively |
| Easy to construct; no statistical expertise required | Risk of duplicate cause entries |
| Applicable across industries and domains | Subject to facilitator/team bias |
| Integrates well with 5 Whys and Pareto | Does not directly identify root cause (requires 5 Whys) |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Define the Ishikawa diagram and list the six Ms used in its construction for manufacturing processes.**

#### Model Answer:
The Ishikawa diagram, also known as the **Fishbone Diagram** or **Cause-and-Effect Diagram**, is a structured qualitative tool developed by **Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa in 1968** to identify, organize, and analyze the potential root causes of a problem (the effect). It is used in the **Analyze phase of DMAIC**.

The **6Ms** used in manufacturing are:
1. **Man** – People, training, skills.
2. **Machine** – Equipment, tools, machinery.
3. **Material** – Raw materials, inputs, consumables.
4. **Method** – Procedures, processes, workflows.
5. **Measurement** – Gauges, data systems, inspection.
6. **Mother Nature** – Environmental conditions like temperature and humidity.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the 6 Ms listing)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
**Differentiate between the 6Ms and 8Ps frameworks of the Ishikawa diagram. When is each applied?**

#### Model Answer:

| Basis | **6Ms Framework** | **8Ps Framework** |
|-------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Domain | Manufacturing / production | Service industry / marketing |
| Categories | Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature | Price, Promotion, People, Process, Place, Policies, Product, Procedure |
| Focus | Physical inputs and process variables | Customer-facing and strategic variables |
| Application | Defects, rejections, downtime | Customer complaints, churn, service failures |

The **6Ms** is applied when the problem originates from a **production or operational** process, while the **8Ps** is applied for problems in **service delivery, marketing, or sales contexts**.

*(3 marks: 2 for the table/differentiation, 1 for the application context)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each module carries one Part B question of **14 marks** with **internal choice** (Q.A or Q.B). Sub-parts typically carry **7 + 7 marks**. Both choices are provided below for parallel practice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Module 1 Choice Option 1]

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1, Set A]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the construction methodology of the Ishikawa diagram with a suitable manufacturing example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** How does the Ishikawa diagram integrate with the **5 Whys** and **Pareto analysis** in the Analyze phase of a Six Sigma DMAIC project? Illustrate with a hypothetical case. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Construction Methodology (7 marks):**

The Ishikawa diagram is constructed in the following structured steps:

1. **Define the Effect:** Write a clear, measurable problem statement at the "head" of the fish. *Example: "Surface roughness of CNC-machined bracket exceeds 1.6 μm Ra specification in 18% of batch B-220."*

2. **Draw the Spine:** Draw a horizontal arrow pointing right toward the effect.

3. **Identify Major Categories:** Apply the **6Ms** — Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature — and draw diagonal branches off the spine.

4. **Brainstorm Causes:** Conduct a **cross-functional team brainstorming session** (4–8 members from production, quality, maintenance, design) listing potential causes under each category. *Example:*
   - *Man:* Operator not trained on new tool path.
   - *Machine:* Spindle runout above tolerance.
   - *Material:* Aluminum 6061 batch hardness variation.

5. **Attach Sub-causes:** Use the **5 Whys** technique to drill down each main cause.

6. **Mark Vital Few:** Use **Pareto** to identify the 2–3 dominant causes contributing ~80% of the problem.

7. **Document and Validate:** Confirm vital few via data (regression, hypothesis testing) before proceeding to the Improve phase.

**(b) Integration with 5 Whys and Pareto (7 marks):**

The Ishikawa diagram is rarely used in isolation; it is the **central hub** in the Analyze-phase toolset.

- **5 Whys Integration:** Once a main cause (e.g., "machine spindle runout") is plotted on the Ishikawa rib, the team asks iteratively *Why?* to trace it down to a root cause (e.g., *Why runout? → Bearing worn → Why worn? → No preventive maintenance schedule*). This 5-Whys chain is then drawn as a sub-branch of the Ishikawa rib.

- **Pareto Integration:** After all causes are plotted, the team estimates or measures the frequency/contribution of each cause. The causes are then ranked on a **Pareto chart** (descending bars with cumulative %). The top 2–3 causes (the "vital few") — typically accounting for **70–80%** of the defect — are flagged on the Ishikawa diagram as priority targets.

- **Hypothetical Case:** In a hospital ICU, "Hospital-Acquired Infection rate rose from 4% to 9%." The Ishikawa identifies causes under 6Ms (e.g., *Method: Hand hygiene protocol not followed*). The 5 Whys drills down: *Why not followed? → Staff unaware of new WHO protocol → Training gap*. The Pareto chart shows that **hand hygiene + catheter care** together account for 78% of infections, validating the vital few for the Improve phase.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Module 1 Choice Option 2]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Set B]**
**(a)** Discuss the **strengths and limitations** of the Ishikawa diagram as a root-cause analysis tool. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a detailed Ishikawa diagram (using 6Ms) for the following problem: *"Customer complaints about delayed order delivery have increased by 35% in the e-commerce warehouse over the last quarter."* Identify the vital few causes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Strengths and Limitations (7 marks):**

**Strengths:**
1. **Visual clarity:** Enables all team members, regardless of technical expertise, to understand the cause structure.
2. **Structured brainstorming:** Prevents random discussion by forcing systematic category-wise analysis.
3. **Versatility:** Applicable in manufacturing, healthcare, IT, service, and administrative processes.
4. **Foundation for deeper analysis:** Acts as a launchpad for **5 Whys**, **FMEA**, and **Pareto**.
5. **Cross-functional collaboration:** Promotes dialogue between departments.
6. **Simple to construct:** Requires no statistical tools.

**Limitations:**
1. **No quantification:** Does not show the relative magnitude or interaction of causes.
2. **Clutter risk:** With 50+ causes, the diagram becomes unreadable.
3. **Bias-prone:** Output depends on team composition and brainstorming quality.
4. **Duplicate causes:** Same root cause may appear under multiple categories.
5. **Does not identify the root cause by itself** — needs supplementary tools (5 Whys).
6. **Static:** Does not capture time-dependent or dynamic interactions of variables.

**(b) Ishikawa Diagram for Delayed E-commerce Delivery (7 marks):**

**Effect:** Customer complaints on delayed delivery increased by **35%** in e-commerce warehouse.

**6Ms Analysis:**

| Category | Identified Causes |
|----------|-------------------|
| **Man** | Warehouse staff shortage during festive season; inadequate picker training on WMS software. |
| **Machine** | Conveyor belt breakdown twice/week; WMS server downtime during peak hours. |
| **Material** | Inconsistent packaging material quality causing rework; label printer ribbon shortage. |
| **Method** | Order routing algorithm outdated; no wave-planning optimization; single-shift picking strategy. |
| **Measurement** | Real-time delivery KPI dashboard not updated; carrier performance not tracked daily. |
| **Mother Nature** | Monsoon-related regional transport delays; power outages affecting WMS. |

**Vital Few (Pareto Analysis):**
1. **WMS server downtime** (~30% contribution).
2. **Inadequate picker training** (~25% contribution).
3. **Outdated order routing algorithm** (~20% contribution).

**Combined Vital Few Contribution ≈ 75%**, validating Pareto's 80/20 principle.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Ishikawa Questions — KTU 2024 Pattern:**
>
> 1. **Mistake:** Listing only **4Ms** instead of 6Ms. → **Loss: 1 mark.** Always write the full 6Ms unless the question specifies otherwise.
> 2. **Mistake:** Confusing **Ishikawa** with **Pareto** or **5 Whys** as standalone tools. → **Loss: 1–2 marks.** Remember: Ishikawa is a **qualitative categorization** tool; 5 Whys drills down; Pareto prioritizes.
> 3. **Mistake:** Not mentioning the **inventor (Kaoru Ishikawa)** or the **year (1968)**. → **Loss: 0.5–1 mark** in definition questions.
> 4. **Mistake:** Drawing the diagram without categorizing causes properly into the 6Ms. → **Loss: 2 marks.** Examiners expect **structured branching**, not a random list.
> 5. **Mistake:** Skipping the **DMAIC Analyze phase** reference. → **Loss: 1 mark.** Always anchor Ishikawa in the DMAIC framework.
> 6. **Mistake:** Confusing the **6Ms** with the **8Ps** or **4Ss**. → **Loss: 1 mark.** Specify the domain context clearly.
> 7. **Mistake:** Not identifying the **vital few** when asked. → **Loss: 2 marks** in 14-mark case-study questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ishikawa Diagram**

- ✅ **Inventor:** Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa (1968, Kawasaki shipyards, Japan).
- ✅ **Alternative Names:** Fishbone, Cause-and-Effect, Herringbone diagram.
- ✅ **Classification:** One of the **7 Basic QC Tools** (registered by JUSE).
- ✅ **DMAIC Phase:** Primarily used in the **Analyze** phase.
- ✅ **Standard Framework (Manufacturing):** **6Ms** — Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature.
- ✅ **Service Framework:** **8Ps** — Price, Promotion, People, Process, Place, Policies, Product, Procedure.
- ✅ **Software/IT Framework:** **4Ss** — Surroundings, Suppliers, Systems, Skills.
- ✅ **Companion Tools:** **5 Whys** (drill down), **Pareto** (prioritize), **FMEA** (risk quantification).
- ✅ **Construction Sequence:** Define Effect → Draw Spine → Add 6Ms Ribs → Brainstorm Causes → 5-Whys Drill-Down → Pareto Rank → Identify Vital Few.
- ✅ **Strengths:** Visual, structured, versatile, simple, promotes team collaboration.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Qualitative only, clutter-prone, bias-prone, no cause-interaction shown, doesn't pinpoint root cause alone.
- ✅ **Best Practice:** Limit to **20–30 causes max**; always validate vital few with **statistical data** before Improve phase.
- ✅ **Mnemonic to remember 6Ms:** **"Many Machines Make Messes, Measuring Mother Nature"** → Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature.

---

> **End of Module 1 — Ishikawa Notes | PEMRT752 — SIX SIGMA | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Taguchi - Their Main Contributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Taguchi - Their Main Contributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Who is Genichi Taguchi?

**Genichi Taguchi** (1924–2012) was a Japanese engineer and statistician often regarded as the father of modern **Robust Design** and **Quality Engineering**. His philosophy transformed quality management from a reactive inspection activity into a proactive engineering discipline. KTU 2024 Scheme specifically recognizes Taguchi as the pioneer who redefined quality not as conformance to specifications, but as **minimum loss to society**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMRT752 Module 1):**
> Taguchi's main contributions center on (1) the **Quality Loss Function (QLF)**, (2) the **Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio**, (3) **Orthogonal Arrays (OA)**, and (4) the **Three-Stage Design Philosophy** (System, Parameter, Tolerance Design).

## 1.2 Taguchi's Main Contributions — The Big Picture

Taguchi contributed **five revolutionary ideas** to the world of Six Sigma and quality engineering. These are not isolated techniques; they form a **unified philosophy** for designing products and processes that are inherently robust to real-world variation.

| # | Contribution | One-Line Meaning |
|---|--------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Quality Loss Function (QLF)** | Quantifies the financial loss a customer experiences from any deviation from the target. |
| 2 | **Robust Design** | Engineering design that performs consistently under varied noise conditions. |
| 3 | **Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio** | A single metric measuring performance stability across noise factors. |
| 4 | **Orthogonal Arrays (OA)** | A fractional experimental design technique to study many factors economically. |
