| 5 | **Three-Stage Design (System → Parameter → Tolerance)** | A hierarchical design strategy that minimizes cost and maximizes quality. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Biryani Pot" Story 🍲

Imagine a chef in a Kerala hotel preparing biryani in a large pot:

- **The Target (m)**: The ideal taste the chef wants every customer to experience.
- **Variation (Δy)**: Some days the salt is slightly more, some days the masala is less.
- **Traditional Thinking**: "As long as the salt is within ±2 g of recipe, it's fine."
- **Taguchi's Thinking**: "Even 1 g of extra salt causes a *small but real* loss of customer satisfaction — and a 5 g deviation causes a *much larger* loss."

The chef's job (per Taguchi) is not just to stay within limits, but to **design the recipe so that small variations in ingredients or cooking time barely change the taste**. This is *Robust Design*. The chef measures how stable the taste is using a single number: the **S/N ratio**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook for Exam:** Taguchi = "Loss Function + Robust Design + S/N Ratio + Orthogonal Arrays." Master these four pillars, and Module 1 questions become straightforward.

## 1.4 Why KTU Includes Taguchi in Six Sigma

Six Sigma aims at **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. Taguchi's methods help achieve this *by design* rather than *by inspection*, which is the very heart of the **DMAIC/DMADV** philosophy. KTU examiners frequently frame Part A questions on Taguchi's loss function because it is mathematically elegant and easy to test.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Contribution #1 — The Quality Loss Function (QLF)

### 2.1.1 Traditional vs. Taguchi View of Quality

| Aspect | Traditional View | Taguchi's View |
|--------|------------------|----------------|
| Goal | Stay within tolerance | Minimize deviation from target |
| Loss function | Step function (binary) | Smooth quadratic curve |
| Cost at boundary | Zero (still "in spec") | Already incurring loss |
| Customer focus | Specification compliance | Loss to society |

> [!NOTE]
> **Binary Tolerance Thinking (Step Function):**
> $L(y) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } m - \Delta_0 \leq y \leq m + \Delta_0 \\ A_0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taguchi's Continuous Loss Function (Quadratic):**
> $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$
> where $k$ is the **quality loss coefficient**.

### 2.1.2 The Quadratic Loss Function — Geometric Meaning

The QLF is a **parabola** opening upward, with its minimum (zero loss) at the target value $m$. Any deviation $y - m$ produces a loss that grows quadratically.

- At $y = m$: $L = 0$ (perfect product, no loss).
- At $y = m \pm \Delta_0$: $L = A_0$ (functional limit loss).
- Beyond $\Delta_0$: Product is non-functional; loss rises steeply.

## 2.2 Contribution #2 — Robust Design Philosophy

Taguchi introduced the concept of designing products and processes that are **insensitive to noise factors** (also called uncontrollable factors). Examples of noise factors:

- Temperature variation
- Humidity
- Raw material batch differences
- Operator skill variation
- Machine wear over time

**Robust Design** splits factors into three categories:

1. **Control Factors**: Variables the engineer can set (e.g., material type, dimensions).
2. **Noise Factors**: Uncontrollable variations (e.g., ambient temperature).
3. **Signal Factors**: Input levels that customers control (e.g., knob position).

The design goal: choose control factor levels so that performance is **maximally stable** across all noise conditions.

## 2.3 Contribution #3 — Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio

The S/N ratio is a single statistic that captures both the **mean performance** and the **variability**. Taguchi defined it differently for three quality characteristics:

| Quality Type | Target | S/N Formula | Application |
|--------------|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Smaller-is-better** | Minimize | $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$ | Defects, impurities, wear |
| **Larger-is-better** | Maximize | $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum \dfrac{1}{y_i^2}\right)$ | Strength, life, yield |
| **Nominal-is-best** | Hit target exactly | $\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\bar{y}^2}{s^2}\right)$ or $10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\mu^2}{\sigma^2}\right)$ | Dimensions, voltage, pressure |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "Nominal-is-best" with "Target-is-best." They are identical concepts — both refer to a desired middle value $m$. KTU examiners will not deduct marks for using either term.

## 2.4 Contribution #4 — Orthogonal Arrays (OA)

Orthogonal arrays are **predefined experimental layouts** that let engineers study many factors with few runs. The most common arrays used in KTU coursework:

| Array | Factors (max) | Runs (n) | Use Case |
|-------|---------------|----------|----------|
| $L_4$ | 3 (2-level) | 4 | Small screening studies |
| $L_8$ | 7 (2-level) | 8 | Standard 2-level screening |
| $L_9$ | 4 (3-level) | 9 | 3-level factor studies |
| $L_{16}$ | 15 (2-level) | 16 | Large screening studies |
| $L_{27}$ | 13 (3-level) | 27 | Complex 3-level studies |

Notation explained: $L_{n}(2^k)$ means **n runs** studying up to **k factors at 2 levels** each.

## 2.5 Contribution #5 — Three-Stage Design Hierarchy

Taguchi structured the entire design process into three sequential stages:

1. **System Design**: Conceptual/functional design by engineers (analogous to R&D initial concept).
2. **Parameter Design**: Identifying optimal control factor levels — this is where Taguchi's S/N ratios and OA are heavily used.
3. **Tolerance Design**: Tightening tolerances only where necessary, to reduce manufacturing cost.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When a KTU question asks "What is the most cost-effective stage?" — the answer is **Parameter Design**, because it achieves robustness *without tightening tolerances* (which would increase production cost).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Boundary/Unit |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$ | Loss at quality value $y$ | Currency (₹/$/¥) |
| 2 | $k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$ | Quality loss coefficient | Currency per unit² |
| 3 | $A_0$ | Loss at functional limit | Currency |
| 4 | $\Delta_0$ | Tolerance from target | Same unit as $y$ |
| 5 | $\eta_{SB} = -10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$ | S/N, smaller-is-better | Decibels (dB) |
| 6 | $\eta_{LB} = -10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum \dfrac{1}{y_i^2}\right)$ | S/N, larger-is-better | dB |
| 7 | $\eta_{NB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\bar{y}^2}{s^2}\right)$ | S/N, nominal-is-best | dB |
| 8 | $\eta_{NB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\mu^2}{\sigma^2}\right)$ | S/N nominal (population) | dB |
| 9 | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$ | Sample mean | Same as $y$ |
| 10 | $s^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | Sample variance | $y^2$ units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas power **Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)** projects in industries ranging from semiconductor fabrication (Intel, TSMC) to automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors). The QLF specifically is used in **warranty cost modeling** and **supplier quality agreements**.

## 2.7 Why Taguchi Matters in Engineering

| Industry | Taguchi Application |
|----------|---------------------|
| Automotive | Designing engines robust to fuel-quality variation |
| Electronics | Reducing signal distortion across temperature ranges |
| Manufacturing | Lowering defect rates without expensive tooling |
| Healthcare | Drug formulation robust to patient metabolism variation |
| Software | Reducing bug sensitivity to operating environment |

The unifying theme: **engineer the design once, so the product works in many conditions without rework**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Quality Loss Coefficient $k$ from Specification Boundaries

### Problem Statement

A bolt's diameter target is $m = 10$ mm. The functional tolerance limit is $\Delta_0 = 0.5$ mm. If a bolt at the functional limit causes a warranty claim of $A_0 = ₹400$ per bolt, find the loss function and the loss for a bolt actually produced at $y = 10.3$ mm.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$m = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta_0 = 0.5 \text{ mm}, \quad A_0 = ₹400$$

**Step 2 — Compute the quality loss coefficient $k$.**

By definition, when $|y - m| = \Delta_0$, the loss equals $A_0$. Substituting into $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= k \cdot \Delta_0^2 \\
k &= \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} \\
k &= \frac{400}{(0.5)^2} \\
k &= \frac{400}{0.25} \\
k &= ₹1600 \text{ per mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the explicit loss function for this product.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(y) &= 1600 \cdot (y - 10)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the loss for a bolt at $y = 10.3$ mm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(10.3) &= 1600 \cdot (10.3 - 10)^2 \\
L(10.3) &= 1600 \cdot (0.3)^2 \\
L(10.3) &= 1600 \cdot 0.09 \\
L(10.3) &= ₹144
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Although 10.3 mm is *within* the traditional tolerance (10 ± 0.5), the customer is already experiencing a **₹144 loss per bolt**. This is the central insight of Taguchi's QLF.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: S/N Ratio for "Smaller-is-Better" Characteristic

### Problem Statement

Five measurements of impurity content (in ppm) in a chemical batch are: $y_1 = 3, y_2 = 5, y_4 = 4, y_5 = 2, y_5 = 6$. Compute the S/N ratio using the smaller-is-better formula.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify $n$ and tabulate $y_i^2$.**

| $i$ | $y_i$ (ppm) | $y_i^2$ |
|-----|-------------|---------|
| 1 | 3 | 9 |
| 2 | 5 | 25 |
| 3 | 4 | 16 |
| 4 | 2 | 4 |
| 5 | 6 | 36 |

$n = 5$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean of squares (MS).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MS} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2 \\
&= \frac{9 + 25 + 16 + 4 + 36}{5} \\
&= \frac{90}{5} \\
&= 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the S/N formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{SB} &= -10 \log_{10}(\text{MS}) \\
&= -10 \log_{10}(18) \\
&= -10 \times 1.2553 \\
&= -12.553 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A more negative dB value indicates worse performance. If a competitor's process yields $-18$ dB, our process is **better by ~5.45 dB**, which is a substantial quality improvement.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: S/N Ratio for "Nominal-is-Best" Characteristic

### Problem Statement

Six resistors have target value $R = 100\ \Omega$. Measured values (in $\Omega$): 98, 102, 99, 101, 100, 100. Compute the S/N ratio.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the mean $\bar{y}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{y} &= \frac{98 + 102 + 99 + 101 + 100 + 100}{6} \\
&= \frac{600}{6} \\
&= 100\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sample variance $s^2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s^2 &= \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2 \\
&= \frac{(98-100)^2 + (102-100)^2 + (99-100)^2 + (101-100)^2 + (100-100)^2 + (100-100)^2}{6-1} \\
&= \frac{4 + 4 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 0}{5} \\
&= \frac{10}{5} \\
&= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the nominal-is-best S/N formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{NB} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\bar{y}^2}{s^2}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{100^2}{2}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}(5000) \\
&= 10 \times 3.6990 \\
&= 36.99 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** A high $\eta_{NB}$ means the process is both **accurate** (mean near target) and **precise** (low variance). KTU examiners love this dual interpretation.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: S/N Ratio for "Larger-is-Better" Characteristic

### Problem Statement

Tensile strength measurements (MPa) for 4 samples: 510, 525, 500, 515. Compute the S/N ratio.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Tabulate $1/y_i^2$.**

| $i$ | $y_i$ | $1/y_i^2$ |
|-----|-------|-----------|
| 1 | 510 | $1/260100 \approx 3.8447 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 2 | 525 | $1/275625 \approx 3.6281 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 3 | 500 | $1/250000 = 4.0000 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| 4 | 515 | $1/265225 \approx 3.7704 \times 10^{-6}$ |

**Step 2 — Compute the mean of $1/y_i^2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MS}_{inv} &= \frac{3.8447 + 3.6281 + 4.0000 + 3.7704}{4} \times 10^{-6} \\
&= \frac{15.2432}{4} \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 3.8108 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{LB} &= -10 \log_{10}(\text{MS}_{inv}) \\
&= -10 \log_{10}(3.8108 \times 10^{-6}) \\
&= -10 \times (-5.4190) \\
&= 54.19 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For larger-is-better, the S/N is positive (good).** A higher $\eta_{LB}$ means the product consistently performs at higher strength.

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Quality Type | Mean Treatment | Variance Treatment | Higher $\eta$ is Better? | Typical Industry Example |
|--------------|----------------|--------------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Smaller-is-better | $\bar{y} \to 0$ | $s^2 \to 0$ | Yes (less negative) | Surface roughness, defects |
| Larger-is-better | $\bar{y} \to \infty$ | $s^2 \to 0$ | Yes (more positive) | Battery life, bond strength |
| Nominal-is-best | $\bar{y} \to m$ | $s^2 \to 0$ | Yes (more positive) | Shaft diameter, voltage |

## 3.6 Worked Example: Complete QLF + S/N Problem

A shaft diameter target is $m = 50$ mm. The functional tolerance is $\Delta_0 = 1$ mm, and the loss at the boundary is $A_0 = ₹1000$.

**(a) Find the loss function.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} = \frac{1000}{1^2} = 1000 \text{ ₹/mm}^2 \\
L(y) &= 1000 \cdot (y - 50)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Loss for $y = 50.4$ mm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(50.4) &= 1000 \cdot (0.4)^2 = 1000 \cdot 0.16 = ₹160
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Suppose a competing process achieves $s^2 = 0.1\ \text{mm}^2$ with $\bar{y} = 50$ mm. Compute the S/N ratio (nominal-is-best).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{NB} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{50^2}{0.1}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(25000) = 10 \times 4.3979 = 43.98 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Interpret:** The process is centered on target ($\bar{y} = 50$) and has very low variance ($s^2 = 0.1$). The S/N of 43.98 dB indicates excellent quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total QLF Loss Across Production Batch:** If a batch of 1000 units is produced with mean $\bar{y} = 50.05$ mm and $s^2 = 0.05\ \text{mm}^2$, the **expected loss per unit** is $E[L] = k \cdot (\sigma^2 + \mu^2 - 2m\mu + m^2) = k \cdot [(\bar{y}-m)^2 + s^2]$. Total batch loss: $1000 \times 1000 \times [0.0025 + 0.05] = ₹52,500$. This is the **manufacturing cost of deviation** — and it is *invisible* under traditional tolerance-based thinking.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taguchi's Quality Loss Function — Geometric Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Design Stage] --> B{Target Value m}
    B --> C[Set Loss Function L = k y minus m squared]
    C --> D{Compute k from A0 and Delta0}
    D --> E[Identify Quality Characteristic Type]
    E --> F1[Smaller is Better]
    E --> F2[Larger is Better]
    E --> F3[Nominal is Best]
    F1 --> G1[Use S/N minus 10 log mean of y squared]
    F2 --> G2[Use S/N minus 10 log mean of 1 over y squared]
    F3 --> G3[Use S/N 10 log mean squared over variance]
    G1 --> H[Run Orthogonal Array Experiment]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Identify Optimal Control Factor Levels]
    I --> J[Confirm Robust Performance]
    J --> K[Finalize Parameter Design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Start at the top with the product design stage, identify the quality type (smaller/larger/nominal), select the appropriate S/N formula, run the orthogonal array, and pick the best parameter combination.

## 4.2 Taguchi's Three-Stage Design Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[System Design] --> S2[Parameter Design]
    S2 --> S3[Tolerance Design]
    S1 -.low cost.-> S1
    S2 -.medium cost.-> S2
    S3 -.high cost.-> S3
    S2 --> OA[Orthogonal Array Experiments]
    OA --> SN[S/N Ratio Analysis]
    SN --> OPT[Optimal Level Selection]
    OPT --> VER[Verification Run]
    VER -->|Pass| DONE[Process Locked]
    VER -->|Fail| S2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost Increases Left-to-Right:** System Design is cheapest, Tolerance Design is most expensive. Taguchi's genius was pushing engineers to achieve quality at the **Parameter Design** stage so they rarely need to spend money on **Tolerance Design**.

## 4.3 S/N Ratio Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Quality Characteristic] --> Q1{Is the Target a Minimum Value?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| SB[Smaller is Better]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is the Target a Maximum Value?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| LB[Larger is Better]
    Q2 -->|No| NB[Nominal is Best]
    SB --> SBF[Apply eta equals minus 10 log mean of y squared]
    LB --> LBF[Apply eta equals minus 10 log mean of 1 over y squared]
    NB --> NBF[Apply eta equals 10 log mean squared over variance]
```

## 4.4 Orthogonal Array Conceptual Layout (L8)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EXP[Experiment Plan L8] --> C1[Column 1 Factor A]
    EXP --> C2[Column 2 Factor B]
    EXP --> C3[Column 3 Factor C]
    EXP --> C4[Column 4 Factor D]
    EXP --> C5[Column 5 Factor E]
    EXP --> C6[Column 6 Interaction AB]
    EXP --> C7[Column 7 Interaction AC]
    C1 --> R1[Run 1 to 8]
    C2 --> R1
    C3 --> R1
    C4 --> R1
    C5 --> R1
    C6 --> R1
    C7 --> R1
    R1 --> OUT[Compute S/N for each run]
    OUT --> ANA[ANOVA on S/N values]
    ANA --> OPT[Choose best level per factor]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight from the L8 Layout:** Only **8 runs** are needed to study **up to 7 factors at 2 levels each**. A full factorial would require $2^7 = 128$ runs. This is the power of orthogonal arrays — **94% reduction in experimental effort**.

## 4.5 Contribution-to-Industry Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    QLF[Quality Loss Function] --> WC[Warranty Cost Modeling]
    QLF --> SUP[Supplier Quality Contracts]
    RD[Robust Design] --> PROD[Product Engineering]
    RD --> PROC[Process Engineering]
    SN[Signal to Noise Ratio] --> OPT[Parameter Optimization]
    SN --> MET[Single Performance Metric]
    OA[Orthogonal Arrays] --> DOE[Design of Experiments]
    OA --> SCR[Factor Screening]
    TSD[Three Stage Design] --> STD[Standardized Workflow]
    TSD --> QC[Cost Effective Quality]
```

This block diagram illustrates how each of Taguchi's five main contributions maps to a specific engineering activity, making it clear to KTU examiners that the contributions are **interconnected**, not isolated.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Taguchi's Quality Loss Function. Explain its significance over the traditional step-function loss model. [3 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 1]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Quality Loss Function (QLF)** proposed by Genichi Taguchi expresses the loss incurred by society as a continuous, quadratic function of the deviation of a quality characteristic from its target value. Mathematically, it is given by:

$$
L(y) = k(y - m)^2
$$

where $L(y)$ is the loss at quality value $y$, $m$ is the target value, and $k$ is the quality loss coefficient computed as $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^2$.

**Significance over the traditional step-function model:**

1. The traditional view treats loss as zero inside the tolerance band and a fixed cost $A_0$ outside it — a discontinuous step function.
2. Taguchi's QLF recognizes that **loss starts accumulating the moment a product deviates from the target**, even when the deviation is well within specification limits.
3. This drives engineers to **center the process on the target** and **reduce variance simultaneously**, rather than relying solely on specification enforcement.
4. The QLF also provides a **monetary value** to quality deviations, enabling cost-of-quality calculations across the supply chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the formula, and 1 mark for the significance comparison. Always draw the parabolic curve and the step function side-by-side for full credit.

---

### Question 2: List and briefly explain any three main contributions of Genichi Taguchi to quality engineering. [3 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 1]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Quality Loss Function (QLF):** A continuous quadratic function $L(y) = k(y-m)^2$ that quantifies the loss to society from any deviation of a quality characteristic from its target value. It replaces the conventional "in-spec / out-of-spec" binary thinking.

2. **Robust Design:** A design philosophy that makes products and processes **insensitive to noise factors** (uncontrollable sources of variation such as temperature, humidity, or material lot differences). Robust design is achieved by optimally choosing control factor levels rather than tightening tolerances.

3. **Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio:** A performance metric that combines the mean and variance of a quality characteristic into a single decibel value. Higher S/N ratios indicate better quality. There are three types: smaller-is-better, larger-is-better, and nominal-is-best.

> [!TIP]
> **Acceptable Alternatives:** Students may also list **Orthogonal Arrays** or **Three-Stage Design (System → Parameter → Tolerance)** as their three contributions. KTU examiners will award full credit for any correct combination from Taguchi's recognized body of work.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain in detail the Quality Loss Function with a suitable diagram. A shaft has a target diameter of 25 mm. If the functional tolerance is ±0.5 mm and the loss at the functional limit is ₹800, determine: (i) the loss coefficient $k$, (ii) the loss function, and (iii) the loss for a shaft produced at 24.7 mm. [7 Marks]

#### (b) Discuss Taguchi's Three-Stage Design approach. Why is Parameter Design considered the most cost-effective stage? [7 Marks]

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Quality Loss Function Derivation

**Diagram Description (to be drawn in exam):**
- X-axis: Shaft diameter $y$ (mm), ranging from 24.0 to 26.0.
- Y-axis: Loss $L(y)$ in ₹.
- Plot the parabolic curve $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$ with vertex at $(25, 0)$.
- Mark the points $(\Delta_0 = \pm 0.5, A_0 = 800)$ on the curve.
- Compare with the traditional step function showing zero loss inside the band and ₹800 outside.

**Given data:**

$$m = 25 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta_0 = 0.5 \text{ mm}, \quad A_0 = ₹800$$

**(i) Loss coefficient $k$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} \\
&= \frac{800}{(0.5)^2} \\
&= \frac{800}{0.25} \\
&= ₹3200 \text{ per mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Loss function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(y) &= 3200 \cdot (y - 25)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Loss for $y = 24.7$ mm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(24.7) &= 3200 \cdot (24.7 - 25)^2 \\
&= 3200 \cdot (-0.3)^2 \\
&= 3200 \cdot 0.09 \\
&= ₹288
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note (1 mark for diagram, 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii)).**

---

#### Part (b) — Three-Stage Design Approach

Taguchi divided the entire product/process design activity into three sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — System Design:**
- This is the conceptual and functional design stage.
- Engineers decide the basic configuration, the operating principle, and the materials of the product.
- Example: Choosing a chain drive vs. a belt drive for a particular application.
- It is the **least expensive** stage but requires the most creativity.

**Stage 2 — Parameter Design:**
- Once the system is chosen, engineers identify the **control factors** (e.g., dimensions, operating voltages, material grades) and their levels.
- Taguchi's **Orthogonal Arrays** and **S/N ratios** are used to find the **optimal combination** of control factors that minimizes the effect of noise factors.
- This is where **robustness is achieved without added cost**, because tolerance levels are not tightened.

**Stage 3 — Tolerance Design:**
- This is the final fine-tuning stage.
- Tolerances are tightened **only on those factors** that significantly affect the S/N ratio after parameter design.
- It is the **most expensive** stage because tighter tolerances require better machines, more inspections, and higher reject rates.

**Why Parameter Design is the most cost-effective stage:**

1. It achieves quality improvement **without increasing manufacturing cost** — tolerances are not tightened, so production tooling and procedures remain unchanged.
2. It uses **few experiments** (via orthogonal arrays) to find optimal settings, minimizing design cost.
3. It produces a product that is **robust by design**, reducing warranty claims and field failures.
4. It pushes the engineer to be **creative with control factors** rather than relying on inspection-based control.

| Stage | Cost | Goal | Taguchi Tool |
|-------|------|------|--------------|
| System Design | Low | Create concept | Engineering creativity |
| Parameter Design | Medium | Achieve robustness | OA + S/N Ratio |
| Tolerance Design | High | Fine-tune specs | Statistical analysis |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Exam:** **S-P-T** = System → Parameter → Tolerance. "**P**arameter design is **P**owerful" — the most cost-effective stage.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) What is the Signal-to-Noise (S/N) ratio in Taguchi's methodology? Derive the S/N ratio for the "nominal-is-best" characteristic. The mean and standard deviation of 8 measurements of a resistor are 100.2 Ω and 0.5 Ω respectively. Compute the S/N ratio. [7 Marks]

#### (b) Explain the concept of Robust Design. Differentiate between control factors, noise factors, and signal factors with engineering examples. [7 Marks]

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — S/N Ratio Derivation for Nominal-is-Best

**Definition:**
The **Signal-to-Noise (S/N) ratio** is a single performance metric proposed by Taguchi that combines the mean and variability of a quality characteristic into one decibel value. It is a logarithmic measure of robustness, defined as:

$$
\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\text{Signal Power}}{\text{Noise Power}}\right)
$$

**Derivation for Nominal-is-Best:**

For the nominal-is-best case, the target is a specific value $m$. The output $y$ can be modeled as:

$$
y = m + \varepsilon
$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the random error (noise) with mean 0 and variance $\sigma^2$.

The **signal** is the mean value $\mu$ (squared to represent signal power: $\mu^2$).
The **noise** is the variance $\sigma^2$.

Therefore, the S/N ratio is:

$$
\eta_{NB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\mu^2}{\sigma^2}\right)
$$

Using sample estimates:

$$
\eta_{NB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\bar{y}^2}{s^2}\right)
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given $\bar{y} = 100.2\ \Omega$ and $s = 0.5\ \Omega$, hence $s^2 = 0.25\ \Omega^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{NB} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{(100.2)^2}{0.25}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{10040.04}{0.25}\right) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}(40160.16) \\
&= 10 \times 4.6038 \\
&= 46.04 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher S/N = Better Quality.** For nominal-is-best, both centering (mean → target) and variance reduction improve the S/N.

---

#### Part (b) — Robust Design and Factor Types

**Concept of Robust Design:**

Robust Design is Taguchi's engineering philosophy of designing products and processes such that their performance is **minimally affected by sources of variation** (noise). Instead of controlling or eliminating noise, the design is made **insensitive** to it. The output of a robust design process is a product that works consistently across varied environmental and operating conditions.

**Factor Types:**

| Factor Type | Definition | Engineer's Control | Example |
|-------------|------------|---------------------|---------|
| **Control Factor** | Variable the engineer can set in the design | Fully controllable | Bolt diameter, spring stiffness, resistor value |
| **Noise Factor** | Uncontrollable source of variation in real-world use | Cannot control | Ambient temperature, humidity, raw material batch |
| **Signal Factor** | Input level that the customer intentionally varies | Set by user | Volume knob position, gear shift level, accelerator pedal position |

**Engineering Examples:**

1. **Automotive Engine (Control: Compression Ratio, Noise: Fuel Quality, Signal: Accelerator Pedal):** A robust engine delivers smooth power across varying fuel grades without redesign.
2. **Mobile Phone (Control: Circuit Component Values, Noise: Drop Impact, Signal: Touch Intensity):** A robust touchscreen works whether the user has dry or wet fingers.
3. **Solar Panel (Control: Cell Material, Noise: Cloud Cover, Signal: Sunlight Angle):** A robust panel produces near-rated output even under intermittent shading.

**Why Robust Design Matters:**

- Reduces warranty costs.
- Improves customer satisfaction.
- Lowers inspection costs.
- Enables use of cheaper components while maintaining quality.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse *signal factors* with *control factors*. Remember — **signal factors are set by the customer**, control factors are set by the **engineer**. Mixing them up will cost you at least 1 mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Unit Errors in QLF:** Forgetting to square $\Delta_0$ in the denominator of $k = A_0/\Delta_0^2$ — losing 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Sign Confusion in S/N Ratios:** Writing the larger-is-better formula without the negative sign. Always include $-10$ at the front.
> 3. **Sample vs. Population Variance:** Using $n$ instead of $n-1$ in the denominator of $s^2$. KTU expects the **unbiased estimator** ($n-1$).
> 4. **Mixing Up the Three S/N Types:** Always verify the quality type from the problem statement before selecting the formula.
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram in QLF Questions:** A question worth 7 marks *without* the loss function curve is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Taguchi's Main Contributions:**

- ✅ **Taguchi's Big Idea:** Quality = minimum loss to society, achieved *by design* not *by inspection*.
- ✅ **QLF Formula:** $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$ — always **continuous**, always **quadratic**, never a step function.
- ✅ **Loss Coefficient:** $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^2$ — derived from the boundary condition.
- ✅ **Three S/N Ratio Types:**
   - **Smaller-is-better:** $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$ — for defects, impurities.
   - **Larger-is-better:** $\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum \frac{1}{y_i^2}\right)$ — for strength, life.
   - **Nominal-is-best:** $\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\bar{y}^2}{s^2}\right)$ — for dimensions, voltage.
- ✅ **Higher S/N = Better Quality** in all three cases.
- ✅ **Three-Stage Design:** System Design → Parameter Design → Tolerance Design. Parameter Design is the **cheapest** way to achieve robustness.
- ✅ **Orthogonal Arrays** reduce experiments dramatically (e.g., $L_8$ needs only 8 runs for 7 factors).
- ✅ **Robust Design Goal:** Minimize the effect of *noise factors* on the *signal* response.
- ✅ **Factor Vocabulary:** Control (engineer sets) | Noise (uncontrollable) | Signal (customer sets).
- ✅ **Units Matter:** $k$ has units of currency per unit² of $y$ (e.g., ₹/mm²).
- ✅ **Decibel Unit:** All S/N ratios are expressed in **dB**.
- ✅ **Real-World Taguchi Industries:** Toyota, Ford, Boeing, Intel, Samsung, Tata Motors, and major pharmaceutical firms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Obstacles to TQM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Obstacles to TQM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Obstacles to TQM (Total Quality Management)** are the strategic, cultural, organizational, and operational barriers that prevent the successful planning, deployment, and sustenance of a Total Quality Management philosophy within an organization. According to KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature aligned with standard quality literature (e.g., frameworks discussed by Juran, Deming, and Ishikawa), these obstacles manifest as **resistance vectors** that deflect the organization away from continuous improvement (Kaizen) and customer-centric excellence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1 of PEMRT752, students must be able to **identify, classify, and propose countermeasures** for the major obstacles to TQM implementation. This forms a **2-mark short answer** or **sub-part of a 7-mark question** consistently across university examinations.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Imagine you decide to transform your home into a smart, energy-efficient household. You buy solar panels, smart meters, and new appliances — but then you encounter "obstacles":
- Your **family refuses to change** their habits (Resistance to Change)
- You **didn't budget** properly (Lack of Resources)
- You **install the panels but never check the inverter** (Lack of Sustained Focus)
- Your **neighbors laugh** at the new setup (Cultural Barriers)

Similarly, in organizations, TQM is a beautiful philosophy on paper, but its implementation often **collapses under the weight of human, strategic, and structural obstacles**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:** An obstacle to TQM is any **systemic impedance** — be it human, financial, procedural, or cultural — that limits an organization's ability to achieve **total customer satisfaction** through **continuous improvement** and **employee involvement**.

### Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU-Aligned)
- **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)** — measured in monetary units (currency)
- **Employee Involvement Index** — measured as **% participation**
- **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)** — measured on a **1–5** or **1–10 scale**
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — measured as a **percentage (%)**
- **Kaizen** — Japanese term for continuous improvement (a TQM pillar)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Classification of TQM Obstacles
The KTU 2024 syllabus organizes TQM obstacles into **five primary categories**. Each category contains specific sub-obstacles that examiners frequently test.

#### Category A: Strategic & Leadership Obstacles
1. **Lack of Top Management Commitment** — Leaders do not walk the talk.
2. **Inadequate Strategic Planning** — TQM is launched without a roadmap.
3. **Short-Term Financial Focus** — Emphasis on quarterly profits over long-term quality gains.
4. **Misalignment with Business Goals** — TQM treated as a standalone program.

#### Category B: Cultural & Human Obstacles
1. **Employee Resistance to Change** — Fear of the unknown, job loss anxiety.
2. **Lack of Empowerment** — Frontline workers cannot make decisions.
3. **Insufficient Training & Education** — Workers lack statistical/process tools.
4. **Poor Internal Communication** — Vision does not percolate down the hierarchy.

#### Category C: Structural & Operational Obstacles
1. **Over-Reliance on Quality Control Department** — Quality treated as one department's job, not everyone's.
2. **Inadequate Process Documentation** — Lack of Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).
3. **Vendor/Supplier Resistance** — Supply chain not aligned with TQM.

#### Category D: Measurement & Data Obstacles
1. **Lack of Performance Metrics** — No KPIs to track progress.
2. **Focusing on Symptoms, Not Root Causes** — Treating problems superficially.
3. **Insufficient Use of Statistical Tools** — SPC, Pareto, Ishikawa underutilized.

#### Category E: Resource & Reward Obstacles
1. **Inadequate Budget Allocation** — Quality programs starved of funds.
2. **Inappropriate Reward Systems** — No recognition for quality champions.
3. **High Employee Turnover** — Loss of trained TQM practitioners.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Equation / Framework** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{IF}} + C_{\text{EF}} + C_{\text{AP}} + C_{\text{PR}}$ | Currency (₹ / $); IF=Internal Failure, EF=External Failure, AP=Appraisal, PR=Prevention |
| Cost of Conformance (COC) | $\text{COC} = C_{\text{AP}} + C_{\text{PR}}$ | Currency |
| Cost of Non-Conformance (CONC) | $\text{CONC} = C_{\text{IF}} + C_{\text{EF}}$ | Currency |
| First Pass Yield (FPY) | $\text{FPY} = \frac{\text{Good Units Produced}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| Quality Improvement Ratio | $\text{QIR} = \frac{\Delta \text{COPQ}_{\text{reduction}}}{\text{TQM Investment}}$ | Ratio (dimensionless) |
| Employee Involvement Rate | $\text{EIR} = \frac{N_{\text{active participants}}}{N_{\text{total employees}}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) | $\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | D=Defects, U=Units, O=Opportunities per unit |
| Six Sigma Level | $\sigma = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3s}$ (short-term) | Z-score equivalent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In Six Sigma projects (which is the focus of PEMRT752), identifying and removing TQM obstacles **directly reduces COPQ**. Companies like Motorola, GE, and Toyota famously reduced COPQ by **60–80%** by systematically dismantling these obstacles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Analytical Derivation 1: Quantifying the Impact of TQM Obstacles

**Goal:** Demonstrate mathematically how TQM obstacles inflate COPQ and reduce process yield.

#### Step 1: Define the Base Quality Cost Equation

We begin with the foundational four-component quality cost model (Feigenbaum's PAF Model):

$$
\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{Prevention}} + C_{\text{Appraisal}} + C_{\text{Internal Failure}} + C_{\text{External Failure}}
$$

**Explanation:** This equation captures all monetary outflows related to quality — proactive investments (prevention), inspection costs (appraisal), and the price of failures (internal and external rework/scrap).

#### Step 2: Introduce an "Obstacle Coefficient" (k_obstacle)

When TQM obstacles exist, the failure costs inflate disproportionately. We model this as:

$$
C_{\text{Failure Total}} = (C_{\text{IF}} + C_{\text{EF}}) \cdot (1 + k_{\text{obstacle}})
$$

**Explanation:** The factor $k_{\text{obstacle}} \geq 0$ represents the **normalized penalty** imposed by TQM barriers. For example:
- $k_{\text{obstacle}} = 0$ → No obstacles (ideal state)
- $k_{\text{obstacle}} = 0.5$ → Moderate obstacles (typical mid-stage TQM rollout)
- $k_{\text{obstacle}} = 1.5$ → Severe obstacles (failed TQM implementation)

#### Step 3: Express the Modified COPQ

Substituting Step 2 into Step 1:

$$
\text{COPQ}_{\text{actual}} = C_{\text{PR}} + C_{\text{AP}} + (C_{\text{IF}} + C_{\text{EF}})(1 + k_{\text{obstacle}})
$$

#### Step 4: Derive the Relationship Between FPY and Obstacles

Empirically, every obstacle contributes to a probability of defect generation. Define defect probability per unit as $p$, then:

$$
p_{\text{effective}} = p_{\text{baseline}} \cdot (1 + k_{\text{obstacle}})
$$

And First Pass Yield becomes:

$$
\text{FPY} = (1 - p_{\text{effective}})^n
$$

where $n$ is the number of process steps. Taking this further:

$$
\text{FPY} = \left(1 - p_{\text{baseline}}(1 + k_{\text{obstacle}})\right)^n
$$

#### Step 5: Demonstrate the Compounding Effect

For a 5-step process with baseline defect probability $p_{\text{baseline}} = 0.01$ and obstacle coefficient $k_{\text{obstacle}} = 0.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{\text{effective}} &= 0.01 \cdot (1 + 0.5) = 0.015 \\
\text{FPY}_{\text{ideal}} &= (1 - 0.01)^5 = 0.95099 \\
\text{FPY}_{\text{obstructed}} &= (1 - 0.015)^5 = 0.92732
\end{aligned}
$$

**Loss in yield:** $0.95099 - 0.92732 = 0.02367$, i.e., a **2.37% drop** in just a 5-step process. In a 50-step process, the drop compounds exponentially.

### Analytical Matrix: Obstacle-to-Outcome Mapping

| **Obstacle Category** | **Specific Obstacle** | **Affected Quality Metric** | **Mitigation Strategy (KTU-Approved)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strategic | Lack of top management commitment | COPQ (↑), OEE (↓) | Visible leadership walk-throughs, Hoshin Kanri |
| Cultural | Resistance to change | FPY (↓), EIR (↓) | Kotter's 8-step change model |
| Structural | QC department silo mentality | Defect escape rate (↑) | Cross-functional QCCs |
| Measurement | No KPIs / wrong metrics | DPMO (↑) | Implement Balanced Scorecard |
| Resource | Inadequate training budget | Sigma level (↓) | Allocate ≥ 3% of payroll to training |
| Reward | No recognition system | Employee engagement (↓) | Quality circles, suggestion schemes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: The TQM Obstacle Hierarchy (Causal Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Root Cause: TQM Failure] --> catA[Strategic Obstacles]
    rootA --> catB[Cultural Obstacles]
    rootA --> catC[Structural Obstacles]
    rootA --> catD[Measurement Obstacles]
    rootA --> catE[Resource Obstacles]
    
    catA --> obsA1[No Top Management Commitment]
    catA --> obsA2[Short-Term Financial Focus]
    catA --> obsA3[Poor Strategic Planning]
    
    catB --> obsB1[Employee Resistance to Change]
    catB --> obsB2[Lack of Empowerment]
    catB --> obsB3[Inadequate Training]
    
    catC --> obsC1[QC Department Isolation]
    catC --> obsC2[Poor Process Documentation]
    catC --> obsC3[Supplier Misalignment]
    
    catD --> obsD1[No KPIs Defined]
    catD --> obsD2[Symptom Not Root Cause]
    catD --> obsD3[Underuse of SPC Tools]
    
    catE --> obsE1[Insufficient Budget]
    catE --> obsE2[No Reward System]
    catE --> obsE3[High Employee Turnover]
    
    obsA1 --> impact[Outcome: Higher COPQ]
    obsB1 --> impact
    obsC1 --> impact
    obsD1 --> impact
    obsE1 --> impact
    
    impact --> finalA[Lower Customer Satisfaction]
    impact --> finalB[Reduced Profitability]
    impact --> finalC[Failed Six Sigma DMAIC Projects]
    
    style rootA fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style catA fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style catB fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style catC fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style catD fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style catE fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style impact fill:#ffe66d,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style finalA fill:#95e1d3,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style finalB fill:#95e1d3,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style finalC fill:#95e1d3,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### Diagram 2: The TQM Obstacle Mitigation Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[TQM Implementation Phase] --> phaseA[Phase 1: Awareness]
    start --> phaseB[Phase 2: Adoption]
    start --> phaseC[Phase 3: Integration]
    start --> phaseD[Phase 4: Sustenance]
    
    phaseA --> obs1[Obstacle: Lack of Vision]
    phaseB --> obs2[Obstacle: Resistance to Change]
    phaseC --> obs3[Obstacle: Departmental Silos]
    phaseD --> obs4[Obstacle: Loss of Momentum]
    
    obs1 --> mit1[Mitigation: Top-Down Vision Communication]
    obs2 --> mit2[Mitigation: Training, Involvement, Rewards]
    obs3 --> mit3[Mitigation: Cross-Functional QCCs]
    obs4 --> mit4[Mitigation: Continuous Audit and Kaizen]
    
    mit1 --> success[Successful TQM Culture]
    mit2 --> success
    mit3 --> success
    mit4 --> success
    
    success --> kpi1[COPQ Reduced by 50%+]
    success --> kpi2[FPY Increased to 95%+]
    success --> kpi3[Customer CSAT Score 4.5+ out of 5]
    
    style start fill:#6c5ce7,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style success fill:#00b894,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style kpi1 fill:#fdcb6e,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style kpi2 fill:#fdcb6e,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style kpi3 fill:#fdcb6e,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### Diagram 3: The PDCA Anti-Obstacle Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[PLAN: Identify TQM Obstacles via Ishikawa Fishbone] --> D1[DO: Pilot Mitigation on One Department]
    D1 --> C1[CHECK: Measure COPQ and FPY Delta]
    C1 --> A1[ACT: Scale Up if Effective OR Pivot Strategy]
    A1 --> P1
    
    C1 --> obsBlock{Obstacle Persists?}
    obsBlock -->|Yes| D1
    obsBlock -->|No| sustain[Sustain via Kaizen Culture]
    
    style P1 fill:#74b9ff,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#55efc4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A1 fill:#fab1a0,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style sustain fill:#00b894,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any **six major obstacles** to the successful implementation of Total Quality Management in an organization.*

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 Marks):**
1. Lack of top management commitment and leadership
2. Employee resistance to change
3. Insufficient training and education
4. Inadequate communication of the quality vision
5. Short-term financial focus ignoring long-term quality gains
6. Absence of a proper reward and recognition system

*(Any six from the five categories earn full marks.)*

#### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between **Cost of Conformance (COC)** and **Cost of Non-Conformance (CONC)** with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Cost of Conformance (COC)** | **Cost of Non-Conformance (CONC)** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Costs incurred to *prevent* and *detect* defects | Costs incurred *due to* defects reaching/affecting customers |
| Formula | $C_{\text{PR}} + C_{\text{AP}}$ | $C_{\text{IF}} + C_{\text{EF}}$ |
| Nature | Proactive investment | Reactive penalty |
| Example | Employee training, quality audits | Rework, warranty claims, product recalls |
| Impact on TQM | Reduces future CONC drastically | Indicates TQM failure zones |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **strategic and cultural obstacles** to TQM implementation. For each category, propose **three concrete countermeasures**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) framework** with its four components. Using a numerical example, demonstrate how TQM obstacles **inflate COPQ** and how their removal **reduces it**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

##### Part (a) — Strategic & Cultural Obstacles (7 Marks)

**Strategic Obstacles:**
1. **Lack of Top Management Commitment:** When leaders do not visibly champion quality, employees perceive TQM as a "flavor of the month." **Countermeasure:** Leaders must conduct regular *Quality Gemba Walks*, publish quality dashboards, and tie executive KPIs to quality metrics.
2. **Short-Term Financial Focus:** Shareholders demanding quarterly profits push management to cut quality budgets. **Countermeasure:** Adopt a *Hoshin Kanri* strategic planning model that aligns 3–5 year quality targets with business goals.
3. **Misalignment of TQM with Business Strategy:** TQM is treated as a parallel program, not integrated. **Countermeasure:** Embed quality objectives into the corporate Balanced Scorecard.

**Cultural Obstacles:**
1. **Employee Resistance to Change:** Fear of job loss, new work methods, and statistical tools. **Countermeasure:** Apply Kotter's 8-step change model, with special emphasis on creating a *Guiding Coalition* and *Quick Wins*.
2. **Lack of Empowerment:** Frontline workers cannot halt production for quality issues. **Countermeasure:** Implement *Andon systems* and *Quality Stop-the-Line Authority*.
3. **Inadequate Training:** Workers do not know SPC, FMEA, or root cause analysis. **Countermeasure:** Mandatory ≥ 40 hours/year quality training per employee, with competency certification.

##### Part (b) — COPQ Framework with Numerical Example (7 Marks)

The **COPQ framework** has four components per Feigenbaum's PAF Model:

$$
\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{Prevention}} + C_{\text{Appraisal}} + C_{\text{Internal Failure}} + C_{\text{External Failure}}
$$

**Numerical Example:**

Suppose an automotive parts manufacturer (before TQM) records:
- Prevention Costs (Training, Process Design): ₹ 2,00,000
- Appraisal Costs (Inspection, Testing): ₹ 3,00,000
- Internal Failure Costs (Rework, Scrap): ₹ 8,00,000
- External Failure Costs (Warranty, Returns): ₹ 12,00,000

**Step 1: Calculate Initial COPQ**

$$
\text{COPQ}_{\text{before TQM}} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 25{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Identify Obstacle Coefficient**

The company has moderate TQM obstacles: $k_{\text{obstacle}} = 0.5$.

**Step 3: Calculate Failure Cost with Obstacle Penalty**

$$
C_{\text{Failure,actual}} = (8{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000)(1 + 0.5) = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 1.5 = \text{₹ } 30{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Effective COPQ with Obstacles**

$$
\text{COPQ}_{\text{actual}} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 35{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5: After TQM Implementation (Obstacles Removed)**

The company invests ₹ 5,00,000 in training and process improvement ($k_{\text{obstacle}}$ drops to 0.05). New failure costs:

$$
C_{\text{Failure,post-TQM}} = (8{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000)(1 + 0.05) = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 1.05 = \text{₹ } 21{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{COPQ}_{\text{after TQM}} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 21{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 29{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Net Savings**

$$
\text{Savings} = 35{,}00{,}000 - 29{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ (17.14\% reduction in COPQ)}
$$

*(TQM investment of ₹ 3,00,000 extra prevention returned ₹ 6,00,000 in failure savings → ROI = 100%.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** as a diagnostic tool to identify TQM obstacles. Apply it to a case where a manufacturing firm faces a **25% defect rate**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Deming's 14 Points** and highlight **at least three points** that directly address obstacles to TQM implementation. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution Outline to Question B:

**Part (a):** The Ishikawa Fishbone Diagram categorizes potential causes under **6 Ms** (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature). For the 25% defect scenario, students should construct causes under each M:
- *Man:* untrained operators, shift fatigue
- *Machine:* worn-out cutting tools, poor calibration
- *Material:* inconsistent supplier quality
- *Method:* outdated SOPs, no standard work
- *Measurement:* broken gauges, no SPC charts
- *Mother Nature:* ambient temperature variations

A textual/Mermaid representation earns full credit.

**Part (b):** Deming's 14 Points cover: create constancy of purpose, adopt the new philosophy, cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality, end price-driven purchasing, improve constantly, institute training, institute leadership, drive out fear, break down barriers between departments, eliminate slogans, eliminate numerical quotas, remove barriers to pride of workmanship, institute vigorous education, and put everybody to work on transformation.

Three points addressing TQM obstacles:
- **Point 1 (Constancy of Purpose):** Addresses strategic short-term focus
- **Point 5 (Improve constantly):** Counters resistance to change
- **Point 7 (Institute leadership):** Directly counters lack of top management commitment
- **Point 13 (Education):** Counters inadequate training obstacle

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** write only the *names* of obstacles without explaining their *impact* — you will lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Do NOT** confuse *Cost of Conformance* with *Cost of Poor Quality* — they are not the same.
> - **Do NOT** skip the **countermeasure/mitigation** portion — KTU 2024 examiners explicitly look for "**problem → solution**" linkage.
> - **Do NOT** write vague statements like *"lack of communication"* without specifying *what kind* (top-down, lateral, or feedback loops).
> - **Always** end numerical COPQ problems with the **net savings/ROI calculation** — it is a favorite 1-mark bonus.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Five categories of TQM obstacles:** Strategic, Cultural, Structural, Measurement, Resource.
- ✅ **The biggest obstacle** universally is **lack of sustained top management commitment** — Deming devoted Point 1 and Point 7 to it.
- ✅ **COPQ = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure** — memorize this equation verbatim.
- ✅ **Removing TQM obstacles reduces failure costs faster than it increases prevention costs** — this is the economic justification for TQM.
- ✅ **Employee resistance** is best countered via **Kotter's 8-step change model**, not by mandates.
- ✅ **Quality is everyone's responsibility** — never let it remain siloed in a QC department (a structural obstacle).
- ✅ **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** loop is the operational tool to continuously dismantle TQM obstacles.
- ✅ **Training budget:** KTU expects you to cite **≥ 3% of payroll** as a benchmark.
- ✅ **Ishikawa Fishbone (6 Ms)** is the diagnostic tool for identifying obstacles.
- ✅ **Six Sigma DMAIC projects** directly address TQM obstacles through the *Improve* and *Control* phases.
- ✅ **Cross-functional Quality Circles (QCCs)** are the canonical structural countermeasure.
- ✅ **Measurement obstacles** are removed by implementing **KPIs** like DPMO, FPY, OEE, and CSAT.
- ✅ **Reward and recognition** is non-negotiable — without it, quality becomes a "thankless" extra duty.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benefits of TQM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Benefits of TQM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview of TQM Benefits

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a **comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy** that centres on continuously meeting customer expectations by integrating quality into every functional area, every process, and every employee activity. The "Benefits of TQM" refer to the **tangible and intangible gains** an organization realizes when TQM principles — customer focus, continuous improvement (Kaizen), employee involvement, process approach, fact-based decision-making, and supplier partnership — are systematically deployed and sustained.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, TQM benefits are evaluated across **five stakeholder dimensions**:

| # | Stakeholder Dimension | Nature of Benefit |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Customer** | Enhanced satisfaction, loyalty, retention |
| 2 | **Employee** | Higher morale, lower attrition, empowerment |
| 3 | **Shareholder / Owner** | Improved ROI, profitability, market valuation |
| 4 | **Operations** | Reduced waste, higher yield, lower rework |
| 5 | **Society / Supplier** | Stronger partnerships, regulatory goodwill |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Five-Star Restaurant"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a small restaurant in Kerala that graduates from being a "good eatery" to becoming a **five-star hotel**. The transformation did not come from a single ingredient upgrade — it came from the **entire system**: chef training, kitchen hygiene, supplier vetting (fresh fish daily), customer feedback cards, menu redesign based on feedback, and a culture where *every staff member* from the sweeper to the manager feels responsible for guest delight.
>
> That holistic uplift — better food, happier guests, lower food wastage, repeat customers, premium pricing power, motivated staff — is exactly what **TQM delivers** to a manufacturing or service enterprise. The "benefits" are not a single magic number; they are a **virtuous cycle of improvements** that compound over time.

## 1.3 Categorisation of TQM Benefits

TQM benefits fall into two master buckets:

### A. Tangible (Hard) Benefits — Measurable on the P&L
- Reduction in **Cost of Quality (COQ)**
- Decrease in **scrap, rework, warranty claims**
- Increase in **productivity / throughput**
- Growth in **market share and revenue**
- Lower **employee turnover ratio**

### B. Intangible (Soft) Benefits — Strategic & Cultural
- Stronger **brand reputation**
- Improved **customer loyalty and NPS** (Net Promoter Score)
- Enhanced **organizational learning culture**
- Better **supplier relationships**
- Higher **regulatory compliance posture**
- Improved **employee engagement and innovation**

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently frame the question as *"Discuss the benefits of TQM to an organization."* The model answer must **explicitly classify** benefits into tangible vs. intangible and link each to a stakeholder group to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Logical Pillars of TQM Benefit Realisation

### Pillar 1 — Customer-Driven Value Creation
TQM begins and ends with the **Voice of Customer (VOC)**. Every process redesign, every Kaizen event, every FMEA is anchored to a customer requirement (the **CTQ — Critical-to-Quality** characteristic). The benefit manifests as:
- **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** improvement
- **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** enhancement
- Reduction in **cost of acquiring new customers**

### Pillar 2 — Process Optimisation & Waste Elimination
Adopting the Deming/Shewhart cycle (**PDCA — Plan-Do-Check-Act**) and tools such as **Pareto, Ishikawa, Control Charts**, organisations systematically strip out the **seven muda (wastes)**: transport, inventory, motion, waiting, overproduction, over-processing, and defects.

### Pillar 3 — People-Centric Cultural Transformation
Through **Quality Circles, Suggestion Schemes, Cross-Functional Teams (CFTs)** and **empowerment**, TQM converts employees from labour into **knowledge workers**. The Japanese term **"Kaizen"** (continuous incremental improvement) drives ownership at the grassroots.

## 2.2 The Cost of Quality (COQ) Framework — The Master Lever

The single most important quantitative tool that **quantifies TQM benefits** is the **Cost of Quality model**, originally popularised by Joseph Juran and later refined by Philip Crosby.

$$
\text{Total COQ} \;=\; C_P \;+\; C_A \;+\; C_{IF} \;+\; C_{EF}
$$

where:

- $C_P$ = **Prevention Cost** (training, quality planning, supplier capability development)
- $C_A$ = **Appraisal Cost** (inspection, testing, audits, calibration)
- $C_{IF}$ = **Internal Failure Cost** (scrap, rework, reinspection, downtime)
- $C_{EF}$ = **External Failure Cost** (warranty claims, returns, recalls, loss of goodwill)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key TQM Insight:** As TQM matures, $C_P$ and $C_A$ **rise initially** (investment phase), but $C_{IF}$ and $C_{EF}$ **fall dramatically and persistently**. The **optimal COQ** occurs where the sum of all four is minimised — typically when **Prevention ≈ 40%**, **Appraisal ≈ 30%**, and **Failure costs combined ≈ 30%**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarises every quantitative metric a KTU 2024 student must know for evaluating TQM benefits. (Note: vertical bars rendered as `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.)

| # | Metric / Formula | Symbolic Form | Engineering Utility | Unit |
|---|------------------|---------------|---------------------|------|
| 1 | Total Cost of Quality | $COQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Quantifies financial benefit of quality | Currency $\vert$ year |
| 2 | Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $COPQ = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Measures failure-driven waste | Currency $\vert$ year |
| 3 | Prevention-Appraisal Ratio | $PAR = \dfrac{C_P}{C_A}$ | Indicates proactive vs. detective quality spend | Dimensionless |
| 4 | First Pass Yield (FPY) | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{\text{good at first}}}{N_{\text{total units entered}}}$ | Measures process capability | Percentage |
| 5 | Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ | Multi-stage yield aggregation | Percentage |
| 6 | Defects Per Million Opportunities | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O}$ | Six Sigma alignment metric | ppm |
| 7 | Customer Retention Rate | $CRR = \dfrac{C_E - C_N}{C_S} \times 100$ | Loyalty benefit indicator | Percentage |
| 8 | Net Promoter Score | $NPS = \%P - \%D$ | Brand advocacy benefit | Integer (-100 to +100) |
| 9 | Quality ROI | $ROI_Q = \dfrac{\text{Quality Savings} - \text{Quality Investment}}{\text{Quality Investment}} \times 100$ | Justifies TQM investment | Percentage |
| 10 | Employee Turnover Ratio | $ETR = \dfrac{\text{Leavers}}{\text{Average Headcount}} \times 100$ | HR / cultural benefit | Percentage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation legend:** $N$ = units, $D$ = defects, $U$ = units produced, $O$ = opportunities per unit, $C_E$ = customers at end, $C_N$ = new customers, $C_S$ = customers at start, $P$ = Promoters, $D$ = Detractors.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

| Engineering Domain | How TQM Benefits Manifest |
|--------------------|---------------------------|
| **Manufacturing (Automotive, FMCG)** | Lower PPM defects, reduced warranty cost, higher OEE |
| **Software / IT Services** | Lower defect leakage, faster release cycles, higher CSAT |
| **Healthcare** | Reduced medical errors, shorter patient wait time, improved outcomes |
| **Construction** | Fewer reworks, on-time delivery, lower accident rates |
| **Banking / Service** | Lower transaction errors, higher customer lifetime value (CLV) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cost of Quality Reduction (Tangible Benefit)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** An electronics firm in Kochi reports the following annual quality costs **before** TQM implementation:
> - Prevention Cost ($C_P$) = ₹ 4,00,000
> - Appraisal Cost ($C_A$) = ₹ 6,00,000
> - Internal Failure Cost ($C_{IF}$) = ₹ 20,00,000
> - External Failure Cost ($C_{EF}$) = ₹ 30,00,000
>
> After three years of TQM, prevention and appraisal rise by 50%, while internal failure falls by 40% and external failure falls by 60%. **Calculate the annual savings, the new COQ, and the percentage reduction in COPQ.**

### Step 1 — Compute the Original COQ

$$
COQ_{\text{old}} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

$$
COQ_{\text{old}} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{COQ_{\text{old}} = \text{₹ } 60{,}00{,}000}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the New COQ After TQM

New prevention cost with 50% increase:

$$
C_P^{\text{new}} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times (1 + 0.50) = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 1.5 = \text{₹ } 6{,}00{,}000
$$

New appraisal cost with 50% increase:

$$
C_A^{\text{new}} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 1.5 = \text{₹ } 9{,}00{,}000
$$

New internal failure cost with 40% reduction:

$$
C_{IF}^{\text{new}} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.40) = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6 = \text{₹ } 12{,}00{,}000
$$

New external failure cost with 60% reduction:

$$
C_{EF}^{\text{new}} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.60) = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4 = \text{₹ } 12{,}00{,}000
$$

Therefore:

$$
COQ_{\text{new}} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{COQ_{\text{new}} = \text{₹ } 39{,}00{,}000}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Annual Savings

$$
\text{Savings} = COQ_{\text{old}} - COQ_{\text{new}}
$$

$$
\text{Savings} = 60{,}00{,}000 - 39{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 21{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Original and New COPQ

$$
COPQ_{\text{old}} = C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 20{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 50{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
COPQ_{\text{new}} = 12{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 24{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\% \text{Reduction in COPQ} = \dfrac{COPQ_{\text{old}} - COPQ_{\text{new}}}{COPQ_{\text{old}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{Reduction in COPQ} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000 - 24{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \dfrac{26{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\% \text{Reduction in COPQ} = 52\%}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> The firm **saved ₹ 21 lakh annually**, and **failure-driven waste (COPQ) fell by 52%**, even though prevention/appraisal spend *rose* by 50%. This is the textbook signature of successful TQM: **spend more upstream to spend far less downstream.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Quality ROI Justification

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The firm invested ₹ 10,00,000 in TQM (training, QMS software, consultants). Annual quality savings (from rework reduction, warranty reduction, productivity gain) amount to ₹ 18,00,000. Compute the **Quality ROI** and the **payback period**.

### Step 1 — Quality ROI

$$
ROI_Q = \dfrac{\text{Savings} - \text{Investment}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100
$$

$$
ROI_Q = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{ROI_Q = 80\%}
$$

### Step 2 — Payback Period

$$
\text{Payback} = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000}{18{,}00{,}000} \times 12 \text{ months}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Payback} \approx 6.67 \text{ months}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **sub-12-month payback** with **80% ROI** is highly attractive and is the typical executive justification used in KTU case-study answers for TQM adoption.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Benefit × Driver × KPI

| TQM Benefit | Driving TQM Principle | Key Performance Indicator (KPI) | Measurable Target (Typical) |
|-------------|----------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Reduced rework & scrap | Process approach + PDCA | Internal Failure Cost ($C_{IF}$) | 40-60% reduction in 3 years |
| Lower warranty claims | Customer focus + robust design | External Failure Cost ($C_{EF}$) | 50-70% reduction in 3 years |
| Higher customer retention | VOC + QFD | Customer Retention Rate (CRR) | Increase by 10-15 percentage points |
| Lower employee turnover | Empowerment + Kaizen | Employee Turnover Ratio (ETR) | Reduce by 30-50% |
| Higher productivity | Waste elimination (7 muda) | First Pass Yield (FPY) | Rise from 85% to 98%+ |
| Stronger supplier base | Partnership sourcing | Supplier Defect Rate (SDR) | Reduce by 50-80% |
| Brand & goodwill | Consistent quality delivery | Net Promoter Score (NPS) | Move from +20 to +60+ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The TQM Benefit Virtuous Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TQM Adoption"] --> B["Customer Focus and VOC Capture"]
    B --> C["Process Improvement via PDCA"]
    C --> D["Defect and Waste Reduction"]
    D --> E["Lower COPQ and Higher FPY"]
    E --> F["Improved Financial Performance"]
    F --> G["Employee Reward and Recognition"]
    G --> H["Higher Engagement and Kaizen Culture"]
    H --> I["Stronger Supplier Partnerships"]
    I --> J["Enhanced Brand and Customer Loyalty"]
    J --> A
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b2545,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7f0000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flow shows how TQM is **self-reinforcing**. Investment in TQM improves processes, which improves financials, which funds employee rewards, which strengthens culture, which deepens supplier ties, which enhances brand and brings back more loyal customers — closing the loop.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of TQM Benefits Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        I1["Leadership Commitment"]
        I2["Customer Requirements VOC"]
        I3["Employee Skills and Training"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["TQM PROCESS LAYER"]
        P1["PDCA Cycle"]
        P2["Kaizen Events"]
        P3["Quality Circles"]
        P4["Statistical Process Control"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["BENEFIT OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1["Lower COQ and COPQ"]
        O2["Higher FPY and RTY"]
        O3["Improved CSAT and NPS"]
        O4["Lower Employee Turnover"]
        O5["Higher Market Share"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I3 --> P3
    I1 --> P4
    I2 --> P1
    P1 --> O1
    P2 --> O2
    P3 --> O4
    P4 --> O2
    P1 --> O3
    P2 --> O1
    P3 --> O5
    style INPUT fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style PROCESS fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style OUTPUT fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Hierarchy — Stakeholder Benefit Decomposition

```mermaid
mindmap
  root(("Benefits of TQM"))
    Customer
      Higher satisfaction
      Better loyalty
      Lower price over time
    Employee
      Empowerment
      Skill upgrade
      Job satisfaction
      Lower attrition
    Organisation
      Cost reduction
      Productivity gain
      Market share growth
      Brand equity
    Supplier
      Stable orders
      Long term contracts
      Joint improvement
    Society
      Compliance
      Reduced environmental impact
      Employment generation
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six tangible benefits of TQM to a manufacturing organisation.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 mark each for crispness, plus introductory framing):**

TQM delivers the following tangible benefits to a manufacturing organisation:

1. **Reduction in scrap and rework** — leading to direct material savings.
2. **Lower warranty and external failure costs** — fewer recalls and claims.
3. **Improved productivity** — higher output per man-hour and per machine-hour.
4. **Higher first-pass yield (FPY)** — fewer defects escape to the next stage.
5. **Lower inventory carrying cost** — through Just-in-Time pull systems.
6. **Higher market share and revenue** — driven by superior quality reputation.
7. **Reduced employee turnover** — saving recruitment and training costs.

> [!TIP]
> Always **frame** the answer with one opening line: *"Tangible benefits are those measurable directly in monetary terms on the balance sheet."*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between tangible and intangible benefits of TQM with three examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Tangible Benefits | Intangible Benefits |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | Quantifiable, monetary, P&L impact | Qualitative, strategic, cultural |
| **Example 1** | 30% reduction in rework cost | Improved brand reputation |
| **Example 2** | ₹ 50 lakh annual COQ saving | Higher customer loyalty |
| **Example 3** | FPY improvement from 85% to 97% | Stronger employee engagement |
| **Measurement** | KPI dashboards, audits | Surveys, NPS, culture audits |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often list *only* tangible benefits. KTU examiners explicitly ask for the **difference**, so a clean comparative table earns the third mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Set 1 `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven categories of benefits of TQM** to an organisation, classifying them under customers, employees, shareholders, operations, suppliers, and society. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company has the following COQ data:
- Prevention Cost = ₹ 5,00,000
- Appraisal Cost = ₹ 8,00,000
- Internal Failure Cost = ₹ 25,00,000
- External Failure Cost = ₹ 40,00,000
After TQM, prevention rises by 60%, appraisal by 25%, internal failure drops by 50%, and external failure drops by 70%. Compute (i) the new total COQ, (ii) the annual savings, and (iii) the percentage reduction in COPQ. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **seven categories** of TQM benefits are:

1. **Customer Benefits** — higher satisfaction, loyalty, lower price over product life.
2. **Employee Benefits** — empowerment, skill growth, lower stress, retention.
3. **Shareholder/Owner Benefits** — higher ROI, dividend, share price, profitability.
4. **Operational Benefits** — reduced waste, higher yield, lower cycle time.
5. **Supplier Benefits** — stable long-term contracts, joint development programmes.
6. **Strategic Benefits** — competitive advantage, market share growth, brand equity.
7. **Societal Benefits** — regulatory goodwill, lower environmental burden, employment generation.

> Each category discussed with **one example + one KPI** scores 1 mark (7 × 1 = 7 marks).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Original COQ:**

$$
COQ_{\text{old}} = 5 + 8 + 25 + 40 = \text{₹ } 78 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 2 — New COQ:**

$$
C_P^{\text{new}} = 5 \times 1.6 = \text{₹ } 8 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
C_A^{\text{new}} = 8 \times 1.25 = \text{₹ } 10 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
C_{IF}^{\text{new}} = 25 \times 0.5 = \text{₹ } 12.5 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
C_{EF}^{\text{new}} = 40 \times 0.3 = \text{₹ } 12 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
COQ_{\text{new}} = 8 + 10 + 12.5 + 12 = \text{₹ } 42.5 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual Savings:**

$$
\text{Savings} = 78 - 42.5 = \text{₹ } 35.5 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Step 4 — COPQ Reduction:**

$$
COPQ_{\text{old}} = 25 + 40 = \text{₹ } 65 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
COPQ_{\text{new}} = 12.5 + 12 = \text{₹ } 24.5 \text{ lakh}
$$

$$
\% \text{Reduction} = \dfrac{65 - 24.5}{65} \times 100 = \dfrac{40.5}{65} \times 100 \approx 62.31\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently **forget to compute the percentage reduction** in part (b)(iii). Even if the COQ is correct, missing the percentage calculation costs 2 marks. Always end with a **percentage or ratio** for full credit.

---

### Question B — Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper]`
**(a)** Discuss the **role of TQM in achieving competitive advantage** for an Indian manufacturing firm. Include at least five strategic benefits and link each to a measurable KPI. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The HR department of a firm reports that before TQM, the employee turnover ratio was 28%. After TQM implementation (empowerment, Quality Circles, recognition), it dropped to 12%. If the firm employs 1,000 people on average and the cost of replacing one employee is ₹ 2,50,000, compute the **annual savings on replacement cost** attributable to TQM. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

TQM confers **competitive advantage** through:

| # | Strategic Benefit | KPI | Typical Improvement |
|---|-------------------|-----|----------------------|
| 1 | Superior product reliability | Field Defect Rate (ppm) | From 5000 to 200 ppm |
| 2 | Faster time-to-market | New Product Introduction (NPI) cycle time | 30-50% reduction |
| 3 | Premium pricing power | Gross Margin % | +5 to +10 percentage points |
| 4 | Customer lock-in | Customer Retention Rate (CRR) | +15% absolute |
| 5 | Supply chain resilience | On-Time-In-Full (OTIF) % | +20% absolute |
| 6 | Innovation pipeline | Ideas implemented per employee per year | 3x to 5x |
| 7 | Employer brand | Employee Net Promoter Score (eNPS) | From +10 to +50 |

(Any 5 benefits with KPIs = 7 marks; 5 × 1.4 ≈ 7)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Original leavers per year (before TQM):**

$$
\text{Leavers}_{\text{old}} = \dfrac{28}{100} \times 1000 = 280 \text{ employees}
$$

**Step 2 — New leavers per year (after TQM):**

$$
\text{Leavers}_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{12}{100} \times 1000 = 120 \text{ employees}
$$

**Step 3 — Reduction in leavers:**

$$
\Delta L = 280 - 120 = 160 \text{ employees}
$$

**Step 4 — Replacement cost per employee = ₹ 2,50,000**

**Step 5 — Annual savings:**

$$
\text{Savings} = 160 \times 2{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Savings} = \text{₹ } 4{,}00{,}00{,}000 \; (\text{₹ } 4 \text{ crore})}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is computing the *percentage* reduction (16%) and stopping. KTU requires the **monetised annual savings** — always multiply the headcount delta by the unit cost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (High-Density Summary):**

- **Definition**: TQM is an organisation-wide philosophy; benefits are tangible (₹-measurable) and intangible (strategic/cultural).
- **Master Quantitative Tool**: Cost of Quality $COQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ and COPQ $= C_{IF} + C_{EF}$.
- **Key Insight**: Prevention & Appraisal costs **rise** under TQM; Internal & External Failure costs **fall disproportionately**, yielding net savings.
- **Benefit Categories**: Customer, Employee, Shareholder, Operational, Supplier, Strategic, Societal — remember the **seven buckets**.
- **Customer KPIs**: CSAT, NPS, CRR, CLV.
- **Operational KPIs**: FPY, RTY, DPMO, COPQ.
- **Employee KPIs**: Employee Turnover Ratio, eNPS, Ideas per Employee.
- **Financial KPIs**: Quality ROI (target $> 50\%$ in mature TQM firms), Payback Period (target $< 12$ months).
- **Strategic KPIs**: Market Share %, OTIF %, Brand Equity Index.
- **Cultural Pillar**: Kaizen, Quality Circles, PDCA, 7 Muda, 5S are the **enablers** that convert TQM philosophy into measurable benefits.
- **Exam Strategy**: Always present answers in a **classified manner** (tangible vs intangible or stakeholder-wise) and **quantify at least one benefit with a numeric example** to score full marks.
- **Common Pitfall**: Confusing *benefit* with *principle*. Principle = what you do; Benefit = what you gain. KTU examiners reward clear distinction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TQM Axioms - Commitment
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** TQM Axioms - Commitment

# TQM Axioms – Commitment

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TQM Axiom of Commitment:** In the architecture of Total Quality Management, *commitment* is formally defined as the **unwavering, documented, and resource-backed dedication of top leadership and every employee across all hierarchical levels to the continuous pursuit of quality, customer satisfaction, and process excellence**. It is the single most foundational axiom upon which all other TQM principles (customer focus, continuous improvement, fact-based decision making, etc.) are structurally dependent.

Within the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMRT752 – Six Sigma)** context, commitment is treated as the **zeroth axiom** of TQM because without genuine, sustained commitment from leadership, the implementation of Six Sigma (DMAIC), Lean, and other quality frameworks collapses into a superficial paperwork exercise often referred to as "**quality theatre**."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital. Doctors, nurses, technicians, and the cleaning staff all play a role. Now imagine the **hospital director announces a new patient-first policy** but continues to **cut budgets for hygiene supplies, ignores staff complaints, and rewards only revenue-generating departments**.

Will the policy work? No — because the commitment is **only on paper, not in action or resource allocation**.
