**TQM Commitment works exactly the same way.** It is not a slogan on a wall poster; it is the *visible, daily, measurable behaviour* of leaders who allocate money, time, training, and political capital to quality. If the CEO does not personally review defect data, no one below will care about it either.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Commitment in TQM is **top-down** (leadership must demonstrate it first) and **bottom-up** (employees must feel empowered to act on it). Both vectors must align, or the system fails.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of TQM Commitment

| Pillar | Who Owns It | Core Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Leadership Commitment** | Top Management (CEO, Board) | Quality goals in strategic plan, % of budget allocated |
| **Employee Commitment** | Middle Management & Workforce | Training hours/person, participation in CI projects |
| **Customer Commitment** | Entire Organization | Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI), Net Promoter Score (NPS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever the question says *"Discuss the role of commitment in TQM,"* always structure your answer around these **three pillars**. Examiners award marks for systematic categorization.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Commitment is the *Axiom* (Not Just a Principle)

The word **axiom** in TQM is borrowed from Euclidean geometry — it is a **self-evident truth requiring no proof**. Commitment qualifies as an axiom because:

1. **No quality system survives without it** — every documented TQM failure (Ford Pinto, Boeing 737 MAX, Theranos) traces back to absent leadership commitment.
2. **It precedes all other TQM tools** — Six Sigma's *Define* phase literally begins with a **Project Charter signed by a Champion (executive sponsor)**. No charter = no commitment = no project.
3. **It is non-negotiable** — you cannot partially implement commitment. Either leaders walk the talk or they do not.

### 2.2 The Five Levels of Commitment (Adapted from Senge & TQM Literature)

Following a structured hierarchy used in KTU reference materials:

- **Level 1 — Compliance Commitment:** Doing only what regulation/policy demands (minimum baseline).
- **Level 2 — Personal Commitment:** Individual employee "buys in" emotionally to quality goals.
- **Level 3 — Team Commitment:** Cross-functional teams own quality metrics collectively.
- **Level 4 — Organizational Commitment:** Quality is embedded in vision, mission, KPIs, and reward systems.
- **Level 5 — Societal Commitment:** Organization extends quality thinking to suppliers, community, and environment (Total Responsibility).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While TQM Commitment is qualitative, it is **measured quantitatively** using the metrics below. KTU 2024 Scheme questions frequently ask you to *compute* or *interpret* these.

| Metric | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Employee Commitment Index (ECI)** | $ECI = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i}{n \cdot S_{max}} \times 100$ | $S_i$ = survey score of employee $i$; $n$ = total respondents; $S_{max}$ = max possible score | **Percentage (%)** |
| **Leadership Commitment Score (LCS)** | $LCS = w_1 \cdot B + w_2 \cdot A + w_3 \cdot R$ | $B$ = budget fraction to quality; $A$ = audit participation; $R$ = recognition events held; $w_i$ = weights summing to 1 | **Score (0–100)** |
| **Six Sigma Project Sponsorship Rate (PSSR)** | $PSSR = \dfrac{N_{sponsored}}{N_{proposed}} \times 100$ | $N_{sponsored}$ = projects with executive sponsor; $N_{proposed}$ = projects initiated | **Percentage (%)** |
| **Training-Hours per Employee (THE)** | $THE = \dfrac{\text{Total Quality Training Hours}}{\text{Number of Employees}}$ | Aggregate quality training exposure | **Hours / Year** |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = C_{internal\ failure} + C_{external\ failure} + C_{appraisal} + C_{prevention}$ | Four cost categories of quality | **Currency (₹, \$, €)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safety Note:** Notice that absolute value notation uses `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) instead of the bare pipe `\vert x\vert`, because raw `|` characters break the markdown table structure. KTU answer sheets require similar notation discipline.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Toyota Production System's success is 80 % attributed to leadership commitment to *Kaizen* (continuous improvement).
- **Healthcare:** Hospital-acquired infection (HAI) rates drop measurably when CEOs personally conduct *Gemba walks*.
- **Software Engineering:** Google's Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) culture succeeds because executive commitment protects "**error budgets**" from product-feature pressure.
- **Service Industry:** Airlines with CEO-led safety reviews (Emirates, Singapore Airlines) consistently rank top in safety audits.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Numerical Framework

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing Employee Commitment Index (ECI)

**Problem Statement:** A KTU-affiliated engineering college surveyed **120 employees** about their commitment to the newly implemented Six Sigma program. Each employee rated their commitment on a scale of **1 to 5**. The total sum of all ratings was **468**.

**Step 1 — Identify Variables**

$$
n = 120,\quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i = 468,\quad S_{max} = 5
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into ECI Formula**

$$
ECI = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i}{n \cdot S_{max}} \times 100
$$

$$
ECI = \frac{468}{120 \cdot 5} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify Denominator**

$$
ECI = \frac{468}{600} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Final Evaluation**

$$
ECI = 0.78 \times 100 = \mathbf{78\%}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (Mandatory for KTU Full Marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> An ECI of **78 %** indicates a **moderately high** but **not yet excellent** commitment level. KTU examiners expect students to comment that values **above 85 %** signal a *world-class* TQM culture, while values **below 60 %** signal systemic disengagement requiring intervention.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Leadership Commitment Score (LCS)

**Problem Statement:** A Six Sigma Champion at an automotive firm has the following data for the last fiscal year:
- Quality budget fraction: **$B = 0.15$**
- Executive audit participation: **$A = 0.80$** (80 % of audits attended)
- Recognition events held: **$R = 0.60$** (3 of 5 planned events conducted)

Assume weights: **$w_1 = 0.4$** (budget), **$w_2 = 0.4$** (audit), **$w_3 = 0.2$** (recognition).

**Step 1 — Verify Weight Constraint**

$$
w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 0.4 + 0.4 + 0.2 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into LCS Formula**

$$
LCS = w_1 \cdot B + w_2 \cdot A + w_3 \cdot R
$$

$$
LCS = (0.4 \times 0.15) + (0.4 \times 0.80) + (0.2 \times 0.60)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Each Term**

$$
LCS = 0.06 + 0.32 + 0.12
$$

**Step 4 — Sum**

$$
LCS = \mathbf{0.50\ (or\ 50\%)}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An LCS of **50 %** is **dangerously low** for a quality-critical industry like automotive. KTU exam answers should recommend: (i) increasing quality budget to **at least 5 %**, (ii) mandatory attendance by CEO at monthly audits, and (iii) instituting a **monthly quality awards ceremony**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Barriers vs. Enablers of TQM Commitment

| Dimension | Common Barrier | Recommended Enabler | KTU-Expected Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Leadership** | Verbal support, no resource backing | Personal involvement in quality reviews | "Walking the Gemba" |
| **Measurement** | Tracking only financial KPIs | Tracking process-level KPIs (DPMO, Cp, Cpk) | Aligns with Six Sigma |
| **Reward** | Rewarding only output volume | Rewarding defect reduction and improvement ideas | Reinforces behaviour |
| **Training** | One-time induction only | Continuous learning (≥40 hours/year) | Builds capability |
| **Communication** | Top-down directives only | Two-way dialogue, suggestion schemes | Builds ownership |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Commitment Cascade Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how commitment cascades from the Boardroom down to the shop-floor, and how feedback loops ensure continuous reinforcement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boardroom Strategic Commitment] --> B[Executive Sponsorship of Six Sigma]
    B --> C[Allocation of Resources Budget and Time]
    C --> D[Appointment of Champions and Black Belts]
    D --> E[Project Charter Sign-off]
    E --> F[DMAIC Project Execution]
    F --> G[Employee Empowerment and Training]
    G --> H[Process Improvements and Defect Reduction]
    H --> I[Customer Satisfaction Increase]
    I --> J[Measurable Business Results]
    J --> K[Leadership Reinforcement and Recognition]
    K --> A
    G -.Feedback.-> A
    H -.Feedback.-> B
```

### 4.2 The Commitment Maturity Ladder (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Level 1 Compliance Commitment] --> L2[Level 2 Personal Commitment]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Team Commitment]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Organizational Commitment]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 Societal Commitment]
    L1:::base
    L2:::base
    L3:::mid
    L4:::high
    L5:::peak
    classDef base fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#d39e00,color:#000
    classDef high fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    classDef peak fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Commitment Feedback System in Six Sigma

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Leadership Commitment Input] --> P2[Process: Define Phase Charter]
    P2 --> P3[Process: Measure Baseline Sigma Level]
    P3 --> P4[Process: Analyze Root Causes]
    P4 --> P5[Process: Improve Solutions]
    P5 --> P6[Process: Control Sustain Gains]
    P6 --> P7[Output: Improved Quality and Sigma Level]
    P7 --> P8[Feedback to Leadership KPI Dashboard]
    P8 --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The outer loop (P1 → P8 → P1) represents **strategic commitment renewal**, while the inner sequence (P2 → P6) represents the operational **DMAIC cycle**. The two are coupled — without the outer loop, the inner DMAIC loses steam.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the TQM axiom of commitment. Why is it considered foundational?*

**Model Answer:**
TQM commitment is the documented, resource-backed dedication of leadership and employees to continuous quality improvement. It is foundational because no quality system — including Six Sigma — can sustain itself without leadership sponsorship, budget allocation, and cultural reinforcement. Without commitment, DMAIC projects become paperwork exercises without real change.

*(Expected length: 3–4 sentences + one example. Award full marks for: definition + reason + example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three metrics used to measure commitment in a TQM organization.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Employee Commitment Index (ECI)** — derived from employee surveys.
2. **Leadership Commitment Score (LCS)** — weighted index of budget, audit participation, and recognition events.
3. **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — aggregate of failure, appraisal, and prevention costs.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **three pillars of TQM commitment** with suitable examples for each pillar. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A firm surveyed **200 employees** on a 1–5 commitment scale and obtained a total score of **720**. Compute the ECI and interpret the result with recommendations. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The **three pillars** of TQM commitment are:

1. **Leadership Commitment (Top Management):**
   The CEO and senior leaders must visibly champion quality — through budget allocation, personal participation in quality reviews (e.g., *Gemba walks*), and inclusion of quality goals in the strategic plan.
   *Example:* *Akio Toyoda of Toyota personally reviews quality defects on the assembly line.*

2. **Employee Commitment (Workforce):**
   Frontline employees must be trained, empowered, and rewarded for identifying defects and suggesting improvements.
   *Example:* *Toyota's suggestion system generates over **2 million ideas per year** from employees.*

3. **Customer Commitment (External Focus):**
   The organization must commit to understanding and meeting evolving customer needs, measuring satisfaction systematically.
   *Example:* *Amazon's customer obsession principle drives reverse-logistics and refund policies.*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify Variables**

$$
n = 200,\quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i = 720,\quad S_{max} = 5
$$

**Step 2 — Apply ECI Formula**

$$
ECI = \frac{720}{200 \times 5} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify**

$$
ECI = \frac{720}{1000} \times 100 = \mathbf{72\%}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation & Recommendations:**

- An ECI of **72 %** is *moderate* — acceptable but not world-class.
- **Recommendations:** (i) increase Six Sigma training hours from current level to ≥ **40 hours/year/employee**; (ii) launch a *Suggestion of the Month* recognition program; (iii) conduct *skip-level* meetings between executives and frontline workers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Discuss the **five levels of commitment maturity** in a TQM organization with a neat diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compute the **Leadership Commitment Score (LCS)** given: $B = 0.20$, $A = 0.90$, $R = 0.50$, with weights $w_1 = 0.5$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$. Interpret and recommend. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The five levels are:

| Level | Name | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **L1** | Compliance | Only what law/policy demands |
| **L2** | Personal | Individual emotional buy-in |
| **L3** | Team | Cross-functional team ownership |
| **L4** | Organizational | Quality embedded in strategy, KPIs, rewards |
| **L5** | Societal | Extended to suppliers, community, environment |

*(Refer to the Mermaid "Commitment Maturity Ladder" diagram in Section 4.2.)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Verify Weights**

$$
0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
LCS = (0.5 \times 0.20) + (0.3 \times 0.90) + (0.2 \times 0.50)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate**

$$
LCS = 0.10 + 0.27 + 0.10 = \mathbf{0.47}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

LCS = **47 %** is **below acceptable** for sustained TQM success.

**Recommendations:** Raise quality budget fraction to ≥ 0.05, make executive audits 100 % mandatory, and conduct quarterly recognition ceremonies to lift $R$ above 0.80.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in TQM Commitment Answers:**
> 1. **Defining commitment as just "motivation"** — examiners expect *resource-backed* and *visible* commitment. Mere slogans = 0 marks.
> 2. **Skipping the numerical interpretation** — when a question asks you to compute ECI/LCS, students who only solve the math without *interpreting* the result lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Not categorizing into the three pillars** — unstructured answers are penalized. Always use bullet tables or labeled sections.
> 4. **Forgetting the formula's denominator units** — confusing $S_{max}$ with $S_{min}$ or omitting the multiplication by 100 (for percentage) is a frequent error.
> 5. **Ignoring the feedback loop** — commitment is **not linear**; it is cyclical. Examiners reward answers that mention *continuous renewal* and *feedback*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Commitment is the *zeroth axiom*** of TQM — it precedes all tools, techniques, and frameworks.
- **Three Pillars:** Leadership, Employee, Customer commitment — all three must align.
- **Five Levels:** Compliance → Personal → Team → Organizational → Societal.
- **Key Metrics to Memorize:**
  - $ECI = \frac{\sum S_i}{n \cdot S_{max}} \times 100$
  - $LCS = w_1 B + w_2 A + w_3 R$
  - $COPQ = C_{internal} + C_{external} + C_{appraisal} + C_{prevention}$
- **Six Sigma Link:** DMAIC projects *require* a signed Project Charter from an executive **Champion** — this is the operational embodiment of leadership commitment.
- **Feedback Loops Matter:** Commitment is not a one-time declaration; it must be renewed through recognition, measurement, and visible leadership action.
- **Always Interpret Numbers:** A formula without interpretation = incomplete answer in KTU exams.
- **Use Real-World Examples:** Toyota, Amazon, Tata, Infosys, Apollo Hospitals — at least one example in a 7-mark answer boosts marks significantly.
- **Cite the Cycle:** PDCA (Deming Cycle) and DMAIC both depend on committed leadership for sustained success.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra for this Topic:**
> *"Commitment is not a poster on the wall — it is the budget line, the signed charter, the Gemba walk, and the reward cheque, repeated every quarter until quality becomes the organization's culture."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scientific Knowledge and Involvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

# MODULE 2: SCIENTIFIC KNOWLEDGE AND INVOLVEMENT

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Scientific Knowledge in Six Sigma** is defined as the systematic, empirical, and data-driven understanding of processes, variation, and customer requirements, acquired through rigorous application of the **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) methodology, statistical tools, and the scientific method.

In the context of Six Sigma, scientific knowledge refers to the structured body of information built upon:
- **Empirical Evidence**: Observable, measurable data collected from actual process outputs.
- **Statistical Inference**: Probability-based conclusions drawn from sample data.
- **Hypothesis Testing**: Validated assumptions about cause-and-effect relationships.
- **Reproducibility**: The ability to replicate results under controlled conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Involvement in Six Sigma** refers to the structured engagement of stakeholders across hierarchical levels (Champions, Master Black Belts, Black Belts, Green Belts, Yellow Belts, and process owners) who collectively drive process improvement using scientific knowledge.

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames this concept as the **intersection of human collaboration and empirical reasoning**, where scientific knowledge without structured involvement yields isolated insights, and involvement without scientific rigor yields subjective, non-reproducible results.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Surgery Team:**

Imagine a complex surgical operation. The **scientific knowledge** is analogous to the medical protocols, diagnostic instruments (MRI, blood tests), and evidence-based medicine. The **involvement** is the coordinated team — the surgeon (Black Belt), anesthesiologist (Green Belt), nurses (Yellow Belts), hospital administrator (Champion), and the patient (Customer).

Just as surgery fails if the surgeon has knowledge but no team, or if the team exists but ignores medical science, Six Sigma fails when either scientific knowledge OR involvement is missing. The **integration** of both ensures the operation (process) heals (improves) successfully.

### 1.1.3 Key Terminology in Bold

- **DMAIC**: Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control — the core Six Sigma problem-solving roadmap.
- **VOC (Voice of Customer)**: The documented requirements and expectations expressed by the customer.
- **VOP (Voice of Process)**: The measurable capability of a process to deliver consistent output.
- **SIPOC**: Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers — high-level process mapping.
- **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)**: Measurable characteristics whose performance directly affects customer satisfaction.
- **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)**: The standard Six Sigma quality metric.

### 1.1.4 The Scientific Method in Six Sigma

The scientific method, when applied to Six Sigma, follows a structured sequence:

1. **Observation** of process behavior
2. **Question Formulation** regarding root causes
3. **Hypothesis Development** about cause-effect relationships
4. **Experimentation and Data Collection** through designed tests
5. **Analysis and Conclusion** based on statistical evidence
6. **Implementation and Standardization** of validated improvements

This mirrors the DMAIC structure, making Six Sigma essentially an industrial-scale application of the scientific method.

### 1.1.5 Levels of Involvement in Six Sigma

| Role | Responsibility | Involvement Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Champion** | Strategic oversight, resource allocation | High (Strategic) |
| **Master Black Belt** | Mentoring, advanced statistics | High (Tactical) |
| **Black Belt** | Project leadership, full-time improvement | Very High (Project) |
| **Green Belt** | Part-time improvement, team support | Moderate (Team) |
| **Yellow Belt** | Basic awareness, data collection | Low (Supportive) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that **scientific knowledge and involvement are not independent**; they form a **feedback loop** where scientific findings guide involvement priorities, and involvement expands the knowledge base through collaboration.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 The Knowledge Hierarchy (DIKW Pyramid) in Six Sigma

Six Sigma scientific knowledge progresses through four cognitive tiers:

- **Data (D)**: Raw observations, measurements, counts. Example: 1,247 customer complaints logged in Q1.
- **Information (I)**: Contextualized data with meaning. Example: 73% of complaints relate to delivery delays.
- **Knowledge (K)**: Patterns and relationships extracted from information. Example: Delivery delays correlate with monsoon season logistics.
- **Wisdom (W)**: Actionable understanding guiding decisions. Example: Pre-positioning inventory before June reduces delays by 40%.

### 2.1.2 The Involvement Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Kano Model Integration:** The Kano Model categorizes customer requirements into:
> - **Must-Be Quality (Basic)**: Expected features; absence causes dissatisfaction.
> - **One-Dimensional Quality (Performance)**: Satisfaction is proportional to fulfillment.
> - **Attractive Quality (Excitement)**: Unexpected features that delight customers.
> - **Indifferent Quality**: Neither satisfies nor dissatisfies.
> - **Reverse Quality**: Causes dissatisfaction when present.

Scientific knowledge informs **which Kano category** a CTQ falls into, and involvement ensures the right stakeholders address each category.

### 2.1.3 Statistical Foundation of Scientific Knowledge

Six Sigma scientific knowledge rests on three statistical pillars:

1. **Descriptive Statistics**: Mean ($\mu$), Median, Mode, Standard Deviation ($\sigma$), Range, Variance ($\sigma^2$).
2. **Inferential Statistics**: Confidence intervals, hypothesis testing ($p$-value, $\alpha$-risk, $\beta$-risk), regression analysis.
3. **Process Capability**: $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $\sigma$-level, DPMO.

### 2.1.4 The 1.5 Sigma Shift

A foundational concept in Six Sigma scientific knowledge is the **1.5$\sigma$ shift**, where Motorola's original framework incorporated a long-term mean shift to account for real-world process drift:

$$\text{Long-Term Mean} = \text{Short-Term Mean} + 1.5\sigma$$

This implies that a process nominally operating at 6$\sigma$ in the short term is modeled as operating at 4.5$\sigma$ long-term, corresponding to **3.4 DPMO**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Units / Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process Sigma Level** | $Z = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ or $\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | Capability assessment |
| **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$ | Defects per million | Defect rate quantification |
| **Process Yield** | $Y = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Opportunities}}$ | Percentage (0–100%) | First-pass yield |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Percentage | Multi-stage process yield |
| **Cp Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $\geq 1.33$ acceptable | Process spread vs. tolerance |
| **Cpk Index** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\geq 1.33$ acceptable | Centering + spread |
| **Sigma Level from DPMO** | $\sigma = \Phi^{-1}\left(1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{1{,}000{,}000}\right) + 1.5$ | Continuous | DPMO-to-sigma conversion |
| **VOC-to-CTQ Translation** | $\text{CTQ} = f(\text{VOC, Kano Category})$ | Categorical | Customer requirement mapping |
| **Six Sigma Cost Savings** | $\text{Savings} = \sum (\text{Defect Reduction} \times \text{Cost per Defect})$ | Currency (INR/USD) | Financial justification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The DPMO metric is universally used in manufacturing (automotive, semiconductor, pharmaceutical) and service industries (BPO, banking, healthcare) to benchmark process quality globally.

### 2.2.1 Boundary Conditions

- $C_p \geq C_{pk}$ always (equality implies perfect centering).
- $\sigma$ must be strictly positive.
- DPMO values range from $0$ (perfect) to $1{,}000{,}000$ (every opportunity defective).
- $\Phi^{-1}$ denotes the inverse standard normal CDF (quantile function).

## 2.3 The Involvement-Improvement Causal Chain

Scientific knowledge drives involvement decisions through this chain:

$$\text{VOC Capture} \rightarrow \text{CTQ Identification} \rightarrow \text{SIPOC Mapping} \rightarrow \text{Data Collection} \rightarrow \text{Statistical Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Root Cause Validation} \rightarrow \text{Improvement Implementation} \rightarrow \text{Control Standardization}$$

Each arrow represents an **involvement touchpoint** where specific roles (Black Belts, Green Belts, Champions) contribute domain expertise.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting DPMO to Sigma Level

The conversion from DPMO to a sigma level is foundational to Six Sigma scientific knowledge. Below is the complete derivation.

### Step 1: Define Defect Probability

Let $p$ denote the probability of a defect per opportunity:

$$
p = \frac{\text{DPMO}}{1{,}000{,}000}
$$

### Step 2: Determine the Non-Defect Probability

The probability of conforming (non-defect) per opportunity is:

$$
q = 1 - p
$$

### Step 3: Convert to Z-Score (Short-Term)

Using the inverse standard normal cumulative distribution function $\Phi^{-1}$:

$$
Z_{\text{short}} = \Phi^{-1}(q)
$$

For example, if DPMO = $3.4$ (the Six Sigma target), then:

$$
p = \frac{3.4}{1{,}000{,}000} = 3.4 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
q = 1 - 3.4 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0.9999999966
$$

$$
Z_{\text{short}} = \Phi^{-1}(0.9999999966) \approx 4.5
$$

### Step 4: Apply the 1.5$\sigma$ Shift for Long-Term Performance

$$
Z_{\text{long}} = Z_{\text{short}} + 1.5 = 4.5 + 1.5 = 6.0
$$

This confirms that **3.4 DPMO corresponds to a 6$\sigma$ long-term process capability**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$

### Step 1: Define Upper and Lower Capability

Given specification limits $USL$, $LSL$, process mean $\mu$, and standard deviation $\sigma$:

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \quad C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

### Step 2: $C_{pk}$ as the Minimum

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})
$$

### Step 3: Sigma Level from $C_{pk}$

The short-term sigma level is:

$$
Z_{\text{st}} = 3 \times C_{pk}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- $USL = 110$ mm
- $LSL = 90$ mm
- $\mu = 100$ mm
- $\sigma = 2$ mm

**Computation:**

$$
C_{pu} = \frac{110 - 100}{3 \times 2} = \frac{10}{6} = 1.667
$$

$$
C_{pl} = \frac{100 - 90}{3 \times 2} = \frac{10}{6} = 1.667
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.667, 1.667) = 1.667
$$

Since $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$, the process is **capable and centered**.

### Conversion to DPMO

$$
Z_{\text{st}} = 3 \times 1.667 = 5.0
$$

$$
Z_{\text{long}} = 5.0 + 1.5 = 6.5
$$

$$
p = \Phi(-6.5) \approx 4.0 \times 10^{-11}
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} \approx 0.04
$$

This represents an **excellent** process, characteristic of mature Six Sigma implementations.

## 3.3 Derivation: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

For a process with $n$ sequential stages, each having yield $Y_i$:

$$
\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \cdots \times Y_n
$$

### Example

A 4-stage process has individual yields $Y_1 = 0.98$, $Y_2 = 0.97$, $Y_3 = 0.99$, $Y_4 = 0.96$:

$$
\text{RTY} = 0.98 \times 0.97 \times 0.99 \times 0.96
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
0.98 \times 0.97 = 0.9506
$$

$$
0.9506 \times 0.99 = 0.941094
$$

$$
0.941094 \times 0.96 = 0.903450
$$

$$
\text{RTY} = 90.35\%
$$

This means only **90.35%** of units pass all four stages defect-free, even though each stage individually exceeds 96%.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Knowledge to Involvement

| Scientific Knowledge Element | Primary Statistical Tool | Primary Involver | Decision Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voice of Customer (VOC) | Surveys, Kano Analysis | Marketing + Champion | CTQ Tree |
| Baseline Process Performance | Control Charts, Histograms | Black Belt + Green Belt | Sigma Level |
| Root Cause Identification | Pareto, Fishbone, Regression | Black Belt + SMEs | Validated Causes |
| Improvement Verification | DOE, Hypothesis Testing | Black Belt + Master BB | Optimized Settings |
| Sustainability | SPC, FMEA | Process Owner + Champion | Control Plan |
