## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Quality Vision Statement. How does it differ from a Quality Mission Statement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Quality Vision Statement** is a concise, future-oriented declaration (one or two sentences) that describes *what the organisation aspires to become* with respect to quality over a long horizon of 5–10 years. It is qualitative, inspirational, and owned by the CEO/Board.
>
> A **Quality Mission Statement** defines *what the organisation does, for whom, and how*, answering Drucker's question *"What is our business?"* It is more concrete, identifies stakeholders, and is stable for 3–5 years.
>
> **Key differences:**
> - **Time horizon:** Vision = 5–10 years; Mission = 3–5 years.
> - **Focus:** Vision = aspiration/destination; Mission = purpose and approach.
> - **Owner:** Vision = CEO/Board; Mission = Top Management Committee.
> - **Style:** Vision = evocative; Mission = descriptive and stakeholder-oriented.

*(Award 1 mark for vision definition, 1 mark for mission definition, 1 mark for the differentiation table — marking scheme as per KTU.)*

---

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the SMART framework? Why is it mandatory for Quality Objectives under Six Sigma?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **SMART** is an acronym for **Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound**. It is a five-attribute filter that every Quality Objective must satisfy before deployment.
>
> It is **mandatory** under Six Sigma because:
> 1. Six Sigma is a **data-driven** methodology; an objective without a measurement system (the "M" in SMART) cannot be tracked.
> 2. The **DMAIC Control phase** requires time-stamped targets (the "T" in SMART) to confirm sustained gains.
> 3. CTQ traceability (the "R" in SMART) ensures the project is aligned with the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** and not internally invented.
> 4. The "A" in SMART prevents setting impossible targets that demotivate Black Belts.
> 5. The "S" in SMART eliminates ambiguity, which is the root cause of scope creep in Define phase.

*(Award 1 mark for acronym expansion, 2 marks for the five justifications.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, the question paper provides internal choice between two sub-questions. Both options are given below with full marking-scheme-aligned solutions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four-layer hierarchy of Quality Statements** (Vision, Mission, Policy, Objectives). For each layer, state the owner, time horizon, and a one-line example from a manufacturing context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Six Sigma project at an aluminium extrusion plant achieved a yield improvement from $Y_1 = 0.9993$ to $Y_2 = 0.99966$. Compute (i) the short-term sigma level before and after, and (ii) the percentage reduction in DPMO. Comment on whether the new sigma level satisfies the classical Six Sigma target. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four-Layer Hierarchy (7 marks)**

| Layer | Document | Owner | Time Horizon | Example (Aluminium Plant) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quality **Vision** | CEO / Board | 5–10 years | "To be the most trusted and defect-free aluminium extrusions brand in South Asia by 2030." |
| 2 | Quality **Mission** | Top Mgmt / Quality Council | 3–5 years | "To deliver precision aluminium profiles to automotive and aerospace customers through zero-defect manufacturing, on-time, every time." |
| 3 | Quality **Policy** | Quality Council | 2–3 years | "We commit to comply with ISO 9001:2015 and AS9100, eliminate non-conformances, and continually improve our sigma level by 0.5 each year." |
| 4 | Quality **Objectives** | Process Owners | Quarterly/Annual | "Reduce surface-defect DPMO from 500 to 100 on Line-2 by 31 March 2026." |

The **logical cascade** is: Vision sets the direction → Mission defines the business → Policy commits to principles → Objectives quantify the success. Each layer must be **traceable upward** to its parent.

*(1 mark per layer, 3 marks for traceability explanation. Total 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) Sigma Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Initial DPMO.**

$$
\text{DPMO}_1 = (1 - Y_1) \times 10^{6} = (1 - 0.9993) \times 10^{6} = 0.0007 \times 10^{6} = 700 \;\text{ppm}
$$

**Step 2 — Final DPMO.**

$$
\text{DPMO}_2 = (1 - Y_2) \times 10^{6} = (1 - 0.99966) \times 10^{6} = 0.00034 \times 10^{6} = 340 \;\text{ppm}
$$

**Step 3 — Short-term sigma level (initial).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{0,1} &= \Phi^{-1}(0.9993) \\
        &= 3.20 \\
L_1     &= z_{0,1} + 1.5 \\
        &= 3.20 + 1.5 \\
        &= 4.70 \;\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Short-term sigma level (final).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{0,2} &= \Phi^{-1}(0.99966) \\
        &= 3.40 \\
L_2     &= z_{0,2} + 1.5 \\
        &= 3.40 + 1.5 \\
        &= 4.90 \;\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage reduction in DPMO.**

$$
\Delta\,\text{DPMO}\,\% = \frac{\text{DPMO}_1 - \text{DPMO}_2}{\text{DPMO}_1} \times 100\% = \frac{700 - 340}{700} \times 100\% = 51.43\%
$$

**Step 6 — Comment on Six Sigma conformance.**

The final sigma level is $L_2 = 4.90\sigma$, which is **below** the classical Six Sigma target of $6\sigma$ (i.e., 3.4 DPMO). The plant has achieved a 0.20-sigma improvement and a 51.43% defect reduction, but the journey is not complete — the next phase of DMAIC or a new project must target another 1.10σ improvement to reach Six Sigma status.

*(1 mark DPMO₁, 1 mark DPMO₂, 1.5 marks L₁, 1.5 marks L₂, 1 mark percentage, 1 mark comment. Total 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2 requirements** for Quality Policy. How does a well-formed policy differ from a generic motivational slogan? Provide a comparative table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive component firm declares the following four quality objectives for FY 2024–25. Audit each against the SMART framework, compute the **SMART coverage rate**, and rewrite the non-SMART objectives in SMART form. **(7 marks)**

- $O_1$: "Improve customer satisfaction."
- $O_2$: "Reduce warranty claims to 0.8% by 31 March 2025."
- $O_3$: "Train all employees on Six Sigma."
- $O_4$: "Achieve first-pass yield of 99.2% on the CNC line by Q2 2025."

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2 (7 marks)**

The standard requires that the Quality Policy:

1. Is **appropriate** to the purpose and context of the organisation.
2. Provides a **framework** for setting quality objectives.
3. Includes a **commitment to satisfy** applicable customer, statutory, and regulatory requirements.
4. Includes a **commitment to continual improvement** of the QMS.

**Comparative table (slogan vs. policy):**

| Attribute | Motivational Slogan | ISO 9001:2015 Quality Policy |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Marketing / PR dept | Quality Council + Top Mgmt |
| Measurability | None | Inherently measurable via linked objectives |
| Compliance binding | No | Yes (auditable) |
| Review cycle | Years / never | At least annually in MRM |
| Continual improvement | Absent | Mandatory commitment |
| Stakeholder commitment | Vague | Specific (customer + statutory) |
| Examples | "Quality is our motto" | "We commit to deliver zero-defect components, comply with IATF 16949, and improve our sigma level by 0.5 annually." |

*(3 marks for the 4 sub-clauses, 4 marks for the comparative table with explanation. Total 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) SMART Audit and Rewrite (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Audit each objective.**

| Objective | S | M | A | R | T | SMART? | Issue |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $O_1$ "Improve customer satisfaction" | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | No | No metric, no deadline |
| $O_2$ "Reduce warranty claims to 0.8% by 31 Mar 2025" | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **Yes** | — |
| $O_3$ "Train all employees on Six Sigma" | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | No | No metric (% trained), no deadline |
| $O_4$ "Achieve FPY of 99.2% on CNC line by Q2 2025" | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **Yes** | — |

**Step 2 — Coverage rate.**

$$
\mathcal{C} = \frac{2}{4} \times 100\% = 50\%
$$

**Step 3 — SMART rewrites.**

- $O_1'{:}$ *"Increase the Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI, measured by quarterly survey) from 82% to 92% by 31 December 2024."* (Now: Specific ✓, Measurable ✓, Achievable ✓, Relevant ✓, Time-bound ✓)
- $O_3'{:}$ *"Train 100% of the production workforce (250 employees) on Six Sigma Yellow Belt curriculum and certify at least 80% by 30 September 2024."* (Now: Specific ✓, Measurable ✓, Achievable ✓, Relevant ✓, Time-bound ✓)

**Step 4 — Revised coverage rate.**

$$
\mathcal{C}_{\text{revised}} = \frac{4}{4} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

*(1 mark audit table, 1 mark coverage, 2 marks rewrites, 1 mark revised coverage, 2 marks for proper SMART justification. Total 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Vision and Objective (–2 marks):** A vision that contains a number or a deadline is *automatically* an objective, not a vision. Examiners will deduct.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift (–1 mark):** The classic Motorola convention $L = z_0 + 1.5$ is *not* optional in Indian university papers. If you write $L = \Phi^{-1}(Y)$ directly, you lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting units (–1 mark):** Always state *ppm* for DPMO, *σ* for sigma level, *INR* for COPQ.
> 4. **Missing owner of each statement layer (–1 mark):** Examiners explicitly test whether you know *who* signs each layer.
> 5. **Using "we" in vision (–0.5 mark):** A vision is organisational, not personal. Use "the organisation shall…" or a third-person aspiration.
> 6. **Not tracing CTQ to objective (–1 mark):** Every DMAIC project answer must close the loop back to the originating Quality Objective.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 20 Rapid-Revision Bullets — Quality Statements (Module 2, PEMRT752)**
>
> 1. **Four layers:** Vision → Mission → Policy → Objectives (top-down cascade).
> 2. **Vision** is qualitative, future-oriented, 5–10 years, CEO-owned.
> 3. **Mission** answers *"What is our business?"* — 3–5 years, Top Mgmt-owned.
> 4. **Policy** is the formal, auditable commitment per **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 5.2**.
> 5. **Objectives** must be **SMART**: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
> 6. The **CTQ tree** converts customer verbatims into measurable specifications.
> 7. **Hoshin Kanri** (Policy Deployment) cascades objectives to departments.
> 8. The **1.5σ shift** is the Motorola convention for long-term drift.
> 9. **Six Sigma target** = **3.4 DPMO** = **99.99966% yield**.
> 10. **Process capability targets:** $C_p \geq 1.67$, $C_{pk} \geq 1.50$.
> 11. **Yield-to-sigma formula:** $L = \Phi^{-1}(Y) + 1.5$.
> 12. **DPMO formula:** $\text{DPMO} = (1-Y) \times 10^{6}$.
> 13. **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure.
> 14. **Feigenbaum 1:10 rule:** ₹1 in prevention saves ₹10 in failure cost.
> 15. **SMART Coverage Rate** $\mathcal{C}$ = % of objectives satisfying all five SMART criteria.
> 16. **Quality Vision must not contain numbers or deadlines.**
> 17. **Quality Objectives must contain numbers, units, baselines, and deadlines.**
> 18. **DMAIC projects are *derived* from objectives, not invented by Black Belts.**
> 19. **Management Review Meeting (MRM)** reviews objectives at least quarterly.
> 20. **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** closes the loop from Control Plan back to objectives.

> **End of Module 2 — Quality Statements Notes**
> *Mapped to KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcomes: CO2 (Formulate quality statements aligned with organisational strategy), CO3 (Apply DMAIC Define-phase tools).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customer Satisfaction - Customer Perception of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Customer Satisfaction - Customer Perception of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)** is a measure of the degree to which a product, service, or overall experience meets or exceeds the expectations of a customer. In the context of Six Sigma (DMAIC framework), it is quantified as the difference between the customer's **Expected Quality (EQ)** and the **Perceived Quality (PQ)** of a delivered offering.

**Customer Perception of Quality (CPQ)** is the subjective mental image, impression, or belief a customer forms about a product or service based on their individual needs, past experiences, brand reputation, marketing communication, and the gap between what was promised and what was actually delivered.

Mathematically, the foundational relationship used across the Six Sigma body of knowledge is expressed as:

$$\text{Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)} = \text{Perceived Quality (PQ)} - \text{Expected Quality (EQ)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT752 Module 2):** Every QFD, Kano, and VOC study in Six Sigma ultimately rolls back to the master equation **CSAT = PQ − EQ**. If PQ ≥ EQ → the customer is *satisfied*; if PQ < EQ → the customer is *dissatisfied* and a *gap* (σ_variance) exists in the process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Restaurant Dinner" Analogy**
> Imagine you walk into a restaurant that advertises a "5-Star Gourmet Experience" (this sets your **Expected Quality**). You sit down, the waiter is rude, the soup arrives cold, and the steak is overcooked. Your **Perceived Quality** plummets. Even though you technically ate a meal, your **Customer Satisfaction** is deeply negative because *PQ ≪ EQ*. Now imagine the same restaurant quietly delivers a flawless, unforgettable 7-course meal. Your PQ *exceeds* your EQ, and you leave a glowing Google review — that is *delight*. Six Sigma exists to engineer the conditions under which PQ ≥ EQ, consistently.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Anchors

- **Voice of Customer (VOC):** The structured capture of customer requirements, expectations, preferences, and dislikes, expressed *in the customer's own words*.
- **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ):** The specific, measurable characteristics of a product or service that are critical to achieving customer satisfaction.
- **Kano Model:** A classification framework that sorts customer preferences into Basic, Performance, and Excitement features.
- **Gap Analysis:** The systematic identification of the difference between customer expectations and the actual delivered performance.

The **Customer Perception of Quality** in Six Sigma literature is governed by the seminal *Parasuraman–Zeithaml–Berry* **SERVQUAL** framework, which identifies **5 key dimensions** of perceived service quality:

| # | SERVQUAL Dimension | Plain English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tangibles** | Physical facilities, equipment, and personnel appearance |
| 2 | **Reliability** | Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately |
| 3 | **Responsiveness** | Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service |
| 4 | **Assurance** | Knowledge and courtesy of employees, and their ability to inspire trust |
| 5 | **Empathy** | Caring, individualized attention provided to customers |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember SERVQUAL:** "*The **R**eal **R**esponse **A**ssures **E**mpathy in **T**angible form*" → **T, R, R, A, E**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Gaps Model of Service Quality (Parasuraman et al., 1985)

Customer dissatisfaction is rarely random; it is the *symptom* of one or more structural gaps inside the service provider's organization. The Five Gaps Model is the analytical engine of Six Sigma's *Define* and *Measure* phases.

1. **Gap 1 — The Knowledge Gap:** Difference between *what customers expect* and *what management thinks they expect*. Caused by insufficient market research and upward communication failures.
2. **Gap 2 — The Standards Gap:** Difference between *management's perception* of customer expectations and the *service quality specifications* actually established.
3. **Gap 3 — The Delivery Gap:** Difference between *service specifications* and the *actual service performance* delivered by front-line employees.
4. **Gap 4 — The Communication Gap:** Difference between *service delivered* and *what is communicated externally* (advertising/promises) to the customer.
5. **Gap 5 — The Customer Gap:** The cumulative result of Gaps 1–4; it is the difference between *expected service* and *perceived service* — i.e., the actual CSAT score.

The unifying closure equation is:

$$\text{Gap}_5 = f(\text{Gap}_1, \text{Gap}_2, \text{Gap}_3, \text{Gap}_4)$$

Where $f$ is a directional function indicating that **any positive gap upstream cascades into Gap 5** unless explicitly closed.

### 2.2 Kano Model — Classification of Customer Needs

Dr. Noriaki Kano's 1984 model classifies product/service attributes into three categories based on their ability to cause satisfaction or dissatisfaction:

| Kano Category | Effect on Satisfaction when Absent | Effect on Satisfaction when Present |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic / Must-Be (M)** | Extreme dissatisfaction | No measurable increase (taken for granted) |
| **Performance / One-Dimensional (O)** | Dissatisfaction | Linear increase in satisfaction |
| **Excitement / Attractive (A)** | No dissatisfaction | High delight (exponential word-of-mouth) |
| Indifferent (I) | No effect | No effect |
| Reverse (R) | Delight | Dissatisfaction |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Customer Satisfaction Index | $\text{CSAT} = PQ - EQ$ | Dimensionless score |
| Kano Net Promoter Logic | $\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D$ | Where P = Promoters, D = Detractors |
| VOC-to-CTQ Tree (QFD Step) | $\text{CTQ}_i = f(\text{VOC}_j)$ | Functional dependency |
| Gap Closure Function | $\text{Gap}_5 = \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i \cdot \text{Gap}_i$ | Weighted sum, weights sum to 1 |
| Six Sigma DPMO | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O}$ | Defects per million opportunities |
| Rolled Throughput Yield | $Y_{rt} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Product of stage yields |
| Customer Effort Score (CES) | $\text{CES} = \text{Avg}(\text{Effort Rating 1--7})$ | Lower is better |
| SERVQUAL Mean Score | $\text{SQ} = \dfrac{1}{5}\sum_{d=1}^{5} (P_d - E_d)$ | Per-dimension expectation minus perception |
| Standard Normal CSAT Z-Score | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Used in voice-of-customer normality tests |
| AHP Pairwise Priority Vector | $w_i = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\dfrac{a_{ij}}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} a_{kj}}$ | Normalized eigenvector approximation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The equation $\text{CSAT} = PQ - EQ$ is the *single most expected* formula on a Six Sigma exam paper. Memorize it, state it, and apply it in at least one numerical question.

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering Projects

In a Six Sigma project for a manufacturing line, **CTQ flow-down** from VOC happens like this:

1. VOC: "I want my car to start every morning without a hitch." (Customer's *need*)
2. CTQ: *Mean Time to First Failure ≥ 36 months* (engineered metric)
3. CTQ Operational Definition: "Time until first no-start event during ambient temperatures between 5°C and 40°C."

This translation of fuzzy perception into a precise measurable is the *soul* of customer satisfaction work in Six Sigma.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From SERVQUAL Raw Score to the Customer Perception Index (CPI)

The SERVQUAL instrument is administered as a paired-question survey: each attribute $d \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ is rated twice, once for **Expectation ($E_d$)** and once for **Perception ($P_d$)**, both on a **7-point Likert scale** (1 = strongly disagree, 7 = strongly agree).

**Step 1 — Define the per-dimension gap:**

For each SERVQUAL dimension $d$, the perceived gap is the algebraic difference:

$$G_d = P_d - E_d$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate the five dimensions into the composite SERVQUAL score:**

Since each dimension is assumed to contribute equally to overall perception in the unweighted (classical) form:

$$SQ_{unweighted} = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{d=1}^{5} G_d$$

**Step 3 — Apply customer-prioritized weights $w_d$ (from AHP or regression):**

When the firm has derived dimension weights through Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) such that $\sum_{d=1}^{5} w_d = 1$, the weighted SERVQUAL score is:

$$SQ_{weighted} = \sum_{d=1}^{5} w_d \cdot G_d$$

**Step 4 — Normalize to a 0–100 Customer Perception Index (CPI):**

Because Likert values range across $[-6, +6]$, the weighted gap $SQ_{weighted}$ must be rescaled:

$$\text{CPI} = \left( \frac{SQ_{weighted} + 6}{12} \right) \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation thresholds:**

| CPI Range | Strategic Interpretation |
|---|---|
| 90 – 100 | **World-class loyalty**, exponential referral rate |
| 75 – 89 | **Healthy satisfaction**, defend with continuous improvement |
| 50 – 74 | **Vulnerable**, customer defection likely within 12 months |
| 25 – 49 | **Acute dissatisfaction**, immediate DMAIC intervention required |
| 0 – 24 | **Reputation crisis**, possible product recall or service shutdown |

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement:** A Six Sigma Black Belt project at a Kerala-based telecom operator collects the following SERVQUAL data from 1 customer (a simplified example for illustration):

| Dimension $d$ | Expectation $E_d$ | Perception $P_d$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Tangibles | 6 | 4 |
| 2. Reliability | 7 | 5 |
| 3. Responsiveness | 6 | 6 |
| 4. Assurance | 7 | 5 |
| 5. Empathy | 5 | 3 |

AHP-derived weights are $w = (0.10, 0.35, 0.20, 0.25, 0.10)$.

**Step A — Compute per-dimension gaps $G_d = P_d - E_d$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_1 &= 4 - 6 = -2 \\
G_2 &= 5 - 7 = -2 \\
G_3 &= 6 - 6 = 0 \\
G_4 &= 5 - 7 = -2 \\
G_5 &= 3 - 5 = -2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Compute the unweighted SERVQUAL score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SQ_{unweighted} &= \frac{1}{5}\big((-2) + (-2) + 0 + (-2) + (-2)\big) \\
&= \frac{1}{5}\big(-8\big) \\
&= -1.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Compute the weighted SERVQUAL score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SQ_{weighted} &= (0.10)(-2) + (0.35)(-2) + (0.20)(0) + (0.25)(-2) + (0.10)(-2) \\
&= -0.20 - 0.70 + 0 - 0.50 - 0.20 \\
&= -1.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Normalize to CPI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPI} &= \left( \frac{-1.60 + 6}{12} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \left( \frac{4.40}{12} \right) \times 100 \\
&\approx 36.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — Strategic verdict:**

A CPI of **36.67** places this telecom operator in the *acute dissatisfaction* zone (25–49). The Black Belt must launch a DMAIC project, prioritizing the **Reliability** and **Assurance** dimensions since they carry the highest AHP weights (0.35 and 0.25) and contribute the largest negative gaps.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Linking VOC → CTQ → Process Metric

| VOC Statement (Customer Words) | Kano Class | CTQ (Engineered) | Lower Spec Limit (LSL) | Upper Spec Limit (USL) | Process Sigma |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| "My ATM should always dispense cash." | Basic (M) | Cash-dispense success rate | 99.9% | 100% | 4.5σ |
| "Call centre should answer in 3 rings." | Performance (O) | Average speed of answer (ASA) | 0 s | 20 s | 4.0σ |
| "Bank app should feel premium." | Excitement (A) | UX NPS score | 50 | 100 | 3.5σ |
| "Cheque book delivered in 7 days." | Basic (M) | Lead time (days) | 1 | 7 | 4.0σ |
| "Manager knows my name." | Excitement (A) | Personalized service index | 70% | 100% | 3.0σ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** When a 14-mark question asks "Explain the conversion of VOC to CTQ", structure the answer as the **5-row analytical matrix above** — definitions, Kano class, CTQ name, units, and a 1-line example. Examiners reward this structured tabular answer with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.3 Kano Model Decision Flow


### 4.4 DMAIC — Customer Satisfaction Hooks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D[DEFINE VOC CTQ Project Charter] --> M[MEASURE SIPOC Kano SERVQUAL]
    M --> A[ANALYZE Pareto Fishbone 5 Whys]

    I --> C[CONTROL Control Plan SPC]

    style D fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style M fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style I fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style C fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMRT752]**
*Define Customer Perception of Quality. List any four SERVQUAL dimensions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Customer Perception of Quality is the subjective assessment a customer makes about a product or service, based on the comparison between what was expected and what was actually experienced. It is a mental construct, not an objective physical property, and is shaped by personal needs, prior experiences, and external communications. The five SERVQUAL dimensions, of which any four are: (1) **Tangibles** — physical evidence and appearance, (2) **Reliability** — dependable and accurate performance, (3) **Responsiveness** — promptness of service, (4) **Assurance** — staff knowledge inspiring trust, and (5) **Empathy** — caring individualized attention. *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for 4 dimensions with 0.5 each.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
*State the fundamental equation linking Customer Satisfaction to Expected and Perceived Quality. Briefly explain the three Kano categories.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The fundamental relationship is $\text{CSAT} = PQ - EQ$, where $PQ$ is Perceived Quality and $EQ$ is Expected Quality. If $PQ \geq EQ$, satisfaction is positive; if $PQ < EQ$, dissatisfaction results. The three Kano categories are: (i) **Basic (Must-Be)** features whose absence causes extreme dissatisfaction but whose presence is taken for granted (e.g., a car that starts); (ii) **Performance (One-Dimensional)** features where satisfaction scales linearly with performance (e.g., fuel efficiency); and (iii) **Excitement (Attractive)** features whose presence causes disproportionate delight and word-of-mouth (e.g., heated seats in an economy car). *(1 mark for formula + 2 marks for Kano explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module internal choice is provided. Students must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B, not both. Each carries 14 marks and is split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — "Five Gaps + Numerical SERVQUAL"

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain in detail the Five Gaps Model of Service Quality. Illustrate how each gap contributes to the final customer perception gap (Gap 5).*

**Model Answer (Part a — 7 marks):**
The Five Gaps Model, developed by Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry in 1985, is a diagnostic framework that traces the root causes of customer dissatisfaction. **Gap 1 — Knowledge Gap** is the difference between customer expectations and management's perception of those expectations; it is closed by better market research and upward communication. **Gap 2 — Standards Gap** is the difference between management perception and the formal service-quality specifications; it is closed by translating perceptions into measurable standards. **Gap 3 — Delivery Gap** is the difference between specifications and actual front-line service delivery; it is closed through training, empowerment, and process standardization. **Gap 4 — Communication Gap** is the difference between service delivered and the promises made in external advertising; over-promising inflates expectations and creates Gap 5. Finally, **Gap 5 — Service Quality Gap** is the cumulative result:

$$\text{Gap}_5 = \sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i \cdot \text{Gap}_i$$

The model prescribes that **closing the upstream gaps is the only reliable way to close Gap 5**, because any positive gap in 1, 2, 3, or 4 *cascades* into a larger customer-perceived gap. *(7 marks: 1 mark per gap × 4 + 1 mark for Gap 5 equation + 1 mark for cascade logic + 1 mark for conclusion.)*

**(b) [7 marks]** *A retail bank collected SERVQUAL data on five dimensions. Compute (i) the unweighted SERVQUAL score, (ii) the weighted SERVQUAL score using AHP weights $w = (0.15, 0.30, 0.15, 0.25, 0.15)$, and (iii) the Customer Perception Index (CPI) on a 0–100 scale. Interpret the result.*

| Dimension $d$ | $E_d$ | $P_d$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Tangibles | 5 | 4 |
| 2. Reliability | 7 | 4 |
| 3. Responsiveness | 6 | 5 |
| 4. Assurance | 6 | 4 |
| 5. Empathy | 5 | 3 |

**Model Answer (Part b — 7 marks):**

*Step (i) — Per-dimension gaps:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_1 &= 4 - 5 = -1 \\
G_2 &= 4 - 7 = -3 \\
G_3 &= 5 - 6 = -1 \\
G_4 &= 4 - 6 = -2 \\
G_5 &= 3 - 5 = -2
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step (ii) — Unweighted SERVQUAL:*

$$SQ_{unweighted} = \frac{1}{5}\big((-1) + (-3) + (-1) + (-2) + (-2)\big) = \frac{-9}{5} = -1.80$$

*Step (iii) — Weighted SERVQUAL:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
SQ_{weighted} &= (0.15)(-1) + (0.30)(-3) + (0.15)(-1) + (0.25)(-2) + (0.15)(-2) \\
&= -0.15 - 0.90 - 0.15 - 0.50 - 0.30 \\
&= -2.00
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step (iv) — Customer Perception Index:*

$$\text{CPI} = \left( \frac{-2.00 + 6}{12} \right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{4}{12}\right) \times 100 \approx 33.33$$

*Step (v) — Interpretation:* A CPI of **33.33** places the bank in the **acute dissatisfaction** zone (25–49), implying customer defection is imminent. The DMAIC project should prioritize **Reliability** (largest negative gap, highest weight) and **Assurance** as the primary improvement levers. *(7 marks: 1 mark each for Steps i, ii, iii; 2 marks for Step iv CPI calculation; 1 mark for interpretation.)*

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — "Kano + VOC/CTQ Translation"

**(a) [7 marks]** *Discuss the Kano Model in detail. How does it help a Six Sigma team prioritize features for maximum customer satisfaction?*

**Model Answer (Part a — 7 marks):**
The Kano Model, developed by Professor Noriaki Kano in 1984, classifies product/service features into four primary categories based on their ability to generate satisfaction or dissatisfaction. **Basic (Must-Be) attributes** are threshold requirements — their absence causes intense dissatisfaction, but their presence yields no incremental satisfaction (e.g., safe wiring in a car). **Performance (One-Dimensional) attributes** follow a linear relationship: the better the performance, the higher the satisfaction (e.g., mileage, processing speed). **Excitement (Attractive) attributes** are unexpected delights that disproportionately drive loyalty and word-of-mouth (e.g., gesture-controlled infotainment). **Indifferent attributes** do not affect satisfaction in either direction and should be de-prioritized to save cost.

In a Six Sigma DMAIC project, the Kano Model is used during the *Define* and *Measure* phases as follows: (1) **First**, eliminate failures in Basic attributes — these are non-negotiable prerequisites. (2) **Second**, optimize Performance attributes to push the process mean toward the Best-in-Class target. (3) **Third**, selectively invest in Excitement attributes to differentiate and delight. (4) **Fourth**, prune Indifferent attributes to reduce process complexity, waste, and variation. This prioritized roadmap ensures that the team's finite improvement budget is deployed where it yields the highest *customer-perception delta* per unit cost. *(7 marks: 1.5 marks per Kano category × 4 = 6 marks + 1 mark for DMAIC application.)*

**(b) [7 marks]** *A restaurant chain captures the following VOC statements from focus groups. Translate each into a CTQ with units and an operational definition. Classify each CTQ into its Kano category.*

| VOC Statement |
|---|
| (i) "The food must be hot when served." |
| (ii) "I want more vegetarian options on the menu." |
| (iii) "The waiter should remember my name." |
| (iv) "The bill should be accurate to the last paisa." |

**Model Answer (Part b — 7 marks):**

| # | VOC | CTQ | Units | Operational Definition | Kano Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Food must be hot when served | Plate temperature at service | °C | Temperature measured at table-edge within 30 s of placement, using calibrated IR thermometer | **Basic (M)** — absence ruins the meal, presence is taken for granted |
| (ii) | More vegetarian options | Vegetarian dish variety | Count | Number of distinct veg main-course items on the menu, refreshed quarterly | **Performance (O)** — more variety linearly raises satisfaction |
| (iii) | Waiter remembers my name | Personalized greeting rate | % of repeat customers | Percentage of repeat customers greeted by first name within 5 s of seating | **Excitement (A)** — unexpected, drives loyalty and referrals |
| (iv) | Bill accurate to last paisa | Billing error rate | Errors per 1000 bills | Number of bills with ≥1 incorrect line item per 1000 generated | **Basic (M)** — billing errors are non-negotiable defects |

The Six Sigma team should (a) eliminate any current failures in (i) and (iv) first, (b) benchmark the chain's vegetarian count against Best-in-Class for (ii), and (c) pilot a CRM-based name-recognition program for (iii) as a delight differentiator. *(7 marks: 1.5 marks per VOC row × 4 = 6 marks + 1 mark for prioritization roadmap.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** write "Customer Satisfaction = Customer Perception" — examiners specifically test whether you can distinguish the *equation* (CSAT = PQ − EQ) from its *components*. Loss: 2 marks.
> 2. **Do not** confuse **Gap 4 (Communication Gap)** with **Gap 5 (Service Quality Gap)**. Gap 4 is *internal* (between delivery and external messaging); Gap 5 is *external* (the customer's actual experience). Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Do not** forget the **operational definition** in CTQ translations. Writing "CTQ: Hot food" gets 0.5 marks; writing "CTQ: Plate temperature ≥ 65°C at the table, measured by IR thermometer within 30 s of placement" gets full 1.5 marks.
> 4. **Do not** skip the **weight normalization check** in AHP/SERVQUAL questions. Always state $\sum w_d = 1$ before computing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Master equation:** $\text{CSAT} = PQ - EQ$. Memorize verbatim.
- 🔑 **SERVQUAL has 5 dimensions:** Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy (TREA-E order).
- 🔑 **Five Gaps Model:** Gap 1 = Knowledge, Gap 2 = Standards, Gap 3 = Delivery, Gap 4 = Communication, Gap 5 = Service Quality (cumulative).
- 🔑 **Kano's three primary classes:** Basic (M), Performance (O), Excitement (A). Basic → fix first, Performance → optimize, Excitement → differentiate.
- 🔑 **VOC is the customer's voice** (qualitative, often emotional). **CTQ is the engineered metric** (quantitative, measurable, with LSL/USL).
- 🔑 **KPOV vs NPOV:** Key Process Output Variable (what the process produces) vs Noise/Non-controllable Process Output Variable (what the customer sees).
- 🔑 **CPI normalization** maps $[-6, +6]$ Likert gap onto $[0, 100]$ via $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{SQ + 6}{12} \times 100$.
- 🔑 **AHP weights must sum to 1.** Always state this as a verification step in numericals.
- 🔑 **CSAT in DMAIC:** Defined in *Define*, Measured in *Measure*, Root-caused in *Analyze*, Improved in *Improve*, Sustained in *Control*.
- 🔑 **For 14-mark KTU answers**, structure as: (i) Theory, (ii) Equation, (iii) Numerical computation, (iv) Interpretation/Recommendation.
- 🔑 **For 3-mark KTU answers**, structure as: (i) One-sentence definition, (ii) Bullet-point expansion, (iii) A concrete example or real-world hook.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customer Retention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Customer Retention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Customer Retention** is defined as the set of strategic activities, processes, and Six-Sigma-aligned methodologies employed by an organization to reduce customer defection (churn), maximize the **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)**, and sustain long-term mutually beneficial relationships with existing customers. In the context of **Six Sigma's DMAIC framework**, customer retention is treated as a **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** output variable, where the "Voice of the Customer (VOC)" drives the **Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control** cycle aimed at keeping defect-free, loyal customers.

In Lean Six Sigma terminology, customer retention is often expressed through the metric **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)**, which measures the percentage of customers an organization retains over a specified period. It is the mathematical inverse of the **Customer Churn Rate (CCR)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Customer Retention like a Marriage, Not a First Date.**
> Acquiring a new customer is like asking someone on a first date — it costs time, money, and effort. Retention is keeping the relationship healthy long-term so neither partner walks away. In Six Sigma, we treat each "break-up" (customer leaving) as a **Defect (DPMO)** that we must statistically eliminate.

Imagine you own a small coffee shop. Every morning, 100 customers walk in. If 85 of them return the next morning, your **Daily Retention Rate is 85%**. The 15 who didn't return are your **"defects"** for the day. Over a year, this 15% daily churn could wipe out your entire customer base. Six Sigma asks: *How do we shrink that 15% to near 0% using data, not guesswork?*

That is the essence of Customer Retention in Six Sigma.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** | The measurable characteristic that defines customer satisfaction |
| **VOC (Voice of the Customer)** | Captured customer expectations/preferences |
| **Churn** | The rate at which customers stop doing business |
| **CLV** | Customer Lifetime Value — total revenue expected from a customer |
| **NPS** | Net Promoter Score — loyalty proxy metric |
| **DPMO** | Defects Per Million Opportunities — Six Sigma defect unit |

---

### 1.4 Why Customer Retention Matters in Six Sigma

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A core principle taught in Module 2 is the **Pareto (80/20) Reality of Customers** — typically, **80% of a company's revenue comes from just 20% of its existing (retained) customers**. Acquiring a new customer is **5 to 7 times costlier** than retaining an existing one (institute-certified industry benchmark).

In Six Sigma, reducing variation in the **customer experience** leads directly to higher retention, which in turn lowers process defects, reduces waste (Lean alignment), and boosts the **process sigma level** of the business.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Retention Pyramid

Customer Retention in Six Sigma is structured as a hierarchy. Each layer strengthens the one below it.

1. **Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)** — *Foundation layer*. A customer must first be satisfied with the product/service quality.
2. **Customer Loyalty** — Built upon repeated positive experiences and trust.
3. **Customer Retention** — The behavioral outcome: the customer keeps buying.
4. **Customer Advocacy** — The peak: retained customers become promoters (NPS).

---

### 2.2 The Six Sigma DMAIC Alignment with Customer Retention

| DMAIC Phase | Application to Customer Retention |
|---|---|
| **Define** | Identify the retention problem. Define CTQs (e.g., "reduce churn by 20% in 6 months"). Capture VOC. |
| **Measure** | Collect baseline data: current CRR, CCR, CLV, NPS, CSAT scores. Establish DPMO. |
| **Analyze** | Use Pareto, Fishbone (Ishikawa), and Regression to find root causes of churn. |
| **Improve** | Pilot solutions (loyalty programs, service redesign, defect elimination). Validate with statistical tools. |
| **Control** | Lock improvements using **SPC control charts**, SOPs, and ongoing VOC monitoring. |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Formulas — Memorize These.**

$$
\text{CRR} = \left( \frac{E - N}{S} \right) \times 100
$$

Where:
- $\text{CRR}$ = Customer Retention Rate (%)
- $E$ = Customers at end of period
- $N$ = New customers acquired during period
- $S$ = Customers at start of period

$$
\text{CCR} = \frac{\text{Customers Lost During Period}}{\text{Customers at Start of Period}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(R_t - C_t) \cdot r_t}{(1 + d)^t}
$$

Where:
- $R_t$ = Revenue from customer in period $t$
- $C_t$ = Cost of serving customer in period $t$
- $r_t$ = Retention probability in period $t$
- $d$ = Discount rate
- $T$ = Time horizon

$$
\text{NPS} = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities} \times \text{Number of Units}} \times 1{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
\sigma\text{-Level} = Z + 1.5 \quad (\text{with short-term shift})
$$

For Six Sigma quality, $\sigma = 6.0$ which corresponds to **3.4 DPMO** — meaning only 3.4 customers out of every million are "defects" (churn).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Telecom Industry**: Airtel/Jio use Six Sigma retention models to reduce SIM churn.
- **Banking**: SBI uses CRR analytics to retain high-value fixed-deposit customers.
- **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart)**: Uses CLV and NPS-driven retention loops.
- **Manufacturing**: B2B OEMs retain clients by driving **Zero-Defect (6σ)** deliveries.

---

### 2.5 The Three Pillars of Customer Retention

1. **Value Delivery** — Consistent quality (Six Sigma's job).
2. **Trust & Relationship** — Emotional/transactional loyalty.
3. **Switching Cost Engineering** — Making it inconvenient/involuntary to leave (loyalty cards, integrations, subscriptions).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example 1 — CRR Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A telecom company had **12,000 customers at the start of Q1 2024**. During the quarter, they lost **800 customers** to churn but acquired **2,500 new customers**. At the end of Q1, they have **13,700 customers**. Calculate the **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** and the **Customer Churn Rate (CCR)**.

---

#### Step 1 — Identify Variables

$$
S = 12{,}000, \quad N = 2{,}500, \quad E = 13{,}700
$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the CRR Formula

$$
\text{CRR} = \left( \frac{E - N}{S} \right) \times 100
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\text{CRR} = \left( \frac{13{,}700 - 2{,}500}{12{,}000} \right) \times 100
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Numerator

$$
\text{Numerator} = 13{,}700 - 2{,}500 = 11{,}200
$$

#### Step 4 — Divide and Convert to Percentage

$$
\text{CRR} = \left( \frac{11{,}200}{12{,}000} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{CRR} = 0.9333 \times 100 = 93.33\%
$$

#### Step 5 — Compute CCR

$$
\text{CCR} = \frac{800}{12{,}000} \times 100 = 6.67\%
$$

#### Step 6 — Verification (CRR + New Customers should equal End)

$$
\text{Retained} = 93.33\% \text{ of } 12{,}000 = 11{,}200
$$

$$
11{,}200 + 2{,}500 = 13{,}700 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Answer:** CRR = **93.33%**, CCR = **6.67%**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 2 — DPMO and Sigma Level for Churn

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A SaaS company serves **500,000 customers/month**. They record **750 churn complaints** in a month. Each customer interaction has **5 opportunities** for a defect (billing, support, uptime, UI, communication). Calculate DPMO and identify the **Sigma Level**.

---

#### Step 1 — Apply the DPMO Formula

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Opportunities} \times \text{Units}} \times 1{,}000{,}000
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Total Opportunities

$$
\text{Total Opportunities} = 500{,}000 \times 5 = 2{,}500{,}000
$$

#### Step 3 — Plug Into Formula

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{750}{2{,}500{,}000} \times 1{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = 0.0003 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 300
$$

#### Step 4 — Convert DPMO to Sigma Level

Using the standard conversion table:

| DPMO | Sigma Level |
|---|---|
| 3.4 | 6.0 |
| 233 | 5.0 |
| **300** | **≈ 4.85** |
| 6,210 | 4.0 |

**Final Answer:** DPMO = **300**, Sigma Level ≈ **4.85σ**. This is *short of Six Sigma (6σ)*, indicating significant retention improvement opportunities exist — exactly the gap the DMAIC project would close.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Net Promoter Score (NPS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** An e-commerce firm surveys **2,000 customers**. Results: **1,100 Promoters**, **600 Passives**, **300 Detractors**. Calculate NPS.

---

#### Step 1 — Compute % Promoters

$$
\%P = \frac{1{,}100}{2{,}000} \times 100 = 55\%
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute % Detractors

$$
\%D = \frac{300}{2{,}000} \times 100 = 15\%
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute NPS

$$
\text{NPS} = 55 - 15 = 40
$$

**Final Answer:** NPS = **+40** — interpreted as a *Strong* retention signal (NPS above 30 is generally considered excellent).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Metric | Formula | Industry Benchmark | Six Sigma Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CRR** | $\frac{E-N}{S} \times 100$ | 85–90% | $\geq$ 95% |
| **CCR** | $\frac{\text{Lost}}{S} \times 100$ | 5–10% | $\leq$ 3% |
| **NPS** | $\%P - \%D$ | +10 to +30 | $\geq$ +50 |
| **DPMO (Churn)** | $\frac{D}{O \times U} \times 10^6$ | ~6,210 (4σ) | 3.4 (6σ) |
| **CLV** | $\sum \frac{(R_t - C_t)r_t}{(1+d)^t}$ | Maximize | Continuously rising |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — DMAIC Flow for Customer Retention

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define: Identify CTQ for Retention] --> B[Measure: Baseline CRR, CCR, NPS]
    B --> C[Analyze: Root Cause via Fishbone]
    C --> D[Improve: Pilot Retention Strategies]
    D --> E[Control: SPC Charts + SOP Lock-In]
    E --> F[Improved Customer Retention Outcome]
    F --> G[Continuous Monitoring Loop]
    G --> A
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Customer Retention Strategy Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Customer Satisfaction] --> P2[Customer Loyalty]
    P2 --> P3[Customer Retention]
    P3 --> P4[Customer Advocacy NPS]
    P4 --> P5[Brand Equity Growth]
    P5 --> P6[Higher CLV and Revenue]
    P1 -.Six Sigma Quality.-> P2
    P2 -.VOC Feedback.-> P3
    P3 -.Loyalty Programs.-> P4
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Fishbone Root-Cause Map for Churn

```mermaid
fishbone
    Problem[Customer Churn Defects]
    Cause1[Product Defects]
    Cause2[Service Delays]
    Cause3[Billing Errors]
    Cause4[Poor Communication]
    Cause5[High Price]
    Cause6[Competitor Offer]
    Cause1 --> Problem
    Cause2 --> Problem
    Cause3 --> Problem
    Cause4 --> Problem
    Cause5 --> Problem
    Cause6 --> Problem
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Retention System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        VOC[Voice of Customer Data]
        TXN[Transaction History]
        SUP[Support Tickets]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS
        SEG[Customer Segmentation]
        SCRN[Churn Risk Scoring]
        TRG[Trigger Retention Action]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        LOY[Loyalty Offer]
        WIN[Win-back Campaign]
        SVC[Service Recovery]
    end
    VOC --> SEG
    TXN --> SCRN
    SUP --> SCRN
    SEG --> SCRN
    SCRN --> TRG
    TRG --> LOY
    TRG --> WIN
    TRG --> SVC
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define Customer Retention Rate (CRR). State its formula.**
**Model Answer:**
Customer Retention Rate is the percentage of existing customers a company retains over a defined period, indicating the effectiveness of its retention strategies.

$$
\text{CRR} = \frac{(E - N)}{S} \times 100
$$

Where $E$ = end-period customers, $N$ = new customers, $S$ = start-period customers. A higher CRR signals strong customer loyalty and efficient Six Sigma process output.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Distinguish between Customer Retention and Customer Acquisition.**
**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Customer Retention | Customer Acquisition |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Keeping existing customers | Gaining new customers |
| **Cost** | 5–7x lower | Higher |
| **Six Sigma Stage** | Control + Improve | Define + Measure |
| **Metric Focus** | CRR, NPS | Conversion Rate |
| **Time to ROI** | Faster | Slower |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2, Sample Paper 2024]**

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Choice

**Q(a)** Define Customer Retention in the context of Six Sigma. Explain its significance with reference to **CTQ**, **VOC**, and **CLV**. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Customer Retention in Six Sigma refers to the systematic, data-driven effort to keep customers engaged, satisfied, and loyal, eliminating the "defects" that cause them to defect to competitors. It is a measurable CTQ output directly tied to revenue stability.

- **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality):** Retention is a CTQ because it is a measurable indicator of process quality from the customer's perspective.
- **VOC (Voice of Customer):** VOC captures expectations (response time, defect-free deliveries, billing accuracy) that drive retention.
- **CLV (Customer Lifetime Value):** Higher retention directly inflates CLV — a retained customer contributes revenue across many periods, exponentially raising profitability.

Retention is significant because acquiring new customers costs 5–7x more, and loyal customers generate higher margins through repeat purchases and referrals.

---

**Q(b)** A regional bank started Q2 with **8,000 active savings account customers**. During the quarter, **300 customers closed** their accounts and **1,200 new accounts** were opened. At the end of Q2, the bank had **8,900 customers**. Compute the **CRR** and **CCR**, and interpret the result in Six Sigma terms. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Given:
$$
S = 8{,}000, \quad N = 1{,}200, \quad E = 8{,}900, \quad \text{Lost} = 300
$$

**Step 1 — CRR Calculation:**

$$
\text{CRR} = \frac{8{,}900 - 1{,}200}{8{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{7{,}700}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 96.25\%
$$

**Step 2 — CCR Calculation:**

$$
\text{CCR} = \frac{300}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 3.75\%
$$

**Step 3 — Six Sigma Interpretation:**

The bank's CRR of 96.25% is **good but below the world-class Six Sigma benchmark of ~99.99966%**. The CCR of 3.75% indicates room for DMAIC-driven improvement — likely through reducing service defects (wait time, complaint resolution, account errors). A control plan with monthly SPC tracking of CRR is recommended.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q(a)** Explain the **DMAIC framework** as applied to a customer retention improvement project in an e-commerce company. List the tools used in each phase. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| DMAIC Phase | Activity | Tools Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Define** | Identify churn problem; set CTQ | Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC |
| **Measure** | Baseline CRR, CCR, NPS, CSAT | Data Collection Plan, Pareto Chart |
| **Analyze** | Root-cause churn drivers | Fishbone, 5-Whys, Regression |
| **Improve** | Pilot loyalty program, redesign checkout | FMEA, DOE, Pilot Testing |
| **Control** | Sustain gains, monitor CRR | Control Charts, SOPs |

In an e-commerce setting, DMAIC ensures that retention is not based on intuition but on statistically validated defect reduction.

---

**Q(b)** A SaaS firm has **100,000 subscribers**. In a month, **200 churn events** are recorded. Each customer has **10 defect opportunities** (login, billing, support, uptime, etc.). Calculate **DPMO** and identify the **Sigma Level**. If the firm targets **5σ quality**, how many defects per million opportunities are acceptable? *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total Opportunities:**

$$
100{,}000 \times 10 = 1{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — DPMO Calculation:**

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{200}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 200
$$

**Step 3 — Sigma Level Lookup:**

From the standard conversion table, **DPMO = 200 corresponds to approximately 5.1σ**.

**Step 4 — DPMO at 5σ target:**

The 5σ benchmark is **233 DPMO**.

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The firm is operating at **5.1σ — slightly above 5σ**. To reach true Six Sigma (6σ), the churn must drop to **3.4 DPMO**, i.e., only **0.34 churn events per million opportunities**. This represents a dramatic improvement opportunity via DMAIC.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Avoid These!**
> 1. **Confusing CRR with CCR:** Many students invert the formula. Remember: CRR = retained; CCR = lost.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract new customers (N) in CRR:** The formula $\frac{E-N}{S}$ is the *real* retention — students often wrongly compute $\frac{E}{S}$.
> 3. **Mixing DPMO definitions:** DPMO uses *opportunities per unit*, not just units.
> 4. **Skipping Six Sigma context:** KTU expects every retention answer to mention DMAIC, CTQ, VOC, or DPMO — pure marketing-style answers lose marks.
> 5. **Not converting percentages:** CRR is asked as a percentage — show the $\times 100$ step explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Customer Retention in Six Sigma**

- **Customer Retention** = minimizing churn, maximizing CLV via Six Sigma methods.
- Core metrics: **CRR**, **CCR**, **CLV**, **NPS**, **CSAT**, **DPMO**, **σ-level**.
- Formula: $\text{CRR} = \frac{E-N}{S} \times 100$ — *always subtract new customers*.
- Six Sigma target retention process sigma level = **6σ** (3.4 DPMO).
- DMAIC is the **master framework** — every retention project must traverse all 5 phases.
- **CTQ** defines what "retention quality" means; **VOC** captures customer expectations.
- Retention is **5–7x cheaper** than acquisition — every KTU question expects this fact.
- **Fishbone, Pareto, SPC, FMEA** are the dominant tools across DMAIC for retention.
- **NPS** = %Promoters − %Detractors; range −100 to +100; > +30 is excellent.
- **Sigma Level** for 3.4 DPMO = **6.0σ** (with 1.5σ short-term shift convention).
- Always present retention numbers with **units, period, and benchmark** in KTU answers.
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** rises geometrically with retention probability.
- A Six Sigma-aligned organization treats every churn event as a **measurable defect**.
- Retention strategies: loyalty programs, service recovery, switching-cost design, personalization.
- **Trust + Value + Cost** = the three-pillar retention framework.
- Continuous monitoring (Control phase) prevents retention gains from decaying over time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Employee Involvement - Motivation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Employee Involvement - Motivation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Employee Involvement (EI)** in the Six Sigma framework refers to the systematic, structured, and sustained participation of employees at all hierarchical levels of an organization in quality improvement initiatives, problem-solving teams, and process optimization projects. As defined by the KTU 2024 syllabus under the Six Sigma methodology, Employee Involvement represents the *human capital dimension* of the DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) lifecycle, ensuring that quality is not merely a management directive but a grassroots cultural reality.

**Motivation**, in the context of Six Sigma, is defined as the internal psychological drive and external incentivization mechanism that energizes, directs, and sustains employee behavior toward achieving organizational quality objectives, defect reduction targets, and continuous improvement (CI) goals. It is the *force multiplier* that converts mandated participation into voluntary, enthusiastic engagement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Employee Involvement and Motivation are the **two pillars of the "People" dimension** in the Six Sigma framework. Without motivated employees, even the most rigorously designed DMAIC project will fail at the execution stage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Real-World Analogy: The Cycling Team**

Imagine a professional cycling team preparing for the Tour de France:
- **Employee Involvement** is like having the entire team — from the lead cyclist to the mechanic, the nutritionist, the physiotherapist, and even the water-bottle handler — actively participating in the race strategy. Everyone knows their role, contributes ideas, and is accountable.
- **Motivation** is the *inner fire* — the burning desire of each team member to win. Some are motivated intrinsically (the joy of cycling, personal ambition), while others are motivated extrinsically (prize money, sponsorship deals, medals, fame).

In Six Sigma, you need **both**: the structural participation (EI) AND the psychological drive (Motivation). A cyclist on the team with no motivation will drop out at the first hill. A highly motivated individual with no defined role will ride in circles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box**
> - **Employee Involvement** = *Who participates* and *How they participate* (Structural element)
> - **Motivation** = *Why they participate* and *How intensely* (Psychological element)
> - Together, they form the **People Pillar** of the Six Sigma house.

## 1.3 Standard Six Sigma Metrics Related to People

The following key metrics are used in KTU-evaluated problems:

- **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)**: Standardized measure of process performance.
- **Sigma Level (Z)**: Statistical measure of process capability, with **6σ = 3.4 DPMO** as the world-class benchmark.
- **Employee Engagement Index (EEI)**: A composite score (typically 0–100) measuring the degree of emotional commitment employees have to the organization.
- **Participation Rate**: The percentage of total employees actively engaged in Six Sigma projects at any given time.

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Target | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process Sigma Level | Z | $\geq 4.5$ | Sigma |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities | DPMO | $\leq 233$ (for 5σ) | defects/million |
| Employee Engagement Index | EEI | $\geq 75$ | points (0–100) |
| Project Participation Rate | PPR | $\geq 60$ | percent (%) |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Motivation in Six Sigma

Six Sigma draws heavily from classical and contemporary management theories. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to understand the following motivation theories and their direct application to quality management:

### 2.1.1 Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs (1943)

Abraham Maslow proposed a five-tier hierarchical model of human needs. In a Six Sigma context, an organization must satisfy lower-level needs before an employee can be motivated to participate in voluntary quality initiatives.

**The Five Levels (bottom to top):**
1. **Physiological Needs** — Salary, working conditions, job security.
2. **Safety Needs** — Stable employment, safe work environment, predictable career path.
3. **Social Needs (Belongingness)** — Team membership, Belonging to a Six Sigma project team, peer recognition.
4. **Esteem Needs** — Recognition as a Green Belt/Black Belt, certifications, awards, promotions.
5. **Self-Actualization** — Creative problem-solving, mentoring others, leading transformational change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Sigma Application:** The **Green Belt and Black Belt certification programs** directly address Levels 3, 4, and 5 of Maslow's hierarchy by providing team membership, recognition, and self-actualization opportunities.

### 2.1.2 Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory (1959)

Frederick Herzberg distinguished between:
- **Hygiene Factors** (Extrinsic): Salary, company policy, supervision, working conditions. Their *absence* causes dissatisfaction, but their *presence* does NOT motivate.
- **Motivators** (Intrinsic): Achievement, recognition, the work itself, responsibility, advancement. These *actively drive* higher performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Six Sigma project participation is a classic **Motivator** in Herzberg's framework. It provides achievement (defect reduction), recognition (Black Belt status), responsibility (project ownership), and advancement (career growth).

### 2.1.3 McGregor's Theory X and Theory Y (1960)

Douglas McGregor's two contrasting management philosophies:

**Theory X** (Traditional/Authoritarian):
- Employees dislike work, avoid responsibility, and must be coerced.
- In a Six Sigma context, this leads to **mandated participation**, which results in low engagement and superficial compliance.

**Theory Y** (Modern/Participative):
- Employees are self-directed, seek responsibility, and are creative.
- In a Six Sigma context, this leads to **voluntary, empowered engagement**, which is the hallmark of mature Six Sigma cultures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Sigma Insight:** Successful Six Sigma deployments (Motorola, GE, Honeywell) operate under **Theory Y** assumptions. Champions, Master Black Belts, and Black Belts are entrusted with significant autonomy and decision-making authority.

### 2.1.4 Vroom's Expectancy Theory (1964)

Victor Vroom's model states that motivation is a function of three beliefs:

$$M = E \times I \times V$$

Where:
- $M$ = **Motivation** (force to act)
- $E$ = **Expectancy** (belief that effort will lead to performance)
- $I$ = **Instrumentality** (belief that performance will lead to a reward)
- $V$ = **Valence** (value the individual places on the reward)

**Six Sigma Application:** For an employee to be motivated to lead a DMAIC project, they must believe:
- $E$: "My analytical effort *will* solve this problem" (training provides this).
- $I$: "If I reduce defects, I *will* be promoted to Black Belt" (HR systems provide this).
- $V$: "I *value* becoming a Black Belt" (certification recognition provides this).

If *any* of the three components is zero, the total motivation $M$ collapses to zero.

### 2.1.5 Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic Motivation

| Dimension | Intrinsic Motivation | Extrinsic Motivation |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Internal satisfaction | External rewards |
| **Drivers** | Curiosity, mastery, purpose | Money, awards, promotions |
| **Sustainability** | Long-lasting, self-reinforcing | Short-term, diminishing returns |
| **Six Sigma Link** | Problem-solving joy, mastery of statistics | Belt certification, financial bonuses |
| **KTU Term** | Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan) | Operant Conditioning (Skinner) |

### 2.1.6 The ARCTIC Model of Employee Involvement

A Six Sigma-specific framework for structuring EI:

- **A** — *Align* employees with quality goals
- **R** — *Recognize* contributions publicly
- **C** — *Coach* through mentoring and training
- **T** — *Trust* with autonomy and decision authority
- **I** — *Include* in cross-functional teams
- **C** — *Celebrate* project successes

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vroom's Expectancy | $M = E \times I \times V$ | $M, E, I, V$ are dimensionless probabilities/values in $[0,1]$ | Calculating employee motivation force |
| Employee Engagement Index | $EEI = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i}{n \times S_{\max}} \times 100$ | $S_i$ = individual engagement score, $S_{\max}$ = max scale value, $n$ = number of respondents | Survey-based engagement measurement |
| Project Participation Rate | $PPR = \dfrac{N_{\text{active}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | $N_{\text{active}}$ = active participants, $N_{\text{total}}$ = total employees | Six Sigma deployment health check |
| Six Sigma Yield | $Y = e^{-DPMO / 1{,}000{,}000}$ | $Y$ = first-pass yield (decimal), $DPMO$ = defects per million opps. | Linking people performance to process |
| DPMO Conversion | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{U \times O}$ | $D$ = defects, $U$ = units, $O$ = opportunities per unit | Project-level defect tracking |
| Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $COPQ = C_{\text{internal}} + C_{\text{external}}$ | $C$ in monetary units (INR/USD) | Justifying Six Sigma investment |
| Motivation Multiplier | $M_{\text{org}} = M_{\text{intrinsic}} + k \cdot M_{\text{extrinsic}}$ | $k$ = cultural weight factor (typically $0.3 \le k \le 0.7$) | Total organizational motivation force |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU Exams:** In Vroom's formula, *any zero value* for $E$, $I$, or $V$ results in $M = 0$, regardless of the other two values. Examiners frequently test this multiplicative property.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Linking Employee Engagement to Process Sigma Level

A common KTU-style problem asks you to derive the relationship between employee participation and the resulting improvement in Sigma level. We will develop this step-by-step.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define the Engagement-Performance Function

Let us model the relationship between the Employee Engagement Index (EEI) and the process yield $Y$. Empirical Six Sigma literature (Snee, 2010; Harry, 1998) suggests a *sigmoidal* relationship:

$$Y = Y_{\min} + (Y_{\max} - Y_{\min}) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + e^{-k(EEI - EEI_0)}}$$

Where:
- $Y_{\min}$ = baseline yield when engagement is zero
- $Y_{\max}$ = theoretical maximum yield (often $\approx 1.0$)
- $k$ = steepness of the engagement-performance curve
- $EEI_0$ = inflection point (EEI at which yield improvement accelerates)

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Convert Yield to DPMO

Using the standard Six Sigma yield-DPMO relationship:

$$DPMO = 1{,}000{,}000 \cdot (1 - Y)$$

This step converts the engagement-driven yield improvement into a tangible defect metric.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Convert DPMO to Sigma Level

The Sigma level $Z$ is obtained via the inverse of the standard normal CDF. For engineering approximations:

$$Z \approx 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \cdot \ln(DPMO)}$$

This is the *Bender approximation* commonly used in Six Sigma textbooks for rapid calculation.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A manufacturing unit has a baseline yield $Y_{\min} = 0.85$ with $EEI_0 = 50$. If the engagement rises to $EEI = 80$ with $k = 0.08$, calculate:
(a) The new yield $Y$
(b) The new DPMO
(c) The improvement in Sigma level (assume baseline DPMO = 66,807, corresponding to $3\sigma$)

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**Part (a) — New Yield:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y &= Y_{\min} + (Y_{\max} - Y_{\min}) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + e^{-k(EEI - EEI_0)}} \\
&= 0.85 + (1.00 - 0.85) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.08 \times (80 - 50)}} \\
&= 0.85 + 0.15 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.08 \times 30}} \\
&= 0.85 + 0.15 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + e^{-2.4}}
\end{aligned}$$

Now, computing the exponential:

$$e^{-2.4} \approx 0.0907$$

Substituting back:

$$\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.85 + 0.15 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + 0.0907} \\
&= 0.85 + 0.15 \cdot \frac{1}{1.0907} \\
&= 0.85 + 0.15 \cdot 0.9169 \\
&= 0.85 + 0.1375 \\
&= 0.9875
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, **$Y = 0.9875$ (or 98.75%)**.

**Part (b) — New DPMO:**

$$\begin{aligned}
DPMO_{\text{new}} &= 1{,}000{,}000 \times (1 - Y) \\
&= 1{,}000{,}000 \times (1 - 0.9875) \\
&= 1{,}000{,}000 \times 0.0125 \\
&= 12{,}500
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, **$DPMO_{\text{new}} = 12{,}500$ defects per million opportunities**.

**Part (c) — Sigma Level Improvement:**

For the baseline ($3\sigma$), $DPMO_{\text{baseline}} = 66{,}807$.

For the new state, $DPMO_{\text{new}} = 12{,}500$.

Using the Bender approximation:

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{baseline}} &= 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \cdot \ln(66{,}807)} \\
&= 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \times 11.109} \\
&= 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 24.673} \\
&= 0.8406 + \sqrt{4.697} \\
&= 0.8406 + 2.167 \\
&\approx 3.008
\end{aligned}$$

For the new state:

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{\text{new}} &= 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \cdot \ln(12{,}500)} \\
&= 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \times 9.433} \\
&= 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 20.951} \\
&= 0.8406 + \sqrt{8.419} \\
&= 0.8406 + 2.902 \\
&\approx 3.743
\end{aligned}$$

**Improvement in Sigma level:**

$$\Delta Z = Z_{\text{new}} - Z_{\text{baseline}} = 3.743 - 3.008 = 0.735 \text{ sigma}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** By raising the Employee Engagement Index from 50 to 80, the process improved by approximately **0.74 sigma levels**, demonstrating a tangible engineering outcome from a *people-focused* intervention.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Motivation Theory × Six Sigma Practice

The following matrix maps each motivation theory to a concrete Six Sigma practice. This is a high-yield KTU question format:

| Motivation Theory | Core Principle | Six Sigma Application | Failure Mode if Ignored |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Maslow** | Hierarchical needs | Belt certification hierarchy (Green→Black→Master) | Employees stuck at safety needs, no initiative |
| **Herzberg** | Hygiene + Motivators | Project recognition + competitive salary | Well-paid but disengaged workforce |
| **McGregor (Y)** | Self-direction | Empowered project teams with budget authority | Micromanaged teams, slow decision-making |
| **Vroom** | $E \times I \times V$ | Clear links: training → certification → promotion | Untrained staff expected to deliver results |
| **ARCTIC Model** | Holistic involvement | Structured EI program | Ad-hoc, inconsistent engagement |

## 3.3 Derivation: The Motivation-Decay Function

A critical insight in sustained Six Sigma programs is that motivation *decays* over time after the initial enthusiasm. We model this as an exponential decay:

$$M(t) = M_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t} + M_{\infty}$$

Where:
- $M(t)$ = motivation level at time $t$ (in months)
- $M_0$ = initial motivation surge (post-training)
- $\lambda$ = decay constant (organization-specific)
- $M_{\infty}$ = baseline (sustained) motivation
- $t$ = time elapsed

**Interpretation:** Without continuous reinforcement (recognition, new challenges, visible wins), the initial motivation $M_0$ decays toward a lower baseline $M_{\infty}$. This is why mature Six Sigma programs schedule *regular kaizen events* and *project closure celebrations* to refresh $M_0$.
