P_{\text{Packaging}} &= \dfrac{10}{120} \times 100 = 8.33\% \\[4pt]
P_{\text{Attitude}} &= \dfrac{4}{120} \times 100 = 3.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $CF_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CF_{\text{Delay}} &= 56 \\[4pt]
CF_{\text{Quality}} &= 56 + 32 = 88 \\[4pt]
CF_{\text{Billing}} &= 88 + 18 = 106 \\[4pt]
CF_{\text{Packaging}} &= 106 + 10 = 116 \\[4pt]
CF_{\text{Attitude}} &= 116 + 4 = 120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $CP_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CP_{\text{Delay}} &= \dfrac{56}{120} \times 100 = 46.67\% \\[4pt]
CP_{\text{Quality}} &= \dfrac{88}{120} \times 100 = 73.33\% \\[4pt]
CP_{\text{Billing}} &= \dfrac{106}{120} \times 100 = 88.33\% \\[4pt]
CP_{\text{Packaging}} &= \dfrac{116}{120} \times 100 = 96.67\% \\[4pt]
CP_{\text{Attitude}} &= \dfrac{120}{120} \times 100 = 100.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b) — Identify Vital Few and State Pareto Diagram Conclusion (7 Marks)

**Pareto Table:**

| Rank | Category | $f_i$ | $P_i$ (%) | $CF_i$ | $CP_i$ (%) | Vital Few? |
|------|----------|-------|-----------|--------|------------|------------|
| 1 | Delivery Delay | 56 | 46.67 | 56 | 46.67 | — |
| 2 | Product Quality | 32 | 26.67 | 88 | 73.33 | — |
| 3 | Billing Errors | 18 | 15.00 | 106 | **88.33** | ✔ (crosses 80%) |
| 4 | Poor Packaging | 10 | 8.33 | 116 | 96.67 | ✗ |
| 5 | Staff Attitude | 4 | 3.33 | 120 | 100.00 | ✗ |

**Conclusion:**
- **Vital Few**: **Delivery Delay, Product Quality, and Billing Errors** — these three categories together account for **88.33%** of all complaints, crossing the 80% Pareto threshold at Billing Errors.
- **Trivial Many**: Poor Packaging and Staff Attitude, together contributing only **11.67%**.
- **Recommendation**: The firm must prioritize process improvements in delivery logistics, product quality inspection, and billing systems before addressing packaging or staff training.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **OPTION 2** (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Slot]**
**The following data represents the types of defects observed in a printed circuit board (PCB) assembly line during a one-week study. Construct the Pareto Diagram, draw it, and interpret the Vital Few.**

| Defect Type | Count |
|-------------|-------|
| Solder Bridge | 45 |
| Missing Component | 25 |
| Lifted Pad | 18 |
| Solder Splash | 10 |
| Misalignment | 7 |
| Wrong Component | 5 |

### Part (a) — Pareto Table Construction with Calculations (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Grand Total:**

$$N = 45 + 25 + 18 + 10 + 7 + 5 = 110$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative Computations:**

| Rank | Defect | $f_i$ | $P_i$ (%) | $CF_i$ | $CP_i$ (%) |
|------|--------|-------|-----------|--------|------------|
| 1 | Solder Bridge | 45 | 40.91 | 45 | 40.91 |
| 2 | Missing Component | 25 | 22.73 | 70 | 63.64 |
| 3 | Lifted Pad | 18 | 16.36 | 88 | 80.00 |
| 4 | Solder Splash | 10 | 9.09 | 98 | 89.09 |
| 5 | Misalignment | 7 | 6.36 | 105 | 95.45 |
| 6 | Wrong Component | 5 | 4.55 | 110 | 100.00 |

**Step 3 — Validation:**

$$\sum P_i = 40.91 + 22.73 + 16.36 + 9.09 + 6.36 + 4.55 = 100.00\% \;\; \checkmark$$

$$CP_6 = 100\% = \dfrac{N}{N} \times 100 \;\; \checkmark$$

### Part (b) — Pareto Diagram Sketch and Interpretation (7 Marks)

**Pareto Diagram Sketch Description (to be drawn on answer sheet):**

```
PCB Defect Pareto Diagram
100 |                                          ●----- 100%
 90 |                                  ●-------/
 80 |- - - - - - - - - - - - - - ●- - -/- - - - 80% Threshold
 70 |                       ●----/  /
 60 |                  ●---/      /
 50 |   ▓▓▓▓           /         /
 40 |   ▓▓▓▓  ▓▓▓▓    /         /
 30 |   ▓▓▓▓  ▓▓▓▓    /         /
 20 |   ▓▓▓▓  ▓▓▓▓ ▓▓▓▓       /
 10 |   ▓▓▓▓  ▓▓▓▓ ▓▓▓▓ ▓▓▓▓ /
  0 |___▓▓▓▓__▓▓▓▓_▓▓▓▓_▓▓▓▓__▓▓▓▓__▓▓▓▓______
       Solder   Miss.  Lifted Solder Misal.  Wrong
       Bridge   Comp.   Pad   Splash         Comp.
        45       25      18     10     7      5

      |--------- VITAL FEW ---------|  |--TRIVIAL MANY--|
```

**Interpretation:**
- The cumulative curve crosses the **80% threshold at Lifted Pad** ($CP_3 = 80.00\%$).
- **Vital Few**: **Solder Bridge, Missing Component, and Lifted Pad** (cumulatively 80%).
- **Trivial Many**: Solder Splash, Misalignment, and Wrong Component (cumulatively 20%).
- **Engineering Insight**: Soldering-related defects dominate — likely pointing to reflow oven temperature profiling or stencil printing quality as the root system. The team should investigate solder paste deposition first.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Pareto Principle = 80/20 Rule**, adapted to quality by **Juran** from Pareto's 1896 economic observation.
- ⭐ Pareto Diagram = **Descending Bars** (frequency, left Y-axis) **+ Cumulative Curve** (percentage, right Y-axis 0–100%).
- ⭐ **Always sort categories in descending order** before plotting — non-negotiable.
- ⭐ **Vital Few** = categories whose cumulative contribution first crosses **80%**.
- ⭐ **Trivial Many** = remaining right-side categories contributing ≤ 20%.
- ⭐ Two axes are **mandatory**: primary axis for counts, secondary axis for cumulative %.
- ⭐ Pareto Diagram is a **prioritization tool**, NOT a root-cause analysis tool (use Fishbone / 5-Whys next).
- ⭐ It is one of the **Seven Basic Quality Tools** of Six Sigma's DMAIC framework.
- ⭐ **Validation checks**: $\sum P_i = 100\%$, $CF_n = N$, $CP_n = 100\%$.
- ⭐ Pareto Diagram is most effective when categories are **mutually exclusive** and data is collected over a **defined, consistent time window**.
- ⭐ **Before / After Pareto comparison** is a powerful Six Sigma technique to demonstrate DMAIC project improvement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cause and Effect Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cause and Effect Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Cause and Effect Diagram (CED)**, also known as the **Ishikawa Diagram** or **Fishbone Diagram** (named after its visual resemblance to the skeletal structure of a fish), is a structured graphical analytical tool developed by Japanese quality statistician **Professor Kaoru Ishikawa** in **1968**. It is used in Six Sigma's **DMAIC (Define–Measure–Analyze–Improve–Control)** methodology, specifically during the **Analyze Phase**, to systematically identify, classify, and explore the potential root causes of a problem (the "effect") and organize them into related categories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Statement (Board Exam Ready):**
> *"A Cause and Effect Diagram is a pictorial representation that displays in detail all the possible causes of a given problem (effect) and classifies them into logical categories, helping to identify the most likely root causes for further statistical investigation."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detective's Sketch Board"

Imagine a detective arriving at a complex crime scene (the **Problem/Effect**). Instead of randomly interrogating suspects, the detective pins a large board on the wall and draws a single arrow pointing right toward the crime description. Along this central arrow, the detective creates labeled branches — *Suspects*, *Weapons*, *Motive*, *Location*, *Opportunity* — and under each branch, lists every possible contributing factor.

Each small branch is a potential cause; the central spine is the effect. The fishbone works in exactly this way — it forces a team to **think broadly** across multiple cause-categories before drilling down to specific root causes. It is the visual equivalent of a **brainstorming session that has been forced to organize itself**.

### 1.3 Why It Matters in Six Sigma

> [!NOTE]
> - **Purpose:** Moves a Six Sigma team from *symptoms* to *causes* systematically.
> - **Phase Used:** Primarily the **Analyze Phase** of DMAIC.
> - **Synergy:** Works in tandem with tools like the **Pareto Chart**, **5-Why Analysis**, **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**, and **Regression Analysis**.
> - **Output:** A visual "knowledge map" of all plausible causes, which then drives data collection and hypothesis testing.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Effect** | The problem, symptom, or undesirable outcome being investigated (head of fish). |
| **Cause** | A factor that contributes to creating the effect (fish bones). |
| **Branch (Category)** | A logical grouping of related causes (e.g., Man, Machine, Material). |
| **Spine** | The central horizontal arrow leading to the effect. |
| **Sub-cause** | A more granular cause listed under a primary branch. |
| **Root Cause** | The fundamental underlying factor that, if corrected, prevents recurrence. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of the Fishbone — Six Standard Cause Categories (The 6Ms)

The traditional manufacturing version of the fishbone uses six universally accepted categories, known as the **6Ms**:

1. **Man (Workforce / People)** — Skills, training, experience, motivation, human error, fatigue.
2. **Machine (Equipment)** — Capability, age, maintenance schedule, calibration, breakdowns.
3. **Material (Raw Inputs)** — Quality, supplier consistency, storage conditions, specifications.
4. **Method (Process / Procedure)** — Work instructions, standard operating procedures, process design.
5. **Measurement (Inspection / Metrics)** — Gauge accuracy, calibration, sampling plan, data integrity.
6. **Mother Nature (Environment)** — Temperature, humidity, lighting, dust, vibration, ergonomics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Service Industry Adaptation — The 8Ps:** When applied to services or transactional processes, the categories are modified to **8Ps**: *People, Policies, Procedures, Plant/Technology, Programs, Product, Price, Promotion* — students should recognize both versions for KTU 2024 exam questions.

### 2.2 Theoretical Basis — Why the Fishbone Works

The fishbone is grounded in the **theory of structured brainstorming** and the **logic of cause-and-effect relationships**. It transforms the abstract, scattered human thought process into a hierarchical taxonomy by:

- **Divergent Thinking Trigger:** Forces enumeration of all possible angles.
- **Convergent Thinking Trigger:** Once branches are populated, teams vote on likely causes to drive *convergent* analysis.
- **Cognitive Scaffolding:** The 6M/8P scaffold prevents "tunnel vision" on a single cause category.

### 2.3 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Theory)

The diagram is constructed via the following theoretical sequence:

1. **Define the Effect:** Place the precise problem statement at the "head" of the fish.
2. **Draw the Spine:** A horizontal arrow flowing left-to-right toward the effect.
3. **Identify Categories:** Use 6Ms (manufacturing) or 8Ps (service).
4. **Brainstorm Sub-causes:** Use sticky notes on a whiteboard during a team session; *no criticism allowed* in the divergence phase.
5. **Validate Causes:** Cross-check each sub-cause against actual data (process mapping, Pareto, regression).
6. **Highlight Vital Few:** Color-code or box the 20% of causes that Pareto analysis suggests drive 80% of the problem.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table for Board Exam Recall:**

| Parameter | Specification | KTU Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|
| **Inventor** | Kaoru Ishikawa | "Ishi" = stone; fish made of stone (skeleton). |
| **Year of Origin** | 1968 | Late 1960s quality revolution in Japan. |
| **Also Known As** | Fishbone, Ishikawa, Herringbone | Three aliases — remember all three. |
| **DMAIC Phase** | **Analyze** phase (also Define) | "A" for Analyze and "A" for Activity mapping. |
| **Standard Categories** | 6 Ms (Manufacturing) | Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature. |
| **Service Categories** | 8 Ps (Services) | People, Policies, Procedures, Plant, Programs, Product, Price, Promotion. |
| **Related Tool** | 5-Why Analysis | Used *after* fishbone on each sub-cause. |
| **Decision Rule** | Brainstorm = no criticism | Use sticky notes during divergence. |
| **Limitation** | Shows possibilities, not probabilities | Always pair with data-driven validation. |
| **Output Type** | Qualitative / Categorical | Not statistical — must be paired with Pareto. |

### 2.5 Engineering / Industrial Utility

In a **manufacturing Six Sigma project**:
- Reducing **defect rates** in a semiconductor fabrication line.
- Investigating **downtime** in an automotive assembly plant.
- Tracing **customer complaints** back to a specific supplier batch.

In **service / IT sectors**:
- Pinpointing why **customer churn** is rising.
- Identifying why **hospital patient wait times** are increasing.
- Diagnosing **software deployment failures** across multiple DevOps stages.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Construction & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Exhaustive Construction Procedure (Board Exam Walkthrough)

> **Example Problem Statement (Model KTU Question):**
> *"In a CNC machining cell of a precision components factory, the rejection rate of a critical shaft has risen from 2% to 8% over the last quarter. Construct a Cause and Effect Diagram to systematically identify potential causes."*

#### **Step 1 — Define the Effect Precisely**

The effect must be a **measurable, unambiguous statement**. Avoid vague phrases like "Quality is bad."

$$ \text{Effect} \;=\; \text{"Reject Rate of Critical Shaft has increased from 2\% to 8\%"} $$

This precision ensures the team is solving the *right* problem. The effect is written in a box at the rightmost "head" of the fish, with a horizontal arrow pointing toward it.

#### **Step 2 — Draw the Central Spine**

A single horizontal arrow is drawn from left to right, terminating at the effect box. This spine represents the **logical flow of cause leading to effect**.

#### **Step 3 — Identify the Six Categories**

For this manufacturing scenario, the 6Ms apply:

$$
\text{Categories} \;=\; \{\text{Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature}\}
$$

Each category becomes a diagonal "rib" angled toward the spine (≈ 60° angle to the central line).

#### **Step 4 — Populate Sub-causes via Brainstorming**

Below is the exhaustive sub-cause enumeration for this case:

| Category | Identified Sub-causes |
|---|---|
| **Man (M₁)** | Operator skill gap; Shift handover miscommunication; Inadequate training on new CNC program. |
| **Machine (M₂)** | Spindle bearing wear; Coolant pump failure; Tool holder runout exceeding 0.02 mm; CNC controller firmware glitch. |
| **Material (M₃)** | Incoming bar stock hardness variation (out of spec by 5 HRC); Supplier batch change without approval; Storage area humidity causing surface oxidation. |
| **Method (M₄)** | Revised cutting parameters not documented; SOP last updated 3 years ago; No standardized tool-change interval. |
| **Measurement (M₅)** | CMM calibration overdue by 2 months; Inconsistent sampling plan (5 vs. 10 parts); Operator reading error on digital micrometer. |
| **Mother Nature (M₆)** | Ambient temperature drift beyond 22 °C ± 3 °C range; Vibration from adjacent press shop; Humidity not controlled. |

#### **Step 5 — Validate Each Sub-cause Against Data**

For each listed sub-cause, the team must answer:
- Is there **data** confirming this contributes?
- Does a **Pareto chart** of recent defects show correlation?
- Is the cause **measurable** for hypothesis testing?

Only validated causes proceed to the **5-Why drill-down** in the next step.

#### **Step 6 — 5-Why Drill-Down Example (Worked Out)**

Take the sub-cause: *"Tool holder runout exceeding 0.02 mm."*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Why 1:} \quad & \text{Why is runout exceeding spec?} \\
\quad & \rightarrow \text{Because the tool holder is worn.} \\
\text{Why 2:} \quad & \text{Why is the tool holder worn?} \\
\quad & \rightarrow \text{Because replacement interval is not enforced.} \\
\text{Why 3:} \quad & \text{Why is the replacement interval not enforced?} \\
\quad & \rightarrow \text{Because the maintenance schedule lacks a trigger.} \\
\text{Why 4:} \quad & \text{Why does the schedule lack a trigger?} \\
\quad & \rightarrow \text{Because the SOP does not specify hours/cycles.} \\
\text{Why 5:} \quad & \text{Why doesn't the SOP specify hours/cycles?} \\
\quad & \rightarrow \text{Root Cause: SOP last updated 3 years ago, predating new CNC machine.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **root cause** is therefore: *"Outdated Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) lacking preventive-maintenance triggers."*

#### **Step 7 — Highlight Vital Few Causes**

A Pareto chart is overlaid on the validated causes, and typically **2–3 root causes** emerge. For our case:
1. Outdated SOP (≈ 45% contribution).
2. Operator training gap on new CNC program (≈ 30%).
3. CMM calibration overdue (≈ 15%).

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fishbone vs. Other Tools

| Criteria | Fishbone | 5-Why | FMEA | Pareto Chart |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Output Type** | Qualitative | Qualitative | Quantitative (RPN) | Quantitative |
| **Categorization** | Yes (6Ms/8Ps) | No | No | No |
| **Phase of DMAIC** | Analyze | Analyze | Improve / Analyze | Measure / Analyze |
| **Statistically Valid?** | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Best For** | Broad cause ID | Drilling one cause | Risk ranking | Prioritizing causes |
| **Used With Fishbone?** | — | Yes (post-construction) | Yes (post-validation) | Yes (post-brainstorm) |

### 3.3 Quantitative Pitfalls in Fishbone Use

The fishbone is **qualitative**. Students must NOT treat it as a statistical proof. Common student mistakes:

- ❌ Drawing only **2–3 branches** because they "feel" sufficient.
- ❌ Listing **effects** as causes (e.g., "Defect" as a cause of "Defect").
- ❌ Skipping the **data validation** step.
- ❌ Using **only one person's ideas** instead of cross-functional brainstorming.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Fishbone Block-Diagram (6M Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture of the Cause and Effect Diagram** using the 6M manufacturing categories. Each node represents a category branch, and sub-nodes enumerate the canonical sub-causes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    effectNode["Effect: Reject Rate of Critical Shaft 2 to 8 percent"]
    spineNode["Central Spine - Cause flows left to right"]
    manCat["M1 Man Workforce"]
    machineCat["M2 Machine Equipment"]
    materialCat["M3 Material Inputs"]
    methodCat["M4 Method Process"]
    measurementCat["M5 Measurement Inspection"]
    natureCat["M6 Mother Nature Environment"]
    manSub1["Operator skill gap"]
    manSub2["Shift handover miscommunication"]
    manSub3["Inadequate CNC training"]
    machineSub1["Spindle bearing wear"]
    machineSub2["Coolant pump failure"]
    machineSub3["Tool holder runout over 0.02 mm"]
    materialSub1["Bar stock hardness variation"]
    materialSub2["Supplier batch change unapproved"]
    materialSub3["Storage humidity causing oxidation"]
    methodSub1["Cutting parameters undocumented"]
    methodSub2["SOP outdated by 3 years"]
    methodSub3["No standardized tool change interval"]
    measureSub1["CMM calibration overdue 2 months"]
    measureSub2["Inconsistent sampling plan"]
    measureSub3["Micrometer operator reading error"]
    natureSub1["Ambient temperature drift"]
    natureSub2["Vibration from press shop"]
    natureSub3["Humidity not controlled"]
    spineNode --> effectNode
    manCat --- spineNode
    machineCat --- spineNode
    materialCat --- spineNode
    methodCat --- spineNode
    measurementCat --- spineNode
    natureCat --- spineNode
    manCat --> manSub1
    manCat --> manSub2
    manCat --> manSub3
    machineCat --> machineSub1
    machineCat --> machineSub2
    machineCat --> machineSub3
    materialCat --> materialSub1
    materialCat --> materialSub2
    materialCat --> materialSub3
    methodCat --> methodSub1
    methodCat --> methodSub2
    methodCat --> methodSub3
    measurementCat --> measureSub1
    measurementCat --> measureSub2
    measurementCat --> measureSub3
    natureCat --> natureSub1
    natureCat --> natureSub2
    natureCat --> natureSub3
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Construction Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["Start Problem Statement"]
    defEff["Step 1 Define Effect Measurably"]
    drawSpine["Step 2 Draw Horizontal Spine"]
    idCats["Step 3 Identify 6M or 8P Categories"]
    brainstorm["Step 4 Brainstorm Sub-causes No Criticism"]
    validate["Step 5 Validate with Process Data and Pareto"]
    drill["Step 6 Apply 5-Why on Each Vital Cause"]
    highlight["Step 7 Highlight Vital Few Root Causes"]
    dmaicLink["Output feeds Analyze Phase of DMAIC"]
    startNode --> defEff
    defEff --> drawSpine
    drawSpine --> idCats
    idCats --> brainstorm
    brainstorm --> validate
    validate --> drill
    drill --> highlight
    highlight --> dmaicLink
```

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Fishbone Head Structure

For quick classroom blackboard reproduction:

```
                                      
       MAN ─────┐                      
                \                      
    MACHINE ────\──┐                  
                   \                   ╔══════════════════════════════╗
   MATERIAL ───────\─────────────────▶ ║  EFFECT: HIGH REJECT RATE   ║
                   /                   ╚══════════════════════════════╝
    METHOD ──────/──┘                  
                /                      
  MEASUREMENT ─┘                      
                                      
   MOTHER NATURE ─┐                   
                  \                   
                   ─── (Spine) ──▶  [ EFFECT BOX ]
```

### 4.4 Description of Visual Layout

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Direction:** Always left → right. The spine represents the cause-to-effect chain.
> **Rib Angle:** Each category branch is drawn at approximately **60° to the spine**, sloping toward the effect box.
> **Sub-causes:** Are listed as smaller "fish bones" branching off the category rib, parallel to the central spine (forming a sub-tree).
> **Color Coding (Industry Practice):** Red = validated critical cause, Yellow = suspected, Green = eliminated.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
Define the Cause and Effect Diagram. Who invented it and in which phase of DMAIC is it primarily used?

**Model Answer:**
The Cause and Effect Diagram (also called the Fishbone or Ishikawa Diagram) is a structured graphical tool used to identify, classify, and explore the potential root causes of a problem (effect) by organizing them into logical categories such as Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, and Mother Nature. It was invented by **Professor Kaoru Ishikawa** in **1968** and is primarily used in the **Analyze Phase** of the DMAIC methodology.

*(2 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for inventor/phase)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
List the six standard categories (6Ms) used in a manufacturing Cause and Effect Diagram.

**Model Answer:**
The six standard categories are:

1. **Man** (Workforce / People)
2. **Machine** (Equipment)
3. **Material** (Raw Inputs)
4. **Method** (Process / Procedure)
5. **Measurement** (Inspection / Metrics)
6. **Mother Nature** (Environment)

*(2 marks: ½ mark each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

In an automotive assembly plant, the **paint defect rate has risen from 1.5% to 6%** over the last two months. As a Six Sigma Green Belt trainee, you are asked to lead the Analyze phase.

**(a)** Construct a detailed **Cause and Effect Diagram** for this problem, populating all 6M categories with at least three relevant sub-causes each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **how you would validate the identified causes** and **which subsequent Six Sigma tools** you would use to drill down to the vital few root causes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Construction of the Cause and Effect Diagram:**

**Step 1 — Effect Statement:**
$$ \text{Effect} = \text{"Paint Defect Rate increased from 1.5\% to 6\%"} $$

**Step 2 — Draw Spine:** A horizontal arrow points from left to right, terminating at the effect box.

**Step 3 — Six Categories with Sub-causes:**

| Category | Sub-causes (3 each) |
|---|---|
| **Man** | Painter skill gap on new robotic spray gun; Inadequate PPE causing rework; Shift change without proper handover notes. |
| **Machine** | Spray nozzle clogging; HVLP (High Volume Low Pressure) gun pressure drift; Robotic arm path deviation by 1.5 mm. |
| **Material** | New paint supplier batch with higher viscosity; Primer coat thickness variation; Improper storage of paint cans (exposed to sun). |
| **Method** | Booth airflow not balanced after recent HVAC modification; Flash-off time reduced to cut cycle; Painting SOP outdated (2019 version). |
| **Measurement** | Spectrophotometer calibration overdue; Visual inspector fatigue; Sampling plan reduced from AQL 1.0 to 2.5. |
| **Mother Nature** | Humidity crossed 75% (above 60% spec); Temperature dropped to 18 °C; Dust ingress from open bay door. |

*(7 marks: 1 for effect + 1 for spine + 1 per category × 6 sub-categories well populated — examiner allocates marks per category.)*

---

**(b) Validation and Drill-Down to Vital Few Root Causes:**

**Validation Approach:**

1. **Cross-functional Team Review:** Convene paint-shop operators, maintenance engineers, quality inspectors, and supplier quality engineers to **vote** on the most plausible causes using a **multi-voting technique**.

2. **Pareto Chart Construction:** Plot the frequency of each defect type (runs, sags, orange peel, color mismatch) from the last 60 days. The Pareto reveals which sub-causes statistically dominate.

3. **Process Mapping & Data Correlation:** Overlay each candidate cause on a process map and pull **real-time data** from the paint shop SCADA system.

4. **Hypothesis Testing (Statistical):**
   - Use a **chi-square test** for categorical cause-vs-defect correlation.
   - Apply a **2-sample t-test** to compare defect rates before and after the HVAC modification.

5. **5-Why Drill-Down:** For each validated vital cause, apply 5-Why analysis to extract the root cause.

**Subsequent Six Sigma Tools Used:**

| Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **5-Why Analysis** | Drill each vital cause to its root. |
| **Pareto Chart** | Prioritize causes by frequency contribution. |
| **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** | Quantify Risk Priority Number (RPN). |
| **Design of Experiments (DOE)** | Test multiple factors simultaneously. |
| **Regression Analysis** | Establish cause-effect quantification. |
| **Control Plan** | Implement and sustain solutions (Control Phase). |

**Conclusion:** The validated vital few root causes — likely to be **(i) HVAC airflow imbalance** and **(ii) outdated SOP** — are then carried forward to the **Improve Phase** of DMAIC for solution design.

*(7 marks: 2 for validation methodology + 2 for tools + 2 for drill-down rationale + 1 for DMAIC linkage.)*

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

A leading IT service provider observes a **25% increase in customer-call escalation rates** at its Tier-1 support center. The Operations Director wants a Six Sigma analysis.

**(a)** Construct a Cause and Effect Diagram suitable for a **service environment**, listing at least four sub-causes under each relevant category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **6Ms** framework with the **8Ps** framework and justify which is more appropriate for this service case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Service-Oriented Cause and Effect Diagram (8Ps):**

**Effect Statement:**
$$ \text{Effect} = \text{"Customer Call Escalation Rate increased by 25\%"} $$

**8P Sub-causes:**

| Category | Sub-causes (4 each) |
|---|---|
| **People** | Agent training on new CRM module; High attrition (12% monthly); Language barrier in regional calls; Stress-induced errors. |
| **Policies** | Escalation policy lacks clear thresholds; Refund policy confusion; SLA penalty structure unclear; New compliance regulation not cascaded. |
| **Procedures** | Call script outdated; Knowledge base articles missing 30% of cases; Handover protocol to Tier-2 lacks case summary; QA review cycle too long (21 days). |
| **Plant / Technology** | CRM software latency (avg 4 sec); Outage on 12-Aug; IVR menu has 9 options causing drop-off; Old VoIP system causing choppy audio. |
| **Programs** | New customer onboarding program rushed; Loyalty program refunds delayed; Training program reduced from 4 weeks to 2; Mentor program discontinued. |
| **Product** | New mobile app version causing login failures; Banking product terms complex; Subscription renewal friction; Multiple product variants confusing agents. |
| **Price** | Discount disputes rising; Pricing page inconsistencies across regions; Refund credit delays; Currency conversion errors. |
| **Promotion** | Marketing campaign overselling features; Email campaign triggering wrong segment; Social media response time 48 hrs; Influencer promo with wrong product specs. |

*(7 marks: 1 for effect + 1 for spine + 5 for categories populated with ≥4 sub-causes.)*

---

**(b) Comparison of 6Ms vs. 8Ps:**

| Criterion | 6Ms (Manufacturing) | 8Ps (Service) |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Kaoru Ishikawa, 1968 | Extended framework for services, 1990s. |
| **Number of Categories** | 6 | 8 |
| **Industry Fit** | Tangible production environments. | Intangible service / transactional processes. |
| **Categories Reflect** | Physical inputs and outputs. | Customer experience and intangible offerings. |
| **Includes Environmental Factor?** | Yes (Mother Nature). | Embedded within Plant/Technology. |
| **Includes Marketing?** | No. | Yes (Promotion). |
| **Includes Pricing?** | No. | Yes (Price). |

**Justification for this Case:**
Since the problem is in an **IT service center** (intangible output, customer-facing, no physical raw material), the **8Ps framework is more appropriate**. The 6Ms would inadequately cover **Pricing disputes, Marketing-driven complaints, and Product complexity** — all of which are central to escalation behavior in a service environment. The 8Ps also explicitly include **Policies and Programs**, which are dominant levers in IT services.

*(7 marks: 3 for comparison table + 2 for identifying service-specific drivers + 2 for justification.)*

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Vague Effect Statements** — Examiners deduct up to 1 mark if the effect is not measurable (e.g., "Quality is bad" vs. "Reject rate rose from 2% to 8%").
> 2. **Listing Effects as Causes** — Writing "Defect" as a cause under "Material" is a critical error.
> 3. **Fewer than Three Sub-causes per Category** — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly asks for *at least three* sub-causes; missing sub-causes cost ½ to 1 mark each.
> 4. **No Mention of Validation Step** — A fishbone without data validation is incomplete; examiners expect a Pareto or statistical step.
> 5. **Confusing 6Ms with 8Ps** — Applying manufacturing categories to a service question loses easy marks.
> 6. **Skipping DMAIC Linkage** — Always conclude with the phrase *"This output feeds the Improve Phase of DMAIC"* to fetch the concluding mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inventor:** Kaoru Ishikawa, 1968.
- **Other Names:** Fishbone Diagram, Ishikawa Diagram, Herringbone Diagram.
- **DMAIC Phase:** Analyze (also used in Define for problem scoping).
- **6Ms (Manufacturing):** Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature.
- **8Ps (Service):** People, Policies, Procedures, Plant/Technology, Programs, Product, Price, Promotion.
- **Construction:** Effect → Spine → Categories → Brainstorm Sub-causes → Validate → Highlight Vital Few.
- **Validation Tools:** Pareto Chart, 5-Why, FMEA, Regression, DOE.
- **Limitations:** Qualitative only — does NOT statistically prove causation.
- **Best Practice:** Pair with data-driven tools before taking corrective action.
- **Board Tip:** Always end your answer with **"This analysis feeds the Improve Phase of DMAIC."**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scatter Diagram and Control Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scatter Diagram and Control Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Scatter Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Scatter Diagram):**
> A **Scatter Diagram** (also called a Scatter Plot or Correlation Chart) is a graphical statistical tool that displays the relationship between two quantitative variables by plotting ordered pairs $(x, y)$ on a Cartesian plane, where the horizontal axis represents the *independent (cause) variable* and the vertical axis represents the *dependent (effect) variable*. It is used to identify, visualize, and qualitatively estimate the strength and direction of association between paired numerical data.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
Imagine you are a doctor who suspects that "the more hours a student studies, the higher their exam score." To *test* this hunch, you collect data from 30 students: study hours and their corresponding marks. Now, instead of staring at two long columns of numbers, you simply plot a dot for each student — Study Hours on the bottom (X) and Marks on the side (Y). If the dots form an upward sloping cloud, your hunch is correct. If they form a downward cloud, the opposite is true. If they look like a random blob of stars in the night sky, there is no clear relationship. That visual "dot cloud" is exactly what a Scatter Diagram is.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Scatter Diagrams Matter in Six Sigma:**
> Scatter Diagrams are a primary tool of the **Analyze Phase** of the DMAIC cycle. They are used to verify suspected root causes (potential X's) against process outputs (Y's) before committing to expensive experimental confirmation via Design of Experiments (DOE).

---

### 1.2 Control Charts

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Control Chart):**
> A **Control Chart** (also known as a Shewhart Chart, named after Dr. Walter A. Shewhart, 1924) is a time-series graphical tool used in Statistical Process Control (SPC) to monitor, control, and improve a process over time by distinguishing between *common cause variation* (inherent to the process) and *special cause variation* (due to external assignable factors). It consists of a **Center Line (CL)** representing the process mean, an **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** and a **Lower Control Limit (LCL)**, both typically set at **±3 standard deviations (3σ)** from the mean.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
Think of a Control Chart as the "heart-rate monitor" of a manufacturing or service process. Just as a hospital monitor beeps loudly when your heart rate suddenly spikes or crashes (indicating something abnormal), a Control Chart beeps visually when plotted points fall outside the UCL/LCL boundaries. As long as the heartbeat stays within normal range, doctors leave the patient alone. Similarly, as long as the process points stay within control limits, engineers leave the process alone. The moment an out-of-control signal appears, they intervene to fix the root cause.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3-Sigma Rule (Empirical Foundation):**
> For any normally distributed process, approximately **99.73%** of all observations will fall within ±3 standard deviations of the mean. This is the statistical foundation of control limits — a point outside ±3σ has only a **0.27% probability** of occurring by chance, signaling a statistically significant shift.

---

### 1.3 Categories of Control Charts (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Category | Sub-Type | Data Measured | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Variables Charts** | $\bar{X}$-chart (Mean) | Continuous | Average of subgroup |
| | R-chart (Range) | Continuous | Variability within subgroup |
| | S-chart (Std Dev) | Continuous | Variability for large $n$ |
| | I-chart (Individuals) | Continuous | Slow-rate / batch data |
| | MR-chart (Moving Range) | Continuous | Variation between individual readings |
| **Attribute Charts** | p-chart | Defectives (proportion) | Variable sample size |
| | np-chart | Number of defectives | Constant sample size |
| | c-chart | Number of defects | Constant inspection unit |
| | u-chart | Defects per unit | Variable inspection unit |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Scatter Diagram — Correlation Theory

When two variables $x$ and $y$ are plotted, the resulting cloud exhibits one of six classical patterns. The KTU syllabus requires you to recognize **all six** patterns and explain the strength of correlation using the **Pearson Correlation Coefficient (r)**.

**The Six Correlation Patterns:**

1. **Strong Positive Correlation** ($r \approx +1$): Dots cluster tightly around an upward-sloping straight line. (e.g., Study hours $\uparrow$ ⇒ Marks $\uparrow$)
2. **Moderate Positive Correlation** ($r \approx +0.6$ to $+0.8$): Dots trend upward but with noticeable scatter.
3. **Weak Positive Correlation** ($r \approx +0.2$ to $+0.4$): Slight upward trend, lots of noise.
4. **No Correlation** ($r \approx 0$): Random blob, no discernible trend.
5. **Strong Negative Correlation** ($r \approx -1$): Dots cluster tightly around a downward-sloping line. (e.g., Machine age $\uparrow$ ⇒ Output quality $\downarrow$)
6. **Curvilinear / Non-Linear Correlation**: $r$ may be near zero but a clear parabolic or U-shape exists. **Caution**: $r$ only measures *linear* association.

**The Pearson Correlation Coefficient Formula:**

$$r = \frac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{\sqrt{\left[n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2\right]\left[n \sum y^2 - (\sum y)^2\right]}}$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of paired observations
- $x$ = independent variable (cause)
- $y$ = dependent variable (effect)
- Range: $-1 \le r \le +1$

---

### 2.2 Control Charts — Operational Logic

Control charts answer one fundamental engineering question: **"Is my process behaving today the way it behaved yesterday, or has something changed?"**

**The Five Operational Steps (KTU Mandatory):**

1. **Collect Subgroup Data**: Take $k$ subgroups, each of size $n$ (typically $n = 4$ or $n = 5$ for variables charts).
2. **Compute Subgroup Statistics**: Calculate the mean ($\bar{X}_i$) and range ($R_i$) for each subgroup.
3. **Compute Grand Means**: $\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k}$ and $\bar{R} = \frac{\sum R_i}{k}$.
4. **Compute Control Limits** using tabulated constants (for variables) or standard deviation (for attributes).
5. **Plot and Interpret**: Plot points chronologically; investigate any point violating the 3σ rules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UCL ≠ Specification Limit (USL/LSL).**
> Control limits describe the *voice of the process* (what the process actually does). Specification limits describe the *voice of the customer* (what the process must do). They are completely independent. A process can be in statistical control but still produce defective parts (low Cpk), or it can have wide control limits but still meet specifications.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Chart Type | Center Line (CL) | UCL Formula | LCL Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$\bar{X}$-chart** | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Monitor process mean |
| **R-chart** | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ | Monitor process variability |
| **p-chart** | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | Fraction defectives, $n$ variable |
| **np-chart** | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | Number defectives, $n$ constant |
| **c-chart** | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Defects per unit, $n$ constant |
| **u-chart** | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | Defects per unit, $n$ variable |

**Standard Control Chart Constants Table (KTU Mandatory for $n = 2$ to $n = 7$):**

| Subgroup Size $n$ | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ | $E_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 | 2.660 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 | 1.772 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 | 1.457 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 | 1.290 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 | 1.184 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 | 1.109 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of These Tools:**
> - **Scatter Diagrams** are used in Root Cause Analysis (Ishikawa + Scatter), Process Validation, and Six Sigma's *Measure* and *Analyze* phases.
> - **Control Charts** are the backbone of Statistical Process Control (SPC) in industries such as semiconductor manufacturing (TSMC, Intel), automotive (Toyota, Ford), healthcare (blood glucose monitoring, surgical outcomes), and software (defect tracking dashboards).
> - In Six Sigma's **Control Phase**, these charts provide the *sustained monitoring mechanism* that prevents the project improvements from regressing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 3-Sigma Control Limits

The control limits are *derived* (not arbitrarily chosen) from the Central Limit Theorem and the properties of the normal distribution. Below is the rigorous derivation.

**Step 1: Subgroup Mean Distribution**
If individual measurements $X$ are normally distributed with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, then for a subgroup of size $n$, the subgroup mean $\bar{X}$ is also normally distributed with:

$$\mu_{\bar{X}} = \mu \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

This follows directly from the Central Limit Theorem.

**Step 2: Applying the Empirical (68-95-99.73) Rule**
For a normal distribution, the probability that an observation falls within $\pm 3$ standard deviations of the mean is **99.73%**. We set control limits at this confidence level.

$$UCL = \mu_{\bar{X}} + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu + \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

$$CL = \mu_{\bar{X}} = \mu$$

$$LCL = \mu_{\bar{X}} - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu - \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3: Replacing Unknown Population Parameters with Sample Estimates**
In practice, the true $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are unknown. We substitute:
- $\mu \rightarrow \bar{\bar{X}}$ (the grand mean of subgroup means)
- $\sigma \rightarrow \bar{R}/d_2$ (where $d_2$ is a tabulated constant relating range to standard deviation)

**Step 4: Combining Constants — The Birth of $A_2$**

$$UCL = \bar{\bar{X}} + \frac{3(\bar{R}/d_2)}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Substituting the standard constant $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$, we obtain the final working formula:

$$\boxed{UCL = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\bar{R} \qquad CL = \bar{\bar{X}} \qquad LCL = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2\bar{R}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Constructing an $\bar{X}$-chart and R-chart

**Problem Statement:**
A bottling plant fills 500 ml bottles. Five subgroups, each of size $n = 4$, are taken. The subgroup means and ranges are:

| Subgroup $i$ | $\bar{X}_i$ (ml) | $R_i$ (ml) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 501.0 | 3 |
| 2 | 499.5 | 2 |
| 3 | 500.5 | 4 |
| 4 | 498.0 | 3 |
| 5 | 501.0 | 2 |

**Step 1: Compute the Grand Mean**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k} = \frac{501.0 + 499.5 + 500.5 + 498.0 + 501.0}{5} = \frac{2500.0}{5} = 500.0 \text{ ml}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Average Range**

$$\bar{R} = \frac{\sum R_i}{k} = \frac{3 + 2 + 4 + 3 + 2}{5} = \frac{14}{5} = 2.8 \text{ ml}$$

**Step 3: Look up $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ for $n = 4$**

From the constants table: $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$.

**Step 4: Compute $\bar{X}$-chart Limits**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\bar{R} = 500.0 + (0.729)(2.8) = 500.0 + 2.0412 = 502.04 \text{ ml}$$

$$CL_{\bar{X}} = 500.0 \text{ ml}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2\bar{R} = 500.0 - 2.0412 = 497.96 \text{ ml}$$

**Step 5: Compute R-chart Limits**

$$UCL_R = D_4\bar{R} = (2.282)(2.8) = 6.39 \text{ ml}$$

$$CL_R = \bar{R} = 2.8 \text{ ml}$$

$$LCL_R = D_3\bar{R} = (0)(2.8) = 0 \text{ ml}$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**

All five subgroup means (498.0, 499.5, 500.5, 501.0, 501.0) lie inside $[497.96, 502.04]$. All five ranges lie inside $[0, 6.39]$. Therefore, the process is **in statistical control**. No special causes are evident.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Scatter Diagram and Correlation Coefficient

**Problem Statement:**
A Six Sigma team suspects that oven temperature ($x$, in °C) affects cake hardness ($y$, in N). They collect 5 paired observations:

| Trial | $x$ (°C) | $y$ (N) | $x^2$ | $y^2$ | $xy$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 150 | 20 | 22500 | 400 | 3000 |
| 2 | 160 | 25 | 25600 | 625 | 4000 |
| 3 | 170 | 35 | 28900 | 1225 | 5950 |
| 4 | 180 | 40 | 32400 | 1600 | 7200 |
| 5 | 190 | 45 | 36100 | 2025 | 8550 |
| **Σ** | **850** | **165** | **145500** | **5875** | **28700** |

**Step 1: Apply the Correlation Formula**

$$r = \frac{n \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{\sqrt{[n \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2][n \sum y^2 - (\sum y)^2]}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values**

$$r = \frac{5(28700) - (850)(165)}{\sqrt{[5(145500) - (850)^2][5(5875) - (165)^2]}}$$

**Step 3: Simplify Numerator**

$$\text{Numerator} = 143500 - 140250 = 3250$$

**Step 4: Simplify First Bracket in Denominator**

$$5(145500) - (850)^2 = 727500 - 722500 = 5000$$

**Step 5: Simplify Second Bracket in Denominator**

$$5(5875) - (165)^2 = 29375 - 27225 = 2150$$

**Step 6: Compute Final Value**

$$r = \frac{3250}{\sqrt{5000 \times 2150}} = \frac{3250}{\sqrt{10{,}750{,}000}} = \frac{3250}{3278.68} \approx 0.991$$

**Step 7: Interpretation**

$r \approx +0.99$ indicates a **very strong positive linear correlation** between oven temperature and cake hardness. The team should consider temperature as a likely root cause (potential X) and proceed to confirmation testing.

---

### 3.4 Out-of-Control Signals (Western Electric / Nelson Rules)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A single point outside control limits is NOT the only signal of instability.** KTU expects students to know at least the following four supplementary rules:

1. **Rule 1**: One point beyond ±3σ (the basic rule).
2. **Rule 2**: Two out of three successive points beyond ±2σ on the same side.
3. **Rule 3**: Four out of five successive points beyond ±1σ on the same side.
4. **Rule 4**: Eight or more successive points on the same side of the center line (a *run*).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Decision Flow for Selecting the Correct Control Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Process Data Available?] --> B{Data Type?}
    B -- Continuous Measurement --> C{Sample Size n Constant?}
    C -- Yes, Small n 2 to 5 --> D[Use X-bar and R Chart]
    C -- Yes, n greater than 10 --> E[Use X-bar and S Chart]
    C -- No, n equals 1 --> F[Use I and MR Chart]
    B -- Discrete or Categorical --> G{Defective Units or Defects?}
    G -- Defective Units Proportion --> H{Sample Size Constant?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use np Chart]
    H -- No --> J[Use p Chart]
    G -- Number of Defects --> K{Inspection Unit Constant?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Use c Chart]
    K -- No --> M[Use u Chart]
    D --> N[End: Selected Chart Ready for Plotting]
    E --> N
    F --> N
    I --> N
    J --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Out-of-Control Diagnosis Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Subgroup Plotted on Control Chart] --> B{Point Beyond UCL or LCL?}
    B -- YES --> C[Rule 1 Triggered: Investigate Immediately]
    B -- NO --> D{2 of 3 Consecutive Points Beyond 2 Sigma?}
    D -- YES --> C
    D -- NO --> E{4 of 5 Consecutive Points Beyond 1 Sigma?}
    E -- YES --> C
    E -- NO --> F{8 Consecutive Points on One Side of CL?}
    F -- YES --> C
    F -- NO --> G[Process Still In Control]
    C --> H[Identify Special Cause Using Ishikawa or 5-Why]
    H --> I[Remove Special Cause and Recompute Limits]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Layout of a Standard X-bar Control Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[UCL Upper Control Limit Line] --> B[Subgroup Mean 1 Plot]
    B --> C[Subgroup Mean 2 Plot]
    C --> D[Subgroup Mean 3 Plot]
    D --> E[Subgroup Mean 4 Plot]
    E --> F[Subgroup Mean 5 Plot]
    F --> G[CL Center Line representing Grand Mean]
    G --> H[Subgroup Mean 6 Plot]
    H --> I[Subgroup Mean 7 Plot]
    I --> J[Subgroup Mean 8 Plot]
    J --> K[Subgroup Mean 9 Plot]
    K --> L[Subgroup Mean 10 Plot]
    L --> M[LCL Lower Control Limit Line]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Layout — Scatter Diagram Interpretation Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ZoneA [Strong Positive Correlation: r close to plus 1]
        A1[Points cluster tightly along upward sloping line]
    end
    subgraph ZoneB [Strong Negative Correlation: r close to minus 1]
        B1[Points cluster tightly along downward sloping line]
    end
    subgraph ZoneC [No Linear Correlation: r close to 0]
        C1[Random cloud of points with no discernible trend]
    end
    subgraph ZoneD [Curvilinear Correlation]
        D1[Parabolic or U-shaped pattern: r may be near 0 but relationship exists]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q1.** Define a Scatter Diagram. Mention any **two** correlation patterns it can reveal.
**Model Answer:**
A Scatter Diagram is a graphical tool that plots paired data $(x, y)$ on a Cartesian plane to study the relationship between two variables. Two correlation patterns are: (1) **Positive Correlation** — as $x$ increases, $y$ also increases (e.g., study hours vs. marks, $r > 0$), and (2) **Negative Correlation** — as $x$ increases, $y$ decreases (e.g., machine wear vs. output quality, $r < 0$). Other patterns include no correlation and curvilinear correlation.

---

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Q2.** What are the **three control limits** of a Shewhart control chart, and at what statistical distance are they set?
**Model Answer:**
The three control limits are: (1) **Upper Control Limit (UCL)**, (2) **Center Line (CL)** representing the process mean, and (3) **Lower Control Limit (LCL)**. They are set at **±3 standard deviations (3σ)** from the process mean, which captures approximately **99.73%** of common-cause variation per the empirical rule of normal distribution.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** use Specification Limits (USL/LSL) instead of Control Limits (UCL/LCL). This is the most common error; marks are deducted immediately.
> - **Do NOT** forget to state the tabulated constant (e.g., $A_2 = 0.729$ for $n = 4$). Without the constant value, the limit calculation earns zero marks.
> - **Do NOT** interpret the correlation coefficient $r$ as causation. $r = 0.99$ only proves *association*, not *cause-effect*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> - Always write the **units** (ml, °C, N, kg) in your final answer; missing units lose 0.5–1 mark per answer.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** A CNC machining process produces shafts with target diameter **25.00 mm**. Five subgroups of size $n = 4$ are taken and the following data recorded:

| Subgroup | $\bar{X}_i$ (mm) | $R_i$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 25.10 | 0.30 |
| 2 | 25.20 | 0.40 |
| 3 | 25.05 | 0.35 |
| 4 | 24.95 | 0.25 |
| 5 | 25.15 | 0.30 |

Construct an **$\bar{X}$-chart** and an **R-chart** for this process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Grand Mean**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{25.10 + 25.20 + 25.05 + 24.95 + 25.15}{5} = \frac{125.45}{5} = 25.09 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute Average Range**

$$\bar{R} = \frac{0.30 + 0.40 + 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.30}{5} = \frac{1.60}{5} = 0.32 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Look up constants for $n = 4$**

$A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$.

**Step 4: $\bar{X}$-chart limits**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 25.09 + (0.729)(0.32) = 25.09 + 0.233 = 25.323 \text{ mm}$$

$$CL_{\bar{X}} = 25.09 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 25.09 - 0.233 = 24.857 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: R-chart limits**

$$UCL_R = (2.282)(0.32) = 0.730 \text{ mm}$$

$$CL_R = 0.32 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_R = (0)(0.32) = 0.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**

All five $\bar{X}_i$ values lie within $[24.857, 25.323]$ and all five $R_i$ values lie within $[0, 0.730]$. Therefore, **the process is in statistical control**. However, note that the grand mean (25.09) is offset from the target (25.00), suggesting a *common-cause* centering issue that should be addressed by adjusting the machine.

---

**(b)** Explain the **concept, structure, and utility** of a Scatter Diagram. Describe the **four correlation patterns** it can identify and compute the **Pearson correlation coefficient** for the following data: **(7 marks)**

| $x$ | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 3 | 5 | 8 | 9 | 12 |

**Model Solution:**

**Concept and Structure:** A Scatter Diagram plots paired $(x, y)$ data on a two-dimensional plane to study the relationship between a presumed cause ($x$, on horizontal axis) and an effect ($y$, on vertical axis). It is qualitative for visual pattern recognition but can be quantified using $r$.

**Four Correlation Patterns:**
1. **Strong Positive Correlation** (dots trend up tightly).
2. **Strong Negative Correlation** (dots trend down tightly).
3. **No Correlation** (random cloud, no trend).
4. **Curvilinear Correlation** (parabolic or U-shape; $r$ alone is insufficient).

**Computation:**

| $x$ | $y$ | $x^2$ | $y^2$ | $xy$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 3 | 4 | 9 | 6 |
| 4 | 5 | 16 | 25 | 20 |
| 6 | 8 | 36 | 64 | 48 |
| 8 | 9 | 64 | 81 | 72 |
| 10 | 12 | 100 | 144 | 120 |
| **Σ = 30** | **Σ = 37** | **Σ = 220** | **Σ = 323** | **Σ = 266** |

$$r = \frac{5(266) - (30)(37)}{\sqrt{[5(220) - (30)^2][5(323) - (37)^2]}}$$

$$r = \frac{1330 - 1110}{\sqrt{[1100 - 900][1615 - 1369]}} = \frac{220}{\sqrt{(200)(246)}} = \frac{220}{\sqrt{49200}} = \frac{220}{221.81} \approx 0.992$$

**Interpretation:** $r \approx +0.992$ indicates a **very strong positive linear correlation**. Variable $x$ is a strong candidate cause for variations in $y$.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** A call center receives complaints. Over 10 days, the number of complaints recorded daily is: 8, 10, 7, 12, 9, 11, 8, 13, 10, 9. Construct a **c-chart** and comment on the stability of the process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the Center Line (Average Defects per Day)**

$$\bar{c} = \frac{8 + 10 + 7 + 12 + 9 + 11 + 8 + 13 + 10 + 9}{10} = \frac{97}{10} = 9.7 \text{ defects/day}$$

**Step 2: Compute Standard Deviation**

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\bar{c}} = \sqrt{9.7} \approx 3.114$$

**Step 3: Compute Control Limits**

$$UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}} = 9.7 + 3(3.114) = 9.7 + 9.343 = 19.04$$

$$CL_c = 9.7$$

$$LCL_c = 9.7 - 9.343 = 0.36 \quad (\text{rounded to } 0)$$

**Step 4: Check Each Subgroup**

| Day | $c_i$ | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8 | Inside |
| 2 | 10 | Inside |
| 3 | 7 | Inside |
| 4 | 12 | Inside |
| 5 | 9 | Inside |
| 6 | 11 | Inside |
| 7 | 8 | Inside |
| 8 | 13 | Inside |
| 9 | 10 | Inside |
| 10 | 9 | Inside |

**Step 5: Conclusion**

All 10 daily complaint counts fall inside the control limits $[0.36, 19.04]$. The process is **in statistical control** with respect to the number of complaints.

---

**(b)** Define a **Control Chart**. With a neat sketch, explain its structure. Discuss **why** control limits are set at ±3σ and not at ±2σ or ±4σ. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A Control Chart is a time-series plot of a process statistic (e.g., $\bar{X}$, $R$, $p$, $c$) with a center line (CL) and upper/lower control limits (UCL/LCL), used to detect special-cause variation and maintain statistical process control.

**Structure (ASCII Sketch):**

```
        UCL ───────────────────────────  +3σ
                                         │
       Day1   Day2   Day3   Day4   Day5  │  (points)
          •      •      •      •      •  │
                                         │
        CL  ───────────────────────────   μ
                                         │
                                         │
                                         │
        LCL ───────────────────────────  -3σ
        ─────────────────────────────────
              Time →
```

**Why ±3σ and not ±2σ or ±4σ?**

The choice of 3σ is the optimal **economic-statistical balance** discovered by Dr. Walter Shewhart (1924):

| Choice | Probability of False Alarm (Type I Error) | Probability of Missing Real Shift (Type II Error) | Economic Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| ±2σ | 4.55% — Too high | Low | Many false stoppages; wasted investigation costs |
| ±3σ | 0.27% — Optimal | Moderate | **Balanced** — minimum total cost of operation + monitoring |
| ±4σ | 0.006% — Very low | High | Real shifts go undetected; defective products reach customers |

**Conclusion:** The **3σ limit minimizes the average economic loss** by balancing false alarms against missed detections. This is the global industrial standard codified in ISO 9001, IATF 16949, and ASQ Six Sigma Body of Knowledge.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Scatter Diagram** is a *qualitative* correlation tool; the **Pearson $r$** quantifies only **linear** association (range $-1$ to $+1$).
- [ ] **Curvilinear relationships** give $r \approx 0$ even when a strong non-linear link exists — always plot before computing $r$.
- [ ] **Correlation $\ne$ Causation.** Always follow up a strong $r$ with DOE or hypothesis testing.
- [ ] **Control Chart** uses **3σ** limits based on **99.73%** empirical coverage of normal distribution.
- [ ] **CL = process mean**, **UCL/LCL = ±3σ limits**, *not* specification limits.
- [ ] **Variables charts** require continuous data; **Attribute charts** require discrete or pass/fail data.
- [ ] Use **$\bar{X}$-R charts** for $n = 2$ to $5$; use **$\bar{X}$-S charts** for $n \ge 10$.
- [ ] Use **p-chart** for variable sample size defectives; **np-chart** for constant sample size defectives.
- [ ] Use **c-chart** for variable inspection unit defects; **u-chart** for constant inspection unit defects.
- [ ] Constants $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$, $d_2$, $E_2$ depend on subgroup size $n$ — **always cite the constant value with the limit calculation**.
- [ ] **Out-of-control signals** include: any point beyond ±3σ, 2-of-3 beyond ±2σ, 4-of-5 beyond ±1σ, or 8 consecutive points on one side.
- [ ] A process can be **in statistical control** but **incapable** of meeting specifications — this is a *common-cause* problem requiring fundamental process redesign.
- [ ] **In Six Sigma DMAIC**: Scatter Diagrams belong to the **Analyze** phase (root cause validation), and Control Charts belong to the **Control** phase (sustained monitoring).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Benchmarking — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Benchmarking** is the continuous, systematic process of measuring an organization's products, services, processes, and performance against those of **best-in-class organizations** anywhere in the world, with the explicit objective of identifying improvement gaps and incorporating superior practices to enhance competitive position.

The term was coined by **Robert C. Camp** of Xerox Corporation in the late 1970s. Camp defined benchmarking as:

> *"The search for industry best practices that lead to superior performance."*

In the Six Sigma (**DMAIC — Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control**) framework, Benchmarking sits primarily within the **Measure** and **Analyze** phases, providing the external reference standard against which a process's **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** characteristics are compared.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Competitive Analysis:**
> Benchmarking is **not** spying on competitors. It is a structured, ethical, and cooperative learning discipline that focuses on **processes** rather than on **products** or **prices**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Building

Imagine a student preparing for the KTU semester exam. Instead of blindly studying, the student:

1. Identifies the **university topper** (the benchmark partner).
2. Examines the topper's **study process** — number of hours, revision cycles, reference books, mock tests.
3. Compares it with **their own process** — gap identification.
4. **Adapts** the better methods and **improves** performance.

**The student = Your organization**
**The topper = Best-in-class performer (benchmark partner)**
**Study process = Business process**
**Marks = Performance metric (Sigma level, DPMO, Yield)**

This is exactly what benchmarking achieves — it is **learning from the best** rather than reinventing the wheel.

## 1.3 Why Benchmarking in Six Sigma?

| Driver | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Performance Gap Recognition** | Reveals how far current performance lags world-class standards. |
| **Goal Setting** | Provides realistic, externally validated targets for Six Sigma projects. |
| **Best Practice Transfer** | Avoids trial-and-error by adopting proven processes. |
| **Cultural Transformation** | Shifts mindset from "good enough" to "world-class." |
| **Strategic Alignment** | Links improvement initiatives to external market realities. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Six Sigma Metric:** Performance in benchmarking is often quantified using **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** and **Sigma Level (Z)**. World-class performance typically corresponds to **≥ 4.5 Sigma** (≈ **3.4 DPMO**).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Phases of the Benchmarking Process (Robert Camp's Model)

Benchmarking is not a one-time event — it is a **closed-loop, continuous cycle**. Robert Camp formalized it into five sequential phases (often called the **PDCA-aligned Benchmarking Cycle**):

### Phase 1 — PLAN
- Identify **what to benchmark** (process, function, metric).
- Select the **benchmarking partner** (internal or external).
- Define the **data collection methodology**.
- Establish **measurement systems** and **performance metrics**.

### Phase 2 — COLLECT (What / Who / How)
- Gather data on **your own process** (internal baseline).
- Gather data on the **partner's process** (external reference).
- Use site visits, surveys, interviews, public databases (e.g., APQC — American Productivity & Quality Center).
- Maintain confidentiality and ethical conduct.

### Phase 3 — ANALYZE
- Compute the **performance gap** between current and best-in-class.
- Identify **root causes** of the gap (using Pareto, Fishbone, Regression).
- Determine **why** the partner performs better (causal drivers, not just numbers).

### Phase 4 — ADAPT
- Translate the partner's superior practices into **your organizational context**.
- Design the **future state** process map.
- Pilot and validate before full-scale rollout.

### Phase 5 — IMPROVE (Monitor)
- Implement the redesigned process.
- Track KPIs continuously.
- Re-benchmark periodically (because "best" keeps moving).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** Camp's original model had 10 steps; the 5-phase simplified model (Plan–Collect–Analyze–Adapt–Improve) is what KTU examiners expect in the 2024 scheme.

## 2.2 The Four Pillars of Benchmarking (Camp's Benchmarking Code of Conduct)

Ethical benchmarking is governed by **four principles**:

| Pillar | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Principle 1: Reciprocity** | Share information mutually with partners; do not be a "free rider." |
| **Principle 2: Analogy** | Only benchmark practices applicable to **your** business model. |
| **Principle 3: Integrity** | Be honest about what you observed and what you will do with it. |
| **Principle 4: Confidentiality** | Treat all partner data as strictly confidential; obtain written NDAs. |

## 2.3 Types of Benchmarking (Critical for KTU)

| Type | Focus | Data Source | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal Benchmarking** | Comparison **between units/departments** of the **same organization** | Internal reports, internal audits | Comparing yield of Plant A vs. Plant B in the same firm |
| **Competitive (External) Benchmarking** | Direct comparison with **direct competitors** | Reverse engineering, annual reports, public data | Tata Motors benchmarking against Maruti Suzuki |
| **Functional Benchmarking** | Comparison with **leaders in similar functions** in **any industry** | Site visits, conferences, APQC databases | A hospital's billing process benchmarked against an airline's ticketing system |
| **Generic (Best-in-Class) Benchmarking** | Comparison with the **absolute best performer**, regardless of industry | Cross-industry research | A bakery studying Toyota's lean production system |
| **Strategic Benchmarking** | Comparison of **strategic decisions and business models** | Case studies, alliances | Studying Netflix's pivot to disrupt Blockbuster |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The four most tested types are **Internal, Competitive, Functional, and Generic**. Always cite **Robert Camp (Xerox)** as the originator when a definitional question appears.

## 2.4 The Benchmarking Gap Formula (High-Yield Mathematical Anchor)

The performance gap is the foundational quantification in benchmarking. Two complementary formulations are used:

### (a) Absolute Gap

$$G_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}}$$

Where:
- $G_{\text{abs}}$ = Absolute performance gap (positive ⇒ underperforming)
- $P_{\text{best}}$ = Best-in-class performance value
- $P_{\text{current}}$ = Current organization performance value

### (b) Relative (Percentage) Gap

$$G_{\text{rel}} = \frac{P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{best}}} \times 100\%$$

### (c) Improvement Multiple

$$M_{\text{improve}} = \frac{P_{\text{best}}}{P_{\text{current}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When **DPMO** is the metric, a *lower* value is better, so the gap formula **inverts** the sign. Always check whether "higher = better" or "lower = better" before applying.

## 2.5 Benchmarking-to-Six-Sigma Integration Formula

Once a benchmarked DPMO target is established, the corresponding **Sigma Level (Z)** is computed using the standard Six Sigma yield-to-sigma conversion:

$$Z = \Phi^{-1}\left(1 - \text{DPMO} \times 10^{-6}\right) + 1.5$$

Where:
- $Z$ = Process sigma level
- $\Phi^{-1}$ = Inverse of the standard normal CDF
- The **+1.5 shift** accounts for long-term process drift

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | Usage in Benchmarking |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Absolute Gap | $G_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}}$ | Same units as metric | Direct performance deficit |
| 2 | Relative Gap (%) | $G_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{best}}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Normalized comparison across metrics |
| 3 | Improvement Multiple | $M_{\text{improve}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{best}}}{P_{\text{current}}}$ | Dimensionless | "How many times better" |
| 4 | DPMO Definition | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | Defects per million opportunities | Baseline metric |
| 5 | Yield | $Y = \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}}\right)^{U}$ | Fraction (0–1) | First-pass yield |
| 6 | Sigma Level | $Z = \Phi^{-1}(1 - \text{DPMO}/10^{6}) + 1.5$ | Sigma units | Translates benchmark to sigma |
| 7 | Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Fraction (0–1) | Multi-process comparison |
| 8 | Process Capability | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | Process spread vs. spec |
| 9 | Cp to DPMO Conversion | $\text{DPMO} \approx 10^{6} \cdot \Phi(-3 C_p)$ | DPMO | Quick Cp → DPMO estimate |

> **Units Reminder:** DPMO is in *defects per million opportunities* (dimensionless ratio); Sigma level $Z$ is in *standard deviations*; Gap can be in any native metric units.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** A foundry benchmarks its **scrap rate (%)** against the global leader to set Six Sigma project targets.
- **Software Industry:** Comparing **mean-time-to-failure (MTTF)** of servers against AWS-class best-in-class datacenters.
- **Healthcare:** Benchmarking **door-to-balloon time** in cardiac units across hospitals.
- **Process Engineering:** Comparing **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** across plants — a direct Functional Benchmarking use case.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Benchmarking Gap Calculation with Sigma Conversion

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style numerical):**
> A Six Sigma project team in *Kerala Cycles Pvt. Ltd.* measures its current frame-welding defect rate at **4,500 DPMO**. After studying a world-class Japanese competitor, the team identifies that the benchmarked defect rate is **233 DPMO**. Compute:
> 1. The absolute gap in DPMO.
> 2. The relative percentage gap.
> 3. The improvement multiple required.
> 4. The current and benchmarked Sigma Levels.
> 5. The Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) assuming the welding station has **3 sub-steps**, each yielding the current DPMO, and benchmark if all three operate at the benchmarked DPMO.

---

### Step 1 — Absolute Gap in DPMO

We apply the absolute gap formula:

$$G_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}}$$

Substituting $P_{\text{best}} = 233$ and $P_{\text{current}} = 4500$:

$$G_{\text{abs}} = 233 - 4500 = -4267 \text{ DPMO}$$

The negative sign indicates that for DPMO (lower = better), the current process is *worse* than benchmark. Reporting convention: **Gap = 4267 DPMO above benchmark**.

---

### Step 2 — Relative Percentage Gap

Using the percentage-gap formula:

$$G_{\text{rel}} = \frac{P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{best}}} \times 100\%$$

$$G_{\text{rel}} = \frac{233 - 4500}{233} \times 100\%$$

$$G_{\text{rel}} = \frac{-4267}{233} \times 100\%$$

$$G_{\text{rel}} \approx -1831.33\%$$

Magnitude interpretation: The current process produces **~18.3× more defects** than the benchmark partner.

---

### Step 3 — Improvement Multiple

$$M_{\text{improve}} = \frac{P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{best}}} = \frac{4500}{233}$$

$$M_{\text{improve}} \approx 19.31$$

The welding process must improve by a factor of **≈ 19.31** to match the benchmark. This is a **stretch goal**, signalling that a step-change (not incremental Kaizen) is needed.

---

### Step 4 — Sigma Level Conversion

We use the long-term sigma equation:

$$Z = \Phi^{-1}\left(1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6}\right) + 1.5$$

**Current process:**

$$Z_{\text{current}} = \Phi^{-1}\left(1 - \frac{4500}{10^6}\right) + 1.5$$

$$Z_{\text{current}} = \Phi^{-1}(0.9955) + 1.5$$

From standard normal tables: $\Phi^{-1}(0.9955) \approx 2.62$

$$Z_{\text{current}} \approx 2.62 + 1.5 = 4.12 \sigma$$

**Benchmarked process:**

$$Z_{\text{best}} = \Phi^{-1}\left(1 - \frac{233}{10^6}\right) + 1.5$$

$$Z_{\text{best}} = \Phi^{-1}(0.999767) + 1.5$$

From standard normal tables: $\Phi^{-1}(0.999767) \approx 3.50$

$$Z_{\text{best}} \approx 3.50 + 1.5 = 5.00 \sigma$$

> **Sigma gap = 5.00 − 4.12 = 0.88 σ.**

---

### Step 5 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

First-pass yield for a single step from DPMO:

$$Y = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6}$$

**Current single-step yield:**

$$Y_{\text{current}} = 1 - \frac{4500}{10^6} = 1 - 0.0045 = 0.9955$$

**RTY across 3 current steps:**

$$\text{RTY}_{\text{current}} = (Y_{\text{current}})^3 = (0.9955)^3$$

$$\text{RTY}_{\text{current}} \approx 0.9866 \quad \text{(or 98.66\%)}$$

**Benchmarked single-step yield:**

$$Y_{\text{best}} = 1 - \frac{233}{10^6} = 1 - 0.000233 = 0.999767$$

**Benchmarked RTY across 3 steps:**

$$\text{RTY}_{\text{best}} = (0.999767)^3 \approx 0.999301 \quad \text{(or 99.9301\%)}$$

**RTY improvement (delta):**

$$\Delta \text{RTY} = 0.999301 - 0.9866 = 0.012701 \quad (\approx 1.27 \text{ percentage points})$$

---

## 3.2 Benchmarking Decision Matrix (When to Use Which Type)

| Scenario | Recommended Benchmarking Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Multiple plants with same process | **Internal** | Easiest data access; comparable conditions |
| Trying to leapfrog direct rival | **Competitive** | Same industry, same customer expectations |
| Looking for innovation beyond industry norms | **Functional** | Best practice transfer across functions |
| Aspirational target — "world's best" | **Generic** | Sets absolute ceiling of excellence |
| Re-architecting business model | **Strategic** | Long-horizon, broad-scope learning |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Table — Pros & Cons of Each Benchmarking Type

| Type | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|
| Internal | Low cost; fast; high data accuracy | Limited innovation; only known practices |
| Competitive | Industry-relevant; directly applicable | Data scarcity; legal/ethical risks; partners often refuse |
| Functional | High innovation potential; willing partners | Requires adaptation; cultural/industry fit issues |
| Generic | Truly best-in-class; breakthrough insights | Highest cost; slowest; maximum adaptation effort |
| Strategic | Long-term competitive advantage | Requires executive sponsorship; high risk |

---

## 3.4 Quick-Reference Benchmarking Selection Heuristic

$$
\text{Choose Benchmarking Type} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Internal}, & \text{if intra-org learning suffices} \\
\text{Competitive}, & \text{if rivals hold the answer} \\
\text{Functional}, & \text{if the function (not industry) defines excellence} \\
\text{Generic}, & \text{if world-class is the standard} \\
\text{Strategic}, & \text{if business-model redefinition is the goal}
\end{cases}
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five-Phase Benchmarking Cycle (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Phase 1 PLAN - Identify subject - Select partner - Define metrics"] --> B["Phase 2 COLLECT - Internal baseline data - External partner data - Site visits & surveys"]
    B --> C["Phase 3 ANALYZE - Compute gap - Root cause analysis - Determine best practices"]
    C --> D["Phase 4 ADAPT - Translate practices - Design future state - Pilot validation"]
    D --> E["Phase 5 IMPROVE - Full rollout - Monitor KPIs - Continuous re-benchmark"]
    E --> A
```

> The arrow returning from *Improve* to *Plan* denotes that benchmarking is a **continuous closed-loop cycle**, not a one-off project.

## 4.2 Types of Benchmarking — Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["BENCHMARKING"] --> R1["INTERNAL"]
    R --> R2["EXTERNAL"]
    R2 --> R2A["COMPETITIVE"]
    R2 --> R2B["FUNCTIONAL"]
    R2 --> R2C["GENERIC"]
    R --> R3["STRATEGIC"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Benchmarking within DMAIC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D["D DEFINE - CTQ identification"] --> M["M MEASURE - Baseline data"]
    M --> BN["BENCHMARKING MODULE - External reference - Gap computation"]
    BN --> A["A ANALYZE - Root cause of gap"]
    A --> I["I IMPROVE - Best practice adoption"]
    I --> C["C CONTROL - Sustain gains - Re-benchmark trigger"]
    C -.feedback.-> M
```

> [!NOTE]
> Benchmarking plugs in primarily at the **Measure → Analyze** boundary, feeding external best-in-class data into the analytical phase.

## 4.4 Gap Analysis Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Current Performance Pcur"] --> SUB["Subtract"]
    P2["Benchmark Performance Pbest"] --> SUB
    SUB --> G["Gap Gabs = Pbest - Pcur"]
    G --> DEC["Decision - Acceptable or Close Gap"]
    DEC --> ACT["Action Plan - ADAPT phase"]
```

## 4.5 Benchmarking Code of Conduct — Pillars Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CC["CODE OF CONDUCT Robert Camp"] --> P1["RECIPROCITY Share data mutually"]
    CC --> P2["ANALOGY Benchmark like processes"]
    CC --> P3["INTEGRITY Honest reporting"]
    CC --> P4["CONFIDENTIALITY Protect partner data"]
```
