# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define benchmarking. List any four types of benchmarking.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
> Benchmarking is the continuous and systematic process of measuring an organization's processes, products, and services against those of the best-in-class performers in any industry, with the goal of identifying performance gaps and adopting superior practices.

**Four Types:**
1. **Internal Benchmarking** — Comparing similar processes across units within the same organization.
2. **Competitive Benchmarking** — Comparing against direct industry competitors.
3. **Functional Benchmarking** — Comparing similar business functions across different industries.
4. **Generic Benchmarking** — Comparing against the absolute best-in-class regardless of industry.

> A fifth type, **Strategic Benchmarking**, may also be cited for full credit.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 each × 4 types)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State any two principles of the Benchmarking Code of Conduct.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Benchmarking Code of Conduct**, formalized by Robert Camp, has four principles:

1. **Principle of Reciprocity:** Information should be exchanged mutually; partners must not act as one-way free riders.
2. **Principle of Confidentiality:** All partner data must be treated as strictly confidential, protected by formal NDAs and secure storage.

*(Additional principles for reference: Analogy and Integrity.)*

*(3 marks: 1.5 each × 2)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five phases of the benchmarking process** as given by Robert Camp. Mention the typical tools used in each phase. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A hospital's current **average patient discharge time** is **5.2 hours**. The benchmark partner hospital reports **2.8 hours**. Compute:
1. The absolute and relative gap.
2. The improvement multiple.
3. If discharge time must fall to **3.0 hours** in six months, what **percentage reduction per month** (linear) is needed? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Five Phases of Benchmarking (Robert Camp):**

**Phase 1 — PLAN**
- Decide **what** to benchmark (the CTQ process).
- Identify the **benchmark partner**.
- Define KPIs and data collection plan.
- *Tools used:* Project charter, SIPOC diagram, Gantt chart, CTQ tree.

**Phase 2 — COLLECT**
- Gather **internal baseline** data.
- Collect **partner data** through surveys, site visits, public databases (e.g., APQC).
- *Tools used:* Questionnaires, structured interviews, data-collection templates, NDA forms.

**Phase 3 — ANALYZE**
- Compute **performance gap**.
- Identify **root causes** of the gap.
- Determine the **why** behind partner superiority.
- *Tools used:* Pareto chart, Fishbone diagram, regression, gap analysis matrix.

**Phase 4 — ADAPT**
- Translate partner's best practices into **your** organizational context.
- Design **future state** process map.
- Pilot and refine.
- *Tools used:* Future-state process map, FMEA, simulation, pilot PDCA cycles.

**Phase 5 — IMPROVE**
- Deploy the redesigned process organization-wide.
- Monitor KPIs continuously.
- **Re-benchmark** to catch up when "best" moves.
- *Tools used:* Control charts, dashboards, Six Sigma control plans.

> *(7 marks: ~1.4 marks per phase including tools.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:**
- $P_{\text{current}} = 5.2$ hours
- $P_{\text{best}} = 2.8$ hours
- Target: $P_{\text{target}} = 3.0$ hours in **6 months** (linear reduction)

**Step 1 — Absolute Gap:**

$$G_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}} = 2.8 - 5.2 = -2.4 \text{ hours}$$

> Magnitude = **2.4 hours** above benchmark.

**Step 2 — Relative Gap:**

$$G_{\text{rel}} = \frac{2.8 - 5.2}{2.8} \times 100\%$$

$$G_{\text{rel}} = \frac{-2.4}{2.8} \times 100\% \approx -85.71\%$$

> The current discharge time is **~85.71% higher** than benchmark.

**Step 3 — Improvement Multiple:**

$$M_{\text{improve}} = \frac{P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{best}}} = \frac{5.2}{2.8} = \frac{26}{14} = \frac{13}{7} \approx 1.857$$

> The process must improve by a factor of **≈ 1.86** to match benchmark.

**Step 4 — Linear Monthly Reduction Rate (Target Path):**

Required reduction over 6 months:

$$\Delta_{\text{total}} = 5.2 - 3.0 = 2.2 \text{ hours}$$

Linear per-month reduction:

$$\Delta_{\text{monthly}} = \frac{\Delta_{\text{total}}}{6} = \frac{2.2}{6} \approx 0.3667 \text{ hours/month}$$

Percentage reduction per month (relative to **starting value** $P_{\text{current}} = 5.2$):

$$r_{\text{monthly}} = \frac{0.3667}{5.2} \times 100\% \approx 7.05\% \text{ per month}$$

> *(Note: If the question intends percentage of the *current* value at the start of each month (geometric), the answer would differ slightly. Linear interpretation is preferred for KTU unless otherwise stated.)*

> *(7 marks: 2 for gap, 1 for multiple, 4 for monthly percentage.)*

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Differentiate between **Internal, Competitive, Functional, and Generic Benchmarking** with one industry example each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Six Sigma team in a call center measures its current **call abandonment rate** at **8.5%**. After benchmarking, the world-class target is **2.0%**. The team's project goal is to reach **3.0%** within four months.
1. Compute the absolute and percentage gap to the world-class target.
2. Compute the percentage reduction needed (relative to current) to reach the **3.0%** intermediate target.
3. Convert both current and world-class rates to **DPMO equivalents** assuming **1 defect opportunity per call**, then compute the corresponding Sigma Levels (long-term). *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Differentiation Table (7 marks: 1.75 each):**

| Type | Comparison With | Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal** | Other units of the **same** organization | Comparing first-pass yield of Line A vs. Line B in Infosys Mysore campus |
| **Competitive** | **Direct competitors** in same industry | Reliance Jio benchmarking 4G latency against Bharti Airtel |
| **Functional** | Best-in-class in **same function** in any industry | Apollo Hospital's appointment scheduling benchmarked against Indian Railways reservation system |
| **Generic** | **Absolute best** anywhere regardless of industry | Toyota Production System studied by an Indian bakery for process flow |

---

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:**
- $P_{\text{current}} = 8.5\%$
- $P_{\text{best}} = 2.0\%$
- $P_{\text{target}} = 3.0\%$
- 1 opportunity per call

**Step 1 — Absolute Gap to World-Class:**

$$G_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}} = 2.0\% - 8.5\% = -6.5 \text{ percentage points}$$

**Step 2 — Percentage Gap:**

$$G_{\text{rel}} = \frac{2.0 - 8.5}{2.0} \times 100\% = -325\%$$

> The current rate is **325% above** the benchmark (i.e., 4.25× higher).

**Step 3 — Reduction Needed to Reach 3.0%:**

$$\Delta P = 8.5 - 3.0 = 5.5 \text{ percentage points}$$

$$r_{\text{needed}} = \frac{5.5}{8.5} \times 100\% \approx 64.71\%$$

> A **64.71% reduction** in abandonment rate is required.

**Step 4 — Convert to DPMO:**

DPMO = Defects × 10⁶ / (Units × Opportunities). With 1 opportunity/call, defect rate (in fraction) directly scales to DPMO.

$$P_{\text{current}} = 0.085 \Rightarrow \text{DPMO}_{\text{current}} = 0.085 \times 10^6 = 85{,}000$$

$$P_{\text{best}} = 0.020 \Rightarrow \text{DPMO}_{\text{best}} = 0.020 \times 10^6 = 20{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Sigma Levels:**

$$Z_{\text{current}} = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{85{,}000}{10^6}\right) + 1.5 = \Phi^{-1}(0.915) + 1.5$$

$$\Phi^{-1}(0.915) \approx 1.37 \Rightarrow Z_{\text{current}} \approx 1.37 + 1.5 = 2.87 \sigma$$

$$Z_{\text{best}} = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{20{,}000}{10^6}\right) + 1.5 = \Phi^{-1}(0.980) + 1.5$$

$$\Phi^{-1}(0.980) \approx 2.05 \Rightarrow Z_{\text{best}} \approx 2.05 + 1.5 = 3.55 \sigma$$

> **Sigma improvement required: ≈ 0.68 σ.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign confusion in Gap Formula** — Always state explicitly whether "lower = better" (DPMO, defects, time) or "higher = better" (yield, sigma). The KTU key awards marks only if the sign is interpreted correctly.
> 2. **Forgetting the +1.5 σ shift** — When converting DPMO to Sigma Level, examiners check for the long-term adjustment. Marks deducted if only the short-term conversion is given.
> 3. **Confusing Benchmarking with Competitive Analysis** — Writing "benchmarking = spying on competitors" costs 1–2 marks. Use the words *"ethical, cooperative, process-focused."*
> 4. **Mixing up the phases** — Many students write "Plan → Do → Check → Act" (PDCA) instead of Camp's five phases (Plan → Collect → Analyze → Adapt → Improve). This is a wrong attribution; PDCA is **Deming's cycle**, not Camp's.
> 5. **Missing tools for each phase** — A phase explained without a tool (Pareto, Fishbone, FMEA, Control Chart) loses up to 1 mark.
> 6. **In numericals, not showing units** — Always carry units (hours, %, DPMO, σ) explicitly in every line.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Benchmarking =** Continuous, systematic comparison against best-in-class — coined by **Robert Camp (Xerox, 1970s)**.
- [ ] **Five Phases:** **Plan → Collect → Analyze → Adapt → Improve** (closed loop, not PDCA).
- [ ] **Four Types (core):** Internal, Competitive, Functional, Generic — plus **Strategic** as a fifth.
- [ ] **Code of Conduct (4 principles):** Reciprocity, Analogy, **Integrity**, **Confidentiality**.
- [ ] **Gap Formulas:** Absolute = $P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}}$; Relative % = $\frac{P_{\text{best}} - P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{best}}} \times 100$.
- [ ] **Direction of "better" matters** — invert the formula for DPMO / defects / time.
- [ ] **DPMO Conversion:** $Z = \Phi^{-1}(1 - \text{DPMO}/10^6) + 1.5$ (always include the +1.5 long-term shift).
- [ ] **Internal Benchmarking** = same org; **Competitive** = rival; **Functional** = same function in any industry; **Generic** = absolute best.
- [ ] **Best practice transfer**, not product/price copying.
- [ ] **RTY** across $n$ steps = $\prod Y_i$ — useful for multi-stage benchmark comparisons.
- [ ] **Benchmarking integrates into DMAIC** primarily at **Measure → Analyze** boundary.
- [ ] **Pitfall:** Don't attribute Camp's cycle to Deming or to PDCA — they are distinct frameworks.
- [ ] **Always carry units** in numericals (hours, %, DPMO, σ).
- [ ] **Ethical obligation** — never benchmark without an NDA and reciprocity commitment.
- [ ] **Continuous, not one-off** — re-benchmark because "best" keeps evolving.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: New Management Tools - Affinity Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** New Management Tools - Affinity Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Affinity Diagram (KJ Method):** An Affinity Diagram is a **qualitative problem-solving and idea-organizing tool** that gathers a large number of ideas, opinions, facts, or issues and groups them into natural, mutually exclusive categories based on their **affinity (natural relationship)**. The technique is formally known as the **KJ Method**, named after its inventor, Japanese anthropologist **Jiro Kawakita (1968)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Six Sigma framework, the Affinity Diagram is classified as one of the **"Seven New Management and Planning Tools"** (the others being the Interrelationship Digraph, Tree Diagram, Matrix Diagram, Matrix Data Analysis, Process Decision Program Chart, and Activity Network Diagram). Unlike the "Seven Old Tools" (which are primarily statistical — Ishikawa, Pareto, Histogram, etc.), the New Tools focus on **planning, communication, and idea synthesis**, making them indispensable during the **Define** and **Analyze** phases of the **DMAIC** lifecycle.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Sorting Laundry":** Imagine you have just returned from a vacation with a massive pile of dirty clothes scattered on the floor. Before washing, you instinctively sort them into groups — whites, dark colors, delicates, and towels. You did not predefine the categories in a manual; you simply looked at the clothes and let their *natural similarity* determine the groups. The **Affinity Diagram** operates on exactly the same principle. Instead of clothes, you sort **ideas, opinions, or problem statements**. Instead of hampers, you create **logical clusters** that the team naturally recognizes. Each "load of laundry" then represents a **theme** (such as *Training Gaps*, *Equipment Issues*, or *Process Delays*), and each "laundry item" represents an individual **card or sticky note** generated during a brainstorming session.

### 1.3 When to Use the Affinity Diagram

The Affinity Diagram is invoked under the following conditions in a Six Sigma project:

- When the problem statement is **broad, vague, or unstructured**.
- When ideas need to be **clustered before root-cause analysis** (Ishikawa construction).
- During **Voice of Customer (VOC)** data reduction from open-ended surveys.
- When a cross-functional team needs to reach **consensus on categorical structure**.
- After **brainstorming sessions** where 50–100+ raw ideas have been generated.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Properties

- **Categorical Validity:** A measure (informal) of how cleanly ideas fit into their assigned affinity groups.
- **Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive (MECE):** A quality target — every card belongs to exactly one group, and every card belongs somewhere.
- **Iteration Count:** Typically **2–4 rounds** of re-grouping are required for convergence.
- **Recommended Card Count:** **40–100 cards** for a single session (per JUSE/JCQ standards).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Sigma Phase Mapping:** The Affinity Diagram is most heavily used in the **Define** phase (to structure the project scope) and the **Analyze** phase (to organize potential causes before feeding them into a Fishbone / 5-Why analysis).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

The Affinity Diagram rests on three underlying principles from cognitive psychology and group dynamics:

1. **Synthesizing Thinking (Yutori-hakuri):** Moving from fragmented individual thoughts toward a **unified, integrated picture** of the problem.
2. **Inductive Grouping:** Categories are *emergent* rather than *predefined*. The team defines the taxonomy by observing the data, not the other way around.
3. **Democratization of Knowledge:** Every participant's idea carries equal weight; hierarchy is suppressed during card placement.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The procedure for constructing an Affinity Diagram follows a disciplined protocol:

- **Step 1 — Frame the Issue:** Write the problem statement as a single concise sentence at the top of the workspace. Example: *"Why is on-time delivery slipping in Q3?"*

- **Step 2 — Generate Raw Data:** Through brainstorming, customer interviews, or field observation, capture each distinct idea, observation, or fact on a separate **3x5 card or sticky note**. Use one sentence per card, written legibly.

- **Step 3 — Display Cards Randomly:** Spread all cards on a wall or large table without any predetermined order. The **lack of order** is intentional — it forces inductive reasoning.

- **Step 4 — Silently Cluster:** Team members move cards into clusters based on *natural affinity* — without discussion or voting. Silence prevents groupthink and ensures independent judgment.

- **Step 5 — Name Each Cluster (Header Cards):** Once clusters stabilize, write a **header card** (often a different color) summarizing the theme of each group. Use **1–3 words**.

- **Step 6 — Resolve Outliers & Duplicates:** Cards that resist grouping are either reworded, merged with a near-neighbor, or placed in a "Miscellaneous / To Investigate" group for follow-up.

- **Step 7 — Draw the Final Diagram:** Place header cards at the top of each column with member cards arrayed beneath, often color-coded by priority.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Rule | Application Context | Notation / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **KJ Method** | Kawakita-Jiro Method (1968) | Origin nomenclature | — |
| **Card Count** | Optimal 40–100 per session | Brainstorming yield control | $N_{cards}$ |
| **Group Count** | Typically 5–10 affinity groups | MECE partitioning target | $N_{groups}$ |
| **Naming Length** | 1–3 words per header card | Concision standard | words |
| **Iteration Rounds** | 2–4 clustering passes | Convergence rule | rounds |
| **Affinity Strength** | Qualitative measure of intra-group similarity | Quality of grouping | high / med / low |
| **MECE Compliance** | $\text{MECE} = 1$ if partitioning is exact | Logical test | 0 or 1 |
| **DMAIC Phase** | Define and Analyze phases | Lifecycle placement | — |
| **Brainstorming Time** | 30–60 minutes per session | Standard facilitator guide | minutes |
| **Time-to-Cluster** | 20–40 minutes silent phase | Standard facilitator guide | minutes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why No Quantitative Formula?** Unlike Shewhart charts or DOE, the Affinity Diagram is a **qualitative synthesis tool**. Its value lies in pattern recognition, not numerical precision. KTU examiners, however, often test the *process logic* and *when-to-use* criteria, so memorize the procedural steps above.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In **manufacturing**, Affinity Diagrams consolidate open-ended operator feedback about machine downtime into actionable maintenance categories. In **software engineering**, Agile retrospectives use mini-affinity exercises to cluster Sprint impediments. In **healthcare Six Sigma projects**, nursing teams affinity-map patient complaint cards before feeding themes into a Fishbone Diagram for root-cause analysis. The tool's strength is its ability to **transform qualitative noise into a structured, decision-ready taxonomy**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — When to Use vs. When to Avoid

The following decision matrix guides tool selection. It is a **deterministic analytical artifact** that KTU examiners frequently test:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision} &= f(\text{Data Type}, \text{Team Size}, \text{Problem Clarity}) \\
\text{If Data Type} &= \text{Qualitative} \;\land\; \text{Team Size} > 3 \;\land\; \text{Problem Clarity} = \text{Low}, \\
\text{Then} \;&\text{Tool} = \text{Affinity Diagram} \\
\text{Else If Data Type} &= \text{Qualitative} \;\land\; \text{Problem Clarity} = \text{High}, \\
\text{Then} \;&\text{Tool} = \text{Tree Diagram} \\
\text{Else If Data Type} &= \text{Quantitative} \;\land\; \text{Problem Clarity} = \text{Low}, \\
\text{Then} \;&\text{Tool} = \text{Pareto Chart}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Logical Derivation of the Clustering Algorithm

The Affinity Diagram's clustering step can be modeled as a **set-partitioning operation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } C &= \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_N\} \quad \text{(set of N raw cards)} \\
\text{Goal:} &\quad \text{Partition } C \text{ into } k \text{ subsets } G_1, G_2, \dots, G_k \\
\text{Subject to:} &\quad \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} G_i = C \quad \text{(collectively exhaustive)} \\
&\quad G_i \cap G_j = \emptyset \quad \forall\, i \neq j \quad \text{(mutually exclusive)} \\
\text{Objective:} &\quad \max \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Affinity}(G_i) \quad \text{(intra-group similarity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where intra-group affinity is qualitatively judged as:

$$
\text{Affinity}(G_i) = \frac{1}{\binom{\vert G_i \vert}{2}} \sum_{a \in G_i} \sum_{b \in G_i, b > a} \text{Sim}(a,b)
$$

with $\text{Sim}(a,b) \in \{\text{high}, \text{medium}, \text{low}\}$ judged by team consensus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The MECE conditions above explain why outliers are critical — they reveal either a missing group or an improperly worded card. **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **collectively exhaustive** constraint and silently discard ideas; this violates Six Sigma's principle of data integrity.

### 3.3 Convergence Rule — Iteration Count

Empirical guidelines from JUSE (Japanese Union of Scientists and Engineers) suggest:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iterations Required} &= \left\lceil \log_2(N_{cards}) \right\rceil \quad \text{(theoretical minimum)} \\
\text{Practical Bound} &= 2 \leq R \leq 4 \quad \text{(JUSE empirical range)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $N_{cards} = 64$: $\lceil \log_2(64) \rceil = 6$, but the JUSE practical rule caps iterations at 4 because participant fatigue degrades judgment quality beyond that point.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example

**Problem:** A hospital Six Sigma Black Belt receives 48 patient complaint cards. Construct an Affinity Diagram framework.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A: Frame the Issue} &\rightarrow \text{"Why are patients dissatisfied in Ward 5?"} \\
\text{Step B: Capture 48 Cards} &\rightarrow C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_{48}\} \\
\text{Step C: Silent Cluster} &\rightarrow G_1, G_2, G_3, G_4, G_5, G_6 \text{ (6 affinity groups)} \\
\text{Step D: Header Cards} &\rightarrow \{ \text{Staffing}, \text{Facilities}, \text{Wait Time}, \\
&\quad\;\; \text{Communication}, \text{Food}, \text{Cleanliness} \} \\
\text{Step E: Verify MECE} &\rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{6} \vert G_i \vert = 48 \;\checkmark \\
\text{Step F: Hand Off} &\rightarrow \text{Feed themes into Fishbone Diagram}
\end{aligned}
$$

This output now serves as the **input structure** for downstream tools like the Interrelationship Digraph or Tree Diagram.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Affinity Diagram Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: Problem Identified]) --> A1[Step 1: Frame the Issue Statement]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Brainstorm and Capture Cards]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Display Cards Randomly]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Silent Clustering by Team]
    A4 --> A5{Step 5: Are Clusters Stable?}
    A5 -- No --> A4
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Step 6: Write Header Cards for Each Group]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Resolve Outliers and Duplicates]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8: Verify MECE Compliance]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9: Finalize Affinity Diagram]
    A9 --> A10([Output: Structured Themes for Fishbone or Tree Diagram])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Tool Integration in DMAIC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEFINE_PHASE[Define Phase]
        VOC[Voice of Customer Capture] --> RM[Raw Ideas and Complaints]
        RM --> AD1[Affinity Diagram Construction]
    end
    subgraph ANALYZE_PHASE[Analyze Phase]
        AD1 --> AD2[Affinity Diagram Themes]
        AD2 --> ID[Interrelationship Digraph]
        ID --> FB[Fishbone Ishikawa Diagram]
        FB --> RCA[Root Cause Identification]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL_PHASE[Control Phase]
        RCA --> IMP[Improvement Actions]
        IMP --> CTL[Control Plan]
    end
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Card-to-Group Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PROB([Problem Statement: Why is on-time delivery slipping in Q3]):::root
    PROB --> G1[Group A: Training Gaps]
    PROB --> G2[Group B: Equipment Failures]
    PROB --> G3[Group C: Process Delays]
    PROB --> G4[Group D: Communication Lapses]
    PROB --> G5[Group E: Documentation Errors]
    G1 --> C1a[Card: Operators unaware of new SOP]
    G1 --> C1b[Card: No refresher training in 18 months]
    G2 --> C2a[Card: CNC Machine 3 vibration fault]
    G2 --> C2b[Card: Forklift battery aging]
    G3 --> C3a[Card: Raw material arrival 2 days late]
    G3 --> C3b[Card: Quality hold causes rerouting]
    G4 --> C4a[Card: Shift handover incomplete]
    G5 --> C5a[Card: Work order missing revision tag]
    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Schematic:** Each "Group" (Group A through Group E) represents a final affinity cluster, and each "Card" beneath it represents one raw observation that has been inductively sorted into that group. The header card sits visually above its member cards in an actual wall diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Affinity Diagram. State any two situations in which it is preferred over other quality tools.*

**Model Answer:**
The Affinity Diagram, also known as the **KJ Method** (developed by Jiro Kawakita in 1968), is a qualitative problem-solving tool that organizes a large number of ideas, facts, or opinions into natural groups based on their **affinity or natural relationship**. Each group is then summarized under a concise header card.

It is preferred when:
1. The problem statement is **broad, vague, or unstructured** and requires clarification before statistical analysis.
2. A large volume of **qualitative data** (e.g., open-ended customer feedback, brainstorming output) must be reduced into a manageable number of themes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the seven New Management and Planning Tools. Why are they called "New" as opposed to the "Old" tools?*

**Model Answer:**
The seven New Management and Planning Tools are:
1. Affinity Diagram
2. Interrelationship Digraph
3. Tree Diagram
4. Matrix Diagram
5. Matrix Data Analysis
6. Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)
7. Activity Network Diagram (Arrow Diagram)

They are called "New" because they were developed primarily for **planning, communication, and managing qualitative information**, whereas the "Old" tools (histograms, Pareto charts, control charts, Ishikawa diagrams, flowcharts, scatter diagrams, check sheets) were designed primarily for **statistical data analysis** of numerical process data.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
*(a)* Explain the **step-by-step procedure** for constructing an Affinity Diagram. Mention the role of silent clustering. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* A manufacturing firm collects 60 customer complaints in a Six Sigma project. Explain how you would use an Affinity Diagram to organize them and describe how the output feeds into subsequent DMAIC tools. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Frame the Issue (Step 1):** Write the problem as one concise question at the top of the workspace.
2. **Generate Cards (Step 2):** Through brainstorming, interviews, or observation, record each distinct idea on a separate card or sticky note. Use one sentence per card.
3. **Display Randomly (Step 3):** Spread all cards without any order. The disorder is intentional and forces inductive reasoning.
4. **Silent Clustering (Step 4):** Team members silently move cards into clusters based on perceived affinity. **No discussion is allowed.** Silence prevents dominant personalities from biasing the groupings and ensures each participant contributes independent judgment.
5. **Header Cards (Step 5):** Once clusters stabilize, write a 1–3 word header card summarizing each group.
6. **Resolve Outliers (Step 6):** Reassign, merge, or reword cards that resist grouping.
7. **Verify MECE (Step 7):** Confirm that every card belongs to exactly one group (MECE compliance).
8. **Finalize (Step 8):** Display the diagram with clear header–member visual hierarchy.

> Role of Silent Clustering: It enforces **independent judgment**, eliminates **groupthink**, and produces **robust, reproducible** groupings.

**Part (b) — 60 Complaint Cards Workflow:**

1. **Define Phase:** Write the issue statement — *"Why are customers dissatisfied with Product X?"*
2. **Capture:** Log all 60 complaints verbatim on individual cards.
3. **Cluster:** Silently group into 6–8 affinity headers (e.g., *Packaging Damage*, *Late Delivery*, *Wrong Specs*, *Poor Documentation*, *Billing Errors*, *After-Sales Support*, *Color Mismatch*, *Allergen Labeling*).
4. **Verify:** Confirm $\sum \vert G_i \vert = 60$ (MECE check).
5. **Hand Off to Analyze Phase:** The affinity groups become the **main branches of a Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram**, with member cards becoming potential **cause statements**.
6. **Downstream Tools:** Use an **Interrelationship Digraph** to identify which affinity group has the strongest causal influence, then a **Pareto Chart** on the group's frequency to prioritize root causes.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**
*(a)* Compare the **Affinity Diagram** with the **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** in terms of purpose, data type, and DMAIC phase placement. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* Discuss the **MECE principle** as applied to the Affinity Diagram. What is the consequence of violating it, and how is it verified? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparative Analysis:**

| Dimension | Affinity Diagram | Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Organize unstructured qualitative data into themes | Identify potential causes of a specific effect |
| **Data Type** | Purely qualitative (ideas, opinions) | Qualitative causes attached to a quantitative effect |
| **Structure** | Emergent clusters (no predefined categories) | Predefined branches (5M + 1E, 4P, etc.) |
| **DMAIC Phase** | Define / Analyze (input stage) | Analyze (root cause exploration) |
| **Input Source** | Brainstorming, VOC, complaints | Outputs of Affinity Diagram, 5-Whys |
| **Output** | Theme clusters | Cause-and-effect hypotheses |

**Part (b) — MECE Principle:**

MECE stands for **Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive** — a partitioning standard inherited from consulting practice (Barbara Minto / McKinsey).

- **Mutually Exclusive:** No card appears in more than one group. Duplicate cards are merged.
- **Collectively Exhaustive:** Every card from the original set is placed in some group; no idea is silently discarded.

**Verification:** Count check —

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} \vert G_i \vert = N_{cards}
$$

**Consequence of Violation:** Loss of ideas, biased prioritization, and erosion of the team's trust in the tool. In a Six Sigma project, this could lead to **missed root causes** and **project failure**, as the team unknowingly optimized on a subset of the actual problem space.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Skipping the silent clustering rationale:** Students often describe the steps but forget to explain *why* silence is critical. Examiners award partial marks only when the cognitive principle (anti-groupthink, independent judgment) is explicitly stated.
> 2. **Confusing Affinity with Fishbone:** These are distinct tools in different phases. Writing that "Affinity Diagram identifies root causes" is a **factual error** worth losing 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the KJ attribution:** Always credit Jiro Kawakita (1968) and JUSE — KTU examiners reward historical accuracy.
> 4. **Omitting MECE compliance:** Any answer on Affinity Diagram construction that does not verify partition completeness is treated as incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Affinity Diagram**
> - Also known as the **KJ Method**, invented by **Jiro Kawakita (1968)**.
> - Belongs to the **Seven New Management and Planning Tools** (qualitative/communication family).
> - **Primary DMAIC use:** Define and Analyze phases.
> - Ideal when the problem is **broad, vague, or qualitative-rich**.
> - Optimal card count: **40–100**; typical groups: **5–10**.
> - Header cards must be **1–3 words** capturing the cluster essence.
> - **Silent clustering** is non-negotiable — it eliminates groupthink.
> - Output must satisfy **MECE** (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive).
> - Hand-off tools: **Fishbone Diagram, Interrelationship Digraph, Tree Diagram**.
> - Distinguished from Fishbone by its **emergent** (not predefined) category structure.
> - Convergence requires **2–4 iterations** of re-clustering.
> - **Anti-pattern:** Discarding outlier cards silently = data integrity violation.
> - **Examiner trigger keywords:** "KJ Method," "affinity," "silent clustering," "MECE," "emergent groups."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inter Relationship Digraphs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inter Relationship Digraphs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inter Relationship Digraph (IRD)** — Also known as the **Interrelationship Diagram**, is a pictorial representation of all the relevant cause-and-effect relationships among a group of issues or factors that contribute to a single effect or problem. It is a quality management tool used during the **Analyze phase** of the **DMAIC** methodology of Six Sigma to move from a list of unrelated factors to a clear understanding of their logical and interconnected influences.

The Inter Relationship Digraph was originally developed by the **Japanese quality expert Dr. Jiro Kawakita** in the form of the **Affinity Diagram** (KJ Method), and was later refined for analytical root-cause studies by quality practitioners. It is officially listed as one of the **Seven Management and Planning Tools** of quality.

In simple engineering terms, an IRD is a **directed graph** where:

- **Nodes** represent factors / causes / issues.
- **Directed arrows** represent logical influence of one factor over another.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it is used in Six Sigma:** When multiple factors (e.g., 8–15) appear to influence a problem, simple brainstorming creates confusion. The IRD organizes these factors into a structured web of influence so that the **key drivers (root causes)** and the **key outcomes (effects)** can be mathematically and visually identified.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a college classroom where many students are failing in a subject. If you just brainstorm, you get 10 reasons: poor attendance, weak basics, difficult syllabus, lack of motivation, poor teaching, noise in class, no remedial classes, parental pressure, hostel distractions, and smartphone addiction.

Now imagine drawing **arrows between them**:
- *Lack of motivation* → *Poor attendance*
- *Poor attendance* → *Weak basics*
- *Weak basics* → *Poor exam performance*
- *Smartphone addiction* → *Lack of motivation*

Suddenly, a **chain of influence** emerges! A few factors like *Lack of motivation* and *Weak basics* have **many arrows pointing OUT** (they drive others). A few like *Poor exam performance* have **many arrows pointing IN** (they are driven). 

> **The IRD does exactly this — it transforms a flat list of causes into a structured network showing who drives whom.**

Formally, a factor that drives many others is called a **Driver (Key Cause)** and a factor that is driven by many others is called an **Outcome (Key Effect)**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Driver / Key Cause:** Factor with maximum **out-degree** (arrows pointing OUT).
> - **Outcome / Key Effect:** Factor with maximum **in-degree** (arrows pointing IN).
> - **Central Factor:** Factor with the **highest total degree** (in + out).
> - **Bidirectional Arrow ($\leftrightarrow$):** Indicates mutual influence.
> - **Out-degree ($d^+(v)$):** Number of arrows leaving vertex $v$.
> - **In-degree ($d^-(v)$):** Number of arrows entering vertex $v$.
> - **Total degree:** $d(v) = d^+(v) + d^-(v)$.

---

## 1.4 When to Use the Inter Relationship Digraph

The IRD is deployed when:

1. A problem is **complex with multiple interrelated causes**.
2. Traditional **fishbone / Ishikawa diagrams** become cluttered.
3. The team needs to **prioritize** which causes to investigate first.
4. During the **Analyze phase of DMAIC** to identify vital few X's (input variables).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The IRD is based on **graph theory** — a branch of discrete mathematics. Each factor becomes a node, and every "A causes B" statement becomes a directed edge. The digraph (directed graph) thus produced is analyzed for **degree centrality** to identify the most influential factors.

### 2.1.1 Mathematical Representation

Let the set of factors be $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}$.

Let the set of directed relationships be $E \subseteq V \times V$, where each ordered pair $(v_i, v_j)$ represents the relationship *"$v_i$ causes/influences $v_j$"*.

The graph $G = (V, E)$ is a **directed graph (digraph)**.

For each vertex $v \in V$:

$$
d^+(v) \;=\; \bigl\lvert \{\, u \in V \mid (v, u) \in E \,\} \bigr\rvert
$$

$$
d^-(v) \;=\; \bigl\lvert \{\, u \in V \mid (u, v) \in E \,\} \bigr\vert
$$

$$
d(v) \;=\; d^+(v) + d^-(v)
$$

> **Where:**
> - $d^+(v)$ = **Out-degree** (number of factors that $v$ influences).
> - $d^-(v)$ = **In-degree** (number of factors that influence $v$).
> - $d(v)$ = **Total degree** of vertex $v$.
> - $\bigl\vert \cdot \bigr\vert$ denotes cardinality (count).

---

## 2.2 Standard Arrow Notation (KTU Board Expectation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three types of arrows are used in a standard IRD:**
>
> | Arrow Symbol | Meaning | Influence |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | $\rightarrow$ | Direct cause-effect (A causes B) | A drives B |
> | $\leftarrow$ | Reverse indication | B drives A |
> | $\leftrightarrow$ | Mutual / Bidirectional | A and B cause each other |
>
> **Bidirectional arrows count as +1 to BOTH $d^+$ and $d^-$ of the two nodes involved.**

---

## 2.3 Construction Logic — Structured Steps

The Six Sigma team follows a **five-step structured procedure** to construct an IRD:

1. **Step 1 — Factor Listing:** Place the problem statement at the **center** of the page. Arrange all identified factors (usually 8–15) as **circles or boxes** around it.
2. **Step 2 — Relationship Identification:** Discuss each pair of factors: *"Does A cause B?"* Draw the appropriate arrow if yes.
3. **Step 3 — Count Degrees:** Count out-degree $d^+$ and in-degree $d^-$ for every factor.
4. **Step 4 — Identify Drivers and Outcomes:** Mark factors with the **highest out-degree** as **drivers** (enclose in a square). Mark factors with the **highest in-degree** as **outcomes** (enclose in a circle or double border).
5. **Step 5 — Interpret and Prioritize:** Focus improvement efforts on the **drivers** because influencing them propagates to many downstream factors.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantitative elements tested in KTU exams:**

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Out-degree of vertex $v$ | $d^+(v) = \bigl\vert N^+(v) \bigr\vert$ | $0 \le d^+(v) \le n-1$ |
| In-degree of vertex $v$ | $d^-(v) = \bigl\vert N^-(v) \bigr\vert$ | $0 \le d^-(v) \le n-1$ |
| Total degree | $d(v) = d^+(v) + d^-(v)$ | $0 \le d(v) \le 2(n-1)$ |
| Sum of all out-degrees | $\sum_{v \in V} d^+(v) = \bigl\vert E \bigr\vert$ | Equals total arrows |
| Sum of all in-degrees | $\sum_{v \in V} d^-(v) = \bigl\vert E \bigr\vert$ | Equals total arrows |
| Number of factors | $n = \bigl\vert V \bigr\vert$ | Usually $8 \le n \le 15$ |
| Bidirectional weight | Each $\leftrightarrow$ counted twice | Once in $d^+$, once in $d^-$ |

Where $N^+(v)$ is the set of out-neighbours of $v$, and $N^-(v)$ is the set of in-neighbours.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The IRD is extensively used in:

- **Manufacturing:** Identifying root causes of defects (e.g., surface roughness, dimensional variance).
- **Healthcare:** Analyzing causes of patient wait time in hospitals.
- **Software Engineering:** Mapping causes of bug generation in the SDLC.
- **Service Industry:** Reducing customer churn by mapping service-failure factors.
- **Education:** Improving student performance by mapping academic and non-academic factors.

The IRD is a **cognitive simplification tool** — it allows decision-makers to focus on **2–3 vital drivers** instead of chasing 10+ factors simultaneously, saving time, money, and effort.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: A Six Sigma Case Study

**Scenario (Manufacturing Context):**

> A Six Sigma Black Belt team at an automotive parts manufacturing plant is investigating **"Excessive Surface Defects in Brake Discs"**. After brainstorming, the team identifies **8 key factors** influencing the problem. Construct an Inter Relationship Digraph and identify the **Drivers, Outcomes, and Central factor**.

The 8 factors identified are:

| Code | Factor |
| :--- | :--- |
| $F_1$ | Poor Raw Material Quality |
| $F_2$ | Outdated CNC Machines |
| $F_3$ | Inadequate Operator Training |
| $F_4$ | Improper Tool Calibration |
| $F_5$ | High Workshop Temperature |
| $F_6$ | Lack of Preventive Maintenance |
| $F_7$ | Poor Process Documentation |
| $F_8$ | Surface Defects (the problem itself) |

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction

#### Step 1 — Pairwise Logical Analysis

The team evaluates every ordered pair $(F_i, F_j)$ and decides whether $F_i$ causes $F_j$. The logical inferences used are:

$$
F_1 \rightarrow F_8 \quad \text{(Raw material directly affects defect)}
$$

$$
F_2 \rightarrow F_8 \quad \text{(Outdated machines produce defects)}
$$

$$
F_3 \rightarrow F_8 \quad \text{(Untrained operators cause defects)}
$$

$$
F_6 \rightarrow F_4 \quad \text{(No maintenance leads to miscalibration)}
$$

$$
F_6 \rightarrow F_2 \quad \text{(No maintenance causes machine wear/dating)}
$$

$$
F_7 \rightarrow F_3 \quad \text{(Poor docs cause improper training)}
$$

$$
F_5 \rightarrow F_2 \quad \text{(Heat accelerates machine aging)}
$$

$$
F_4 \rightarrow F_8 \quad \text{(Miscalibration causes defects)}
$$

$$
F_3 \rightarrow F_6 \quad \text{(Untrained staff skip maintenance)}
$$

$$
F_7 \rightarrow F_4 \quad \text{(Bad docs lead to wrong calibration)}
$$

$$
F_6 \leftrightarrow F_7 \quad \text{(No maintenance + bad docs are mutually reinforcing)}
$$

#### Step 2 — Tabulating the Relationship Matrix

We construct an $8 \times 8$ matrix $M$ where:

$$
M_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } F_i \rightarrow F_j \text{ exists} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

For the bidirectional pair $(F_6, F_7)$: $M_{67} = M_{76} = 1$.

| From $\backslash$ To | $F_1$ | $F_2$ | $F_3$ | $F_4$ | $F_5$ | $F_6$ | $F_7$ | $F_8$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $F_1$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| $F_2$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| $F_3$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| $F_4$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| $F_5$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $F_6$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $F_7$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| $F_8$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

#### Step 3 — Degree Computation

The **out-degree** $d^+(F_i)$ is the sum of the $i$-th row, and the **in-degree** $d^-(F_i)$ is the sum of the $i$-th column.

$$
d^+(F_1) = M_{1,1} + M_{1,2} + \dots + M_{1,8} = 0+0+0+0+0+0+0+1 = 1
$$

$$
d^+(F_2) = 0+0+0+0+0+0+0+1 = 1
$$

$$
d^+(F_3) = 0+0+0+0+0+1+0+1 = 2
$$

$$
d^+(F_4) = 0+0+0+0+0+0+0+1 = 1
$$

$$
d^+(F_5) = 0+1+0+0+0+0+0+0 = 1
$$

$$
d^+(F_6) = 0+1+0+1+0+0+1+0 = 3
$$

$$
d^+(F_7) = 0+0+1+1+0+1+0+0 = 3
$$

$$
d^+(F_8) = 0+0+0+0+0+0+0+0 = 0
$$

**Verification of sum of out-degrees:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{8} d^+(F_i) = 1+1+2+1+1+3+3+0 = 12
$$

This equals the total number of directed edges $\bigl\vert E \bigr\vert = 12$. ✓

Now the **in-degree** $d^-(F_i)$ is the sum of the $i$-th column:

$$
d^-(F_1) = 0, \quad d^-(F_2) = 0+0+0+0+1+1+0+0 = 2
$$

$$
d^-(F_3) = 0+0+0+0+0+0+1+0 = 1
$$

$$
d^-(F_4) = 0+0+0+0+0+1+1+0 = 2
$$

$$
d^-(F_5) = 0, \quad d^-(F_6) = 0+0+1+0+0+0+1+0 = 2
$$

$$
d^-(F_7) = 0+0+0+0+0+1+0+0 = 1
$$

$$
d^-(F_8) = 1+1+1+1+0+0+0+0 = 4
$$

**Verification of sum of in-degrees:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{8} d^-(F_i) = 0+2+1+2+0+2+1+4 = 12 \quad ✓
$$

This matches $|E| = 12$ as expected by the **Handshaking Lemma** of directed graphs.

#### Step 4 — Total Degree Computation

$$
d(F_i) = d^+(F_i) + d^-(F_i)
$$

| Factor | $d^+$ | $d^-$ | $d$ (Total) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $F_1$ Poor Raw Material | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| $F_2$ Outdated Machines | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| $F_3$ Inadequate Training | 2 | 1 | 3 |
| $F_4$ Improper Calibration | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| $F_5$ High Temperature | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| $F_6$ No Maintenance | 3 | 2 | **5** |
| $F_7$ Poor Documentation | 3 | 1 | 4 |
| $F_8$ Surface Defects | 0 | 4 | 4 |

#### Step 5 — Interpretation Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drivers (Highest Out-Degree):** $F_6$ and $F_7$ each with $d^+ = 3$.
> **Outcome (Highest In-Degree):** $F_8$ with $d^- = 4$.
> **Central Factor (Highest Total Degree):** $F_6$ with $d = 5$.

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- $F_6$ (Lack of Preventive Maintenance) is the **key root cause** — fixing it influences $F_2$, $F_4$, and $F_7$.
- $F_8$ (Surface Defects) is the **symptom / outcome** — it is driven by four upstream factors.
- The Six Sigma team must prioritize implementing a **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** program and **revising process documentation** to attack $F_6$ and $F_7$ first.

This analytical structure follows the Six Sigma principle of attacking the **vital few X's** rather than the **trivial many**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inter Relationship Digraph — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagrams (a) the **functional architecture** of the IRD construction process, and (b) the **directed graph** of the worked example.

### 4.1.1 Process Flow — Building an IRD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Define Problem Statement] --> B[Brainstorm Factors 8 to 15 Nodes]
    B --> C[Place Factors as Nodes around Center]
    C --> D[Conduct Pairwise Analysis Does A cause B]
    D --> E{Influence Exists}
    E -->|Yes| F[Draw Directed Arrow]
    E -->|No| G[No Connection]
    F --> H[Count Outdegree and Indegree]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Identify Drivers Highest Outdegree]
    H --> J[Identify Outcomes Highest Indegree]
    H --> K[Identify Central Factor Highest Total Degree]
    I --> L[Prioritize Root Cause Investigation]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[END Action Plan for Vital Few Drivers]
```

### 4.1.2 Directed Graph of the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[F1 Raw Material] --> F8[F8 Surface Defects]
    F2[F2 Outdated Machines] --> F8
    F3[F3 Inadequate Training] --> F8
    F4[F4 Improper Calibration] --> F8
    F5[F5 High Temperature] --> F2
    F6[F6 No Maintenance DRIVER] --> F2
    F6 --> F4
    F7[F7 Poor Docs DRIVER] --> F3
    F7 --> F4
    F3 --> F6
    F6 <--> F7
    F8[OUTCOME F8 Surface Defects]
```

### 4.1.3 Degree Centrality Heatmap Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ANALYSIS["DEGREE ANALYSIS MATRIX"]
        direction TB
        R1[F1 out 1 in 0 total 1] 
        R2[F2 out 1 in 2 total 3]
        R3[F3 out 2 in 1 total 3]
        R4[F4 out 1 in 2 total 3]
        R5[F5 out 1 in 0 total 1]
        R6[F6 out 3 in 2 total 5 CENTRAL]
        R7[F7 out 3 in 1 total 4]
        R8[F8 out 0 in 4 total 4 OUTCOME]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - The first diagram shows the **procedural flow** for constructing an IRD.
> - The second shows the **digraph topology** with $F_6$ and $F_7$ as drivers (enclosed in square-like boxes by convention) and $F_8$ as the outcome.
> - The third summarizes the **degree metrics** computed using graph theory.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

### Q1. Define Inter Relationship Digraph. Mention any two situations where it is preferred over a fishbone diagram.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Inter Relationship Digraph (IRD)** is a pictorial tool used in the **Analyze phase of DMAIC** that displays all the cause-and-effect relationships among a group of interrelated factors using nodes and directed arrows, allowing identification of **key drivers** (factors with maximum out-degree) and **key outcomes** (factors with maximum in-degree).

It is preferred over a fishbone diagram when:

1. The problem involves **multiple factors that influence each other reciprocally**, not just a one-way flow from categories to effect.
2. The fishbone diagram becomes **too cluttered** due to many interrelated causes (typically more than 8–10).
3. The team needs to mathematically **prioritize** root causes using out-degree and in-degree centrality.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

### Q2. Differentiate between a Driver and an Outcome factor in an Inter Relationship Digraph.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Driver (Key Cause) | Outcome (Key Effect) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Factor that causes / influences many others | Factor that is caused / influenced by many others |
| Degree Metric | **Maximum out-degree** $d^+(v)$ | **Maximum in-degree** $d^-(v)$ |
| Strategic Action | Investigate and **fix first** | Monitor as it will **automatically improve** |
| Graphical Convention | Enclosed in a **square** | Enclosed in a **circle / double border** |
| Typical Role | Root cause upstream in the chain | Symptom downstream of the chain |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 Syllabus]**

### Question A — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the concept and purpose of an **Inter Relationship Digraph** with a suitable block diagram. List the **steps involved** in constructing one. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Six Sigma project team identified **6 factors** ($A, B, C, D, E, F$) influencing customer complaints. The pairwise causal relationships established are:
$A \rightarrow B$, $A \rightarrow D$, $B \rightarrow C$, $B \rightarrow F$, $C \rightarrow F$, $D \rightarrow E$, $D \rightarrow F$, $E \rightarrow F$.

Construct the **relationship matrix**, compute the **out-degree, in-degree, and total degree** of every factor, and identify the **driver, outcome, and central factor**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):** Inter Relationship Digraph is a graphical analytical tool of the Seven Management and Planning Tools that depicts cause-and-effect relationships among multiple interrelated factors as a directed graph, used in the Analyze phase of DMAIC.

**Purpose (2 marks):**

- Identifies **root causes** (drivers) versus **symptoms** (outcomes).
- Helps prioritize improvement projects by attacking **vital few** causes.
- Reveals **hidden feedback loops** that fishbone diagrams cannot capture.
- Facilitates **team consensus** on cause-effect logic.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1.1 Mermaid diagram in these notes.

**Steps (2 marks):**

1. Define problem statement clearly.
2. List 8–15 influencing factors.
3. Place factors as nodes around a central problem.
4. Conduct pairwise cause-effect analysis.
5. Draw directed arrows for each influence.
6. Count out-degree and in-degree for each factor.
7. Identify drivers (max out-degree), outcomes (max in-degree), central factor (max total degree).
8. Prioritize action plan on drivers.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Construct the $6 \times 6$ Relationship Matrix:**

Rows are "From", columns are "To". Enter 1 wherever the directed influence exists.

| From $\backslash$ To | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ | $F$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $A$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| $B$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| $C$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| $D$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| $E$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| $F$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 2 — Compute Out-Degrees (Row Sums):**

$$
d^+(A) = 2, \quad d^+(B) = 2, \quad d^+(C) = 1, \quad d^+(D) = 2, \quad d^+(E) = 1, \quad d^+(F) = 0
$$

**Sum check:** $2+2+1+2+1+0 = 8$ directed edges ✓ (matches the 8 arrows listed).

**Step 3 — Compute In-Degrees (Column Sums):**

$$
d^-(A) = 0, \quad d^-(B) = 1, \quad d^-(C) = 1, \quad d^-(D) = 1, \quad d^-(E) = 1, \quad d^-(F) = 4
$$

**Sum check:** $0+1+1+1+1+4 = 8$ ✓

**Step 4 — Compute Total Degrees and Tabulate:**

| Factor | $d^+$ | $d^-$ | $d$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $A$ | 2 | 0 | 2 |
| $B$ | 2 | 1 | 3 |
| $C$ | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| $D$ | 2 | 1 | 3 |
| $E$ | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| $F$ | 0 | 4 | **4** |

**Step 5 — Identification:**

- **Drivers (Tie at $d^+ = 2$):** $A$, $B$, $D$ are joint drivers.
- **Outcome (Highest In-Degree):** $F$ with $d^- = 4$ — this is the customer complaint itself.
- **Central Factor (Highest Total Degree):** $F$ with $d = 4$.

**Conclusion (KTU Board Style):** Factor $F$ acts as both the central and outcome variable. The team must focus on jointly attacking $A$, $B$, and $D$ because influencing these three drivers will cascade benefits down to $C$, $E$, and ultimately eliminate $F$ (customer complaints).

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the **difference between Affinity Diagram and Inter Relationship Digraph**. Why is IRD considered a more analytical tool? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit identifies 5 causes ($P, Q, R, S, T$) for high rejection rate with the following relationships:
$P \rightarrow Q$, $P \rightarrow R$, $Q \leftrightarrow R$, $R \rightarrow S$, $S \rightarrow T$, $T \rightarrow Q$.

Draw the digraph, tabulate the degree matrix, and identify the **driver, outcome, and central factor**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) [7 marks]

| Aspect | Affinity Diagram | Inter Relationship Digraph |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Origin | Dr. Jiro Kawikita (KJ Method) | Derived from Affinity + Cause-Effect logic |
| Purpose | **Grouping** ideas into natural clusters | **Mapping** causal relationships between groups |
| Structure | Hierarchical card sorting | Directed network (graph) |
| Direction | No cause-effect direction | Clear arrows showing direction of influence |
| Quantitative Output | None (qualitative only) | **Out-degree, in-degree, centrality metrics** |
| Phase in DMAIC | Define / Measure | Analyze |
| Analysis Type | Subjective clustering | Objective graph-theoretic analysis |

**Why IRD is more analytical (3 marks):**

1. It produces **numerical degree metrics** that allow objective prioritization.
2. It reveals **feedback loops** (e.g., $Q \leftrightarrow R$) that affinity cannot.
3. It transforms qualitative brainstorming into a **mathematical graph**, enabling validation by data.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Edgelist verification:**

From the problem:

$$
E = \{ (P,Q), (P,R), (Q,R), (R,Q), (R,S), (S,T), (T,Q) \}
$$

Total $\bigl\vert E \bigr\vert = 7$ directed edges.

**Step 2 — Relationship Matrix:**

| From $\backslash$ To | $P$ | $Q$ | $R$ | $S$ | $T$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $P$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| $Q$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| $R$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $S$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| $T$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

**Step 3 — Degree Computation:**

$$
d^+(P) = 2, \quad d^+(Q) = 1, \quad d^+(R) = 2, \quad d^+(S) = 1, \quad d^+(T) = 1
$$

$$
d^-(P) = 0, \quad d^-(Q) = 3, \quad d^-(R) = 2, \quad d^-(S) = 1, \quad d^-(T) = 1
$$

**Sum check:** $\sum d^+ = 2+1+2+1+1 = 7$ ✓ and $\sum d^- = 0+3+2+1+1 = 7$ ✓

**Step 4 — Total Degree Table:**

| Factor | $d^+$ | $d^-$ | $d$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $P$ | 2 | 0 | 2 |
| $Q$ | 1 | 3 | 4 |
| $R$ | 2 | 2 | **4** |
| $S$ | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| $T$ | 1 | 1 | 2 |

**Step 5 — Identification:**

- **Driver (Highest Out-Degree):** Tie between $P$ and $R$ with $d^+ = 2$.
- **Outcome (Highest In-Degree):** $Q$ with $d^- = 3$.
- **Central Factors:** $Q$ and $R$ tied at $d = 4$.

**Conclusion:** $R$ is the most strategically important factor — it is both a **strong driver** (pushes to $Q$ and $S$) and is **central** in the network. Eliminating $R$ will break the feedback loop with $Q$ and reduce downstream rejections at $T$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing in-degree with out-degree:** Students often swap the definitions of driver and outcome. **Mnemonic:** Out = leaves the node (drives others); In = enters the node (is driven).
> 2. **Forgetting bidirectional arrows count twice:** A $\leftrightarrow B$ contributes $+1$ to BOTH nodes' out-degrees AND in-degrees.
> 3. **Not verifying the Handshaking Lemma:** Always confirm $\sum d^+ = \sum d^- = \bigl\vert E \bigr\vert$. Examiners give **partial credit** for this verification.
> 4. **Missing the feedback loop notation:** Bidirectional pairs must be marked clearly; otherwise the relationship matrix will be incomplete.
> 5. **Skipping the matrix construction:** KTU examiners expect a tabular matrix in 7-mark problems. Drawing only the network graph without tabulation loses up to 2 marks.
> 6. **Choosing the wrong "central" factor:** Central factor is based on **total degree**, not out-degree. Do not confuse it with the driver.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Inter Relationship Digraphs:**

- ⭐ The IRD is a **graph-theoretic tool** belonging to the **Seven Management and Planning Tools** used in the **Analyze phase of DMAIC**.
- ⭐ Nodes = Factors; Directed Arrows = Causal Influences.
- ⭐ **Driver = Maximum Out-degree** $d^+(v)$.
- ⭐ **Outcome = Maximum In-degree** $d^-(v)$.
- ⭐ **Central Factor = Maximum Total Degree** $d(v) = d^+(v) + d^-(v)$.
- ⭐ Bidirectional arrows contribute to **both** $d^+$ and $d^-$.
- ⭐ The **Handshaking Lemma** must always hold: $\sum d^+(v) = \sum d^-(v) = \bigl\vert E \bigr\vert$.
- ⭐ Construction steps: **Define → List → Place → Pairwise → Draw → Count → Identify → Prioritize**.
- ⭐ Preferred over Fishbone when causes are **mutually reinforcing**, **feedback loops exist**, or **prioritization** is required.
- ⭐ The 5 marks problem typically expects: digraph + matrix + degrees + identification of driver/outcome/central factor.
- ⭐ The 14 marks problem typically expects: theory explanation + worked numerical example with full matrix construction.
- ⭐ Always **verify** your degree sum equals the number of arrows before concluding.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tree Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tree Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Tree Diagram** in the context of Six Sigma is a graphical analytical tool used to map out a sequence of events, decisions, probabilities, or causal factors in a hierarchical, branching structure. It systematically decomposes a complex problem into progressively smaller sub-components, enabling the analyst to visualize cause-and-effect relationships, calculate probabilities of outcomes, or trace the logical paths leading to a process failure or success.

In Six Sigma's **DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control)** methodology, tree diagrams serve as critical analytical instruments in the **Analyze** phase for root cause identification and probabilistic reasoning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Tree Diagram is a structured, hierarchical branching representation that displays a sequence of interconnected events, conditions, or decisions, used in Six Sigma for probability computation, fault analysis (FTA), event tree analysis (ETA), and root cause decomposition.

There are four principal types of tree diagrams applied across the DMAIC lifecycle:

| Type | Primary Phase | Core Purpose |
|------|---------------|--------------|
| **Probability Tree** | Measure / Analyze | Calculate joint and marginal probabilities |
| **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** | Analyze | Trace top-level failures to root causes (top-down) |
| **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** | Analyze | Map outcomes from initiating events (bottom-up) |
| **Decision Tree** | Improve | Compare alternative solutions with payoffs |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the top of a real **tree**. The trunk represents your main problem (say, "Defective Product"). As you look upward, the trunk splits into two major branches (e.g., "Material Defect" and "Process Defect"). Each of those branches then splits again into smaller branches ("Wrong Alloy Grade", "Contamination", "Incorrect Temperature", "Human Error"), and so on, until you reach the **leaves** — the fundamental root causes.

Each junction (node) is a **decision or probabilistic point**, and each branch is an **outcome or sub-event**. By tracing down the tree, you can:

- **Quantify** the probability of reaching any specific leaf (root cause).
- **Decompose** a complex failure into manageable, addressable factors.
- **Visualize** the entire logical landscape of a problem on a single page.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Medical Diagnosis:** When a doctor diagnoses an illness, they use a mental tree diagram. "Fever?" → "Yes" → "Above 102°F?" → "Yes" → "Accompanied by cough?" ... each branch leading closer to the actual disease. A Tree Diagram formalizes this intuitive human reasoning into a rigorous engineering tool.

## 1.3 Standard Probabilistic Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Block:**
> - **Root Node:** The starting point of the tree (the initial event or condition).
> - **Branch:** A line representing an outcome or sub-event.
> - **Child Node / Intermediate Node:** A point where further branching occurs.
> - **Leaf / End Node:** Terminal outcomes where no further branching is done.
> - **Path:** A unique sequence of branches from root to a leaf.
> - **Joint Probability:** The probability of all events along a specific path occurring together.
> - **Marginal Probability:** The probability of a single event, summed over all paths containing it.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

While tree diagrams are not bound by physical constants, Six Sigma applications often use:

- **Defect Rate (DPMO)** = **1.5** defects per million opportunities (industry standard benchmark).
- **Sigma Level (Z)** — measured in standard deviations.
- **Probability values** always constrained to $0 \leq P \leq 1$.
- The sum of probabilities of all branches from any single node = **1.0** (certainty).

---

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics of Tree Diagrams

A tree diagram functions on three foundational logical principles:

### Principle 1: **Multiplication Rule (Sequential / Joint Events)**

When two or more events occur **in sequence along a single path**, the probability of all of them occurring together is the **product** of their individual conditional probabilities.

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$$

This is applied **along each path** from root to leaf.

### Principle 2: **Addition Rule (Mutually Exclusive Outcomes)**

When two or more **distinct paths** lead to the same outcome or to mutually exclusive outcomes, the total probability is the **sum** of the individual path probabilities.

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) \quad \text{when } A \cap B = \emptyset$$

### Principle 3: **Completeness Rule (Exhaustive Branching)**

At every internal node, the sum of probabilities of all branches emanating from that node must equal **1**, because one of the outcomes *must* occur.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) = 1$$

where $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n$ are exhaustive branches from a given node.

## 2.2 Construction Steps (Standard KTU Methodology)

1. **Define the root event** — the problem, decision, or initial condition.
2. **Identify primary branches** — the major categories of outcomes, causes, or decisions.
3. **Assign probabilities** to each branch (must sum to 1 at each node).
4. **Extend to sub-branches** — break each primary branch into further sub-events.
5. **Calculate path probabilities** — multiply along each path.
6. **Aggregate outcomes** — sum path probabilities for any event of interest.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Mathematical Form | Application Context |
|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Multiplication Rule | $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$ | Sequential independent/dependent events along a path |
| General Multiplication | $P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i \mid \text{prior events})$ | Probability of a complete path |
| Addition Rule (Disjoint) | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$ | Mutually exclusive outcomes across paths |
| Addition Rule (General) | $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$ | Non-exclusive events |
| Conditional Probability | $P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$ | Reverse-engineering tree probabilities |
| Total Probability | $P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)$ | Marginal probability at root |
| Bayes' Theorem | $P(B_i \mid A) = \dfrac{P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B_j) \cdot P(A \mid B_j)}$ | Posterior probabilities from trees |
| Complement Rule | $P(A^c) = 1 - P(A)$ | "At least one" type problems |
| Exhaustive Sum | $\sum_{i} P(B_i \mid \text{node}) = 1$ | Validation constraint at each node |
| FTA AND-Gate | $P(\text{Top Event}) = \prod_{i} P(\text{Input Event}_i)$ | All inputs must occur |
| FTA OR-Gate | $P(\text{Top Event}) = 1 - \prod_{i} (1 - P(\text{Input Event}_i))$ | Any input triggers failure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Notation Note (Anti-Break Rule):** Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, never use vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ inside markdown table cells. Conditional notation $P(A \mid B)$ uses `\mid` instead of $\vert$ to preserve table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tree diagrams are foundational in:

- **Aerospace & Nuclear Safety:** Fault Tree Analysis for catastrophic failure prevention (e.g., NASA, ISRO mission failure analysis).
- **Manufacturing:** Root cause identification of yield losses in semiconductor fabs.
- **Medical Devices:** Risk analysis per ISO 14971.
- **Software Engineering:** Decision trees in test case prioritization.
- **Supply Chain:** Probability of delivery failure cascading through multi-tier suppliers.
- **Six Sigma Black Belt Projects:** Quantifying process capability risk under varying input conditions.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Probability Tree (KTU Style 14-Mark Format)

### Problem Statement:

A Six Sigma team is investigating a manufacturing process producing microchips. Historical data shows:

- **Probability of Machine A being used:** $P(A) = 0.6$
- **Probability of Machine B being used:** $P(B) = 0.4$
- **Defect rate given Machine A:** $P(D \mid A) = 0.02$
- **Defect rate given Machine B:** $P(D \mid B) = 0.05$

**Required:**
1. Draw the tree diagram.
2. Find the probability that a randomly selected chip is defective.
3. Given that a chip is defective, find the probability that it came from Machine B.

### Step 1 — Identify the Root Event and Primary Branches

The root event is **"Select a chip"**, which branches into:
- $A$: Machine A used
- $B$: Machine B used

Each then branches into:
- $D$: Defective
- $D^c$: Not defective

### Step 2 — Construct the Probability Tree (Logical Layout)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Root} &\to A \; (0.6) \to D \; (0.02) \quad \Rightarrow \text{Path: } A \cap D \\
\text{Root} &\to A \; (0.6) \to D^c \; (0.98) \quad \Rightarrow \text{Path: } A \cap D^c \\
\text{Root} &\to B \; (0.4) \to D \; (0.05) \quad \Rightarrow \text{Path: } B \cap D \\
\text{Root} &\to B \; (0.4) \to D^c \; (0.95) \quad \Rightarrow \text{Path: } B \cap D^c
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Each Path Probability (Multiplication Rule)

**Path 1: $A \cap D$ (Defective from Machine A)**

$$P(A \cap D) = P(A) \cdot P(D \mid A) = 0.6 \times 0.02 = 0.012$$

**Path 2: $A \cap D^c$ (Non-defective from Machine A)**

$$P(A \cap D^c) = P(A) \cdot P(D^c \mid A) = 0.6 \times 0.98 = 0.588$$

**Path 3: $B \cap D$ (Defective from Machine B)**

$$P(B \cap D) = P(B) \cdot P(D \mid B) = 0.4 \times 0.05 = 0.020$$

**Path 4: $B \cap D^c$ (Non-defective from Machine B)**

$$P(B \cap D^c) = P(B) \cdot P(D^c \mid B) = 0.4 \times 0.95 = 0.380$$

### Step 4 — Validation (Exhaustive Sum Check)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= 0.012 + 0.588 + 0.020 + 0.380 \\
&= 0.600 + 0.400 \\
&= 1.000 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms all paths are accounted for.

### Step 5 — Total Probability of a Defective Chip (Law of Total Probability)

The defective outcome $D$ can occur via **two distinct paths**: $A \cap D$ or $B \cap D$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(D) &= P(A \cap D) + P(B \cap D) \\
&= 0.012 + 0.020 \\
&= 0.032
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Approximately **3.2%** of all chips produced are defective.

### Step 6 — Posterior Probability via Bayes' Theorem

We seek $P(B \mid D)$, i.e., the probability that a defective chip came from Machine B.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(B \mid D) &= \frac{P(B \cap D)}{P(D)} \\
&= \frac{P(B) \cdot P(D \mid B)}{P(A) \cdot P(D \mid A) + P(B) \cdot P(D \mid B)} \\
&= \frac{0.4 \times 0.05}{0.032} \\
&= \frac{0.020}{0.032} \\
&= 0.625
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Given that a chip is defective, there is a **62.5% probability** it originated from Machine B. This signals Machine B as the higher-risk contributor and the Six Sigma team's primary target for improvement.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) Worked Example

### Problem Statement:

In a chemical reactor system, a **Pressure Overload (TOP EVENT)** occurs if **both** of the following occur simultaneously:
- **A:** Pressure Relief Valve Fails (with $P(A) = 0.01$)
- **B:** Temperature Sensor Fails (with $P(B) = 0.02$)

The system uses an **AND-gate** logic in the fault tree (both failures must coincide).

**Required:** Calculate the probability of the top event (Pressure Overload).

### Step 1 — Identify the Logic Gate

The fault tree uses an **AND-gate**, meaning:

$$\text{Top Event} = A \cap B$$

### Step 2 — Apply the AND-Gate FTA Formula

For an AND-gate with independent inputs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Top Event}) &= P(A \cap B) \\
&= P(A) \cdot P(B) \\
&= 0.01 \times 0.02 \\
&= 0.0002
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Express in Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= P(\text{Top Event}) \times 10^6 \\
&= 0.0002 \times 10^6 \\
&= 200 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This corresponds to roughly a **5.5 σ** process capability — excellent, but the team may still target improvement to **6 σ** (3.4 DPMO).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — OR-Gate FTA Worked Example

### Problem Statement:

A circuit board fails if **any one** of three components fails:
- $C_1$: Resistor fails, $P(C_1) = 0.005$
- $C_2$: Capacitor fails, $P(C_2) = 0.003$
- $C_3$: IC chip fails, $P(C_3) = 0.002$

The fault tree uses an **OR-gate** (any single failure causes top event).

**Required:** Probability of circuit board failure.

### Step 1 — Identify Logic and Apply OR-Gate Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Failure}) &= 1 - P(\text{All survive}) \\
&= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{3} (1 - P(C_i)) \\
&= 1 - (1 - 0.005)(1 - 0.003)(1 - 0.002) \\
&= 1 - (0.995)(0.997)(0.998) \\
&= 1 - 0.99002 \\
&= 0.00998
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Approximately **0.998%** failure rate, equivalent to **9,980 DPMO**.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a Tree Diagram. Mention any two types of tree diagrams used in Six Sigma analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

A Tree Diagram is a structured, hierarchical graphical tool used in Six Sigma to display a sequence of events, decisions, probabilities, or causal factors in a branching structure. It decomposes complex problems into simpler sub-components for analytical clarity.

**Two types:**
1. **Probability Tree** — used to calculate joint and conditional probabilities along sequential paths.
2. **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** — used to trace a top-level failure event back to its root causes using AND/OR logical gates.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for two types = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: State the Multiplication Rule and the Addition Rule as applied to probability tree diagrams.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Multiplication Rule:** The probability of two or more events occurring together along a single path is the product of their conditional probabilities:
$$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$$

- **Addition Rule:** The probability of mutually exclusive outcomes occurring across different paths equals the sum of their individual path probabilities:
$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A Six Sigma project analyzes a call center process. Historical data reveals:**
- **60%** of calls are received during **Peak Hours**; **40%** during **Off-Peak Hours**.
- The probability that a call is **resolved in the first attempt** given Peak Hours is **0.70**.
- The probability that a call is resolved in the first attempt given Off-Peak Hours is **0.85**.

**(a) Draw the probability tree diagram and compute the probability that a randomly selected call is resolved in the first attempt. (7 marks)**

**(b) Given that a call was NOT resolved in the first attempt, find the probability that it occurred during Peak Hours. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A:

**Part (a) — Tree Construction and Total Probability of First-Call Resolution**

**Step 1: Define events.**
- $P$ = Peak Hours, $P(P) = 0.60$
- $O$ = Off-Peak Hours, $P(O) = 0.40$
- $R$ = Resolved in first attempt
- $R^c$ = Not resolved

**Step 2: Branch probabilities.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(R \mid P) &= 0.70 &\Rightarrow P(R^c \mid P) &= 0.30 \\
P(R \mid O) &= 0.85 &\Rightarrow P(R^c \mid O) &= 0.15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate path probabilities (Multiplication Rule).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(P \cap R) &= 0.60 \times 0.70 = 0.420 \\
P(P \cap R^c) &= 0.60 \times 0.30 = 0.180 \\
P(O \cap R) &= 0.40 \times 0.85 = 0.340 \\
P(O \cap R^c) &= 0.40 \times 0.15 = 0.060
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Validate exhaustiveness.**

$$0.420 + 0.180 + 0.340 + 0.060 = 1.000 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Total probability of first-call resolution (Addition Rule across paths).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(R) &= P(P \cap R) + P(O \cap R) \\
&= 0.420 + 0.340 \\
&= \mathbf{0.760}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (a):** Probability that a randomly selected call is resolved on first attempt = **0.760 (76.0%)**.

*(Marks: 2 for tree, 2 for path probabilities, 2 for total, 1 for validation = 7 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — Conditional Probability via Bayes' Theorem**

We want $P(P \mid R^c)$ — the probability the call was during Peak Hours given it was NOT resolved.

**Step 1: Identify relevant paths.**
The event $R^c$ occurs via two paths: $P \cap R^c$ (prob 0.180) and $O \cap R^c$ (prob 0.060).

**Step 2: Total probability of non-resolution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(R^c) &= P(P \cap R^c) + P(O \cap R^c) \\
&= 0.180 + 0.060 \\
&= 0.240
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Bayes' Theorem (conditional probability).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(P \mid R^c) &= \frac{P(P \cap R^c)}{P(R^c)} \\
&= \frac{P(P) \cdot P(R^c \mid P)}{P(P) \cdot P(R^c \mid P) + P(O) \cdot P(R^c \mid O)} \\
&= \frac{0.60 \times 0.30}{0.240} \\
&= \frac{0.180}{0.240} \\
&= \mathbf{0.750}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (b):** Given a call was not resolved, there is a **75.0% probability** it occurred during Peak Hours. This indicates Peak Hours is the primary target for process improvement.

*(Marks: 2 for identifying paths, 2 for total of $R^c$, 2 for Bayes application, 1 for final answer = 7 marks)*

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: A Six Sigma Black Belt project conducts a Fault Tree Analysis on a packaging line. The system fails (TOP EVENT: Packaging Line Failure) if any ONE of three sub-systems fails:**
- **S1:** Conveyor Motor Failure, with $P(S1) = 0.02$
- **S2:** Sensor Malfunction, with $P(S2) = 0.03$
- **S3:** Sealing Unit Failure, with $P(S3) = 0.01$

**The three sub-systems operate independently and are connected via an OR-gate.**

**(a) Draw the fault tree diagram and calculate the probability of the top event (packaging line failure). (7 marks)**

**(b) If the team successfully reduces $P(S2)$ from 0.03 to 0.01 through a Six Sigma improvement initiative, what is the new probability of the top event? Comment on the improvement in DPMO. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B:

**Part (a) — Fault Tree Construction and OR-Gate Probability**

**Step 1: Draw the fault tree.**
- TOP EVENT: Packaging Line Failure
- OR-GATE with three inputs: S1, S2, S3

**Step 2: Apply the OR-Gate FTA formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Top Event}) &= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{3} \big(1 - P(S_i)\big) \\
&= 1 - (1 - 0.02)(1 - 0.03)(1 - 0.01) \\
&= 1 - (0.98)(0.97)(0.99)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute intermediate products.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.98 \times 0.97 &= 0.9506 \\
0.9506 \times 0.99 &= 0.941094
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Final probability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Top Event}) &= 1 - 0.941094 \\
&= \mathbf{0.058906 \approx 0.0589}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (a):** Probability of packaging line failure = **0.0589 (5.89%)**, equivalent to **58,906 DPMO**.

*(Marks: 2 for tree, 2 for formula, 2 for arithmetic, 1 for final answer = 7 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — Post-Improvement Fault Tree Probability**

After improvement, the new probabilities are:
- $P(S1) = 0.02$ (unchanged)
- $P(S2) = 0.01$ (improved from 0.03)
- $P(S3) = 0.01$ (unchanged)

**Step 1: Re-apply the OR-Gate formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Top Event}_{\text{new}}) &= 1 - (1 - 0.02)(1 - 0.01)(1 - 0.01) \\
&= 1 - (0.98)(0.99)(0.99)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute intermediate products.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.99 \times 0.99 &= 0.9801 \\
0.98 \times 0.9801 &= 0.960498
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Final new probability.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Top Event}_{\text{new}}) &= 1 - 0.960498 \\
&= \mathbf{0.039502 \approx 0.0395}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute DPMO comparison.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO}_{\text{old}} &= 0.058906 \times 10^6 = 58{,}906 \\
\text{DPMO}_{\text{new}} &= 0.039502 \times 10^6 = 39{,}502 \\
\Delta \text{DPMO} &= 58{,}906 - 39{,}502 = 19{,}404 \text{ reduction}
\end{aligned}
$$
