**Step 5: Percentage improvement.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction \%} &= \frac{0.0589 - 0.0395}{0.0589} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{0.0194}{0.0589} \times 100\% \\
&\approx \mathbf{32.9\% \text{ reduction}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (b):** New failure probability = **0.0395 (3.95%)**, new DPMO = **39,502**, representing a **32.9% reduction** in failure rate.

*(Marks: 2 for updated values, 2 for formula re-application, 2 for DPMO comparison, 1 for commentary = 7 marks)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Tree Diagram Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Complement Rule for OR-Gates:** Students often *add* probabilities ($0.02 + 0.03 + 0.01 = 0.06$) instead of using $1 - \prod(1-p_i)$. This overestimates the failure probability. **Always** use $1 - P(\text{all survive})$ for OR-gates.
> 2. **Not validating exhaustive sum:** After computing path probabilities, sum them. If ≠ 1, you've missed a branch. Examiners award 1 mark for this validation.
> 3. **Confusing $P(A \mid B)$ with $P(B \mid A)$:** In Bayes' problems, students often invert the conditioning event. Read carefully: "Given X, find Y" → $P(Y \mid X)$.
> 4. **Mixing AND-gate and OR-gate formulas:** AND-gate = product; OR-gate = complement of product of complements. Examiners look for the correct formula choice.
> 5. **Skipping units / DPMO conversion:** When problem implies Six Sigma context, mention DPMO or sigma level. This is a 1-mark "value-add" line examiners appreciate.
> 6. **Not stating the assumption of independence:** For FTA problems, explicitly state "assuming independent failure events" — failure to do so costs 1 mark.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Tree Diagram = hierarchical branching tool for sequences of events/decisions/probabilities.
>
> ✅ **Four Types:** Probability Tree, Fault Tree (FTA), Event Tree (ETA), Decision Tree.
>
> ✅ **Multiplication Rule:** Use along a single path — $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$.
>
> ✅ **Addition Rule:** Use across multiple paths to same outcome — sum of path probabilities.
>
> ✅ **Exhaustive Constraint:** Probabilities at every node must sum to 1.
>
> ✅ **AND-Gate (FTA):** $P(\text{Top}) = \prod P(\text{Inputs})$.
>
> ✅ **OR-Gate (FTA):** $P(\text{Top}) = 1 - \prod (1 - P(\text{Input}_i))$.
>
> ✅ **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(B_i \mid A) = \dfrac{P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)}{\sum_j P(B_j) \cdot P(A \mid B_j)}$.
>
> ✅ **Law of Total Probability:** $P(A) = \sum P(B_i) \cdot P(A \mid B_i)$.
>
> ✅ **DPMO Conversion:** $\text{DPMO} = P(\text{defect}) \times 10^6$.
>
> ✅ **DMAIC Phase Mapping:** Tree diagrams are most heavily used in **Analyze** phase, secondarily in **Improve** (decision trees).
>
> ✅ **Always validate:** Sum of all path probabilities = 1.000.
>
> ✅ **State assumptions:** Independence of branches, exhaustive event sets, etc.
>
> ✅ **Engineering Utility:** Aerospace, nuclear, semiconductor, medical devices, supply chain risk.
>
> ✅ **Common Pitfall:** Adding probabilities for OR-gates (wrong); use complement rule instead.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matrix Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Matrix Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Matrix Diagram** is one of the **New Seven Management and Planning Tools (New 7 QC Tools)** used in Six Sigma and Total Quality Management (TQM). It is defined as a **graphical planning tool** that systematically displays the **inter-relationships between two, three, or more groups of information**, where each group is represented along a separate dimension (row, column, layer, or roof), and symbols placed at the intersections indicate the **strength and nature of the relationship** between the corresponding elements.

Mathematically and operationally, a Matrix Diagram transforms qualitative reasoning into a **structured relational map**, allowing engineers and managers to:

- Identify the **strength of relationships** (strong, moderate, weak, none)
- Pinpoint **responsibilities** (cross-functional accountability)
- Discover **gaps and bottlenecks** in process design
- Support **House of Quality (QFD)** integration in DFSS (Design for Six Sigma)

### 1.1.1 The Six Standard Forms of Matrix Diagram

| Form | Shape | Dimensions | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **L-Shaped** | L | **2** groups (Row × Column) | Two-variable correlation |
| **T-Shaped** | T | **3** groups (one central, two peripheral) | Responsibility + Task + Department |
| **Y-Shaped** | Y | **3** groups in triangular layout | Three interrelated sets sharing common base |
| **C-Shaped** | C (3D cube) | **3** groups shown on 3 axes | Multi-variable process analysis |
| **X-Shaped** | X | **4** groups (paired diagonally) | Cross-functional coordination |
| **Roof-Shaped** | ▽ (Roof on top) | **1** base group + pairwise correlation | Used **on top of L-matrix** as QFD roof |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a Matrix Diagram as a "Relationship Detective Board."** Imagine a crime investigation room where a detective pins up photographs of **suspects on the left wall**, **weapons on the right wall**, and **crime locations on the back wall**. Red strings between them indicate **strong links** (the suspect definitely used that weapon at that location), yellow strings for **possible links**, and no string means **no connection found**. The Matrix Diagram does exactly this — but with engineering data, customer requirements, departments, or process variables.

### 🎯 Real-World Engineering Analogy: **The Marriage Matchmaker**

Picture a marriage bureau that maintains a compatibility chart:
- **Rows:** Grooms (with qualifications, salary, hobbies)
- **Columns:** Brides (with preferences, age, location)
- **Symbol at intersection:**
  - ☉ = **Perfect match** (Strong Relationship)
  - ○ = **Possible match** (Moderate Relationship)
  - △ = **Weak Link** (Weak Relationship)
  - (Blank) = **No match** (No Relationship)

A Six Sigma analyst constructs an identical board, replacing *grooms* with **process causes** and *brides* with **process outputs** — instantly visualizing which cause most strongly drives which output.

---

## 1.3 Standard Symbols Used in Matrix Diagrams

| Symbol | Meaning | Visual Cue |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **◎ (Double Circle)** | **Strong** positive relationship (high priority) | Solid, two concentric circles |
| **○ (Single Circle)** | **Moderate** relationship | One circle |
| **△ (Triangle)** | **Weak** relationship | Outlined triangle |
| **(Blank)** | **No** relationship | Empty cell |

> [!TIP]
> Some organizations also use **numbered scales (1–9)** to quantify the strength instead of symbols, especially when feeding data into a House of Quality (HOQ).

---

## 1.4 Primary Objectives of a Matrix Diagram

1. **Identify** relationships between factors that may not be obvious.
2. **Strengthen** understanding of complex, multi-variable problems.
3. **Assign** responsibilities clearly across cross-functional teams.
4. **Detect** gaps where no process currently addresses a critical need.
5. **Support QFD (Quality Function Deployment)** by linking customer "Whats" with engineering "Hows."

---

## 1.5 Role of Matrix Diagram in the DMAIC Framework

| DMAIC Phase | Application of Matrix Diagram |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Define** | Map stakeholder requirements vs. project goals |
| **Measure** | Link Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) tree items to data sources |
| **Analyze** | Correlate root causes with process Y's (outputs) |
| **Improve** | Assign responsibility for corrective actions (RACI-style) |
| **Control** | Cross-link control plan items with SOPs and KPIs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Construction Logic (Step-Wise)

A Matrix Diagram is built using the following structured logic — examiners love asking these steps:

1. **Step 1 — Define the Groups (Items A, B, C, …)**
   - Identify 2 to 4 groups of related information (e.g., Customer Needs, Engineering Requirements, Departments, Process Steps).
2. **Step 2 — List Elements within Each Group**
   - Enumerate individual items vertically/horizontally (typically 5–25 items per group).
3. **Step 3 — Construct the Matrix Grid**
   - Draw axes corresponding to the chosen form (L, T, Y, C, X, Roof).
4. **Step 4 — Insert Relationship Symbols**
   - Use standardized symbols (◎, ○, △) at intersections after team consensus or statistical correlation.
5. **Step 5 — Analyze Patterns**
   - Look for **clusters** (high relationships), **gaps** (no relationships), and **bottlenecks** (single point of failure).
6. **Step 6 — Translate Findings into Action**
   - Convert matrix insights into action plans, responsibility matrices (RACI), or QFD relationships.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Classification — Six Forms Explained

### 🔷 2.2.1 L-Shaped Matrix
- **Dimensions:** 2 groups → Group A (rows) vs Group B (columns)
- **Intersection Cells:** Show relationship between ONE item from A and ONE from B.
- **Use Case:** "Which Customer Requirement is delivered by Which Engineering Specification?"

### 🔷 2.2.2 T-Shaped Matrix
- **Dimensions:** 3 groups (e.g., A-B and B-C combined; A and C do not directly intersect).
- **Use Case:** Linking **Tasks ↔ Departments ↔ Customer Requirements** in parallel T-matrices.

### 🔷 2.2.3 Y-Shaped Matrix
- **Dimensions:** 3 groups arranged in a triangular (Y) layout.
- **Use Case:** Three sets sharing a common base variable (e.g., Same Product → Different Markets → Different Functions).

### 🔷 2.2.4 C-Shaped Matrix
- **Dimensions:** 3 groups shown on the **three axes of a 3D cube**.
- **Use Case:** When analyzing a complex multivariate problem where the **triple intersection** of three variables is meaningful.

### 🔷 2.2.5 X-Shaped Matrix
- **Dimensions:** 4 groups arranged around a central point (no center element).
- **Use Case:** Cross-functional coordination between **4 interdependent departments**.

### 🔷 2.2.6 Roof-Shaped Matrix
- **Dimensions:** A single base matrix (typically L-shape) with a **roof** on top showing pairwise correlations among Group B items.
- **Use Case:** Used **exclusively on the roof of a House of Quality (QFD)** to identify **trade-offs** between engineering requirements (e.g., Strength vs. Cost).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Strong Relationship Weight** | $W_s = 9$ | Highest priority on HOQ |
| 2 | **Moderate Relationship Weight** | $W_m = 3$ | Mid priority |
| 3 | **Weak Relationship Weight** | $W_w = 1$ | Low priority |
| 4 | **No Relationship Weight** | $W_n = 0$ | No contribution |
| 5 | **Total Relationship Score (per item)** | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_{ij}$ | Sum of all column weights |
| 6 | **Weighted Importance** | $WI_k = \sum_{i=1}^{m} (S_i \times I_{ik})$ | Where $I_{ik}$ = importance rating |
| 7 | **Normalized Score** | $N_k = \dfrac{WI_k}{\max(WI)}$ | Range: 0 to 1 |
| 8 | **Coverage Ratio** | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{actual}}{N_{total}}$ | Fraction of non-blank cells |
| 9 | **Density (Rho)** | $\rho \geq 0.30$ | Indicates meaningful matrix |
| 10 | **QFD House Roof Trade-off** | $T_{ab} = \text{sign}(\partial Y_a / \partial Y_b)$ | Negative $T$ = trade-off |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Matrix Diagram is **not merely an academic diagram** — it is a frontline engineering tool in:

| Industry | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Automotive** | QFD — linking Voice of Customer (VOC) to engineering specs in DFSS |
| **Aerospace** | Cross-functional responsibility mapping (Design ↔ QA ↔ Production ↔ Supply Chain) |
| **Software Engineering** | Mapping Requirements ↔ Test Cases ↔ Modules (RTM — Requirements Traceability Matrix) |
| **Healthcare** | Linking Symptoms ↔ Diseases ↔ Treatments for clinical decision support |
| **Manufacturing** | Failure Mode cataloging — linking Failure Modes ↔ Effects ↔ Causes (precursor to FMEA) |
| **Civil Engineering** | Project stakeholder mapping — linking Deliverables ↔ Contractors ↔ Milestones |

---

## 2.5 Relationship to Other New 7 QC Tools

The New Seven Tools (developed by the **Japanese Society for Quality Control — JSQC**, popularized by Mizuno, Ishikawa, and others) are:

1. **Affinity Diagram** (KJ Method) — Grouping language data
2. **Inter-relationship Digraph** — Cause-effect network mapping
3. **Tree Diagram** — Hierarchical decomposition
4. **Matrix Diagram** ← *(this topic)*
5. **Matrix Data Analysis (MDA)** — Quantitative matrix analysis
6. **Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)** — Risk-aware planning tree
7. **Activity Network Diagram (AND)** — CPM/PERT for scheduling

> The **Matrix Diagram** specifically converts the **logical relationships** discovered by the Affinity Diagram and Tree Diagram into a **compact, visual relational grid**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: L-Shaped Matrix — Customer Voice to Engineering Specs

### 📋 Problem Statement
An automotive Six Sigma team has identified **3 Customer Requirements (CRs)** and **4 Engineering Specifications (ESs)**. The team has rated the relationship between each CR and each ES on a scale of 9 (Strong), 3 (Moderate), 1 (Weak), and 0 (None).

**Customer Requirements:**
- CR1: *Better Fuel Efficiency*
- CR2: *Lower Maintenance Cost*
- CR3: *Improved Safety*

**Engineering Specifications:**
- ES1: *Engine Displacement (cc)*
- ES2: *Material Grade (Yield Strength)*
- ES3: *Service Interval (months)*
- ES4: *Sensor Count (units)*

**Given Relationship Matrix $R$:**

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix}
9 & 3 & 1 & 0 \\
3 & 1 & 9 & 0 \\
0 & 9 & 3 & 9
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Where rows represent CR1, CR2, CR3 and columns represent ES1, ES2, ES3, ES4.

**Customer Importance Vector $I$ (from VOC survey, scale 1–5):**

$$
I = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 4 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 🧮 Step 1 — Compute Relationship Score per Engineering Specification

The total relationship score of each ES is the sum of the weights in its column.

$$
S_{ES1} = 9 + 3 + 0 = 12
$$

$$
S_{ES2} = 3 + 1 + 9 = 13
$$

$$
S_{ES3} = 1 + 9 + 3 = 13
$$

$$
S_{ES4} = 0 + 0 + 9 = 9
$$

### 🧮 Step 2 — Compute Weighted Importance $WI$

The weighted importance of each ES is given by $WI_k = \sum_i R_{ik} \cdot I_i$.

$$
WI_1 = (9)(5) + (3)(4) + (0)(5) = 45 + 12 + 0 = 57
$$

$$
WI_2 = (3)(5) + (1)(4) + (9)(5) = 15 + 4 + 45 = 64
$$

$$
WI_3 = (1)(5) + (9)(4) + (3)(5) = 5 + 36 + 15 = 56
$$

$$
WI_4 = (0)(5) + (0)(4) + (9)(5) = 0 + 0 + 45 = 45
$$

### 🧮 Step 3 — Normalize Scores (Range 0 to 1)

The maximum weighted importance is $\max(WI) = 64$.

$$
N_1 = \frac{57}{64} \approx 0.891
$$

$$
N_2 = \frac{64}{64} = 1.000
$$

$$
N_3 = \frac{56}{64} \approx 0.875
$$

$$
N_4 = \frac{45}{64} \approx 0.703
$$

### ✅ Step 4 — Rank Engineering Specifications by Priority

| Rank | ES | Specification | Weighted Importance | Normalized Score |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **1** | ES2 | Material Grade | 64 | 1.000 |
| **2** | ES1 | Engine Displacement | 57 | 0.891 |
| **3** | ES3 | Service Interval | 56 | 0.875 |
| **4** | ES4 | Sensor Count | 45 | 0.703 |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Although the team may have intuitively focused on Engine Displacement for fuel efficiency, the matrix reveals that **Material Grade (ES2)** has the highest aggregate importance — it impacts all three customer requirements simultaneously. This is a classic Matrix-Diagram-driven strategic insight.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Coverage Ratio and Density Calculation

### 📋 Problem Statement
Given a 4×5 L-matrix with the following occupied (non-blank) cells:
- Row 1: 3 occupied
- Row 2: 4 occupied
- Row 3: 2 occupied
- Row 4: 5 occupied

Total cells = $4 \times 5 = 20$.

### 🧮 Step 1 — Count Occupied Cells

$$
N_{actual} = 3 + 4 + 2 + 5 = 14
$$

### 🧮 Step 2 — Calculate Coverage Ratio

$$
\rho = \frac{N_{actual}}{N_{total}} = \frac{14}{20} = 0.70
$$

### ✅ Step 3 — Interpretation

Since $\rho = 0.70 > 0.30$ (the conventional threshold), the matrix is **meaningfully populated** and provides reliable relational insights.

> [!NOTE]
> A matrix with $\rho < 0.20$ is considered **sparse** and may require re-investigation of the underlying variables — possibly using an **Affinity Diagram** first to ensure the right groups are compared.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: T-Shaped Matrix — Responsibility Allocation

### 📋 Problem Statement
A T-matrix is constructed with:
- **Group A (Tasks):** T1, T2, T3
- **Group B (Departments):** D1 (Production), D2 (Quality), D3 (R&D)
- **Group C (Customer Requirements):** CR1, CR2

The two constituent L-matrices are:

**A–B Matrix (Task ↔ Department):**

$$
R_{AB} = \begin{bmatrix}
9 & 3 & 0 \\
3 & 9 & 1 \\
0 & 3 & 9
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**B–C Matrix (Department ↔ CR):**

$$
R_{BC} = \begin{bmatrix}
3 & 0 \\
9 & 3 \\
1 & 9
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 🧮 Step 1 — Verify the Linking Group

The **linking group** is Department (B). Both A–B and B–C matrices reference B's elements.

### 🧮 Step 2 — Calculate Effective Task ↔ CR Relationship via Department Routing

For T1 → D1 → CR1: $9 \times 3 = 27$
For T1 → D1 → CR2: $9 \times 0 = 0$
For T2 → D2 → CR1: $9 \times 9 = 81$ (Strongest!)
For T3 → D3 → CR2: $9 \times 9 = 81$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The T-matrix reveals that **Task T2, executed by Department D2, most strongly impacts CR1**, and **Task T3, executed by Department D3, most strongly impacts CR2**. This is precisely how a T-matrix supports **cross-functional accountability mapping** in Six Sigma project management.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Roof-Shaped Matrix Trade-Off Identification

### 📋 Problem Statement
A Roof-Matrix is constructed on top of the L-matrix (from Example 1) to assess trade-offs among the 4 Engineering Specifications (ES1–ES4). Negative correlations indicate trade-offs; positive correlations indicate synergy.

### 🧮 Step 1 — Pairwise Correlation Matrix

$$
\text{Trade-off Matrix } T = \begin{bmatrix}
- & - & + & 0 \\
- & - & - & + \\
+ & - & - & - \\
0 & + & - & -
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Where:
- "$+$" = **Synergistic** (improving one improves the other)
- "$-$" = **Trade-off** (improving one degrades the other)
- "$0$" = **Independent**

### 🧮 Step 2 — Identify the Strongest Trade-Off

The pair (ES2, ES3) and (ES3, ES4) both show "$-$" — confirming that improvements in **Material Grade (ES2)** often come at the cost of **Service Interval (ES3)** — a known metallurgical reality (harder alloys are harder to service).

> [!TIP]
> The Roof-matrix does **not assign numeric weights**; it provides a **qualitative dependency map**. For numeric trade-off analysis, an **MDA (Matrix Data Analysis)** or **multi-variable regression** must be performed.

---

## 3.5 Matrix Diagram — Construction Checklist (For Exam)

1. **Identify problem** and decide which dimensions to compare.
2. **Select the appropriate form** (L, T, Y, C, X, Roof).
3. **Enumerate items** in each group (limit to 5–25 per group).
4. **Place items** along rows/columns/layers.
5. **Apply relationship symbols** using team consensus or correlation data.
6. **Analyze patterns**: clusters, gaps, bottlenecks.
7. **Translate insights** into actionable plans (RACI, QFD, FMEA inputs).
8. **Validate** with stakeholders before finalization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Mermaid Diagram — Six Forms of Matrix Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Matrix Diagram: New 7 QC Tool] --> B[L-Shaped]
    A --> C[T-Shaped]
    A --> D[Y-Shaped]
    A --> E[C-Shaped]
    A --> F[X-Shaped]
    A --> G[Roof-Shaped]

    B --> B1[2 Groups]
    B --> B2[Customer Req vs Engineering Spec]

    C --> C1[3 Groups]
    C --> C2[Task-Department-CR Mapping]

    D --> D1[3 Groups Triangular]
    D --> D2[Product-Market-Function]

    E --> E1[3 Groups 3D Cube]
    E --> E2[Multivariate Analysis]

    F --> F1[4 Groups Central]
    F --> F2[Cross-Functional Coordination]

    G --> G1[Roof on L-Matrix]
    G --> G2[QFD Trade-off Identification]
```

---

## 4.2 L-Shaped Matrix — Detailed Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1[CR1: Fuel Efficiency] --> M1((9))
    R1 --> M2((3))
    R1 --> M3((1))
    R1 --> M4((0))

    R2[CR2: Low Maintenance] --> M5((3))
    R2 --> M6((1))
    R2 --> M7((9))
    R2 --> M8((0))

    R3[CR3: High Safety] --> M9((0))
    R3 --> M10((9))
    R3 --> M11((3))
    R3 --> M12((9))

    M1 --- C1[ES1: Engine Disp]
    M2 --- C2[ES2: Material]
    M3 --- C3[ES3: Service]
    M4 --- C4[ES4: Sensors]
    M5 --- C1
    M6 --- C2
    M7 --- C3
    M8 --- C4
    M9 --- C1
    M10 --- C2
    M11 --- C3
    M12 --- C4
```

---

## 4.3 T-Shaped Matrix — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph TOP[Top of T: Customer Requirements]
        CR_A[CR1: Efficiency]
        CR_B[CR2: Safety]
        CR_C[CR3: Cost]
    end

    subgraph MID[Middle Stem: Departments - Linking Group]
        D_A[Production]
        D_B[Quality]
        D_C[R and D]
    end

    subgraph BOT[Bottom of T: Tasks]
        T1[T1: Assembly]
        T2[T2: Inspection]
        T3[T3: Design]
    end

    CR_A --- D_A
    CR_A --- D_B
    CR_B --- D_B
    CR_B --- D_C
    CR_C --- D_C
    D_A --- T1
    D_B --- T2
    D_C --- T3
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Flow — When to Use Which Matrix Form

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Start: Define Comparison Problem] --> Q1{How many groups?}

    Q1 -->|2 Groups| Q2{Relationship is qualitative?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| LForm[Use L-Shaped]
    Q2 -->|No| MDA[Use Matrix Data Analysis MDA]

    Q1 -->|3 Groups| Q3{Is one group the common link?}
    Q3 -->|Yes, linear stem| TForm[Use T-Shaped]
    Q3 -->|Triangular layout| YForm[Use Y-Shaped]
    Q3 -->|3D intersection matters| CForm[Use C-Shaped]

    Q1 -->|4 Groups| Q4{Two pairs intersecting?}
    Q4 -->|Yes, diagonal| XForm[Use X-Shaped]
    Q4 -->|Need trade-offs on top of L-matrix| RForm[Use Roof-Shaped]
```

---

## 4.5 Integration of Matrix Diagram with DMAIC + QFD

```mermaid
graph LR
    VOC[Voice of Customer] --> QFD[House of Quality QFD]
    QFD --> L[L-Shaped: Whats vs Hows]
    L --> Roof[Roof-Matrix: Trade-offs]
    Roof --> T[T-Shaped: Responsibility]
    T --> DMAIC_I[DMAIC Improve Phase]
    DMAIC_I --> KPC[Key Process Controls]
    KPC --> C[C-Shaped: Multivariate Analysis]
    C --> DMAIC_C[DMAIC Control Phase]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This flow shows how the **Matrix Diagram is not a standalone tool** but a **family of tools** that interlock with QFD, FMEA, and DMAIC to support the full Six Sigma lifecycle.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Matrix Diagram. List the different types of Matrix Diagrams used in quality management.**

**Model Answer:**
A Matrix Diagram is one of the New Seven Management and Planning Tools used to systematically display the relationships between two or more groups of information, where the strength of relationships is indicated at the intersection cells using standardized symbols.

The six types of Matrix Diagrams are:
1. **L-Shaped** (2 groups, row × column)
2. **T-Shaped** (3 groups with one linking group)
3. **Y-Shaped** (3 groups in triangular arrangement)
4. **C-Shaped** (3 groups in a 3D cube configuration)
5. **X-Shaped** (4 groups arranged around a central point)
6. **Roof-Shaped** (used atop an L-matrix for trade-off analysis)

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for listing types correctly)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What are the standard symbols used in a Matrix Diagram to denote relationship strength?**

**Model Answer:**

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| ◎ (Double Circle) | Strong relationship |
| ○ (Single Circle) | Moderate relationship |
| △ (Triangle) | Weak relationship |
| (Blank) | No relationship |

Some organizations use numeric weights: **9** (Strong), **3** (Moderate), **1** (Weak), **0** (None).

*(3 marks — full list expected for full marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 ESE Rule: Module Internal Choice — **Answer any ONE from the following two choices.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **L-Shaped Matrix Diagram** with a suitable example. Discuss its role in the **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Roof-Shaped Matrix Diagram**? How is it used to identify **trade-offs** between engineering specifications? Illustrate with an example. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**L-Shaped Matrix Diagram:**
The L-shaped matrix is the most commonly used form. It relates **two groups of information** — one placed along the vertical axis (rows) and another along the horizontal axis (columns). Each cell at the intersection indicates the **strength of the relationship** between the corresponding row and column items.

**Example:** In an automotive project:
- **Rows (Group A):** Customer Requirements (Fuel Efficiency, Comfort, Safety, Cost)
- **Columns (Group B):** Engineering Specifications (Engine CC, Wheelbase, Brake Type, Sensor Count)

The cells show symbols (◎, ○, △, blank) indicating strong, moderate, weak, or no relationship.

**Role in QFD:**
In the **House of Quality (HOQ)**, the L-matrix forms the **central body** linking:
- **Customer Requirements (Whats)** — left wall of the house
- **Engineering Specifications (Hows)** — ceiling/roof base of the house

This linkage allows the team to compute **weighted importance** for each engineering spec, prioritizing design effort.

**Construction Steps:**
1. List CRs vertically.
2. List ESs horizontally.
3. Populate cells with relationship symbols.
4. Calculate weighted importance using the formula:
   $$WI_k = \sum_{i=1}^{m} R_{ik} \cdot I_i$$
5. Rank ESs and prioritize development.

*(7 marks — 2 for definition, 3 for example, 2 for QFD role)*

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Roof-Shaped Matrix Diagram:**
The roof-shaped matrix is a **triangular matrix** placed on top of an L-shaped matrix. It shows the **pairwise correlations** among the items in **one of the groups** (typically the Engineering Specifications) of the underlying L-matrix.

**Purpose:**
- Identifies **trade-offs** (negative correlations)
- Identifies **synergies** (positive correlations)
- Identifies **independencies** (no correlation)

**Example:**

Consider three engineering specs:
- **ES1:** Engine Power (kW)
- **ES2:** Fuel Efficiency (km/L)
- **ES3:** Vehicle Weight (kg)

Roof matrix (using symbols: $+$ for synergy, $-$ for trade-off):

$$
\text{Roof} = \begin{bmatrix}
- & - & + \\
& - & - \\
& & -
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- (ES1, ES2) = $-$ → Trade-off: Higher engine power reduces fuel efficiency.
- (ES1, ES3) = $+$ → Synergy: Engine power and weight grow together.
- (ES2, ES3) = $-$ → Trade-off: Heavier vehicles are less fuel-efficient.

**Engineering Decision:** The team must **balance** these trade-offs, possibly using **Taguchi loss functions** or **multi-objective optimization** in the Improve phase of DMAIC.

*(7 marks — 2 for definition, 3 for example trade-offs, 2 for engineering decision context)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **T-Shaped and Y-Shaped Matrix Diagrams**. When would you prefer each in a Six Sigma project? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **Matrix Data Analysis** interpretation framework for a 3×3 L-matrix with the following relationship weights and compute the **total relationship score** and **normalized scores** for each engineering specification:

**Relationship Matrix:**

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix}
9 & 3 & 0 \\
3 & 9 & 1 \\
0 & 3 & 9
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Customer Importance Vector:** $I = [5, 4, 5]^T$

**(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**T-Shaped Matrix Diagram:**
A T-shaped matrix combines **two L-shaped matrices** sharing a common group as the **stem** of the T. It involves **three groups** of information total, where the middle group links the two outer groups.

**Structure:**
- **Top bar of T:** Group A (e.g., Customer Requirements)
- **Stem of T:** Group B (e.g., Departments) — *linking group*
- **Bottom bar of T:** Group C (e.g., Tasks)

**Use Case:** Cross-functional Six Sigma project where customer requirements must be linked to internal tasks via departmental responsibility.

**Y-Shaped Matrix Diagram:**
A Y-shaped matrix arranges **three groups** of information in a **triangular** (Y) layout where each pair of groups is related. There is no central linking group; instead, each group is on the tip of one branch, with pairwise relations shown along the branches.

**Structure:**
- **Branch 1:** Group A
- **Branch 2:** Group B
- **Branch 3:** Group C
- **Center of Y:** Pairwise relationship cells

**Use Case:** When three sets of factors share a **common base variable** but are not hierarchically linked. Example: same product delivered to three different markets (A, B, C) with three different functions (P, Q, R) — the Y-matrix shows how each market-function combination interacts.

**Preference Rule:**

| Scenario | Prefer T-Shaped | Prefer Y-Shaped |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Linking group exists | ✅ | ❌ |
| Three equal-priority groups | ❌ | ✅ |
| Cross-functional responsibility needed | ✅ | ❌ |
| Product-Market-Function analysis | ❌ | ✅ |

*(7 marks — 3 T-shape, 3 Y-shape, 1 preference rule)*

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Relationship Score per ES (column sum):**

$$
S_{ES1} = 9 + 3 + 0 = 12
$$

$$
S_{ES2} = 3 + 1 + 3 = 7
$$

$$
S_{ES3} = 0 + 1 + 9 = 10
$$

> [!NOTE]
> *(Note: Row 2, Col 3 = 1; Row 3, Col 2 = 3; corrected based on the matrix definition where rows = CRs and columns = ESs.)*

**Step 2 — Compute Weighted Importance:**

$$
WI_1 = (9)(5) + (3)(4) + (0)(5) = 45 + 12 + 0 = 57
$$

$$
WI_2 = (3)(5) + (9)(4) + (3)(5) = 15 + 36 + 15 = 66
$$

$$
WI_3 = (0)(5) + (1)(4) + (9)(5) = 0 + 4 + 45 = 49
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize Scores:**

$$
N_1 = \frac{57}{66} \approx 0.864
$$

$$
N_2 = \frac{66}{66} = 1.000
$$

$$
N_3 = \frac{49}{66} \approx 0.742
$$

**Final Ranking Table:**

| Rank | ES | Weighted Importance | Normalized Score |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **1** | ES2 | 66 | 1.000 |
| **2** | ES1 | 57 | 0.864 |
| **3** | ES3 | 49 | 0.742 |

**Conclusion:** Engineering Specification 2 (ES2) has the highest weighted importance and should be prioritized for design optimization in the DMAIC Improve phase.

*(7 marks — 2 for column-sum logic, 3 for weighted importance, 2 for ranking & conclusion)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Lose 2–4 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the New 7 QC Tools with the Old 7 QC Tools.** The Matrix Diagram belongs to the **NEW** seven (planning tools), NOT the old seven (Pareto, Ishikawa, etc.). Examiners mark this strictly.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the relationship weights** (9, 3, 1, 0). Some texts use different weights (e.g., 5, 3, 1, 0). Always declare which scale you are using.
> 3. **Mixing up rows and columns in the matrix.** In an L-matrix, the customer requirements (CRs) are conventionally placed on the **left (rows)** and engineering specs (ESs) on the **top (columns)**. Reversing this leads to wrong $WI$ calculations.
> 4. **Not mentioning the linking group in T-matrices.** Examiners expect an explicit statement: *"The linking group is Department B."*
> 5. **Confusing Roof-matrix with Correlation matrix.** Roof-matrix is **qualitative** (synergy / trade-off symbols), not numeric. Don't insert Pearson coefficients in a Roof-matrix question.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Matrix Diagram = New 7 QC Tool** (Planning Tool, not Diagnostic).
- ⭐ **Six Forms:** L, T, Y, C, X, Roof — each for a different number of groups (2, 3, 3, 3, 4, and roof).
- ⭐ **Symbols:** ◎ (Strong = 9), ○ (Moderate = 3), △ (Weak = 1), Blank (None = 0).
- ⭐ **L-Matrix** is the foundation; **Roof-Matrix** sits on top for trade-off analysis.
- ⭐ **T-Matrix** uses a **linking group** (the stem); A↔B and B↔C — no A↔C intersection.
- ⭐ **Y-Matrix** = three groups, no central stem; pairwise relations on the branches.
- ⭐ **C-Matrix** = 3D cube — used when triple intersections of variables matter.
- ⭐ **X-Matrix** = four groups diagonally paired — no central element.
- ⭐ **Roof-Matrix** is essential for the **House of Quality (QFD)** in DFSS.
- ⭐ **Weighted Importance Formula:** $WI_k = \sum_{i=1}^{m} R_{ik} \cdot I_i$.
- ⭐ **Normalization Formula:** $N_k = WI_k / \max(WI)$.
- ⭐ **Coverage Ratio Threshold:** $\rho \geq 0.30$ for a meaningful matrix.
- ⭐ **Density Check:** If matrix is too sparse (<20% filled), revisit data using an Affinity Diagram first.
- ⭐ **Integration:** Matrix Diagram → QFD → FMEA → DMAIC → Six Sigma Output.
- ⭐ **Use Case Limitation:** Do NOT use Matrix Diagram for **large datasets (>25 items per group)** — switch to **Matrix Data Analysis (MDA)** instead.
- ⭐ **Exam Tip:** Always state the matrix form, the groups, the relationship symbols used, AND the resulting action plan.

---

> ✅ **End of Module 3 Notes — Matrix Diagram | PEMRT752 Six Sigma | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prioritization Matrices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Prioritization Matrices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Prioritization Matrix** is a structured, quantitative **Lean Six Sigma decision-making tool** used primarily in the **Analyze** and **Improve** phases of the **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) methodology. It is a **L-shaped matrix** that enables a project team to objectively rank, score, and prioritize a list of competing alternatives (such as potential root causes, failure modes, solution options, or project proposals) against a set of weighted selection criteria.

The matrix converts subjective, qualitative judgments into a defensible, numerical score using a **multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA)** framework, thereby eliminating personal bias and political influence from the selection process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMRT752, Module 3):**
> "A Prioritization Matrix is an L-shaped matrix used to identify and rank issues, problems, solutions, or actions based on weighted criteria, providing a structured approach to compare options against multiple factors of differing importance."

The three principal variants studied under the KTU syllabus are:

| Variant | Full Form | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|
| **P-Matrix** | Problem/Process Matrix | Prioritizing *problems* or *projects* |
| **X-Matrix** | Complexity/Effort-Effect Matrix | Prioritizing *potential solutions* |
| **F-Matrix** | Failure Mode Matrix (FMEA) | Prioritizing *failure modes* of a process |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Travelling Agent" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Choosing a Restaurant with Friends**
>
> Imagine five friends trying to pick a restaurant. Each friend cares about different things — one wants **low cost**, another wants **good ambience**, a third wants **vegetarian options**, etc. Instead of arguing for an hour, the group does the following:
>
> 1. **Lists criteria** (cost, ambience, food type, distance) — *the columns of the L-matrix*.
> 2. **Assigns weights** to each criterion based on group consensus (e.g., cost = 4, ambience = 2).
> 3. **Lists options** (5 restaurants) — *the rows of the L-matrix*.
> 4. **Scores** every restaurant on every criterion (1–5 scale).
> 5. **Multiplies and sums** to get a final weighted total.
> 6. The restaurant with the **highest score wins** — no arguments, no bias.
>
> A Prioritization Matrix does *exactly this* for a Six Sigma team facing dozens of potential root causes or improvement ideas. It replaces the loudest voice in the room with a **transparent mathematical verdict**.

## 1.3 Why Prioritization Matrices are Essential in Six Sigma

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a real DMAIC project, a Green Belt or Black Belt may identify **30–100 potential causes** from a Fishbone diagram or **20+ solution ideas** from a brainstorming session. It is mathematically and economically infeasible to address all of them at once. A Prioritization Matrix is the **filtering engine** that concentrates limited project resources on the **"vital few"** (Pareto's 80/20 principle) instead of the "trivial many."

**Key Engineering / Management Benefits:**
- **Objectivity:** Removes emotional and political bias.
- **Defensibility:** Creates an audit trail of *why* an option was selected.
- **Team Alignment:** Forces consensus on weighting criteria.
- **Time & Cost Efficiency:** Prevents wasted resources on low-impact items.
- **DMAIC Compliance:** Required documentation in **Phase Gate Reviews** at most organizations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The L-Matrix Anatomy

A Prioritization Matrix is called an **"L-Matrix"** because of its shape — the L is formed by the **weighted criteria row** running across the top (the horizontal arm) and the **list of options/alternatives** running down the side (the vertical arm). The interior cell is the **multiplication product of weight × score**.

### 2.1.1 The Three Building Blocks

1. **List of Options (Y-Axis / Rows):** The alternatives to be ranked — failure modes, root causes, solutions, or projects.
2. **List of Criteria (X-Axis / Columns):** The factors by which each option will be evaluated — cost, ease of implementation, customer impact, etc.
3. **Weight of each Criterion (Top Row):** A numerical importance factor assigned to each criterion, with the **sum of weights = 1.0** (or 100%) for normalized weighted scoring.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Construction Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard 7-Step Methodology (Slight Variation of Kepner-Tregoe & Pyzdek's Six Sigma Handbook):**

**Step 1 — Identify the Decision Objective**
Clearly define what is being prioritized (e.g., "Which solution should we pilot first?"). A fuzzy objective produces a useless matrix.

**Step 2 — List All Options / Alternatives**
Brainstorm exhaustively. Typical Six Sigma tools used here: Affinity Diagram, Nominal Group Technique, or Mind Mapping.

**Step 3 — Identify Selection Criteria**
Criteria must be **MECE** (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive). Common examples: Cost, Time, Customer Impact, Risk, Strategic Alignment, Ease of Implementation.

**Step 4 — Assign Weights to Each Criterion**
The team assigns a weight (often 1–5 or 1–10) to each criterion. Weights represent **relative importance** — a weight of 5 means "5 times more important" than a weight of 1.

**Step 5 — Score Each Option Against Each Criterion**
Each option receives a raw score (typically on a 1–5 or 1–10 scale). Use a **scoring rubric** to ensure consistency among team members.

**Step 6 — Calculate Weighted Score and Rank**
Multiply the weight by the score for every cell, then sum across the row to get the **Total Weighted Score (TWS)**. Rank options in descending order of TWS.

**Step 7 — Validate, Document, and Escalate**
The top-ranked options are pursued. The matrix is attached to the project charter as supporting evidence.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are examinable. Memorize the formula, the meaning of each variable, and the typical scale.**

| # | Formula | Variable Definitions | Typical Scale / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TWS_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \cdot S_{ij}$ | $TWS_i$ = Total Weighted Score of option $i$; $W_j$ = Weight of criterion $j$; $S_{ij}$ = Score of option $i$ on criterion $j$; $n$ = number of criteria | $TWS$ is dimensionless (unitless score) |
| 2 | $W_j^{norm} = \dfrac{W_j^{raw}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j^{raw}}$ | $W_j^{norm}$ = Normalized weight (0 to 1); $W_j^{raw}$ = Raw weight assigned by team | Sum = 1.0 (or 100%) |
| 3 | $TWS_i^{norm} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j^{norm} \cdot S_{ij}$ | Normalized variant — useful when comparing across different projects | $TWS^{norm}$ lies in the same range as $S_{ij}$ (e.g., 1 to 5) |
| 4 | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | $RPN$ = Risk Priority Number; $S$ = Severity (1–10); $O$ = Occurrence (1–10); $D$ = Detection (1–10) | Range: 1 to 1000 |
| 5 | $\bar{S} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i$ | Mean score of a criterion (used for inter-rater reliability) | Same scale as $S_i$ |
| 6 | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$ | Coefficient of Variation — measures team disagreement on a score | Percentage (%) |

## 2.4 The FMEA Matrix (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)

The **FMEA Matrix** is a specialized form of the Prioritization Matrix, mandated by **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)** and **IATF 16949:2016** for the automotive industry, but applicable across all manufacturing and service sectors.

**Three Critical Variables:**

- **Severity (S):** How serious is the consequence to the customer? (1 = negligible, 10 = hazardous without warning)
- **Occurrence (O):** How frequently does the failure mode happen? (1 = very rare, 10 = very high / failure is inevitable)
- **Detection (D):** How likely is the current control to *detect* the failure *before* it reaches the customer? (1 = almost certain detection, 10 = no detection capability)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Many students think **Detection** means "how easy it is to find the failure." This is the *opposite* of the FMEA definition. In FMEA, a **high Detection score is BAD** because it means the failure is hard to detect. Always remember: **High RPN = Bad, High S = Bad, High O = Bad, High D = Bad.**

## 2.5 The X-Matrix (Solution Selection Matrix)

The **X-Matrix**, also called the **Ease-Effect Matrix** or **Cost-Benefit Matrix**, is plotted as a 2-D scatter plot to visualize solution options on two axes:

- **X-Axis (Horizontal):** Ease of Implementation (or Low Cost)
- **Y-Axis (Vertical):** Impact / Benefit (or Customer Satisfaction Gain)

The matrix is divided into four quadrants:
- **Top-Left (Quick Wins):** High impact, easy to implement — **Do First**
- **Top-Right (Major Projects):** High impact, difficult — **Plan Carefully**
- **Bottom-Left (Fill-Ins):** Low impact, easy — **Do if time permits**
- **Bottom-Right (Thankless Tasks):** Low impact, difficult — **Avoid**

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in the Real World

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications:**
> - **Automotive (IATF 16949):** Mandatory FMEA for every new part design.
> - **Healthcare:** Prioritizing patient safety risks (NPSF/HPI frameworks).
> - **IT Service Management (ITIL 4):** Prioritizing incidents, problems, and change requests.
> - **Pharma (FDA Process Validation):** FMEA used in Stage 1 Risk Assessment.
> - **Aerospace (AS9100):** FMEA + Prioritization Matrix is core to APQP and PPAP submissions.
> - **Banking/Finance:** Prioritizing Six Sigma projects in the annual portfolio review.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Standard Weighted Prioritization (Process Improvement Solutions)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (KTU-Style)

A Six Sigma Green Belt at an automotive components plant has identified **four potential solutions** to reduce surface-defect rejection rate on a CNC-machined bracket. The team has agreed on **three selection criteria**. Build the Prioritization Matrix and recommend the solution to be piloted.

**Solutions (Rows):**
- S1: Replace cutting tool with coated carbide variant
- S2: Implement Statistical Process Control (SPC) on coolant pressure
- S3: Train operators on chip-load adjustment (Kaizen)
- S4: Install automated in-line vision inspection system

**Criteria (Columns) & Weights:**
- C1: Impact on Defect Reduction (Weight = 5)
- C2: Ease of Implementation (Weight = 3)
- C3: Cost of Implementation — *lower is better* (Weight = 2)

> **Total Raw Weight = 5 + 3 + 2 = 10**

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Normalize the Weights

Using the formula $W_j^{norm} = \dfrac{W_j^{raw}}{\sum W_j^{raw}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{C1}^{norm} &= \frac{5}{10} = 0.50 \\[4pt]
W_{C2}^{norm} &= \frac{3}{10} = 0.30 \\[4pt]
W_{C3}^{norm} &= \frac{2}{10} = 0.20 \\[4pt]
\text{Check: } 0.50 + 0.30 + 0.20 &= 1.00 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Raw Score Assignment (1 to 5 scale)

| Solution | C1: Impact | C2: Ease | C3: Low Cost (inverted) |
|---|---|---|---|
| S1: Coated Carbide Tool | 4 | 4 | 2 |
| S2: SPC on Coolant | 5 | 2 | 4 |
| S3: Operator Training | 2 | 5 | 5 |
| S4: Vision Inspection | 5 | 1 | 1 |

> **Note on C3 (Cost):** Since lower cost is *better*, the team inverted the cost ranking so that a high score = low cost. Cost ranking was: S3 (cheapest, score 5) > S2 (score 4) > S1 (score 2) > S4 (most expensive, score 1).

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Calculate Weighted Scores (Cell-by-Cell)

Using $TWS_i = \sum W_j^{norm} \cdot S_{ij}$:

**Solution S1 (Coated Carbide Tool):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
TWS_{S1} &= (0.50 \times 4) + (0.30 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 2) \\
&= 2.00 + 1.20 + 0.40 \\
&= 3.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution S2 (SPC on Coolant):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
TWS_{S2} &= (0.50 \times 5) + (0.30 \times 2) + (0.20 \times 4) \\
&= 2.50 + 0.60 + 0.80 \\
&= 3.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution S3 (Operator Training):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
TWS_{S3} &= (0.50 \times 2) + (0.30 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 5) \\
&= 1.00 + 1.50 + 1.00 \\
&= 3.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution S4 (Vision Inspection):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
TWS_{S4} &= (0.50 \times 5) + (0.30 \times 1) + (0.20 \times 1) \\
&= 2.50 + 0.30 + 0.20 \\
&= 3.00
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Final Rank and Recommendation

| Rank | Solution | TWS | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | S2: SPC on Coolant Pressure | **3.90** | **PILOT FIRST** |
| 2 | S1: Coated Carbide Tool | 3.60 | Pilot Second |
| 3 | S3: Operator Training | 3.50 | Pilot Third |
| 4 | S4: Vision Inspection | 3.00 | Defer / Re-evaluate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Although S4 (Vision Inspection) had the highest raw impact score, its prohibitive cost and complexity pulled its TWS to last place. The team should **pilot S2 first** because it scores well across all three weighted criteria, especially the highest-weighted one (Impact).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — FMEA / Risk Priority Number (RPN) Calculation

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A Six Sigma team has identified **three failure modes** for a soldering process on a printed circuit board (PCB). Compute the RPN and recommend the failure mode to address first.

### 3.2.2 Data Table

| ID | Failure Mode | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FM1 | Cold solder joint | 8 | 6 | 4 |
| FM2 | Solder bridge (short circuit) | 9 | 3 | 7 |
| FM3 | Insufficient solder fillet | 6 | 7 | 5 |

### 3.2.3 Step 1 — Apply the RPN Formula: $RPN = S \times O \times D$

**Failure Mode 1 — Cold Solder Joint:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{FM1} &= 8 \times 6 \times 4 \\
&= 48 \times 4 \\
&= 192
\end{aligned}
$$

**Failure Mode 2 — Solder Bridge:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{FM2} &= 9 \times 3 \times 7 \\
&= 27 \times 7 \\
&= 189
\end{aligned}
$$

**Failure Mode 3 — Insufficient Solder Fillet:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{FM3} &= 6 \times 7 \times 5 \\
&= 42 \times 5 \\
&= 210
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 2 — Rank and Recommend

| Rank | Failure Mode | S | O | D | RPN | Action Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | FM3: Insufficient Solder Fillet | 6 | 7 | 5 | **210** | **Address First** (RPN > 200) |
| 2 | FM1: Cold Solder Joint | 8 | 6 | 4 | 192 | Address Second |
| 3 | FM2: Solder Bridge | 9 | 3 | 7 | 189 | Monitor / Re-score after improvement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Rule of Thumb:** Any RPN above **200** is considered a "red flag" requiring immediate Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA). FM3 (RPN = 210) crosses this threshold and must be addressed first, even though FM1 has higher Severity.

### 3.2.5 Step 3 — Improvement Action Plan (RPN Reduction)

For FM3, the team would design a **Recommended Action** (e.g., automated solder-paste volume check), then re-score the RPN to confirm reduction.

**Hypothetical Post-Action Scores:** $S = 6, O = 2, D = 3$
$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{FM3}^{new} &= 6 \times 2 \times 3 = 36
\end{aligned}
$$

**RPN Reduction Percentage:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} &= \frac{210 - 36}{210} \times 100\% = \frac{174}{210} \times 100\% \approx 82.86\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An RPN reduction of **> 80%** is generally accepted as strong evidence of process improvement and is a key metric in the **Control phase** to demonstrate the project's success to the **Champion** and **Process Owner**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Prioritization Matrix Construction Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the complete logical flow of building and applying a Prioritization Matrix, from project initiation through continuous improvement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start DMAIC Analyze Phase] --> B[Identify Decision Objective]
    B --> C[List All Options / Alternatives]
    C --> D[Brainstorm via Affinity Diagram or NGT]
    D --> E[Identify Selection Criteria MECE]
    E --> F[Assign Raw Weights via Team Consensus]
    F --> G[Normalize Weights Sum equals 1]
    G --> H[Define Scoring Rubric 1 to 5 scale]
    H --> I[Score Each Option Against Each Criterion]
    I --> J{Calculate TWS}
    J --> K[Rank Options in Descending TWS]
    K --> L{Top Ranked Option meets threshold}
    L -->|Yes| M[Attach Matrix to Charter and Pilot]
    L -->|No| N[Re-evaluate Weights or Criteria]
    N --> F
    M --> O[Monitor Outcomes in Control Phase]
    O --> P[Re-score if New Data Available]
    P --> I
    P --> Q[End of Prioritization Loop]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Architecture: P-Matrix vs X-Matrix vs F-Matrix

The following Mermaid block compares the three major variants of Prioritization Matrices studied in Module 3, mapping their inputs, structure, and typical use within the DMAIC lifecycle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input1 [Input Options]
        O1[Root Causes from Fishbone]
        O2[Solution Ideas from Brainstorm]
        O3[Potential Failure Modes]
    end

    subgraph MatrixTypes [Three Prioritization Matrix Variants]
        direction TB
        PMatrix[P-Matrix Problem Prioritization]
        XMatrix[X-Matrix Solution Selection Ease vs Impact]
        FMatrix[F-Matrix FMEA RPN equals S times O times D]
    end

    subgraph Output1 [Decision Output]
        D1[Top Vital Few Problems]
        D2[Quick Wins and Major Projects]
        D3[Highest RPN Failure Modes to Fix]
    end

    O1 --> PMatrix
    O2 --> XMatrix
    O3 --> FMatrix
    PMatrix --> D1
    XMatrix --> D2
    FMatrix --> D3
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FMEA Worksheet

The following Mermaid block maps the standard AIAG-VDA FMEA worksheet architecture — the most heavily used prioritization tool in regulated industries.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FMEA Header: Part Number, Process Step, Date, Team] --> B[Item / Function Column]
    B --> C[Potential Failure Mode Column]
    C --> D[Potential Effect on Customer Column]
    D --> E[Potential Cause Column]
    E --> F[Current Process Controls]
    F --> G[Score S Severity 1 to 10]
    F --> H[Score O Occurrence 1 to 10]
    F --> I[Score D Detection 1 to 10]
    G --> J[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{RPN greater than 200}
    K -->|Yes| L[Recommended Action Required]
    K -->|No| M[Monitor Only]
    L --> N[Action Taken Column]
    N --> O[Re-score S O D Post Action]
    O --> P[New RPN and Percent Reduction]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions are short, definition/analytical questions testing **Course Outcome CO3** at the **Remember / Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define a Prioritization Matrix. Mention any two of its variants used in Six Sigma projects.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Prioritization Matrix** is a structured quantitative decision-making tool used in the **Analyze and Improve phases** of the **DMAIC** methodology. It ranks competing options (such as causes, solutions, or failure modes) against a set of **weighted selection criteria**, producing an objective **Total Weighted Score (TWS)** for each option so that the team can focus on the highest-impact items.

**Two Variants:**
1. **P-Matrix (Problem Prioritization Matrix):** Used to rank potential root causes or problems identified from tools like Fishbone or Pareto charts.
2. **FMEA Matrix (F-Matrix):** Used to rank potential failure modes of a process using the **Risk Priority Number (RPN) = Severity × Occurrence × Detection**.

*(Acceptable alternative: X-Matrix for solution selection.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Write the formula for the Risk Priority Number (RPN) used in FMEA and explain what each variable means.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

**Where:**
- **S (Severity):** A score from 1 to 10 rating how serious the impact of the failure would be on the customer (1 = negligible, 10 = hazardous).
- **O (Occurrence):** A score from 1 to 10 rating how frequently the failure mode happens (1 = very rare, 10 = very frequent / inevitable).
- **D (Detection):** A score from 1 to 10 rating the inability of current controls to detect the failure before it reaches the customer (1 = almost certain detection, 10 = no detection mechanism).

> The RPN ranges from **1 to 1000**. Higher RPN indicates a more critical failure mode requiring immediate corrective action. A common industrial threshold for action is **RPN > 200**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE) carry **14 marks** each, typically divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students choose one question from a Module, and two full questions are compulsory.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A hospital Six Sigma team has identified three quality improvement projects to reduce patient wait time in the Out-Patient Department (OPD). The team has agreed upon four selection criteria with raw weights as given below. Build a Prioritization Matrix using normalized weights and recommend the project to be executed first.**

**Projects (Options):**
- **P1:** Implement Online Appointment Scheduling System
- **P2:** Increase the Number of Registration Counters (from 3 to 6)
- **P3:** Deploy a Real-Time Digital Queue Display System

**Criteria and Raw Weights:**
- C1: Impact on Patient Wait-Time Reduction (Raw Weight = 8)
- C2: Cost of Implementation (Raw Weight = 5)
- C3: Ease of Implementation (Raw Weight = 4)
- C4: Patient Satisfaction Improvement Potential (Raw Weight = 3)

> **Note:** Score each project on a **1 to 5 scale** where 5 = best.

| Project | C1 Impact | C2 Cost | C3 Ease | C4 Satisfaction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1: Online Scheduling | 5 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| P2: More Counters | 4 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| P3: Digital Queue | 3 | 3 | 4 | 3 |

---

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Normalize the Weights and Construct the L-Matrix

**Step 1 — Normalize the Weights:**

Sum of raw weights = $8 + 5 + 4 + 3 = 20$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{C1}^{norm} &= \frac{8}{20} = 0.40 \\[4pt]
W_{C2}^{norm} &= \frac{5}{20} = 0.25 \\[4pt]
W_{C3}^{norm} &= \frac{4}{20} = 0.20 \\[4pt]
W_{C4}^{norm} &= \frac{3}{20} = 0.15 \\[4pt]
\text{Sum Check: } 0.40 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 &= 1.00 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Weighted Scores per Cell (Weight × Score):**

**Project P1 (Online Scheduling):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Weighted score} &= (0.40 \times 5) + (0.25 \times 2) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.15 \times 4) \\
&= 2.00 + 0.50 + 0.60 + 0.60 \\
&= 3.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project P2 (More Counters):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Weighted score} &= (0.40 \times 4) + (0.25 \times 1) + (0.20 \times 2) + (0.15 \times 3) \\
&= 1.60 + 0.25 + 0.40 + 0.45 \\
&= 2.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project P3 (Digital Queue):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Weighted score} &= (0.40 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 3) \\
&= 1.20 + 0.75 + 0.80 + 0.45 \\
&= 3.20
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Rank, Interpret, and Recommend

**Final Ranked Matrix:**

| Rank | Project | C1 (0.40) | C2 (0.25) | C3 (0.20) | C4 (0.15) | TWS | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | P1: Online Scheduling | 2.00 | 0.50 | 0.60 | 0.60 | **3.70** | **Execute First** |
| 2 | P3: Digital Queue | 1.20 | 0.75 | 0.80 | 0.45 | 3.20 | Execute Second |
| 3 | P2: More Counters | 1.60 | 0.25 | 0.40 | 0.45 | 2.70 | Defer |

**Interpretation & Recommendation:**

- **Project P1 (Online Appointment Scheduling)** has the **highest TWS of 3.70** and should be **executed first**.
- It scores a perfect 5 on the highest-weighted criterion (C1: Impact), confirming it has the strongest potential to reduce wait times.
- Although **P2 (More Counters)** scored well on Impact (4), its high cost (C2 = 1) and difficulty of implementation (C3 = 2) dragged its TWS to the bottom.
- **P3 (Digital Queue)** is a balanced second choice; the team should plan it as a follow-up project after P1 matures.
- **Strategic Insight:** This result aligns with **Lean Six Sigma philosophy** — process redesign (preventing wait from occurring) is preferred over adding resources (hiring more counters), which is consistent with Toyota Production System principles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Loss of Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize the weights** — many students multiply by raw weights and skip the normalization step, leading to inflated TWS values. The normalization to sum = 1 is **mandatory** for full credit.
> 2. **Confusing "Cost" direction** — Cost is a *negative* criterion; lower is better. Students often forget to invert the score, which silently flips the recommendation.
> 3. **Skipping the sum-check** — always verify $\sum W_j = 1$ to catch arithmetic errors. Examiners explicitly look for this line.
> 4. **No final recommendation** — the matrix alone is incomplete. A 14-mark question **must** end with a clear recommendation and a brief justification linked back to the highest-weighted criterion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: An automotive component manufacturer is performing a Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) on a brake disc machining process. Five failure modes have been identified. The team has scored each on Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D) on a 1 to 10 scale. Calculate the Risk Priority Number (RPN) for each, rank the failure modes, and propose a corrective action strategy for the highest-priority failure.**

| ID | Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FM1 | Surface roughness exceeds tolerance (Ra > 1.6 μm) | 7 | 5 | 4 |
| FM2 | Disc thickness variation (runout > 0.05 mm) | 9 | 4 | 6 |
| FM3 | Micro-cracks in material (visible under magnifier) | 10 | 2 | 8 |
| FM4 | Excessive burr on edges | 4 | 7 | 3 |
| FM5 | Hardness below specification (HRB < 85) | 8 | 3 | 5 |

---

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Compute RPN and Rank

**Formula:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$

**FM1 — Surface Roughness Exceeds Tolerance:**
$$
RPN_{FM1} = 7 \times 5 \times 4 = 140
$$

**FM2 — Disc Thickness Variation:**
$$
RPN_{FM2} = 9 \times 4 \times 6 = 216
$$

**FM3 — Micro-Cracks in Material:**
$$
RPN_{FM3} = 10 \times 2 \times 8 = 160
$$

**FM4 — Excessive Burr on Edges:**
$$
RPN_{FM4} = 4 \times 7 \times 3 = 84
$$

**FM5 — Hardness Below Specification:**
$$
RPN_{FM5} = 8 \times 3 \times 5 = 120
$$

**Ranked FMEA Table:**

| Rank | FM ID | Failure Mode | S | O | D | RPN | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **FM2** | **Disc Thickness Variation** | 9 | 4 | 6 | **216** | **Above Threshold (200) — Act First** |
| 2 | FM3 | Micro-Cracks | 10 | 2 | 8 | 160 | High Severity — Improve Detection |
| 3 | FM1 | Surface Roughness | 7 | 5 | 4 | 140 | Monitor |
| 4 | FM5 | Low Hardness | 8 | 3 | 5 | 120 | Monitor |
| 5 | FM4 | Excessive Burr | 4 | 7 | 3 | 84 | Low Priority |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Corrective Action Strategy for FM2

**Why FM2 is Critical:**
- **RPN = 216**, the only failure mode exceeding the **200 threshold**.
- **Severity = 9** (very high): a thickness runout causes brake judder and disc wobble, leading to customer safety concerns and warranty claims.
- **Detection = 6** (moderately poor): the current inspection relies on sampling, not 100% inspection.

**Proposed Corrective Actions (Multi-Layered Strategy):**

1. **Poka-Yoke (Mistake-Proofing) Detection:** Install a **100% in-process laser runout gauge** on the CNC machine to provide real-time SPC feedback. This addresses the Detection (D) score.

2. **Process Control Improvement:** Implement a **Statistical Process Control (SPC) chart** on the lathe feed-rate, with control limits set at ±3σ. Any out-of-control point auto-stops the machine. This addresses Occurrence (O).

3. **Root Cause Verification:** Apply **5-Why Analysis** to the thickness variation. A typical root cause is tool wear drift; installing a tool-life counter linked to the CNC will eliminate the cause, not just detect it.

4. **Supplier Material Verification:** Conduct **incoming material hardness and grain-structure testing** to rule out raw-material variability as a contributing factor.

**Expected Post-Action Re-Score (Hypothetical):** $S = 9, O = 2, D = 2$

$$
RPN_{FM2}^{new} = 9 \times 2 \times 2 = 36
$$

**Percentage RPN Reduction:**
$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{216 - 36}{216} \times 100\% \approx 83.33\%
$$

> An **RPN reduction > 80%** is the standard benchmark for closing out an FMEA action item and updating the **Control Plan** in the IATF 16949 PPAP package.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — FMEA Questions:**
> 1. **Misinterpreting Detection:** Students frequently invert the Detection scale. A *high* D = *poor* detection. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this conceptual error.
> 2. **No RPN threshold cited:** Always state the threshold (typically RPN > 200) when declaring which FMs need action. Without it, the recommendation appears arbitrary.
> 3. **Treating RPN as the sole metric:** Modern FMEA (post-2019 AIAG-VDA) also considers **S alone** and **Action Priority (AP)**. FM3 with Severity = 10 would *also* warrant attention even though its RPN (160) is lower — note this in the answer to earn full marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the post-action re-score:** An FMEA is not complete without a verification step showing the new RPN. Examiners expect this closure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Prioritization Matrices" (Module 3)**

- ✅ A **Prioritization Matrix** is an L-shaped, multi-criteria decision tool used in the **Analyze and Improve** phases of DMAIC.
- ✅ Its purpose is to **convert subjective opinions into objective numerical scores** that rank alternatives.
- ✅ The **three main variants** are **P-Matrix** (problems), **X-Matrix** (solutions), and **F-Matrix / FMEA** (failure modes).
- ✅ The core formula is $TWS_i = \sum W_j \times S_{ij}$ — sum the products of (weight × score) across all criteria.
- ✅ Weights **must be normalized** so that $\sum W_j = 1.0$ (or 100%).
- ✅ The standard scoring scale is **1 to 5** (or 1 to 10); the scale must be defined *before* scoring begins to ensure consistency.
- ✅ The **FMEA RPN formula** is $RPN = S \times O \times D$, with a range of **1 to 1000**.
- ✅ In FMEA, a **high Detection (D) score is BAD** — it indicates poor ability to find the failure.
- ✅ The **industry action threshold** for RPN is typically **> 200**; any FM above this requires a Corrective Action Plan.
- ✅ The **X-Matrix** plots solutions on **Ease (X) vs. Impact (Y)**; the top-left quadrant is the "Quick Wins" zone.
- ✅ **Pareto Alignment:** The matrix operationalizes the **80/20 principle** by surfacing the "vital few" items from the "trivial many."
- ✅ **Documentation is mandatory:** The completed matrix must be attached to the **Project Charter** and the **Phase Gate Review** submission.
- ✅ **Inter-rater reliability** should be checked using the Coefficient of Variation ($CV = \sigma / \bar{x}$) when multiple team members score independently.
- ✅ **Cost-type criteria must be inverted** before scoring (lower cost = higher score), otherwise the recommendation direction flips.
- ✅ **Post-action re-scoring** of RPN with a percentage reduction calculation is the standard evidence of improvement in the **Control** phase.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)** is a systematic, visual problem-solving and planning methodology used in quality management to anticipate potential obstacles, identify countermeasures, and establish contingency pathways for implementing a plan. It is classified as one of the **Seven New (Management) Tools for Quality Control**, originally developed in Japan during the **1960s–1970s** within the Quality Control circles movement. PDPC is used to dynamically map the sequence of steps in a plan while simultaneously projecting "what-if" branches to address deviations, failures, or unforeseen risks before they occur.

In the context of **Six Sigma (DMAIC framework)**, PDPC is extensively deployed in the **Improve (I)** and **Control (C)** phases to map process improvements, design robust countermeasures, and embed fail-safe mechanisms directly into the updated process flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PDPC is a *proactive* (not reactive) tool. Unlike Fishbone or Pareto charts that analyze *past* defects, PDPC is used to plan the *future* execution of a Six Sigma project by charting probable deviations **before** they materialize.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are planning a **road trip from Kerala to Delhi by car**. The straightforward plan is: Kerala → Karnataka → Maharashtra → Madhya Pradesh → Delhi. However, a wise traveler would think:

- "What if the Bangalore route has heavy traffic?" → *Contingency:* Take the Coimbatore-Salem bypass.
- "What if my car breaks down in Maharashtra?" → *Contingency:* Have the service center helpline saved; carry a spare kit.
- "What if it rains heavily in MP?" → *Contingency:* Postpone travel by 6 hours.

The PDPC works exactly like this contingency map. It takes your **ideal route (process plan)** and visually projects every possible detour, hazard, and fallback alternative so that, when the unexpected happens, the team does not panic — they simply follow the pre-planned branch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metric Associated:** PDPC itself is not a numerical tool. It works in conjunction with tools like **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**, where the **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** values from FMEA directly inform the *priority* of branches in the PDPC.

### Two Variants of PDPC
| Variant | Use Case | Visual Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Type 1: Sequential (Form-First) PDPC** | Used when the process flow is **fixed** and contingencies are derived for each step. | Vertical/hierarchical tree expanding from the start node. |
| **Type 2: Priority-Based (Goal-First) PDPC** | Used when the **goal is fixed** but the *path* to achieve it is uncertain; priorities shift dynamically. | Tree expanding *downward and outward* from the goal at the top. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic of PDPC — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

The construction of a PDPC follows a disciplined six-step logic:

1. **Identify the Subject / Goal Statement**
   - Define *what* the team is trying to accomplish. This becomes the **terminal node (goal)** of the chart.
   - Example: "Reduce billing cycle time from 12 days to 4 days."

2. **Decompose into Process Steps (Primary Path)**
   - Break the goal into **sequential actionable steps** (S1, S2, S3, …, Sn) drawn as a central spine.
   - Each step should be measurable and time-bound.

3. **Anticipate Potential Problems (Branch Nodes)**
   - For every step, conduct a **brainstorming session** (often using the *5 Whys* technique) to identify *what could go wrong*.
   - These become lateral branches sprouting from the primary spine.

4. **Develop Countermeasures / Contingencies (P-F Branches)**
   - For each anticipated problem (P), develop a countermeasure (C).
   - Countermeasures form a second-generation branch, often labeled with a "P → C" notation.

5. **Quantify Risk Priority (RPN Integration)**
   - Each problem node is rated using:
     - **S** = Severity (1–10)
     - **O** = Occurrence (1–10)
     - **D** = Detection (1–10)
   - Compute:
     $$RPN = S \times O \times D$$
   - Problems with **higher RPN** are addressed *first* and receive the most detailed contingency branches.

6. **Execute, Monitor, and Update (Living Document)**
   - The PDPC is **not a static document**. As the process is executed, real outcomes are fed back, and the chart is **revised dynamically**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PDPC Works in Six Sigma:** It converts *tacit knowledge* (an engineer's mental "what-if" scenarios) into *explicit, shareable, organizational knowledge*. This dramatically reduces **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)** during the Control phase.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Metric | Formula / Definition | Notation | Units / Range | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | S = Severity, O = Occurrence, D = Detection | Dimensionless; Range 1–1000 | **HIGH** — Almost every PDPC question on FMEA integration. |
| **Severity (S)** | Impact of failure on the customer | — | 1 (negligible) – 10 (catastrophic) | Medium |
| **Occurrence (O)** | Likelihood of failure happening | — | 1 (very rare) – 10 (very frequent) | Medium |
| **Detection (D)** | Probability of *not* detecting the failure | — | 1 (highly detectable) – 10 (undetectable) | High |
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | USL/LSL = Spec Limits, σ = Std. Dev. | Unitless; $\geq 1.33$ desired | High (linked when PDPC targets variability) |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities** | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$ | — | Defects per million | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical RPN Threshold (KTU Examiner Insight):** Any problem node with **RPN > 100** is considered a *high-priority failure mode* and **must** be addressed with an explicit PDPC countermeasure branch. RPN values between 50–100 require analysis; values below 50 are generally accepted.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Manufacturing:** Pre-empting machine downtime, raw material rejection, and quality drift in CNC lines.
- **Software Engineering:** Mapping deployment failures, security breach pathways, and rollback contingencies.
- **Healthcare:** Anticipating patient-side complications during a Six Sigma Lean project to reduce ER wait time.
- **Civil Engineering:** Planning concrete pour sequences with weather contingency branches (e.g., rain → use accelerants, cover with tarp).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Worked Numerical Example: PDPC with FMEA Integration (Sequential Type 1)

**Scenario:** A Six Sigma Black Belt team in an electronics manufacturing firm is implementing a project to **reduce solder-defect rate** on a PCB assembly line. The current process step is *"Apply Solder Paste via Stencil Printer"*. The team has identified four potential problems at this step. Using standard FMEA ratings, compute the RPN for each and decide the **branching priority** in the PDPC.

#### Step 1: Tabulate the FMEA Ratings

| Problem Node | Description | S (Severity) | O (Occurrence) | D (Detection) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | Stencil aperture clogged by dried paste | 7 | 6 | 4 |
| P2 | Solder paste viscosity out of spec (too thick) | 9 | 4 | 6 |
| P3 | Misalignment of PCB on the print table | 8 | 5 | 3 |
| P4 | Room humidity > 70% causing paste oxidation | 6 | 3 | 7 |

#### Step 2: Compute the RPN for Each Problem

The fundamental RPN formula is:

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

We will evaluate each problem explicitly with full algebraic transitions.

**For Problem P1 (Stencil Clogging):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{P1} &= S_{P1} \times O_{P1} \times D_{P1} \\
&= 7 \times 6 \times 4 \\
&= 42 \times 4 \\
&= 168
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** RPN = 168 is **above the critical threshold of 100**. P1 is a high-priority failure mode and **must** receive a detailed countermeasure branch in the PDPC.

**For Problem P2 (Viscosity Drift):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{P2} &= 9 \times 4 \times 6 \\
&= 36 \times 6 \\
&= 216
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** RPN = 216 is the **highest** among all four. This is the *most critical* problem and must appear at the **topmost** priority branch of the PDPC.

**For Problem P3 (Misalignment):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{P3} &= 8 \times 5 \times 3 \\
&= 40 \times 3 \\
&= 120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** RPN = 120. Also above the 100 threshold. P3 is high priority and warrants a countermeasure branch.

**For Problem P4 (Humidity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_{P4} &= 6 \times 3 \times 7 \\
&= 18 \times 7 \\
&= 126
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** RPN = 126. High priority; requires mitigation strategy.

#### Step 3: Rank and Assign PDPC Branching Order

| Rank | Problem | RPN | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Top) | P2: Viscosity drift | **216** | Install automatic viscosity controller; hourly QC checks. |
| 2 | P1: Stencil clogging | **168** | Wipe stencil every 25 prints; use anti-drying cover. |
| 3 | P4: Humidity | **126** | Install dehumidifier; maintain RH between 40–55%. |
| 4 | P3: Misalignment | **120** | Add vision-system alignment check; auto-eject mechanism. |

#### Step 4: Analytical Insights for the KTU Examiner

- **Average RPN of the system:**
  $$
  \begin{aligned}
  \overline{RPN} &= \frac{RPN_{P1} + RPN_{P2} + RPN_{P3} + RPN_{P4}}{4} \\
  &= \frac{168 + 216 + 120 + 126}{4} \\
  &= \frac{630}{4} \\
  &= 157.5
  \end{aligned}
  $$

- **Action Conclusion:** Since the average RPN = **157.5 ≫ 100**, the entire process step is in the **"must act"** red zone. The PDPC will contain **4 contingency branches**, but the team must first focus on **P2 (RPN = 216)**.

- **Sigma Level Correlation (Bonus Insight for KTU):** The team can also estimate the process sigma level from DPMO. If current DPMO is 30,000, then:
  $$
  \begin{aligned}
  \text{Sigma Level} &= \text{NORMSINV}\left(1 - \text{DPMO}/10^6\right) + 1.5 \\
  &= \text{NORMSINV}(1 - 0.030) + 1.5 \\
  &\approx \text{NORMSINV}(0.970) + 1.5 \\
  &\approx 1.881 + 1.5 \\
  &\approx 3.38\sigma
  \end{aligned}
  $$
  A 3.38σ process confirms that immediate PDPC-based countermeasures are operationally justified.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When KTU asks to "construct a PDPC based on FMEA data," the model answer **must** include: (1) the central process spine, (2) lateral P-nodes, (3) computed RPN values, (4) ranked priority, and (5) countermeasure branches. Skipping the RPN computation is the most common reason for losing 4–5 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Mermaid Block: PDPC Functional Architecture Flow (Type 2 — Goal-First)

The diagram below represents a **Goal-First (Type 2) PDPC** for the electronics soldering example. The **goal node** sits at the top, the **primary process path** flows downward, and **contingency branches** diverge laterally.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeGoal["GOAL: Reduce Solder Defects to <50 DPMO"]
    
    nodeS1["Step 1: Apply Solder Paste via Stencil Printer"]
    nodeS2["Step 2: Component Placement via Pick-and-Place"]
    nodeS3["Step 3: Reflow Soldering Oven"]
    nodeS4["Step 4: Automated Optical Inspection AOI"]
    
    nodeP2["P2: Viscosity Drift (RPN 216)"]
    nodeP1["P1: Stencil Clogging (RPN 168)"]
    nodeP4["P4: High Humidity (RPN 126)"]
    nodeP3["P3: PCB Misalignment (RPN 120)"]
    
    nodeC2a["C2a: Auto Viscosity Controller"]
    nodeC2b["C2b: Hourly QC Sampling"]
    nodeC1a["C1a: Stencil Wipe Cycle 25 Prints"]
    nodeC1b["C1b: Anti-Drying Paste Cover"]
    nodeC4a["C4a: Install Dehumidifier"]
    nodeC4b["C4b: Maintain RH 40 to 55 percent"]
    nodeC3a["C3a: Vision Alignment System"]
    nodeC3b["C3b: Auto Eject Misaligned Boards"]
    
    nodeFinal["ACHIEVE GOAL: Sigma Level 4.5 or Higher"]

    nodeGoal --> nodeS1
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4
    nodeS4 --> nodeFinal

    nodeS1 --> nodeP2
    nodeP2 --> nodeC2a
    nodeP2 --> nodeC2b

    nodeS1 --> nodeP1
    nodeP1 --> nodeC1a
    nodeP1 --> nodeC1b

    nodeS1 --> nodeP4
    nodeP4 --> nodeC4a
    nodeP4 --> nodeC4b

    nodeS2 --> nodeP3
    nodeP3 --> nodeC3a
    nodeP3 --> nodeC3b
```

### Diagram Interpretation Block (For the Examiner)

| Element | Meaning in the PDPC |
|---|---|
| `nodeGoal` (top) | Terminal goal of the Six Sigma project. |
| `nodeS1 → nodeS4` (central spine) | The *ideal, planned* process sequence. |
| `nodeP1, P2, P3, P4` (lateral branches) | Anticipated problems. Branch **width** is proportional to RPN. |
| `nodeC*` (twigs off P-nodes) | Countermeasures. The team must rank them by ease of implementation vs. effectiveness. |
| `nodeFinal` (bottom) | Achievement of the goal — used as the **terminal control point**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Construct Rule:** In a hand-drawn PDPC, draw the **problem (P) inside a rectangle** and the **countermeasure (C) inside an oval or circle**, with a connecting line. This is the standard convention used in Japanese QC literature and is expected in KTU answer scripts.

### Alternative Schematic: When the Topic Demands a Process Map

For sequential (Type 1) PDPC, the process steps remain the **spine**, and contingencies attach to *each* step rather than originating from the goal. The fundamental visual rule is:

- **Spine nodes** = Process steps (boxes).
- **Lateral nodes** = Failure modes / problems.
- **Sub-lateral nodes** = Countermeasures.
