Conclusion (1 Mark)** Six Sigma is the *culmination* of a century of statistical quality evolution — from Shewhart's control charts → Deming's PDSA → Smith's 6σ concept → Motorola's corporate launch → GE's global popularization. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)** **Given:** $\mu = 50$ mm, $\sigma = 0.8$ mm, Specification limits $= \pm 6\sigma$. **(i) Upper and Lower Specification Limits (2 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} USL &= \mu + 6\sigma = 50 + 6(0.8) = 50 + 4.8 = 54.8 \text{ mm} \\ LSL &= \mu - 6\sigma = 50 - 6(0.8) = 50 - 4.8 = 45.2 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Process Capability Index $C_p$ (2 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{54.8 - 45.2}{6 \times 0.8} = \frac{9.6}{4.8} = 2.0 \end{aligned} $$ A $C_p$ of **2.0** indicates excellent process capability (Six Sigma capable). **(iii) DPMO with 1.5σ Shift (3 Marks):** The effective distance from the shifted mean to the nearest spec limit: $$ \begin{aligned} L_{eff} &= 6 - 1.5 = 4.5\sigma \end{aligned} $$ From the standard normal table, the one-sided tail area at $Z = 4.5$: $$ \begin{aligned} P(Z > 4.5) &= 1 - \Phi(4.5) \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-6} \\ \text{DPMO} &= 3.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6 = \boxed{3.4 \text{ DPMO}} \end{aligned} $$ This confirms the process operates at true Six Sigma quality. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **birth of Six Sigma at Motorola**, highlighting the contributions of Bill Smith and Bob Galvin. Discuss the role of the **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (1988)** in popularizing Six Sigma. (7 Marks) **(b)** Compare the contributions of the following quality pioneers and explain how each one contributed to the eventual emergence of Six Sigma: (i) **Walter Shewhart** (ii) **W. Edwards Deming** (iii) **Genichi Taguchi** (iv) **Joseph Juran** (7 Marks) --- > [!NOTE] > **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Birth of Six Sigma at Motorola (7 Marks)** **1. Background (1 Mark)** In the early 1980s, Motorola was facing severe competition from Japanese firms in the electronics and communications markets. The company was suffering from high field-failure rates despite acceptable factory inspection results. **2. Bill Smith's Contribution (2 Marks)** **Bill Smith**, a senior quality engineer in Motorola's Land Mobile Products Division, recognized that traditional end-of-line quality metrics were insufficient. In 1986, he wrote an internal memo arguing that *true quality must be measured by field reliability*. He proposed that processes be designed to operate at **±6σ** with a **1.5σ long-term shift**, yielding a target of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. **3. Bob Galvin's Corporate Launch (2 Marks)** **Bob Galvin**, then-CEO of Motorola, championed Smith's idea and in **1987** officially launched Six Sigma as a company-wide initiative. He set an aggressive goal of **"10x quality improvement in 5 years"** and tied executive bonuses to quality metrics. Motorola's documented savings exceeded **$2.2 billion** by the mid-1990s. **4. Malcolm Baldrige Award 1988 (2 Marks)** Motorola became the **first organization to win the Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award in 1988** while running a Six Sigma program. This prestigious award provided *external, third-party validation* of Six Sigma's effectiveness, which: - Convinced other U.S. firms that Six Sigma was credible. - Triggered a wave of corporate adoptions, most notably at AlliedSignal (1994) and GE (1995). **Part (b) — Comparative Analysis of Quality Pioneers (7 Marks)** | Pioneer | Key Contribution | Linkage to Six Sigma (Marks) | |---|---|---| | **Walter Shewhart** (1920s, Bell Labs) | Developed the **Statistical Quality Control (SQC) chart**; introduced the concept of *common cause* vs *special cause* variation. | Forms the **statistical backbone** of the Six Sigma **Control** phase. (1.75 marks) | | **W. Edwards Deming** (1950s) | Created the **PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act)** cycle; trained Japanese industry in statistical quality post-WWII. | The PDSA cycle is the **direct precursor to DMAIC**. (1.75 marks) | | **Genichi Taguchi** (1960s) | Developed the **Quality Loss Function** — argued that *any* deviation from target causes quality loss, not just out-of-spec. | Forms the basis of **Robust Design** in Six Sigma's **Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)** methodology. (1.75 marks) | | **Joseph Juran** (1950s) | Defined quality as *"fitness for use"*; developed the **Juran Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Control, Improvement). | Provides the **three-phase structure** mirrored in Six Sigma's **Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control**. (1.75 marks) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Synthesis (Bonus 1 Mark)** > These four pioneers, working across four decades, collectively provided the **statistical, philosophical, design, and managerial foundations** that Bill Smith and Motorola consolidated into the unified **Six Sigma methodology** in 1987. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Six Sigma History Questions** > 1. **Confusing Bill Smith with Jack Welch** — Bill Smith *created* the Six Sigma concept at Motorola (1981–1987); Jack Welch *popularized* it globally at GE (1995). Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this confusion. > 2. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift** — The 3.4 DPMO is **derived** from 4.5σ (not 6σ) due to the 1.5σ long-term mean shift. Students who compute DPMO at 6σ (≈ 2 PPB) will lose full marks. > 3. **Mixing up PDSA and DMAIC** — PDSA = Deming (4 steps). DMAIC = Six Sigma (5 steps). Do not interchange. > 4. **Forgetting the Malcolm Baldrige Award year (1988)** — This is a high-yield fact; examiners specifically test it. > 5. **Ignoring AlliedSignal (1994)** — Between Motorola and GE, AlliedSignal under Larry Bossidy was the *bridge* adopter. Often tested as a 2-mark short note. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — History of Six Sigma** - ✅ Six Sigma's name comes from the **6 standard deviations** between the process mean and specification limit. - ✅ **Bill Smith (Motorola, 1981–1986)** is the **Father of Six Sigma**; he proposed the 3.4 DPMO concept. - ✅ **Bob Galvin (Motorola CEO, 1987)** officially launched Six Sigma as a corporate program. - ✅ Motorola won the **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award in 1988** — first public validation. - ✅ **Jack Welch (GE, 1995)** made Six Sigma a global phenomenon with $1B training investment and $10B+ savings. - ✅ **AlliedSignal (Larry Bossidy, 1994)** was the bridge adopter between Motorola and GE. - ✅ **3.4 DPMO** comes from the **1.5σ long-term shift assumption** — effective tail = **4.5σ**. - ✅ **DMAIC = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control** (5 steps). - ✅ **Six Sigma quality = 3.4 DPMO ≈ 99.99966% accuracy.** - ✅ **Lean Six Sigma (2000s)** = Six Sigma + Lean Manufacturing (waste elimination). - ✅ Modern Six Sigma integrates with **Industry 4.0, AI, and Big Data analytics**. - ✅ Historical foundations: **Shewhart (1920s) → Deming (1950s) → Smith (1981) → Galvin (1987) → Welch (1995)**. > [!NOTE] > **The 5 Names to Memorize for KTU Exams:** > 1. **Walter Shewhart** — Control Charts > 2. **W. Edwards Deming** — PDSA Cycle > 3. **Bill Smith** — Six Sigma Concept > 4. **Bob Galvin** — Corporate Launch > 5. **Jack Welch** — Global Adoption ---

topics.title: Working of Six Sigma
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working of Six Sigma

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Working of Six Sigma** refers to the systematic, data-driven operational framework that organizations deploy to identify and eliminate process defects, minimize variability, and drive continuous improvement toward near-perfect quality. Mathematically, a process operating at a **Six Sigma quality level** produces no more than **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**, which translates to a process yield of **99.99966%**.

The working methodology of Six Sigma is built upon two foundational pillars:

1. **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) — applied for improving existing processes.
2. **DFSS / DMADV** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify) — applied for designing new processes or products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The "Working of Six Sigma" topic encompasses the step-by-step execution of the DMAIC roadmap, the role of statistical tools in each phase, the role-play of project champions, Master Black Belts, Black Belts, and Green Belts, and the integration of Lean concepts with Six Sigma (Lean Six Sigma).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the captain of a large passenger ship navigating through a narrow canal. Your goal is to deliver **every passenger safely** to the destination without anyone falling overboard. Six Sigma is essentially the navigation system that:

- **Measures** how far off-course the ship drifts (Measure).
- **Identifies** the reasons (currents, winds, engine issues) causing drift (Analyze).
- **Implements** corrective actions like adjusting the rudder or speed (Improve).
- **Locks in** the new course using automated controls so the ship never drifts again (Control).

In a manufacturing plant, the "canal" represents the **upper and lower specification limits (USL/LSL)** that a product must fall within. The "ship" is every individual unit produced, and "falling overboard" means producing a defect. The narrower your process variation (standard deviation **σ**), the fewer products fall outside specification — and that is precisely what **working of Six Sigma** mathematically enforces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** The term "Six Sigma" comes from the Greek letter **σ (sigma)** representing **standard deviation**. A Six Sigma process is one whose tolerance limits are **±6σ** away from the process mean.

## 1.3 Standard Six Sigma Quality Levels

| Quality Level | DPMO | Process Yield (%) | Cost of Poor Quality |
|:-------------:|:----:|:-----------------:|:--------------------:|
| 2 Sigma | 308,537 | 69.1% | High |
| 3 Sigma | 66,807 | 93.3% | Moderate |
| 4 Sigma | 6,210 | 99.38% | Low |
| 5 Sigma | 233 | 99.977% | Very Low |
| **6 Sigma** | **3.4** | **99.99966%** | **Near Zero** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DMAIC Roadmap — How Six Sigma Actually Works

The operational backbone of Six Sigma is the **DMAIC** five-phase methodology. Each phase uses specific statistical and project-management tools.

### Phase 1: Define
- **Purpose:** Identify the problem, customers (CTQs — Critical to Quality), and project scope.
- **Tools Used:** Project Charter, Voice of Customer (VoC), SIPOC diagram, COPIS, CTQ Tree.
- **Key Output:** A clearly defined problem statement, project goals, and scope boundary.

### Phase 2: Measure
- **Purpose:** Quantify the current performance baseline and establish data collection plans.
- **Tools Used:** Process Mapping, Data Collection Plan, Gage R&R, Pareto Charts, Control Charts, Capability Analysis.
- **Key Output:** Baseline sigma level, baseline DPMO, validated measurement system.

### Phase 3: Analyze
- **Purpose:** Identify the root causes of defects and variation.
- **Tools Used:** Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone / Ishikawa) Diagrams, 5-Whys Analysis, Regression, Hypothesis Testing, Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA).
- **Key Output:** List of statistically validated root causes (the vital few).

### Phase 4: Improve
- **Purpose:** Develop, pilot, and implement solutions to eliminate root causes.
- **Tools Used:** Design of Experiments (DOE), Lean Tools (5S, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke), Solution Pilot, Cost-Benefit Analysis.
- **Key Output:** Implemented solutions, improved sigma level, validated gains.

### Phase 5: Control
- **Purpose:** Lock in improvements so the gains are sustained over time.
- **Tools Used:** Statistical Process Control (SPC) Charts, Control Plans, Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), Mistake-Proofing, Documentation.
- **Key Output:** Updated process documentation, ongoing monitoring system, project closure.

## 2.2 The DFSS / DMADV Roadmap (Designing for Six Sigma)

When the existing process is so broken that improvement is impractical, a new process is designed using **DFSS**, commonly executed as **DMADV**:

| Phase | Function | Focus |
|:-----:|:--------|:------|
| **D**efine | Identify customer requirements and design goals | Voice of Customer |
| **M**easure | Measure CTQs, capabilities, risks | QFD, Benchmarking |
| **A**nalyze | Analyze options and select design concept | Concept Selection Matrix |
| **D**esign | Design detailed process / product | DOE, FMEA, Simulation |
| **V**erify | Verify design performance via pilot | Pilot Run, Validation Testing |

## 2.3 The Six Sigma Organization (Belt System)

Six Sigma "works" through a structured hierarchy of trained professionals:

- **Executive Leadership:** Sets strategic vision and approves projects.
- **Champions:** Select projects, sponsor Black Belts, remove roadblocks.
- **Master Black Belts:** Train and mentor Black Belts; lead enterprise-wide initiatives.
- **Black Belts:** Lead individual projects; typically 4–6 projects per year.
- **Green Belts:** Support Black Belts; lead smaller projects within their functional area.
- **Yellow Belts:** Participate as team members.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Belt certification is earned through formal training (classroom + project), statistical competency tests, and demonstrated project savings — not by attending a workshop alone.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below uses `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe `|` to preserve markdown table syntax. Use these formulas in your numerical answer sheets.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:-------:|:-----------|:------|
| 1 | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | defects / million |
| 2 | $\text{Yield} = 1 - \dfrac{DPMO}{10^6}$ | First-pass yield | dimensionless (ratio) |
| 3 | $RTY = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times \cdots \times Y_n$ | Rolled Throughput Yield across n stages | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability index | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability index (centered) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $Z_{\text{bench}} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Benchmark Z (short-term sigma) | standard deviations |
| 7 | $Z_{\text{long}} = Z_{\text{bench}} + 1.5$ | Long-term sigma (with 1.5σ shift) | standard deviations |
| 8 | $\sigma = \dfrac{R_{\text{mean}}}{d_2}$ | Process standard deviation from R-chart | variable units |

Where:
- $D$ = Total number of defects observed
- $U$ = Number of units produced
- $O$ = Number of defect opportunities per unit
- $\mu$ = Process mean
- $R_{\text{mean}}$ = Average range from subgroup data
- $d_2$ = Control chart constant (depends on subgroup size $n$)
- **1.5σ shift** = Motorola's empirical allowance for long-term process drift

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The "working of Six Sigma" framework is widely applied in:

- **Manufacturing:** Reducing assembly line defects in automotive and electronics sectors.
- **Software Engineering:** Reducing bug rates in software releases.
- **Healthcare:** Reducing medication errors and patient wait times.
- **Finance:** Reducing transaction processing errors.
- **Service Industry:** Reducing call center call-handling time variability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of DPMO and Sigma Level from Process Data

### Worked Example Problem
A manufacturing cell produces **U = 1,000 units per day**. Inspection reveals **D = 150 defects** across **O = 5 opportunities per unit** over a 5-day observation period.

**Step 1 — Compute Total Units and Total Defects:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Units } U_{\text{total}} &= 1{,}000 \times 5 = 5{,}000 \text{ units} \\[4pt]
\text{Total Opportunities } &= U_{\text{total}} \times O \\[4pt]
&= 5{,}000 \times 5 \\[4pt]
&= 25{,}000 \text{ opportunities}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the DPMO Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPMO &= \dfrac{D_{\text{total}} \times 10^6}{U_{\text{total}} \times O} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{150 \times 10^6}{5{,}000 \times 5} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{150{,}000{,}000}{25{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 6{,}000 \text{ defects per million opportunities}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert DPMO to Process Yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Yield} &= 1 - \dfrac{DPMO}{10^6} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \dfrac{6{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.006 \\[4pt]
&= 0.994 \;=\; 99.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert DPMO to Sigma Level (using Motorola's 1.5σ shift table):**

Using the standard Six Sigma yield table:

| Yield | Sigma Level (with 1.5σ shift) |
|:-----:|:----------------------------:|
| 99.4% | ≈ 3.8 σ |
| 99.73% | 4.0 σ |
| 99.99966% | 6.0 σ |

Therefore, **this process operates at approximately 3.8 sigma level**, meaning it must be improved to reach world-class 6σ quality.

## 3.2 Derivation of Process Capability Index (Cp)

### Worked Example Problem
A CNC lathe produces a shaft diameter with specification limits **USL = 25.05 mm** and **LSL = 24.95 mm**. From 25 sample subgroups of size 5, the average range $R_{\text{mean}} = 0.02$ mm.

**Step 1 — Find d₂ Constant:**
For subgroup size $n = 5$, the standard control chart constant is:

$$
d_2 = 2.326
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Process Standard Deviation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \dfrac{R_{\text{mean}}}{d_2} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.02}{2.326} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.008599 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cp:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{25.05 - 24.95}{6 \times 0.008599} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.10}{0.051594} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.94
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

A $C_p \geq 1.33$ is generally acceptable, and $C_p \geq 1.67$ is preferred. Here $C_p \approx 1.94$, indicating the process spread is well within tolerance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** $C_p$ only measures spread — it does **not** check if the process is centered. $C_{pk}$ addresses centering and is the more complete capability indicator.

## 3.3 Derivation of Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

### Worked Example Problem
A four-stage production process has stage yields:
$Y_1 = 0.99$, $Y_2 = 0.98$, $Y_3 = 0.97$, $Y_4 = 0.96$.

**Step 1 — Identify Each Stage Yield:**

Each $Y_i$ is the first-pass yield at stage $i$.

**Step 2 — Multiply Stage Yields:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTY &= Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4 \\[4pt]
&= 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.97 \times 0.96 \\[4pt]
&= 0.99 \times 0.98 = 0.9702 \\[4pt]
&= 0.9702 \times 0.97 = 0.941094 \\[4pt]
&= 0.941094 \times 0.96 = 0.903450
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final Output:**

$$
RTY = 0.90345 \; \approx \; 90.35\%
$$

This means only about **90.35%** of units pass through all four stages without rework, even though each stage individually appears acceptable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This multiplicative nature of RTY explains why even small per-stage improvements have a compounding effect on overall process quality — a Six Sigma "working" insight that justifies project investment.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Phase | Core Question | Statistical Tool | Project Output |
|:-----:|:--------------|:-----------------|:--------------|
| Define | What is the problem? | SIPOC, VoC | Project Charter |
| Measure | How bad is it now? | Control Charts, MSA | Baseline Sigma |
| Analyze | Why is it bad? | Regression, FMEA | Root Causes |
| Improve | What fixes it? | DOE, Lean Tools | Validated Solutions |
| Control | How do we hold gains? | SPC, SOPs | Sustained Improvements |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DMAIC Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Problem and Customer CTQs] --> B[DEFINE: Project Charter, SIPOC]
    B --> C[MEASURE: Data Collection, MSA, Baseline Sigma]
    C --> D[ANALYZE: Fishbone, FMEA, Hypothesis Tests]
    D --> E[IMPROVE: DOE, Pilot, Solution Implementation]
    E --> F[CONTROL: SPC Charts, SOPs, Control Plan]
    F --> G{Quality Sustained?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Project Closure and Documentation]
    G -- No --> D
    H --> I[Project Handover to Process Owner]
```

## 4.2 Six Sigma Belt Hierarchy (Organizational Working)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Executive Leadership and CEO] --> B[Champions: Sponsor Projects]
    B --> C[Master Black Belts: Train and Mentor]
    C --> D[Black Belts: Lead Projects]
    D --> E[Green Belts: Support Projects]
    E --> F[Yellow Belts: Team Members]
    D --> G[Project Savings to Organization]
    C --> G
    B --> G
    A --> G
```

## 4.3 DMADV / DFSS Flow (For New Process Design)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DEFINE: Customer Needs and Design Goals] --> B[MEASURE: CTQs, Risk, Capability Targets]
    B --> C[ANALYZE: Concept Generation and Selection]
    C --> D[DESIGN: Detailed Design, DOE, FMEA, Simulation]
    D --> E[VERIFY: Pilot Run and Validation Testing]
    E --> F{Quality Validated?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Production Rollout]
    F -- No --> C
```

## 4.4 DMAIC Phase Deliverables (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph phase1[DMAIC Phase Mapping]
        D1[Define Phase] --> D2[Project Charter Deliverable]
        M1[Measure Phase] --> M2[Baseline Sigma Report]
        A1[Analyze Phase] --> A2[Root Cause Document]
        I1[Improve Phase] --> I2[Solution Pilot Results]
        C1[Control Phase] --> C2[Control Plan and SOPs]
    end
    D2 --> M1
    M2 --> A1
    A1 --> I1
    I1 --> C1
    C2 --> OUT[Final Project Closure and Savings Recognition]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define Six Sigma. Briefly explain the DPMO metric used in its measurement.*

**Model Answer:**

Six Sigma is a disciplined, data-driven methodology that seeks to eliminate process defects and minimize variation to a level of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**, corresponding to a yield of **99.99966%**.

**DPMO Formula:**

$$
DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O}
$$

where $D$ = total defects, $U$ = total units, $O$ = opportunities per unit. DPMO provides a normalized quality metric that allows comparison across processes, industries, and product lines. It serves as the baseline in the **Measure** phase and the benchmark in the **Improve** phase.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List the five phases of DMAIC and state the primary objective of the "Improve" phase.*

**Model Answer:**

The five phases of DMAIC are:

1. **D**efine — Identify problem, customer, and project scope.
2. **M**easure — Quantify current performance and baseline sigma.
3. **A**nalyze — Determine root causes of variation and defects.
4. **I**mprove — Develop and implement solutions to eliminate root causes.
5. **C**ontrol — Sustain the gains through monitoring and standardization.

The **primary objective of the Improve phase** is to develop, pilot-test, and implement statistically validated solutions that eliminate the identified root causes, thereby shifting the process from its baseline sigma level toward the target Six Sigma level. Tools used include Design of Experiments (DOE), Poka-Yoke, Kaizen events, and pilot validation runs.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (a) and (b)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Choice A]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Explain in detail the DMAIC methodology. Describe the tools used in each phase and the deliverables produced.*

**Model Answer:**

The DMAIC methodology is the operational roadmap for improving existing processes in a Six Sigma project. Each phase has a defined purpose, tools, and deliverables:

**1. Define Phase:**
- **Purpose:** Clearly identify the problem, customers, CTQs, and project scope.
- **Tools:** Project Charter, SIPOC (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers) Diagram, Voice of Customer (VoC), CTQ Tree.
- **Deliverable:** Approved Project Charter and problem statement.

**2. Measure Phase:**
- **Purpose:** Establish the baseline performance of the process.
- **Tools:** Process Maps, Data Collection Plans, Gage R&R (Measurement System Analysis), Control Charts, Pareto Charts.
- **Deliverable:** Baseline sigma level and validated measurement system.

**3. Analyze Phase:**
- **Purpose:** Identify the vital few root causes of defects and variation.
- **Tools:** Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram, 5-Whys Analysis, Hypothesis Testing (t-test, ANOVA), Regression Analysis, FMEA.
- **Deliverable:** Statistically validated root cause list.

**4. Improve Phase:**
- **Purpose:** Generate, test, and implement corrective solutions.
- **Tools:** Design of Experiments (DOE), Lean Tools (5S, Poka-Yoke, Kaizen), Pilot Studies, Cost-Benefit Analysis.
- **Deliverable:** Improved sigma level with documented gains.

**5. Control Phase:**
- **Purpose:** Lock in improvements and sustain them long term.
- **Tools:** SPC Charts (X-bar, R, p, c), Control Plans, Standard Operating Procedures, Mistake-Proofing.
- **Deliverable:** Updated process documentation and ongoing monitoring system.

The five phases are sequential but iterative — a poorly controlled process may return to the Analyze phase.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*A manufacturing line produces 8,000 units/day over a 7-day study. A total of 280 defects are found, with 4 defect opportunities per unit. Compute the DPMO, the process yield, and identify the approximate sigma level.*

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Units per day = 8,000
- Study duration = 7 days
- Total units $U = 8{,}000 \times 7 = 56{,}000$
- Total defects $D = 280$
- Opportunities per unit $O = 4$

**Step 1 — Compute DPMO:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPMO &= \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{280 \times 10^6}{56{,}000 \times 4} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{280{,}000{,}000}{224{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}250 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Process Yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Yield} &= 1 - \dfrac{DPMO}{10^6} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - \dfrac{1{,}250}{1{,}000{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 1 - 0.00125 \\[4pt]
&= 0.99875 \;=\; 99.875\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify Approximate Sigma Level:**

A yield of 99.875% with the 1.5σ shift corresponds to approximately **4.6 σ** (using Motorola's yield-to-sigma conversion table).

**Conclusion:** The process is operating at roughly 4.6 sigma and needs improvement to reach the Six Sigma target of 3.4 DPMO.

---

### Question B — Option (a) and (b) [Internal Choice Alternative]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4 Choice B]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Describe the DFSS (Design for Six Sigma) methodology with its DMADV phases. How does it differ from DMAIC?*

**Model Answer:**

**DFSS Overview:**
Design for Six Sigma is applied when the existing process is incapable of meeting specifications or when designing a new product/process from scratch. It uses the **DMADV** roadmap.

**DMADV Phases:**

| Phase | Purpose | Key Output |
|:-----:|:--------|:-----------|
| **D**efine | Translate VoC into design goals | Design Plan |
| **M**easure | Define CTQs, risks, capabilities | QFD Matrix, Risk Plan |
| **A**nalyze | Develop and select design concepts | Concept Selection Matrix |
| **D**esign | Detailed design, simulation, DOE | Prototype and Validated Design |
| **V**erify | Pilot test, validate performance | Production-Ready Design |

**Difference between DMAIC and DMADV:**

| Aspect | DMAIC | DMADV (DFSS) |
|:-------|:------|:-------------|
| Application | Improving existing processes | Designing new processes/products |
| Phases | 5 (D-M-A-I-C) | 5 (D-M-A-D-V) |
| Goal | Eliminate defects in existing process | Build quality into the design upfront |
| Trigger | Performance gap identified | New product/process introduction |
| Phase 4 Focus | Improve (solutions) | Design (detailed engineering) |
| Closure | Control (sustain gains) | Verify (validate design) |

**Conclusion:** DMAIC corrects problems in existing processes; DMADV/DFSS prevents problems from being designed into new processes. Together they form the complete "working" framework of Six Sigma.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*A four-stage process has stage yields of 99.5%, 99.0%, 98.5%, and 99.2%. Compute (i) the Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) and (ii) the sigma level of the combined process, assuming 1.5σ shift.*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $Y_1 = 0.995$
- $Y_2 = 0.990$
- $Y_3 = 0.985$
- $Y_4 = 0.992$

**Step 1 — Compute RTY:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTY &= Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4 \\[4pt]
&= 0.995 \times 0.990 \times 0.985 \times 0.992 \\[4pt]
&= 0.995 \times 0.990 = 0.98505 \\[4pt]
&= 0.98505 \times 0.985 = 0.970024 \\[4pt]
&= 0.970024 \times 0.992 = 0.962264
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
RTY \approx 96.23\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Equivalent DPMO:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPMO &= (1 - RTY) \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= (1 - 0.962264) \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= 0.037736 \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= 37{,}736 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify Sigma Level:**

A DPMO of 37,736 corresponds to approximately **3.5 σ** (using Motorola's yield-sigma conversion table with 1.5σ shift).

**Conclusion:** Despite each stage appearing excellent (>98.5% yield), the combined process operates only at ~3.5 sigma. This demonstrates the multiplicative penalty of sequential stages — a fundamental Six Sigma working insight that justifies investing in improvements across all stages, not just one.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **DPMO vs. PPM Confusion:** Students frequently confuse DPMO with PPM. Remember: DPMO uses *opportunities*, while PPM uses *units*. KTU examiners deduct marks if opportunities are ignored.
> 2. **Sigma Shift:** Always state the 1.5σ Motorola shift assumption explicitly when converting yield to sigma level. Without mentioning it, you lose 1 mark on a 7-mark question.
> 3. **DMAIC vs. DMADV Order:** DMAIC ends with **Control**, DMADV ends with **Verify**. Confusing these is a common error worth 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Units in Cp Calculation:** Always convert mm/inch consistently. Forgetting to divide by 6σ (using 3σ instead) is a frequent arithmetic trap.
> 5. **RTY vs. Average Yield:** Do NOT average the stage yields — they must be **multiplied**. This is one of the highest-mark-loss items in Six Sigma questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 99.99966% yield = 6 standard deviations within specification limits.**
- ✅ **Working of Six Sigma** is operationalized through the **DMAIC** (improve existing) and **DMADV / DFSS** (design new) roadmaps.
- ✅ **DMAIC** phases: Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control. Each phase has specific tools and deliverables.
- ✅ **Belt Hierarchy:** Champion → Master Black Belt → Black Belt → Green Belt → Yellow Belt — defines accountability for the working of Six Sigma in organizations.
- ✅ **DPMO Formula:** $DPMO = (D \times 10^6) / (U \times O)$ — always include *Opportunities per unit*.
- ✅ **Yield Formula:** $\text{Yield} = 1 - DPMO/10^6$ — this is first-pass yield, distinct from RTY.
- ✅ **RTY Formula:** $RTY = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \cdots \times Y_n$ — never average stage yields.
- ✅ **Cp Formula:** $C_p = (USL - LSL)/(6\sigma)$ — measures spread only; does not detect off-center processes.
- ✅ **Cpk Formula:** $C_{pk} = \min((USL-\mu)/(3\sigma),(\mu-LSL)/(3\sigma))$ — accounts for process centering.
- ✅ **1.5σ Motorola Shift** is the universally accepted long-term correction when converting yield to sigma level.
- ✅ **Lean Six Sigma Integration:** Combining Lean (waste elimination) with Six Sigma (variation reduction) produces faster, more comprehensive improvements.
- ✅ **Sustainability requires Control:** Without the Control phase, Six Sigma projects lose their gains within months.
- ✅ **DMAIC is iterative:** If control fails, the project loops back to Analyze or Improve.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Capability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Capability** is a statistical measure of the inherent ability of a manufacturing or service process to produce output within specified tolerance limits. It quantifies the **predictable, repeatable, and stable performance** of a process under controlled conditions, expressed as the ratio of the **specification width (Tolerance)** to the **process spread (Natural Variation of 6σ)**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme – Six Sigma (PEMRT752)**, Process Capability is treated as the central evaluation metric in both the **Measure (M)** and **Analyze (A)** phases of the **DMAIC** roadmap. It bridges raw process data with managerial decision-making by translating statistical variation into business-grade KPIs.

The four canonical capability indices studied under this module are:

- **Cp** — Process Capability Index (Potential capability, ignores centering)
- **Cpk** — Process Capability Index (Actual capability, includes centering)
- **Pp** — Process Performance Index (Overall capability, long-term view)
- **Ppk** — Process Performance Index (Overall capability with centering)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Terminology (per KTU Module-4 syllabus):**
> - **USL** – Upper Specification Limit
> - **LSL** – Lower Specification Limit
> - **σ (Sigma)** – Standard Deviation
> - **μ (Mu)** – Process Mean
> - **Tolerance (T)** = USL − LSL

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you are a **fancy coffee shop barista** filling cups of cappuccino. The customer has placed an order:

> *"I want my cup filled between **150 ml (LSL)** and **250 ml (USL)** — not a drop less, not a drop more."*

Now, every morning you pour cups using a machine. Some days you pour **180 ml**, some days **220 ml**, and occasionally **160 ml** or **240 ml**.

- The **range 150–250 ml** is the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** — what the customer demands.
- The **range within which you actually pour** every day is the **Voice of the Process (VOP)** — what your machine naturally produces.

> [!TIP]
> **Process Capability asks ONE simple question:**
> *"Is the Voice of the Process comfortably fitting inside the Voice of the Customer?"*

- If your pouring variation (say 180–220 ml) sits **entirely inside** 150–250 ml → **Capable process** ✅
- If your pouring variation occasionally spills outside (155 ml) → **Incapable process** ❌

The **Process Capability Index (Cp/Cpk)** is simply a **mathematical score** that tells the manager — in a single number — *how comfortably the process fits inside the spec window*. **Higher score = better fit = lower defects = higher sigma quality.**

---

## 1.3 Why Process Capability Matters in Six Sigma

In Six Sigma philosophy, **every process has a sigma level**, and the goal is to reach **6σ (3.4 DPMO)**. Process Capability is the *measurement tool* that determines where a process currently stands on the sigma journey.

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Cpk Equivalent | Yield (%) |
|:-----------:|:----:|:--------------:|:---------:|
| 2σ | 308,537 | 0.67 | 69.1% |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 1.00 | 93.3% |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 1.33 | 99.38% |
| 5σ | 233 | 1.67 | 99.977% |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **2.00** | **99.9997%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Benchmark (KTU Expected Answer):** A **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is generally considered the **minimum acceptable capability** for an existing process. A **Cpk ≥ 1.67** corresponds to a **5σ quality level**. **Cpk ≥ 2.00** is the world-class **6σ benchmark**.

---

## 1.4 The Two-Spec vs One-Spec Distinction

| Specification Type | Description | Index Used | Use Case |
|:------------------:|:-----------:|:----------:|:--------:|
| **Two-Sided Spec** | Both USL and LSL defined | Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk | Shaft diameter, voltage, weight |
| **One-Sided Spec** | Only USL or only LSL defined | Cp (as Cpk) | Strength (≥ value), impurities (≤ value) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often apply Cp blindly to one-sided specifications. For one-sided specs, **Cp = Cpk automatically** because the unbounded side has no "centering" penalty.

---

## 1.5 Short-Term vs Long-Term Capability

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Conceptual Distinction:**
> - **Short-term Capability (Cp, Cpk):** Uses **within-subgroup variation (σ_within)** measured using **R-bar/d₂** or **S-bar/c₄** from control charts. Represents the **best the process can do** when only common-cause variation exists.
> - **Long-term Performance (Pp, Ppk):** Uses **overall variation (σ_overall = standard deviation of all data)**. Represents **actual customer experience**, including drift, shifts, and special-cause variation over time.
> - **Rule of Thumb:** σ_overall ≈ **1.5 × σ_within** (this is the Motorola Six Sigma convention for the 1.5σ shift).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundation: The Normal Distribution Assumption

All capability indices in this module assume the process output follows a **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution**, characterized by:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$$

**Key Property (KTU High-Yield):**

$$P(\mu - 3\sigma \leq x \leq \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 0.9973 \; (99.73\%)$$

This means **6σ of process spread** captures **99.73%** of all output under normal conditions. The capability ratio then compares this 6σ spread against the **Tolerance window** defined by the customer.

---

## 2.2 The Master Capability Formula (Logical Derivation)

Let:
- **T** = Tolerance = USL − LSL
- **Process Spread** = 6σ (six standard deviations)
- **Distance from mean to nearest spec** = min(USL−μ, μ−LSL)

The conceptual logic is:

1. **Numerator:** Customer tolerance window (Voice of Customer) — how wide is the acceptable zone?
2. **Denominator:** Actual process variation (Voice of Process) — how much does the process naturally swing?
3. **Ratio:** How many "process widths" fit inside the spec window?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Logic Rule:** *The higher the ratio, the more room the process has inside the spec, the fewer defects produced.*

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All capability formulas below are KTU-board-exam-verifiable and must be memorized verbatim.**

### Table 2.1: Master Capability Index Formulas

| # | Index | Formula | Mathematical Meaning | When to Use |
|:-:|:-----:|:-------:|:--------------------:|:-----------:|
| 1 | **Cp (Process Capability Index)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Tolerance ÷ 6σ spread | Two-sided spec, centered process |
| 2 | **CPU (Upper Capability)** | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | Distance from μ to USL in σ units | Two-sided spec, evaluate upper side |
| 3 | **CPL (Lower Capability)** | $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$ | Distance from μ to LSL in σ units | Two-sided spec, evaluate lower side |
| 4 | **Cpk (Adjusted Capability Index)** | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ | Minimum of CPU and CPL | Two-sided spec, any centering |
| 5 | **Cpk (One-Sided Spec Equivalent)** | $C_{pk} = C_p = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | Same as Cp | One-sided spec (only USL exists) |
| 6 | **Pp (Process Performance Index)** | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{overall}}$ | Same as Cp but uses σ_overall | Long-term overall capability |
| 7 | **Ppk (Overall Performance)** | $P_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma_{overall}}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma_{overall}}\right)$ | Same as Cpk but uses σ_overall | Long-term actual customer experience |
| 8 | **Sigma Level (Z-score)** | $Z = 3 \times C_{pk}$ | σ distance from μ to nearest spec | Conversion to DPMO/Yield |
| 9 | **Sigma Level (StDev units)** | $\sigma_{level} = 3 C_{pk} + 1.5$ | Includes 1.5σ Motorola shift | Six Sigma convention |
| 10 | **DPMO Conversion** | $DPMO = 1{,}000{,}000 \times (1 - Y_{process})$ | Yield-based metric | Six Sigma reporting |

### Table 2.2: Estimating σ (Sigma) — KTU Practical Rules

| Method | Formula | Best For |
|:------:|:-------:|:--------:|
| **R-bar / d₂** | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | Subgroup size 2–10 |
| **S-bar / c₄** | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{S}}{c_4}$ | Subgroup size > 10 |
| **Pooled Std Dev** | $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{S}}{c_4}$ from control chart | X-bar/R, X-bar/S charts |
| **Direct Sample** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Single large dataset |

### Table 2.3: Cp / Cpk Interpretation Scale (KTU Standard)

| Cp Value | Cpk Value | Process Status | Defect Rate Approx. | Action Required |
|:--------:|:---------:|:--------------:|:-------------------:|:----------------|
| < 1.00 | < 1.00 | **Incapable** | > 27,000 ppm | Major rework or redesign |
| 1.00 – 1.33 | 1.00 – 1.33 | **Marginal** | ~1,000–27,000 ppm | Continuous monitoring |
| 1.33 – 1.50 | 1.33 – 1.50 | **Capable** | ~63–1,000 ppm | Standard production |
| 1.50 – 1.67 | 1.50 – 1.67 | **Excellent** | ~1–63 ppm | Lean operations |
| 1.67 – 2.00 | 1.67 – 2.00 | **Superior** | < 1 ppm | World-class / 5σ |
| ≥ 2.00 | ≥ 2.00 | **Six Sigma** | ≤ 3.4 ppm | **Six Sigma target met** |

---

## 2.4 The Centering Problem — Why Cp and Cpk Differ

This is a **frequently tested KTU concept**. The difference between Cp and Cpk reveals process centering:

- **Cp = Cpk** → Process is **perfectly centered** at the midpoint of specifications.
- **Cp > Cpk** → Process is **off-center**; the Cpk penalty increases with off-center magnitude.
- **Cp < Cpk** → **Impossible mathematically.** This indicates a calculation error in the exam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Centering Rule of Thumb:**
> The **off-center shift** Δ is given by:
> $$\Delta = \dfrac{(USL + LSL)/2 - \mu}{(USL - LSL)/2} \times 100\%$$
> - If |Δ| ≤ 5% → Cp ≈ Cpk (negligible shift)
> - If |Δ| > 25% → Severe off-center condition; Cpk << Cp

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Process | Spec Window | Typical Cpk Target |
|:--------:|:-------:|:-----------:|:------------------:|
| **Automotive** | Engine bore diameter | ± 0.05 mm | ≥ 1.67 |
| **Semiconductor** | Wafer thickness | ± 0.5 μm | ≥ 2.00 |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Tablet weight | ± 5% | ≥ 1.50 |
| **Aerospace** | Turbine blade profile | ± 0.01 mm | ≥ 1.67 |
| **Banking** | Loan processing time | ≤ 24 hours | ≥ 1.33 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Application Note:** Process Capability is not just for manufacturing. In **service industries (Banking, Healthcare, IT)**, the same indices apply where "defects" are redefined as service errors, delays, or compliance failures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cp from First Principles

**Starting Point:** The Normal Distribution property that ±3σ covers 99.73% of output.

**Step 1 — Define Process Spread:**
$$\text{Process Spread} = \mu + 3\sigma - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma$$

**Step 2 — Define Tolerance (Customer Voice):**
$$\text{Tolerance } T = USL - LSL$$

**Step 3 — Form the Ratio:**
The "Capability" is naturally the ratio of the customer-allowed window to the process-natural window:
$$C_p = \frac{\text{Customer Window}}{\text{Process Window}} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $C_p = 1.0$ → Process spread exactly equals spec window (process is barely fitting, ~2700 ppm defects).
- If $C_p = 2.0$ → Process spread is half of spec window (excellent, ~0.002 ppm defects).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cpk from CPU and CPL

**Step 1 — Define one-sided distances from mean to each spec limit:**
$$d_U = USL - \mu \quad \text{and} \quad d_L = \mu - LSL$$

**Step 2 — Convert these distances into σ-units (i.e., Z-scores):**
$$Z_U = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} \quad \text{and} \quad Z_L = \frac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma}$$

**Step 3 — Halve the Z-scores (because ±3σ covers 99.73%, so 3σ is the half-spread):**
$$C_{pu} = \frac{Z_U}{3} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{Z_L}{3} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

**Step 4 — Take the minimum** (the limiting side dictates the worst-case capability):
$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MIN?** The process is only as capable as its worst-performing side. If the upper side is fine but the lower side is failing, defects still occur — so the minimum governs the true capability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard)

### **Problem Statement:**
A CNC machining process produces steel shafts. The specification limits are:
- **USL = 25.10 mm**
- **LSL = 24.90 mm**

From a control chart, the process parameters are:
- **Process Mean (μ) = 25.02 mm**
- **Standard Deviation (σ) = 0.03 mm**

**Required:** Calculate Cp, CPU, CPL, Cpk, and determine the process sigma level.

### **Step 1: Calculate Tolerance (T)**
$$T = USL - LSL = 25.10 - 24.90 = 0.20 \; \text{mm}$$

### **Step 2: Calculate Process Spread**
$$6\sigma = 6 \times 0.03 = 0.18 \; \text{mm}$$

### **Step 3: Calculate Cp**
$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{0.20}{0.18} = 1.111$$

**Interpretation:** The process width occupies **1.111 process-widths** of the spec window — i.e., 90% of the customer tolerance is being used by natural variation.

### **Step 4: Calculate CPU (Upper Capability)**
$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.10 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.03} = \frac{0.08}{0.09} = 0.889$$

### **Step 5: Calculate CPL (Lower Capability)**
$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.02 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.03} = \frac{0.12}{0.09} = 1.333$$

### **Step 6: Calculate Cpk**
$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = \min(0.889, 1.333) = 0.889$$

### **Step 7: Interpret the Result**
- Cp = 1.111 (potential capability — process could be capable if centered)
- Cpk = 0.889 (**actual capability — process is off-center and INCAPABLE**)
- The upper side (CPU = 0.889) is the **limiting side** because the mean (25.02) is shifted **upward**, closer to USL.

### **Step 8: Calculate Sigma Level (Z-score)**
$$Z_{benchmark} = 3 \times C_{pk} = 3 \times 0.889 = 2.667 \; \sigma$$

With the Motorola 1.5σ shift convention:
$$\sigma_{level} = Z_{benchmark} + 1.5 = 2.667 + 1.5 = 4.167 \; \sigma \; (\approx 4.17\sigma)$$

### **Step 9: Estimate DPMO (Using Standard Normal Tables)**
At Z = 2.667, the area under the curve beyond the upper tail:
$$P(\text{defect upper}) = \Phi(-2.667) \approx 0.00383 \;\; (3830 \; \text{ppm})$$
$$P(\text{defect lower}) = \Phi(-4.000) \approx 0.0000317 \;\; (31.7 \; \text{ppm})$$
$$DPMO_{total} = (3830 + 31.7) \times 1000 \approx 3{,}861{,}700 \; \text{... wait, recalibrated}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correct DPMO calculation (in ppm first):**
> $$DPMO = (3830 + 31.7) \times 1000 = 3{,}861{,}700 \text{ defects per million opportunities}$$
> This corresponds to approximately **2.4σ quality**, which is critically low.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flow for Choosing the Right Index

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix in every KTU exam problem to avoid picking the wrong index.**

| Question to Ask | If YES → Use | If NO → Use |
|:----------------|:------------:|:-----------:|
| Is the process stable (in statistical control)? | Cp, Cpk (short-term capability) | First stabilize, then recalculate |
| Are both USL and LSL defined? | Cp / Cpk formulas with min() | CPU or CPL only (Cpk = Cp) |
| Is the data from one large sample (n ≥ 30)? | Use σ_overall = s (sample std dev) | Use σ_within = R̄/d₂ from subgroups |
| Are you reporting customer experience? | Pp, Ppk (long-term performance) | Cp, Cpk (machine potential) |
| Is process mean exactly at mid-spec? | Cp ≈ Cpk (no penalty) | Cp > Cpk (penalty active) |

---

## 3.5 Conversion Formulas (KTU Frequently Asked)

**Yield (%) to DPMO:**
$$DPMO = \frac{1{,}000{,}000 \times (100 - Y\%)}{100}$$

**DPMO to Sigma Level (using Motorola table):**
$$\sigma_{level} = \text{Lookup value from Motorola Sigma Conversion Table}$$

**Cpk to Approximate Yield (Two-sided spec):**
$$Y \approx 2\Phi(3C_{pk}) - 1$$

**Cpk to DPMO (Standard Formula):**
$$DPMO \approx 1{,}000{,}000 \times [2 - 2\Phi(3C_{pk})]$$

---

## 3.6 The 1.5σ Shift — Motorola Six Sigma Convention

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Conceptual Note:**
> Motorola's founders (Smith, 1986) observed that **real processes shift by approximately 1.5σ over the long run** due to drifts in mean, equipment wear, environmental changes, etc. Therefore:
> $$\sigma_{long-term} = \sigma_{short-term} + 1.5$$
> This is **NOT a statistical theorem** but an **engineering convention** adopted to set Six Sigma apart from "Three Sigma Quality."

**Practical Implication:**
- Short-term Cpk = 2.00 → Process is at 6σ in the short term.
- Long-term Ppk (with 1.5σ shift) = 1.50 → Process is at 6σ in the long term (i.e., 3.4 DPMO).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Visual Concept: Cp vs Cpk Centering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Two-Sided Specification] --> B{Is Process Centered?}
    B -- Yes, μ at midpoint --> C[Process Spread fits USL and LSL equally]
    B -- No, μ shifted --> D[Process spread fits one side tighter]
    C --> E["Use Cp = Cpk (Maximum Capability)"]
    D --> F["Use Cpk = MIN of CPU and CPL (Penalty Applied)"]
    E --> G[Defects on Both Sides Equal]
    F --> H[Defects Concentrated on Limiting Side]
    G --> I[Symmetric Defect Distribution]
    H --> J[Skewed Defect Distribution]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Process Capability Evaluation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Phase1[Phase 1: Data Collection]
        S1[Collect Sample Data n>=30] --> S2[Verify Normality - Histogram or Normality Test]
        S2 --> S3{Is Data Normal?}
        S3 -- No --> S4[Apply Box-Cox or Transform]
        S4 --> S2
        S3 -- Yes --> S5[Estimate Sigma - Use Rbar over d2 or Sample Std Dev]
    end

    subgraph Phase2[Phase 2: Index Calculation]
        S5 --> C1[Determine USL and LSL from Customer]
        C1 --> C2[Compute Cp equals USL minus LSL over 6 sigma]
        C1 --> C3[Compute CPU equals USL minus mu over 3 sigma]
        C1 --> C4[Compute CPL equals mu minus LSL over 3 sigma]
        C3 --> C5[Compute Cpk equals MIN of CPU CPL]
        C4 --> C5
    end

    subgraph Phase3[Phase 3: Decision]
        C2 --> D1{Cpk greater than or equal 1.33}
        C5 --> D1
        D1 -- Yes --> D2[Process is Capable - Proceed to Production]
        D1 -- No --> D3[Process is Incapable - Root Cause Analysis Required]
        D3 --> D4[Apply DMAIC Analyze and Improve Phases]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Chart: Cp / Cpk Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Process Output Data]) --> Norm{Normality Check}
    Norm -- Pass --> Sub[Subgroup Data Available?]
    Norm -- Fail --> Trans[Box-Cox Transform]
    Trans --> Norm
    Sub -- Yes --> Within["Compute Sigma within equals Rbar over d2"]
    Sub -- No --> Overall["Compute Sigma overall equals Sample Std Dev"]
    Within --> CpCal[Calculate Cp]
    Overall --> PpCal[Calculate Pp]
    CpCal --> CpkCal[Calculate CPU and CPL]
    CpkCal --> CpkVal[Take MIN for Cpk]
    PpCal --> PpkCal[Calculate Ppk similarly]
    CpkVal --> Interp{Compare with Benchmarks}
    PpkCal --> Interp
    Interp -- Cpk greater than 2.00 --> World[World Class Six Sigma Achieved]
    Interp -- 1.67 to 2.00 --> FiveSigma[Five Sigma Quality]
    Interp -- 1.33 to 1.67 --> Good[Acceptable Capable Process]
    Interp -- Less than 1.33 --> Action[Improvement Action Required]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Cp vs Cpk vs Pp vs Ppk

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Variation[Variation Type]
        V1[Short Term Within Subgroup] --> V2[Long Term Overall Drift Included]
    end
    subgraph Index[Index Family]
        V1 --> CpIndex[Cp - Potential Capability]
        V1 --> CpkIndex[Cpk - Adjusted Capability]
        V2 --> PpIndex[Pp - Performance Index]
        V2 --> PpkIndex[Ppk - Adjusted Performance]
    end
    subgraph Centering[Centering Adjustment]
        CpIndex --> CpIgnore[Ignores Centering]
        CpkIndex --> CpkMin[Uses MIN of CPU CPL]
        PpIndex --> PpIgnore[Ignores Centering]
        PpkIndex --> PpkMin[Uses MIN of Upper Lower]
    end
    subgraph Usage[Engineering Use]
        CpIgnore --> UE1[Machine Potential Benchmark]
        CpkMin --> UE2[Actual Short Term Performance]
        PpIgnore --> UE3[Overall Process Behavior]
        PpkMin --> UE4[Customer Experience Index]
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Concept Map: Relationship Between Six Sigma Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Specification USL LSL] --> B[Process Data mu and sigma]
    B --> C[Capability Index Cp Cpk]
    C --> D[Sigma Level Z equals 3 Cpk]
    D --> E[Six Sigma Level with 1.5 sigma shift]
    E --> F[DPMO Lookup Table]
    F --> G[Yield Percentage]
    G --> H[Cost of Poor Quality COPQ]
    H --> I[Business Decision]
    B --> P[Performance Index Pp Ppk]
    P --> J[Long Term Sigma Level]
    J --> K[Process Stability Indicator]
```

---

## 4.6 Visual Comparison: Capable vs Incapable Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Good[Capable Process Cp Cpk greater than 1.33]
        G1[USL = 10.10] --- G2[LSL = 9.90]
        G3[Mu = 10.00] --- G4[6 Sigma Spread = 0.06]
        G4 --> G5[Fits Comfortably Inside Spec]
    end
    subgraph Bad[Incapable Process Cp Cpk less than 1.00]
        B1[USL = 10.10] --- B2[LSL = 9.90]
        B3[Mu = 10.05] --- B4[6 Sigma Spread = 0.30]
        B4 --> B5[Spills Outside Spec Producing Defects]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define Process Capability Index (Cp). State the formula and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Process Capability Index (Cp) is a statistical measure of the ability of a process to produce output within the customer-defined specification limits, assuming the process is **perfectly centered** at the midpoint of the specifications.

> **Formula:**
> $$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

> **Significance:**
> 1. It quantifies how many "process widths" (6σ spreads) fit inside the customer tolerance window.
> 2. **Cp = 1.0** → Process barely fits (≈ 2700 ppm defects).
> 3. **Cp ≥ 1.33** → Industry-standard minimum for a capable process.
> 4. **Cp ≥ 1.67** → 5σ quality, world-class performance.
> 5. **Cp does NOT account for centering** — a process can have high Cp but still produce defects if off-center. Hence, **Cpk** is the more reliable metric.

**(Marks Distribution: 1M definition, 1M formula, 1M significance explanation.)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Cp and Cpk. Why is Cpk considered a more realistic capability measure?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Cp | Cpk |
|:------:|:--:|:---:|
| Formula | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ |
| Accounts for Centering? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Reflects Actual Customer Experience? | Partial — only if process is centered | ✅ Fully |
| Penalty for Off-centering? | No | Yes — reduces value |

> **Why Cpk is more realistic:** In real industrial processes, the process mean rarely sits exactly at the midpoint of specifications. Cp ignores this reality and overestimates capability. Cpk penalizes the index based on how far the mean drifts toward either spec limit, thereby reflecting the **true defect-producing potential** of the process. **Hence, Cpk is the index used for production-release decisions.**

**(Marks Distribution: 1.5M comparison table, 1.5M explanation.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper, Module 4]**

A precision bearing manufacturing process has the following characteristics:

- **USL = 12.020 mm**
- **LSL = 11.980 mm**
- **Sample Size (n) = 50**
- **Sample Mean ($\bar{x}$) = 11.995 mm**
- **Sample Standard Deviation (s) = 0.008 mm**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Calculate the process capability indices Cp, CPU, CPL, and Cpk. Comment on whether the process is capable.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the Parameters**
$$USL = 12.020, \quad LSL = 11.980$$
$$\mu \approx \bar{x} = 11.995, \quad \sigma \approx s = 0.008$$

**Step 2: Calculate Tolerance**
$$T = USL - LSL = 12.020 - 11.980 = 0.040 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 3: Calculate 6σ (Process Spread)**
$$6\sigma = 6 \times 0.008 = 0.048 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Cp**
$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{0.040}{0.048} = 0.833$$

**Step 5: Calculate CPU**
$$C_{pu} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{12.020 - 11.995}{3 \times 0.008} = \frac{0.025}{0.024} = 1.042$$

**Step 6: Calculate CPL**
$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{11.995 - 11.980}{3 \times 0.008} = \frac{0.015}{0.024} = 0.625$$

**Step 7: Calculate Cpk**
$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = \min(1.042, 0.625) = 0.625$$

**Step 8: Interpretation**
- Cp = 0.833 < 1.00 → **Process spread exceeds spec window; the process is not even potentially capable.**
- Cpk = 0.625 → **The process is severely off-center; lower side is the limiting side (CPL < CPU).**
- **Verdict:** ❌ **Process is INCAPABLE.** Immediate corrective action is required:
  - Reduce variation (σ) through Six Sigma tools (DOE, control charts).
  - Re-center the process mean at 12.000 mm.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**If the process mean is re-centered to the mid-specification value of 12.000 mm (keeping σ unchanged), recalculate Cpk and compute the corresponding sigma level and DPMO.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: New Parameters**
$$\mu_{new} = 12.000, \quad \sigma = 0.008 \; (\text{unchanged})$$

**Step 2: Recalculate CPU and CPL**
$$C_{pu}^{new} = \frac{12.020 - 12.000}{0.024} = \frac{0.020}{0.024} = 0.833$$
$$C_{pl}^{new} = \frac{12.000 - 11.980}{0.024} = \frac{0.020}{0.024} = 0.833$$

**Step 3: New Cpk**
$$C_{pk}^{new} = \min(0.833, 0.833) = 0.833 = C_p$$

**Step 4: Compute Sigma Level (Short-term Z)**
$$Z = 3 \times C_{pk}^{new} = 3 \times 0.833 = 2.500 \; \sigma$$

**Step 5: Apply Motorola 1.5σ Shift**
$$\sigma_{level} = Z + 1.5 = 2.500 + 1.5 = 4.000 \; \sigma \; (\text{i.e., 4σ process})$$

**Step 6: Estimate DPMO (Using Standard Normal Tables)**
At Z = 2.500, area beyond upper tail = Φ(−2.500) ≈ 0.00621 = 6210 ppm.
By symmetry (since process is now centered), defects occur on both sides equally.
$$\text{Total ppm} = 2 \times 6210 = 12{,}420 \; \text{ppm}$$
$$DPMO = 12{,}420 \times 1000 = 12{,}420{,}000 \; \text{(per million opportunities)}$$

Wait — rechecking: **1 ppm = 1 defect per million opportunities.** So:
$$\text{DPMO} = 12{,}420 \text{ defects per million opportunities}$$

**Step 7: Conclusion**
- Even after re-centering, **Cpk = 0.833 < 1.00** → Process is still incapable because the **inherent variation (σ) is too large**.
- To achieve Cpk ≥ 1.33, σ must be reduced to:
$$\sigma_{required} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6 \times 1.33} = \frac{0.040}{7.98} = 0.00501 \; \text{mm}$$

This represents a **~37% reduction in σ** — achievable through **variation reduction techniques** (Kaizen, DMAIC, DOE).

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper, Module 4]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the significance of the 1.5σ shift convention in Six Sigma. How does it relate to Process Capability?**

**Model Answer:**

The **1.5σ shift convention** was introduced by **Motorola engineer Bill Smith in 1986** to account for the **long-term drift** of process means that occurs in real-world manufacturing environments.

**Significance:**
1. **Empirical Observation:** Smith found that over long periods, process means drift by approximately **1.5σ** due to:
   - Gradual tool wear
   - Environmental changes (temperature, humidity)
   - Material batch variations
   - Operator shifts and skill differences
2. **Conservative Benchmark:** It ensures that a "Six Sigma" process actually delivers world-class performance over time, not just instantaneously.
3. **Bridge between Cp and Pp:** The 1.5σ shift connects short-term capability (Cp/Cpk) with long-term performance (Pp/Ppk).

**Mathematical Relationship:**
$$\sigma_{long-term} = \sigma_{short-term} + 1.5$$

**Effect on Capability:**
- A short-term Cpk = 2.00 → 6σ in short-term benchmark.
- After 1.5σ drift → long-term Z = 6 − 1.5 = 4.5σ, which corresponds to **3.4 DPMO** (the famous Six Sigma benchmark).
- Without the 1.5σ shift, "6σ" would be defined at Z = 6, giving essentially zero defects — an **unrealistic goal**.

**Conclusion:** The 1.5σ convention makes Six Sigma a **practical, achievable, and conservative** quality target, ensuring that certified Six Sigma processes truly deliver < 3.4 DPMO over their operational lifetime.

**(Marks Distribution: 2M empirical basis, 2M mathematical form, 2M relationship to capability, 1M conclusion.)**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A pharmaceutical tablet-filling process has: USL = 510 mg, LSL = 490 mg, μ = 501 mg, σ = 2.0 mg. Calculate Cp, Cpk, the sigma level, and the expected DPMO. Comment on the suitability for pharmaceutical production.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate Cp**
$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{510 - 490}{6 \times 2.0} = \frac{20}{12} = 1.667$$

**Step 2: Calculate CPU and CPL**
$$C_{pu} = \frac{510 - 501}{3 \times 2.0} = \frac{9}{6} = 1.500$$
$$C_{pl} = \frac{501 - 490}{3 \times 2.0} = \frac{11}{6} = 1.833$$

**Step 3: Calculate Cpk**
$$C_{pk} = \min(1.500, 1.833) = 1.500$$

**Step 4: Interpret**
- Cp = 1.667 → Process is **potentially capable** (5σ potential).
- Cpk = 1.500 → Process is **actually capable but off-center** (upper side is limiting; process mean shifted upward toward USL).

**Step 5: Sigma Level (Short-term Z)**
$$Z = 3 \times C_{pk} = 3 \times 1.500 = 4.500 \; \sigma$$

**Step 6: Six Sigma Level (with 1.5σ shift)**
$$\sigma_{level} = 4.500 + 1.5 = 6.000 \; \sigma \; \text{(Six Sigma achieved!)}$$

**Step 7: DPMO (Using Standard Normal Table)**
At Z = 4.500, Φ(−4.500) ≈ 0.0000034 = 3.4 ppm (one-sided).
By symmetry, total ppm ≈ 2 × 3.4 = 6.8 ppm.
$$DPMO \approx 6{,}800 \text{ defects per million opportunities}$$

**Step 8: Pharmaceutical Suitability**
- **Pharma Industry Standard:** Cpk ≥ 1.50 is generally acceptable; Cpk ≥ 1.67 is preferred.
- **Current Status:** Cpk = 1.500 → **Marginally acceptable** but ideally should be improved.
- **Recommendation:**
  - **Re-center the process** to μ = 500 mg (mid-spec) → Cpk becomes 1.667.
  - **Reduce σ** from 2.0 to 1.5 mg → Cpk becomes 2.0 (world-class).
- **Conclusion:** The process is suitable for **non-critical drugs** in its current state but should be improved for **critical-dose medications** where every milligram matters.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Zones (Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Forgetting the MIN():** Students often report only CPU or only CPL when asked for Cpk, losing 2 marks. **Always write both** and then take the minimum.
> 2. **Confusing σ_within and σ_overall:** Cp/Cpk uses σ_within (R-bar/d₂); Pp/Ppk uses σ_overall (sample std dev). Mixing them up costs 3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift in Sigma Level calculation:** The question may ask for "Six Sigma Level" specifically — that requires the 1.5σ adjustment. Without it, you'll understate the benchmark.
> 4. **Unit Mismatch:** If USL and σ have different units, the formula yields wrong results. Always verify units match before substitution.
> 5. **One-Sided Spec Trap:** If only USL is given (no LSL), then **Cp = Cpk = CPU**. Writing LSL = 0 is a common but incorrect assumption.
> 6. **Negativity Check:** Cp and Cpk cannot be negative. If your calculation yields a negative value, you likely swapped USL−μ with μ−LSL. Check direction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print this for exam day!)**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- **Cp** = (USL − LSL) / 6σ → Potential capability (ignores centering)
- **Cpk** = MIN(CPU, CPL) → Actual capability (includes centering penalty)
- **Pp** = (USL − LSL) / 6σ_overall → Long-term performance (no centering)
- **Ppk** = MIN(PPU, PPL) → Long-term actual performance (with centering)

### ✅ Key Formulas to Memorize
- $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$
- $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$
- $Z_{benchmark} = 3 \times C_{pk}$
- $\sigma_{level} = 3C_{pk} + 1.5$ (Motorola convention)
- $DPMO = 1{,}000{,}000 \times (1 - Y_{process})$

### ✅ Benchmark Values (Industry Standard)
- Cpk < 1.00 → **Incapable** (process not fit for production)
- 1.00 ≤ Cpk < 1.33 → **Marginal** (continuous monitoring)
- 1.33 ≤ Cpk < 1.67 → **Capable** (acceptable for most industries)
- 1.67 ≤ Cpk < 2.00 → **Excellent** (5σ quality)
- Cpk ≥ 2.00 → **Six Sigma / World-Class**

### ✅ Critical Distinctions
- **Cp vs Cpk:** Cp = potential; Cpk = actual (with centering).
- **Short-term vs Long-term:** Cp/Cpk uses σ_within; Pp/Ppk uses σ_overall.
- **Cp = Cpk** → Process perfectly centered.
- **Cp > Cpk** → Process off-center (always this direction; never the reverse).
- **1.5σ shift** → Motorola convention for long-term drift.

### ✅ Practical Decision Triggers
- If **Cpk < 1.00** → Process produces > 27,000 ppm defects → STOP, redesign.
- If **Cpk in [1.00, 1.33]** → Tight monitoring, Six Sigma project to improve.
- If **Cpk ≥ 1.67** → Process is mature; focus on cost reduction, not capability improvement.
- If **Cp high but Cpk low** → Off-centering is the issue; re-center the mean first (cheapest fix).
- If **Cp low AND Cpk low** → Variation is too large; need σ reduction (DOE, Kaizen).

### ✅ Mnemonics for Exam
- **"Cp sees the window, Cpk sees the placement of the curtain."**
- **"CPU = how far from USL; CPL = how far from LSL; Cpk = the smaller gap."**
- **"Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 2.00 Cpk = 6σ with 1.5σ shift."**

### ✅ Formula Selection Quick-Guide
- One-sided spec → Use CPU or CPL only.
- Two-sided spec, centered → Cp = Cpk.
- Two-sided spec, off-center → Cpk = MIN(CPU, CPL).
- Long-term reporting → Use Pp, Ppk instead of Cp, Cpk.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability - Meaning, Significance and Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Capability - Meaning, Significance and Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Capability (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> *Process Capability* is a **statistical measure of the inherent ability of a manufacturing or service process to produce output that conforms to the engineering specification limits (USL and LSL)**. It quantifies the *fitness-for-use* of a stable process by comparing the *natural spread of the process* (typically $\pm 3\sigma$ from the mean) against the *tolerance band* allowed by the customer or design.

In Six Sigma terminology, a process is considered **capable** when its total process variation ($6\sigma$ spread) is comfortably smaller than the design tolerance window (USL $-$ LSL). The KTU syllabus frames this as the *baseline eligibility test* of a process before any DMAIC improvement project can deliver real financial benefits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Standardised by KTU:**
> - **USL** = Upper Specification Limit (engineering maximum)
> - **LSL** = Lower Specification Limit (engineering minimum)
> - **T** = Tolerance = USL $-$ LSL
> - **$\mu$** = Process Mean (centre of the natural distribution)
> - **$\sigma$** = Process Standard Deviation (measure of inherent spread)
> - **Voice of Customer (VOC)** drives specification limits; **Voice of Process (VOP)** drives capability.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **newspaper delivery boy on a bicycle** who must throw rolled-up papers onto porches.

- The **porch width** = *Specification Width* (USL $-$ LSL). It is what the customer (homeowner) accepts.
- The **scatter of where the papers actually land** = *Process Variation* ($6\sigma$).
- If the scatter (papers flying all over the road) is much smaller than the porch width, the boy is *capable*. If the scatter is wider, half the papers land on the lawn or street — *defects*.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Quick Recall — "SPC":**
> **S**pecification limits are fixed by **engineering / customer**.
> **P**rocess limits are determined by **statistical reality** (the $\pm 3\sigma$ bell curve).
> **C**apability = how much "slack" exists between these two worlds.

In Six Sigma, we engineer a process so that **the process variation is so small that even after the mean shifts by $\pm 1.5\sigma$**, the defects per million opportunities (DPMO) fall to **3.4** — the heart of the Six Sigma philosophy.

### 1.3 Standard Capability Metrics (Quick Reference)

| Metric | Symbol | What It Measures | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process Capability Index | $C_p$ | Potential capability (assumes centred process) | Higher is better |
| Process Capability Index (actual) | $C_{pk}$ | Real capability accounting for centring | Higher is better |
| Process Performance Index | $P_p$ | Long-term overall capability | Higher is better |
| Process Performance Index (actual) | $P_{pk}$ | Long-term capability with centring | Higher is better |
| Process Capability Ratio (Taguchi) | $C_{pm}$ | Capability targeting a nominal value | Higher is better |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse $\sigma_{within}$ with $\sigma_{overall}$.** $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ use the *within-subgroup* standard deviation (short-term, $\sigma_{st}$), whereas $P_p$ and $P_{pk}$ use the *overall* standard deviation (long-term, $\sigma_{lt}$). Mixing them up is a guaranteed KTU mark-deduction trap.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Chain Behind Process Capability

Process capability is **not a single number** — it is a structured evaluation built on five logical steps. Mastering this chain is the single highest-yield investment for KTU Part B questions.

1. **Step 1 — Establish Statistical Control**
   Before measuring capability, the process *must* be in statistical control. Use **X-bar and R / X-bar and S control charts**. If points fall outside control limits or exhibit non-random patterns, capability analysis is *invalid*. First *stabilise*, then *capabilise*.

2. **Step 2 — Verify Normality**
   Process capability indices assume the *output variable is normally distributed*. Verify using:
   - **Histogram visual inspection** (bell-shape)
   - **Normal Probability Plot** (straight-line test)
   - **Anderson-Darling / Shapiro-Wilk formal tests**

3. **Step 3 — Estimate $\mu$ and $\sigma$**
   - $\mu$ = average of all sample measurements.
   - **$\sigma_{within}$** = $\bar{R}/d_2$ (using subgroup ranges) **or** $\bar{S}/c_4$ (using subgroup standard deviations).
   - **$\sigma_{overall}$** = standard deviation of *all* individual observations pooled together (use $s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$).

4. **Step 4 — Compute the Index**
   Plug the estimated $\mu$ and the correct $\sigma$ into the appropriate formula ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, $P_{pk}$).

5. **Step 5 — Interpret Against Standards**
   Benchmark against the industry-accepted minimums. The KTU-mandated benchmarks are summarised later in this section.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All variables and boundary conditions are listed below. Memorise the boxed relationships — they appear in nearly every Part B question.

| # | Formula | Variable Meaning | Typical Units / Range | Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{within}}$ | Potential capability (centred process) | dimensionless; $C_p \geq 1$ acceptable | Requires $\mu$ at the midpoint of specs |
| 2 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma_{within}}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma_{within}}\right)$ | Actual capability including centring | dimensionless; $C_{pk} = C_p$ when centred | Uses $\sigma_{within}$ (short-term) |
| 3 | $C_{pk} = C_p \,(1 - k)$ where $k = \dfrac{\vert \mu - M \vert}{T/2}$ | Centring factor $k$ in $[0,1]$ | dimensionless | $M = (USL+LSL)/2$, $T = USL-LSL$ |
| 4 | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{overall}}$ | Long-term overall performance | dimensionless | Uses $\sigma_{overall}$ (long-term) |
| 5 | $P_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma_{overall}}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma_{overall}}\right)$ | Long-term actual performance | dimensionless | $P_{pk} \leq C_{pk}$ in stable processes |
| 6 | $C_{pm} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T_0)^2}}$ | Taguchi's loss-based capability | dimensionless | $T_0$ = target nominal value |
| 7 | $\text{Process Spread} = 6\sigma$ | Natural process tolerance | same as measured variable | Captures $\approx 99.73\%$ of output |
| 8 | $Z_{bench} = \min(Z_{USL}, Z_{LSL})$ | Sigma level in defects/million | dimensionless | $Z = (SL - \mu)/\sigma$ |
| 9 | $\text{DPMO} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times P(\text{defect})$ | Defects per million opportunities | integer count | DPMO $= 3.4$ at $4.5\sigma$ short-term |
| 10 | $Z_{lt} = Z_{st} + 1.5$ | Long-term sigma with $1.5\sigma$ shift | dimensionless | Standard Six Sigma convention |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $1.5\sigma$ Shift Rule (Six Sigma Philosophy):** Motorola empirically observed that real processes drift by approximately $1.5\sigma$ over the long term. A *short-term* $Z$ of $6\sigma$ therefore yields a *long-term* $Z$ of $4.5\sigma$ (with $3.4$ DPMO). This is the numerical foundation of the "Six Sigma" brand.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing (Mechanical/Civil):** Verifying that a CNC-machined shaft diameter stays within ISO 286 tolerance grades.
- **Electronics (ECE/EEE):** Ensuring the resistance of a printed resistor falls inside the $\pm 5\%$ band.
- **Software/Services:** Monitoring call-centre average handling time (AHT) against an upper SLA.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Validating tablet weight uniformity under USFDA process validation guidelines.
- **Automotive (IATF 16949):** Mandatory $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ for *Key* characteristics, $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for *Significant* characteristics — a frequent KTU viva question.

### 2.4 KTU Industry Benchmark Table (High-Yield for Theory)

| $C_{pk}$ / $P_{pk}$ Value | Process Condition | DPMO (approx., centred) | Typical KTU Inference |
|---|---|---|---|
| $< 1.00$ | **Incapable** — needs fundamental redesign | $> 66{,}807$ | Process must be re-engineered |
| $1.00 - 1.33$ | **Marginal** — monitor continuously | $6{,}210 - 66{,}807$ | Marginal; capable but not Six Sigma |
| $1.33 - 1.67$ | **Capable** — industry standard minimum | $233 - 6{,}210$ | Acceptable for most industries |
| $1.67 - 2.00$ | **Highly Capable** | $3.4 - 233$ | Six Sigma territory begins |
| $\geq 2.00$ | **World-Class / Six Sigma** | $< 3.4$ | True $6\sigma$ process |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the $C_p$ Formula

The *potential* capability of a centred, normal process compares the **engineering tolerance band** to the **natural process spread** ($6\sigma$, covering $99.73\%$ of output).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Engineering Tolerance} &= USL - LSL \\
\text{Natural Process Spread} &= \mu + 3\sigma - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma \\
\text{Define Capability Index } C_p &= \frac{\text{Engineering Tolerance}}{\text{Natural Process Spread}} \\
\therefore \; C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical commentary:** $C_p$ is a *dimensionless ratio*. If $C_p = 1$, the process *just barely* fits inside the spec window. If $C_p = 2$, the process is using only half the tolerance — comfortably capable. Six Sigma targeting demands $C_p \geq 2.0$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the $C_{pk}$ Formula (One-Sided Capability)

For a non-centred process, the spread may be acceptable, but the *position* may be wrong. We need to check **each side independently** and take the *worst case* — the "min" operator — because the side closer to its specification limit fails first.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance from mean to USL} &= USL - \mu \\
\text{Distance from mean to LSL} &= \mu - LSL \\
\text{One-sided capability (upper)} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} \\
\text{One-sided capability (lower)} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \\
\therefore \; C_{pk} &= \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical commentary:** The minimum operator guarantees the *worst-performing side* drives the index. A $C_{pk}$ close to $C_p$ means the process is well-centred; a $C_{pk}$ far below $C_p$ indicates severe centring drift — *Centring is a management problem; spread is an engineering problem.*

### 3.3 Derivation of the Centring Factor $k$

The ratio $k$ measures how far the process mean is from the *target* $M$ (the midpoint of specifications), normalised by half the tolerance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \frac{USL + LSL}{2} \\
\text{Deviation from centre} &= \vert \mu - M \vert \\
\text{Half-tolerance} &= \frac{T}{2} = \frac{USL - LSL}{2} \\
k &= \frac{\vert \mu - M \vert}{T/2} \\
C_{pk} &= C_p (1 - k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical commentary:** $k = 0$ implies $\mu = M$ (perfectly centred), so $C_{pk} = C_p$. $k = 1$ implies $\mu$ is touching a spec limit, so $C_{pk} = 0$ (incapable regardless of spread).

### 3.4 Worked Example 1 — $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ Computation

> **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A CNC milling process produces shafts. The USL is $25.20$ mm and LSL is $24.80$ mm. A sample of $n = 50$ shafts gives $\bar{x} = 24.92$ mm and a within-subgroup standard deviation of $\sigma_{within} = 0.06$ mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, the centring factor $k$, and interpret the result.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= USL - LSL = 25.20 - 24.80 = 0.40 \text{ mm} \\
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{within}} = \frac{0.40}{6 \times 0.06} = \frac{0.40}{0.36} = 1.111 \\
C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.20 - 24.92}{3 \times 0.06} = \frac{0.28}{0.18} = 1.556 \\
C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{24.92 - 24.80}{3 \times 0.06} = \frac{0.12}{0.18} = 0.667 \\
C_{pk} &= \min(1.556,\; 0.667) = 0.667 \\
M &= \frac{25.20 + 24.80}{2} = 25.00 \text{ mm} \\
k &= \frac{\vert \mu - M \vert}{T/2} = \frac{\vert 24.92 - 25.00 \vert}{0.20} = \frac{0.08}{0.20} = 0.40 \\
C_{pk} \text{ (verify)} &= C_p (1 - k) = 1.111 \times (1 - 0.40) = 1.111 \times 0.60 = 0.667 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation (Model Answer Phrasing for KTU):**
> $C_p = 1.111$ indicates the *spread* is acceptable, but $C_{pk} = 0.667 < 1.00$ reveals the *process is off-centre towards the LSL*. The process is **incapable**. Recommendation: **re-centre the machine** (set-up adjustment) — the spread is fine, the mean has drifted. This is a *Centring Problem* with $k = 0.40$.
