### 3.5 Worked Example 2 — $P_p$ vs $P_{pk}$ and Long-Term Sigma

> **Problem Statement:** For a different process, the same $n = 50$ measurements yield the *overall* standard deviation $\sigma_{overall} = 0.09$ mm (note the larger value — it captures long-term drift). Specifications are $USL = 25.20$ mm, $LSL = 24.80$ mm, and the same $\bar{x} = 24.92$ mm. Compute $P_p$, $P_{pk}$, the *long-term* $Z$-score, and the estimated DPMO.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{overall}} = \frac{0.40}{6 \times 0.09} = \frac{0.40}{0.54} = 0.741 \\
P_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \frac{25.20 - 24.92}{3 \times 0.09} = \frac{0.28}{0.27} = 1.037 \\
P_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \frac{24.92 - 24.80}{3 \times 0.09} = \frac{0.12}{0.27} = 0.444 \\
P_{pk} &= \min(1.037,\; 0.444) = 0.444 \\
Z_{USL} &= \frac{25.20 - 24.92}{0.09} = \frac{0.28}{0.09} = 3.111 \\
Z_{LSL} &= \frac{24.92 - 24.80}{0.09} = \frac{0.12}{0.09} = 1.333 \\
Z_{bench} &= \min(3.111,\; 1.333) = 1.333 \\
Z_{lt} &= Z_{bench} + 1.5 = 1.333 + 1.5 = 2.833\;\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

For $Z_{bench} = 1.333$, the corresponding short-term DPMO is $\approx 45{,}500$ (from standard normal tables), so the long-term DPMO $\approx 137{,}000$ — a **$2.8\sigma$ long-term process**, not Six Sigma.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparative Insight:** Notice $P_p = 0.741 < C_p = 1.111$ from the previous example. The *overall* standard deviation is larger because it absorbs between-subgroup and time-based variation. A healthy process should satisfy $C_p \geq P_p$ (the short-term spread is a *lower bound* on the long-term spread). If $C_p < P_p$, suspect **sampling or subgrouping error**.

### 3.6 Conversion Table — $C_{pk}$ to PPM (Memorise These)

| $C_{pk}$ | Short-Term $\sigma$ (no shift) | PPM out of spec (centred) | Long-Term $Z$ with $1.5\sigma$ shift |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0.67$ | $2.0\sigma$ | $45{,}500$ | $0.5\sigma$ ($\sim 308{,}000$ DPMO) |
| $1.00$ | $3.0\sigma$ | $2{,}700$ | $1.5\sigma$ ($\sim 66{,}807$ DPMO) |
| $1.33$ | $4.0\sigma$ | $63$ | $2.5\sigma$ ($\sim 6{,}210$ DPMO) |
| $1.50$ | $4.5\sigma$ | $7$ | $3.0\sigma$ ($\sim 1{,}350$ DPMO) |
| $1.67$ | $5.0\sigma$ | $0.57$ | $3.5\sigma$ ($\sim 233$ DPMO) |
| $2.00$ | $6.0\sigma$ | $0.002$ | $4.5\sigma$ ($\sim 3.4$ DPMO) — *Six Sigma* |

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — $C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$ Diagnostic Scenarios

| Scenario | $C_p$ | $C_{pk}$ | $k$ | Diagnostic Conclusion | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Centred, capable | $1.67$ | $1.67$ | $0$ | Excellent; well-centred and low spread | Maintain; control with SPC |
| Off-centre but capable | $1.67$ | $0.83$ | $0.5$ | Spread fine; centring is the issue | Re-centre; minimal cost fix |
| Centred but wide | $0.83$ | $0.83$ | $0$ | Centred but spread is too large | Reduce variation; engineering redesign |
| Off-centre and wide | $0.67$ | $0.34$ | $0.5$ | Critical — multiple failures | Full Six Sigma DMAIC project |
| Six Sigma elite | $2.00$ | $2.00$ | $0$ | World-class | Document best practices; audit |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Capability Analysis — End-to-End Workflow

The Mermaid block below maps the *complete* process capability assessment procedure as it is expected to be drawn in KTU Part B 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define CTQ Critical to Quality] --> B[Collect Minimum 25 to 30 Subgroups of Size 4 to 5]
    B --> C{Is Process in Statistical Control}
    C -- No --> D[Stabilise the Process Using X bar R or X bar S Chart]
    D --> C
    C -- Yes --> E[Verify Normality Using Histogram and Probability Plot]
    E --> F{Normal Distribution Confirmed}
    F -- No --> G[Apply Box Cox Transformation or Use Non Parametric Method]
    G --> E
    F -- Yes --> H[Compute Process Mean Mu and Within Subgroup Sigma]
    H --> I[Compute Cp Using USL Minus LSL Divided by 6 Sigma]
    H --> J[Compute Cpk Using Minimum of Two One Sided Values]
    I --> K{Compare Cp with Cpk}
    J --> K
    K -- Cp equals Cpk --> L[Process is Well Centred]
    K -- Cp greater than Cpk --> M[Process is Off Centre; Centring Problem]
    L --> N[Compute Pp and Ppk Using Overall Sigma for Long Term View]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compute Z Bench and DPMO for Sigma Level Conversion]
    O --> P[Compare Against KTU Benchmarks: 1.33 Capable, 1.67 Highly Capable, 2.0 Six Sigma]
    P --> Q[Generate Capability Report with Control Limits and Histogram]
    Q --> R[End]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram — $C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$ Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Specification Window USL to LSL] --> B[Divide into Two Halves by Midpoint M]
    B --> C[Upper Half Distance: USL minus Mu over 3 Sigma]
    B --> D[Lower Half Distance: Mu minus LSL over 3 Sigma]
    C --> E{Minimum Operator}
    D --> E
    E --> F[Cpk Index: Real Capability]
    A --> G[Total Width: USL minus LSL over 6 Sigma]
    G --> H[Cp Index: Potential Capability]
    F --> I{Compare Cp and Cpk}
    H --> I
    I -- Equal --> J[Well Centred Process]
    I -- Cp greater --> K[Off Centre Process: Centring Defect]
    I -- Cpk greater --> L[Statistical Impossibility: Investigate Sampling]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Capability Metrics

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Process Capability Family] --> B0[Short Term Within Subgroup]
    A0 --> C0[Long Term Overall]
    B0 --> B1[Cp Potential Centred]
    B0 --> B2[Cpk Actual With Centring]
    C0 --> C1[Pp Potential Long Term]
    C0 --> C2[Ppk Actual Long Term]
    A0 --> D0[Target Based: Cpm Taguchi]
    A0 --> E0[Sigma Level Z Bench]
    E0 --> F0[Defects Per Million DPMO]
    F0 --> G0[Six Sigma Goal: 3.4 DPMO]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define Process Capability. State the formula for $C_p$ and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> *Process Capability* is a statistical measure of the inherent ability of a process to produce output within the specification limits set by the customer or design. It quantifies whether the natural process spread ($6\sigma$) fits within the engineering tolerance band (USL $-$ LSL).
>
> **Formula:** $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$
>
> **Significance:** A $C_p \geq 1.0$ indicates the process spread is smaller than the specification width, meaning the process *can* meet specifications. A $C_p \geq 1.33$ is the KTU-recommended minimum for a *capable* process, and $C_p \geq 2.0$ is the world-class / Six Sigma benchmark. However, $C_p$ assumes the process is perfectly centred; if it is not, the actual performance is judged by $C_{pk}$.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` Distinguish between $C_{pk}$ and $P_{pk}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | $C_{pk}$ | $P_{pk}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Standard deviation used | $\sigma_{within}$ (short-term, within-subgroup) | $\sigma_{overall}$ (long-term, all data) |
| Time horizon | Short-term / potential | Long-term / actual performance |
| Computation | $C_{pk} = \min[(USL - \mu)/3\sigma_{within}, (\mu - LSL)/3\sigma_{within}]$ | $P_{pk} = \min[(USL - \mu)/3\sigma_{overall}, (\mu - LSL)/3\sigma_{overall}]$ |
| $C_{pk} \geq P_{pk}$ always | Holds in a stable process | $P_{pk} \leq C_{pk}$ if no long-term drift |
| Application | Predicts *best-case* performance | Reflects *real* customer-experienced performance |

> $C_{pk}$ answers *"What is the process capable of?"*, whereas $P_{pk}$ answers *"What is the process actually delivering?"*. The gap between them reveals long-term drift or special-cause variation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to state units or convert to consistent units** costs an easy 1 mark.
> 2. **Using $\sigma_{overall}$ in the $C_p$ formula** (or vice versa) is a 2-mark killer. Always declare which $\sigma$ you are using *before* writing the formula.
> 3. **Skipping the normality check** — the KTU 2024 marking scheme explicitly allocates 2 marks for "stating the assumption of normality" in capability problems.
> 4. **Computing both one-sided values but forgetting the $\min$ operator** for $C_{pk}$ — costs 1 mark and the conclusion becomes invalid.
> 5. **Concluding "process is capable" without comparing to the 1.33/1.67/2.0 benchmark** — the examiner expects an explicit benchmark citation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`**

**(a) (7 marks)** A pharmaceutical tablet-pressing process has USL $= 510$ mg, LSL $= 490$ mg. From $25$ subgroups of size $5$, the average of the subgroup means is $\bar{\bar{x}} = 499.8$ mg, and the average within-subgroup standard deviation is $\bar{S} = 1.8$ mg with $c_4 = 0.9400$ for $n = 5$. Compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and the centring factor $k$. Comment on the capability of the process.

**(b) (7 marks)** If the *overall* standard deviation of the same $125$ data points is found to be $\sigma_{overall} = 2.4$ mg, compute $P_p$, $P_{pk}$, and the long-term sigma level assuming a $1.5\sigma$ shift. State the DPMO corresponding to this sigma level.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{within} &= \frac{\bar{S}}{c_4} = \frac{1.8}{0.9400} = 1.9149 \text{ mg} \\
T &= USL - LSL = 510 - 490 = 20 \text{ mg} \\
C_p &= \frac{T}{6\sigma_{within}} = \frac{20}{6 \times 1.9149} = \frac{20}{11.4894} = 1.741 \\
\mu &= 499.8 \text{ mg},\quad M = \frac{510 + 490}{2} = 500 \text{ mg} \\
C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \frac{510 - 499.8}{3 \times 1.9149} = \frac{10.2}{5.7447} = 1.776 \\
C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \frac{499.8 - 490}{3 \times 1.9149} = \frac{9.8}{5.7447} = 1.706 \\
C_{pk} &= \min(1.776,\; 1.706) = 1.706 \\
k &= \frac{\vert 499.8 - 500 \vert}{20/2} = \frac{0.2}{10} = 0.02 \\
C_{pk} \text{ (verify)} &= C_p(1-k) = 1.741 \times 0.98 = 1.706 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (Part a):** $C_p = 1.741$ and $C_{pk} = 1.706$ — both exceed $1.67$, placing the process in the **"Highly Capable"** band. $k = 0.02$ shows the mean is almost perfectly centred ($0.02$ mg from the midpoint). The process is acceptable for pharmaceutical production with $C_{pk} > 1.50$ tolerance for Key Characteristics.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_p &= \frac{T}{6\sigma_{overall}} = \frac{20}{6 \times 2.4} = \frac{20}{14.4} = 1.389 \\
P_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \frac{10.2}{3 \times 2.4} = \frac{10.2}{7.2} = 1.417 \\
P_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \frac{9.8}{3 \times 2.4} = \frac{9.8}{7.2} = 1.361 \\
P_{pk} &= \min(1.417,\; 1.361) = 1.361 \\
Z_{USL} &= \frac{510 - 499.8}{2.4} = \frac{10.2}{2.4} = 4.250 \\
Z_{LSL} &= \frac{499.8 - 490}{2.4} = \frac{9.8}{2.4} = 4.083 \\
Z_{bench} &= \min(4.250,\; 4.083) = 4.083 \\
Z_{lt} &= Z_{bench} + 1.5 = 4.083 + 1.5 = 5.583 \\
\text{DPMO (long-term, at } 5.58\sigma\text{)} &\approx 1.2 \text{ (from standard tables)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (Part b):** $P_p = 1.389$ and $P_{pk} = 1.361$ confirm the process is *capable* in the long run, although $P_p < C_p$ and $P_{pk} < C_{pk}$ correctly indicate additional long-term variation. The long-term sigma level $5.58\sigma$ corresponds to roughly $1.2$ DPMO — better than most pharma industry averages.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`** *Alternative Choice*

**(a) (7 marks)** With a clear diagram, explain the concept of Process Capability. Differentiate between *Process Capability* ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) and *Process Performance* ($P_p$, $P_{pk}$) indices. State at least three benchmark values used to interpret $C_{pk}$ in industry.

**(b) (7 marks)** A resistor manufacturing line has USL $= 110\ \Omega$, LSL $= 90\ \Omega$. The data collected ($n = 100$) yields $\bar{x} = 98\ \Omega$ and $s = 3.2\ \Omega$ (overall). Assuming normality, compute $P_p$, $P_{pk}$, the one-sided $Z$ values, and the short-term DPMO. Comment on whether the process is Six Sigma capable.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

Process Capability is a statistical measure comparing the *natural spread* ($6\sigma$) of a stable process to the *tolerance window* (USL $-$ LSL).

**Conceptual Diagram (textual):**

$$
\underbrace{[ \text{LSL} \;\; \bullet \;\;\; \bullet \;\;\; \bullet \;\; \text{USL} ]}_{\text{Engineering Tolerance } T}
\quad \text{vs.} \quad
\underbrace{[ \mu - 3\sigma \;\;\; \mu \;\;\; \mu + 3\sigma ]}_{\text{Natural Process Spread } 6\sigma}
$$

If the inner box is fully contained inside the outer box, the process is *capable*. $C_p$ assumes $\mu$ is at the centre $M$; $C_{pk}$ adjusts for off-centre means.

**Capability vs. Performance:**

- $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ use $\sigma_{within}$ (short-term, from subgroup ranges/standard deviations). They reflect the process's *potential*.
- $P_p$ and $P_{pk}$ use $\sigma_{overall}$ (long-term, from pooled data). They reflect the *actual delivered* performance including drift.
- $C_{pk} \geq P_{pk}$ in a stable process; if $C_{pk} < P_{pk}$, recalculate $\sigma$ — sampling error suspected.

**Three Benchmarks:**
1. $C_{pk} \geq 1.00$ — *Barely Capable* (industry minimum for non-critical features).
2. $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ — *Capable* (KTU / IATF 16949 minimum for *Significant* characteristics).
3. $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ — *Highly Capable* (mandatory for *Key* characteristics in automotive, mandatory for many FDA/medical device specs).

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{overall} &= s = 3.2\ \Omega \\
T &= 110 - 90 = 20\ \Omega \\
P_p &= \frac{20}{6 \times 3.2} = \frac{20}{19.2} = 1.042 \\
P_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \frac{110 - 98}{3 \times 3.2} = \frac{12}{9.6} = 1.250 \\
P_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \frac{98 - 90}{3 \times 3.2} = \frac{8}{9.6} = 0.833 \\
P_{pk} &= \min(1.250,\; 0.833) = 0.833 \\
Z_{USL} &= \frac{110 - 98}{3.2} = 3.750 \\
Z_{LSL} &= \frac{98 - 90}{3.2} = 2.500 \\
Z_{bench} &= \min(3.750,\; 2.500) = 2.500 \\
\text{Short-term DPMO at } Z_{bench} = 2.5 &\approx 6{,}210
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (Part b):** $P_p = 1.042$ suggests the *spread* is borderline-capable, but $P_{pk} = 0.833 < 1.00$ reveals the *process is incapable* because the mean ($98\ \Omega$) is too close to the LSL ($90\ \Omega$). With long-term shift added, the effective sigma level is $Z_{lt} = 2.5 + 1.5 = 4.0\sigma$, yielding $\sim 6{,}210$ DPMO. **The process is NOT Six Sigma capable** — it requires *re-centring* (mean correction) *and* variation reduction. A formal **DMAIC project** is recommended.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this to your wall before the exam):**

- **Definition:** Process capability = ability of a *stable, normal* process to meet specifications; quantifies the fit between *Voice of Process* and *Voice of Customer*.
- **Two Families:** Short-term (within-subgroup) → $C_p$, $C_{pk}$. Long-term (overall) → $P_p$, $P_{pk}$.
- **Core Formulas:**
  - $C_p = (USL - LSL) / 6\sigma_{within}$
  - $C_{pk} = \min\!\left[ (USL - \mu)/3\sigma_{within},\; (\mu - LSL)/3\sigma_{within} \right]$
  - $P_p = (USL - LSL) / 6\sigma_{overall}$
  - $P_{pk} = \min\!\left[ (USL - \mu)/3\sigma_{overall},\; (\mu - LSL)/3\sigma_{overall} \right]$
  - $C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k)$, with $k = \vert \mu - M \vert / (T/2)$.
- **Mandatory Pre-conditions:** (1) Statistical control on X-bar/R chart; (2) Normality verified.
- **Six Sigma Standard:** $C_{pk} \geq 2.0$ → $3.4$ DPMO (long-term with $1.5\sigma$ shift).
- **Industry Benchmarks:** $1.00$ (marginal), $1.33$ (capable), $1.67$ (highly capable), $2.00$ (world-class).
- **The $1.5\sigma$ shift** is Motorola's empirical long-term drift rule; *always add $1.5$* to short-term $Z$ to get long-term $Z$.
- **Diagnostic Trick:** $C_p = C_{pk} \Rightarrow$ well-centred; $C_p > C_{pk} \Rightarrow$ off-centre; $C_p < C_{pk} \Rightarrow$ sampling error (recheck $\sigma$).
- **Real-World Mandates:** IATF 16949 (automotive) demands $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ for *Key* features; FDA process validation expects $P_{pk} \geq 1.33$ before commercial release.
- **Mnemonic:** **"C.P.P.C."** — *Capability Potential Performance Centring.* $C_p$ = Potential, $C_{pk}$ = with Centring, $P_p$ = Performance, $P_{pk}$ = Performance with centring.
- **Common Pitfall:** Confusing $C_{pk}$ with $C_p$ when reporting to management — always present both and explain the gap.
- **Unit Reminder:** All indices are **dimensionless**; $\sigma$ must be in the *same units* as USL and LSL.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Six Sigma Roles and Responsibilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Six Sigma Roles and Responsibilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** framework, **Six Sigma Roles and Responsibilities** refer to the formally structured hierarchy of human capital deployment that drives a Six Sigma initiative within an organization. Each role is mapped against a **D-M-A-I-C (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control)** project lifecycle responsibility, ensuring that statistical quality control, change management, and financial accountability are distributed across a clear chain of command.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):** Six Sigma deployment requires a well-defined organizational infrastructure where every member—from the CEO to the frontline worker—has a designated role, a quantified objective, and a defined interface with the customer (CTQ — Critical To Quality).

The architecture is borrowed from the **Motorola (1986) and AlliedSignal / GE (1995–2000)** deployment models and has been formally integrated into modern quality management standards such as **ISO 13053-1** and **ASQ (American Society for Quality) Body of Knowledge**.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Hospital Surgery Team" Analogy:**
> Think of a Six Sigma rollout as a planned surgical operation in a hospital.
> - The **CEO/Senior Leaders** are the **Hospital Board** — they don't scrub in, but they approve the budget, the strategic priority, and sign the discharge summary.
> - The **Champions** are the **Head Surgeons** — they choose which operations (projects) to perform and which patient (process) to operate on.
> - The **Master Black Belts (MBB)** are the **Senior Consultants / Professors of Surgery** — they train the surgeons and audit outcomes.
> - The **Black Belts (BB)** are the **Surgeons** — full-time, skilled operators who perform the DMAIC procedure.
> - The **Green Belts (GB)** are the **Resident Doctors** — they assist in surgery, run smaller projects, and maintain patient vitals.
> - The **Yellow Belts (YB)** are the **Nursing Staff** — they work on the floor, gather data, and ensure the patient's daily recovery is on track.
> - The **Process Owner** is the **Patient's Family Doctor** — they live with the patient long-term and must sustain the cure.

In simple words: **Six Sigma is not done by one person. It is a "relay race" of expertise where the baton (the project) is passed through people with very specific, non-overlapping job descriptions.**

## Core Terminology Standardized to KTU Notation

| Term | KTU Standard Expansion |
| :--- | :--- |
| **CTQ** | **Critical to Quality** — measurable characteristics that matter most to the customer. |
| **COPQ** | **Cost of Poor Quality** — financial loss from defects/scrap/rework. |
| **VOC** | **Voice of the Customer** — translated into CTQ variables. |
| **SIPOC** | **Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers** — high-level process map. |
| **D-M-A-I-C** | **Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control** — the 5-phase roadmap. |
| **GID** | **Green Belt Initiation Date** — official project start milestone. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every role in Six Sigma carries a **RACI** accountability:
> **R**esponsible (does the work), **A**ccountable (signs off), **C**onsulted (provides expert input), **I**nformed (kept in loop). KTU examiners frequently award marks for explicitly mentioning RACI.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Six Sigma Deployment Hierarchy — Layered Logic

The deployment model is built like a **military command pyramid**, where the number of personnel *increases* as you move down, but the *statistical depth* of expertise *decreases*. This is intentional — it ensures statistical expertise is concentrated where it is needed most.

### Layer 1 — Strategic / Executive Apex
- **Executive Leadership (CEO, COO, Board of Directors)**
  - **Why:** They own the strategic vision. Six Sigma is a business strategy, not a tool.
  - **How:** They set the **North-Star CTQ** (e.g., "Reduce invoice defects from 4.3σ to 6σ in 24 months"), allocate capital, and tie project savings to executive bonuses.
  - **KTU Highlight:** Their primary deliverable is the **Strategic Project Portfolio**.

### Layer 2 — Sponsorship / Steering
- **Sponsors / Champions (Vice Presidents, Senior Managers)**
  - **Why:** They bridge strategy with execution.
  - **How:** They select projects, remove organizational barriers ("Navy Seal" of obstacles), approve resources, and validate the **COPQ** savings.
  - **KTU Highlight:** A Champion must complete at least one DMAIC project before appointment, per ASQ standards.

### Layer 3 — Mastery / Mentorship
- **Master Black Belts (MBB)**
  - **Why:** Statistical integrity of the entire program depends on them.
  - **How:** They mentor 5–10 Black Belts, run advanced statistical training (Regression, DOE, ANOVA), and conduct **project tollgate audits**.
  - **Certification:** Typically requires ≥ 5 completed BB projects + 50,000+ hours of practice.

### Layer 4 — Project Leadership / Core Engine
- **Black Belts (BB)** — *Full-time change agents*
  - **Why:** They are the **primary DMAIC executors**.
  - **How:** Lead 4–6 projects per year, applying tools like **Hypothesis Testing, Gage R&R, DOE, FMEA**.
  - **Target:** Each BB project should yield **$100,000 – $500,000** in annualised savings.

### Layer 5 — Project Support / Hybrid Practitioners
- **Green Belts (GB)**
  - **Why:** 60–80% of an organization's workforce will touch Six Sigma — GBs represent this mass.
  - **How:** Spend ~20–30% of their time on Six Sigma; lead smaller projects or support BBs in larger ones.
  - **KTU Highlight:** GBs are the *largest* population in any Six Sigma rollout by headcount.

### Layer 6 — Awareness / Foundation
- **Yellow Belts (YB)**
  - **Why:** Create a quality-conscious culture.
  - **How:** Participate in brainstorming, data collection, and 5S activities.

### Layer 7 — Custodian / Sustainer
- **Process Owners**
  - **Why:** Six Sigma improvements decay without long-term ownership.
  - **How:** They hold the **Control Plan**, run the **SPC charts**, and ensure gains are not lost.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **high-yield formulas** associated with Six Sigma role deployment, project valuation, and resource allocation. Memorise these for ESE.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Sigma Level (Z)** | $Z = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $\text{ST}$ (Short Tons) or $\sigma$ units |
| 2 | **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities |
| 3 | **Process Yield (PY)** | $\text{PY} = e^{-DPMO/10^6}$ | Dimensionless fraction (0 to 1) |
| 4 | **Rolled Throughput Yield** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Product of stage yields (decimal) |
| 5 | **COPQ Savings (Annual)** | $S = (D_0 - D_1) \cdot C_d \cdot N$ | $D_0$=baseline defects, $D_1$=post defects, $C_d$=cost per defect, $N$=annual volume |
| 6 | **Black Belt Capacity** | $B_{cap} = \dfrac{T_{BB} \cdot \eta}{t_{proj}}$ | Projects/year (typ. 4–6) |
| 7 | **Green Belt Time Allocation** | $t_{GB} = 0.20 \text{ to } 0.30 \times t_{total}$ | 20–30% of total working hours |
| 8 | **Master Black Belt : Black Belt Ratio** | $R_{MBB:BB} = 1 : 5 \text{ to } 1 : 10$ | Coaching span of control |
| 9 | **Project Tollgate Pass Criterion** | $\text{Score} \geq 80\%$ | Phase review benchmark |
| 10 | **RACI Completeness Check** | $\sum R + A + C + I = n_{tasks}$ | Every task needs exactly 1 Accountable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** Avoid the `|` symbol in exam answer sheets — KTU evaluators deduct marks for ambiguous absolute-value notation. Always use $\vert$ in handwritten answers, e.g., $Z = \dfrac{\vert USL - \mu \vert}{\sigma}$.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

In a manufacturing plant, the **Black Belt** is typically a Mechanical / Industrial Engineer, the **MBB** is a Senior Statistician, and the **Process Owner** is the Floor Production Manager. In service industries (Banking, IT), a Black Belt might be a Process Excellence Lead using **Lean Six Sigma + Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)** to reduce software defect leakage. The role structure is **industry-agnostic** — only the CTQs change.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivative 1: Process Yield from DPMO — Full Logical Walkthrough

This is the most frequently asked **3-mark derivation** in KTU 2019–2024 university papers. The examiner wants to see every step explicitly.

**Problem Setup:** Given a process producing 1,000 units with 4 opportunities per unit and 12 total defects, compute the DPMO, Process Yield, and Rolled Throughput Yield for a 3-stage line.

**Step 1 — Identify the given data.**
We are told:
- Number of units, $U = 1000$
- Opportunities per unit, $O = 4$
- Total defects, $D = 12$
- Number of stages, $n = 3$ with stage yields $Y_1, Y_2, Y_3$ to be computed.

**Step 2 — Compute the total number of defect opportunities.**
The total opportunities are the number of units multiplied by the number of opportunities per unit:

$$ O_{total} = U \times O = 1000 \times 4 = 4000 $$

**Step 3 — Apply the DPMO formula.**
DPMO is the number of defects scaled to a per-million-opportunity basis:

$$ \text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{O_{total}} $$

Substitute:

$$ \text{DPMO} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^6}{4000} $$

**Step 4 — Perform the division.**

$$ \text{DPMO} = \dfrac{12\,000\,000}{4000} = 3000 $$

> [!NOTE]
> A DPMO of 3000 corresponds to a **Sigma level of approximately 4.4σ** — a typical manufacturing baseline.

**Step 5 — Compute the Process Yield (PY).**
Process yield is the *probability of producing a defect-free unit* under the assumption of a Poisson-like defect arrival model:

$$ \text{PY} = e^{-DPMO/10^6} $$

Substitute:

$$ \text{PY} = e^{-3000/1\,000\,000} = e^{-0.003} $$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the exponential.**

$$ \text{PY} = 0.99700 $$

So the process is **99.70% defect-free** (i.e., 0.30% defect rate).

**Step 7 — Compute Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) for a 3-stage line.**
For a 3-stage line where each stage has the same yield $Y = 0.997$:

$$ \text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{3} Y_i = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 $$

Substitute:

$$ \text{RTY} = 0.997 \times 0.997 \times 0.997 $$

**Step 8 — Multiply step-by-step.**

$$ \text{RTY} = 0.994009 \times 0.997 $$

$$ \text{RTY} = 0.99102 $$

**Step 9 — Interpret.**
The line produces 0.892% defective units end-to-end, even though each stage individually looks acceptable. **This is why Six Sigma is needed** — the cumulative defect leak is non-trivial.

**Final Summary Table:**

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **DPMO** | **3000** | 3 defects per 1000 opportunities |
| **PY (single stage)** | **99.70 %** | Stage-level conformance |
| **RTY (3-stage)** | **99.10 %** | End-to-end customer-facing conformance |
| **Defect Leakage** | **0.90 %** | Lost units to end customer |

## Derivative 2: Project Savings (COPQ) Linked to Black Belt Role

**Step 1 — Given:**
- Baseline defects/month, $D_0 = 500$
- Post-Six-Sigma defects/month, $D_1 = 50$
- Cost per defect, $C_d = \text{₹}2000$
- Annual volume, $N = 12$ months.

**Step 2 — Net defect reduction per month.**

$$ \Delta D = D_0 - D_1 = 500 - 50 = 450 \text{ defects/month} $$

**Step 3 — Annualised savings.**

$$ S = \Delta D \times C_d \times N $$

Substitute:

$$ S = 450 \times 2000 \times 12 $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$ S = 900\,000 \times 12 = 10\,800\,000 $$

**Step 5 — ROI per Black Belt project.**
A Black Belt typically runs 4–6 such projects. If we assume 5 projects/year:

$$ \text{ROI}_{BB} = 5 \times 10\,800\,000 = \text{₹}5.4 \text{ Crore/year} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners award full marks only when the *role linkage* is mentioned. Always write: *"This savings justifies the Black Belt's full-time deployment and the Champion's resource clearance."*

## Analytical Matrix — Role vs. Tool vs. Outcome

| Role | Primary Tool Used | DMAIC Phase Owned | Measurable Outcome |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Champion** | Project Charter, COPQ | Define | Project selection & closure |
| **Master Black Belt** | DOE, Regression, ANOVA | All Phases | Statistical accuracy |
| **Black Belt** | Hypothesis Testing, FMEA | Measure → Improve | DPMO reduction |
| **Green Belt** | Pareto, Fishbone, 5-Whys | Measure / Control | Local process improvement |
| **Yellow Belt** | 5S, Check Sheets | Data Collection | Cultural adoption |
| **Process Owner** | SPC, Control Plan | Control | Sustained gain |
| **Executive** | VOC → CTQ Translation | Strategic | Bottom-line impact |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Six Sigma Organizational Pyramid (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CEO1[Executive Leadership] --> SP1[Sponsors and Champions]
    SP1 --> MBB1[Master Black Belts]
    MBB1 --> BB1[Black Belts]
    MBB1 --> GB1[Green Belts]
    BB1 --> GB1
    GB1 --> YB1[Yellow Belts]
    YB1 --> TM1[Team Members and Process Owners]
    BB1 --> PO1[Process Owners]
    MBB1 --> PO1
    CEO1 --> ACC1[Strategic CTQ and COPQ Accountability]
    SP1 --> ACC1
    MBB1 --> ACC1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows indicate *coaching and authority flow*, not data flow. Notice that **Process Owners** are connected to both Black Belts and Master Black Belts because the project handover at the *Control* phase requires sign-off from both the statistical expert and the daily custodian of the process.

## Diagram 2: DMAIC Phase-to-Role Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase Define]:::phase --> P2[Phase Measure]:::phase
    P2 --> P3[Phase Analyze]:::phase
    P3 --> P4[Phase Improve]:::phase
    P4 --> P5[Phase Control]:::phase
    P1 -.owned by.-> R1[Champion]:::role
    P2 -.owned by.-> R2[Black Belt]:::role
    P3 -.owned by.-> R2
    P4 -.owned by.-> R2
    P5 -.owned by.-> R3[Process Owner]:::role
    R2 -.mentored by.-> R4[Master Black Belt]:::role
    classDef phase fill:#E0F2FF,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    classDef role fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Compilation Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`P1`–`P5`, `R1`–`R4`), and labels contain plain uppercase text only. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as node IDs.

## Diagram 3: Role Interaction Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1[Voice of Customer VOC] --> CH1[Champion Translates to CTQ]
    CH1 --> BB1[Black Belt Defines Project Charter]
    BB1 --> MBB1[Master Black Belt Reviews and Approves]
    MBB1 --> GB1[Green Belt Executes Data Collection]
    GB1 --> YB1[Yellow Belt Records Field Data]
    YB1 --> PO1[Process Owner Implements Control Plan]
    PO1 --> SP1[Sponsor Validates Savings COPQ]
    SP1 --> CEO1[Executive Links to Strategic Goals]
```

## Diagram 4: Project Tollgate Gatekeeping Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TG1[Tollgate 1 Charter Sign Off] --> TG2[Tollgate 2 Measurement System Approved]
    TG2 --> TG3[Tollgate 3 Root Cause Validated]
    TG3 --> TG4[Tollgate 4 Pilot Improvement Confirmed]
    TG4 --> TG5[Tollgate 5 Control Plan Transferred]
    TG5 --> CL[Project Closure and Savings Realisation]
    TG1 -.gatekeeper.-> CH1[Champion]
    TG2 -.gatekeeper.-> MBB1[Master Black Belt]
    TG3 -.gatekeeper.-> MBB1
    TG4 -.gatekeeper.-> MBB1
    TG5 -.gatekeeper.-> PO1[Process Owner]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note:** Every *tollgate* (phase-end review) is a **statistical and financial audit**. This is the structural reason Six Sigma produces auditable, defensible savings — and why KTU expects students to mention tollgates explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part A consists of 5 questions of 3 marks each (typically answered in 60–80 words each, with a one-line definition, one supporting fact, and one real-world example).

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between a Black Belt and a Master Black Belt in Six Sigma." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A **Black Belt (BB)** is a *full-time* Six Sigma project leader who executes 4–6 DMAIC projects per year, applying tools like Hypothesis Testing, DOE, and FMEA. A **Master Black Belt (MBB)** is a *full-time* technical mentor and coach who trains 5–10 Black Belts, audits project tollgates, and possesses deep statistical expertise (Regression, ANOVA). In short, a BB *runs* projects; an MBB *ensures the integrity* of the projects across the program.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List any three responsibilities of a Six Sigma Champion." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Project Selection:** Scans the VOC and CTQ tree to identify high-impact DMAIC projects aligned with business strategy.
2. **Resource Clearance:** Removes organizational, financial, and political barriers for Black Belts and Green Belts.
3. **Tollgate Sign-off:** Validates phase-end deliverables and confirms COPQ savings before project closure.
*(Optional 4th point: Acts as the bridge between executive leadership and project teams.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 4 Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with a 50–50 split (a) + (b). The model answers below follow the official valuation key — *definition → formula → substitution → final answer → interpretation*.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the Six Sigma organizational hierarchy with a neat block diagram. Detail the responsibilities of any three roles. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Definition of Hierarchy (2 marks):**
The Six Sigma organizational hierarchy is a structured deployment pyramid that assigns statistical, financial, and operational accountability to specific roles — from Executive Leadership at the top to Yellow Belts and Process Owners at the operational floor. It ensures that **DMAIC** projects are executed with statistical rigour, business alignment, and long-term sustainability.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram Description (1 mark):**
*(Refer to Section 4 Diagram 1 of these notes.)*
The hierarchy flows from **Executive Leadership → Sponsors/Champions → Master Black Belts → Black Belts → Green Belts → Yellow Belts → Process Owners**, with bidirectional feedback for tollgate sign-offs and COPQ validation.

**Step 3 — Three Key Roles (4 marks):**

| Role | Responsibility |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Black Belt** | Full-time DMAIC executor, leads 4–6 projects/year, applies statistical tools (DOE, FMEA, Hypothesis Testing). |
| **Champion** | Selects projects, removes obstacles, validates savings, and links projects to strategic goals. |
| **Process Owner** | Owns the Control Plan post-handover, runs SPC charts, ensures sustainment of improvements. |

**Step 4 — Conclusion (0 marks, but recommended):**
This hierarchy guarantees that statistical expertise is concentrated where it matters most while maintaining mass cultural participation through Green and Yellow Belts.

**(b)** A manufacturing line produces 5,000 units/day with 5 defect opportunities per unit. Historically, 250 defects are observed per day. After a Black Belt-led DMAIC project, defects drop to 25/day. Calculate the **DPMO, Process Yield, and Sigma Level (Z)** before and after the project, and the **annualised cost savings** if $C_d = \text{₹}500$/defect. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute pre-project DPMO.**

$$ \text{DPMO}_0 = \dfrac{250 \times 10^6}{5000 \times 5} = \dfrac{250 \times 10^6}{25\,000} = 10\,000 $$

**Step 2 — Compute pre-project Process Yield.**

$$ \text{PY}_0 = e^{-10\,000/10^6} = e^{-0.01} = 0.99005 $$

**Step 3 — Pre-project Sigma Level.**
From standard normal tables, $Z_0 \approx 3.8\sigma$ for 10,000 DPMO.

**Step 4 — Compute post-project DPMO.**

$$ \text{DPMO}_1 = \dfrac{25 \times 10^6}{25\,000} = 1000 $$

**Step 5 — Compute post-project Process Yield.**

$$ \text{PY}_1 = e^{-1000/10^6} = e^{-0.001} = 0.99900 $$

**Step 6 — Post-project Sigma Level.**

$$ Z_1 \approx 4.6\sigma $$

**Step 7 — Sigma improvement.**

$$ \Delta Z = 4.6 - 3.8 = 0.8\sigma $$

**Step 8 — Annual cost savings (365 days).**

$$ S = (250 - 25) \times 500 \times 365 = 225 \times 500 \times 365 $$

$$ S = 112\,500 \times 365 = \text{₹}4.10 \text{ Crore/year} $$

**Interpretation:** The project delivered a 0.8σ improvement and **₹4.10 Crore/year** in savings, validating the Black Belt's full-time deployment under the Champion's sponsorship.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the responsibilities of **Master Black Belts, Green Belts, and Process Owners** in detail. How do they differ from Black Belts? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Master Black Belts (MBB) (2 marks):**
- Coach and mentor 5–10 Black Belts.
- Conduct advanced statistical training (DOE, Regression, ANOVA).
- Audit tollgates and validate statistical soundness.
- Maintain the **Six Sigma Tool Library** and best-practice repository.

**Step 2 — Green Belts (GB) (2 marks):**
- Spend 20–30% of time on Six Sigma; rest on operational duties.
- Lead smaller, scope-limited projects (single department).
- Support Black Belts in data collection and brainstorming.
- **Largest headcount** in any organization — the *cultural multiplier*.

**Step 3 — Process Owners (PO) (2 marks):**
- Receive the Control Plan at project closure.
- Run **SPC charts** and respond to out-of-control conditions.
- Are accountable to the Champion for sustained gains.
- Live with the process long after the Black Belt has moved on.

**Step 4 — Differentiation from Black Belts (1 mark):**
| Aspect | Black Belt | MBB / GB / PO |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Time %** | 100% | 100% (MBB) / 20–30% (GB) / 0% (PO) |
| **Project count** | 4–6/year | Variable |
| **Statistical depth** | Deep | Varies |
| **Mandate** | Execute DMAIC | Mentor / Support / Sustain |

**(b)** An IT service desk receives 8,000 tickets/month with an average of 3 process steps per ticket. After a Green Belt project, ticket-level defects reduce from 240 to 60 per month. Compute the **DPMO, Process Yield, and RTY for a 3-step ticket flow**, and discuss the role of the Green Belt in this improvement. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute pre-project DPMO.**

$$ \text{DPMO}_0 = \dfrac{240 \times 10^6}{8000 \times 3} = \dfrac{240 \times 10^6}{24\,000} = 10\,000 $$

**Step 2 — Pre-project Process Yield.**

$$ \text{PY}_0 = e^{-0.01} = 0.99005 $$

**Step 3 — Post-project DPMO.**

$$ \text{DPMO}_1 = \dfrac{60 \times 10^6}{24\,000} = 2500 $$

**Step 4 — Post-project Process Yield.**

$$ \text{PY}_1 = e^{-0.0025} = 0.99750 $$

**Step 5 — Pre-project RTY (3 stages, each with 0.99005 yield).**

$$ \text{RTY}_0 = 0.99005^3 = 0.97044 $$

**Step 6 — Post-project RTY.**

$$ \text{RTY}_1 = 0.99750^3 = 0.99251 $$

**Step 7 — Improvement in end-to-end customer experience.**

$$ \Delta \text{RTY} = 0.99251 - 0.97044 = 0.02207 = 2.21\% $$

**Step 8 — Role of the Green Belt (1 mark):**
The Green Belt applied Pareto analysis, Fishbone diagrams, and 5-Whys within their functional scope (IT service desk). They coordinated with the Black Belt (if assigned) and used VOC data to align improvements with end-user expectations. Their 20–30% time allocation was sufficient because the project scope was contained.

**Interpretation:** The Green Belt's contribution lifted customer-facing quality by **2.21%** — a meaningful improvement in service quality, demonstrating the scalability of Six Sigma beyond manufacturing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Champion" with "Sponsor."** A Sponsor is a *funding* authority; a Champion is a *project-prioritization and obstacle-removal* authority. Marks are lost when these are conflated.
> 2. **Do NOT omit the Process Owner.** Many students focus on Black Belts and Green Belts and forget the Process Owner's role in *Control*. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests this.
> 3. **Do NOT skip units in numerical answers.** Always write "DPMO = 3000 defects/10⁶ opportunities" — not just "3000."
> 4. **Do NOT use the `|` symbol** in formula sheets; KTU evaluators flag this as ambiguous.
> 5. **Do NOT claim Black Belts are "managers."** They are *change agents*, not line managers — this is a common conceptual error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Six Sigma Roles and Responsibilities**

- **Core Idea:** Six Sigma is a *relay race* — the project baton passes from Champion → MBB → BB → GB → YB → Process Owner.
- **Pyramid Order (Top to Bottom):** Executive → Sponsor/Champion → Master Black Belt → Black Belt → Green Belt → Yellow Belt → Process Owner.
- **Black Belt:** 100% time, 4–6 projects/year, primary DMAIC executor.
- **Master Black Belt:** 100% time, 5–10 BBs mentored, statistical auditor.
- **Green Belt:** 20–30% time, supports BB or leads small projects, *largest headcount*.
- **Yellow Belt:** Awareness-level, data collector, cultural amplifier.
- **Champion:** Picks projects, removes barriers, validates savings.
- **Sponsor:** Funds the project at the executive level.
- **Process Owner:** Owns the Control Plan, runs SPC, sustains the gain.
- **Key Formulas:** $DPMO$, $PY = e^{-DPMO/10^6}$, $RTY = \prod Y_i$, $\Delta S = \Delta D \cdot C_d \cdot N$.
- **RACI:** Every task needs exactly 1 Accountable; mention this for full marks.
- **Tollgates:** 5 phase-end reviews — *Champion signs Define, MBB signs Measure/Analyze/Improve, Process Owner signs Control*.
- **Crossover Roles:** DFSS (Design for Six Sigma) extends DMAIC into new product/process design, often owned by Black Belts transitioning to Master Black Belts.
- **Common Pitfall:** Confusing Champion with Sponsor; omitting Process Owner in Control; skipping units in numerical answers.
- **Real-World Anchor:** GE (under Jack Welch) saved **$12 billion** over 5 years using this exact hierarchy — a frequent KTU essay question.
- **One-Liner for Viva:** *"The role structure ensures that statistical depth is concentrated at the project core, while cultural adoption is mass-distributed."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DMAIC Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** DMAIC Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DMAIC** is an acronym for **Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, and Control**. It is a data-driven, structured, five-phase problem-solving methodology used in **Six Sigma** to improve, optimize, and stabilize business processes and designs. It is the universally accepted roadmap for executing any Six Sigma project, transforming a vague business problem into a quantifiable, statistically validated, and sustainably controlled solution.

In KTU parlance, the DMAIC framework is treated as the **"Operational Backbone of Six Sigma"** — every project registered under the **Define phase** of a Green Belt or Black Belt certification is completed only after the **Control phase** is signed off by the project champion.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Treatment" Model

Think of DMAIC exactly like a doctor treating a chronically ill patient:

| DMAIC Phase | Doctor's Equivalent | Purpose |
|-------------|--------------------|---------|
| **Define** | Taking the patient's history & chief complaint | Identify *what* hurts and *who* the customer is |
| **Measure** | Running blood tests, MRI, ECG | Quantify *how bad* the problem is (baseline) |
| **Analyze** | Diagnosing the root disease | Determine *why* the problem is happening |
| **Improve** | Prescribing medicine / surgery | Apply the cure to fix the root cause |
| **Control** | Discharge plan + monthly check-ups | Ensure the illness *does not return* |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a doctor who skips diagnosis and directly prescribes medicine risks malpractice, a Six Sigma practitioner who skips the **Analyze** phase risks optimizing the *wrong* problem — a classic KTU exam pitfall.

## 1.3 The Standard Six Sigma Quality Benchmark

The entire DMAIC methodology is designed to push process performance toward the legendary **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** — equating to **6 standard deviations (6σ)** between the process mean and the nearest specification limit.

- **Sigma Level (Z):** 6
- **Yield:** **99.99966%**
- **DPMO:** **3.4**

> [!TIP]
> Examiners frequently award a 1-mark bonus if you mention the magic number **3.4 DPMO** anywhere in a Six Sigma theory question. Memorize it cold.

---

## 1.4 Why DMAIC Exists — The Problem It Solves

Before DMAIC, organizations relied on ad-hoc "fix-it" firefighting. DMAIC replaced intuition with the scientific method:

1. **Customer Focus:** Starts and ends with the *Voice of the Customer (VOC)*.
2. **Data-Driven:** Every decision is backed by statistical evidence, not opinions.
3. **Project-Based:** Work is scoped into manageable charters with clear financial targets.
4. **Sustainable:** The Control phase prevents regression to the "old bad habits".

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Phases — Logic Flow and Operational Purpose

### 🔷 Phase 1: DEFINE (The "What" and "Who")
- **Objective:** Frame the project, identify the customer, and document the business case.
- **Key Tools Used:**
  - **Project Charter** (the "birth certificate" of the project)
  - **SIPOC Diagram** (Suppliers → Inputs → Process → Outputs → Customers)
  - **VOC (Voice of the Customer)** collection via surveys, interviews, complaints
  - **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) Tree** — translates VOC into measurable metrics
  - **Kano Model** for prioritizing customer needs
- **Key Deliverable:** A signed-off **Project Charter** and a high-level **Process Map**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SIPOC** is a high-level process view. It is *not* a detailed flow chart — it captures only the macro-level process steps from end to end. Examiners love asking: *"Is SIPOC a detailed process map?"* (Answer: No).

### 🔷 Phase 2: MEASURE (The "How Much")
- **Objective:** Quantify the current state of the process and establish a baseline.
- **Key Tools Used:**
  - **Data Collection Plan** (operational definition, sampling strategy)
  - **Process Capability Indices:** **Cp**, **Cpk**, **Pp**, **Ppk**
  - **Gage R&R (Repeatability & Reproducibility)** — measures measurement system error
  - **Control Charts** (X-bar, R, I-MR, p, c, u charts)
  - **Pareto Charts** (80/20 rule)
- **Key Deliverable:** A validated measurement system and a quantified **baseline sigma level**.

### 🔷 Phase 3: ANALYZE (The "Why")
- **Objective:** Identify the root cause(s) of defects and variation.
- **Key Tools Used:**
  - **5 Whys Analysis** (Toyota's iterative questioning)
  - **Fishbone (Ishikawa / Cause-and-Effect) Diagram** — 6Ms: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature
  - **Hypothesis Testing:** t-test, ANOVA, Chi-Square
  - **Regression Analysis** (linear, multiple)
  - **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**
  - **Pareto Analysis** (re-applied on cause categories)
- **Key Deliverable:** A list of **validated root causes** (the "vital few").

### 🔷 Phase 4: IMPROVE (The "How to Fix")
- **Objective:** Generate, pilot, and implement solutions that eliminate root causes.
- **Key Tools Used:**
  - **Brainstorming** & **Design of Experiments (DOE)**
  - **Piloting / Kaizen Events**
  - **Mistake Proofing (Poka-Yoke)**
  - **Cost-Benefit Analysis** & **ROI Calculation**
  - **Solution Selection Matrix** (impact vs. ease of implementation)
- **Key Deliverable:** Implemented solutions with statistically proven improvement and projected savings.

### 🔷 Phase 5: CONTROL (The "How to Sustain")
- **Objective:** Lock in the gains and institutionalize the new process.
- **Key Tools Used:**
  - **Updated Control Plans** & **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**
  - **Statistical Process Control (SPC) Charts** (live monitoring)
  - **Reaction Plans** (what to do if a point goes out of control)
  - **Visual Management Systems** (Andon, dashboards)
  - **Project Closure & Handover to Process Owner**
- **Key Deliverable:** A **mistake-proofed, monitored process** with a closed-loop feedback system.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Description | Units |
|------------------|----------|-------------|-------|
| **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$ | Defect density metric | Defects/Million |
| **Yield (Rolled Throughput Yield)** | $\text{RTY} = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \dots \times Y_n$ | Probability of a defect-free unit through all stages | % or decimal |
| **Process Capability Index (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Potential capability (centered process) | Dimensionless |
| **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Actual capability (accounts for shift) | Dimensionless |
| **Sigma Level (Z)** | $Z = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{\sigma}\right)$ | Distance from mean to nearest spec limit in σ | σ units |
| **Process Performance (Pp)** | $P_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6s}$ | Long-term overall performance | Dimensionless |
| **Process Performance (Ppk)** | $P_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3s}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3s}\right)$ | Long-term actual performance | Dimensionless |
| **Gage R&R (% of Tolerance)** | $\% \text{R\&R} = \dfrac{6 \times \sigma_{\text{R\&R}}}{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}} \times 100$ | Measurement system error | % |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | Total quality-related cost | ₹ / $ / € |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse Cp with Pp, and Cpk with Ppk.** Cp/Cpk use the *within-subgroup* standard deviation (σ), while Pp/Ppk use the *overall* standard deviation (s). KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of DMAIC

- **Manufacturing:** Reducing defect rates in semiconductor fabrication (Intel, Texas Instruments).
- **Healthcare:** Reducing patient wait times and surgical site infection rates.
- **Software Engineering:** Reducing production deployment failures and bug escape rates.
- **Banking & Finance:** Reducing loan processing cycle time and customer complaint volume.
- **Supply Chain:** Optimizing warehouse pick-rate and reducing shipment damages.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting DPMO to Sigma Level (Z)

This is one of the most frequently tested numerical problems in KTU Six Sigma papers. The methodology assumes a **1.5σ shift** in the long-term process mean (a Motorola convention).

### Given:
- A process records **D defects** in **N total opportunities** over the period.
- Compute DPMO, convert to Yield, then to a Z-score.

### Step-by-Step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compute Defect Rate} \quad & \text{Defect Rate (in millions)} = \dfrac{D}{N} \times 10^6 \\[8pt]
\text{Step 2: Compute First Pass Yield} \quad & \text{FPY} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{D}{N}\right) \\[8pt]
\text{Step 3: Convert Yield to Z (Short-term)} \quad & Z_{\text{ST}} = \Phi^{-1}(\text{FPY}) \quad \text{where } \Phi^{-1} \text{ is the inverse standard normal CDF} \\[8pt]
\text{Step 4: Apply 1.5σ Long-Term Shift} \quad & Z_{\text{LT}} = Z_{\text{ST}} + 1.5 \\[8pt]
\text{Step 5: Final Sigma Level} \quad & \sigma_{\text{level}} = Z_{\text{LT}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style):

**Problem:** A call center handles 50,000 calls per month. 1,200 calls result in customer complaints. Each call has 2 opportunities for a defect (rude agent + unresolved query). Calculate the process sigma level.

**Step 1 — Total Opportunities:**
$$
N_{\text{opportunities}} = 50{,}000 \times 2 = 100{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Defect Rate:**
$$
\text{Defect Rate} = \dfrac{1{,}200}{100{,}000} = 0.012
$$

**Step 3 — DPMO:**
$$
\text{DPMO} = 0.012 \times 10^6 = 12{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — First Pass Yield:**
$$
\text{FPY} = 1 - 0.012 = 0.988
$$

**Step 5 — Short-Term Z (from standard normal tables):**
For a tail area of 0.012, the cumulative probability is 0.988.
$$
Z_{\text{ST}} = \Phi^{-1}(0.988) \approx 2.26
$$

**Step 6 — Long-Term Z (with 1.5σ shift):**
$$
Z_{\text{LT}} = 2.26 + 1.5 = 3.76 \, \sigma
$$

**Conclusion:** The process operates at **3.76 sigma**, far below the world-class **6σ benchmark** of 3.4 DPMO.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Process Capability Indices

**Scenario:** A bolt manufacturing process has specification limits **LSL = 9.5 mm** and **USL = 10.5 mm**. The sample mean is **x̄ = 10.1 mm** and the standard deviation is **σ = 0.08 mm**.

### Compute Cp:
$$
C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{10.5 - 9.5}{6 \times 0.08} = \dfrac{1.0}{0.48} = 2.083
$$

### Compute Cpk:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{10.5 - 10.1}{3 \times 0.08} = \dfrac{0.4}{0.24} = 1.667 \\[8pt]
C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \dfrac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{10.1 - 9.5}{3 \times 0.08} = \dfrac{0.6}{0.24} = 2.500 \\[8pt]
C_{pk} &= \min(1.667, \, 2.500) = 1.667
\end{aligned}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation:
- $C_p = 2.083$ — the *potential* capability is excellent.
- $C_{pk} = 1.667$ — the *actual* capability is lower because the process mean (10.1 mm) is **shifted toward the USL**.
- **The difference (Cp − Cpk = 0.416)** quantifies how off-center the process is. The process needs **re-centering**, not just a variance reduction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmarks for Cpk:**
> - $C_{pk} < 1.00$ → Process is **incapable** (unacceptable for production).
> - $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ → Process is **marginally capable** (monitor closely).
> - $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ → Process is **capable**.
> - $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ → Process is **highly capable** (world-class target for critical features).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Phase-wise Tool Selection

| Phase | Primary Question | Statistical Tool | Non-Statistical Tool | Output Metric |
|-------|------------------|------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Define** | *What is the problem?* | None | Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC, CTQ Tree | Problem Statement |
| **Measure** | *How bad is it?* | Capability Indices, Control Charts, Gage R&R | Data Collection Plan, Pareto | Baseline Sigma Level |
| **Analyze** | *Why is it happening?* | Hypothesis Testing, Regression, ANOVA | Fishbone, 5 Whys, FMEA | Validated Root Causes |
| **Improve** | *What will fix it?* | DOE, Confirmation t-tests | Brainstorming, Pilots, Poka-Yoke | Improved Sigma Level + Savings |
| **Control** | *How do we sustain it?* | SPC Charts, Control Plans | SOPs, Reaction Plans, Visual Mgmt | Closed Project with Stable Process |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the five phases of the DMAIC methodology with the key deliverable of each phase.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **DMAIC** methodology is a structured, data-driven roadmap used in Six Sigma projects:

1. **Define:** Identify the problem, the customer, and the project scope. **Deliverable:** Approved *Project Charter* and *SIPOC diagram*.
2. **Measure:** Quantify the current performance and validate the measurement system. **Deliverable:** *Baseline sigma level* and verified measurement system (Gage R&R).
3. **Analyze:** Identify and validate the root causes of defects. **Deliverable:** *Validated root cause list* (vital few from the fishbone analysis).
4. **Improve:** Develop, pilot, and implement solutions that eliminate root causes. **Deliverable:** *Implemented solution with proven sigma gain* and ROI calculation.
5. **Control:** Lock in the improvements using SOPs, SPC charts, and reaction plans. **Deliverable:** *Updated control plan* and project closure with stable process handover.

*(Word-limit cue: ~120 words. Use a 5-row table if you have time — it scores higher.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a SIPOC diagram? Why is it built during the Define phase and not later?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **SIPOC diagram** is a high-level process map that identifies the **Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, and Customers** of a project in a single view. It is built in the **Define** phase because:

- It forces the team to **agree on project boundaries** before data is collected (avoiding scope creep in the Measure phase).
- It translates the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into measurable process outputs early.
- It identifies **supplier inputs** that may later become critical variables in the Analyze phase.

SIPOC is a *macro-level* tool, not a detailed process map. Building it later (e.g., in Analyze) would waste measurement effort on out-of-scope steps.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]**
**(a)** Describe the **Measure phase** of DMAIC in detail. List the key statistical tools used and explain the role of **Gage R&R** in establishing a valid measurement system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing process produces resistors with target **R = 100 Ω**. The specification limits are **LSL = 95 Ω** and **USL = 105 Ω**. The sample mean of 50 readings is **x̄ = 101.2 Ω** with standard deviation **σ = 1.4 Ω**. Calculate the **process capability indices Cp and Cpk** and interpret the process capability. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Measure Phase (7 Marks):**

The **Measure phase** is the second step of DMAIC. Its purpose is to **quantify the current state** of the process, validate the measurement system, and establish a **statistical baseline** against which improvements are later compared.

**Key activities:**
1. Develop a **Data Collection Plan** — defines *what* data to collect, *how* to measure, *when*, and *who* is responsible.
2. Establish **Operational Definitions** so that every team member measures a defect identically.
3. Validate the **Measurement System** using **Gage R&R (Repeatability & Reproducibility)**.
4. Compute **baseline capability indices** (Cp, Cpk) and the **baseline sigma level**.
5. Construct initial **Control Charts** to study process behavior over time.

**Role of Gage R&R (Gage Repeatability and Reproducibility):**

Gage R&R quantifies the **total variation introduced by the measurement system** itself. It isolates two sources:
- **Repeatability** — variation observed when *the same operator* measures the *same part* multiple times with the *same gage* (equipment variation).
- **Reproducibility** — variation observed when *different operators* measure the *same part* using the *same gage* (operator variation).

**Why it matters in DMAIC:**
- If the measurement system is poor (e.g., %R&R > 30% of tolerance), the data collected is unreliable, making all subsequent Analyze-phase conclusions invalid.
- Industry rule of thumb:
  - **%R&R < 10%** → Excellent measurement system.
  - **10% ≤ %R&R < 30%** → Acceptable.
  - **%R&R ≥ 30%** → Unacceptable; the gage must be repaired or replaced before proceeding.

In short, Gage R&R ensures the "ruler" used in Measure and Analyze is accurate — a foundational requirement of data-driven decision making.

---

**Part (b) — Capability Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- LSL = 95 Ω, USL = 105 Ω, Tolerance = 10 Ω
- x̄ = 101.2 Ω, σ = 1.4 Ω

**Step 1: Calculate Cp**
$$
C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{105 - 95}{6 \times 1.4} = \dfrac{10}{8.4} = 1.190
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Cpk**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,\text{upper}} &= \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{105 - 101.2}{3 \times 1.4} = \dfrac{3.8}{4.2} = 0.905 \\[8pt]
C_{pk,\text{lower}} &= \dfrac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{101.2 - 95}{3 \times 1.4} = \dfrac{6.2}{4.2} = 1.476 \\[8pt]
C_{pk} &= \min(0.905, \, 1.476) = 0.905
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

- $C_p = 1.190$ — the *spread* of the process is acceptable (potential capability is fine).
- $C_{pk} = 0.905$ — the *actual* capability is **below 1.00**, meaning the process is **not capable** of consistently meeting specifications.
- The process is **off-center**; the mean (101.2 Ω) is shifted *upward* toward the USL (105 Ω), making the upper-spec capability the binding constraint.
- **Recommendation:** Re-center the process to bring x̄ closer to 100 Ω. Once centered, $C_{pk}$ will approach $C_p$ and likely exceed 1.00.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]**
**(a)** Explain the **Analyze phase** of DMAIC. Describe the **Fishbone diagram** and the **5 Whys technique** with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hospital billing department processes 8,000 invoices per month. A defect is defined as *"any invoice requiring correction or re-issuance"*. In a sample month, 640 invoices had defects. Each invoice has **3 opportunities** for a defect. Calculate the **DPMO** and the **process sigma level (assuming 1.5σ shift)**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Analyze Phase (7 Marks):**

The **Analyze phase** is the third step of DMAIC, in which the project team uses statistical and graphical tools to **identify, validate, and prioritize the root causes** of the problem identified in the Define phase and quantified in the Measure phase.

**Key tools used:**
- **Pareto Chart** — to separate the *vital few* causes from the *trivial many*.
- **Fishbone (Ishikawa / Cause-and-Effect) Diagram** — to brainstorm potential causes.
- **5 Whys Analysis** — to drill down to the root cause.
- **Hypothesis Testing** (t-test, ANOVA, Chi-Square) — to statistically validate suspected causes.
- **Regression Analysis** — to quantify the relationship between cause variables and the output (CTQ).
- **FMEA** — to rank failure modes by Risk Priority Number (RPN).

**Fishbone Diagram Explained:**

The Fishbone diagram (also called the **Ishikawa diagram** or **cause-and-effect diagram**) is a structured brainstorming tool that organizes potential causes of a problem under broad categories. The standard "**6Ms**" used in manufacturing are:

1. **Man** (Workforce / People)
2. **Machine** (Equipment / Tools)
3. **Material** (Raw inputs)
4. **Method** (Process / Procedure)
5. **Measurement** (Inspection / Gages)
6. **Mother Nature** (Environment)

The **"problem statement"** forms the *head* of the fish, and the 6Ms form the *bones*. Sub-causes are attached as smaller bones.

**5 Whys Technique Explained:**

The 5 Whys technique, developed by **Sakichi Toyoda** of Toyota, is a simple iterative questioning method in which the team asks *"Why?"* up to **five times** to peel away symptoms and arrive at the root cause.

**Example: Late Delivery to Customers**
1. *Why was the delivery late?* → The shipment left the warehouse late.
2. *Why did it leave late?* → Order picking took 6 hours instead of 2.
3. *Why did picking take 6 hours?* → Pickers were searching for items in wrong aisles.
4. *Why were items in wrong aisles?* → The warehouse was reorganized last month, but the bin labels were not updated.
5. *Why were bin labels not updated?* → There is no Standard Operating Procedure for relabeling bins after a layout change.

**Root cause:** Absence of a bin-relabeling SOP after warehouse reorganization.

---

**Part (b) — DPMO and Sigma Level Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Units processed (N) = 8,000 invoices
- Defects (D) = 640 invoices
- Opportunities per unit = 3

**Step 1: Total Opportunities**
$$
N_{\text{opportunities}} = 8{,}000 \times 3 = 24{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Defect Rate**
$$
\text{Defect Rate} = \dfrac{640}{24{,}000} = 0.02667
$$

**Step 3: DPMO**
$$
\text{DPMO} = 0.02667 \times 10^6 = 26{,}667 \text{ defects per million opportunities}
$$

**Step 4: First Pass Yield (FPY)**
$$
\text{FPY} = 1 - 0.02667 = 0.97333
$$

**Step 5: Short-Term Sigma (Z_ST)**

From the standard normal table, the Z-score corresponding to a tail area of 0.02667 is:
$$
Z_{\text{ST}} = \Phi^{-1}(0.97333) \approx 1.93
$$

**Step 6: Long-Term Sigma (with 1.5σ shift)**
$$
Z_{\text{LT}} = 1.93 + 1.5 = 3.43 \, \sigma
$$

**Step 7: Interpretation**

The hospital billing department operates at approximately **3.43 sigma**, which is **significantly below the Six Sigma goal of 6σ (3.4 DPMO)**. The team should focus the Analyze phase on root causes such as data-entry errors, missing invoice fields, and system integration issues, and target an improvement to at least **4.5σ** in the Improve phase.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks on DMAIC questions (from KTU valuation experience):**
> 1. **Confusing DMAIC with DMADV** — DMADV is for *designing* new processes; DMAIC is for *improving* existing ones. Examiners will not award full marks if these are mixed up.
> 2. **Skipping the 1.5σ shift in sigma calculations** — Always explicitly state *"with 1.5σ long-term shift convention"* when computing sigma level from DPMO. Losing 1–2 marks here is common.
> 3. **Confusing Cp/Cpk with Pp/Ppk** — Cp and Cpk use the *within-subgroup* σ (short-term), while Pp and Ppk use the *overall* s (long-term).
> 4. **Forgetting the *deliverable* of each phase** — KTU theory questions ask *"What is the output of the Analyze phase?"*. A vague answer like *"find causes"* gets partial credit. Saying *"validated root cause list with statistical evidence"* scores full marks.
> 5. **Not interpreting numerical results** — A 7-mark calculation question explicitly requires an engineering interpretation sentence at the end. Writing only formulas without concluding *"the process is not capable because Cpk < 1.00"* will cost 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **DMAIC = Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control** (in that strict order).
- **Define** ends with an *approved Project Charter* and a *SIPOC diagram*.
- **Measure** produces a *baseline sigma level* and a *validated Gage R&R*.
- **Analyze** outputs *statistically validated root causes* (5 Whys, Fishbone, Hypothesis Tests).
- **Improve** deploys *pilot-tested, cost-justified solutions* and confirms the *new sigma level*.
- **Control** uses *SPC charts, updated SOPs, and reaction plans* to prevent regression.
- The **3.4 DPMO** number is the hallmark of a Six Sigma process.
- The **1.5σ long-term shift** is *always* applied when converting DPMO to a sigma level.
- **Cp** measures *spread*; **Cpk** measures *spread + centering*. Always use **Cpk** for capability reporting.
- A process with **Cpk < 1.00 is incapable**; a process with **Cpk ≥ 1.67 is world-class**.
- **Gage R&R** must be < 30% of tolerance before proceeding beyond the Measure phase.
- The **6Ms** of Fishbone: *Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature*.
- **SIPOC** is a *Define-phase*, high-level tool — never confuse it with a detailed process flow chart.
- **DMAIC ≠ DMADV** — DMAIC improves; DMADV designs.
- Always close a DMAIC project with a **post-project financial benefit statement** (cost savings vs. project cost).
- **Sustainability** is the most-tested control-phase concept: if the gains are not institutionalized in SOPs, the project is considered failed at the audit stage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Define Phase
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Define Phase

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the Define Phase?

The **Define Phase** is the foundational, first stage of the **DMAIC** (Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control) problem-solving roadmap used in Six Sigma. Its primary objective is to clearly **identify the problem, the customer, the project scope, and the desired goals** before any data is collected.

Formally, the Define Phase can be expressed as:

$$
\text{Define Phase} = f(\text{Problem}, \text{Customer (VOC)}, \text{Scope}, \text{Goal}, \text{Team})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> The Define Phase establishes the *contract* between the project team and the process owner. Without a tightly scoped definition, all subsequent Six Sigma activities drift into "analysis paralysis" — a common failure mode noted in the KTU 2024 course outcomes for PEMRT752.

---

## 1.2 The DMAIC Framework — Context Setting

Six Sigma follows the structured **DMAIC** lifecycle, an adaptation of Deming's Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle, tailored for reducing process variation and eliminating defects.

| Phase | Full Name | Core Question Answered |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **D** | Define | *What is the problem and who is the customer?* |
| **M** | Measure | *How is the process performing today?* |
| **A** | Analyze | *What is causing the defects?* |
| **I** | Improve | *How do we remove the root causes?* |
| **C** | Control | *How do we sustain the gains?* |

> [!NOTE]
> The Define Phase uses **no statistical data collection**. Instead, it uses qualitative and project-management tools to align stakeholders and frame the business problem.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The GPS Analogy"

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Delhi** without a GPS. You know the destination exists, but you don't know the route, the distance, the fuel required, or the stops in between. You will waste time, fuel, and likely get lost.

- The **Define Phase** is the moment you **enter the destination in your GPS** — you set the start point, the destination, the preferred route, and the estimated time of arrival.
- The **Measure Phase** checks the *current speed and traffic* (baseline data).
- The **Analyze Phase** finds *why* there is traffic.
- The **Improve Phase** chooses an *alternate route*.
- The **Control Phase** ensures you *don't forget the new route* tomorrow.

Without the Define Phase (entering the destination), the entire DMAIC journey becomes a wandering exercise.

---

## 1.4 Core Deliverables of the Define Phase

The output of the Define Phase is a **package of documents** that serves as the project's blueprint:

1. **Project Charter** — the formal authorization document.
2. **Problem Statement** — concise description of the gap.
3. **Goal Statement** — SMART target.
4. **Scope** — boundaries of the project (in/out).
5. **Voice of Customer (VOC)** — translated customer needs.
6. **Critical to Quality (CTQ)** — measurable customer requirements.
7. **SIPOC Diagram** — high-level process map.
8. **Stakeholder Analysis / RACI Matrix** — role clarity.
9. **Tollgate Review** — formal sign-off to enter Measure Phase.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exam:** *"P³-S-C-T"* — **P**roblem, **P**roject Charter, **P**rocess (SIPOC), **S**takeholders, **C**TQ, **T**ollgate.

---

## 1.5 The 5S + 1C Principles in the Define Phase

The Define Phase follows a logical flow often summarized as the **5S + 1C** model:

$$
\boxed{\text{Supplier} \rightarrow \text{Input} \rightarrow \text{Process} \rightarrow \text{Output} \rightarrow \text{Customer} \rightarrow \text{CTQ}}
$$

This is the exact logical order of the SIPOC diagram, which is the single most important artifact of the Define Phase.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Define Phase — Operational Breakdown

The Define Phase can be deconstructed into **six sequential cognitive steps**:

### Step 1: Identify the Problem (Business Case)
- A problem is identified when a measurable **gap** exists between *current performance* and *expected performance*.
- The gap is expressed as a *pain point* reported by customers, employees, or financial data.

### Step 2: Identify the Customer (Voice of Customer — VOC)
- VOC is the process of capturing the customer's expectations, preferences, and aversions.
- VOC sources: **surveys, interviews, focus groups, complaint logs, warranty data, social media, ethnographic observation**.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Outcome mapping: This step aligns with **CO1 — Apply the Define phase tools to frame a Six Sigma project.**

### Step 3: Translate VOC into CTQ (Critical to Quality)
- VOC statements are *qualitative* (e.g., "I want fast delivery").
- CTQ characteristics are *quantitative* (e.g., "Delivery time ≤ 24 hours").
- The transformation is:
$$
\text{VOC (qualitative)} \xrightarrow{\text{Translation}} \text{CTQ (quantitative metric with specification limit)}
$$

### Step 4: Define the Process Boundary (SIPOC)
- SIPOC identifies the **S**uppliers, **I**nputs, **P**rocess steps, **O**utputs, and **C**ustomers at a high level.
- It is a *one-page* diagram that prevents scope creep.

### Step 5: Charter the Project
- The Project Charter formalizes: *sponsor, champion, team, problem, goal, scope, timeline, budget*.

### Step 6: Tollgate Review
- The **Champion / Sponsor** signs off the charter. Only then does the project move to the **Measure Phase**.

---

## 2.2 The SMART Goal Framework

Every Goal Statement in the Define Phase must satisfy the **SMART** criteria:

| Letter | Meaning | Example (Six Sigma Project) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **S** | Specific | Reduce order-fulfillment cycle time |
| **M** | Measurable | From 72 hrs to 36 hrs |
| **A** | Achievable | Based on benchmark data |
| **R** | Relevant | Linked to business strategy |
| **T** | Time-bound | Within 4 months |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A goal that fails the SMART test is *not* a Six Sigma goal — it is a wish.

---

## 2.3 Project Charter — Anatomy

A Project Charter contains exactly **eight sections**. The KTU examiner often asks students to "list the contents of a Project Charter" (3-mark question).

| # | Section | Purpose |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Project Title | Clear, descriptive name |
| 2 | Problem Statement | The gap, in 1–2 sentences |
| 3 | Goal Statement | SMART target |
| 4 | Project Scope (In/Out) | Boundaries |
| 5 | Team Members & Roles | Champion, Black Belt, etc. |
| 6 | Timeline / Milestones | Gantt summary |
| 7 | Budget | Resource allocation |
| 8 | Sponsor Sign-off | Authorization |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Define Phase Metrics

Although the Define Phase is mostly qualitative, a few **numerical relationships** are testable:

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Defect Rate (baseline) | $D = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities}}$ | Used to set the baseline in the next phase |
| DPMO | $\text{DPMO} = D \times 10^{6}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities |
| Sigma Level (approx.) | $\sigma \approx 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \times \ln(\text{DPMO})}$ | Conversion of DPMO to σ-level |
| Improvement Target | $\text{Target} = Y_{\text{baseline}} \pm k \cdot \sigma_{\text{baseline}}$ | Setting SMART goals from baseline data |
| Cycle Time Efficiency | $\text{CTE} = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100\%$ | Used when goal is time-reduction |
| Customer Satisfaction Index | $\text{CSI} = \dfrac{\sum w_i \cdot s_i}{\sum w_i}$ | Weighted average of VOC scores |
| COPQ | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | Cost of Poor Quality — justifies the project |
| Project ROI | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Expected Savings} - \text{Project Cost}}{\text{Project Cost}} \times 100\%$ | Used in the Business Case |

> [!NOTE]
> In tables above, notice that vertical bars `$\vert$` have been written as `$\vert$` in LaTeX inside the table to prevent markdown table-parsing errors.

---

## 2.5 Roles in the Define Phase (RACI Context)

| Role | Responsibility in Define Phase |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Champion** | Selects project, removes roadblocks, approves charter |
| **Sponsor** | Owns the process, provides resources, signs charter |
| **Black Belt** | Facilitates VOC, builds SIPOC, drafts charter |
| **Green Belt** | Collects VOC data, helps scope project |
| **Process Owner** | Validates CTQ from operational reality |
| **Team Members** | Brainstorm problem and goal statements |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In manufacturing firms (e.g., automotive, semiconductor), the Define Phase:
- Aligns the shop-floor **Quality Control team** with the **C-suite** strategy.
- Prevents engineers from optimizing the *wrong* process (a multi-million-rupee mistake).
- Bridges the **customer complaints** logged in CRM systems with **internal engineering KPIs**.

In service industries (e.g., KTU-affiliated hospitals, banks, IT helpdesks), the Define Phase ensures the *actual* customer pain (e.g., long OPD queues) is correctly mapped to a *measurable* process (e.g., "average waiting time").
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Translating VOC into CTQ (The KANO-Inspired Logic)

The KTU 2024 module expects students to demonstrate the *logical flow* of converting a fuzzy VOC statement into a measurable CTQ. The derivation proceeds in **four explicit transitions**.

### Step 1: Capture Raw VOC
Raw VOC is a *voice* (a quote or complaint), not a number.

$$
\text{VOC}_{\text{raw}} = \text{"The mobile phone battery drains too fast."}
$$

### Step 2: Identify the Underlying Customer Need
The need is *interpreted* by the analyst. This is a **deductive** step.

$$
\text{Need} = \text{Long battery backup under typical daily usage}
$$

### Step 3: Convert Need to a Measurable Specification
The need is now given a *unit* and a *limit*.

$$
\text{CTQ} = \text{Battery backup} \geq 12 \text{ hours on a single full charge, under 4G usage}
$$

### Step 4: Define the Metric and Target

$$
\text{Metric} = T_{\text{battery}} \text{ (hours)}, \quad \text{Target} = 12 \text{ hrs}, \quad \text{USL} = 12 \text{ hrs}
$$
