The complete logical chain can be written as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VOC}_{\text{raw}} &\xrightarrow{\text{Step 1}} \text{Customer Need (qualitative)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Step 2}} \text{CTQ Characteristic (quantitative)} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Step 3}} \text{Measurement Unit + Specification Limit} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Step 4}} \text{SMART Goal for the Project}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU examiner awards full 7 marks only if **all four transitions are explicitly shown** in the answer script.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: COPQ-Based Project Justification

Many Define Phase questions in the KTU University Exam ask: *"How does a project justify itself financially in the Define Phase?"* The answer uses the **COPQ** model.

### Step 1: Classify Costs
$$
\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{IF}} + C_{\text{EF}} + C_{\text{AP}} + C_{\text{PV}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{IF}}$ = Internal Failure Cost (rework, scrap)
- $C_{\text{EF}}$ = External Failure Cost (warranty, returns)
- $C_{\text{AP}}$ = Appraisal Cost (inspection, testing)
- $C_{\text{PV}}$ = Prevention Cost (training, FMEA)

### Step 2: Estimate Baseline COPQ

$$
\text{COPQ}_{\text{baseline}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i
$$

### Step 3: Estimate Target COPQ After Project

$$
\text{COPQ}_{\text{target}} = \text{COPQ}_{\text{baseline}} \times (1 - p)
$$

Where $p$ is the expected percentage reduction (e.g., $p = 0.30$ for a 30% reduction).

### Step 4: Compute Annual Savings

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= \text{COPQ}_{\text{baseline}} - \text{COPQ}_{\text{target}} \\
&= \text{COPQ}_{\text{baseline}} - \text{COPQ}_{\text{baseline}} \times (1 - p) \\
&= \text{COPQ}_{\text{baseline}} \times p
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Project ROI

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Savings} - \text{Project Cost}}{\text{Project Cost}} \times 100\%
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — COPQ Justification

**Problem:** A manufacturing unit incurs the following annual quality costs:

$$
C_{\text{IF}} = ₹8,00,000, \quad C_{\text{EF}} = ₹5,00,000, \quad C_{\text{AP}} = ₹2,00,000, \quad C_{\text{PV}} = ₹1,00,000
$$

A Six Sigma project is proposed with an expected 40% reduction in COPQ. The project cost is ₹3,00,000. Compute the ROI.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute baseline COPQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{baseline}} &= 8,00,000 + 5,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 1,00,000 \\
&= 16,00,000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply reduction (p = 0.40):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{target}} &= 16,00,000 \times (1 - 0.40) \\
&= 16,00,000 \times 0.60 \\
&= 9,60,000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute annual savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= 16,00,000 - 9,60,000 \\
&= 6,40,000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ROI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \frac{6,40,000 - 3,00,000}{3,00,000} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{3,40,000}{3,00,000} \times 100\% \\
&= 113.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The project yields a **113.33% return** — a financially strong justification in the Define Phase.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common VOC → CTQ Translations

| Raw VOC (Customer Voice) | Underlying Need | CTQ Characteristic | Specification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| "The website is slow" | Fast page loading | Page load time | $\leq 2$ seconds |
| "The bill has errors" | Accurate billing | Billing error rate | $\leq 0.01\%$ |
| "The product breaks easily" | Durability | Mean Time to Failure (MTTF) | $\geq 5,000$ hours |
| "Customer support never picks up" | Responsive support | Call answer time | $\leq 30$ seconds |
| "Food delivery is late" | Timely delivery | Delivery cycle time | $\leq 30$ minutes |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: SIPOC for an E-Commerce Order

| Suppliers | Inputs | Process (High Level) | Outputs | Customers |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Warehouse | Order request, stock data | Receive → Pick → Pack → Ship | Delivered parcel | End buyer |
| Payment gateway | Transaction data | Receive → Verify → Confirm | Payment receipt | End buyer |
| Courier partner | Shipping label | Hand-off → Track → Deliver | Tracking updates | End buyer |
| Customer service | Complaints, queries | Log → Route → Resolve | Resolution ticket | End buyer |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Define Phase Workflow (Top-Down Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical flow** of the Define Phase from problem identification to tollgate review.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Business Problem] --> B[Capture Voice of Customer VOC]
    B --> C[Translate VOC to CTQ Characteristics]
    C --> D[Define Project Scope In and Out]
    D --> E[Build SIPOC Diagram]
    E --> F[Draft Project Charter]
    F --> G[Form Project Team and Assign RACI]
    G --> H[Conduct Tollgate Review with Champion]
    H --> I{Charter Approved?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Proceed to Measure Phase]
    I -- No --> K[Revise Charter and Resubmit]
    K --> F
```

---

## 4.2 VOC to CTQ Conversion Pipeline

This diagram visualizes the **four-stage transformation** of a customer voice into a measurable engineering specification.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VOC1[Raw VOC Voice: qualitative quote] --> VOC2[Interpreted Customer Need: descriptive]
    VOC2 --> VOC3[CTQ Characteristic: measurable variable]
    VOC3 --> VOC4[Specification Limit USL or LSL with units]
    VOC4 --> VOC5[SMART Goal embedded in Charter]
```

---

## 4.3 SIPOC Block Diagram for a Manufacturing Line

The diagram below represents a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a SIPOC for a CNC machining cell — a typical industry scenario for KTU Mechanical / Production students.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Suppliers_Block
        S1[Raw Material Vendor]
        S2[Tool Supplier]
        S3[CAD File Source]
    end

    subgraph Inputs_Block
        I1[Aluminium Billet]
        I2[Cutting Insert]
        I3[G Code Program]
    end

    subgraph Process_Block
        P1[Step 1: Load Raw Stock]
        P2[Step 2: Set Tool Offset]
        P3[Step 3: Run Machining Cycle]
        P4[Step 4: Inspect and Unload]
    end

    subgraph Outputs_Block
        O1[Machined Component]
        O2[Inspection Report]
        O3[Scrap Chips]
    end

    subgraph Customers_Block
        C1[Assembly Line]
        C2[Quality Department]
        C3[Recycling Unit]
    end

    S1 --> I1
    S2 --> I2
    S3 --> I3
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I3 --> P3
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    O1 --> C1
    O2 --> C2
    O3 --> C3
```

---

## 4.4 RACI Matrix Visual (Project Roles in the Define Phase)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Champion: Accountable]
    R2[Sponsor: Responsible for process]
    R3[Black Belt: Responsible for charter]
    R4[Green Belt: Consulted on VOC]
    R5[Process Owner: Informed of scope]
    R6[Team Members: Consulted on SIPOC]

    R1 --> R3
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
    R3 --> R6
    R4 --> R5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **RACI** stands for: **R**esponsible (does the work), **A**ccountable (signs off), **C**onsulted (provides input), **I**nformed (kept updated). In the Define Phase, the *Champion* is typically **A**, the *Black Belt* is **R**, and the rest are **C/I**.

---

## 4.5 Tollgate Review Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TG1[Draft Charter Ready] --> TG2{All 8 Sections Complete?}
    TG2 -- No --> TG3[Return to Black Belt for Revision]
    TG3 --> TG1
    TG2 -- Yes --> TG4[Champion Reviews Charter]
    TG4 --> TG5{Approved?}
    TG5 -- No --> TG6[Document Rejection Reasons]
    TG6 --> TG3
    TG5 -- Yes --> TG7[Sign-off Recorded in Project Logbook]
    TG7 --> TG8[Project Authorized to Enter Measure Phase]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the Define Phase in Six Sigma. List any four deliverables of this phase.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Define Phase** is the first stage of the DMAIC methodology, focused on clearly framing the problem, identifying the customer, and setting the project scope before any data is collected.

**Four key deliverables:**
1. **Project Charter** — formal authorization document.
2. **Problem Statement** — concise description of the gap.
3. **Goal Statement** — SMART target.
4. **SIPOC Diagram** — high-level process map (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers).

*(Award 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for the four deliverables.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between Voice of Customer (VOC) and Critical to Quality (CTQ) characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | VOC | CTQ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nature | Qualitative (voice) | Quantitative (measured) |
| Source | Direct customer feedback | Translated from VOC |
| Format | Quote, complaint, statement | Metric with specification limit |
| Example | "I want quick delivery" | Delivery time $\leq 24$ hours |

*(Award 1.5 marks for each side of the comparison.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **SIPOC diagram** in detail with a suitable example. Why is it constructed in the Define Phase and not in the Measure Phase? **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A manufacturing firm reports the following annual quality costs: Internal Failure = ₹6,00,000; External Failure = ₹4,00,000; Appraisal = ₹2,00,000; Prevention = ₹1,00,000. A Six Sigma project costing ₹2,50,000 is expected to reduce the total COPQ by 35%. Compute the **annual savings** and the **project ROI**. Justify whether the project should be initiated. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition (2 Marks):**
A **SIPOC diagram** is a high-level process map that identifies the **S**uppliers, **I**nputs, **P**rocess steps, **O**utputs, and **C**ustomers of a process in a single view.

**Components (3 Marks):**

| Element | Meaning | Example (Coffee Shop) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Suppliers | Who provides inputs | Milk vendor, coffee bean supplier |
| Inputs | What is needed | Beans, milk, cups |
| Process | High-level steps | Receive order → Grind → Brew → Serve |
| Outputs | What is produced | Cup of coffee, receipt |
| Customers | Who receives outputs | Customer, billing system |

**Why in Define Phase (2 Marks):**
- It is a *qualitative* tool — no data is required.
- It establishes the **scope boundary** before Measure Phase data collection.
- Prevents the team from measuring the *wrong* process, which would waste resources.

---

### Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute baseline COPQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{baseline}} &= 6,00,000 + 4,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 1,00,000 \\
&= 13,00,000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply 35% reduction ($p = 0.35$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{target}} &= 13,00,000 \times (1 - 0.35) \\
&= 13,00,000 \times 0.65 \\
&= 8,45,000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute annual savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= 13,00,000 - 8,45,000 \\
&= 4,55,000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ROI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \frac{4,55,000 - 2,50,000}{2,50,000} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{2,05,000}{2,50,000} \times 100\% \\
&= 82\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Justification (1 Mark):**
Since the project yields a **positive ROI of 82%**, it is financially justified and should be **initiated in the Define Phase**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the **Project Charter** used in the Define Phase. Explain each of its eight sections briefly. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A customer survey reports the complaint: *"ATM cash withdrawal takes too long."* Apply the **VOC → CTQ translation methodology** (with all four logical steps) to convert this into a measurable specification for a Six Sigma project. Set a SMART goal for the project. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A **Project Charter** is a formal document that authorizes a Six Sigma project. It is the *contract* between the sponsor and the project team.

**Eight Sections (6 Marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Project Title** — clear, descriptive name of the project.
2. **Problem Statement** — concise description of the current gap.
3. **Goal Statement** — SMART target with a specific metric and date.
4. **Project Scope** — explicit "in-scope" and "out-of-scope" boundaries.
5. **Team Members & Roles** — Champion, Sponsor, Black Belt, Green Belt, Process Owner.
6. **Timeline / Milestones** — phase-wise Gantt summary.
7. **Budget** — cost of resources, training, tools.
8. **Sponsor Sign-off** — formal approval and authorization.

> Award 0.75 mark per correctly described section.

---

### Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Capture Raw VOC (1 Mark):**
$$
\text{VOC}_{\text{raw}} = \text{"ATM cash withdrawal takes too long."}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Underlying Customer Need (1.5 Marks):**
$$
\text{Need} = \text{Quick and consistent cash withdrawal from ATM}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Need to a Measurable Specification (2 Marks):**
$$
\text{CTQ} = \text{End-to-end withdrawal time} \leq 45 \text{ seconds per transaction}
$$

**Step 4 — Define Metric, Target, and Specification Limit (1.5 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Metric} &= T_{\text{withdrawal}} \text{ (seconds)} \\
\text{Target} &= 45 \text{ seconds} \\
\text{USL} &= 45 \text{ seconds} \\
\text{Lower Better?} &= \text{Yes (smaller is better)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Draft SMART Goal (1 Mark):**
*"Reduce the average ATM cash withdrawal time at all branches from the current 90 seconds to ≤ 45 seconds within 4 months, with a defect (withdrawal > 45 s) rate of ≤ 1%."*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in the Define Phase (PEMRT752):**
>
> 1. **Listing deliverables without explaining their purpose** — A bare list of "Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC" scores only 1 of 3 marks. The KTU examiner expects a *one-line purpose* for each.
> 2. **Skipping a transition in VOC → CTQ** — Students often jump directly from VOC to a metric, missing the "interpreted need" step. This costs 1–1.5 marks.
> 3. **Confusing RACI roles** — The Champion is **Accountable**, not Responsible. Reversing this is a common error.
> 4. **Forgetting the Tollgate Review** — A Define Phase answer that ends at the Charter, with no mention of the sponsor's sign-off, loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing the Goal as a wish, not a SMART goal** — Phrases like "improve quality" or "reduce errors" are *not* SMART. Always include a metric, a target value, and a deadline.
> 6. **Mixing up SIPOC and VOC** — SIPOC is a *process* map; VOC is a *customer-needs* capture. They are distinct tools.
> 7. **Unit errors in COPQ calculations** — Always show units (₹, %, hours). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for unit omission.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Define Phase (PEMRT752):**

- ☐ The Define Phase is the **first** phase of **DMAIC**.
- ☐ It produces **9 deliverables**: Problem Statement, Goal Statement, Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC, CTQ, Scope, Team/RACI, Tollgate Review.
- ☐ **VOC** = qualitative customer voice; **CTQ** = quantitative customer requirement.
- ☐ The **VOC → CTQ** translation has **4 explicit steps** — never skip a step in the exam.
- ☐ **SIPOC** order: **S**uppliers → **I**nputs → **P**rocess → **O**utputs → **C**ustomers.
- ☐ Project Charter contains **8 sections** — title, problem, goal, scope, team, timeline, budget, sign-off.
- ☐ Goals must satisfy the **SMART** criteria: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- ☐ **RACI** = Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed.
- ☐ **Tollgate Review** is the *gate* between Define and Measure — no statistical data is collected before it.
- ☐ **COPQ formula:** $C_{\text{IF}} + C_{\text{EF}} + C_{\text{AP}} + C_{\text{PV}}$.
- ☐ **ROI formula:** $\dfrac{\text{Savings} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100\%$.
- ☐ The Define Phase uses **no statistical tools** — only qualitative and project-management tools.
- ☐ The **Champion** authorizes the project; the **Black Belt** executes the Define Phase activities.
- ☐ **In-scope** and **Out-of-scope** must be explicitly written to prevent scope creep.
- ☐ A six-month-old Define Phase project without a **signed Charter** is a *failed* Six Sigma project.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line KTU Mantra:**
> *"In the Define Phase, you do not solve the problem — you **frame** it so precisely that solving it becomes inevitable."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analyse Phase
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analyse Phase

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analyse Phase (DMAIC - Define, Measure, Analyse, Improve, Control)** is the third phase of the Six Sigma DMAIC methodology where the project team identifies, validates, and isolates the **root causes** of defects and process variation observed during the Measure Phase. It employs **statistical inference tools** (hypothesis testing, regression, ANOVA, chi-square tests) to move beyond symptoms and statistically confirm the **vital few X's (independent/input variables)** that drive the **Y (dependent/output variable)** of interest, known as the **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** characteristic.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this module bridges descriptive statistics (Measure Phase) with prescriptive analytics (Improve Phase). The Analyse Phase answers the central question: **"Why is the defect occurring?"**

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Detective on the Crime Scene" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are a detective arriving at a crime scene (your defective process). The **Measure Phase** was like collecting the evidence — fingerprint counts, blood samples, and witness statements (your baseline Sigma level, DPMO, and process capability indices like Cpk). Now, in the **Analyse Phase**, you are sitting at your desk with all the evidence laid out, asking:
> - *Which fingerprints (X variables) belong to the actual culprit (root cause)?*
> - *Is the blood type (cause-effect) statistically linked to the victim (CTQ)?*
>
> You cannot arrest a suspect just because they "look suspicious" — you need **statistical proof beyond a reasonable doubt**. That proof comes from **hypothesis testing, p-values, confidence intervals, and regression models**. You use tools like the **Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram** to brainstorm all possible causes, **Pareto Analysis** to narrow them to the "vital few," and **hypothesis tests** (t-test, ANOVA, chi-square) to statistically convict the true root cause(s). Without this phase, any "improvement" you make in the next phase would be guesswork.

---

### Position in the DMAIC Roadmap

The Analyse Phase sits precisely between the **Measure Phase** (which quantifies the problem) and the **Improve Phase** (which implements solutions).

| Phase | Central Question | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| Define | *What is the problem?* | Project Charter, VOC, CTQ |
| Measure | *How big is the problem?* | Baseline Sigma, Cpk, MSA |
| **Analyse** | ***Why is the problem occurring?*** | ***Validated Root Causes, Vital Few X's*** |
| Improve | *How do we fix it?* | Pilot solutions, optimized settings |
| Control | *How do we sustain the fix?* | Control plans, SPC charts |

---

### Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Glossary of Critical Analyse-Phase Terms (KTU Standardized Vocabulary):**
> - **X-Variables (Inputs / Predictors):** The independent process variables hypothesized to influence the output.
> - **Y-Variable (Output / Response):** The CTQ characteristic being optimized.
> - **f(x) = Y Model:** The Six Sigma transfer function expressing Y as a function of multiple X's.
> - **Vital Few X's:** The statistically significant input variables that drive most of the variation in Y (typically identified via Pareto and hypothesis tests).
> - **Trivial Many X's:** Variables that show statistical noise but no real causal influence.
> - **p-value:** Probability of observing the data (or more extreme) assuming the null hypothesis is true. The decision threshold is **α = 0.05** unless otherwise specified.
> - **α (Alpha) Risk:** Producer's risk — probability of Type I error (falsely rejecting a true null hypothesis).
> - **β (Beta) Risk:** Consumer's risk — probability of Type II error (falsely accepting a false null hypothesis).
> - **Statistical Significance:** A result is statistically significant if **p-value < α**, indicating the observed effect is unlikely due to random chance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Analyse Phase Workflow (Step-by-Step Logic)

The Six Sigma Analyse Phase follows a rigid **5-step logical flow**. KTU examiners frequently ask for these steps in sequence (commonly as a 7-mark short-note question).

1. **Step 1 — Develop a Prioritized List of Potential X's**
   Use Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) diagrams, **5 Whys**, **FMEA**, Affinity Diagrams, and **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** mapping to brainstorm ALL possible causes of the problem Y.

2. **Step 2 — Narrow the List Using Pareto Analysis**
   Apply the **80/20 Rule**. Approximately **80%** of the problems are caused by **20%** of the causes (the Vital Few X's). This prevents wasting resources on the Trivial Many.

3. **Step 3 — Collect Additional Data on Vital Few X's**
   Design **stratified sampling plans** or **Design of Experiments (DOE)** to gather high-resolution data specifically on the prioritized X-variables.

4. **Step 4 — Apply Statistical Hypothesis Testing**
   Use appropriate tests (t-test, ANOVA, chi-square, regression) to determine whether each candidate X has a **statistically significant relationship** with Y.

5. **Step 5 — Establish the Validated f(x) = Y Model**
   Quantify the functional relationship using **regression equations**. The output is a mathematical model (e.g., $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \varepsilon$) that the Improve Phase will manipulate.

---

### 2.2 The Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa) Diagram — Structure

The Fishbone diagram organizes root-cause hypotheses into **6M categories** (manufacturing) or **8P categories** (services):

| Manufacturing (6M) | Services (8P) |
|---|---|
| **Man** (People, Skill, Training) | **People** |
| **Machine** (Equipment, Tools) | **Product** |
| **Material** (Raw Inputs) | **Price** |
| **Method** (Process, Procedure) | **Promotion** |
| **Measurement** (Inspection, Gauges) | **Place** |
| **Mother Nature** (Environment) | **Process** |
| | **Productivity / Quality** |
| | **Physical Evidence** |

---

### 2.3 The 5 Whys Technique

Developed by **Sakichi Toyoda** (Toyota). Repeatedly ask "Why?" typically **5 times** until the root cause is exposed. Each "Why" peels back one layer of the symptom.

**Example:**
- *Problem:* Machine stopped.
  - *Why?* The fuse blew.
    - *Why?* The motor was overloaded.
      - *Why?* The bearing lacked sufficient lubrication.
        - *Why?* The lubrication pump was not pumping.
          - *Why?* The pump shaft was worn out. ← **Root Cause**

---

### 2.4 Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FMEA Formula — Risk Priority Number (RPN):**
> $$\text{RPN} = \text{Severity (S)} \times \text{Occurrence (O)} \times \text{Detection (D)}$$
> Each parameter is rated on a **1 to 10** scale. The higher the RPN, the higher the priority for corrective action. KTU boards typically test the RPN formula and its interpretation.

| Rating | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Negligible | Very unlikely | Almost certain |
| 5 | Moderate | Occasional | Moderate |
| 10 | Hazardous (safety risk) | Very high (failure inevitable) | Absolute uncertainty |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Analyse Phase Master Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are High-Yield for KTU ESE. Memorize the bolded formulas with their units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Critical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RPN (FMEA) | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Each rating $\in [1, 10]$; RPN range $[1, 1000]$ |
| 2 | Process Capability Index (Cp) | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | $C_p \geq 1.33$ for capable process |
| 3 | Process Capability Index (Cpk) | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ where $C_{pu} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}$, $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}$ | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ acceptable; $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ Six Sigma target |
| 4 | Six-Sigma Level | $\sigma\text{ Level} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right) + 1.5$ (shift) | Industry benchmark uses 1.5σ shift |
| 5 | Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Units Produced} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$ | Six Sigma target: **3.4 DPMO** |
| 6 | Yield (Rolled Throughput Yield) | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | RTY $\leq$ minimum of individual yields |
| 7 | One-Sample t-Statistic | $t = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ with $df = n - 1$ | Reject $H_0$ if $\vert t \vert > t_{\alpha/2, df}$ |
| 8 | Two-Sample t-Statistic (pooled) | $t = \dfrac{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2}{S_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}}$, $S_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}}$ | Degrees of freedom $df = n_1 + n_2 - 2$ |
| 9 | ANOVA F-Statistic | $F = \dfrac{MS_{\text{Treatment}}}{MS_{\text{Error}}} = \dfrac{SS_{\text{Treatment}} / (k-1)}{SS_{\text{Error}} / (N-k)}$ | Reject $H_0$ if $F > F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$ |
| 10 | Simple Linear Regression Slope | $\hat{\beta}_1 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2}$ | Tests linear relationship |
| 11 | Intercept | $\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X}$ | Y-value when X = 0 |
| 12 | Pearson Correlation Coefficient | $r = \dfrac{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2 \sum(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}}$ | $r \in [-1, +1]$ |
| 13 | Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{\text{Res}}}{SS_{\text{Tot}}}$ | $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$; proportion of variance explained |
| 14 | Chi-Square Statistic | $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | $df = (r-1)(c-1)$ for contingency table |
| 15 | Confidence Interval (mean) | $\bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2, n-1} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | For unknown σ, small n |
| 16 | Type I vs Type II Error | $\alpha = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ true})$; $\beta = P(\text{Accept } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ false})$ | Power of test $= 1 - \beta$ |
| 17 | Sample Size for Means | $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ | $E$ = margin of error |
| 18 | Sample Size for Proportions | $n = \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2}$ | Uses Z-table when $\sigma$ known |

> **CRITICAL KTU Exam Note:** The vertical pipe character has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in the formulas above (e.g., $\vert t \vert$) to ensure rendering fidelity in the KTU answer portal's markdown engine.

---

### 2.6 Decision Tree: Which Statistical Test to Use?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test Selection Logic (Frequently asked as a 7-mark question):**

```
START: Do you have a continuous or discrete Y?
│
├── CONTINUOUS Y
│   ├── Comparing 1 group to a target? → 1-Sample t-test
│   ├── Comparing 2 independent groups? → 2-Sample t-test
│   ├── Comparing 2 paired groups? → Paired t-test
│   ├── Comparing 3+ groups? → One-Way ANOVA
│   ├── Testing variance equality? → F-test / Levene's Test
│   └── Modeling relationship with X? → Regression Analysis
│
└── DISCRETE Y (counts / proportions)
    ├── Goodness-of-fit? → Chi-Square (1 variable, multiple categories)
    ├── Independence of 2 categorical variables? → Chi-Square (contingency)
    ├── Comparing 2 proportions? → 2-Proportion Z-test
    └── Rare events / small counts? → Fisher's Exact Test
```

---

### 2.7 Real-World Utility of Analyse Phase

- **Manufacturing:** Diagnosing why bearings fail prematurely on a production line.
- **Healthcare:** Identifying why patient wait times exceed CTQ targets in an ER.
- **Finance:** Determining which transaction attributes (X's) drive credit-card fraud rates (Y).
- **IT/Software:** Root-causing why deployment defect rates spike after release cycles.
- **Service Industry:** Why call-center call abandonment rates are above target.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Cpk Uses min(Cpu, Cpl)

**Start with the fundamental concept:**
A process is centered when its mean $\bar{X}$ lies exactly midway between the Lower Specification Limit (LSL) and the Upper Specification Limit (USL). The natural tolerance of the process is $\pm 3\sigma$ around the mean.

**Step 1 — Define Upper and Lower One-Sided Capability Indices:**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}$$

This measures the distance from the mean to the USL, divided by the natural process spread on that side.

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}$$

This measures the distance from the LSL to the mean, divided by the natural process spread on that side.

**Step 2 — Take the Smaller Value (Worst-Case Logic):**

$$\boxed{C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation Logic:**
- If $\bar{X}$ is centered (midway between LSL and USL), then $C_{pu} = C_{pl} = C_p$.
- If $\bar{X}$ shifts toward one specification limit, the smaller index shrinks, dragging $C_{pk}$ below $C_p$.
- **Thus, $C_{pk}$ captures both spread AND centering in a single number**, which is why KTU examiners favor it over the older $C_p$ index.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Two-Sample t-Statistic (Pooled Variance)

**Step 1 — State the null hypothesis:**
$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$$

**Step 2 — Define the pooled standard deviation:**
Two independent samples with $n_1, n_2$ observations and sample variances $s_1^2, s_2^2$. Assuming **equal population variances** ($\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = \sigma^2$), the pooled estimate of $\sigma^2$ is the weighted average of the two sample variances:

$$S_p^2 = \frac{(n_1 - 1)s_1^2 + (n_2 - 1)s_2^2}{(n_1 - 1) + (n_2 - 1)} = \frac{(n_1 - 1)s_1^2 + (n_2 - 1)s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}$$

Therefore:

$$S_p = \sqrt{\frac{(n_1 - 1)s_1^2 + (n_2 - 1)s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the standard error of the difference of means:**

$$SE(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) = S_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}}$$

**Step 4 — Form the t-statistic:**

$$t = \frac{(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) - (\mu_1 - \mu_2)_0}{S_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}}}$$

Under $H_0$, $\mu_1 - \mu_2 = 0$, simplifying to:

$$\boxed{t = \frac{\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2}{S_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}}}}$$

**Step 5 — Degrees of freedom:**
$$df = n_1 + n_2 - 2$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule (two-tailed at $\alpha = 0.05$):**
Reject $H_0$ if $\vert t \vert > t_{0.025,\, df}$. Otherwise, conclude that the observed difference is statistically insignificant.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: One-Way ANOVA F-Statistic

**Step 1 — State hypotheses:**
$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \ldots = \mu_k \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \text{At least one } \mu_i \text{ differs}$$

**Step 2 — Partition the total variability (Total Sum of Squares):**

$$SS_{\text{Total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$$

This measures the total variation of all observations around the grand mean $\bar{Y}_{..}$.

**Step 3 — Partition into Treatment and Error components:**

$$SS_{\text{Total}} = SS_{\text{Treatment}} + SS_{\text{Error}}$$

where:

$$SS_{\text{Treatment}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$$

This captures variation **between** group means (effect of treatment).

$$SS_{\text{Error}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i}(Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i.})^2$$

This captures variation **within** groups (random noise).

**Step 4 — Convert to Mean Squares by dividing by degrees of freedom:**

$$MS_{\text{Treatment}} = \frac{SS_{\text{Treatment}}}{k - 1}$$

$$MS_{\text{Error}} = \frac{SS_{\text{Error}}}{N - k}, \quad \text{where } N = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i$$

**Step 5 — Form the F-ratio:**

$$\boxed{F = \frac{MS_{\text{Treatment}}}{MS_{\text{Error}}}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
- A large F value means the between-group variability is much larger than the within-group variability, suggesting at least one group mean is significantly different.
- Reject $H_0$ if $F > F_{\alpha,\, k-1,\, N-k}$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Simple Linear Regression & Hypothesis Test on Slope

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A Six Sigma Black Belt suspects that **oven temperature (X, in °C)** drives **cake hardness (Y, in Newtons)**. The team collects 6 paired observations:

| $i$ | $X_i$ (°C) | $Y_i$ (N) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 150 | 12 |
| 2 | 160 | 15 |
| 3 | 170 | 17 |
| 4 | 180 | 20 |
| 5 | 190 | 22 |
| 6 | 200 | 25 |

**Step 1 — Compute the means:**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{150+160+170+180+190+200}{6} = \frac{1050}{6} = 175 \, °C$$

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{12+15+17+20+22+25}{6} = \frac{111}{6} = 18.5 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sums of squares and cross-products:**

$$\sum(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y}) = (-25)(-6.5) + (-15)(-3.5) + (-5)(-1.5) + (5)(1.5) + (15)(3.5) + (25)(6.5)$$

$$= 162.5 + 52.5 + 7.5 + 7.5 + 52.5 + 162.5 = 445.0$$

$$\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2 = 625 + 225 + 25 + 25 + 225 + 625 = 1750$$

**Step 3 — Compute the slope:**

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{445.0}{1750} = 0.2543 \, \text{N/°C}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the intercept:**

$$\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X} = 18.5 - (0.2543)(175) = 18.5 - 44.5 = -26.0 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Final Regression Equation:**

$$\boxed{\hat{Y} = -26.0 + 0.2543 \, X}$$

**Step 6 — Test the Significance of Slope (t-test on $\hat{\beta}_1$):**

First compute $SS_{\text{Res}}$ and the standard error of slope. Suppose after computing residuals:

$$SS_{\text{Res}} = 0.785, \quad s^2 = \frac{SS_{\text{Res}}}{n - 2} = \frac{0.785}{4} = 0.1963$$

$$SE(\hat{\beta}_1) = \sqrt{\frac{s^2}{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1963}{1750}} = 0.0106$$

$$t_{\text{slope}} = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1}{SE(\hat{\beta}_1)} = \frac{0.2543}{0.0106} \approx 23.99$$

Critical value $t_{0.025,\, 4} = 2.776$. Since $23.99 \gg 2.776$, **reject $H_0$** with extremely high confidence — oven temperature is a statistically significant driver of cake hardness.

**Step 7 — Compute $R^2$:**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{\text{Res}}}{SS_{\text{Tot}}} = 1 - \frac{0.785}{SS_{\text{Tot}}}$$

If $SS_{\text{Tot}} = 113.5$, then $R^2 = 1 - 0.0069 = 0.9931$. **Temperature explains 99.31% of the variation in hardness** — a near-perfect linear relationship.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Chi-Square Test of Independence

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A Six Sigma Green Belt wants to know if **shift (Day vs. Night)** is independent of **defect type (Type A vs. Type B)** in a manufacturing cell.

Observed counts (in a 2×2 contingency table):

|  | Type A | Type B | Row Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Day** | 30 | 20 | 50 |
| **Night** | 15 | 35 | 50 |
| **Column Total** | 45 | 55 | 100 |

**Step 1 — State hypotheses:**
$$H_0: \text{Shift and defect type are independent} \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \text{They are dependent}$$

**Step 2 — Compute expected counts:** $E_{ij} = \dfrac{(\text{Row } i \text{ Total})(\text{Column } j \text{ Total})}{N}$

$$E_{\text{Day, A}} = \frac{50 \times 45}{100} = 22.5$$

$$E_{\text{Day, B}} = \frac{50 \times 55}{100} = 27.5$$

$$E_{\text{Night, A}} = \frac{50 \times 45}{100} = 22.5$$

$$E_{\text{Night, B}} = \frac{50 \times 55}{100} = 27.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute chi-square statistic:**

$$\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}$$

$$\chi^2 = \frac{(30-22.5)^2}{22.5} + \frac{(20-27.5)^2}{27.5} + \frac{(15-22.5)^2}{22.5} + \frac{(35-27.5)^2}{27.5}$$

$$\chi^2 = \frac{56.25}{22.5} + \frac{56.25}{27.5} + \frac{56.25}{22.5} + \frac{56.25}{27.5}$$

$$\chi^2 = 2.500 + 2.045 + 2.500 + 2.045 = 9.090$$

**Step 4 — Determine degrees of freedom:**

$$df = (r - 1)(c - 1) = (2 - 1)(2 - 1) = 1$$

**Step 5 — Decision:** Critical value $\chi^2_{0.05, 1} = 3.841$. Since $9.090 > 3.841$, **reject $H_0$** — shift and defect type are significantly related.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The DMAIC Roadmap with Analyse Phase Detail

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Project Initiated: Customer Complaint] --> nodeD[Define Phase]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Project Charter + SIPOC]
    nodeD1 --> nodeM[Measure Phase]
    nodeM --> nodeM1[Baseline Cpk, DPMO, MSA]
    nodeM1 --> nodeA[Analyse Phase]
    nodeA --> nodeA1[Cause-Effect Fishbone]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2[5 Whys Drill-Down]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Pareto: Vital Few X's]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Hypothesis Testing: t, ANOVA, Chi-Sq]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5[Regression f x = Y Model]
    nodeA5 --> nodeI[Improve Phase]
    nodeI --> nodeI1[DOE + Pilot Solution]
    nodeI1 --> nodeC[Control Phase]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[SPC + Control Plan]
    nodeC1 --> nodeEnd[Defect Reduced to 3.4 DPMO]
```

---

### 4.2 Hypothesis Testing Decision Logic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart1[State Hypothesis H0 and H1] --> nodeStart2[Choose Significance Level alpha]
    nodeStart2 --> nodeStart3[Select Appropriate Test]
    nodeStart3 --> nodeQ1{Is Y continuous?}
    nodeQ1 -- YES --> nodeQ2{Number of groups?}
    nodeQ2 -- 1 group --> nodeT1[One-Sample t Test]
    nodeQ2 -- 2 groups --> nodeQ3{Paired?}
    nodeQ3 -- YES --> nodeT2[Paired t Test]
    nodeQ3 -- NO --> nodeT3[Two-Sample t Test]
    nodeQ2 -- 3 or more --> nodeT4[One Way ANOVA]
    nodeQ1 -- NO --> nodeQ4{Categorical or proportion?}
    nodeQ4 -- Categorical --> nodeT5[Chi Square Test]
    nodeQ4 -- 2 Proportions --> nodeT6[2 Proportion Z Test]
    nodeT1 --> nodeD1[Compute Test Statistic]
    nodeT2 --> nodeD1
    nodeT3 --> nodeD1
    nodeT4 --> nodeD1
    nodeT5 --> nodeD1
    nodeT6 --> nodeD1
    nodeD1 --> nodeD2{Is p value less than alpha?}
    nodeD2 -- YES --> nodeD3[Reject H0: Significant Effect]
    nodeD2 -- NO --> nodeD4[Fail to Reject H0: Insufficient Evidence]
```

---

### 4.3 Ishikawa Fishbone — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeY[CTQ Y: Excessive Defect Rate] --> nodeMan[Man: Operator Skill]
    nodeY --> nodeMach[Machine: Equipment Calibration]
    nodeY --> nodeMat[Material: Raw Input Quality]
    nodeY --> nodeMeth[Method: SOP Adherence]
    nodeY --> nodeMeas[Measurement: Gauge R and R]
    nodeY --> nodeEnv[Mother Nature: Ambient Temperature]
    nodeMan --> nodeManA[Inadequate Training]
    nodeMan --> nodeManB[Fatigue from Long Shifts]
    nodeMach --> nodeMachA[Drift in Pressure Sensor]
    nodeMach --> nodeMachB[Unscheduled Maintenance Overdue]
    nodeMat --> nodeMatA[Supplier Variability]
    nodeMat --> nodeMatB[Storage Humidity]
    nodeMeth --> nodeMethA[Outdated Work Instruction]
    nodeMeth --> nodeMethB[Missing Process Step]
    nodeMeas --> nodeMeasA[Uncalibrated Gauges]
    nodeMeas --> nodeMeasB[Operator Reading Bias]
    nodeEnv --> nodeEnvA[Seasonal Humidity]
    nodeEnv --> nodeEnvB[Power Fluctuation]
```

---

### 4.4 Pareto Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP1[Collect Defect Type Frequencies] --> nodeP2[Sort in Descending Order]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Compute Cumulative Percentage]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4[Plot Bar Chart with Cumulative Curve]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5{Does cumulative reach 80 percent quickly?}
    nodeP5 -- YES --> nodeP6[Identify Vital Few Causes]
    nodeP5 -- NO --> nodeP7[Re stratify Data by Shift, Machine, or Material]
    nodeP7 --> nodeP1
    nodeP6 --> nodeP8[Focus Improvement Resources on Vital Few]
```

---

### 4.5 FMEA Risk Matrix Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeFM1[List all Failure Modes from Fishbone] --> nodeFM2[Rate S Severity 1 to 10]
    nodeFM2 --> nodeFM3[Rate O Occurrence 1 to 10]
    nodeFM3 --> nodeFM4[Rate D Detection 1 to 10]
    nodeFM4 --> nodeFM5[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    nodeFM5 --> nodeFM6{RPN greater than 100?}
    nodeFM6 -- YES --> nodeFM7[High Priority: Action Required Immediately]
    nodeFM6 -- NO --> nodeFM8{RPN between 50 and 100?}
    nodeFM8 -- YES --> nodeFM9[Medium Priority: Monitor and Plan]
    nodeFM8 -- NO --> nodeFM10[Low Priority: Document Only]
```

---

### 4.6 Regression Analysis Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR1[Identify Candidate X Variables] --> nodeR2[Collect Paired X Y Data]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3[Compute Pearson Correlation r]
    nodeR3 --> nodeR4{Absolute r greater than 0.7?}
    nodeR4 -- YES --> nodeR5[Proceed with Linear Regression]
    nodeR4 -- NO --> nodeR6[Discard X: Weak Linear Association]
    nodeR5 --> nodeR7[Estimate Beta 1 Slope and Beta 0 Intercept]
    nodeR7 --> nodeR8[Test Significance of Slope using t test]
    nodeR8 --> nodeR9{p value less than 0.05?}
    nodeR9 -- YES --> nodeR10[Confirm Validated f x equals Y Model]
    nodeR9 -- NO --> nodeR6
    nodeR10 --> nodeR11[Compute R squared to Report Explained Variance]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define the Analyse Phase in the DMAIC methodology. List any three statistical tools used in this phase.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Analyse Phase** is the third stage of the Six Sigma **DMAIC** framework. Its primary objective is to **identify, validate, and statistically confirm the root causes (X-variables)** of the defects or process variation identified during the Measure Phase. It bridges the descriptive statistics of Measure with the prescriptive solutions of Improve.

**Three statistical tools used:**

1. **Hypothesis Testing** (t-test, ANOVA, chi-square) — to validate whether each candidate cause has a statistically significant effect on the CTQ.
2. **Regression Analysis** — to quantify the functional relationship $Y = f(X)$ between outputs and validated inputs.
3. **Pareto Analysis** — to prioritize the "Vital Few" causes from the "Trivial Many" using the 80/20 rule.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing tools, 1 mark for brief explanation of any one tool.)*

---

#### **Question 2: What is the Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA? How is it computed?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is a quantitative metric in **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** used to prioritize potential failure modes for corrective action based on their risk profile.

$$\text{RPN} = \text{Severity (S)} \times \text{Occurrence (O)} \times \text{Detection (D)}$$

- **S (Severity):** Impact of the failure on the customer (1 = negligible, 10 = safety hazard).
- **O (Occurrence):** Likelihood that the failure mode will occur (1 = very rare, 10 = very frequent).
- **D (Detection):** Probability that the failure will go undetected before reaching the customer (1 = highly detectable, 10 = absolutely undetectable).

The RPN ranges from **1 to 1000**. Higher RPN values demand **immediate corrective action**.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Analyse Phase Workflow, Hypothesis Testing, and Process Capability**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five sequential steps of the Analyse Phase** in the DMAIC framework. Discuss how Fishbone diagrams, 5 Whys, and Pareto Analysis are used to narrow down potential causes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Six Sigma project is comparing the tensile strength (in MPa) of cables produced by two suppliers (Supplier A and Supplier B). The team collects the following data:

| Statistic | Supplier A | Supplier B |
|---|---|---|
| Sample size (n) | 25 | 30 |
| Sample mean ($\bar{X}$) | 510 MPa | 518 MPa |
| Sample std dev (s) | 12 MPa | 14 MPa |

Using $\alpha = 0.05$, perform a **two-sample t-test** to determine whether there is a statistically significant difference in the mean tensile strength between the two suppliers. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The Analyse Phase follows a structured 5-step workflow:

**Step 1 — Develop a List of Potential X's (Causes):**
The team uses brainstorming tools such as the **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone / Ishikawa) Diagram** organized around the 6M categories (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature). The **5 Whys** technique iteratively drills down from the symptom (defect) to the underlying root cause by asking "Why?" typically five times.

**Step 2 — Narrow the List Using Pareto Analysis:**
Apply the **80/20 Rule** — roughly 80% of the problems are caused by 20% of the causes. Plot a Pareto chart of all potential X's to separate the **Vital Few** from the **Trivial Many**.

**Step 3 — Collect Stratified Data on Vital Few X's:**
Design a sampling plan (often via **stratification** by shift, machine, or material lot) to gather high-quality data on the prioritized causes.

**Step 4 — Apply Statistical Hypothesis Testing:**
Use formal tests:
- **t-test:** Compare means of two groups.
- **ANOVA:** Compare means of three or more groups.
- **Chi-square:** Test independence of categorical variables.
- **Regression:** Quantify cause-effect relationships.

**Step 5 — Establish the Validated $f(x) = Y$ Model:**
Build a regression equation. Validate via $R^2$, residual analysis, and slope significance testing. The output is a mathematical model that the Improve Phase will manipulate.

*(Award 1–2 marks per step with at least one tool elaborated per step for full marks.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks (Two-Sample t-Test Computation)**

**Step 1 — State hypotheses:**

$$H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu_A \neq \mu_B$$

**Step 2 — Compute the pooled standard deviation:**

$$S_p = \sqrt{\frac{(n_A - 1)s_A^2 + (n_B - 1)s_B^2}{n_A + n_B - 2}}$$

$$S_p = \sqrt{\frac{(25-1)(12)^2 + (30-1)(14)^2}{25+30-2}}$$

$$S_p = \sqrt{\frac{(24)(144) + (29)(196)}{53}}$$

$$S_p = \sqrt{\frac{3456 + 5684}{53}} = \sqrt{\frac{9140}{53}} = \sqrt{172.45} \approx 13.13 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the standard error of difference:**

$$SE = S_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_A} + \frac{1}{n_B}} = 13.13 \sqrt{\frac{1}{25} + \frac{1}{30}}$$

$$SE = 13.13 \sqrt{0.0400 + 0.0333} = 13.13 \sqrt{0.0733} \approx 13.13 \times 0.2708 \approx 3.555 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the t-statistic:**

$$t = \frac{\bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B}{SE} = \frac{510 - 518}{3.555} = \frac{-8}{3.555} \approx -2.250$$

**Step 5 — Determine degrees of freedom and critical value:**

$$df = n_A + n_B - 2 = 25 + 30 - 2 = 53$$

For two-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$, critical value: $t_{0.025,\, 53} \approx 2.006$.

**Step 6 — Decision:**

Since $\vert t \vert = 2.250 > 2.006$, we **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** At the 95% confidence level, there is a statistically significant difference in the mean tensile strength between cables from Supplier A and Supplier B. Supplier B produces cables with a measurably higher mean tensile strength.

*(Award 1 mark per step including final conclusion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): FMEA, Regression, and Chi-Square**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** A Six Sigma team is conducting a **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** for an automotive brake-disc manufacturing process. Compute the **RPN** for the following failure modes and rank them in order of priority for corrective action:

| Failure Mode | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Disc surface cracks | 9 | 5 | 4 |
| Improper thickness | 7 | 6 | 5 |
| Corrosion pitting | 8 | 3 | 7 |
| Off-center bore | 6 | 4 | 3 |

Identify the highest-priority failure mode. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A simple linear regression of **battery life (Y, in hours)** versus **charging temperature (X, in °C)** yields the following results from 10 observations:

$$\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2 = 250, \quad \sum(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y}) = 400$$

$$\bar{X} = 30 \, °C, \quad \bar{Y} = 15 \, \text{hours}, \quad SS_{\text{Res}} = 8.0$$

**Estimate the regression equation** and **test the significance of the slope** at $\alpha = 0.05$ (use $t_{0.025, 8} = 2.306$). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Compute RPN for each failure mode** using $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$:

| Failure Mode | S | O | D | RPN | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Disc surface cracks | 9 | 5 | 4 | **180** | 2 |
| Improper thickness | 7 | 6 | 5 | **210** | **1 (Highest Priority)** |
| Corrosion pitting | 8 | 3 | 7 | **168** | 3 |
| Off-center bore | 6 | 4 | 3 | **72** | 4 |

**Conclusion:** *Improper thickness* of the brake disc has the **highest RPN of 210**, making it the highest-priority failure mode requiring immediate corrective action in the Improve Phase.

*(Award 1.5 marks per row computation, 1 mark for ranking, 0.5 marks for conclusion.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute the slope $\hat{\beta}_1$:**

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2} = \frac{400}{250} = 1.6 \, \text{hours/°C}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the intercept $\hat{\beta}_0$:**

$$\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X} = 15 - (1.6)(30) = 15 - 48 = -33.0 \, \text{hours}$$

**Step 3 — Regression Equation:**

$$\boxed{\hat{Y} = -33.0 + 1.6X}$$

**Step 4 — Standard error of slope:**

$$s^2 = \frac{SS_{\text{Res}}}{n - 2} = \frac{8.0}{10 - 2} = 1.0$$

$$SE(\hat{\beta}_1) = \sqrt{\frac{s^2}{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0}{250}} = \sqrt{0.004} = 0.0632$$

**Step 5 — t-statistic for slope:**

$$t = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1}{SE(\hat{\beta}_1)} = \frac{1.6}{0.0632} \approx 25.32$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**
Since $\vert t \vert = 25.32 \gg t_{0.025, 8} = 2.306$, **reject $H_0$**.

**Conclusion:** Charging temperature is a **highly statistically significant predictor** of battery life. Each 1°C increase in charging temperature is associated with a 1.6-hour increase in battery life within the observed range.

*(Award 1 mark per step with proper conclusion for full marks.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the pooled variance formula structure** — many students confuse the denominator $n_1 + n_2 - 2$ with $n_1 + n_2$ (loses 2 marks).
> 2. **Using Z-table instead of t-table** when $\sigma$ is unknown — only use Z when population standard deviation is given; otherwise always t with $df = n - 1$.
> 3. **Misinterpreting "Fail to Reject" as "Accept"** — we never *accept* $H_0$; we only fail to reject it due to insufficient evidence.
> 4. **Confusing Type I and Type II errors** — Type I (α) = rejecting a true $H_0$; Type II (β) = failing to reject a false $H_0$. Many students reverse these.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify degrees of freedom** in critical value lookups — losing 1 mark per occurrence.
> 6. **In FMEA, rating incorrectly** — Severity is about impact on customer, Occurrence is about frequency of the failure mode itself, and Detection is about likelihood of CATCHING the failure mode *before* it reaches the customer. Mixing these up gives nonsensical RPN.
> 7. **RPN calculation errors** — multiplication must be done in order and verified; rushing causes arithmetic mistakes.
> 8. **In regression, confusing $\beta_0$ (intercept) with $\bar{Y}$ (mean of Y)** — they are equal only when the regression line passes through the mean (which it always does, but the intercept $\beta_0$ is NOT equal to $\bar{Y}$ in general).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Analyse Phase**
>
> **Core Definition:**
> - Analyse Phase is the **third stage of DMAIC** where validated root causes are statistically identified.
> - **Central question:** *"Why is the defect happening?"*
>
> **Brainstorming & Prioritization Tools:**
> - **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** — 6M / 8P categories.
> - **5 Whys** — iterative drill-down from symptom to root cause.
> - **Pareto Analysis** — 80/20 rule, separates Vital Few from Trivial Many.
> - **FMEA** — RPN = $S \times O \times D$, range 1 to 1000.
>
> **Statistical Hypothesis Testing:**
> - **One-sample t-test** — compare group mean to target.
> - **Two-sample t-test** — compare two independent groups (pooled variance formula).
> - **Paired t-test** — compare two related measurements.
> - **ANOVA** — compare 3+ groups (F-ratio = MS_Treatment / MS_Error).
> - **Chi-square** — categorical / contingency tests.
> - **Regression** — quantify $Y = f(X)$ relationship; test $\hat{\beta}_1$ via t-test.
> - **Significance level:** $\alpha = 0.05$ is the KTU default.
> - **Decision rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $p$-value $< \alpha$ or $\vert \text{test statistic} \vert > \text{critical value}$.
>
> **Process Capability (sometimes re-tested in Analyse context):**
> - $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ (does not account for centering).
> - $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ (accounts for both spread and centering).
> - $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ acceptable; $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ Six Sigma target.
> - **Six Sigma metric:** 3.4 DPMO with $1.5\sigma$ shift.
>
> **Key Distinctions to Memorize:**
> - **Type I error (α)** vs **Type II error (β)** — never confuse them.
> - **$C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$** — former ignores centering, latter does not.
> - **Vital Few vs Trivial Many** — Pareto distinguishes them.
> - **Continuous vs Discrete Y** — determines which test to choose.
> - **Reject vs Fail to Reject $H_0$** — we never "accept" $H_0$.
>
> **Output of Analyse Phase:**
> - A **statistically validated list of root causes** (Vital Few X's).
> - A **regression model** $\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 X_1 + \hat{\beta}_2 X_2 + \ldots$
> - **RPN-ranked FMEA** identifying high-priority failure modes.
>
> **Bridge to Improve Phase:**
> - Without a validated list of root causes, any improvement is guesswork. The Analyse Phase provides the scientific foundation for the Improve Phase.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Phase
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Control Phase ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Control Phase** is the fifth and final stage of the **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) methodology in Six Sigma. It is the formal implementation and institutionalization stage where the validated solutions from the Improve phase are deployed to the entire process, standardized through documentation, and locked in via statistical and procedural controls to prevent regression to the pre-improvement state. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Control Phase is the phase of DMAIC where the project team formalizes the improvements, transfers ownership to the process owner, and establishes a **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** framework to monitor, sustain, and continuously improve the gains achieved. The core objectives of the Control Phase are: 1. **Standardization** of the improved process through updated Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), work instructions, and training material. 2. **Implementation** of a measurement and monitoring system (typically SPC charts, control plans, and dashboards). 3. **Sustainment** of the financial and quality gains by transferring process ownership from the project team to the process owner. 4. **Closure** of the Six Sigma project with a Project Closure Document and lessons learned. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Doctor's Aftercare Prescription** > Imagine you visit a doctor for a severe headache. The doctor prescribes medicine (the *Improve* phase solution). After a week, the headache is gone. Now, the doctor writes a prescription for daily exercise, a fixed sleep schedule, and a monthly blood-pressure check-up. If you follow this *control plan*, the headache stays away. If you ignore it, the headache returns within a month. The **Control Phase** is exactly this *aftercare prescription* for a process — it ensures the "cure" (improvement) sticks permanently. In engineering terms, the Control Phase ensures that the **Sigma Level** achieved during the Improve phase does not degrade back toward the baseline. Without controls, processes tend to drift due to material variations, operator changes, machine wear, and environmental shifts. ### 1.3 Key Metrics Standard to the Control Phase - **Process Capability Indices:** $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, $P_{pk}$ (post-implementation verification). - **Control Chart Limits:** **UCL** (Upper Control Limit), **LCL** (Lower Control Limit), **CL** (Center Line). - **Standard Control Limits (3-Sigma):** $UCL = \mu + 3\sigma$, $LCL = \mu - 3\sigma$. - **Process Performance Metrics:** **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities), **RTY** (Rolled Throughput Yield), **Sigma Level** ($Z$-score). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Control Phase The Control Phase operates on a structured four-pillar architecture. Each pillar addresses a specific dimension of process sustainment. #### Pillar 1: Statistical Process Control (SPC) SPC is the mathematical backbone of the Control Phase. It uses control charts to distinguish between two types of process variation: - **Common Cause Variation:** Inherent, random variation present in a stable process. It is predictable and addressed only by changing the process itself. - **Special Cause Variation:** Unpredictable, assignable variation caused by external factors (broken tool, untrained operator). It is identified by points falling outside control limits or violating Western Electric / Nelson Rules. **Operational Logic:** 1. Collect real-time data on critical-to-quality (CTQ) parameters. 2. Plot data points sequentially on a control chart. 3. Calculate the center line as the mean ($\bar{X}$) of the data. 4. Compute control limits using the sample standard deviation and a multiplier (typically $A_2$ for $\bar{X}$ charts). 5. Monitor for out-of-control signals and trigger a corrective action plan when detected. #### Pillar 2: Control Plan A **Control Plan** is a living document (typically a matrix) that specifies: - **What** to control (CTQ characteristics). - **How** to control (measurement system, gauge). - **When** to control (sampling frequency, trigger conditions). - **Who** owns the control (process owner). - **What action** to take on out-of-control conditions (reaction plan). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** A Control Plan is NOT the same as an FMEA. An FMEA is a *risk assessment* tool used in the Analyze phase; a Control Plan is a *sustainment* document created in the Control phase. #### Pillar 3: Documentation & SOP Standardization Every improvement must be codified into: - Updated **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**. - **Training matrices** to certify operators on the new process. - **Visual work instructions** at the workstation. - Updated **PFMEA** (Process Failure Mode and Effects Analysis). #### Pillar 4: Project Closure & Handover The project is formally closed via a **Project Closure Document** containing: - Problem statement, baseline, goal, and final achieved metrics. - Financial savings validation (hard vs. soft savings). - Lessons learned and knowledge transfer to the process owner. - Updated **Process Map**, **SIPOC**, and **Value Stream Map**. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application | |---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------------| | 1 | 3-Sigma Control Limits | $UCL = \mu + 3\sigma$, $LCL = \mu - 3\sigma$ | $\mu$: process mean; $\sigma$: process std dev | All Shewhart charts | | 2 | $\bar{X}$ Chart Control Limits | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$
$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}}$: grand mean; $\bar{R}$: avg range; $A_2$: constant (depends on $n$) | Variable data, subgroups | | 3 | $R$ Chart Control Limits | $UCL_R = D_4 \cdot \bar{R}$
$LCL_R = D_3 \cdot \bar{R}$ | $D_3$, $D_4$: tabulated constants based on $n$ | Monitoring variability | | 4 | Process Capability ($C_p$) | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $USL$, $LSL$: spec limits; $\sigma$: std dev | Process potential | | 5 | Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\mu$: process mean | Centering assessment | | 6 | Six-Sigma Conversion | $\text{Sigma Level} = Z + 1.5$ | $Z$: short-term sigma; $1.5$: shift allowance | $Z$-bench to yield | | 7 | DPMO | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times N}$ | $D$: defects; $U$: opportunities/unit; $N$: units | Quality metric | | 8 | Rolled Throughput Yield | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | $Y_i$: yield of step $i$ | End-to-end yield | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note on Constants:** $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ values are tabulated for subgroup sizes $n = 2$ to $n = 10$. For $n = 5$: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$. For $n = 2$: $A_2 = 1.880$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 3.267$. Always verify $n$ before applying constants. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Control Phase is the *guarantee phase* of any continuous improvement initiative. In manufacturing, it prevents billion-dollar defects in automotive and semiconductor industries by ensuring long-term process stability. In healthcare, it underpins patient safety systems by maintaining surgical checklists and infection-control protocols. In software engineering, it manifests as **Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD)** pipelines and monitoring dashboards, ensuring code quality remains within defined service-level agreements (SLAs). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The 3-Sigma Control Limit Logic The 3-sigma control limit is derived from the **Empirical Rule** of the Normal Distribution and the principle of **minimum economic loss**. **Step 1: Foundation in the Normal Distribution** For any normally distributed variable $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, the probability density is: $$ f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) $$ The cumulative distribution function gives the probability that $X$ falls within a given range. **Step 2: Probability Coverage of 3-Sigma** The proportion of observations within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ is: $$ P(\mu - 3\sigma \le X \le \mu + 3\sigma) = \int_{\mu-3\sigma}^{\mu+3\sigma} f(x)\, dx $$ By symmetry of the Normal curve: $$ P(\mu - 3\sigma \le X \le \mu + 3\sigma) = 2 \cdot \Phi(3) - 1 $$ **Step 3: Standard Normal Lookup** The standard normal table gives $\Phi(3) \approx 0.99865$. Therefore: $$ P(\mu - 3\sigma \le X \le \mu + 3\sigma) = 2(0.99865) - 1 = 0.9973 $$ **Step 4: Economic Justification** This means **99.73%** of common-cause variation lies within 3-sigma. Only **0.27%** (about 1 in 370 points) lies outside — making any point outside a strong, statistically valid signal of a special cause. This trade-off between false alarms and missed detections is economically optimal for most processes. **Step 5: Definition of Control Limits** $$ \begin{aligned} UCL &= \mu + 3\sigma \\ CL &= \mu \\ LCL &= \mu - 3\sigma \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem: $\bar{X}$ and $R$ Chart Construction > **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam - May 2023]:** > A Six Sigma team monitors the torque (in N·m) of a critical bolt in subgroups of size $n = 5$. Twenty subgroups yield a grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}} = 52.0$ N·m and an average range $\bar{R} = 2.4$ N·m. Construct the $\bar{X}$ and $R$ charts. Using $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$. **Solution:** **Step 1: Identify Control Chart Constants for $n = 5$** From standard SPC tables: $$ A_2 = 0.577, \quad D_3 = 0, \quad D_4 = 2.114 $$ **Step 2: Compute $\bar{X}$ Chart Limits** $$ \begin{aligned} UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R} \\ &= 52.0 + (0.577)(2.4) \\ &= 52.0 + 1.3848 \\ &= 53.385 \text{ N·m} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} LCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R} \\ &= 52.0 - (0.577)(2.4) \\ &= 52.0 - 1.3848 \\ &= 50.615 \text{ N·m} \end{aligned} $$ $$ CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} = 52.0 \text{ N·m} $$ **Step 3: Compute $R$ Chart Limits** $$ \begin{aligned} UCL_R &= D_4 \cdot \bar{R} = (2.114)(2.4) = 5.0736 \text{ N·m} \\ CL_R &= \bar{R} = 2.4 \text{ N·m} \\ LCL_R &= D_3 \cdot \bar{R} = (0)(2.4) = 0 \text{ N·m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Interpretation** If any subgroup mean falls outside $[50.615,\ 53.385]$, the process mean is out of control. If any subgroup range exceeds $5.074$, the process variability has increased. The process owner must invoke the **Reaction Plan** from the Control Plan. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Control Chart | Data Type | Subgroup Size | Chart Type | Center Line | Control Limit Basis | |-----------|---------------|------------|-------------|---------------------| | Variable (continuous) | $n > 1$ | $\bar{X}$ & $R$ | $\bar{\bar{X}}$, $\bar{R}$ | $A_2, D_3, D_4$ constants | | Variable (continuous) | $n > 10$ or variable | $\bar{X}$ & $S$ | $\bar{\bar{X}}$, $\bar{S}$ | $B_3, B_4$ constants | | Variable, individuals | $n = 1$ | I-MR (Individuals & Moving Range) | $\bar{X}$, $\overline{MR}$ | $E_2 = 2.66$ constant | | Attribute (pass/fail) | Variable | $p$-chart | $\bar{p}$ | $3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$ | | Attribute (defect count) | Constant | $c$-chart | $\bar{c}$ | $3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | | Attribute (defects per unit) | Variable | $u$-chart | $\bar{u}$ | $3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n}$ | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 DMAIC Macro-Architecture with Control Phase Focus ```mermaid flowchart LR D1["DEFINE"] --> M1["MEASURE"] M1 --> A1["ANALYZE"] A1 --> I1["IMPROVE"] I1 --> C1["CONTROL"] C1 --> SP["Sustain Gains"] C1 --> MO["Monitor SPC"] C1 --> TR["Transfer Ownership"] C1 --> CL["Close Project"] SP --> RE["Continuous Improvement"] MO --> RE TR --> RE CL --> RE ``` ### 4.2 Control Phase Internal Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Validated Solution from Improve Phase"] --> S2["Pilot Implementation"] S2 --> S3{"Pilot Successful?"} S3 -- Yes --> S4["Full Roll-Out"] S3 -- No --> S5["Iterate Solution"] S5 --> S1 S4 --> S6["Develop Control Plan"] S6 --> S7["Implement SPC Charts"] S7 --> S8["Update SOPs and Training"] S8 --> S9["Validate Sigma Level"] S9 --> S10{"Capability Acceptable?"} S10 -- Yes --> S11["Project Closure and Handover"] S10 -- No --> S12["Root Cause Re-analysis"] S12 --> S5 S11 --> S13["Lessons Learned Document"] S13 --> S14["Celebrate and Recognize Team"] ``` ### 4.3 SPC Decision Flowchart for Out-of-Control Signals ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Sample Data Point Plotted"] --> B{"Outside 3 Sigma?"} B -- Yes --> F["STOP Process: Special Cause Detected"] B -- No --> C{"Western Electric Rule Triggered?"} C -- Yes --> F C -- No --> D{"Nelson Rule Triggered?"} D -- Yes --> F D -- No --> E["Continue: Common Cause Variation"] F --> G["Investigate Root Cause"] G --> H["Apply Reaction Plan"] H --> I["Document Corrective Action"] I --> J["Resume Production with Monitoring"] E --> K["Log Data Point"] ``` ### 4.4 Control Plan Structural Template ```mermaid flowchart LR CP1["PROCESS STEP"] --> CP2["CTQ CHARACTERISTIC"] CP2 --> CP3["SPECIFICATION"] CP3 --> CP4["MEASUREMENT SYSTEM"] CP4 --> CP5["SAMPLE SIZE & FREQUENCY"] CP5 --> CP6["CONTROL METHOD"] CP6 --> CP7["REACTION PLAN"] CP7 --> CP8["RESPONSIBLE OWNER"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **"List any three primary objectives of the Control Phase in DMAIC."** **Model Answer:** The three primary objectives of the Control Phase are: 1. **Standardization of the Improved Process:** Codify all improvements into updated Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), work instructions, and training material to ensure consistent execution across all shifts and operators. 2. **Implementation of Statistical Process Control (SPC):** Establish control charts on Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) parameters to detect any deviation from the improved process performance and trigger corrective action before defects occur. 3. **Sustainment of Financial and Quality Gains:** Transfer ownership of the process from the project team to the process owner, ensuring that the achieved cost savings, defect reduction, and Sigma Level improvements persist over the long term. --- #### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **"Differentiate between a Control Plan and an FMEA."** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Control Plan | FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) | |--------|--------------|------------------------------------------| | **Purpose** | Specifies how to monitor and sustain a stable, capable process | Identifies and prioritizes potential failure modes and their risks | | **Phase in DMAIC** | Created in the **Control Phase** | Primarily developed in the **Analyze/Improve Phase** | | **Focus** | Prevention of regression and out-of-control conditions | Prevention of potential failures before they occur | | **Key Output** | Reaction plan, control method, measurement frequency | RPN (Risk Priority Number) for each failure mode | | **Living Document** | Yes, continuously updated after every change | Yes, reviewed periodically and after process changes | --- ### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the Control Phase with the Improve Phase. Remember: **Improve** *develops and pilots* the solution, while **Control** *standardizes, monitors, and sustains* it. Writing pilot-testing content in a Control Phase answer will cost you 4–5 marks immediately. Also, students often forget to mention **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** explicitly — SPC is the *mathematical heart* of the Control Phase and must be cited. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **a) (7 Marks)** Explain the methodology of constructing an $\bar{X}$ and $R$ control chart. Define all constants used. **b) (7 Marks)** A Six Sigma project at a precision bearings manufacturer monitors surface roughness (in $\mu m$) in subgroups of size $n = 4$. The data from 25 subgroups give $\bar{\bar{X}} = 12.0\ \mu m$ and $\bar{R} = 1.6\ \mu m$. Given $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$, construct the $\bar{X}$ and $R$ charts and comment on the process control status. **Model Solution:** **Part (a): Methodology of $\bar{X}$ and $R$ Chart Construction** The $\bar{X}$ and $R$ charts are a paired set used for variable (continuous) data with subgroup sizes typically between $n = 2$ and $n = 10$. The $\bar{X}$ chart monitors the **process mean** (central tendency), while the $R$ chart monitors the **process variability** (spread). **Step 1: Data Collection** Collect $k$ subgroups of size $n$ each. For each subgroup, compute the mean $\bar{X}_i$ and range $R_i = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$. **Step 2: Compute Grand Mean and Average Range** $$ \bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i, \qquad \bar{R} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i $$ **Step 3: Compute Control Limits for $\bar{X}$ Chart** $$ UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}, \quad LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} $$ **Step 4: Compute Control Limits for $R$ Chart** $$ UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}, \quad CL_R = \bar{R}, \quad LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} $$ **Step 5: Definitions of Constants** - **$A_2$:** A tabulated multiplier relating average range to the standard deviation of subgroup means. Defined as $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$, where $d_2$ is the expected mean range factor for a standard normal distribution. - **$D_3$ and $D_4$:** Constants derived from the distribution of the range. $D_3 = 1 - \dfrac{3 d_3}{d_2}$ and $D_4 = 1 + \dfrac{3 d_3}{d_2}$, where $d_3$ is the standard deviation factor of the range. - These constants depend only on the subgroup size $n$ and are obtained from standard SPC reference tables. **Part (b): Chart Construction** **Given:** $n = 4$, $k = 25$, $\bar{\bar{X}} = 12.0\ \mu m$, $\bar{R} = 1.6\ \mu m$, $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$. **$\bar{X}$ Chart Limits:** $$ \begin{aligned} UCL_{\bar{X}} &= 12.0 + (0.729)(1.6) = 12.0 + 1.1664 = 13.166\ \mu m \\ CL_{\bar{X}} &= 12.0\ \mu m \\ LCL_{\bar{X}} &= 12.0 - 1.1664 = 10.834\ \mu m \end{aligned} $$ **$R$ Chart Limits:** $$ \begin{aligned} UCL_R &= (2.282)(1.6) = 3.651\ \mu m \\ CL_R &= 1.6\ \mu m \\ LCL_R &= (0)(1.6) = 0\ \mu m \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The process is in statistical control if all 25 subgroup means fall within $[10.834,\ 13.166]$ and all ranges fall within $[0,\ 3.651]$. Any point outside these limits signals a special cause requiring investigation via the Control Plan's reaction procedure. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **a) (7 Marks)** "The Control Phase is the *guarantee phase* of DMAIC." Discuss the key deliverables of the Control Phase with reference to SPC, Control Plans, and Documentation. **b) (7 Marks)** After implementing the improved process, a Six Sigma team measures $C_{pk}$ for a critical dimension. The specification limits are $USL = 10.02\ mm$ and $LSL = 9.98\ mm$. The process mean is $\mu = 9.998\ mm$ and standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.005\ mm$. Compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and interpret the process capability. **Model Solution:** **Part (a): Key Deliverables of the Control Phase** The Control Phase delivers three primary artifacts that together form the *guarantee* of long-term sustainment. **Deliverable 1: Statistical Process Control (SPC) System** SPC is the mathematical surveillance layer. It uses **Shewhart control charts** ($\bar{X}$-$R$, $I$-$MR$, $p$, $c$, $u$) to detect special-cause variation. The team establishes sampling plans, calculates control limits using 3-sigma logic, and trains operators on Western Electric Rules to identify patterns indicating instability. Without SPC, the organization reverts to inspection (after-the-fact detection) rather than prevention. **Deliverable 2: Control Plan** A Control Plan is the *operational rulebook* for sustaining gains. It is a structured table listing each process step, its CTQ characteristic, the measurement system, sampling frequency, control method (e.g., SPC chart, visual check), and most critically, the **Reaction Plan** — the exact steps to take when an out-of-control signal occurs. The Control Plan transfers accountability to the process owner. **Deliverable 3: Documentation and Standardization** All improvements are codified into updated **SOPs**, **training matrices**, and **visual work instructions**. The **PFMEA** is revised, and the **Process Map** is updated to reflect the new process architecture. This deliverable ensures institutional knowledge — not just personal expertise — sustains the gains. **Part (b): Process Capability Computation** **Given:** $USL = 10.02\ mm$, $LSL = 9.98\ mm$, $\mu = 9.998\ mm$, $\sigma = 0.005\ mm$. **Step 1: Compute $C_p$ (Process Potential)** $$ C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{10.02 - 9.98}{6 \times 0.005} = \frac{0.04}{0.030} = 1.333 $$ **Step 2: Compute $C_{pk}$ (Process Capability Index)** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{pk,\ upper} &= \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{10.02 - 9.998}{3 \times 0.005} = \frac{0.022}{0.015} = 1.467 \\ C_{pk,\ lower} &= \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{9.998 - 9.98}{3 \times 0.005} = \frac{0.018}{0.015} = 1.200 \end{aligned} $$ $$ C_{pk} = \min(1.467,\ 1.200) = 1.200 $$ **Step 3: Interpretation** - **$C_p = 1.333 > 1.33$:** The process *spread* is acceptable relative to specification width; the process *could* be capable if centered. - **$C_{pk} = 1.20 < 1.33$:** The process is **off-center**. Specifically, it is drifting toward the lower specification limit ($9.98$ mm). - **Action:** Re-center the process mean toward the midpoint $\left(\dfrac{USL + LSL}{2} = 10.00\ mm\right)$. This requires a target adjustment of approximately $+0.002$ mm. - **Six Sigma Standard:** For a mature Six Sigma process, $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$ is desired. The current value of $1.20$ indicates room for further improvement before the project is truly closed. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Control Phase:** - The Control Phase is the **fifth and final** stage of DMAIC, focused on *sustainment*, not *improvement*. - The **3-Sigma rule** gives control limits $UCL = \mu + 3\sigma$ and $LCL = \mu - 3\sigma$, capturing **99.73%** of common-cause variation. - For an **$\bar{X}$ chart**, use $A_2 \bar{R}$; for an **$R$ chart**, use $D_3 \bar{R}$ and $D_4 \bar{R}$. - **$C_p$** measures spread; **$C_{pk}$** measures spread **and centering**. $C_{pk} \le C_p$ always. - A **Control Plan** lists: *What, How, When, Who, and What-if* for every CTQ. - **SPC** is the statistical heart of the Control Phase; **Control Plans** are its operational backbone. - **$C_{pk} \ge 1.67$** is the Six Sigma benchmark for a world-class process. - **DPMO** = $\dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times N}$; **Sigma Level** = $Z + 1.5$ (with 1.5-sigma shift). - The project is **closed** only after a Project Closure Document, lessons learned, and ownership transfer. - **Pitfall to avoid:** Confusing the Control Phase with the Improve Phase. Control = monitor & sustain; Improve = develop & pilot.

topics.title: Case Examples of DMAIC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Case Examples of DMAIC ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of DMAIC Case Examples A **DMAIC Case Example** is a real-world or simulated business scenario that walks through the five structured phases of the Six Sigma problem-solving methodology—**Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, and Control**—to illustrate how statistical tools, lean principles, and structured project management converge to resolve chronic quality, cost, or delivery problems. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)** > Case examples are NOT optional reading. KTU examiners frequently ask students to: > - Identify which DMAIC phase a specific tool belongs to. > - Sequence tools correctly within the DMAIC roadmap. > - Justify the selection of a tool for a given problem statement.
