The five phases correspond to a logical funnel that narrows a broad business problem down to verified root causes, validated countermeasures, and embedded controls. | Phase | Purpose | Key Question Answered | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **D – Define** | Frame the project, customer, and scope | *What is the problem and who cares?* | | **M – Measure** | Quantify the current state with data | *How bad is it and how do we know?* | | **A – Analyze** | Identify verified root causes | *Why is it happening?* | | **I – Improve** | Pilot and validate solutions | *What fixes it permanently?* | | **C – Control** | Standardise and sustain gains | *How do we keep the gains?* | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Patient Mechanic" Mental Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy: Visiting a Doctor for Chronic Back Pain** > > - **Define** — The doctor asks: *Where does it hurt? When did it start? What makes it worse?* (Project Charter, VOC, SIPOC) > - **Measure** — The doctor orders an X-ray, blood test, and measures your blood pressure. (Baseline Sigma, DPMO, CTQ data) > - **Analyze** — The doctor studies the X-ray and finds a slipped disc pressing on a nerve. (Pareto, Fishbone, Hypothesis Testing) > - **Improve** — The doctor prescribes physiotherapy, posture correction, and a targeted exercise plan. (Pilot solutions, DOE) > - **Control** — The doctor schedules a follow-up in 3 months and asks you to maintain an exercise log. (SPC charts, Control Plans) > > Just as a patient cannot be cured without each step, a process cannot be sustainably improved without traversing every DMAIC phase. --- ### 1.3 Why KTU Tests Case-Based DMAIC Problems University question papers (especially 14-mark Part B questions) typically present a **scenario paragraph** and ask students to: 1. Identify the appropriate Six Sigma tool for each phase. 2. Calculate baseline **Sigma Level** or **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)**. 3. Suggest controls to sustain improvements. > [!NOTE] > The standard metric used in Six Sigma case studies is the **Sigma Level (Z)** and the corresponding **DPMO** value. Memorising the conversion table is essential for KTU exams. | Sigma Level (Z) | DPMO | Yield (%) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 2σ | 308,537 | 69.1% | | 3σ | 66,807 | 93.3% | | 4σ | 6,210 | 99.38% | | 5σ | 233 | 99.977% | | 6σ | 3.4 | 99.9997% | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The DMAIC Phase Architecture Each phase contains a defined set of deliverables, tools, and decision gates. KTU examiners award marks for correctly mapping tools to phases. #### 2.1.1 DEFINE Phase Tools - **Project Charter** — single-page authorisation document - **VOC (Voice of the Customer)** — Kano Model, CTQ Trees - **SIPOC** — Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers - **VOC → CTQ (Critical to Quality)** translation matrix - **Problem Statement** with baseline and target #### 2.1.2 MEASURE Phase Tools - **Data Collection Plan** - **Process Mapping / Flowcharts** - **Baseline DPMO and Sigma Level calculation** - **Measurement System Analysis (MSA)** — Gage R&R - **Process Capability Indices (Cp, Cpk)** #### 2.1.3 ANALYZE Phase Tools - **Pareto Chart** (80/20 rule) - **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** — 6Ms - **5 Whys Analysis** - **Hypothesis Testing** — t-test, ANOVA, Chi-square - **Regression Analysis** - **Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** #### 2.1.4 IMPROVE Phase Tools - **Design of Experiments (DOE)** - **Pilot Runs** - **Kaizen / Poka-Yoke (Mistake Proofing)** - **Solution Selection Matrix** - **Cost–Benefit Analysis** #### 2.1.5 CONTROL Phase Tools - **Statistical Process Control (SPC) — X-bar & R charts** - **Control Plans** - **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** - **Mistake-Proofing Verification** - **Project Closure & Handover to Process Owner** --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Conversions & Equations) > [!IMPORTANT] > Every formula below is **exam-tested**. KTU questions often supply raw numbers (units produced, defects observed, opportunities per unit) and ask for **DPMO**, **Yield**, or **Sigma Level**. | # | Formula / Symbol | Expression | Units / Notes | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Defects** | Number of non-conformities counted | Count | | 2 | **Opportunities per Unit (O)** | Number of ways a defect can occur per unit | Count | | 3 | **Total Opportunities (TO)** | $\text{Units} \times \text{O}$ | Count | | 4 | **DPU (Defects Per Unit)** | $\text{DPU} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units}}$ | Defects/unit | | 5 | **DPO (Defects Per Opportunity)** | $\text{DPO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{O}} = \dfrac{\text{DPU}}{\text{O}}$ | Defects/opportunity | | 6 | **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \text{DPO} \times 1{,}000{,}000$ | Defects/million opportunities | | 7 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$ | Fraction (0–1) | | 8 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | Fraction (0–1) | | 9 | **Process Capability Index (Cp)** | $\text{Cp} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | | 10 | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $\text{Cpk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Dimensionless | | 11 | **Sigma Level (Z)** | $Z = \Phi^{-1}(1 - \text{DPO}) + 1.5$ | Sigma units (with **1.5σ shift**) | | 12 | **Six Sigma Quality Goal** | $\text{DPMO} = 3.4$ at $Z = 6$ | World-class benchmark | > [!NOTE] > **Convention for KTU exams**: Use $Z = \Phi^{-1}(1 - \text{DPO}) + 1.5$. The "**+1.5σ shift**" accounts for long-term drift in real processes. Some short-term tables omit the 1.5 shift — read the question carefully. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In manufacturing firms (e.g., automotive, electronics, pharma), DMAIC case studies: - Reduce **scrap/rework cost** (typically 5–15% of revenue) - Improve **on-time delivery** from ~85% to ~99% - Lower warranty claims by detecting root causes early In **service industries** (BPO, banking, hospitals), the same framework reduces: - Call handle time, transaction errors, patient wait time, claim processing days > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU paper setters prefer **manufacturing or service examples** with numerical data because they are easily converted into calculation questions worth 7–14 marks. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Case Study A — Automotive Brake Pad Manufacturing Plant > [!NOTE] > **Background**: An automotive Tier-1 supplier produces brake pads. The plant produces **10,000 units/day** across **3 shifts**. Recent customer audits revealed **defect rate = 8%** (i.e., 800 defects/day). Quality cost has risen to ₹1.2 crore/quarter. A Six Sigma Black Belt is assigned the DMAIC project. #### Phase D — DEFINE - **VOC/CTQ**: Customer demands *dimensional accuracy (within ±0.05 mm)*, *friction coefficient stability*, *zero surface cracks*. - **Problem Statement**: *"Reduce the brake-pad rejection rate from 8% to <1% within 6 months, saving ₹80 lakh/quarter."* - **Project Charter**: Sponsor = Plant Head, Champion = Quality Director, Team = 6 cross-functional members. - **SIPOC** constructed. #### Phase M — MEASURE Given: - Units produced per day, $U = 10{,}000$ - Defects per day, $D = 800$ - Opportunities per brake pad, $O = 4$ (dimensional, surface, hardness, friction) **Step 1 — Compute Defects Per Unit (DPU):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPU} &= \frac{D}{U} \\[4pt] &= \frac{800}{10{,}000} \\[4pt] &= 0.08 \;\text{defects/unit} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Defects Per Opportunity (DPO):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPO} &= \frac{D}{U \times O} \\[4pt] &= \frac{800}{10{,}000 \times 4} \\[4pt] &= \frac{800}{40{,}000} \\[4pt] &= 0.02 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute DPMO:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPMO} &= \text{DPO} \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt] &= 0.02 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\[4pt] &= 20{,}000 \;\text{DPMO} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compute Baseline Sigma Level (Z):** Using the standard normal inverse, for $\text{DPO} = 0.02$, $\Phi^{-1}(1 - 0.02) = \Phi^{-1}(0.98) \approx 2.054$. $$ \begin{aligned} Z &= \Phi^{-1}(1 - \text{DPO}) + 1.5 \\[4pt] &= 2.054 + 1.5 \\[4pt] &= 3.554\;\sigma \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation**: The process is operating at roughly **3.55σ** — far below world-class **6σ**. #### Phase A — ANALYZE - **Pareto Analysis** on 800 defects/day revealed: - Surface cracks: 480 (60%) - Hardness out of spec: 200 (25%) - Dimensional: 80 (10%) - Friction: 40 (5%) - **Fishbone Diagram** identified 6M root causes for surface cracks: - **Man**: Operator skill gap in mould loading - **Machine**: Hydraulic press pressure drift (mean = 178 bar vs target = 180 bar) - **Material**: Batch variability in resin supplier - **Method**: Inconsistent curing cycle time - **Measurement**: Vernier calliper not calibrated monthly - **Environment**: Ambient humidity > 65% RH - **5-Whys** on hydraulic press: pressure drift → seal wear → no preventive maintenance schedule → maintenance skipped → no PM plan. #### Phase I — IMPROVE - Introduced **poka-yoke jig** for mould loading (eliminates operator error). - Replaced worn seals and instituted **monthly PM** with check sheet. - Negotiated **incoming material certification** with resin supplier. - Installed **digital humidity controllers** in curing room. - **Pilot run of 5,000 units** showed defects reduced from 800 → 90 per day. **Post-Pilot Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{New DPU} &= \frac{90}{5{,}000} = 0.018 \\[4pt] \text{New DPO} &= \frac{90}{5{,}000 \times 4} = 0.0045 \\[4pt] \text{New DPMO} &= 0.0045 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 4{,}500 \\[4pt] Z_{\text{new}} &= \Phi^{-1}(0.9955) + 1.5 \approx 2.613 + 1.5 = 4.11\sigma \end{aligned} $$ #### Phase C — CONTROL - **SPC charts (X-bar & R)** deployed on press pressure and curing temperature. - **Control Plan** signed off by Plant Head. - **Daily quality dashboard** showing real-time DPMO. - **Operator training SOP** updated; new joiners must complete 40-hour module. > [!TIP] > **KTU Validation Tip**: Whenever you compute DPMO, immediately look up the corresponding Sigma Level. Examiners love to ask: *"If DPMO = 20,000, what is the Sigma Level?"* — answer ≈ 3.55σ. --- ### 3.2 Case Study B — Hospital Outpatient Department (OPD) Wait Time Reduction > [!NOTE] > **Background**: A 400-bed hospital OPD sees 600 patients/day. Average waiting time = **95 minutes** vs target of **30 minutes**. Patient satisfaction score = 2.8/5. The Quality Council initiates a DMAIC project. #### Phase D — DEFINE - **CTQ**: ≤ 30 min wait, ≥ 4.5/5 satisfaction. - **Problem Statement**: *"Reduce average OPD wait time from 95 min to ≤ 30 min within 4 months, while improving satisfaction score to ≥ 4.5/5."* - **SIPOC** drawn: Suppliers (registration desk, doctors, lab) → Process (registration → triage → consultation → pharmacy) → Outputs (treated patient, prescription) → Customer (patient). #### Phase M — MEASURE - **Time study** on 100 random patients showed stage-wise average times: - Registration: 18 min - Waiting for triage: 22 min - Triage: 5 min - Waiting for doctor: 35 min - Consultation: 8 min - Pharmacy: 7 min - **Total = 95 min**. - **Baseline Yield** (patients served within 30 min): 8/100 = 8% → **FPY = 0.08**. #### Phase A — ANALYZE - **Pareto**: 87% of waiting time = (waiting for triage 22 min) + (waiting for doctor 35 min). - **Fishbone (5M+E)** for doctor-wait bottleneck: - **Method**: No appointment slots; walk-in only. - **Man**: Doctors arrive late; no shift discipline. - **Machine**: Token system manual; frequent breakdowns. - **Material**: Insufficient patient files (lost/misplaced). - **Measurement**: No real-time queue visibility. - **Environment**: Single waiting hall serves 4 specialities. #### Phase I — IMPROVE - **Slot-based appointment system** (online + walk-in hybrid). - **Digital token system** with SMS alerts. - **Triage nurse station** moved closer to registration. - **Shift discipline** with biometric attendance. - **Pilot for 30 days**: average wait = **28 minutes**, satisfaction = 4.6/5. #### Phase C — CONTROL - **Daily control chart** on average wait time (target UCL = 35 min). - **SOP** for appointment scheduling. - **Monthly review** with Quality Council. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Case A (Brake Pad) | Case B (OPD Hospital) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Industry Type | Manufacturing (Discrete) | Service (Healthcare) | | Baseline DPMO / Yield | 20,000 DPMO / 92% FPY | 8% FPY (within target) | | Baseline Sigma Level | 3.55σ | N/A (time-based metric) | | Top 2 Root Causes | Press drift + Operator skill | Doctor unpunctuality + No appointments | | Primary Improve Tool | Poka-Yoke + PM schedule | Slot-based scheduling + Digital tokens | | Key Control Tool | SPC X-bar & R chart | Time control chart + SOP | | Quantified Improvement | 20,000 → 4,500 DPMO (3.55σ → 4.11σ) | 95 min → 28 min wait | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 DMAIC Phase Roadmap (Mermaid Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Project Trigger
VOC Complaint] --> B[Phase D - DEFINE
Charter, SIPOC, CTQ] B --> C[Phase M - MEASURE
Baseline DPMO, Sigma Level] C --> D[Phase A - ANALYZE
Pareto, Fishbone, 5 Whys] D --> E[Phase I - IMPROVE
Pilot, DOE, Poka-Yoke] E --> F[Phase C - CONTROL
SPC Chart, SOP, Control Plan] F --> G[Sustained Gains
Project Closure] G --> H[Process Owner
Hand-over] ``` ### 4.2 Brake-Pad Plant DMAIC Tool Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DEFINE D1[Project Charter] D2[SIPOC Diagram] D3[VOC and CTQ Tree] end subgraph MEASURE M1[Baseline DPMO = 20000] M2[MSA Gage R and R] M3[Cp Cpk Calculation] end subgraph ANALYZE A1[Pareto 80 20] A2[5 Whys Analysis] A3[Fishbone 6M] end subgraph IMPROVE I1[Poka Yoke Jig] I2[PM Schedule] I3[DOE Validation] end subgraph CONTROL C1[SPC X-bar R Chart] C2[Control Plan] C3[SOP and Training] end D1 --> M1 --> A1 --> I1 --> C1 D2 --> M2 --> A2 --> I2 --> C2 D3 --> M3 --> A3 --> I3 --> C3 ``` ### 4.3 Hospital OPD Wait-Time Reduction Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Patient Arrives] --> P2[Registration 18 min] P2 --> P3[Wait for Triage 22 min] P3 --> P4[Triage 5 min] P4 --> P5[Wait for Doctor 35 min] P5 --> P6[Consultation 8 min] P6 --> P7[Pharmacy 7 min] P7 --> P8[Exit] style P3 fill:#ffd6d6 style P5 fill:#ffd6d6 style P2 fill:#fff4cc style P6 fill:#fff4cc ``` > [!NOTE] > In the diagram above, the **red-highlighted stages (P3 and P5)** are the bottleneck stages identified by Pareto analysis. The yellow-highlighted stages are value-adding but contain minor improvement opportunities. The DMAIC project focuses 80% of its effort on P3 and P5. ### 4.4 Six Sigma Conversion Logic Block ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Data
Defects, Units, O] --> B[Compute DPU] B --> C[Compute DPO] C --> D[Compute DPMO] D --> E[Lookup Z via Inverse Normal] E --> F[Add 1.5 sigma shift] F --> G[Final Sigma Level Z] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any six tools used in the ANALYZE phase of a DMAIC project.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The six tools used in the ANALYZE phase are: 1. **Pareto Chart** – identifies the vital few causes contributing to 80% of defects. 2. **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** – categorises potential causes under 6Ms (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature). 3. **5-Whys Analysis** – iteratively drills down to root cause. 4. **Hypothesis Testing** – statistically validates suspected causes (t-test, ANOVA, Chi-square). 5. **Regression Analysis** – quantifies the relationship between variables. 6. **Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** – prioritises risks by RPN = Severity × Occurrence × Detection. --- #### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **A process produces 5,000 units with 200 defects. Each unit has 3 opportunities for a defect. Calculate the baseline DPMO and Sigma Level.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPU} &= \frac{200}{5{,}000} = 0.04 \\[4pt] \text{DPO} &= \frac{200}{5{,}000 \times 3} = \frac{200}{15{,}000} \approx 0.01333 \\[4pt] \text{DPMO} &= 0.01333 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \approx 13{,}333 \;\text{DPMO} \\[4pt] Z &= \Phi^{-1}(1 - 0.01333) + 1.5 \approx 2.21 + 1.5 = 3.71\sigma \end{aligned} $$ **Answer**: DPMO ≈ **13,333**; Sigma Level ≈ **3.71σ**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Module-4 Part-B question carries **internal choice**. Both options must be equally demanding. --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** An electronics assembly line produces 8,000 mobile chargers per day. The current rejection rate is 6%. Each charger has 5 inspection points (opportunities for defects). **Calculate the baseline DPMO and Sigma Level.** *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe how you would apply the **DMAIC methodology** to reduce the rejection rate to below 1%. Specify at least **two tools for each phase** and explain their role. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Baseline Calculation (7 marks)** - Units $U = 8{,}000$ - Defects $D = 6\% \times 8{,}000 = 480$ - Opportunities per unit $O = 5$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPU} &= \frac{480}{8{,}000} = 0.06 \\[4pt] \text{DPO} &= \frac{480}{8{,}000 \times 5} = \frac{480}{40{,}000} = 0.012 \\[4pt] \text{DPMO} &= 0.012 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 12{,}000 \;\text{DPMO} \\[4pt] Z &= \Phi^{-1}(1 - 0.012) + 1.5 = 2.257 + 1.5 = 3.76\sigma \end{aligned} $$ **Answer**: Baseline DPMO = **12,000**, Sigma Level = **3.76σ**. **(b) DMAIC Application (7 marks)** | Phase | Tools Selected | Role | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **D – Define** | Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC/CTQ Tree | Scoping: rejection rate > 1% is unacceptable; goal set at <1% with timeline. | | **M – Measure** | Baseline DPMO (above), MSA (Gage R&R), Cp/Cpk | Quantifies current process, validates measurement system. | | **A – Analyze** | Pareto, Fishbone (6M), 5-Whys | Identifies that 70% defects are soldering-related (root cause: tip temperature drift). | | **I – Improve** | DOE (soldering temperature vs defect rate), Poka-Yoke (auto-cutoff at bad temperature) | Validates optimal temperature; embeds mistake-proofing. | | **C – Control** | SPC (X-bar & R on soldering temp), Control Plan, SOP | Sustains improvement; alarms on drift. | **Quantified improvement**: Pilot showed rejection dropped from 480 → 75/day → DPMO = 1,875 → Sigma ≈ 4.42σ. --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** A bank processes **1,200 loan applications/month**. The current process has **35 errors/month** detected at audit. Each application has **8 opportunities** for a defect. **Calculate the DPMO, Rolled Throughput Yield for a 4-step sub-process** with stage yields 0.97, 0.95, 0.93, 0.99, and **interpret** whether the process is Six-Sigma capable. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss a **DMAIC case study of your choice** (manufacturing or service) showing how the IMPROVE phase led to a quantifiable sigma-level gain. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Calculations (7 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPO} &= \frac{35}{1{,}200 \times 8} = \frac{35}{9{,}600} \approx 0.003646 \\[4pt] \text{DPMO} &= 0.003646 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \approx 3{,}646 \;\text{DPMO} \\[4pt] Z &= \Phi^{-1}(1 - 0.003646) + 1.5 = 2.679 + 1.5 = 4.18\sigma \\[4pt] \text{RTY} &= 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.93 \times 0.99 \\[4pt] &= 0.9215 \times 0.9207 \\[4pt] &\approx 0.8485 \;\text{(or 84.85\%)} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation**: Process is at **4.18σ**, NOT Six-Sigma capable (target = 6σ, DPMO = 3.4). RTY of ~85% indicates significant compounded waste across stages. **(b) DMAIC Case Study — IMPROVE Phase Gains (7 marks)** A printed-circuit-board (PCB) manufacturer was at 3.4σ (DPMO = 35,000) due to solder defects. Root causes identified in ANALYZE: solder paste viscosity variation and reflow oven zone temperatures drifting. IMPROVE phase implemented: - **Solder paste viscosity controller** (closed-loop) → ±2% variation (was ±12%). - **Reflow oven DOE** to identify optimal zone profile (Zone 1 = 150°C, Zone 2 = 200°C, Zone 3 = 245°C). - **Poka-yoke fixture** preventing boards from entering oven without paste-inspection clearance. Post-improvement: DPMO dropped from 35,000 to 1,200 → Sigma jumped from **3.4σ to 4.62σ**. Cost-of-poor-quality reduced by **₹42 lakh/year**. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DMAIC Case Study Questions** > > 1. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift** — Many students write $Z = \Phi^{-1}(1 - \text{DPO})$ without adding 1.5. KTU expects the long-term formula. Lose **1 mark** if omitted. > 2. **Tool-to-Phase mismatch** — Mapping "SPC Chart" to the Improve phase (it's a Control tool) costs marks. Memorise the tool-phase table. > 3. **Confusing DPU, DPO, DPMO** — Writing "DPU = 13,333" instead of "DPMO = 13,333" is a unit error that examiners penalise. > 4. **Skipping the "Interpret" step** — KTU questions ending with *"interpret the result"* require a one-line conclusion (e.g., "Process is operating at ~3.7σ, well below Six-Sigma benchmark"). > 5. **No quantified improvement** — In 14-mark questions, you MUST show before-vs-after DPMO/Sigma numbers to score full marks on the Improve phase. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — DMAIC Case Examples** > > - **Define** = Charter + SIPOC + VOC/CTQ. > - **Measure** = DPMO + Sigma Level + Cp/Cpk + MSA. > - **Analyze** = Pareto + Fishbone + 5-Whys + Hypothesis Testing. > - **Improve** = DOE + Pilot + Poka-Yoke + Cost-Benefit. > - **Control** = SPC (X-bar & R) + Control Plan + SOP. > - Always compute **DPMO = (Defects / (Units × O)) × 10⁶**. > - Always convert DPMO → **Sigma Level using $Z = \Phi^{-1}(1 - \text{DPO}) + 1.5$**. > - **6σ benchmark = 3.4 DPMO**; **3σ = 66,807 DPMO**; **4σ = 6,210 DPMO**; **5σ = 233 DPMO**. > - In **service case studies** (hospitals, banks, BPOs), substitute **Yield or Wait Time** as the primary metric. > - **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** = product of all stage yields — useful for multi-step service processes. > - Always close the case study with a **Control Plan + SPC chart recommendation** to sustain gains. > - In exam answers, prefer **tabular presentation** for tool-phase mapping — it is examiner-friendly and saves writing time.

topics.title: Six Sigma in Manufacturing Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Six Sigma in Manufacturing Industries ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Six Sigma in Manufacturing** is a rigorous, data-driven, statistical methodology and quality management philosophy that aims to eliminate defects, reduce process variation, and improve manufacturing yield so that **no more than 3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** occur in any production process. It combines statistical process control, root-cause analysis, and disciplined project execution (DMAIC) to deliver near-perfect manufacturing output. In a manufacturing context, Six Sigma is not just a metric — it is a **project-based strategy** where cross-functional teams use the **DMAIC roadmap (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control)** to solve chronic problems such as high scrap rates, low first-pass yield, excessive rework, machine downtime, and inconsistent product quality on the shop floor. ### 1.2 Key Manufacturing Metrics (Standard KTU-Recognized Terms) | Metric | Full Form | Definition | |---|---|---| | **DPMO** | Defects Per Million Opportunities | Defects scaled to one million defect opportunities | | **DPO** | Defects Per Opportunity | Defects ÷ Opportunities | | **DPU** | Defects Per Unit | Defects ÷ Units | | **FPY** | First Pass Yield | Good units from first attempt ÷ Total units started | | **RTY** | Rolled Throughput Yield | Product of FPYs of all process steps | | **COPQ** | Cost of Poor Quality | Internal + External failure + Appraisal + Prevention costs | | **Cp / Cpk** | Process Capability Indices | Spread and centering of process vs specification limits | ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Master Baker of Manufacturing > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a master baker in a large industrial bakery who must bake **one million identical cakes** such that the weight of each cake, the sugar content, the bake time, and the icing thickness are all *within* strict customer-specified limits. A **Six Sigma baker** is so precise and so consistent that out of one million cakes, only **3 or 4** will be slightly off-spec — and even that is an upper bound. To achieve this, the baker does not just "try harder." The baker **measures everything, identifies sources of variation (oven temperature, humidity, flour batch variation, mixing speed), eliminates the controllable causes, monitors the process with control charts, and locks in the improvements.** This is exactly what Six Sigma does on a manufacturing line — it treats variation as the enemy and uses statistics as the weapon. ### 1.4 Why Six Sigma Matters in Manufacturing Industries Manufacturing is the **natural birthplace of Six Sigma** — it was invented at Motorola in the 1980s to solve semiconductor and electronics manufacturing defects. Today, Six Sigma is used across: - **Discrete manufacturing** (automotive, aerospace, electronics assembly) - **Process manufacturing** (chemicals, pharmaceuticals, food & beverage) - **Continuous flow manufacturing** (steel, paper, oil refining) The core manufacturing problems addressed by Six Sigma are: 1. **Excessive scrap and rework** (wasted material, labor, energy) 2. **Low machine efficiency (OEE losses)** due to variability 3. **Customer warranty claims** from latent defects 4. **Inconsistent product performance** across shifts, batches, suppliers 5. **Long cycle times** caused by inspection buffers and rework loops 6. **High inventory buffers** to shield downstream from upstream variation ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The DMAIC Roadmap Applied to a Manufacturing Shop Floor DMAIC is the **engine of every Six Sigma project**. In a manufacturing environment, each phase has very concrete deliverables: **D — Define Phase (Project Scoping)** - Identify the **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** characteristics of the product (e.g., diameter, hardness, surface roughness, electrical resistance). - Draft the **Project Charter** with problem statement, goal, scope, team, and timeline. - Quantify the **VOC (Voice of Customer)** in measurable engineering terms. - Calculate baseline **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)** to justify the project financially. **M — Measure Phase (Baseline & Data Integrity)** - Develop the **Operational Definition** for every defect (e.g., "a scratch > 2 mm on the visible face"). - Build the **Data Collection Plan** — what data, where, when, who, how. - Construct the **Process Map / SIPOC** (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers). - Validate the **Measurement System** using **Gage R&R** (Repeatability & Reproducibility). - Compute the **baseline DPMO, FPY, RTY, and sigma level** of the process. **A — Analyze Phase (Root Cause Identification)** - Apply **Pareto Analysis** to find the vital few defect categories. - Use **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** to brainstorm potential causes across **6M** categories: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment). - Conduct **5 Whys** analysis on top causes. - Use **statistical tools** — hypothesis tests (t-test, chi-square), regression, correlation, **ANOVA** — to separate signal from noise. - Identify **vital few root causes (Xs)** driving the **Y (defect rate / yield)**. **I — Improve Phase (Solution Piloting & Optimization)** - Generate improvement ideas for each root cause. - Use **Design of Experiments (DOE)** when multiple factors interact. - Run **pilot runs** on a small batch and statistically validate improvement. - Calculate **post-improvement DPMO** and **sigma level lift**. - Document the **cost–benefit** of each solution. **C — Control Phase (Locking the Gains)** - Implement **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charts (X-bar R, p-chart, c-chart, u-chart, EWMA). - Standardize the improved method through **SOPs, PFMEAs, and Work Instructions**. - Hand over to the **process owner** with a **Control Plan**. - Set up a **reaction plan** for out-of-control conditions. - Close the project with a **Lessons Learned document** and a **tollgate review**. ### 2.2 Lean Six Sigma Integration in Manufacturing > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In modern manufacturing, Six Sigma is rarely used alone. It is fused with **Lean Manufacturing** to form **Lean Six Sigma (LSS)**. Lean removes the **muda (waste)** — overproduction, waiting, transport, over-processing, inventory, motion, defects — while Six Sigma removes the **variation**. Together they deliver the **fastest, cheapest, most defect-free process**. The eight classical wastes of Lean (TIMWOODS + Defects) are all directly measurable in a manufacturing plant: - **T**ransport - **I**nventory - **M**otion - **W**aiting - **O**verproduction - **O**ver-processing - **D**efects - **S**kills (underutilization) ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Use `\vert` instead of `|` in any table cell that requires an absolute value, to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $DPO = \dfrac{D}{U \times OP}$ | D = Defects, U = Units, OP = Opportunities per unit | Computing defects per opportunity | | 2 | $DPMO = DPO \times 10^{6}$ | Multiply DPO by one million | Reporting standardized defect rate | | 3 | $DPMO = \dfrac{D}{U \times OP} \times 10^{6}$ | Combined form | Direct DPMO calculation | | 4 | $DPU = \dfrac{D}{U}$ | Defects per unit (multiple defects per unit allowed) | Surface defect density | | 5 | $FPY = \dfrac{U_{good}}{U_{total}}$ | Good units / Total units started | First-pass performance of a single step | | 6 | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_{i}$ | Product of FPYs of n sequential steps | End-to-end yield of a multi-step line | | 7 | $Cp = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | USL = Upper Spec Limit, LSL = Lower Spec Limit, σ = process std dev | Process spread capability | | 8 | $Cpk = \min\!\left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$ | μ = process mean | Process centering capability | | 9 | $\text{COPQ} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure | Project financial justification | | 10 | $\sigma_{LT} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{2 \times 3} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6}$ | Long-term std dev estimated from spec width | Capability benchmarking | | 11 | $Z_{LT} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Long-term Z value (one-sided) | Defect probability lookup | | 12 | $Z_{bench} = Z_{LT} + 1.5\sigma$ | Motorola 1.5σ shift (short-term to long-term) | Linking short-term sigma to long-term DPMO | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas are not academic exercises. On a production line: - A **DPMO of 5,000** is *acceptable* in many non-critical parts; a **DPMO of 233** is **4.5σ**; **DPMO of 3.4** is **6σ**. - A **Cpk of 1.00** means the process is *just capable* (marginally fits); a **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the typical Six Sigma target; **Cpk ≥ 1.67** is world-class (often called "Six Sigma capable" in centered processes). - A **RTY drop from 99% to 90%** across 5 steps means an **eightfold increase in total defects** — a critical Six Sigma insight that often shocks shop-floor managers. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — DPMO & Sigma Level Calculation in a Manufacturing Line > **Problem Statement:** A CNC turning cell produces a shaft with **5 critical quality characteristics** (diameter, length, surface finish, concentricity, hardness). Over a production run of **2,000 shafts**, the inspection team records **48 defects**. Compute: (i) DPU, (ii) DPO, (iii) DPMO, and (iv) the corresponding short-term and long-term sigma levels. **Step 1 — Identify the inputs** We are given: - Number of units produced: $U = 2000$ - Number of defects recorded: $D = 48$ - Number of opportunities per unit: $OP = 5$ (one per CTQ characteristic) **Step 2 — Compute Defects Per Unit (DPU)** The formula is: $$DPU = \frac{D}{U}$$ Substituting the values: $$DPU = \frac{48}{2000} = 0.024 \text{ defects per shaft}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** On average, every shaft leaving the cell carries **0.024 defects** — that is, roughly 1 defect for every 42 shafts. **Step 3 — Compute Defects Per Opportunity (DPO)** The formula is: $$DPO = \frac{D}{U \times OP}$$ Substituting the values: $$DPO = \frac{48}{2000 \times 5} = \frac{48}{10000} = 0.0048$$ **Engineering interpretation:** Out of every 10,000 opportunities to make a defect, the cell is creating 48 defects. This is the base rate that we will scale up. **Step 4 — Compute Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** The formula is: $$DPMO = DPO \times 10^{6}$$ Substituting: $$DPMO = 0.0048 \times 10^{6} = 4800 \text{ DPMO}$$ **Step 5 — Determine the Yield and Sigma Level** Yield in % is: $$Y_{\%} = (1 - DPO) \times 100 = (1 - 0.0048) \times 100 = 99.52\%$$ Now we use the **standard Six Sigma yield-to-sigma conversion table** (long-term, with 1.5σ shift built in): | Yield | Long-term Sigma Level | |---|---| | 99.73% | 4.0 σ | | 99.99966% | 6.0 σ | | 99.87% | 4.5 σ | | 99.52% | ≈ 4.1 σ | Reading from the standard table, a yield of **99.52%** corresponds to a **long-term sigma level of approximately 4.10 σ**. **Short-term sigma** (without the 1.5σ shift) is: $$\sigma_{ST} = \sigma_{LT} + 1.5 = 4.10 + 1.5 = 5.60 \sigma$$ > **Conclusion:** The CNC cell is currently operating at **4.1σ (long-term) / 5.6σ (short-term)**. The Six Sigma project goal will be to push this to **6σ (LT)** by reducing the 48 defects further. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) of an Assembly Line > **Problem Statement:** An assembly line has **4 sequential stations** (Stamping, Welding, Painting, Final Assembly). First-pass yields are observed as: FPY₁ = 0.97, FPY₂ = 0.95, FPY₃ = 0.93, FPY₄ = 0.98. Compute RTY, the total number of defects per 1000 raw inputs, and the equivalent process sigma level. **Step 1 — Define RTY** The formula is: $$RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_{i}$$ **Step 2 — Multiply the four FPYs** $$RTY = 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.93 \times 0.98$$ Let us multiply step by step: $$0.97 \times 0.95 = 0.9215$$ $$0.9215 \times 0.93 = 0.856995$$ $$0.856995 \times 0.98 = 0.839855$$ Therefore: $$RTY = 0.8399 \text{ or } 83.99\%$$ **Step 3 — Compute the number of good units per 1000 raw inputs** $$N_{good} = 1000 \times RTY = 1000 \times 0.8399 = 839.9 \approx 840 \text{ good units}$$ Number of failed units (need rework or are scrap): $$N_{fail} = 1000 - 840 = 160 \text{ units}$$ **Step 4 — Convert RTY to an equivalent DPMO** Assume each assembled product has **6 CTQ opportunities** (e.g., weld integrity, paint coverage, bolt torque, dimensional check, leak test, cosmetic). Then: $$DPMO = (1 - RTY) \times 10^{6} \div OP$$ $$DPMO = (1 - 0.8399) \times 10^{6} \div 6$$ $$DPMO = 0.1601 \times 10^{6} \div 6 = 160100 \div 6 = 26683.33$$ **Step 5 — Convert DPMO to a sigma level** A DPMO of **26,683** corresponds to a yield of **97.33%** and a long-term sigma level of approximately **3.6σ**. > **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Even though each individual station has an FPY of 93–98%, the **end-to-end RTY drops to 84%** because defects *compound multiplicatively*. This is the single most important reason Six Sigma projects in assembly lines focus on **every station simultaneously** rather than only the worst one. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Process Capability (Cp, Cpk) for a Machined Bore > **Problem Statement:** A CNC boring operation produces bores with specification **30.00 ± 0.05 mm** (i.e., USL = 30.05, LSL = 29.95). The process mean is observed at **μ = 30.01 mm** and the long-term standard deviation is **σ = 0.012 mm**. Compute Cp, Cpk, the defect rate, and comment on whether the process is Six Sigma capable. **Step 1 — Compute Cp (Process Spread)** $$Cp = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{30.05 - 29.95}{6 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.10}{0.072} = 1.389$$ **Step 2 — Compute Cpk (Process Centering)** $$Cpu = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{30.05 - 30.01}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.04}{0.036} = 1.111$$ $$Cpl = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{30.01 - 29.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.06}{0.036} = 1.667$$ The lower of the two values dominates: $$Cpk = \min(Cpu,\; Cpl) = \min(1.111,\; 1.667) = 1.111$$ **Step 3 — Interpret the result** | Index | Value | Interpretation | |---|---|---| | Cp | 1.389 | Spread is acceptable; process *could* fit inside the spec window | | Cpk | 1.111 | Process is **off-center** toward the USL; it is capable but not centered | | Status | Marginal | Cpk ≥ 1.33 is preferred for Six Sigma | **Step 4 — Compute expected DPMO from Z values** One-sided Z (upper): $$Z_{upper} = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{30.05 - 30.01}{0.012} = 3.333$$ One-sided Z (lower): $$Z_{lower} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma} = \frac{30.01 - 29.95}{0.012} = 5.000$$ Defect probabilities (using standard normal tail area): - Above USL: $P(Z > 3.333) \approx 0.000429$ → **429 ppm** - Below LSL: $P(Z > 5.000) \approx 0.0000003$ → **≈ 0.3 ppm** Total defects: $$DPMO \approx 429.3 \text{ defects per million opportunities}$$ Equivalent sigma level (with 1.5σ shift) ≈ **4.8σ long-term**. > **Conclusion:** The boring process is **capable in spread (Cp > 1.33)** but **not centered (Cpk = 1.11)**. The Six Sigma recommendation is to **shift the process mean closer to 30.00 mm** to bring Cpk above 1.33 and DPMO below 100. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Six Sigma Tool in Manufacturing | Manufacturing Problem | Best Six Sigma / Lean Tool | DMAIC Phase | |---|---|---| | High scrap rate in CNC machining | Pareto + Fishbone + DOE on cutting parameters | Measure / Analyze / Improve | | Excessive machine downtime | OEE analysis + TPM + Root cause analysis | Analyze / Improve | | High warranty returns | FMEA + Field data Pareto + Regression | Analyze / Improve | | Long changeover time | SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Dies) | Improve | | Variable torque on fasteners | Gage R&R + Control charts | Measure / Control | | Inconsistent weld quality | DOE on welding current / voltage / speed | Improve | | Customer complaint spike | 5 Whys + VOC CTQ tree | Define / Analyze | | Inventory pile-up between stations | Value Stream Mapping + Kanban | Define / Improve | | Paint defects (orange peel, runs) | Ishikawa + Design of Experiments | Analyze / Improve | | Supplier quality issues | Incoming SPC + Capability (Cpk) on supplier | Measure / Control | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 DMAIC Roadmap — Block-Level Functional Architecture for a Manufacturing Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Define Phase
Project Charter and CTQ] A2[Measure Phase
Baseline DPMO and FPY] A3[Analyze Phase
Root Cause Identification] A4[Improve Phase
Pilot and Validate Solutions] A5[Control Phase
SPC and Standardization] B1[Voice of Customer] B2[Data Collection Plan] B3[Pareto and Fishbone] B4[Design of Experiments] B5[Control Charts X-bar R] C1[SIPOC Map] C2[Gage R and R Study] C3[Hypothesis Testing] C4[Cost Benefit Analysis] C5[Control Plan and SOP] D1[COPQ Baseline] D2[Baseline Sigma Level] D3[Validated Root Causes] D4[Improved Sigma Level] D5[Sustained Gains] A1 --> B1 --> C1 --> D1 A2 --> B2 --> C2 --> D2 A3 --> B3 --> C3 --> D3 A4 --> B4 --> C4 --> D4 A5 --> B5 --> C5 --> D5 A1 --> A2 A2 --> A3 A3 --> A4 A4 --> A5 ``` ### 4.2 Six Sigma Defect-Reduction Feedback Loop on the Shop Floor ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Raw Material In] --> P2[Process Step 1] P2 --> P3[Process Step 2] P3 --> P4[Process Step 3] P4 --> P5[Process Step 4] P5 --> P6[Final Inspection] P6 --> P7[Customer] P6 --> Q1[Defect Data Captured] Q1 --> Q2[Pareto Analysis] Q2 --> Q3[Root Cause Analysis] Q3 --> Q4[Countermeasures] Q4 --> P2 Q4 --> P3 Q4 --> P4 Q4 --> P5 P6 --> R1[SPC Charts Active Monitoring] R1 --> R2[Out of Control Action Plan] R2 --> Q3 ``` ### 4.3 Lean Six Sigma Waste Elimination Map for a Manufacturing Cell ```mermaid flowchart TD W1[Overproduction] --> X1[Lean Pull System] W2[Waiting] --> X2[Line Balancing] W3[Transport] --> X3[Cell Layout] W4[Over Processing] --> X4[Value Stream Mapping] W5[Inventory] --> X5[Kanban and Supermarket] W6[Motion] --> X6[5S Workplace] W7[Defects] --> X7[Six Sigma DMAIC] W8[Skills] --> X8[Training and Autonomy] X7 --> Y1[Sigma Level Improvement] X1 --> Y1 X2 --> Y1 X3 --> Y1 X4 --> Y1 X5 --> Y1 X6 --> Y1 X8 --> Y1 Y1 --> Z1[World Class Manufacturing] ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Six Sigma Project in Manufacturing ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph inputLayer [Inputs to Project] I1[Customer Complaints] I2[Scrap Reports] I3[Machine OEE Data] I4[Supplier Rejection Data] end subgraph processLayer [DMAIC Process] D1[Define: Charter and CTQ] M1[Measure: DPMO and FPY] A1[Analyze: Fishbone and DOE] I2c[Improve: Pilot Solution] C1[Control: SPC and SOP] end subgraph toolLayer [Tools Used] T1[Minitab or JMP] T2[FMEA and Pareto] T3[Control Charts] T4[Regression and ANOVA] end subgraph outputLayer [Outcomes] O1[Reduced DPMO] O2[Improved Cpk] O3[Lower COPQ] O4[Higher RTY] end I1 --> D1 I2 --> D1 I3 --> D1 I4 --> D1 D1 --> M1 --> A1 --> I2c --> C1 T1 --> M1 T2 --> A1 T3 --> C1 T4 --> A1 C1 --> O1 C1 --> O2 C1 --> O3 C1 --> O4 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define DPMO. Why is it the preferred metric in Six Sigma manufacturing projects over simple percentage defectives?** **Model Answer:** DPMO stands for **Defects Per Million Opportunities**. It is calculated as: $$DPMO = \frac{D}{U \times OP} \times 10^{6}$$ where $D$ is the number of defects, $U$ is the number of units, and $OP$ is the number of opportunities per unit. DPMO is preferred over simple percentage defectives because: (i) it **accounts for complexity** of the product (more opportunities = more chances of defects), allowing fair comparison between a simple and a complex part; (ii) it is **scalable and standardized** across plants, product lines, and industries; (iii) it maps directly to the **sigma level** of the process, enabling translation to a single quality benchmark; and (iv) it exposes **hidden complexity-driven quality loss** that percentage defectives would mask. In Six Sigma, the **6σ target corresponds to 3.4 DPMO**, which is the universal manufacturing quality goal. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between FPY and RTY. Why is RTY a more powerful indicator of manufacturing line performance?** **Model Answer:** **FPY (First Pass Yield)** is the ratio of good units produced in a single step *without rework* to the total units that entered that step: $$FPY = \frac{U_{good}}{U_{total}}$$ **RTY (Rolled Throughput Yield)** is the product of the FPYs of *all sequential* process steps: $$RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_{i}$$ RTY is more powerful because it captures the **multiplicative compounding of defects** across a multi-step line. For example, four stations each with 95% FPY yield an RTY of $0.95^{4} = 0.815$, meaning the line produces **18.5% defectives** end-to-end even though each station *appears* acceptable in isolation. RTY drives the line operator and the Six Sigma team to **eliminate defects at every station simultaneously**, which is the core manufacturing insight of Six Sigma. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]** **Q. (a)** A welding cell produces chassis sub-assemblies with **4 critical CTQ characteristics**. Over a shift producing **1,500 units**, the quality team records **120 defects**. Compute the DPU, DPO, DPMO, and the corresponding long-term sigma level. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A stamping–welding–painting line has FPYs of 0.96, 0.94, and 0.92 at the three stations. Compute the RTY. If 10,000 raw blanks enter the line in a day, how many defectives (rework + scrap) are produced? Comment on the impact on the plant's daily COPQ assuming a rework cost of ₹400 per defective unit. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** **Step 1 — Given data:** $U = 1500$ units, $D = 120$ defects, $OP = 4$ opportunities per unit. **Step 2 — DPU:** $$DPU = \frac{D}{U} = \frac{120}{1500} = 0.08 \text{ defects per unit}$$ **Step 3 — DPO:** $$DPO = \frac{D}{U \times OP} = \frac{120}{1500 \times 4} = \frac{120}{6000} = 0.02$$ **Step 4 — DPMO:** $$DPMO = 0.02 \times 10^{6} = 20000 \text{ DPMO}$$ **Step 5 — Yield:** $$Y_{\%} = (1 - 0.02) \times 100 = 98.00\%$$ **Step 6 — Sigma level lookup:** From the standard conversion table, a long-term yield of 98.00% corresponds to a **sigma level of approximately 3.7σ** (long-term). The short-term sigma is $3.7 + 1.5 = 5.2\sigma$. > **Interpretation:** The welding cell operates at 3.7σ long-term, well below the 6σ goal. A Six Sigma project would target reducing DPMO from 20,000 toward 3.4 by improving weld parameters (current, voltage, travel speed), electrode dressing schedule, and operator training. --- **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Step 1 — RTY calculation:** $$RTY = 0.96 \times 0.94 \times 0.92$$ Multiplying stepwise: $$0.96 \times 0.94 = 0.9024$$ $$0.9024 \times 0.92 = 0.830208$$ $$RTY = 0.8302 \text{ or } 83.02\%$$ **Step 2 — Defectives per 10,000 raw blanks:** $$N_{good} = 10000 \times 0.8302 = 8302 \text{ good units}$$ $$N_{defective} = 10000 - 8302 = 1698 \text{ defectives}$$ **Step 3 — Daily COPQ impact:** $$COPQ_{daily} = 1698 \times \text{₹}400 = \text{₹}6{,}79{,}200$$ > **Interpretation:** The plant loses approximately **₹6.79 lakh per day** purely in rework cost. A Six Sigma project that improves the three FPYs by just 2 percentage points each (to 0.98, 0.96, 0.94) would raise RTY to 0.8841, reduce defectives to 1159, and **save roughly ₹2.15 lakh per day**, i.e., **over ₹78 crore per year** at 365 working days. This dramatic figure is what justifies DMAIC projects to senior management. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Internal Choice]** **Q. (a)** Explain the **DMAIC methodology** as applied to a manufacturing Six Sigma project. For each phase, list two specific tools and one deliverable. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A CNC turning process produces a shaft with specification **25.00 ± 0.04 mm** (USL = 25.04, LSL = 24.96). The process mean is **μ = 25.015 mm** and the long-term standard deviation is **σ = 0.010 mm**. Compute Cp, Cpk, the upper and lower Z values, and the expected DPMO. State whether the process is Six Sigma capable. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Define Phase** - *Tools:* SIPOC diagram, Project Charter, VOC-CTQ matrix, COPQ estimation. - *Deliverable:* Approved **Project Charter** with problem statement, goal, scope, team, and timeline. **Measure Phase** - *Tools:* Data Collection Plan, Gage R&R, Process Mapping, baseline DPMO/FPY computation, capability histograms. - *Deliverable:* **Validated Measurement System** and a **Baseline Sigma Level** for the process. **Analyze Phase** - *Tools:* Pareto chart, Ishikawa (Fishbone) diagram, 5 Whys analysis, hypothesis tests (t-test, chi-square), regression, ANOVA, FMEA. - *Deliverable:* **Validated list of vital few root causes (Xs)** statistically proven to drive the defect (Y). **Improve Phase** - *Tools:* Brainstorming, Design of Experiments (DOE), pilot runs, mistake-proofing (poka-yoke), cost-benefit analysis. - *Deliverable:* **Implemented, statistically validated improvement** with quantified sigma level lift and financial payback. **Control Phase** - *Tools:* Statistical Process Control (X-bar R, p, c, u charts), Control Plan, Standard Operating Procedures, FMEA update, training. - *Deliverable:* **Sustained gains** monitored via live SPC charts, with a documented **Reaction Plan** for out-of-control signals. --- **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Step 1 — Given data:** $USL = 25.04$ mm, $LSL = 24.96$ mm, $\mu = 25.015$ mm, $\sigma = 0.010$ mm. **Step 2 — Cp:** $$Cp = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.04 - 24.96}{6 \times 0.010} = \frac{0.08}{0.060} = 1.333$$ **Step 3 — Cpk:** $$Cpu = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.04 - 25.015}{3 \times 0.010} = \frac{0.025}{0.030} = 0.833$$ $$Cpl = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.015 - 24.96}{3 \times 0.010} = \frac{0.055}{0.030} = 1.833$$ $$Cpk = \min(0.833,\; 1.833) = 0.833$$ **Step 4 — Z values:** $$Z_{upper} = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{25.04 - 25.015}{0.010} = 2.5$$ $$Z_{lower} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma} = \frac{25.015 - 24.96}{0.010} = 5.5$$ **Step 5 — Defect probabilities (ppm):** - Above USL: $P(Z > 2.5) \approx 6210$ ppm - Below LSL: $P(Z > 5.5) \approx 1.9$ ppm - **Total DPMO ≈ 6212** **Step 6 — Sigma level:** 6212 DPMO corresponds to a long-term yield of 99.38% and a **sigma level of approximately 4.05σ (long-term)**. > **Conclusion:** Although $Cp = 1.333$ (acceptable spread), $Cpk = 0.833$ shows the process is **shifted toward the USL**. It is **NOT Six Sigma capable** — a Six Sigma capable process requires $Cpk \geq 1.50$ (or at minimum $Cpk \geq 1.33$ in production contexts). The corrective action is to **recenter the process mean** to 25.00 mm (i.e., shift the CNC tool offset by $-0.015$ mm) and tighten the standard deviation through improved tooling and tighter statistical process control. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift:** Many students compute short-term sigma and present it as long-term (or vice versa). The 1.5σ shift is what links short-term capability to long-term DPMO. Always declare which one you are reporting. > 2. **Using `|` inside markdown tables:** When writing `|Z|` or `|x|` inside a table cell, the pipe breaks the markdown table structure and the answer renders as garbage. Use `\vert Z \vert` or `\vert x \vert`. > 3. **Mixing up Cp and Cpk:** A common error is to report $Cpk = 1.33$ as "Six Sigma capable." It is not. $Cpk \geq 1.50$ is the canonical Six Sigma target for centered processes. Many KTU paper answers lose 2 marks for missing this distinction. > 4. **Confusing RTY with FPY:** A line with three 95% FPYs is not 95% good — it is 85.7% good. Forgetting to multiply (instead of averaging) is a frequent mistake. > 5. **DPMO with wrong units:** Always multiply by $10^6$ and ensure the denominator is $U \times OP$, not just $U$. Forgetting $OP$ makes the answer off by a factor equal to the number of opportunities. > 6. **Skipping units in final answer:** Always mention "DPMO," "ppm," "mm," "₹" etc. Examiners reward dimensional discipline. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Summary — Must Memorize for KTU Exam** - **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 99.99966% yield** (long-term, with 1.5σ shift). - **DMAIC = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control** — the canonical Six Sigma project roadmap. - **DPMO formula:** $DPMO = \dfrac{D}{U \times OP} \times 10^{6}$. - **RTY = product of FPYs of all sequential steps** — defects *compound*, they do not add. - **DPU = D / U** ; **DPO = D / (U × OP)** ; **DPMO = DPO × 10^6**. - **Cp measures spread** (USL − LSL) ÷ 6σ ; **Cpk measures centering** = min(Cpu, Cpl). - **Cpk ≥ 1.33 = capable ; Cpk ≥ 1.50 = Six Sigma capable ; Cpk ≥ 2.00 = world-class.** - **1.5σ shift** bridges short-term (ST) and long-term (LT) sigma levels: $\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_{ST} - 1.5$. - **Sigma–yield table** (LT): 3σ = 66,807 ppm ; 4σ = 6,210 ppm ; 4.5σ = 1,350 ppm ; 5σ = 233 ppm ; 6σ = 3.4 ppm. - **Tools by phase:** Define → SIPOC, Charter, CTQ tree. Measure → DCP, Gage R&R, FPY, Cpk. Analyze → Pareto, Fishbone, 5 Whys, hypothesis test, regression. Improve → DOE, pilot, poka-yoke. Control → SPC charts, Control Plan, SOP. - **Lean Six Sigma = Lean (waste removal) + Six Sigma (variation removal).** Eight wastes: TIMWOODS + Skills. - **COPQ = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure** — used to financially justify every project. - **FMEA, DOE, SPC, Gage R&R, VSM, Kaizen** are the manufacturing-friendly Six Sigma tools. - **Always state units, state the sigma level type (ST or LT), and use proper LaTeX (no `|` inside markdown tables).** - **Six Sigma was born in manufacturing** (Motorola, 1987, semiconductors) — its strongest domain is still the **shop floor**. - **Green Belt / Black Belt / Master Black Belt** are the certified roles that execute manufacturing Six Sigma projects. - **Vocabulary precision matters in KTU answers:** FPY ≠ RTY, Cp ≠ Cpk, ST sigma ≠ LT sigma, DPU ≠ DPO ≠ DPMO.

topics.title: Six Sigma in Service Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT752 - SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Six Sigma in Service Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Six Sigma in Service Industries** refers to the disciplined, data-driven methodology and quality management framework adapted from manufacturing environments to **transactional and service-oriented processes**, where the "defect" is redefined as any event that fails to meet the **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** specifications of the customer. It aims to reduce process variation, eliminate defects, and improve customer satisfaction in intangible, people-driven operations such as banking, healthcare, hospitality, insurance, telecommunications, and Information Technology Enabled Services (ITES).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight:** In a service context, a *defect* is any failure to meet customer expectations — a wrong billing, a delayed flight, a dropped call, or an unresolved complaint. The goal remains **3.4 DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)**, but the *opportunity* is redefined per transaction.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Restaurant Analogy"

Imagine a fine-dining restaurant. A *manufacturing* defect is a burnt pizza. Easy to count. A *service* defect is:
- The waiter taking 18 minutes to arrive (latency defect)
- The bill having an incorrect total (accuracy defect)
- The waiter forgetting dietary preferences (empathy defect)

You cannot weigh a "smile" or measure a "polite tone" on a caliper. Six Sigma in services therefore uses **proxy metrics** — turnaround time, first-call resolution rate, Net Promoter Score (NPS), customer effort score — and translates them into a **sigma level** equivalent to manufacturing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction Table for Quick Recall**

| Aspect | Manufacturing Six Sigma | Service Six Sigma |
|---|---|---|
| Output type | Tangible product | Intangible transaction |
| Defect visibility | Physical (visible) | Experiential (perceived) |
| Process flow | Linear / assembly line | Cyclical / customer-interactive |
| Data source | Sensors, gauges | Surveys, transaction logs |
| Customer interaction | At the end | Throughout the process |
| Tangible "opportunity" | Easy to define | Must be carefully defined |

## 1.3 Why Service Industries Need a Modified Six Sigma

1. **Intangibility** — services cannot be inventoried or inspected before delivery.
2. **Heterogeneity** — every customer interaction is unique.
3. **Inseparability** — production and consumption occur simultaneously.
4. **Perishability** — an empty airline seat or an unused hospital bed cannot be "stored" for later.

These four characteristics (the **IHIP framework**) make quality control far more challenging than in manufacturing, justifying a specialized Six Sigma service methodology.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Service Six Sigma Framework — Logic Breakdown

### Step 1: Identify the Voice of the Customer (VOC)
Capture what customers *actually* want through:
- **Surveys** (CSAT, NPS, CES)
- **Focus groups** and structured interviews
- **Complaint logs** and social listening
- **Kano model** analysis (basic vs. performance vs. excitement needs)

### Step 2: Translate VOC into Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) Requirements
CTQs in services are typically expressed as:
- **Wait time** $\le$ X minutes
- **Accuracy** $\ge$ 99%
- **First Call Resolution (FCR)** $\ge$ 85%
- **Abandonment rate** $\le$ 5%

### Step 3: Define the "Opportunity"
A single service transaction may contain **multiple opportunities** for a defect. For example, a credit card bill:
- Correct customer name
- Correct account number
- Correct charges
- Correct due date
- Correct minimum payment

That is **5 opportunities per transaction**.

### Step 4: Measure Defects Per Opportunity (DPO) and DPMO

$$\text{DPO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \text{DPO} \times 1{,}000{,}000$$

### Step 5: Convert DPMO to Sigma Level using the Standard Normal Distribution

$$Z_{\text{bench}} = \text{NORMSINV}\left(1 - \text{Defect Rate} + 1.5\,\text{shift}\right)$$

The **1.5 sigma shift** is the industry convention to account for long-term drift.

### Step 6: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) for End-to-End Process

$$\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$

where $Y_i$ is the First Pass Yield of subprocess $i$. Service processes often have many sub-steps, making RTY a critical KTU question.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master this table — every KTU Part B question on this module uses at least one of these formulas.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{DPO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O}$ | Defects Per Opportunity | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{DPMO} = \text{DPO} \times 10^{6}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | ppm equivalent |
| 3 | $\text{Defect Rate} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O}$ | Fractional defect rate | Proportion (0–1) |
| 4 | $\text{Yield} = 1 - \text{Defect Rate}$ | First Pass Yield | Proportion (0–1) |
| 5 | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Rolled Throughput Yield | Proportion (0–1) |
| 6 | $\text{Normality} = \text{RTY}^{(1/n)}$ | Normalized yield per step | Proportion (0–1) |
| 7 | $Z = \text{NORMSINV}(\text{Yield}) + 1.5$ | Sigma level (with 1.5 shift) | Sigma units |
| 8 | $\text{Cycle Time}_{\sigma} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process capability index basis | Time / units |
| 9 | $\text{Customer Effort Score (CES)}$ | Average rating on 1–7 scale | Score |
| 10 | $\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$ | Net Promoter Score | Percentage points |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Management Fields

- **Banking & Finance:** Reducing errors in loan processing, KYC, and ATM reconciliation.
- **Healthcare:** Reducing surgical site infections, patient wait times, and billing errors.
- **IT & BPO:** Improving ticket resolution, software defect density (per KLOC), and SLA adherence.
- **Hospitality:** Reducing check-in time and guest complaints.
- **Telecom:** Reducing dropped calls and improving network uptime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Transactional Six Sigma (TSS)** — a variant by Motorola and GE — is specifically tailored for back-office service processes (order entry, claims processing). It is heavily tested in KTU board exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Sigma Level Calculation for a Banking Process

### Problem Statement
A regional bank processes **2,000 loan applications per month**. Each application has **8 critical data fields** (name, address, income, employment, loan amount, tenure, collateral, signature). Auditing reveals **640 defects** in one month. Calculate:
1. DPO
2. DPMO
3. Defect Rate
4. First Pass Yield
5. Sigma Level (with 1.5 sigma shift)

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the given parameters.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= 2000 \quad \text{(units processed)} \\
O &= 8 \quad \text{(opportunities per unit)} \\
D &= 640 \quad \text{(defects observed)} \\
\text{Total Opportunities} &= U \times O = 2000 \times 8 = 16{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate DPO (Defects Per Opportunity).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPO} &= \frac{D}{U \times O} \\
&= \frac{640}{16{,}000} \\
&= 0.0400 \;\text{defects per opportunity}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This means on average, 4 out of every 100 opportunities contained an error.

**Step 3: Convert DPO to DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \text{DPO} \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 0.0400 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 40{,}000 \;\text{DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Scaling up to a million opportunities makes the metric comparable across processes of different sizes.

**Step 4: Compute the Defect Rate (as a fraction).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Defect Rate} &= \frac{D}{U \times O} = 0.0400 \\
\text{or}\quad \text{Defect Rate (\%)} &= 0.0400 \times 100 = 4.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the First Pass Yield (FPY).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FPY} &= 1 - \text{Defect Rate} \\
&= 1 - 0.0400 \\
&= 0.9600 \;\text{or}\; 96.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* 96% of all opportunities pass without defect.

**Step 6: Convert Yield to a Z-Score (short-term sigma).**

We must find the value $Z$ such that the area under the standard normal curve to the left equals 0.9600.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(Z) &= 0.9600 \\
Z &= \text{NORMSINV}(0.9600) \\
Z &= 1.7507
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This is the number of standard deviations between the process mean and the nearest specification limit.

**Step 7: Apply the 1.5 Sigma Shift for long-term performance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Long-Term Sigma Level} &= Z + 1.5 \\
&= 1.7507 + 1.5 \\
&= 3.2507 \approx 3.25\,\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Conclusion:**
The bank's loan processing operates at approximately **3.25 sigma**, which corresponds to roughly **66,807 DPMO** in standard Six Sigma conversion tables — far below the world-class 6σ benchmark (3.4 DPMO). The bank has significant room for DMAIC improvement.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — DMAIC Applied to Services

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below is a high-yield KTU topic. Memorize how each phase differs in a service context versus manufacturing.

| DMAIC Phase | Manufacturing Focus | Service Industry Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Define** | Product CTQs, supplier specs | VOC, CTQ trees, SIPOC of customer journey |
| **Measure** | Cpk, Cp, gauge R&R | DPMO, CES, CSAT, FCR, RTY |
| **Analyze** | Fishbone on machine, material | Fishbone on People, Process, Policy, Place |
| **Improve** | DOE, poka-yoke | Lean service redesign, Kaizen, error-proofing scripts |
| **Control** | SPC charts, control plans | Standard Operating Procedures (SOP), call audits, dashboards |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: RTY Calculation in a Multi-Stage Service

A hospital patient journey has 4 subprocesses with yields:
- Registration: $Y_1 = 0.95$
- Triage: $Y_2 = 0.92$
- Diagnosis: $Y_3 = 0.90$
- Discharge: $Y_4 = 0.88$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY} &= Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3 \times Y_4 \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.90 \times 0.88 \\
&= 0.6922 \;\text{or}\; 69.22\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* Despite each step having >88% yield, end-to-end only **69.22%** of patients complete a fully error-free journey. This is the **"hidden factory"** phenomenon KTU examiners love to test.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Service Six Sigma DMAIC Flow — Mermaid Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define VOC and CTQ] --> B[SIPOC Process Mapping]
    B --> C[Measure DPMO and Yield]
    C --> D[Analyze Root Causes]
    D --> E[Improve via Lean and Kaizen]
    E --> F[Control via SOP and Audits]
    F --> G[Customer Satisfaction]
    G -.feedback.-> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each node represents a DMAIC milestone. The dashed feedback loop from G back to A represents the closed-loop nature of continuous improvement in service environments — every satisfied (or dissatisfied) customer generates new VOC data.

## 4.2 Service Quality Dimensions — SERVQUAL Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SERVQUAL[SERVQUAL Model]
        direction TB
        S1[Tangibles Physical Evidence]
        S2[Reliability Dependable Service]
        S3[Responsiveness Prompt Service]
        S4[Assurance Trust and Confidence]
        S5[Empathy Caring Individual Attention]
    end
    A[Customer Expectation] --> SERVQUAL
    SERVQUAL --> B[Customer Perception]
    B --> C{Service Gap}
    C --> D[Six Sigma DMAIC Closure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** SERVQUAL measures the gap between customer *expectation* and *perception* across 5 dimensions. Six Sigma DMAIC is used to systematically close this gap. This is a frequently tested conceptual question in KTU exams.

## 4.3 Service Defect Taxonomy — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Service Defect] --> R1[Latency Defect]
    R --> R2[Accuracy Defect]
    R --> R3[Courtesy Defect]
    R --> R4[Completeness Defect]
    R --> R5[Compliance Defect]
    R1 --> M[Counted via DPMO Metric]
    R2 --> M
    R3 --> M
    R4 --> M
    R5 --> M
    M --> Z[Sigma Level Conversion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Service defects are not homogeneous. They span five categories that must all be captured into a single DPMO metric, which is then translated into a sigma level for benchmarking against the world-class 6σ threshold.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Manufacturing Six Sigma and Service Six Sigma. Mention any four differences.**

**Model Answer:**
Manufacturing Six Sigma deals with **tangible products** on an assembly line, where defects are physical (e.g., a cracked component) and easily measurable via gauges. Service Six Sigma deals with **intangible transactions** where defects are experiential (e.g., a wrong bill, rude behavior) and measured via surveys and transactional logs.

**Four key differences:**

1. **Output type** — Product vs. Service transaction.
2. **Customer involvement** — End-of-line in manufacturing vs. throughout in service.
3. **Defect detection** — Visual/automated inspection vs. customer feedback.
4. **Process variation source** — Machine, material, method vs. People, process, policy, place.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is DPMO? How is it calculated for a service process?**

**Model Answer:**
**DPMO** stands for **Defects Per Million Opportunities**. It is a normalized metric that allows comparison of defect rates across processes of different sizes and complexities.

For a service process, the formula is:

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 1{,}000{,}000$$

In services, *units* typically represent transactions (e.g., 1 loan application = 1 unit), and *opportunities per unit* are the CTQ fields within that transaction. For example, a hospital admission form might have 12 opportunities (name, age, ID, diagnosis, allergies, etc.).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the SERVQUAL model and its five dimensions with examples from the banking sector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BPO company processes 5,000 customer orders per week. Each order has 6 critical fields. In a sample week, 450 defects are recorded.
   - (i) Calculate DPO, DPMO, and Defect Rate.
   - (ii) Determine the process sigma level (with 1.5σ shift).
   - (iii) Comment on the process capability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**SERVQUAL Model Definition:**
SERVQUAL (Service Quality Gap Model) was developed by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1988)**. It measures service quality as the gap between customer *expectations* and *perceptions* across five dimensions.

**Five Dimensions with Banking Examples:**

1. **Tangibles** — Physical evidence like clean branches, functional ATMs, neat uniforms of staff.
2. **Reliability** — Ability to perform the promised service dependably, e.g., loan disbursement within the promised 7 working days.
3. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help customers promptly, e.g., call center answering within 20 seconds.
4. **Assurance** — Knowledge and courtesy of staff that inspires trust, e.g., a relationship manager explaining complex FD schemes confidently.
5. **Empathy** — Caring, individualized attention, e.g., dedicated priority banking for senior citizens.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- $U = 5000$ units (orders per week)
- $O = 6$ opportunities per unit
- $D = 450$ defects

**Step 1: Total Opportunities**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Opportunities} &= U \times O \\
&= 5000 \times 6 = 30{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: DPO**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPO} &= \frac{D}{U \times O} \\
&= \frac{450}{30{,}000} \\
&= 0.015
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: DPMO**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= 0.015 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 15{,}000 \;\text{DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Defect Rate and Yield**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Defect Rate} &= 0.015 \;\text{or}\; 1.5\% \\
\text{Yield} &= 1 - 0.015 = 0.985
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Z-Score**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(Z) &= 0.985 \\
Z &= \text{NORMSINV}(0.985) \\
Z &\approx 2.1701
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Long-Term Sigma Level**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{LT}} &= Z + 1.5 \\
&= 2.1701 + 1.5 \\
&= 3.6701 \approx 3.67\,\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Comment on Capability**
The process operates at **3.67 sigma**, which equates to approximately **15,000 DPMO**. This means the BPO produces around 15,000 errors per million opportunities — vastly worse than the world-class 6σ benchmark of 3.4 DPMO. The process is **not capable** of meeting Six Sigma standards and requires intensive DMAIC intervention, especially in the Analyze and Improve phases.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) in service industries. Why is RTY more critical in services than in manufacturing? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A telecom company's customer onboarding has 5 subprocesses. The first-pass yields are: 0.96, 0.94, 0.91, 0.89, 0.85. Calculate:
   - (i) The overall RTY.
   - (ii) The Defect Rate and DPMO for the entire process (assume 1 opportunity per subprocess).
   - (iii) The long-term sigma level of the end-to-end process. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**RTY Definition:**
Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) is the probability that a single unit (or customer transaction) will pass through an entire multi-step process without any defect at any stage. It is the **product of the first-pass yields of all subprocesses**.

$$\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$

**Why RTY is More Critical in Services:**

1. **Many sequential touchpoints** — A typical service journey (e.g., hospital visit) has 10+ customer touchpoints, each a potential failure.
2. **Hidden factory effect** — Even if each step has 95% yield, 10 sequential steps yield only $0.95^{10} \approx 0.598$ or 59.8% end-to-end success.
3. **Cumulative customer dissatisfaction** — Each failure compounds negative perception.
4. **Hard to rework** — Unlike a manufactured widget, a failed service (e.g., a missed hotel reservation) cannot be re-run identically.
5. **Customer churn risk** — Poor RTY directly translates to customer attrition.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given:** $Y_1 = 0.96$, $Y_2 = 0.94$, $Y_3 = 0.91$, $Y_4 = 0.89$, $Y_5 = 0.85$

**Step 1: Compute RTY**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY} &= 0.96 \times 0.94 \times 0.91 \times 0.89 \times 0.85 \\
&= 0.96 \times 0.94 = 0.9024 \\
&= 0.9024 \times 0.91 = 0.8212 \\
&= 0.8212 \times 0.89 = 0.7309 \\
&= 0.7309 \times 0.85 = 0.6213
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\text{RTY} \approx 0.6213 \;\text{or}\; 62.13\%}$$

**Step 2: End-to-End Defect Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Defect Rate} &= 1 - \text{RTY} \\
&= 1 - 0.6213 = 0.3787 \;\text{or}\; 37.87\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: DPMO**
With 5 opportunities per transaction (1 per subprocess):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= 0.3787 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 378{,}700 \;\text{DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Long-Term Sigma Level**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(Z) &= 0.6213 \\
Z &= \text{NORMSINV}(0.6213) \\
Z &\approx 0.3085
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{LT}} &= 0.3085 + 1.5 \\
&= 1.8085 \approx 1.81\,\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**
The end-to-end onboarding process operates at only **1.81 sigma** — catastrophically poor. Roughly 62% of customers complete an error-free onboarding, while 38% experience at least one defect. Urgent DMAIC intervention is mandatory, particularly focusing on subprocesses 4 and 5 (which have the lowest individual yields of 0.89 and 0.85).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift.** The question ALWAYS expects long-term sigma — add 1.5 to the NORMSINV result.
> 2. **Confusing DPMO with percentage.** DPMO is "per million," so always multiply by $10^6$, not 100.
> 3. **Wrong opportunity count.** If a form has 10 fields, the opportunity is 10 per form, NOT 1.
> 4. **Using NORMSDIST instead of NORMSINV.** NORMSDIST gives probability from Z; we need the inverse (NORMSINV) to get Z from yield.
> 5. **Skipping the comment/conclusion.** KTU board examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the qualitative interpretation.
> 6. **RTY mistakes** — students sometimes ADD yields instead of multiplying them. RTY is always a **product**, never a sum.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Six Sigma in services** = data-driven defect reduction in intangible, customer-facing processes.
- ✅ The **IHIP characteristics** (Intangibility, Heterogeneity, Inseparability, Perishability) make services uniquely challenging.
- ✅ **DPMO** is the universal metric: $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$.
- ✅ Always add the **1.5σ shift** when converting yield to a sigma level.
- ✅ **RTY** is the product of all subprocess yields — critical for multi-step service journeys.
- ✅ **SERVQUAL** has 5 dimensions: Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy.
- ✅ DMAIC in services emphasizes **VOC, SOPs, call audits, and people-focused root cause analysis**.
- ✅ **Transactional Six Sigma (TSS)** is the variant used for back-office service work.
- ✅ **CTQ trees** translate fuzzy customer comments into measurable specifications.
- ✅ World-class target: **6σ = 3.4 DPMO** — same target as manufacturing, regardless of industry.
